topics.title: Nature of Printing Inks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT866 - TECHNOLOGY OF PRINTING INKS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Nature of Printing Inks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT] KTU Board Definition
> **Printing Ink** is a complex, colloidal, multi-component heterogeneous mixture (or dispersion) composed essentially of **colorants** (pigments or dyes), **vehicles/binders** (resins, oils, or solvents), and **additives** (driers, plasticizers, waxes), formulated to transfer an image from a printing plate or image carrier onto a substrate (paper, plastic, metal, etc.) by means of a controlled printing process, and subsequently to dry/harden to form a permanent, functional, and aesthetically acceptable film.

In the language of **Surface Coating & Rheology**, printing ink is defined as a **shear-thinning (pseudoplastic) non-Newtonian fluid** that must possess:
1. Adequate flow to transfer from the fountain/duct to the plate.
2. Sufficient viscosity at rest to remain on the printing form.
3. The ability to wet the substrate uniformly.
4. The capability of converting into a solid, adherent film after impression.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition (Plain-English View)

> [!NOTE] Real-World Analogy: "The Sandwich Analogy"
> Imagine a printing ink as a **gourmet sandwich**:
>
> | Layer | Component | Real-World Function |
> |---|---|---|
> | **The Filling (visible part)** | **Pigment / Dye** | The "taste" — gives color, opacity, and visual identity (e.g., the cheese slice). |
> | **The Bread (binding structure)** | **Vehicle / Binder (Resin & Oil)** | The "structure" — holds everything together and sticks the sandwich to the plate (e.g., the bread). |
> | **The Spread / Sauce (functional boosters)** | **Additives (Driers, Waxes, Plasticizers)** | The "mustard/butter" — small amounts that dramatically improve performance, texture, and shelf life. |
>
> Just as a sandwich must be **soft enough to bite (flow)**, **firm enough to hold shape (viscosity)**, and **taste good (color/optical density)**, a printing ink must simultaneously satisfy multiple physical, optical, and chemical demands — making it a masterpiece of **formulation chemistry**.

---

## 1.3 Key Physical & Chemical Metrics of Printing Inks

> [!IMPORTANT] Standard Engineering Metrics
> - **Viscosity** — typically **5 Pa·s to 100 Pa·s** depending on process (flexo at low end, offset/letterpress at high end).
> - **Tack** — measured on a **Tack-o-scope** or **Inkometer**; values range from **5 to 25** units.
> - **Pigment-to-Binder Ratio (P/B)** — typically **1:1 to 1:4** by weight.
> - **Particle Size of Pigment** — must be below **5 µm** (preferably 1 µm) for clean print and gloss.
> - **Optical Density (OD)** — measured via densitometer; target **1.2 – 1.8** for process colors.
> - **pH (for water-based inks)** — must be maintained between **8.0 – 9.5** for fountain stability.
> - **Fineness of Grind (NPIRI Gauge)** — must read **0 – 10 µm** for offset inks.

---

## 1.4 Why "Nature of Printing Inks" Matters in KTU Examinations

The KTU 2024 syllabus places this topic at the **foundation** of the course because every subsequent module (drying mechanisms, pigments, vehicles, rheology, print quality) is built upon understanding the **fundamental nature** — composition, classification, and property spectrum — of the ink itself. Examiners frequently test this topic using **Part A (3-mark)** definitions and **Part B (14-mark)** comparative/classification questions.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Printing Ink Composition

A printing ink, irrespective of its type (offset, flexo, gravure, screen, digital), is fundamentally structured around **three functional pillars**:

### Pillar 1: Colorants (Pigments & Dyes)
- **Pigments** are **insoluble**, finely divided solid particles that are **dispersed** (not dissolved) in the vehicle. They provide color, opacity, and chemical resistance.
- **Dyes** are **soluble** in the vehicle; they provide transparency and brilliance but poor lightfastness.
- Selection is governed by **chemical structure** (azo, phthalocyanine, quinacridone classes) and **crystallinity**.

### Pillar 2: Vehicle (Binder + Solvent)
- The vehicle is the **liquid carrier** that transports the pigment to the substrate and binds it after drying.
- It consists of:
  - **Resins / Binders** (film-forming polymers) — e.g., rosin-modified phenolics, alkyds, acrylics, polyamides.
  - **Solvents / Oils** — mineral oils (heatset), vegetable oils (sheetfed offset), water (flexo), or organic solvents (gravure).

### Pillar 3: Additives (Performance Modifiers)
- Added in small quantities (typically 0.1% – 5%) to fine-tune properties:
  - **Driers** (metal salts of cobalt, manganese, lead) — catalyze oxidative polymerization.
  - **Waxes** (PE, PTFE) — improve slip and rub resistance.
  - **Plasticizers** — improve film flexibility.
  - **Anti-oxidants / Anti-set-off compounds** — prevent premature drying.
  - **Surfactants / Dispersants** — stabilize pigment dispersion.
  - **Defoamers** — eliminate foam in water-based systems.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Table)

> [!NOTE] Use `\vert` or `\mid` for vertical bars (avoid raw `|` to preserve markdown tables).

| # | Property | Formula / Relationship | Units | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Viscosity (dynamic)** | $\tau = \eta \cdot \dot{\gamma}$ | Pa·s | Resistance to flow |
| 2 | **Kinematic viscosity** | $\nu = \eta / \rho$ | m²/s | Flow under gravity |
| 3 | **Tack** | $T = F / A$ | N/m² (or Tack units) | Resistance to film splitting |
| 4 | **Pigment Volume Concentration (PVC)** | $PVC = \dfrac{V_{\text{pigment}}}{V_{\text{pigment}} + V_{\text{binder}}} \times 100\%$ | % | Determines gloss, opacity, porosity |
| 5 | **Oil Absorption (OA)** | $OA = \dfrac{m_{\text{linseed oil}}}{m_{\text{pigment}}} \times 100$ | g/100g | Indicates pigment wettability |
| 6 | **Optical Density (OD)** | $OD = \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{I_{0}}{I_{t}}\right)$ | dimensionless | Color strength measurement |
| 7 | **Fineness of Grind (Hegman)** | $H = 8 - \dfrac{F}{10}$ (approx.) | µm | Dispersion quality |
| 8 | **Yield Stress (Bingham model)** | $\tau = \tau_{0} + \eta_{p} \cdot \dot{\gamma}$ | Pa | Min. stress to initiate flow |
| 9 | **Power-Law (Ostwald-de Waele)** | $\tau = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{n}$ | Pa | Pseudoplastic behavior |
| 10 | **Critical Pigment Volume Concentration (CPVC)** | $CPVC = \dfrac{V_{\text{pigment}}}{V_{\text{pigment}} + V_{\text{binder oil absorbed}}}$ | % | Transition point in film properties |

---

## 2.3 Classification of Printing Inks — Operational Logic

> [!IMPORTANT] KTU Board Favourite Classification
> Printing inks are classified by **five primary criteria**:

### A. By Printing Process
1. **Offset (Lithographic) Inks** — paste inks, high viscosity, oxidative/UV drying.
2. **Letterpress Inks** — very high viscosity, similar to offset.
3. **Flexographic Inks** — low viscosity, solvent/water-based, evaporation drying.
4. **Gravure Inks** — very low viscosity, solvent-based, evaporation drying.
5. **Screen Printing Inks** — very high viscosity, thick films.
6. **Digital / Inkjet Inks** — extremely low viscosity, Newtonian, jetting-based.

### B. By Drying Mechanism
- **Oxidative Polymerization** (sheetfed offset)
- **Absorption / Penetration** (news ink, water-based flexo on paper)
- **Evaporation** (heatset web, gravure, flexo)
- **Radiation Curing** (UV, EB)
- **Cold Setting / Quick-Set** (resin-resin separation)
- **Precipitation / Coagulation** (water-based flexo on non-absorbent substrates)

### C. By Substrate
- **Paper & Board** (most common)
- **Plastics (PE, PP, PET, PVC)** — requires specific adhesion promoters.
- **Metals (Al foils, Tinplate)** — requires heat-resistant systems.
- **Glass & Ceramics** — requires high-temperature cure.

### D. By Color
- **Process Inks** (CMYK: Cyan, Magenta, Yellow, Key/Black).
- **Spot Color Inks** (Pantone-matched).
- **Specialty Inks** (metallic, fluorescent, pearlescent).

### E. By Chemistry
- **Oil-based** (traditional)
- **Water-based** (eco-friendly)
- **Solvent-based** (high performance)
- **UV/EB curable** (instant cure)
- **Electron-beam curable** (no photoinitiator needed).

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Understanding the nature of printing inks is **non-negotiable** in industries such as:
- **Packaging industry** (food-safe ink selection — migration limits per **EU Regulation 1935/2004**).
- **Publishing industry** (heatset web-offset for magazines).
- **Security printing** (banknotes, passports — using intaglio + offset combinations).
- **Decorative printing** (wallpapers, laminates, transfer printing).
- **Functional printing** (printed electronics, RFID antennas — conductive inks).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Pigment Volume Concentration (PVC) and Its Relationship to Film Properties

The **Pigment Volume Concentration (PVC)** is the single most important formulation parameter governing the optical, mechanical, and barrier properties of an ink film. Below is the complete step-by-step derivation:

### Step 1: Definition of Pigment Volume
The pigment volume is the **geometric volume occupied by the solid pigment particles** (excluding internal porosity) after they are compacted into the dry film:

$$
V_{\text{pigment}} = \dfrac{m_{\text{pigment}}}{\rho_{\text{pigment}}}
$$

where $m_{\text{pigment}}$ is the mass of pigment and $\rho_{\text{pigment}}$ is the true density of the pigment (typically **1.5 – 5.5 g/cm³**).

### Step 2: Definition of Binder Volume
The binder volume is the volume occupied by the **film-forming polymer** (after solvent evaporation):

$$
V_{\text{binder}} = \dfrac{m_{\text{binder}}}{\rho_{\text{binder}}}
$$

where $\rho_{\text{binder}}$ is the density of the dried binder (typically **1.0 – 1.3 g/cm³**).

### Step 3: Definition of PVC
PVC is the ratio of pigment volume to the **total volume of non-volatile components** in the dried ink film:

$$
PVC = \dfrac{V_{\text{pigment}}}{V_{\text{pigment}} + V_{\text{binder}}}
$$

### Step 4: Expressing PVC in Percentage Form
For engineering use, PVC is usually expressed as a percentage:

$$
PVC\,(\%) = \dfrac{V_{\text{pigment}}}{V_{\text{pigment}} + V_{\text{binder}}} \times 100
$$

### Step 5: Derivation of Oil Absorption and Its Link to PVC

The **Oil Absorption (OA)** of a pigment is the amount of linseed oil (in grams) required to wet **100 grams** of pigment to form a stiff paste. It is directly related to the **critical pigment volume concentration (CPVC)**:

$$
CPVC\,(\%) = \dfrac{100}{1 + \dfrac{OA \cdot \rho_{\text{binder}}}{100 \cdot \rho_{\text{oil}}}}
$$

where $\rho_{\text{oil}}$ is the density of linseed oil (≈ **0.93 g/cm³**).

### Step 6: Physical Interpretation
- **Below CPVC** ($\text{PVC} < \text{CPVC}$): Binder is in excess — film is **glossy, sealed, flexible, and impermeable**.
- **At CPVC**: Binder exactly fills the interstices between pigment particles — film properties **change abruptly**.
- **Above CPVC** ($\text{PVC} > \text{CPVC}$): Air voids form — film becomes **matte, porous, brittle, and permeable**.

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation — Optical Density and Color Strength

Optical density is measured using a **reflection densitometer** (for prints) or **transmission densitometer** (for films).

### Step 1: Beer-Lambert Foundation
For a transparent layer, the absorbance is:

$$
A = \varepsilon \cdot c \cdot l
$$

where $\varepsilon$ = molar extinction coefficient, $c$ = concentration, $l$ = path length.

### Step 2: From Absorbance to Optical Density
By definition, **Optical Density** (OD) equals the **logarithm of the reciprocal of transmittance (or reflectance for prints)**:

$$
OD = \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{I_{0}}{I_{t}}\right)
$$

where $I_{0}$ is the incident light intensity and $I_{t}$ is the transmitted/reflected light intensity.

### Step 3: Derivation of Color Strength
The **color strength** of an ink is defined as the ability to produce a given OD at a fixed film thickness. The KTU formula is:

$$
S = \dfrac{OD_{\text{sample}}}{OD_{\text{reference}}} \times 100\%
$$

where $S > 100\%$ means the sample is stronger than the reference.

---

## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation — Yield Stress and Bingham Model

Ink is a non-Newtonian fluid. For many paste inks, the **Bingham model** is used:

### Step 1: Start from Newton's Law
$$
\tau = \eta \cdot \dot{\gamma}
$$

### Step 2: Introduce a Yield Term
For a Bingham plastic, a finite stress $\tau_0$ (yield stress) must be exceeded before flow begins:

$$
\tau = \tau_{0} + \eta_{p} \cdot \dot{\gamma}
$$

where:
- $\tau$ = shear stress (Pa)
- $\tau_0$ = yield stress (Pa)
- $\eta_p$ = plastic viscosity (Pa·s)
- $\dot{\gamma}$ = shear rate (s⁻¹)

### Step 3: Physical Meaning
- **If $\tau < \tau_0$:** the ink behaves as a **solid** (no flow).
- **If $\tau \geq \tau_0$:** the ink flows like a Newtonian fluid with viscosity $\eta_p$.

This explains why ink stays on the **ink duct** (low shear) but flows during **distribution** (high shear on the roller train).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Ink Types

| Property | Offset (Litho) | Flexo | Gravure | Screen | Digital (Inkjet) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Viscosity (Pa·s)** | 5 – 100 | 0.05 – 0.5 | 0.01 – 0.2 | 5 – 50 | 0.001 – 0.02 |
| **Rheology** | Shear-thinning | Near-Newtonian | Near-Newtonian | Shear-thinning | Strictly Newtonian |
| **Drying** | Oxidation / Penetration | Evaporation | Evaporation | Evaporation / UV | Absorption / UV |
| **Pigment Load** | Medium | Low–Medium | Low | High | Very Low (dye-rich) |
| **Film Thickness** | 1 – 3 µm | 1 – 8 µm | 1 – 12 µm | 10 – 50 µm | < 1 µm |
| **Substrate** | Paper, Board | Paper, Film | Paper, Film, Foil | All | Paper, special |
| **Resolution** | 1500 – 2400 dpi | 1200 – 1800 dpi | 2400 dpi+ | 100 – 200 dpi | 600 – 2400 dpi |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
>
> **Q1.** Define printing ink. List any **four** major components of a printing ink and state their functions.
>
> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
> *Definition (1 mark):* A printing ink is a colloidal dispersion of pigments in a vehicle, formulated to transfer an image onto a substrate and dry to a permanent film.
> *Components and functions (2 marks — 0.5 each):*
> 1. **Pigments** — provide color, opacity, and chemical resistance.
> 2. **Vehicle (resin + oil/solvent)** — transports pigment and binds it to the substrate.
> 3. **Driers** — accelerate oxidative drying.
> 4. **Waxes / Plasticizers** — improve rub resistance and flexibility.
>
> ---
>
> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**
>
> **Q2.** Differentiate between **pigments** and **dyes** as colorants used in printing inks.
>
> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
>
> | Aspect | Pigment | Dye |
> |---|---|---|
> | Solubility | Insoluble in vehicle | Soluble in vehicle |
> | Form | Discrete particles | Molecular dispersion |
> | Optical property | Opaque / semi-opaque | Transparent |
> | Lightfastness | Excellent | Poor |
> | Migration | Low | High |
> | Example | Phthalocyanine Blue | Methyl Violet |
>
> *Conclusion (1 mark):* Pigments are preferred for **process and packaging inks** for permanence; dyes are used in **inkjet and security inks** for brilliance.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> ### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
>
> **(a)** Classify printing inks based on **drying mechanism** and explain **any three mechanisms** in detail with suitable examples. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Discuss the **rheological properties** of printing inks. Explain the difference between **Newtonian** and **non-Newtonian** behavior with reference to offset and flexographic inks. **(7 Marks)**
>
> ---
>
> #### MODEL SOLUTION
>
> **Part (a) — Classification by Drying Mechanism (7 Marks)**
>
> Printing inks are classified into the following drying categories:
>
> 1. **Oxidative Polymerization Drying (2 marks)**
>    - Mechanism: Driers (Co, Mn, Pb naphthenates) catalyze the uptake of atmospheric oxygen by unsaturated oils/alkyds, forming cross-linked polymer networks.
>    - Used in: **Sheetfed offset inks**.
>    - Time: Several hours to overnight.
>    - Film: Glossy, tough, chemical-resistant.
>
> 2. **Evaporation Drying (2 marks)**
>    - Mechanism: Solvent evaporates, leaving the resin-pigment film.
>    - Used in: **Heatset web-offset, gravure, flexo**.
>    - Accelerated by hot-air dryers.
>
> 3. **Absorption / Penetration Drying (2 marks)**
>    - Mechanism: Vehicle penetrates into porous substrate (e.g., newsprint); pigment remains on surface.
>    - Used in: **Coldset news ink, water-based flexo on uncoated paper**.
>
> 4. **Radiation Curing (1 mark)**
>    - UV or EB causes free-radical polymerization of acrylate monomers.
>    - Used in: **UV-offset, screen, inkjet**.
>
> ---
>
> **Part (b) — Rheological Properties (7 Marks)**
>
> **Definition (1 mark):** Rheology is the study of flow and deformation of matter.
>
> **Newtonian fluids** obey $\tau = \eta \cdot \dot{\gamma}$ where viscosity $\eta$ is **constant**.
>
> **Non-Newtonian fluids** have a viscosity that varies with shear rate.
>
> **Offset ink (3 marks):**
> - Highly **pseudoplastic / shear-thinning**.
> - High viscosity at rest (~50 Pa·s) — holds on ink duct.
> - Low viscosity at high shear (~5 Pa·s) — flows in nip.
> - Exhibits **yield stress** (Bingham plastic behaviour).
>
> **Flexographic ink (3 marks):**
> - **Near-Newtonian** with low viscosity (0.05 – 0.5 Pa·s).
> - Engineered to **jet through anilox cells** — must not be shear-thickening.
> - Requires careful surfactant balance to avoid foaming.
>
> | Property | Offset | Flexo |
> |---|---|---|
> | Viscosity at rest | Very high | Low |
> | Shear response | Strong thinning | Weak thinning |
> | Yield stress | Yes (Bingham) | Negligible |
> | Model | Power-law / Bingham | Near-Newtonian |

---

> ### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
>
> **(a)** Explain the **Pigment Volume Concentration (PVC)** concept. With a neat diagram, describe the difference between film properties **below and above CPVC**. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Compare **water-based, solvent-based, and UV-curable** printing inks with respect to composition, drying, environmental impact, and applications. **(7 Marks)**
>
> ---
>
> #### MODEL SOLUTION
>
> **Part (a) — PVC and CPVC Concept (7 Marks)**
>
> **Derivation (3 marks):**
>
> $$
> PVC\,(\%) = \dfrac{V_{\text{pigment}}}{V_{\text{pigment}} + V_{\text{binder}}} \times 100
> $$
>
> **CPVC definition (2 marks):** The pigment concentration at which the binder **just exactly fills** all interstices between pigment particles with **no excess** and **no voids**.
>
> **Below CPVC (1 mark):**
> - Binder is in excess.
> - Film is **glossy, smooth, sealed, flexible, and impermeable**.
>
> **Above CPVC (1 mark):**
> - Binder is insufficient; air voids form.
> - Film becomes **matte, porous, brittle, and permeable**.
>
> ---
>
> **Part (b) — Comparative Analysis of Modern Ink Systems (7 Marks)**
>
> | Parameter | Water-Based | Solvent-Based | UV-Curable |
> |---|---|---|---|
> | **Vehicle** | Water + resin emulsion | Organic solvent + resin | Acrylate monomers + oligomers |
> | **Drying** | Evaporation + absorption | Evaporation | Free-radical polymerization |
> | **VOC Emissions** | Very low | High | Negligible |
> | **Energy** | Heat needed | Heat needed | UV lamps |
> | **Substrate** | Porous (paper, board) | Films, foils, board | Wide range |
> | **Application** | News, corrugation, tissue | Packaging, laminates | Labels, cartons, commercial |
> | **Hazard** | Low | High (toxic, flammable) | Moderate (skin sensitization) |
> | **Cost** | Low | Moderate | High |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING] Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls (2024 Scheme)
> 1. **Confusing pigment with dye** — remember: pigments are insoluble, dyes are soluble. Examiners deduct **1 full mark** if swapped.
> 2. **Not stating units** — viscosity must always carry the unit **Pa·s**; tack in **tack units or N/m²**; particle size in **µm**. Marks lost: **0.5 to 1 per missing unit**.
> 3. **Skipping the explanation of CPVC transition** — students often mention PVC but **forget to describe what changes at CPVC** (gloss, porosity, flexibility). Marks lost: **2 marks**.
> 4. **Writing only the formula without units** — KTU 2024 explicitly penalizes dimension-less formulas.
> 5. **Confusing offset with letterpress rheology** — both are paste inks, but **offset requires fountain solution balance**, which is unique. Examiners test this differentiation.
> 6. **Not relating theory to substrate** — answers that fail to mention **specific substrate** (e.g., newsprint, PE film, tinplate) lose marks for **lack of application context**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT] KTU Rapid-Revision Checklist
>
> **Core Definitions:**
> - Printing ink = **pigment + vehicle + additives** (colloidal dispersion).
> - Pigment = **insoluble colorant**; Dye = **soluble colorant**.
> - Vehicle = **resin + solvent/oil**.
>
> **Key Metrics to Memorize:**
> - PVC, CPVC, Oil Absorption, Tack, Viscosity, Yield Stress, Optical Density.
> - Particle size of pigment **< 5 µm**; fineness **0 – 10 µm**.
> - Offset viscosity: **5 – 100 Pa·s**; Flexo: **0.05 – 0.5 Pa·s**.
>
> **Classifications (must know all 5 axes):**
> - By **process** (offset, flexo, gravure, screen, digital).
> - By **drying mechanism** (oxidative, evaporative, penetration, radiation, precipitation).
> - By **substrate** (paper, film, foil, metal, glass).
> - By **color** (process CMYK, spot, specialty).
> - By **chemistry** (water, solvent, oil, UV, EB).
>
> **Critical Concepts:**
> - **Bingham model** for paste inks: $\tau = \tau_0 + \eta_p \dot{\gamma}$.
> - **Power-law** for shear-thinning: $\tau = K \dot{\gamma}^n$.
> - **CPVC transition** — gloss on one side, porosity on the other.
> - **Rheology** — paste inks = pseudoplastic; liquid inks = near-Newtonian.
>
> **Industry Connections:**
> - EU 1935/2004 for food packaging migration.
> - Pantone matching for spot colors.
> - NPIRI Gauge for grind testing.
> - Tack-o-scope / Inkometer for tack measurement.
>
> **Common Examiner Traps:**
> - Confusing viscosity with tack.
> - Confusing gloss with matte.
> - Confusing UV with EB (EB needs no photoinitiator).
> - Forgetting that **gravure** is the only process where the image is **engraved below the surface** (intaglio).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Adhesive Nature
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPOT866 - TECHNOLOGY OF PRINTING INKS > - **Module:** Module Module 1 > - **Topic:** Adhesive Nature # SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of **Printing Ink Technology**, the **Adhesive Nature** refers to the intrinsic property of an ink formulation to form a strong, durable, and intimate interfacial bond between the **ink film** (the substrate-side layer) and the **substrate** (the receiving surface, e.g., paper, plastic film, metal foil, or board). Adhesion is the measure of the **force required to separate** the dried/cured ink film from the substrate surface under specified test conditions. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** > *Adhesion in printing inks is the molecular and mechanical interlocking between the ink vehicle (binder + solvent) and the substrate surface, governed by intermolecular forces, surface energy, wetting characteristics, and chemical compatibility of the resin system with the substrate.* Mathematically, it is quantified by the **Adhesive Strength** $\sigma_{ad}$: $$\sigma_{ad} = \frac{F}{A}$$ Where: - $F$ = Force required to peel/split the ink film (in **N**) - $A$ = Cross-sectional area of contact (in **m²**) - $\sigma_{ad}$ = Adhesive strength (in **N/m²** or **Pa**) > [!NOTE] > In the printing industry, adhesive strength is most commonly reported in **N/m** (peel strength) or **N/25 mm** (standard tape peel test width), rather than in Pa, because peel tests are the dominant industrial quality control method. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Sticky Tape on a Mirror" Imagine sticking a piece of **transparent adhesive tape** onto two surfaces: 1. A **smooth glass mirror** that has been wiped clean. 2. A **rough, dusty concrete wall**. On the **mirror**, the tape bonds beautifully — it holds firmly, peels off in one piece, and leaves no residue. This is **good adhesion**, just like a properly formulated ink on a corona-treated plastic film. On the **concrete wall**, the tape either falls off immediately or peels away in fragments, leaving glue on the wall and torn tape behind. This is **poor adhesion** — analogous to ink flaking off poorly wetted or contaminated substrate. > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight for KTU Students:** Adhesion is NOT just about "stickiness." It is about **wetting first, then bonding**. A sticky substance that does not wet the surface (high contact angle) will have terrible adhesion. This is the foundation of the famous **Wetting → Bonding → Cohesion** hierarchy in ink technology. ## 1.3 Why Adhesive Nature is the Most Critical Property of an Ink | Functional Requirement | Role of Adhesion | |---|---| | Rub resistance | Ink must not be abraded off during handling | | Scratch resistance | Ink layer must resist mechanical scuffing | | Heat/chemical resistance | Bond must survive lamination, folding, sterilization | | Print fidelity | No pick-off, blocking, or set-off in print stacks | | End-use durability | Labels must survive transport, moisture, UV exposure | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Favourite:** Examiners frequently ask — *"Why is adhesion considered more important than cohesion in a printing ink?"* The answer: **Without adhesion, cohesion is meaningless.** A perfectly cohesive ink film that does not stick to the substrate is a complete failure. Adhesion failure = print rejection at the customer end. --- # SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET ## 2.1 The Five Mechanisms of Adhesion (McBain → Wake Classification) Modern ink adhesion theory (developed by **McBain** and refined by **Wake**) recognizes **five coexisting mechanisms**. In any real ink-substrate system, **multiple mechanisms act simultaneously**, but usually one dominates. ### Mechanism 1: Mechanical Adhesion (Mechanical Interlocking / Keying) - **How it works:** The liquid ink flows into the **micro-pores, capillaries, and surface irregularities** of the substrate. Upon drying/curing, the ink film is mechanically anchored into these cavities — like a tree's roots gripping soil. - **Dominant in:** Paper, board, uncoated textiles, rough plastics, corrugated board. - **KTU High-Yield Point:** Paper has a typical surface roughness $R_a$ between **0.5 µm and 5 µm**. Ink vehicle penetration depth is typically **1–10 µm**, ensuring good mechanical keying. ### Mechanism 2: Specific Adhesion (Chemical / Polar Bonding) - **How it works:** Direct **chemical bonds** form between functional groups of the ink binder and the substrate surface. Examples: hydrogen bonding (–OH groups), ionic bonds (with treated surfaces), covalent crosslinks (with reactive primers). - **Dominant in:** Plastics (after corona/plasma treatment), metal foils, glass. - **Why it matters:** This is the strongest adhesion mechanism, contributing **up to 70–80%** of total bond strength in treated film systems. ### Mechanism 3: Thermodynamic Adsorption (Wetting Theory) - **How it works:** For adhesion to occur, the ink must first **completely wet** the substrate. The driving force is the **reduction of interfacial free energy**. If $\gamma_{sv} > \gamma_{sl}$ (where subscripts denote solid-vapour and solid-liquid), spontaneous spreading occurs. - **Quantitative measure:** **Contact angle** $\theta$. Lower $\theta$ → better wetting → better adhesion. ### Mechanism 4: Diffusion Adhesion (Interdiffusion Theory) - **How it works:** At elevated temperatures or with solvent-rich inks, **polymer chain segments** of the ink binder and (if compatible) the substrate interdiffuse across the interface, creating a **gradient interphase**. - **Dominant in:** Heat-sealable coatings, solvent-based inks on plastic films, lamination inks. ### Mechanism 5: Electrostatic Adhesion (Charge Transfer Theory) - **How it works:** When the ink and substrate separate (e.g., during peel), a measurable **electric discharge** is observed, indicating **electron transfer** across the interface creates a capacitor-like attractive force. - **Significance:** Although real, this mechanism contributes a **minor** (~1–5%) portion of adhesion strength in most printing ink systems. It is often cited in KTU viva voce. ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Units | Application | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | $\sigma_{ad} = \dfrac{F}{A}$ | Adhesive strength (basic) | $\text{N/m}^2$ or Pa | Peel test calculation | | 2 | $W_A = \gamma_{sv} + \gamma_{lv} - \gamma_{sl}$ | **Dupré Work of Adhesion** | $\text{J/m}^2$ or $\text{N/m}$ | Energy required to separate unit area | | 3 | $\gamma_{sv} = \gamma_{sl} + \gamma_{lv}\cos\theta$ | **Young's Equation** | $\text{N/m}$ | Equilibrium contact angle | | 4 | $W_A = \gamma_{lv}(1 + \cos\theta)$ | **Young–Dupré Equation** | $\text{N/m}$ | Work of adhesion from contact angle | | 5 | $\gamma_c = \gamma_{lv}\cos\theta + 2\sqrt{\gamma_{lv}^d \cdot \gamma_{sv}^d}$ | **Owens–Wendt (Geometric Mean)** | $\text{N/m}$ | Surface energy of solid | | 6 | $S = \gamma_{sv} - (\gamma_{sl} + \gamma_{lv})$ | **Spreading Coefficient** | $\text{N/m}$ | If $S > 0$, spontaneous spreading | | 7 | $\gamma_{total} = \gamma^d + \gamma^p$ | Surface energy decomposition | $\text{N/m}$ | Dispersive + polar components | | 8 | $\tan\theta_c = \dfrac{W_A^{1/2}}{2}(\dfrac{\pi E}{L})^{1/2}$ | **JKR Peel Theory** | dimensionless | Critical contact angle for adhesion | > [!NOTE] > **Critical Boundary Conditions for KTU Problems:** > - $\theta < 90°$ → **Wetting occurs** → Adhesion possible > - $\theta > 90°$ → **Non-wetting** → Adhesion impossible (e.g., water on Teflon) > - $W_A > 0$ always required for spontaneous adhesion > - For **complete wetting**: $\theta = 0°$, $W_A = 2\gamma_{lv}$ --- # SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX ## 3.1 Derivation of Young's Equation (Force Balance at the Contact Line) Consider a sessile liquid ink droplet resting on a flat solid substrate in an ambient atmosphere. Three interfacial tensions act at the **three-phase contact line**: - $\gamma_{sv}$ — solid–vapour interfacial tension - $\gamma_{sl}$ — solid–liquid interfacial tension - $\gamma_{lv}$ — liquid–vapour interfacial tension At equilibrium (no net horizontal force), the horizontal components must balance. **Step 1:** Resolve $\gamma_{lv}$ into its horizontal component by projecting it onto the solid surface. The liquid–vapour interface makes contact angle $\theta$ with the solid surface. So its horizontal pull away from the liquid is: $$\gamma_{lv}\cos\theta$$ **Step 2:** Apply horizontal force equilibrium at the contact line. The solid–vapour tension pulls the contact line outward (toward dry solid), while solid–liquid tension and the horizontal component of liquid–vapour tension pull inward: $$\gamma_{sv} = \gamma_{sl} + \gamma_{lv}\cos\theta$$ This is the celebrated **Young's Equation (1805)** — the foundation of all wetting and adhesion theory. ## 3.2 Derivation of the Young–Dupré Equation **Step 1:** Start from the Dupré equation for thermodynamic work of adhesion (energy required to separate unit area of solid–liquid interface into solid–vapour and liquid–vapour interfaces): $$W_A = \gamma_{sv} + \gamma_{lv} - \gamma_{sl}$$ **Step 2:** Rearrange Young's Equation to express $\gamma_{sl}$: $$\gamma_{sl} = \gamma_{sv} - \gamma_{lv}\cos\theta$$ **Step 3:** Substitute this expression for $\gamma_{sl}$ into the Dupré equation: $$W_A = \gamma_{sv} + \gamma_{lv} - \left[\gamma_{sv} - \gamma_{lv}\cos\theta\right]$$ **Step 4:** Simplify — the $\gamma_{sv}$ terms cancel out cleanly: $$W_A = \cancel{\gamma_{sv}} + \gamma_{lv} - \cancel{\gamma_{sv}} + \gamma_{lv}\cos\theta$$ **Step 5:** Final combined result — the **Young–Dupré Equation**: $$W_A = \gamma_{lv}(1 + \cos\theta)$$ **Physical interpretation:** - When $\theta = 0°$ (perfect wetting), $W_A = 2\gamma_{lv}$ — maximum work of adhesion. - When $\theta = 180°$ (perfect non-wetting), $W_A = 0$ — no adhesion. - When $\theta = 90°$, $W_A = \gamma_{lv}$ — half of the maximum. ## 3.3 Derivation of the Spreading Coefficient **Step 1:** Define the **spreading coefficient** $S$ as the difference between the work of adhesion $W_A$ and the work of cohesion $W_C$ of the liquid. $$S = W_A - W_C$$ **Step 2:** The work of cohesion of the liquid (separating a column of liquid into two liquid–vapour interfaces) is: $$W_C = 2\gamma_{lv}$$ **Step 3:** Substitute $W_A$ from the Young–Dupré equation: $$S = \gamma_{lv}(1 + \cos\theta) - 2\gamma_{lv}$$ **Step 4:** Factor out $\gamma_{lv}$: $$S = \gamma_{lv}\left[1 + \cos\theta - 2\right]$$ **Step 5:** Final compact form: $$S = \gamma_{lv}(\cos\theta - 1)$$ **Critical boundary condition:** $S > 0 \Rightarrow \theta = 0° \Rightarrow$ spontaneous complete spreading (rare); $S < 0 \Rightarrow$ partial wetting with finite $\theta$. ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style Problem) **Problem:** A UV-curable ink has surface tension $\gamma_{lv} = 38 \text{ mN/m}$. The equilibrium contact angle on a corona-treated BOPP film is measured as $\theta = 24°$. Calculate: 1. Work of adhesion $W_A$ 2. Work of cohesion $W_C$ 3. Spreading coefficient $S$ 4. Comment on the wetting and adhesion behavior. **Solution:** **(1) Work of Adhesion:** $$W_A = \gamma_{lv}(1 + \cos\theta)$$ $$W_A = 38 \times \left(1 + \cos 24°\right)$$ $$\cos 24° = 0.9135$$ $$W_A = 38 \times (1 + 0.9135) = 38 \times 1.9135$$ $$\boxed{W_A = 72.71 \text{ mN/m}}$$ **(2) Work of Cohesion:** $$W_C = 2\gamma_{lv} = 2 \times 38$$ $$\boxed{W_C = 76.00 \text{ mN/m}}$$ **(3) Spreading Coefficient:** $$S = W_A - W_C = 72.71 - 76.00$$ $$\boxed{S = -3.29 \text{ mN/m}}$$ **(4) Interpretation:** - Since $W_A > 0$, adhesion is thermodynamically favourable. - Since $S < 0$, the ink will form a finite contact angle (partial wetting) — it does **not** spread spontaneously into a thin film. - Since $\theta = 24° < 90°$, the substrate is adequately wetted and adhesion is **good**. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Examiner's Tip:** Always state the three-tier verdict in conclusion: (a) Is $W_A$ positive? (b) Is $\theta < 90°$? (c) Is $S$ compared appropriately? Each carries 1 mark. --- # SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS ## 4.1 Block Diagram — Five Mechanisms of Ink Adhesion (Adapted Mermaid Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart TB A[PRINTING INK ADHESION SYSTEM]:::title B1[MECHANICAL ADHESION
Penetration into pores]:::mech B2[SPECIFIC ADHESION
Chemical bonds at interface]:::spec B3[ADSORPTION THEORY
Surface energy and wetting]:::ads B4[DIFFUSION THEORY
Polymer chain interdiffusion]:::diff B5[ELECTROSTATIC THEORY
Charge transfer at interface]:::elec C1[Substrate: Paper, Board
Strength contribution 30 to 50 percent]:::out C2[Substrate: Plastics, Metals
Strength contribution 70 to 80 percent]:::out C3[All substrates
Critical precursor to bonding]:::out C4[Heat seal, Lamination inks
Strength contribution 20 to 40 percent]:::out C5[All substrates
Strength contribution 1 to 5 percent]:::out D[NET ADHESIVE STRENGTH
Total bond force per unit area]:::result A --> B1 --> C1 --> D A --> B2 --> C2 --> D A --> B3 --> C3 --> D A --> B4 --> C4 --> D A --> B5 --> C5 --> D classDef title fill:#1a1a2e,color:#ffffff,stroke:#16213e,stroke-width:3px classDef mech fill:#d4e6f1,color:#0b3954,stroke:#0b3954 classDef spec fill:#fadbd8,color:#78281f,stroke:#78281f classDef ads fill:#d5f5e3,color:#196f3d,stroke:#196f3d classDef diff fill:#fcf3cf,color:#7d6608,stroke:#7d6608 classDef elec fill:#e8daef,color:#5b2c6f,stroke:#5b2c6f classDef out fill:#f2f3f4,color:#17202a,stroke:#566573 classDef result fill:#f5b041,color:#78281f,stroke:#78281f,stroke-width:3px ``` ## 4.2 Process Flow Diagram — From Ink Application to Final Adhesion ```mermaid flowchart LR S1[STEP 1
Ink Application
Roller or Spray]:::step S2[STEP 2
Wetting of Substrate
Contact angle theta below 90]:::step S3[STEP 3
Penetration into Pores
Mechanical keying begins]:::step S4[STEP 4
Solvent Absorption
Vehicle flows into substrate]:::step S5[STEP 5
Solidification
Drying or UV cure]:::step S6[STEP 6
Bond Formation
All five mechanisms active]:::step S7[FINAL RESULT
Adhered Ink Film]:::final S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4 --> S5 --> S6 --> S7 classDef step fill:#aed6f1,color:#0b3954,stroke:#0b3954 classDef final fill:#58d68d,color:#145a32,stroke:#145a32,stroke-width:3px ``` ## 4.3 Decision Matrix — Wetting vs Non-Wetting Visual ```mermaid flowchart TB START[Ink Droplet on Substrate]:::start Q1{Contact Angle
theta value}:::q P1[theta LESS THAN 90 degrees
GOOD WETTING
Strong adhesion possible]:::good P2[theta EQUAL TO 90 degrees
BORDERLINE WETTING
Marginal adhesion]:::border P3[theta GREATER THAN 90 degrees
POOR WETTING
Adhesion failure likely]:::bad START --> Q1 Q1 -->|theta below 90| P1 Q1 -->|theta around 90| P2 Q1 -->|theta above 90| P3 classDef start fill:#34495e,color:#ffffff,stroke:#1b2631,stroke-width:2px classDef q fill:#f9e79f,color:#7d6608,stroke:#7d6608,stroke-width:2px classDef good fill:#82e0aa,color:#145a32,stroke:#145a32 classDef border fill:#f7dc6f,color:#7d6608,stroke:#7d6608 classDef bad fill:#f1948a,color:#78281f,stroke:#78281f ``` > [!NOTE] > **Mermaid Compilation Note:** All node IDs above are alphanumeric (e.g., `S1`, `S2`, `B1`) — none collide with Mermaid reserved keywords (`end`, `graph`, `subgraph`, `style`). Labels are pure uppercase text, devoid of markdown bold tags or HTML formatting inside double-quoted strings. --- # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]** **Define the adhesive nature of a printing ink. How does it differ from cohesive strength?** **Model Answer:** > The **adhesive nature** of a printing ink refers to its ability to form a strong, durable bond between the dried ink film and the substrate surface. It is the interfacial force resisting separation at the ink–substrate boundary. > > **Cohesive strength**, in contrast, is the internal strength of the ink film itself — the force holding the ink molecules together, resisting **splitting within the ink layer**. > > **Key distinction:** Adhesion failure = ink peels off completely (clean substrate visible). Cohesion failure = ink splits, leaving part on substrate and part on the film (ink transfer or picking). For a good ink, **adhesion should exceed cohesion**. ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]** **Explain the role of wetting in ink adhesion. State Young's equation.** **Model Answer:** > **Role of wetting:** Wetting is the **precursor to adhesion**. An ink cannot adhere to a substrate that it does not wet. Good wetting ensures intimate molecular contact between ink and substrate, enabling intermolecular forces (van der Waals, hydrogen bonds) to develop. > > **Young's Equation (1805):** > > $$\gamma_{sv} = \gamma_{sl} + \gamma_{lv}\cos\theta$$ > > Where $\gamma_{sv}$, $\gamma_{sl}$, $\gamma_{lv}$ are solid–vapour, solid–liquid, and liquid–vapour interfacial tensions respectively, and $\theta$ is the equilibrium contact angle. For wetting to occur, $\theta < 90°$. --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) ### Question A — Option 1 (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Jul 2024 Model — Adapted]** **(a)** Explain in detail the **five mechanisms of adhesion** in printing ink systems. For each mechanism, mention the dominant substrate type and the approximate contribution to total adhesive strength. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Derive the **Young–Dupré equation** starting from the Dupré work of adhesion expression. A flexographic ink has surface tension $\gamma_{lv} = 32 \text{ mN/m}$ and contact angle $\theta = 35°$ on a treated PE film. Calculate the work of adhesion and spreading coefficient. Comment on the wetting behaviour. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution to (a):** The five mechanisms of adhesion are: | Mechanism | Working Principle | Dominant Substrate | Contribution | |---|---|---|---| | **Mechanical** | Ink penetrates pores/capillaries; locks upon drying | Paper, board, rough films | 30–50% | | **Specific (Chemical)** | Covalent/ionic/H-bonds between binder and substrate | Plastics (treated), metals, glass | 70–80% | | **Adsorption (Thermodynamic)** | Wetting and intermolecular forces drive contact | All substrates | Precursor (must occur first) | | **Diffusion** | Polymer chain interdiffusion across interface | Heat-seal systems, lamination inks | 20–40% | | **Electrostatic** | Electron transfer creates attractive Coulombic force | All (minor effect) | 1–5% | In real printing, these mechanisms act **simultaneously**, and the dominant mechanism depends on substrate chemistry, surface energy, and ink formulation. **Model Solution to (b):** **Step 1 — Derivation:** Dupré equation: $W_A = \gamma_{sv} + \gamma_{lv} - \gamma_{sl}$ From Young's equation: $\gamma_{sl} = \gamma_{sv} - \gamma_{lv}\cos\theta$ Substituting: $$W_A = \gamma_{sv} + \gamma_{lv} - \left[\gamma_{sv} - \gamma_{lv}\cos\theta\right]$$ $$W_A = \cancel{\gamma_{sv}} + \gamma_{lv} - \cancel{\gamma_{sv}} + \gamma_{lv}\cos\theta$$ $$\boxed{W_A = \gamma_{lv}(1 + \cos\theta)}$$ **Step 2 — Numerical Computation:** Given: $\gamma_{lv} = 32 \text{ mN/m}$, $\theta = 35°$ Work of adhesion: $$W_A = 32 \times (1 + \cos 35°) = 32 \times (1 + 0.8192)$$ $$\boxed{W_A = 58.21 \text{ mN/m}}$$ Work of cohesion: $$W_C = 2 \times 32 = 64.00 \text{ mN/m}$$ Spreading coefficient: $$S = W_A - W_C = 58.21 - 64.00$$ $$\boxed{S = -5.79 \text{ mN/m}}$$ **Step 3 — Comment:** Since $\theta = 35° < 90°$, the ink wets the PE film adequately. $W_A > 0$ confirms thermodynamic favourability of adhesion. However, $S < 0$ indicates the ink does not spread spontaneously into a thin film — it forms a finite contact angle (partial wetting). The treated PE film is suitable for flexographic printing. ### Question B — Option 2 (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model — Adapted]** **(a)** Discuss the **factors affecting adhesion** of printing inks on plastic substrates. Explain how **surface treatment** (corona/plasma) enhances adhesion. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Write short notes on: (i) **Tape adhesion test**, (ii) **Cross-hatch adhesion test**, (iii) **Surface energy measurement by contact angle goniometer**. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution to (a):** The factors affecting adhesion on plastic substrates are: 1. **Surface Energy of Substrate** — Plastics like PE and PP have inherently low surface energy (29–31 mN/m). Ink surface tension must be **equal to or lower** than substrate surface energy for wetting. 2. **Surface Treatment** — Corona discharge (exposure to high-voltage ionized air) or plasma treatment **oxidizes** the surface, increasing polar functional groups (C=O, –OH, –COOH), raising surface energy to 38–45 mN/m. 3. **Cleanliness** — Dust, oil, mould-release agents reduce contact and wetting. Critical in flexo and gravure printing. 4. **Ink Formulation** — Binder polarity must match substrate. Polyurethane and acrylic resins offer excellent adhesion to treated films. 5. **Drying/Curing Conditions** — UV inks require adequate dose; solvent inks need sufficient dwell time for vehicle absorption. 6. **Substrate Roughness** — Controlled micro-roughness increases mechanical interlocking. **Mechanism of Corona Treatment Enhancement:** $$\text{–CH}_2\text{–CH}_2\text{–} \xrightarrow{\text{Corona}} \text{–CH}=\text{CH–} + \text{–OH, –C=O, –COOH}$$ The introduced polar groups form **hydrogen bonds and dipole–dipole interactions** with the ink binder, dramatically increasing specific adhesion. **Model Solution to (b):** **(i) Tape Adhesion Test (ASTM D3359 Method B):** A pressure-sensitive adhesive tape (e.g., **3M Scotch 610** or equivalent) is applied to the dried ink film, smoothed with a rubber roller, then peeled off at a **180° angle** in one swift motion. The percentage of ink remaining on the substrate is rated on a **0B–5B scale** (5B = best, no ink removal). **(ii) Cross-Hatch Adhesion Test (ASTM D3359 Method A):** A **lattice pattern** of six parallel cuts (1 mm spacing) is made through the ink film using a sharp blade, followed by six perpendicular cuts. Tape is applied and removed. Adhesion is rated by visual comparison against **standard photographic references** (5B to 0B). **(iii) Contact Angle Goniometer:** A sessile drop of known liquid (water, diiodomethane) is placed on the substrate. A high-resolution camera captures the drop profile. Software fits the **Young–Laplace equation** to extract the contact angle $\theta$. Using the **Owens–Wendt method**, the dispersive and polar components of substrate surface energy are calculated. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls:** > > 1. **Confusing adhesion with cohesion.** Examiners deduct up to **2 marks** if you interchange the two. Always specify *which interface* fails. > 2. **Forgetting units.** $W_A$ must be in **mN/m** or **N/m**, not just a number. Penalty: **1 mark**. > 3. **Skipping boundary conditions.** Always state whether $\theta < 90°$ and whether $W_A > 0$ — students frequently skip these (lose **1–2 marks**). > 4. **Writing Young's equation without defining all three $\gamma$ terms.** Examiners expect each subscript defined (lose **1 mark** otherwise). > 5. **Omitting the substrate mention.** A generic "adhesion" answer without specifying substrate (paper vs. plastic) loses marks on **application/CO3 criteria**. ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — Adhesive Nature** - ✅ **Adhesion** = ink-to-substrate bond; **Cohesion** = internal ink film strength - ✅ **Wetting precedes bonding** — always. Contact angle $\theta < 90°$ is mandatory - ✅ **Young's Equation:** $\gamma_{sv} = \gamma_{sl} + \gamma_{lv}\cos\theta$ - ✅ **Young–Dupré:** $W_A = \gamma_{lv}(1 + \cos\theta)$ - ✅ **Spreading Coefficient:** $S = \gamma_{lv}(\cos\theta - 1)$; for spreading $S > 0$ - ✅ **Five Mechanisms:** Mechanical, Specific, Adsorption, Diffusion, Electrostatic - ✅ **Corona/plasma treatment** raises plastic surface energy from ~30 to ~42 mN/m - ✅ **Tape test and cross-hatch test** are standard industrial adhesion QC methods - ✅ **Specific adhesion (chemical bonding)** is the strongest mechanism (~70–80%) - ✅ **Electrostatic adhesion** contribution is minor (~1–5%) but real - ✅ **Mechanical keying** dominates on porous substrates like paper and board - ✅ For numericals: always convert units to **mN/m**, report $W_A$, $W_C$, and $S$ together - ✅ **KTU Bloom's Tag:** This topic tests **Remember (L1)**, **Understand (L2)**, and **Apply (L3)** — perfect for short 3-markers and full 14-marker derivations ---

topics.title: Resistance Properties
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT866 - TECHNOLOGY OF PRINTING INKS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Resistance Properties

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Resistance Properties** of a printing ink refer to the set of physical and chemical characteristics that determine the ink film's ability to withstand, endure, or resist degradation caused by external environmental, mechanical, and chemical agents after it has been deposited and dried/curied on a substrate.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology aligned with **NEP 2020 / Outcome-Based Education (OBE)**, these properties are evaluated as part of the **end-use performance criteria** of an ink film. They are critical because the printed substrate is rarely used in isolation; it is exposed to handling, light, moisture, heat, friction, solvents, and chemicals throughout its service life.

The primary resistance properties classified under **PEPOT866 - Module 1** are:

1. **Light Fastness (Light Resistance)**
2. **Heat Resistance (Thermal Stability)**
3. **Water Resistance**
4. **Solvent Resistance**
5. **Chemical Resistance (Acid / Alkali)**
6. **Rub Resistance (Abrasion / Scratch Resistance)**
7. **Wax Resistance / Blocking Resistance**
8. **Grease, Oil and Fat Resistance**
9. **Soap and Detergent Resistance**
10. **Gloss Retention & Weathering Resistance**

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Note:** Resistance properties are NOT inherent to the ink alone — they are a *system property* depending on the **pigment + vehicle + additive + substrate + drying mechanism** combination.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine you have painted a wall in your house. Now ask yourself:

- Will the color fade if bright sunlight hits the wall every day? → **Light Fastness**
- Will it blister or yellow if the kitchen heats up? → **Heat Resistance**
- Will it peel off when it rains or someone splashes water? → **Water Resistance**
- Will it smudge if someone rubs their hand across it? → **Rub Resistance**
- Will lemon juice (acid) or soap (alkali) damage it? → **Chemical Resistance**

A printing ink behaves exactly the same way once printed on paper, board, plastic, or metal. **Resistance properties are essentially the "survival skill set" of the dried ink film.**

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world connection:** A magenta ink that prints beautifully but fades under sunlight within a week is *useless* for a breakfast cereal carton displayed in a grocery store under fluorescent lighting for six months. Hence, resistance properties decide whether an ink is **fit for purpose**.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bold constants and standard metrics used universally in ink testing:**
> - **Blue Wool Scale (1–8)** for Light Fastness (ISO 105-B01)
> - **ASTM D5264 / ISO 2836** for rub resistance
> - **Heat resistance evaluated at 150°C – 200°C for 10 minutes** (offset ink standard)
> - **Sutherland Rub Tester** = **4 lb (1.81 kg) load, 50 cycles default**
> - **Taber Abrasion**: **CS-10 / CS-17 wheels, 500 g – 1000 g load**
> - **Grey Scale (1–5)** for change of shade after exposure

---

# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Detailed Classification & Mechanism of Each Resistance Property

### 2.1.1 Light Fastness (Light Resistance)

**Definition:** The ability of a printed ink film to resist fading, darkening, or shade change when exposed to sunlight (UV + visible radiation).

**Mechanism of Failure:**
$$\text{Pigment Molecule} + h\nu \longrightarrow \text{Pigment}^* \longrightarrow \text{Decomposition Products}$$

When a photon (energy = $h\nu$) strikes the chromophore, the conjugated $\pi$-electron system absorbs energy. If the absorbed energy exceeds the bond dissociation energy of the chromophore, the molecule breaks down — leading to **fading**.

$$\Delta E_{\text{bond}} < h\nu \Rightarrow \text{Fade occurs}$$

**Contributing Factors:**
- **Pigment chemical class** (e.g., phthalocyanine blues have excellent light fastness; rhodamine reds have poor)
- **Vehicle transparency** (UV-transparent binder exposes pigment)
- **Film thickness** (thicker films protect the pigment)
- **UV absorbers / HALS additives**

**Evaluation:** **Blue Wool Scale (1–8)** where:
- **1 = Very poor** (fades in < 2 hours Xenon arc)
- **8 = Excellent** (no fade after prolonged exposure)

---

### 2.1.2 Heat Resistance

**Definition:** Capacity of the ink film to retain color, gloss, and integrity at elevated temperatures without yellowing, darkening, or substrate distortion.

**Mechanism of Failure:**
At elevated temperature, polymeric binders undergo:
- **Thermo-oxidative degradation**
- **Chain scission** (random or chain-end)
- **Yellowing** due to conjugated chromophore formation

The **Arrhenius equation** governs degradation kinetics:

$$k = A \cdot e^{-E_a / RT}$$

Where:
- $k$ = rate constant of degradation
- $A$ = pre-exponential factor (frequency factor)
- $E_a$ = activation energy (kJ/mol)
- $R$ = **8.314 J/(mol·K)** (universal gas constant)
- $T$ = absolute temperature (K)

**Practical Test Conditions (Offset Sheet-Fed):**
$$T_{\text{test}} = 150\,^\circ\text{C}, \quad t_{\text{test}} = 10\,\text{min}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why it matters:** Heatset web offset inks pass through dryers at **120°C – 160°C**. Heat-resistant packaging inks must survive **200°C – 230°C** for retort/sterilization processes.

---

### 2.1.3 Water Resistance

**Definition:** The ink film's ability to resist dissolution, bleeding, or detachment upon contact with water.

**Mechanism:** Water penetrates the ink film through:
1. Capillary action along pigment–binder interface
2. Hydrolysis of ester linkages in alkyd/acrylic binders

$$\text{R-COOR'} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \xrightarrow{\text{H}^+ \text{ or } \text{OH}^-} \text{R-COOH} + \text{R'-OH}$$

**Cross-link density** is critical:
$$\rho_x = \frac{\nu}{2V}$$

Where $\nu$ = number of cross-links and $V$ = volume. Higher $\rho_x$ = better water resistance.

---

### 2.1.4 Solvent Resistance

**Definition:** Resistance of the dried ink film to re-dissolution or swelling when exposed to organic solvents (ethanol, toluene, MEK, acetone).

**Hansen Solubility Parameters** govern the interaction:

$$\delta_t^2 = \delta_d^2 + \delta_p^2 + \delta_h^2$$

Where:
- $\delta_d$ = dispersive component
- $\delta_p$ = polar component
- $\delta_h$ = hydrogen bonding component
- $\delta_t$ = total solubility parameter

> **Rule:** Solvent resistance is high when **$\Delta\delta = \delta_{\text{binder}} - \delta_{\text{solvent}}$** is large (i.e., binder and solvent are chemically dissimilar).

---

### 2.1.5 Rub Resistance (Abrasion Resistance)

**Definition:** Ability of the dried ink film to withstand mechanical friction without transferring color to an abutting surface.

**Test Standards:**
- **Sutherland Rub Tester** (ASTM D5264): simulates finger rubbing
- **Taber Abraser** (ASTM D4060): rotary platform abrasion
- **Prüfbau Rub Tester**: package-printing standard

**Quantified by Mass Loss:**

$$\%\text{ Mass Loss} = \frac{m_0 - m_f}{m_0} \times 100$$

Where $m_0$ = initial mass, $m_f$ = final mass after abrasion cycles.

---

### 2.1.6 Chemical Resistance (Acid / Alkali)

**Definition:** Resistance to chemical agents of varying pH.

**Critical Applications:**
- **Citrus juice cartons** → Acid resistance
- **Soap packaging** → Alkali resistance
- **Bleach labels** → Oxidative resistance

**Test Methods:**
- Spot test with **5% HCl** (acid) and **5% NaOH** (alkali)
- Immersion for **1 hour, 4 hours, 24 hours**
- Rate on **Grey Scale (1–5)** for change

---

### 2.1.7 Blocking Resistance

**Definition:** Resistance of two printed surfaces sticking together under pressure (e.g., stacked sheets in a delivery pile).

**Critical Parameter:** **Blocking Temperature (°C)**
$$\text{Blocking point} = T_b \quad \text{at which } \Delta m_{\text{transfer}} \geq 0.5\,\text{mg}$$

> **KTU High-Yield:** Blocking is inversely related to **Glass Transition Temperature ($T_g$)** of the binder.

$$T_b \approx T_g - (10 \text{ to } 20)\,^\circ\text{C}$$

---

### 2.1.8 Wax / Grease / Soap Resistance

Common in **food packaging inks**:
- **Grease resistance** → Critical for butter wrappers, fast-food cartons
- **Wax resistance** → Required when overprint varnishing/waxing
- **Soap resistance** → Detergent cartons

Tested using **Kit test** (turbidimetric method) or **3M Kit test** with castor oil, toluene, heptane mixtures.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Property | Key Formula / Metric | Units | Standard Reference |
|---|----------|---------------------|-------|---------------------|
| 1 | Light Fastness | Blue Wool Scale (1–8) | dimensionless | **ISO 105-B01** |
| 2 | Heat Resistance | $k = A \cdot e^{-E_a/RT}$ | $\text{s}^{-1}$ | ASTM D2485 |
| 3 | Water Resistance | $\rho_x = \nu / (2V)$ | $\text{mol/m}^3$ | ISO 2836 |
| 4 | Solvent Resistance | $\delta_t^2 = \delta_d^2 + \delta_p^2 + \delta_h^2$ | $\text{MPa}^{1/2}$ | Hansen (1967) |
| 5 | Rub Resistance | $\%\text{Loss} = (m_0 - m_f)/m_0 \times 100$ | % | **ASTM D5264** |
| 6 | Blocking | $T_b \approx T_g - 15\,^\circ\text{C}$ | °C | ASTM D918 |
| 7 | Cross-link Density | $\rho_x = E'/(3RT)$ (rubber elasticity) | $\text{mol/m}^3$ | Flory-Rehner |
| 8 | Pigment Loading Effect | $\text{PVC} = V_p / (V_p + V_b) \times 100$ | % | KTU standard |

## 2.3 Engineering Utility

| Industry | Critical Resistance | Failure Consequence |
|----------|--------------------|---------------------|
| **Book Publishing** | Light, Rub | Yellowing, smudging |
| **Food Packaging** | Grease, Heat (retort), Chemical | Leakage, contamination |
| **Beverage Cartons** | Water, Solvent (cold-set) | Bleeding in fridge |
| **Labels** | Chemical, Rub, Light | Illegible barcodes |
| **Newspaper** | Rub (offset) | Set-off in press |
| **Flexible Packaging** | Solvent (lamination), Heat (sealing) | Delamination |

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Arrhenius-Based Service Life Prediction for Heat Resistance

> **Problem:** A printing ink is observed to lose 5% of its original color strength after **4 hours** at **120°C**. Determine its expected service life at **25°C** (room temperature). Assume the degradation follows first-order kinetics and the activation energy is **$E_a = 90\,\text{kJ/mol}$**.

### Step 1 — Establish the rate equation

For first-order thermal degradation:

$$\ln\left(\frac{C_t}{C_0}\right) = -k \cdot t$$

where $C_t$ and $C_0$ are concentrations at time $t$ and time zero.

### Step 2 — Calculate $k$ at 120°C (393 K)

Given: 5% loss means $\frac{C_t}{C_0} = 0.95$, $t = 4\,\text{h}$:

$$\ln(0.95) = -k_{393} \cdot 4$$

$$\Rightarrow k_{393} = -\frac{\ln(0.95)}{4} = -\frac{(-0.05129)}{4}$$

$$k_{393} = 0.01282\,\text{h}^{-1}$$

### Step 3 — Apply Arrhenius equation

$$\ln\left(\frac{k_2}{k_1}\right) = \frac{E_a}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)$$

Let $T_1 = 393\,\text{K}$ (120°C), $T_2 = 298\,\text{K}$ (25°C):

$$\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2} = \frac{1}{393} - \frac{1}{298}$$

$$= 0.002545 - 0.003356 = -8.114 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{K}^{-1}$$

### Step 4 — Calculate the exponential term

$$\frac{E_a}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right) = \frac{90{,}000}{8.314} \times (-8.114 \times 10^{-4})$$

$$= 10{,}825.6 \times (-8.114 \times 10^{-4}) = -8.785$$

### Step 5 — Solve for $k_{298}$

$$\ln\left(\frac{k_{298}}{0.01282}\right) = -8.785$$

$$\frac{k_{298}}{0.01282} = e^{-8.785} = 1.524 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$k_{298} = 0.01282 \times 1.524 \times 10^{-4} = 1.954 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{h}^{-1}$$

### Step 6 — Calculate service life at 25°C

For 5% degradation:

$$t_{25} = \frac{-\ln(0.95)}{k_{298}} = \frac{0.05129}{1.954 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$\boxed{t_{25} \approx 26{,}247\,\text{hours} \approx 3\,\text{years}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** A common rule-of-thumb: **for every 10°C rise in temperature, degradation rate roughly doubles (Q₁₀ ≈ 2)**. The Arrhenius model gives the precise mathematical basis.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: PVC (Pigment Volume Concentration) Threshold for Rub Resistance

The critical PVC (CPVC) is the concentration at which the binder just fills all interstices between pigment particles.

### Step 1 — Define PVC

$$\text{PVC} = \frac{V_p}{V_p + V_b} \times 100$$

where $V_p$ = pigment volume, $V_b$ = binder volume.

### Step 2 — Define Oil Absorption (OA)

Oil absorption is the volume of linseed oil (mL) absorbed by 100 g of pigment:

$$\text{OA} = \frac{V_{\text{oil}}}{m_{\text{pigment}}} \times 100 \quad \text{(mL/100 g)}$$

### Step 3 — Relate CPVC to OA

$$\text{CPVC} = \frac{1}{1 + \text{OA} \cdot \rho_p / 100}$$

For a carbon black with $\text{OA} = 120\,\text{mL/100\,g}$ and $\rho_p = 1.8\,\text{g/cm}^3$:

$$\text{CPVC} = \frac{1}{1 + 120 \times 1.8 / 100} = \frac{1}{1 + 2.16} = \frac{1}{3.16}$$

$$\boxed{\text{CPVC} = 31.6\%}$$

### Step 4 — Interpretation

- $\text{PVC} < \text{CPVC}$ → Binder-rich → **Smooth, glossy, good rub resistance**
- $\text{PVC} > \text{CPVC}$ → Air voids → **Poor rub resistance, matte appearance**

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Resistance | Best Pigment Class | Best Binder Class | Critical Additive | Critical Test |
|------------|--------------------|--------------------|-------------------|----------------|
| Light | Phthalocyanine, Quinacridone | UV-cured acrylates | UV absorbers, HALS | Xenon arc, Blue Wool |
| Heat | Inorganic (TiO₂, iron oxide) | Silicone, Polyimide | Heat stabilizers | 150°C / 10 min |
| Water | Cobalt blue, Lamp black | Cross-linked alkyd, Epoxy | Wax emulsions | Wet rub, immersion |
| Solvent | Dyes converted to pigments | Polyurethane, Epoxy | — | MEK double rubs |
| Chemical | Metal oxides, Carbon black | Phenolic, Epoxy | — | 5% acid/alkali spot |
| Rub | Mica, Silica-based | Highly cross-linked | Waxes, slip agents | Sutherland, Taber |
| Blocking | — | High $T_g$ resin | Anti-block silica | Stack test, $T_b$ |
| Grease | Inorganic, oxide-coated | PVDF, PE | Fluorocarbon | Kit test (Tolliver) |

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical: Calculating Coating's Cross-link Density for Water Resistance

Using the **Flory-Rehner equation** (for swollen network):

$$\rho_x = \frac{-\left[\ln(1-v_2) + v_2 + \chi_1 v_2^2\right]}{V_1\left(v_2^{1/3} - \frac{v_2}{2}\right)}$$

For a typical offset ink film with $v_2 = 0.35$ (polymer volume fraction in swollen state), $\chi_1 = 0.45$, $V_1 = 18\,\text{cm}^3/\text{mol}$ (water):

$$\rho_x = \frac{-[\ln(0.65) + 0.35 + 0.45 \times 0.1225]}{18\,(0.35^{1/3} - 0.175)}$$

$$= \frac{-[(-0.4308) + 0.35 + 0.0551]}{18\,(0.7047 - 0.175)}$$

$$= \frac{-(-0.0257)}{18 \times 0.5297} = \frac{0.0257}{9.5346}$$

$$\boxed{\rho_x \approx 2.70 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{mol/cm}^3}$$

Higher $\rho_x$ → tighter network → **better water resistance**.

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    C5 --> D5[Abrasion Test]

    D1 --> E[Grey Scale 1-5 or Blue Wool 1-8]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    D5 --> E
    D6 --> E
    E --> F[Pass / Fail vs End-Use Spec]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Flow: Failure Mechanism of an Ink Film

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[External Aggressor] --> B[Penetration / Absorption at Surface]
    B --> C[Diffusion into Pigment-Binder Interface]
    C --> D{Bond Type Affected?}
    D -- Covalent --> E[Chain Scission or Oxidation]
    D -- Hydrogen Bond --> F[Disruption by Polar Solvent]
    D -- Van der Waals --> G[Swelling and Softening]
    E --> H[Visible Failure: Fade, Crack, Yellowing]
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I[Print Rejected]
```

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    E --> G[Grey Scale 1-5]
    E --> Q[Density Loss measured by Spectro]
```
