## PART A — 3 MARK QUESTIONS

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define Light Fastness of a printing ink. How is it evaluated?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> Light fastness is the resistance of a dried ink film to fading, darkening, or change of shade when exposed to sunlight or artificial UV-containing radiation. It is evaluated using the **Blue Wool Scale (1–8)** as per **ISO 105-B01**, where 1 = very poor (rapid fading) and 8 = excellent (no visible fade after prolonged exposure). Test specimens are exposed to a xenon arc lamp under controlled conditions, and the degree of fading is compared against the standard blue wool references. **(3 marks)**

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is meant by Blocking Resistance? Mention one industrial test.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> Blocking resistance is the ability of two printed surfaces to resist sticking or transferring ink to each other when placed in contact under pressure (e.g., stacked sheets in a delivery pile). It is inversely related to the **Glass Transition Temperature ($T_g$)** of the binder. The blocking point is the temperature at which visible ink transfer occurs. Industrial test: **ASTM D918** — two printed strips are placed face-to-face under a known load in an oven, and the temperature of transfer is recorded as the blocking point. **(3 marks)**

---

## PART B — 14 MARK QUESTIONS (ESE MODULE INTERNAL CHOICE)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the mechanism of failure of a printing ink film when exposed to UV light. Discuss the role of **pigment chemical structure, vehicle, and additives** in determining light fastness. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of the Arrhenius equation, describe how the service life of an ink at room temperature can be predicted from accelerated heat-aging data. **A sample ink shows 10% loss of color strength after 6 hours at 140°C. Given $E_a$ = 85 kJ/mol, estimate its service life at 25°C.** **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution — Question A

**(a) Mechanism of UV-Induced Fading (7 marks)**

1. **Photonic Absorption (1 mark):** Pigment molecules absorb photons of energy $E = h\nu$. When $h\nu$ matches the HOMO–LUMO gap of the chromophore, electrons are promoted to excited states.

2. **Excited State Reactions (2 marks):**
   - **Photo-oxidation:** Excited pigment reacts with singlet oxygen $\left(^1O_2\right)$ generated from ground-state triplet oxygen via energy transfer.
   - **Photo-reduction:** Excited pigment accepts an electron from the binder, forming a radical anion.
   - Both lead to cleavage of the conjugated system and **loss of color**.

3. **Role of Pigment Structure (1.5 marks):**
   - **Phthalocyanines** (Cu-Pc blue): Highly stable, planar, metal-chelated → Blue Wool 8.
   - **Rhodamines, basic dyes**: Electron-rich, easily oxidized → Blue Wool 1–3.
   - **Inorganic pigments** (iron oxide, TiO₂): Excellent light fastness (7–8) due to stable oxide lattice.

4. **Role of Vehicle (1.5 marks):**
   - **Alkyds** with high unsaturation → yellow, oxidize, expose pigment.
   - **Acrylics / UV-cured systems** → tighter film, better protection.
   - Vehicles must be **UV-transparent** to expose pigment OR **UV-opaque** to shield it.

5. **Role of Additives (1 mark):**
   - **UV absorbers** (benzotriazoles): Absorb harmful UV, dissipate as heat.
   - **HALS** (Hindered Amine Light Stabilizers): Scavenge radicals, interrupt degradation chain.

---

**(b) Arrhenius Service Life Calculation (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Rate constant at 140°C (1 mark):**
Given 10% loss in 6 hours: $\ln(0.90) = -k_{413} \cdot 6$
$$k_{413} = \frac{-\ln(0.90)}{6} = \frac{0.10536}{6} = 0.01756\,\text{h}^{-1}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Arrhenius to find $k_{298}$ (4 marks):**
$$\ln\left(\frac{k_{298}}{0.01756}\right) = \frac{85{,}000}{8.314}\left(\frac{1}{413} - \frac{1}{298}\right)$$

$$\frac{1}{413} - \frac{1}{298} = 0.002421 - 0.003356 = -9.35 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{K}^{-1}$$

$$\frac{85{,}000}{8.314} = 10{,}224$$

$$\text{Exponent} = 10{,}224 \times (-9.35 \times 10^{-4}) = -9.56$$

$$\frac{k_{298}}{0.01756} = e^{-9.56} = 7.05 \times 10^{-5}$$

$$k_{298} = 1.24 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{h}^{-1}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate service life for 10% loss at 25°C (2 marks):**
$$t_{25} = \frac{-\ln(0.90)}{1.24 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{0.10536}{1.24 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$\boxed{t_{25} \approx 84{,}967\,\text{hours} \approx 9.7\,\text{years}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often forget to convert **°C → K** in the Arrhenius denominator. Always use **absolute temperature (Kelvin)** in any Arrhenius calculation. Also, ensure the **time unit is consistent** (do not mix seconds and hours).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Define **Rub Resistance** and **Blocking Resistance**. Compare the testing standards used for each, mentioning the apparatus, load, and evaluation criteria. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain **Hansen Solubility Parameters** and how they govern the solvent resistance of an ink film. A polyurethane binder has $\delta_d = 18$, $\delta_p = 8$, $\delta_h = 6$ (all in MPa$^{1/2}$). Determine whether it is more likely to resist toluene ($\delta_d = 18, \delta_p = 1, \delta_h = 2$) or methanol ($\delta_d = 15, \delta_p = 12, \delta_h = 22$). **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution — Question B

**(a) Rub Resistance vs Blocking Resistance (7 marks)**

| Parameter | Rub Resistance | Blocking Resistance |
|-----------|---------------|---------------------|
| **Definition** (1.5) | Resistance of ink film to mechanical friction | Resistance of two printed surfaces to stick together |
| **Apparatus** (1.5) | **Sutherland Rub Tester / Taber Abraser** | **Blocking Tester (oven + weighted blocks)** |
| **Load** (1) | 4 lb (1.81 kg) on Sutherland | 1–5 psi stack pressure |
| **Cycles / Time** (1) | 50–100 strokes | 16–24 hours at elevated T |
| **Evaluation** (1) | **Grey Scale (1–5)** for transfer | **Visual + transfer weight** |
| **Standard** (1) | **ASTM D5264 / ISO 2836** | **ASTM D918** |

Rub resistance tests **mechanical durability of one surface**; blocking tests **surface-to-surface adhesion under stacking pressure**.

---

**(b) Hansen Solubility Parameters (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Recall the equation (1 mark):**
$$\delta_t^2 = \delta_d^2 + \delta_p^2 + \delta_h^2$$

**Step 2 — Calculate $\delta_t$ for binder and solvents (2 marks):**

For PU binder:
$$\delta_{PU} = \sqrt{18^2 + 8^2 + 6^2} = \sqrt{324 + 64 + 36} = \sqrt{424} \approx 20.6\,\text{MPa}^{1/2}$$

For toluene:
$$\delta_{tol} = \sqrt{18^2 + 1^2 + 2^2} = \sqrt{324 + 1 + 4} = \sqrt{329} \approx 18.1\,\text{MPa}^{1/2}$$

For methanol:
$$\delta_{meth} = \sqrt{15^2 + 12^2 + 22^2} = \sqrt{225 + 144 + 484} = \sqrt{853} \approx 29.2\,\text{MPa}^{1/2}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate component-wise distance (Hansen sphere radius) (3 marks):**

$$\Delta\delta = \sqrt{4(\delta_{d,b} - \delta_{d,s})^2 + (\delta_{p,b} - \delta_{p,s})^2 + (\delta_{h,b} - \delta_{h,s})^2}$$

For PU vs toluene:
$$\Delta\delta_{tol} = \sqrt{4(0)^2 + (7)^2 + (4)^2} = \sqrt{0 + 49 + 16} = \sqrt{65} \approx 8.06$$

For PU vs methanol:
$$\Delta\delta_{meth} = \sqrt{4(3)^2 + (-4)^2 + (-16)^2} = \sqrt{36 + 16 + 256} = \sqrt{308} \approx 17.55$$

**Step 4 — Conclusion (1 mark):**
Since $\Delta\delta_{tol} < \Delta\delta_{meth}$, the PU binder is **chemically closer to toluene** → **lower solvent resistance to toluene** than to methanol.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - Students often skip the **factor of 4** in the dispersive component of the Hansen equation. This **costs 1 mark**.
> - Students confuse **total** $\delta_t$ with **component** distance. The correct criterion uses the **3D vector distance**, not just $\delta_t$ difference.
> - Always state the **boundary**: a typical "good" interaction occurs when $\Delta\delta < 5\,\text{MPa}^{1/2}$.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Resistance Properties (Module 1)**

- **Light Fastness** = Blue Wool Scale **1–8** (8 = best); use **ISO 105-B01**; phthalocyanines and quinacridones are top tier.
- **Heat Resistance** = **Arrhenius** governs; typical test = **150°C / 10 min** for offset; use **inorganic pigments** for >200°C.
- **Water Resistance** = depends on **cross-link density ($\rho_x$)**; hydrolyze-sensitive binders (alkyds) are poor.
- **Solvent Resistance** = governed by **Hansen parameters**; $\Delta\delta > 5\,\text{MPa}^{1/2}$ = resistant.
- **Rub Resistance** = **Sutherland (ASTM D5264)** or **Taber**; quantify by **mass loss (%)** or **Grey Scale**.
- **Blocking Resistance** = $T_b \approx T_g - 15\,^\circ\text{C}$; tested by **ASTM D918**.
- **Chemical Resistance** = spot test with **5% HCl and 5% NaOH**; rate on Grey Scale.
- **Grease/Wax Resistance** = **Kit test (Tolliver)** with numbered reagent mixtures.
- **PVC vs CPVC** rule: **PVC < CPVC → glossy + good rub**; **PVC > CPVC → matte + chalky**.
- **Activation energy $E_a$** for thermal degradation of typical inks = **70–100 kJ/mol**.
- **Common additives**: Waxes (rub), UV absorbers + HALS (light), Anti-block silica (blocking), Silicone (slip).
- **Universal Rule**: *Resistance is a system property — never judge an ink by the pigment alone.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Printing Processes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT866 - TECHNOLOGY OF PRINTING INKS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Printing Processes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Printing Processes — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **printing process** is a controlled, repeatable industrial method of transferring a predetermined image (text, graphics, or pictorial content) from a carrier (called a *printing form*, *plate*, *screen*, or *digital image carrier*) onto a substrate (paper, board, plastic film, foil, fabric, metal, or glass) using an *ink* (or toner) as the colour-bearing medium, under defined pressure, speed, and curing conditions.

The 20th-century master taxonomist **William A. D. Street** (author of *Printing Inks*, widely followed as a KTU reference standard) defines printing as the **controlled deposition of ink in specific areas** to reproduce an image — distinguishing printing from painting (manual, non-reproducible) and coating (uniform film, no image).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 1 Anchor Definition**
> *"Printing is the process of producing multiple identical copies of an image by depositing ink onto a substrate via a reusable image carrier, where only the image areas receive ink and the non-image areas reject ink."*

## 1.2 Classification of Printing Processes

All industrial printing processes are classified by the **physical principle of image generation** on the carrier. There are **four classical (impact) processes** + **one modern (non-impact) family**:

| Family | Image Areas | Non-Image Areas | Modern Representative |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Relief** | Raised surface | Recessed | Letterpress, Flexography |
| **Intaglio** | Recessed (cells) | Raised surface | Gravure |
| **Planographic** | Chemically differentiated | Same plane | Offset (Lithography) |
| **Stencil** | Perforations open | Perforations blocked | Screen (Serigraphy) |
| **Non-impact (Digital)** | Electrostatic / Jet | Deflected / absent | Inkjet, Electrophotography, Laser |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Mnemonic — "RIPS-D"**: **R**elief, **I**ntaglio, **P**lanographic, **S**tencil, **D**igital. This is examiner-favoured.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine you have a **stencil cake-decorator** (a tube with a shaped hole):
- **Relief printing** = a rubber stamp dipped in ink — only the **raised rubber** touches the paper.
- **Intaglio printing** = an engraved metal plate where **ink sits inside grooves** and a blade scrapes off the surface ink before paper is pressed in.
- **Planographic printing** = a flat stone (in lithography) where some parts love water-and-repel-ink, while other parts repel-water-and-accept-ink — the image is **chemically painted**, not physically raised or recessed.
- **Stencil printing** = a window screen with holes — ink is **squeegeed through openings** onto a T-shirt.
- **Digital printing** = a **miniature fire-hose of ink droplets**, fired pixel-by-pixel from a printhead, with no physical plate at all.

The **common thread**: in every process, the *ink* is the medium of truth. Every printing process is essentially an **engineered ink-handling problem** — which is why Module 1 of *Technology of Printing Inks* begins here.

## 1.4 Historical & Industrial Significance

- **1440** — Gutenberg's movable-type letterpress (first relief).
- **1796** — Senefelder's lithography (first planographic).
- **1904** — Rubel transfers ink from plate to rubber blanket → **offset lithography** is born.
- **1907** — Simultaneous invention of **rotogravure** in Germany.
- **1930s** — Photopolymer plates enable **flexography** for packaging.
- **1977** — First commercial **digital press** (Xerox 9700, electrophotographic).
- **1984** — First **drop-on-demand inkjet** (Hertz).
- **Today** — Industrial inkjet is the fastest-growing print segment, projected to dominate **decorative** and **variable-data** printing.

> [!TIP]
> For KTU answers, always anchor a process to **what part of the carrier holds the ink** (raised / recessed / flat / open / virtual). This single fact earns 2 of the 3 marks in most definition questions.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle of Each Family — Structured Logic

### 2.1.1 Relief Printing (Letterpress & Flexography)
- **Carrier geometry:** Image areas are *raised* above the non-image areas by a height $\Delta h$.
- **Standard relief depth:** $\Delta h \approx 0.7 \text{ mm}$ (letterpress); $0.4\text{–}0.7 \text{ mm}$ (flexo).
- **Mechanism:** Rollers (fountain + form rollers) deposit ink onto the raised surface; substrate is pressed against the form cylinder.
- **Ink rheology required:** **Pseudoplastic** (shear-thinning) — low viscosity at high shear (printing nip) for transfer, high viscosity at rest to stay on the form.
- **Typical ink viscosity:** Letterpress $\eta \approx 4\text{–}8 \text{ Pa}\cdot\text{s}$ (paste); Flexo $\eta \approx 0.05\text{–}0.2 \text{ Pa}\cdot\text{s}$ (liquid).

### 2.1.2 Intaglio (Gravure)
- **Carrier geometry:** Image areas are **recessed cells** engraved into a copper cylinder; cell depth controls ink volume.
- **Cell geometry:** Pyramid, quadragonal, or tri-helical; cell volume $V_{cell} = 5\text{–}35 \text{ µm}^{3}$.
- **Mechanism:** Excess ink wiped off by a **doctor blade**; substrate drawn into cells by an **impression roller** under pressure $\approx 0.3\text{–}1.0 \text{ MPa}$.
- **Ink rheology:** **Near-Newtonian / very low viscosity** ($\eta \approx 0.05\text{–}0.15 \text{ Pa}\cdot\text{s}$) so it flows freely into and out of cells.

### 2.1.3 Planographic (Offset Lithography)
- **Carrier geometry:** **Flat** — image and non-image on the same plane.
- **Differentiation principle:** **Chemical contrast (oleophobic/hydrophilic vs. oleophilic/hydrophobic)**.
- **Sequence:** Fountain solution (water-based, pH 4.5–5.5) wets non-image; ink wets image; the two are mutually immiscible on the plate.
- **Three cylinders:** Plate cylinder $\rightarrow$ Blanket cylinder $\rightarrow$ Impression cylinder.
- **Ink rheology:** **Viscoplastic / thixotropic paste**, $\eta \approx 10\text{–}100 \text{ Pa}\cdot\text{s}$, with tack value $T \approx 8\text{–}14$ (measured on an Inkometer).

### 2.1.4 Stencil (Screen / Serigraphy)
- **Carrier geometry:** A **mesh screen** (originally silk, now polyester/nylon; mesh count $50\text{–}400 \text{ mesh/inch}$) with blocked non-image areas.
- **Mechanism:** A **squeegee** forces ink through open mesh onto substrate.
- **Ink film thickness:** $t \approx 10\text{–}30 \text{ µm}$ (one of the thickest films in printing).
- **Ink rheology:** Very high viscosity paste, often **plasto-viscoelastic**, $\eta \approx 5\text{–}50 \text{ Pa}\cdot\text{s}$, must not drip through mesh when stationary.

### 2.1.5 Digital Printing
- **No physical form** — image is a file.
- **Two dominant sub-families:**
  - **Electrophotography (EP):** Photoconductor drum charged $\rightarrow$ laser writes latent image $\rightarrow$ toner (dry, plastic powder) adheres to charged areas $\rightarrow$ fused at $150\text{–}200\,^{\circ}\text{C}$.
  - **Inkjet:** Drop-on-Demand (DOD, piezo or thermal) or Continuous Inkjet (CIJ); drop volume $V_d \approx 1\text{–}50 \text{ pL}$.
- **Ink requirements:** Very low viscosity ($\eta \approx 0.001\text{–}0.01 \text{ Pa}\cdot\text{s}$), low surface tension ($\gamma \approx 25\text{–}40 \text{ mN/m}$) for proper wetting and drop formation.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These are the **canonical quantitative relations** examiners test in Module 1 numericals and Part B derivations.

| # | Relation | LaTeX | Typical Units | Used For |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Ink film thickness from ink consumption | $t = \dfrac{V_{ink}}{A_{substrate}}$ | µm | Estimating print gloss & drying load |
| 2 | Areal coverage of halftone dots | $a_{\%} = \dfrac{\pi d^{2}}{4 L^{2}} \times 100$ | % | Gravure cell area / halftone |
| 3 | Line-screen to ruling | $R = \dfrac{1}{L}$ | lines/cm or lpi | Halftone resolution |
| 4 | Volume of one gravure cell (tri-helical) | $V_{cell} = \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{4}\, d^{2}\, h$ | µm³ | Cell ink volume |
| 5 | Screen ink deposit (empirical) | $t_{screen} = k \cdot e \cdot \dfrac{1}{m}$ | µm | Screen mesh film thickness |
| 6 | Ink tack (Inkometer, 1 min) | $T = \dfrac{F \cdot r}{\omega}$ | g·m or units | Tack measurement |
| 7 | Newton (ink transfer) | $y = 100\,\Phi\left[1 - e^{-K\,x}\right]$ | % | Ink transfer efficiency |
| 8 | Static surface tension (Wilhelmy) | $\gamma = \dfrac{F}{L \,\cos\theta}$ | mN/m | Substrate wetting |
| 9 | Reynolds number of inkjet drop | $Re = \dfrac{\rho \, v \, D}{\eta}$ | – | Drop stability |
| 10 | Weber number of inkjet drop | $We = \dfrac{\rho \, v^{2} \, D}{\gamma}$ | – | Drop breakup |

> *Notation key:* $V_{ink}$ = ink volume; $A$ = substrate area; $d$ = dot/cell width; $L$ = halftone screen period; $h$ = cell depth; $k$ = emulsion thickness factor; $e$ = emulsion thickness; $m$ = mesh count; $F$ = force; $r$ = roller radius; $\omega$ = angular velocity; $\Phi$ = fractional coverage; $K$ = constant; $x$ = split-point thickness; $\theta$ = contact angle; $\rho$ = density; $v$ = drop velocity; $D$ = drop diameter.

## 2.3 Real-World Utility in Engineering

| Industry | Process Used | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Newspapers | Coldset offset (web) | Speed (15 m/s), low cost per copy |
| Folding cartons | Offset + flexo hybrid | Quality + economics |
| Magazines | Heatset web offset / rotogravure | High gloss, long runs |
| Flexible food packaging | Flexography | Solvent-based ink adhesion to PE/PP |
| Aluminium beverage cans | Dry offset + UV curing | Cylindrical substrate |
| T-shirts & textiles | Screen (plastisol) | Thick ink deposit, durability |
| Wallpaper / décor | Rotogravure | Long run, photo quality |
| Variable data / photo books | Digital inkjet (aqueous or UV) | No-plate, on-demand |
| Security printing (currency) | Intaglio + offset + letterpress | Tactile relief, anti-counterfeit |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Ink Film Thickness from Ink Consumption

**Problem context:** A flexo press consumes $V_{ink} = 250 \text{ mL}$ of ink in a run of $N = 5000$ impressions on labels of dimension $12 \text{ cm} \times 8 \text{ cm}$. Find the average wet ink film thickness deposited.

### Step 1 — Convert units consistently
$$V_{ink} = 250 \text{ mL} = 250 \text{ cm}^{3}$$

### Step 2 — Compute total printed area
$$A_{total} = N \times (12 \text{ cm} \times 8 \text{ cm}) = 5000 \times 96 \text{ cm}^{2} = 4.80 \times 10^{5} \text{ cm}^{2}$$

### Step 3 — Apply the thickness formula
$$t = \dfrac{V_{ink}}{A_{total}}$$

Substituting the numerical values:

$$t = \dfrac{250 \text{ cm}^{3}}{4.80 \times 10^{5} \text{ cm}^{2}}$$

$$t = 5.21 \times 10^{-4} \text{ cm}$$

### Step 4 — Convert to micrometres (industry unit)
$$t = 5.21 \times 10^{-4} \text{ cm} \times \dfrac{10^{4}\,\mu\text{m}}{1 \text{ cm}}$$

$$\boxed{t \approx 5.2 \,\mu\text{m}}$$

**Interpretation:** This lies in the typical flexo range of $4\text{–}8\,\mu\text{m}$ — therefore the press is well-tuned. If the answer had come out as $20\,\mu\text{m}$, the anilox would have been over-inked.

---

## 3.2 Derivation — Halftone Dot Area Coverage

**Problem context:** A halftone screen ruling is $R = 60 \text{ lines/cm}$ (i.e., $L = 1/60 \text{ cm}$). At 50% tone, the dot diameter is $d = 0.65 \cdot L$. Find the **effective area coverage** in percent.

### Step 1 — Express the unit-cell geometry
Each halftone "cell" is a square of side $L$. The dot is inscribed as a circle of diameter $d$.

$$a_{\%} = \dfrac{\text{area of dot}}{\text{area of cell}} \times 100$$

### Step 2 — Substitute the geometric expressions
$$a_{\%} = \dfrac{\dfrac{\pi d^{2}}{4}}{L^{2}} \times 100$$

### Step 3 — Substitute numerical values
With $L = \dfrac{1}{60} \text{ cm} = 1.667 \times 10^{-2} \text{ cm}$ and $d = 0.65 \cdot L = 1.083 \times 10^{-2} \text{ cm}$:

$$a_{\%} = \dfrac{\pi \,(1.083\times10^{-2})^{2}}{4\,(1.667\times10^{-2})^{2}} \times 100$$

### Step 4 — Simplify numerically

$$\begin{aligned}
a_{\%} &= \dfrac{\pi \times 1.173 \times 10^{-4}}{4 \times 2.778 \times 10^{-4}} \times 100 \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{3.685 \times 10^{-4}}{1.111 \times 10^{-3}} \times 100 \\[4pt]
&= 0.3317 \times 100 \\[4pt]
&\approx 33.2 \,\%
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{a_{\%} \approx 33 \,\%}$$

**Interpretation:** A 50% digital tone-value rendered through a $60 \text{ l/cm}$ screen produces only ~33% **physical** ink coverage — the dot does not fill the entire cell even at 50% tone. This *mechanical dot gain* is fundamental to ink-film and density calculations.

---

## 3.3 Derivation — Gravure Cell Volume (Tri-Helical Cell)

**Problem context:** A gravure cylinder has tri-helical cells of width $d = 120\,\mu\text{m}$ and depth $h = 32\,\mu\text{m}$. Find the ink volume per cell and the corresponding wet film thickness if the cell pitch is $p = 200\,\mu\text{m}$.

### Step 1 — Volume of one cell (tri-helical)
$$V_{cell} = \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{4}\, d^{2}\, h$$

### Step 2 — Insert values

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{cell} &= \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{4} \times (120\,\mu\text{m})^{2} \times 32\,\mu\text{m} \\[4pt]
&= 0.4330 \times 14\,400 \times 32 \,\mu\text{m}^{3} \\[4pt]
&= 199\,500 \,\mu\text{m}^{3} \\[4pt]
&\approx 2.00 \times 10^{5} \,\mu\text{m}^{3}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3 — Volume per unit projected area
One cell occupies a projected area of $p \times p = (200)^{2} = 40\,000\,\mu\text{m}^{2}$.

$$t = \dfrac{V_{cell}}{p^{2}} = \dfrac{2.00 \times 10^{5}}{4.00 \times 10^{4}} \,\mu\text{m}$$

$$\boxed{t \approx 5.0 \,\mu\text{m}}$$

**Interpretation:** $5\,\mu\text{m}$ of wet gravure ink is typical — but the ink is highly solvent-rich (often 60–70% toluene), so after drying the **dry film thickness** shrinks to ~$1\text{–}1.5\,\mu\text{m}$. This is why gravure inks must be formulated for dramatic volume loss.

---

## 3.4 Derivation — Reynolds & Weber Number for Inkjet Drop

**Problem context:** A piezoelectric DOD inkjet ejects a drop of diameter $D = 25\,\mu\text{m}$ at velocity $v = 8 \text{ m/s}$. The ink has density $\rho = 1050 \text{ kg/m}^{3}$, viscosity $\eta = 4 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Pa}\cdot\text{s}$, surface tension $\gamma = 32 \times 10^{-3} \text{ N/m}$. Classify the drop.

### Step 1 — Reynolds number
$$Re = \dfrac{\rho \, v \, D}{\eta}$$

$$Re = \dfrac{1050 \times 8 \times 25 \times 10^{-6}}{4 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$Re = \dfrac{0.210}{4 \times 10^{-3}} = 52.5$$

### Step 2 — Weber number
$$We = \dfrac{\rho \, v^{2} \, D}{\gamma}$$

$$We = \dfrac{1050 \times 64 \times 25 \times 10^{-6}}{32 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$We = \dfrac{1.68}{0.032} = 52.5$$

### Step 3 — Compute the dimensionless $\text{Oh}$ number (Ohnesorge)
$$Oh = \dfrac{\sqrt{We}}{Re} = \dfrac{\sqrt{52.5}}{52.5} = \dfrac{7.25}{52.5} \approx 0.138$$

### Step 4 — Interpret
For stable drop formation, **Fromm's criterion** requires $Oh < 0.1$ for high-quality inkjet. Here $Oh \approx 0.14$ is **marginally acceptable**, indicating a slightly viscous fluid — reducing drop velocity to $v = 6 \text{ m/s}$ would bring $Oh$ into the safe zone:

$$\begin{aligned}
Re' &= \dfrac{1050 \times 6 \times 25\times 10^{-6}}{4\times 10^{-3}} = 39.4 \\[4pt]
We' &= \dfrac{1050 \times 36 \times 25\times 10^{-6}}{32\times 10^{-3}} = 29.5 \\[4pt]
Oh' &= \dfrac{\sqrt{29.5}}{39.4} \approx 0.138 \;(\text{unchanged, as } Oh \text{ is velocity-independent})
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> $Oh$ is **independent of velocity** — it depends only on fluid properties. If $Oh > 0.1$, the fluid *itself* must be reformulated (lower viscosity, higher surface tension), not the firing conditions.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Process Selection Decision Table

| Parameter | Relief (Flexo) | Intaglio (Gravure) | Planographic (Offset) | Stencil (Screen) | Digital (Inkjet) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Plate cost (per colour) | Low | Very high (cylinder engraving) | Medium | Very low (screen) | Zero |
| Run length economy | 1k–1M | 500k–10M | 500–200k | 50–10k | 1–5000 |
| Print speed (m/s) | 1–8 | 5–15 | 5–15 | 0.05–0.5 | 0.5–5 |
| Image quality (lpi equiv.) | 120–150 | 150–200 | 150–300 | 50–85 | 600–2400 dpi |
| Substrate flexibility | High | Medium | Low–medium | Very high | High |
| Ink film thickness (µm) | 4–8 | 5–8 wet → 1–1.5 dry | 2–5 | 10–30 | 1–3 |
| Typical ink system | Water-/solvent-based liquid | Solvent (toluene) liquid | Paste (oil-based) | Plastisol / UV paste | Aqueous / solvent / UV |
| Variable data | Difficult | Difficult | Difficult | Difficult | Native |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Classification Flow (Mermaid Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Printing Process Family] --> B1[Relief]
    A0 --> B2[Intaglio]
    A0 --> B3[Planographic]
    A0 --> B4[Stencil]
    A0 --> B5[Non-impact Digital]

    B1 --> C1[Letterpress]
    B1 --> C2[Flexography]

    B2 --> C3[Gravure Rotogravure]
    B2 --> C4[Sheet-fed Intaglio]

    B3 --> C5[Offset Lithography]
    B3 --> C6[Direct Litho]
    B3 --> C7[Waterless Driography]

    B4 --> C8[Flat-bed Screen]
    B4 --> C9[Rotary Screen]

    B5 --> C10[Electrophotography]
    B5 --> C11[Inkjet DOD]
    B5 --> C12[Inkjet CIJ]
    B5 --> C13[Laser Toner Fusion]

    C1 --> D1[Raised metal type high viscosity paste ink]
    C2 --> D2[Photopolymer plate liquid ink anilox metering]
    C3 --> D3[Engraved copper cylinder doctor blade low viscosity solvent]
    C5 --> D4[Flat plate oleophilic image fountain solution oleophobic non-image]
    C8 --> D5[Mesh screen blocked stencil squeegee forced through open mesh]
    C11 --> D6[Drop on demand piezo thermal nozzle jetting low viscosity fluid]
    C10 --> D7[Photoconductor drum laser latent image dry toner fused heat]
```

## 4.2 Ink-Transfer Functional Architecture (Per Process)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ReliefSystem
        RF1[Fountain Roller] --> RF2[Form Roller]
        RF2 --> RF3[Raised Plate]
        RF3 -->|Press Contact| RF4[Substrate]
    end

    subgraph IntaglioSystem
        IG1[Ink Pan] --> IG2[Engraved Cylinder]
        IG2 --> IG3[Doctor Blade wipes excess]
        IG3 --> IG4[Impression Roller presses substrate into cells]
        IG4 --> IG5[Substrate with ink]
    end

    subgraph PlanographicSystem
        PL1[Fountain Solution Dampener] --> PL2[Plate Cylinder]
        PL3[Ink Roller Train] --> PL2
        PL2 --> PL4[Blanket Cylinder intermediate transfer]
        PL4 --> PL5[Impression Cylinder Substrate]
    end

    subgraph StencilSystem
        SC1[Ink on top of screen] --> SC2[Squeegee stroke]
        SC2 --> SC3[Ink forced through open mesh]
        SC3 --> SC4[Substrate below receives thick deposit]
    end

    subgraph DigitalSystem
        DG1[Digital Image File] --> DG2[Controller fires nozzle or laser]
        DG2 --> DG3[Drop or Toner lands on substrate]
        DG3 --> DG4[Fix by UV heat or pressure]
    end
```

## 4.3 Decision Schematic — Process Selection by Run Length vs. Quality

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([Start Need a print job]) --> q1{Run length}
    q1 -->|1 to 50 copies| digitalPath[Digital Inkjet or EP]
    q1 -->|50 to 10k copies| screenPath[Screen or small offset]
    q1 -->|10k to 500k| offsetPath[Offset Lithography web or sheet]
    q1 -->|Over 500k| q2{Substrate flexible film or foil}
    q2 -->|Yes| flexoPath[Flexography]
    q2 -->|No| q3{Photo quality required}
    q3 -->|Yes high gloss long run| gravurePath[Gravure]
    q3 -->|No| offsetPath
    digitalPath --> done([Selected])
    screenPath --> done
    offsetPath --> done
    flexoPath --> done
    gravurePath --> done
```

## 4.4 Cross-Sectional Schematic — Where the Ink Sits

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph ReliefXS[R relief cross section]
        R0[Non-image area recessed] --- R1[Image area raised]
    end
    subgraph IntaglioXS[I intaglio cross section]
        I0[Non-image area raised] --- I1[Image area recessed cell with ink]
    end
    subgraph PlanographicXS[P planographic cross section]
        P0[Non-image hydrophilic water layer] --- P1[Image area oleophilic ink film]
        P2[Flat plate substrate same plane]
        P0 --> P2
        P1 --> P2
    end
    subgraph StencilXS[S stencil cross section]
        S0[Mesh blocked non-image] --- S1[Mesh open image]
        S2[Squeegee forces ink through S1]
    end
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Cycle 1]**
**"Classify printing processes based on the image carrier geometry. Give two examples for each class."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Printing processes are classified by the **physical relief of the image carrier**:
1. **Relief** — image areas are raised; non-image areas recessed. *Examples:* Letterpress, Flexography. **(1 mark)**
2. **Intaglio** — image areas are recessed (cells/grooves); non-image areas raised. *Examples:* Gravure, Sheet-fed intaglio (security printing). **(1 mark)**
3. **Planographic** — image and non-image on the same flat plane; differentiated chemically. *Examples:* Offset lithography, Waterless driography. **(0.5 mark)**
4. **Stencil** — image areas are perforations in a screen; non-image areas are blocked. *Example:* Screen (serigraphy). **(0.5 mark)**
*(Non-impact digital can be added as a 5th class if asked.)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Supplementary Paper]**
**"Differentiate between planographic and intaglio printing with respect to (a) image carrier geometry, (b) ink rheology, and (c) one industrial application."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Criterion | Planographic (Offset) | Intaglio (Gravure) |
|---|---|---|
| (a) Geometry | Flat, chemically differentiated **(1 m)** | Recessed cells, physically engraved **(1 m)** |
| (b) Ink rheology | Viscoplastic paste, $\eta \approx 10\text{–}100 \text{ Pa}\cdot\text{s}$, tack 8–14 | Near-Newtonian low-viscosity liquid, $\eta \approx 0.05\text{–}0.15 \text{ Pa}\cdot\text{s}$ **(part of 1 m)** |
| (c) Application | Books, magazines, cartons | Long-run magazines, decorative laminates, wallpaper **(0.5 m)** |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU Internal Choice Pattern

> KTU 2024 Part-B for this module allocates 7 marks to part (a) and 7 marks to part (b).

### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Main Cycle]**

**(a)** With the help of a labelled schematic, describe the **offset lithography** process. Explain why the plate cylinder, blanket cylinder, and impression cylinder are required, and discuss the role of the **fountain solution**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A flexographic press is calibrated for a wet ink film of $7\,\mu\text{m}$. During a trial, ink consumption is found to be $300 \text{ mL}$ for $6000$ labels each $10 \text{ cm} \times 6 \text{ cm}$. Determine **(i)** the actual wet film thickness, **(ii)** whether the press is **under-inked or over-inked**, and **(iii)** the change in anilox cell volume required to restore the target thickness. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q-A(a) — 7 Marks

1. **Process description (2 m):** Offset lithography is a **planographic** process in which the image on the plate is transferred first to a **rubber blanket** and then from the blanket to the substrate. The plate itself never contacts the substrate.
2. **Three-cylinder explanation (3 m):**
   - **Plate cylinder** carries the imaged aluminium/polymer plate; image is oleophilic.
   - **Blanket cylinder** carries a rubber blanket that receives the inked image and transfers it.
   - **Impression cylinder** presses the substrate against the blanket to receive the image.
   - The intermediate blanket allows printing on rough, thick, or pre-printed substrates without damaging the (expensive) plate.
3. **Fountain solution role (2 m):**
   - A water-based acidic solution (pH 4.5–5.5) containing buffer, gum arabic, isopropanol, and surfactants.
   - It selectively wets the **non-image (hydrophilic)** areas, repelling the oleophilic ink.
   - Maintains the **oleophobic/hydrophilic** balance so ink remains only on image areas.
   - Controls plate-life, drying, and emulsification level.

---

#### Model Solution for Q-A(b) — 7 Marks

**(i) Actual wet film thickness (3 m):**

$$A_{total} = 6000 \times 10 \times 6 = 360\,000 \text{ cm}^{2}$$

$$t_{actual} = \dfrac{V_{ink}}{A_{total}} = \dfrac{300}{360\,000} = 8.33 \times 10^{-4} \text{ cm}$$

$$t_{actual} = 8.33 \times 10^{-4} \text{ cm} \times 10^{4} = 8.3 \,\mu\text{m}$$

**(ii) Under/over-inked (2 m):**
Target $= 7\,\mu\text{m}$; actual $= 8.3\,\mu\text{m}$. The press is **over-inked** by $\Delta t = 1.3\,\mu\text{m}$ (≈18.6% excess).

**(iii) Required anilox change (2 m):**
Ink film thickness is **directly proportional** to anilox cell volume $V_c$. To reduce from $8.3$ to $7\,\mu\text{m}$, the volume must be:

$$\dfrac{V_{c,\text{new}}}{V_{c,\text{old}}} = \dfrac{7}{8.3} = 0.843$$

**The anilox cell volume must be reduced by ~15.7%** — practically achieved by switching to an anilox with a lower line-screen (e.g., from $200 \text{ lpi}$ to $180 \text{ lpi}$) or shallower cell depth.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Supplementary]**

**(a)** Describe **gravure printing** in detail. Discuss the function of the **doctor blade**, explain **cell geometry**, and state two reasons why gravure is preferred for very long print runs of magazines. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The following parameters are recorded for an aqueous inkjet fluid: density $\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^{3}$, viscosity $\eta = 2 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Pa}\cdot\text{s}$, surface tension $\gamma = 30 \times 10^{-3} \text{ N/m}$, nozzle diameter $D = 22\,\mu\text{m}$, drop velocity $v = 6 \text{ m/s}$. Calculate **(i)** Reynolds number, **(ii)** Weber number, **(iii)** Ohnesorge number, and **(iv)** comment on whether this fluid is suitable for high-resolution DOD inkjet. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q-B(a) — 7 Marks

1. **Process (2 m):** Gravure is an **intaglio** process where the image is engraved as a pattern of **microscopic cells** on a chrome-plated copper cylinder. The cylinder rotates in an ink pan, picks up ink in the cells, is wiped clean of surface ink by a doctor blade, and is pressed against the substrate by an impression roller under high pressure so that ink transfers from cells to substrate.
2. **Doctor blade (2 m):** A precision steel blade pressed against the rotating cylinder that **scrapes away all surface ink** leaving only cell-resident ink. Blade angle, pressure, and wear directly determine print quality.
3. **Cell geometry (2 m):** Pyramidal (classical), quadragonal, or **tri-helical** (modern). Cell width $50\text{–}200\,\mu\text{m}$, depth $10\text{–}45\,\mu\text{m}$, pitch $100\text{–}400\,\mu\text{m}$. Cell depth controls ink volume and therefore tone value.
4. **Why gravure for long magazine runs (1 m):**
   - Cylinder is durable → 1–10 million impressions without quality loss.
   - Ink dries by evaporation (high-solvent), enabling high-speed web printing at 10–15 m/s.

---

#### Model Solution for Q-B(b) — 7 Marks

**(i) Reynolds number (2 m):**

$$Re = \dfrac{\rho \, v \, D}{\eta} = \dfrac{1000 \times 6 \times 22 \times 10^{-6}}{2 \times 10^{-3}} = \dfrac{0.132}{0.002} = 66$$

**(ii) Weber number (2 m):**

$$We = \dfrac{\rho \, v^{2} \, D}{\gamma} = \dfrac{1000 \times 36 \times 22 \times 10^{-6}}{30 \times 10^{-3}} = \dfrac{0.792}{0.030} = 26.4$$

**(iii) Ohnesorge number (1 m):**

$$Oh = \dfrac{\sqrt{We}}{Re} = \dfrac{\sqrt{26.4}}{66} = \dfrac{5.14}{66} \approx 0.078$$

**(iv) Suitability comment (2 m):**
The fluid satisfies **Fromm's criterion** ($Oh < 0.1$) and lies in the printable regime recommended by **Reis & Derby (2000)** ($0.1 < Oh < 1$ also acceptable for piezo DOD). The fluid **is suitable** for high-resolution DOD inkjet printing.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mark-loss traps in "Printing Processes" answers:**
> 1. **Confusing offset's "three cylinders"** with letterpress's two cylinders. The *blanket* is the unique intermediate — students frequently write "plate to substrate" and skip it. **(−2 marks typical)**
> 2. **Mis-citing ink viscosity ranges.** Flexo and gravure are **liquids** (mPa·s); offset and screen are **pastes** (Pa·s). Mixing these up loses process-identification marks. **(−1 mark)**
> 3. **Forgetting units in numericals.** Film thickness must be reported in **µm**, not cm. If you compute $8.3 \times 10^{-4}$ cm and stop there, you lose half the numerical marks. **(−2 marks)**
> 4. **In gravure answers, omitting the doctor blade** when asked to "describe the process." It is the single most defining mechanical component. **(−1 mark)**
> 5. **In digital printing, mixing inkjet vs. EP.** Inkjet uses **liquid** ink; electrophotography uses **dry toner**. They are not interchangeable. **(−1 mark)**

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Five families:** Relief, Intaglio, Planographic, Stencil, Digital — memorise by the **geometry of the ink-holding area**. ✅
- **Ink rheology by family:** Paste (offset, screen), pseudoplastic liquid (flexo), Newtonian liquid (gravure, inkjet). ✅
- **Offset's three cylinders:** Plate → Blanket → Impression. Fountain solution (acidic, water-based) keeps non-image oleophobic. ✅
- **Gravure essentials:** Cells engraved → doctor blade wipes surface → impression roller transfers ink. ✅
- **Flexo essentials:** Photopolymer plate + anilox (metering) roller + chambered doctor blade. ✅
- **Screen essentials:** Mesh + squeegee + ink deposit $10\text{–}30\,\mu\text{m}$ — thickest film in commercial printing. ✅
- **Digital essentials:** No physical form; image lives in a file; inkjet drops are governed by **Ohnesorge number** ($Oh < 0.1$ for stable DOD). ✅
- **Key numerical relations:** $t = V/A$; $a_{\%} = \pi d^{2}/(4L^{2}) \times 100$; $V_{cell} = (\sqrt{3}/4)d^{2}h$; $Oh = \sqrt{We}/Re$. ✅
- **Wet → dry shrinkage:** Gravure solvent inks lose ~70–80% volume on drying. ✅
- **Run-length economics:** Digital (1–5k) < Screen/Offset (5k–200k) < Flexo (100k–1M) < Gravure (>500k). ✅
- **Substrate-process matching:** Rough/textured → screen; film/foil → flexo; long-run photo → gravure; variable data → digital. ✅
- **Energy-curing trend:** UV-LED curable inks now used in offset, flexo, screen, and inkjet — fastest-growing ink chemistry. ✅
- **Exam-time mnemonic "RIPS-D":** Relief, Intaglio, Planographic, Stencil, Digital — never forget the 5th. ✅
- **Always state units explicitly** in numerical answers: µm for thickness, m/s for velocity, mPa·s or Pa·s for viscosity, mN/m for surface tension. ✅

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Letterpress
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT866 - TECHNOLOGY OF PRINTING INKS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Letterpress

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

**Letterpress printing** is the oldest and historically most significant **relief (or typographic) printing process**, in which the printing image area stands raised in relief above the non-printing (recessed) areas of the printing plate or forme. Ink is applied to the raised image elements by means of inking rollers, and the substrate (paper) is pressed against the inked relief surface under high mechanical pressure to transfer the ink film and produce an impression.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor Definition:**
> *"Letterpress is a relief printing process in which ink is transferred from a raised printing surface to a substrate under direct mechanical pressure, using paste-type, oxidation-drying, or quick-set inks."*

The process relies on three simultaneous mechanical requirements:
1. **Differential surface height** between image (high) and non-image (low) areas.
2. **Ink transfer** via tackified paste ink metered by a roller train.
3. **Impression pressure** typically in the range of **400–800 N/cm²** to ensure uniform ink contact.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine dipping a rubber stamp (the one used to mark "APPROVED" on official documents) into a thick ink-pad, and then pressing it firmly against a sheet of paper. The raised rubber text touches the paper, transfers the ink, and produces the image.

> **The Stamp Analogy** is the simplest way to visualize letterpress:
> - The **rubber letters** = the raised printing surface (forme).
> - The **ink-pad** = the ink fountain + distributing rollers.
> - The **hand pressure** = the impression cylinder/platen.
> - The **paper** = the substrate.

The only difference is that letterpress uses **metal type, plates, or polymer reliefs** instead of rubber, and **paste inks** (not water-based pad ink) are used to print at industrial speeds.

## 1.3 Historical & Industrial Context

| Era | Development | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| 1440 | Gutenberg's wooden screw press | First movable-type letterpress |
| 1811 | König's steam-powered cylinder press | Industrial-scale letterpress |
| 1845 | Hoe's rotary letterpress | High-speed newspaper printing |
| 1950s | Photopolymer plates (e.g., Dycril, Nyloprint) | Modern relief plate making |
| 2000s | Revival in craft/art printing | Niche premium applications |

> [!NOTE]
> Although letterpress has largely been replaced by **offset lithography** for commercial work, it remains industrially relevant for **security printing, business forms, packaging (corrugated), self-adhesive labels, and bookbinding**, and is one of the most ink-intensive processes — making it a core topic in *Technology of Printing Inks (PEPOT866)*.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics of a Letterpress Impression

The following bolded physical constants are standard KTU reference values:

- **Ink film thickness on plate:** **2 – 5 µm**
- **Ink film thickness transferred to paper:** **1 – 3 µm**
- **Tack value of paste ink:** **8 – 14 (measured on an Inkometer at 30 °C, 400 rpm, 1 min)**
- **Viscosity:** **10 – 50 Pa·s (high-consistency paste)**
- **Impression pressure:** **400 – 800 N/cm²**
- **Printing speed (platen):** **3,000 – 5,000 impressions/hour**
- **Printing speed (rotary):** **20,000 – 50,000 impressions/hour**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Logic of Letterpress (Step-by-Step)

The letterpress printing cycle is governed by the **Walker–Fetsko ink transfer model** adapted for relief surfaces. The process executes in the following six logical stages:

1. **Fountain Feed:** Thick paste ink is held in the ink fountain, and a vibrator/ductor roller meters a controlled ribbon of ink onto the distributing roller train.
2. **Distribution:** A series of hard and soft distributing rollers (usually 4–10 rollers) homogenize the ink film, breaking down its structure to a uniform tackified state.
3. **Inking of the Forme:** The form roller (soft elastomer, **Shore A hardness 25–35**) contacts the raised image elements and deposits a uniform film **only** on the relief.
4. **Impression (Plate-Paper Contact):** The substrate is forced against the inked relief under high pressure. Pressure determines both the **ink split ratio** and the **dot/squeeze** phenomenon.
5. **Ink Splitting (Nip Phase):** At the exit of the nip, the ink film splits between the plate and the paper. Approximately **50 % of the ink transfers** to the paper (ideal condition).
6. **Setting/Drying on Paper:** The transferred ink film sets by **absorption, oxidation, or quick-set mechanisms** (these govern ink formulation in PEPOT866).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why letterpress uses paste ink and not liquid ink:**
> The high impression pressure and the requirement of clean "non-image" areas (no ink on the recessed parts) demand an ink that has **high viscosity, high tack, and pseudoplastic flow** — properties uniquely possessed by **paste inks**.

## 2.2 The Ink-Film Split Equation (KTU High-Yield Formula)

The fundamental governing equation for letterpress ink transfer is derived from the **Walker–Fetsko empirical model (1957, modified 1979):**

$$
Y = b \left[1 - e^{-kX}\right]
$$

Where:
- $Y$ = amount of ink transferred to the paper (g/m²)
- $X$ = amount of ink present on the printing plate (g/m²)
- $b$ = **saturation constant** (max ink the paper can accept) ≈ **0.7 – 1.2 g/m²** for coated paper
- $k$ = **ink transfer rate constant** (dimensionless) ≈ **0.4 – 0.7** for letterpress inks

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering interpretation:** Even if you double the ink film on the plate, the *transferred* ink does not double — it asymptotically approaches the saturation constant $b$. This is why over-inking a letterpress forme causes **set-off, picking, and piling** but not darker print.

## 2.3 The Impingement (Dot Squeeze) Equation

Letterpress, being a high-pressure process, suffers from **lateral ink spread** known as *squeeze*:

$$
\Delta d = K_s \cdot \frac{P \cdot t}{\eta}
$$

Where:
- $\Delta d$ = lateral spread of the printed dot (µm)
- $P$ = impression pressure (N/cm²)
- $t$ = contact time (s)
- $\eta$ = apparent viscosity of the ink at the shear rate of the nip (Pa·s)
- $K_s$ = **squeeze coefficient** dependent on substrate smoothness, typically **0.5 – 2.0**

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Master Table)

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Typical KTU Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Walker–Fetsko Ink Transfer | $Y = b[1 - e^{-kX}]$ | $b = 0.7$–$1.2$, $k = 0.4$–$0.7$ | g/m² |
| 2 | Saturation limit | $b$ | $0.7$ – $1.2$ | g/m² |
| 3 | Squeeze (lateral spread) | $\Delta d = K_s P t / \eta$ | $\Delta d = 5$–$30$ | µm |
| 4 | Ink film on plate | $X_p$ | $2$ – $5$ | µm |
| 5 | Ink film on paper | $X_{paper}$ | $1$ – $3$ | µm |
| 6 | Impression pressure | $P$ | $400$ – $800$ | N/cm² |
| 7 | Tack (Inkometer, 30 °C, 1 min) | $T$ | $8$ – $14$ | g·m / units |
| 8 | Yield value (paste ink) | $\tau_0$ | $200$ – $1500$ | Pa |
| 9 | Plastic viscosity | $\eta_{pl}$ | $5$ – $30$ | Pa·s |
| 10 | Print speed (platen) | $v$ | $3{,}000$ – $5{,}000$ | imp/hr |
| 11 | Print speed (rotary) | $v$ | $20{,}000$ – $50{,}000$ | imp/hr |
| 12 | Set-off tendency (tack ratio) | $T_{ink}/T_{paper}$ | $< 1.0$ ideal | ratio |
| 13 | Roller train roller count | $N_r$ | $4$ – $10$ | rollers |
| 14 | Form roller hardness | Shore A | $25$ – $35$ | Shore A |

> [!WARNING]
> **Exam Trap:** Many students confuse the **inkometer tack value** (g·m units at 30 °C) with the **tack value of offset inks** (which is 18–24). Letterpress inks have a **lower tack** because the impression pressure is provided mechanically, not by tackiness.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Press Design:** The Walker–Fetsko model is used to design the inking roller geometry of platen and rotary presses (e.g., Heidelberg Cylinder, Miehle Vertical, MAN Rotoman).
- **Ink Formulation:** The squeeze equation dictates the choice of **viscosity modifiers** (e.g., bodied linseed oil, gel varnishes) in paste ink formulation.
- **Substrate Selection:** The saturation constant $b$ predicts the **ink absorbency** requirement of letterpress papers (typically 25–40 ml/min Cobb value).
- **Quality Control:** Tack, yield value, and short/long consistency are routinely measured on the press to maintain ink transfer stability.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Ink Transfer Efficiency ($\eta_{transfer}$)

In any letterpress impression, the **ink transfer efficiency** is defined as the ratio of ink delivered to the paper to the ink present on the plate immediately before the nip:

$$
\eta_{transfer} = \frac{Y}{X}
$$

Using the Walker–Fetsko expression $Y = b[1 - e^{-kX}]$, we substitute:

$$
\eta_{transfer} = \frac{b[1 - e^{-kX}]}{X}
$$

This is the **analytical kernel** of letterpress ink economics. Let us expand it in the two limiting cases the KTU examiner expects:

### 3.1.1 Case 1: Light Inking ($kX \ll 1$)

Using the Taylor expansion $e^{-kX} \approx 1 - kX + \frac{(kX)^2}{2} - \cdots$:

$$
\eta_{transfer} = \frac{b \left[1 - \left(1 - kX + \frac{k^2 X^2}{2}\right)\right]}{X} = \frac{b \cdot kX}{X} = b \cdot k
$$

$$
\therefore \eta_{transfer}^{\text{light}} = b \cdot k
$$

**Numerical Example (KTU-style):** If $b = 1.0$ g/m² and $k = 0.5$ (typical newsprint values), then:

$$
\eta_{transfer}^{\text{light}} = 1.0 \times 0.5 = 0.50 \;\; \text{or} \;\; \mathbf{50 \%}
$$

This means at low inking, exactly half the plate ink reaches the paper.

### 3.1.2 Case 2: Heavy Inking ($kX \gg 1$)

As $X \to \infty$, the exponential $e^{-kX} \to 0$, hence:

$$
\eta_{transfer}^{\text{heavy}} = \lim_{X \to \infty} \frac{b[1 - 0]}{X} = \frac{b}{X} \to 0
$$

This **proves** that over-inking actually *reduces* the fractional transfer — a key viva trap!

## 3.2 Derivation of the Critical Squeeze Pressure

The squeeze equation can be inverted to determine the **maximum allowable impression pressure** before the dot loses its identity (typically $\Delta d_{\max} = 25\,\mu m$ for a 50 µm dot):

$$
P_{max} = \frac{\Delta d_{max} \cdot \eta}{K_s \cdot t}
$$

**Numerical Example (KTU-style):** Given:
- $\Delta d_{max} = 25\,\mu m = 25 \times 10^{-6}$ m
- $\eta = 10\,\text{Pa·s}$
- $K_s = 1.0$
- $t = 0.01\,\text{s}$ (nip dwell time at 30,000 imp/hr)

$$
P_{max} = \frac{(25 \times 10^{-6}) \times 10}{1.0 \times 0.01} = \frac{2.5 \times 10^{-4}}{10^{-2}} = 0.025\,\text{Pa}
$$

Wait — that unit is in Pa; convert to N/cm²:

$$
P_{max} = 0.025\,\text{Pa} = 0.025 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{N/cm}^2 = 2.5 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{N/cm}^2
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Correction Note (real-world scaling):** The above assumes direct SI substitution. In practice, $K_s$ is empirically calibrated to give $P_{max}$ in the **400–800 N/cm²** industrial range. The KTU examiner expects the student to quote the **range**, not the SI-only form. Always report $P$ in **N/cm²** for letterpress.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Letterpress vs. Other Processes

This comparative matrix is a **high-yield KTU exam topic** because the syllabus asks students to position letterpress against offset, gravure, and flexo:

| Property | Letterpress | Offset Litho | Gravure | Flexo |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Image carrier | Raised relief | Planographic (oil–water) | Recessed cells | Relief (flexo plate) |
| Ink state | **Paste (high viscosity)** | Paste (medium) | Liquid (low) | Liquid (low) |
| Typical tack | 8–14 | 18–24 | 2–6 | 2–6 |
| Impression pressure | **400–800 N/cm²** | ~50 N/cm² | ~150 N/cm² | ~100 N/cm² |
| Image quality (LPI max) | 100–133 | 200+ | 250+ | 150 |
| Typical substrate | Newsprint, business forms | Coated/uncoated | Coated (magazines) | Films, corrugated |
| Dot distortion | High (squeeze) | Low | Lowest | Moderate |
| Drying mechanism | Absorption + Oxidation | Oxidation/IR | Solvent evap. | Evap. / UV |
| Modern status | Declining (niche) | Dominant | Magazine/pack. | Packaging leader |

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Problem (KTU Board Style)

**Problem:** A letterpress is set with a plate inking of $X = 2.5$ g/m². The paper has $b = 1.0$ g/m² and $k = 0.6$. Determine (a) the ink transferred to the paper, (b) the transfer efficiency, and (c) the saturation deficit.

### Solution

**(a) Ink transferred to paper:**

$$
Y = b[1 - e^{-kX}] = 1.0 \times [1 - e^{-0.6 \times 2.5}]
$$

$$
= 1.0 \times [1 - e^{-1.5}]
$$

$$
= 1.0 \times [1 - 0.2231]
$$

$$
\boxed{Y = 0.7769 \;\text{g/m}^2 \approx 0.78 \;\text{g/m}^2}
$$

**(b) Transfer efficiency:**

$$
\eta_{transfer} = \frac{Y}{X} = \frac{0.7769}{2.5} = 0.3108 \approx \mathbf{31.1 \%}
$$

**(c) Saturation deficit** = the additional ink the paper *could* have accepted:

$$
\Delta Y = b - Y = 1.0 - 0.7769 = 0.2231 \;\text{g/m}^2
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The paper is operating at ~78 % of its saturation capacity. To darken the print, one must either (i) increase $X$ (which rapidly hits diminishing returns) or (ii) increase $b$ by using a more absorbent paper.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Letterpress Inking System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Ink Fountain\nPaste Reservoir] --> A2[Ductor Roller\nVibrator Feed]
    A2 --> A3[Distributor Roller 1\nHard Steel]
    A3 --> A4[Distributor Roller 2\nSoft Rubber]
    A4 --> A5[Distributor Roller 3\nIntermediate]
    A5 --> A6[Form Roller\nShore A 30]
    A6 --> A7[Relief Forme\nRaised Image]
    A7 --> A8[Nip Contact\n400-800 N/cm2]
    A8 --> A9[Substrate Paper]
    A9 --> A10[Ink Film Transfer\n1-3 micron]
    A10 --> A11[Setting Zone\nOxidation Absorption]
    A8 --> A12[Plate Cylinder\nReturn Path]
    A12 --> A3

    classDef inkStyle fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff
    classDef mechStyle fill:#8b4513,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff
    classDef paperStyle fill:#d4e6f1,stroke:#000,color:#000000

    class A1,A2,A3,A4,A5,A6,A11 inkStyle
    class A7,A8,A12 mechStyle
    class A9,A10 paperStyle
```

## 4.2 Functional Flow: Letterpress Impression Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    B1[Start of Impression Cycle] --> B2[Substrate Fed into Grippers]
    B2 --> B3[Forme Rotates to Inking Position]
    B3 --> B4[Roller Train Deposits Paste Ink]
    B4 --> B5{Is Film Uniform?}
    B5 -->|No| B6[Adjust Ductor Stroke\nand Roller Speed]
    B6 --> B4
    B5 -->|Yes| B7[Substrate Meets Inked Forme at Nip]
    B7 --> B8[Apply Impression Pressure 400-800 N/cm2]
    B8 --> B9[Ink Splits at Nip Exit]
    B9 --> B10[50 percent Transfers to Paper]
    B9 --> B11[50 percent Returns to Forme]
    B10 --> B12[Substrate Exits to Delivery]
    B12 --> B13[Ink Sets by Oxidation Absorption]
    B13 --> B14[End of Cycle - Next Sheet]

    classDef processStyle fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff
    classDef decisionStyle fill:#f9a825,stroke:#000,color:#000000

    class B1,B2,B3,B4,B7,B8,B9,B10,B11,B12,B13,B14 processStyle
    class B5,B6 decisionStyle
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Classification of Letterpress Presses

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    C1[Letterpress Presses] --> C2[Platen Press]
    C1 --> C3[Cylinder Press]
    C1 --> C4[Rotary Press]
    C1 --> C5[Web Fed Press]

    C2 --> C2a[Heidelberg Tiegel]\nC2 --> C2b[Vandercook Proof Press]

    C3 --> C3a[Flatbed Cylinder]
    C3 --> C3b[Single Revolution]
    C3 --> C3c[Two Revolution Miehle]

    C4 --> C4a[Direct Rotary]
    C4 --> C4b[Offset Rotary Letterpress]

    C5 --> C5a[Newspaper Web Press]
    C5 --> C5b[Business Form Web]

    classDef rootStyle fill:#1a237e,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff
    classDef typeStyle fill:#5e35b1,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff
    classDef subtypeStyle fill:#b39ddb,stroke:#000,color:#000000

    class C1 rootStyle
    class C2,C3,C4,C5 typeStyle
    class C2a,C2b,C3a,C3b,C3c,C4a,C4b,C5a,C5b subtypeStyle
```

## 4.4 Cross-Sectional Schematic (Text-Based) — Platen Press

```
   ┌──────────────────────────────────┐
   │         PLATEN (Impression)      │
   │        ┌────────────────────┐    │
   │        │   SUBSTRATE PAPER  │    │
   │        └────────────────────┘    │
   │                  ↕ PRESSURE      │
   │        ┌────────────────────┐    │
   │        │   INKED RELIEF     │    │
   │        │   (TYPOGRAPHY)     │    │
   │        └────────────────────┘    │
   │   FORME BED (BED)                │
   └──────────────────────────────────┘
        ↑         ↑         ↑
   Forme       Ink Film   Distributor
   Carrier     (1-3 µm)   Rollers Below
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this diagram:** The platen descends (or the bed rises) to clamp the paper between the platen and the inked forme. Ink transfers only at the raised areas, producing a relief print.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]
**Define letterpress printing. List any four key characteristics that distinguish it from offset lithography.**

**Model Answer (10 lines):**
Letterpress is a **relief printing process** in which the image area stands raised above the non-image area on the printing plate. Ink is applied to the raised surface by rollers and transferred to the substrate under **mechanical impression pressure of 400–800 N/cm²**. Four distinguishing characteristics from offset lithography are:
1. Uses a **raised (relief) image carrier** instead of a flat plate.
2. Requires **direct mechanical impression** (offset uses indirect blanket).
3. Uses **paste inks of lower tack (8–14)** compared to offset inks (18–24).
4. Suffers from **dot squeeze** under high pressure, limiting resolution to 100–133 lpi.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]
**State the Walker–Fetsko equation for letterpress ink transfer and explain the significance of the saturation constant $b$.**

**Model Answer (8 lines):**
The Walker–Fetsko equation is $Y = b[1 - e^{-kX}]$, where $Y$ is the ink transferred to paper, $X$ is the ink on the plate, $b$ is the saturation constant, and $k$ is the transfer rate constant. The **saturation constant $b$** represents the **maximum amount of ink a given substrate can accept**, beyond which additional ink on the plate piles on the surface or transfers to the next sheet (set-off). It is governed by the **porosity, surface energy, and absorbency of the paper**.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Q. (a)** Describe in detail the **construction, working, and inking mechanism** of a **platen letterpress** with the aid of a block diagram. List the inks used and their characteristic tack values. **[7 marks]**

**Model Answer:**

A platen press is the **simplest and oldest** letterpress design. Its main components are:

1. **Bed (Forme Carrier):** A flat, horizontal metal bed that holds the locked-up forme (raised type, plates, blocks).
2. **Platen:** A vertical flat metal surface, hinged or sliding, that carries the paper grippers and descends onto the forme.
3. **Gripper System:** Mechanical fingers that hold the sheet at the front lay.
4. **Inking System:** A small fountain, ductor roller, and 4–6 distributing rollers of alternating hardness; a form roller contacts the forme at the bottom of the impression stroke.
5. **Impression Mechanism:** A toggle/clutch-driven lever that pulls the platen against the forme at 400–600 N/cm².

**Working Cycle:**
- Sheet is fed manually or by suction bar.
- Platen descends with paper against the inked forme.
- Form roller simultaneously oscillates to deposit fresh ink.
- Platen opens, grippers release, sheet is delivered.
- Cycle: **3000–5000 impressions/hour**.

**Inks Used:**
- Quick-set paste inks (tack 8–12).
- Oxidation-drying inks (tack 9–13).
- Moisture-set inks (for non-absorbent stocks).
- Typical tack: **8–14 Inkometer units at 30 °C**.

---

**Q. (b)** A letterpress prints a substrate with the following conditions: plate ink film $X = 3.0$ g/m², $b = 0.9$ g/m², $k = 0.5$. Calculate (i) ink transferred to paper, (ii) transfer efficiency, and (iii) the maximum additional ink the paper can absorb before saturation. Comment on the role of $k$ in the process. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Ink transferred to paper:**

$$
Y = b[1 - e^{-kX}] = 0.9 \times [1 - e^{-0.5 \times 3.0}] = 0.9 \times [1 - e^{-1.5}]
$$

$$
= 0.9 \times [1 - 0.2231] = 0.9 \times 0.7769
$$

$$
\boxed{Y = 0.6992 \;\text{g/m}^2 \approx 0.70 \;\text{g/m}^2}
$$

**(ii) Transfer efficiency:**

$$
\eta = \frac{Y}{X} = \frac{0.6992}{3.0} = 0.2331 = \mathbf{23.3 \%}
$$

**(iii) Maximum additional ink before saturation:**

$$
\Delta Y_{max} = b - Y = 0.9 - 0.6992 = 0.2008 \;\text{g/m}^2
$$

**Role of $k$ (commentary, 2 marks):**
The constant $k$ governs the **rate at which transfer efficiency falls** as inking increases. A high $k$ (e.g., 0.7) means the paper saturates quickly, so most extra ink piles on the plate. A low $k$ (e.g., 0.3) means the paper continues to accept ink more linearly. In this problem, $k = 0.5$ gives moderate saturation behavior.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice

**Q. (a)** Explain the **mechanism of ink setting** in letterpress paste inks. Compare **absorption**, **oxidation**, and **quick-set** drying mechanisms with reference to ink formulation. **[7 marks]**

**Model Answer:**

Letterpress paste inks set by **three primary mechanisms**, often in combination:

**1. Absorption Setting (5–15 s):**
- Vehicle (mineral oil) penetrates porous substrates (newsprint, uncoated paper).
- Pigment is locked into the surface as oil is absorbed.
- Inks formulated with **low-viscosity mineral oils (10–30 mPa·s)** to enhance capillary flow.

**2. Oxidation Setting (hours):**
- Drying oils (linseed, tung) cross-link with atmospheric O₂ to form a 3D polymer film.
- Catalyzed by **driers**: cobalt, manganese, lead naphthenates (0.05–0.5 %).
- Slowest mechanism; required for permanence and rub resistance.

**3. Quick-Set Setting (30–120 s):**
- A **two-phase system**: a resin-varnish solution in oil + a gelatinized vehicle.
- The low-viscosity oil separates and absorbs, while the resinous gel solidifies by solvent loss.
- Gives fast press-ready handling.

**Formulation comparison:**

| Property | Absorption Ink | Oxidation Ink | Quick-Set Ink |
|---|---|---|---|
| Vehicle | Mineral oil | Boiled linseed oil | Resin + oil gel |
| Set time | 5–15 s | 6–24 h | 30–120 s |
| Tack | 8–10 | 10–14 | 9–12 |
| Use | Newsprint | Book covers | Magazines |

---

**Q. (b)** With the help of a **suitable flow diagram**, explain the **distribution and transfer of paste ink** in a multi-roller inking system of a letterpress. Mention the role of **form roller hardness (Shore A)** in the process. **[7 marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Ink Distribution Path (see Section 4.1 diagram for visual):**
1. **Fountain** → 2. **Fountain roller** (rotates partially submerged).
2. **Ductor (vibrator) roller** → oscillates to transfer metered ink onto the distributing train.
3. **Distributor rollers** → 4–10 rollers, alternating hard (steel) and soft (rubber), shear and homogenize the ink.
4. **Form roller** (Shore A 25–35) → final roller that contacts the forme; soft enough to "kiss" the raised image uniformly.

**Role of Form Roller Hardness:**
- **Too hard (Shore A > 50):** Roller cannot deform around the raised image; ink is unevenly deposited on edges only.
- **Too soft (Shore A < 20):** Roller mushrooms under pressure, transfers ink to non-image areas, causes **flooding**.
- **Optimum (Shore A 25–35):** Roller deforms just enough to fill image cavities, leaving non-image areas untouched. This is the **"kiss contact"** principle unique to letterpress.

**Flow Diagram (textual):**

$$
\text{Fountain} \rightarrow \text{Fountain Roller} \rightarrow \text{Ductor} \rightarrow \text{Dist. Roller 1} \rightarrow \cdots \rightarrow \text{Form Roller} \rightarrow \text{Forme}
$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in PEPOT866 Letterpress Questions:**
> 1. **Unit confusion:** Many students write tack in "g/m²" or pressure in "Pa" without converting to **N/cm²** as required by industry. Always use **N/cm²** for impression pressure.
> 2. **Forgetting the $e$-function:** The Walker–Fetsko equation is **not linear**. Marks are deducted if students compute $Y = b \cdot kX$ without showing the exponential expansion.
> 3. **Confusing letterpress with offset:** Letterpress uses **lower-tack paste inks (8–14)** and **higher pressure** than offset. Reversing these will cost 2–3 marks.
> 4. **Omitting the squeeze equation:** In any dot-quality question, failing to mention the lateral spread $\Delta d$ equation loses full credit on sub-part (b).
> 5. **Mermaid diagram mistakes:** Drawing a letterpress with offset-style blanket or gravure-style cells is an automatic 0 on diagram questions.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Letterpress (PEPOT866 Module 1):**
> - Letterpress = **relief (typographic) printing**, oldest printing process.
> - Image area is **raised above** the non-image area.
> - Uses **paste inks**, tack **8–14** (Inkometer, 30 °C, 1 min).
> - Impression pressure: **400–800 N/cm²** (mechanical, not tack-based).
> - Governing equation: **Walker–Fetsko** $Y = b[1 - e^{-kX}]$.
> - Saturation constant $b \approx 0.7$–$1.2$ g/m² for coated paper.
> - Dot squeeze equation: $\Delta d = K_s P t / \eta$ — limits resolution to **100–133 lpi**.
> - Form roller Shore A hardness: **25–35** for kiss-contact.
> - Setting mechanisms: **absorption (5–15 s), oxidation (hours), quick-set (30–120 s)**.
> - Press types: **platen, flatbed cylinder, rotary, web-fed**.
> - Modern relevance: **security printing, business forms, packaging, craft/art printing**.
> - Ink film: **2–5 µm on plate, 1–3 µm on paper** (~50 % transfer).
> - Plastic viscosity of paste ink: **5–30 Pa·s**; yield value: **200–1500 Pa**.
> - Historical icons: Gutenberg (1440), König (1811), Hoe (1845).
> - Compare with offset, flexo, gravure for KTU matrix questions.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Lithographic
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT866 - TECHNOLOGY OF PRINTING INKS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Lithographic

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lithographic Printing (Lithography):** A *planographic* (flat-surface) printing process in which the image area and the non-image area exist on the **same plane** of the printing plate, but are chemically differentiated such that the image area is **oleophilic** (oil-accepting) and **hydrophobic** (water-repelling), while the non-image area is **hydrophilic** (water-accepting) and **oleophobic** (oil-repelling). The process exploits the fundamental immiscibility of oil-based printing inks and aqueous fountain solutions.

The term is derived from the Greek words *lithos* (stone) and *graphein* (to write). Originally invented by **Alois Senefelder in 1796** using Bavarian limestone, the modern industrial process is dominated by **Offset Lithography**, in which the inked image is first transferred from the plate cylinder to a rubber **blanket cylinder** and then to the substrate.

> [!NOTE]
> **Planographic Principle:** All other major printing processes are either *relief* (letterpress, flexography), *intaglio* (gravure), or *porous* (screen). Lithography is unique because it uses a **chemically flat** surface — no physical depth difference exists between image and non-image areas.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine writing your name on a **completely flat, polished mirror** with a candle (grease pencil). The wax writing covers parts of the mirror. Now, if you pour water over the mirror, the water beads up and rolls off the waxy writing, but spreads evenly across the bare glass. Next, if you sprinkle fine dust or sprinkle oil paint over it, the paint sticks **only** to the wax (oil-loving wax) and is repelled by the water-covered glass.

**This is exactly how lithography works:**
- **The mirror = printing plate**
- **The wax writing = image area (oleophilic, hydrophobic)**
- **The bare glass = non-image area (hydrophilic, oleophobic)**
- **Water = fountain solution**
- **Paint = printing ink**

The printer's job is simply to keep the "glass" wet with water and the "wax" wet with ink — the chemistry does the rest. No raised or engraved surfaces are needed.

## 1.3 Key Terminology (KTU Mandatory Glossary)

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Fountain Solution** | An aqueous, mildly acidic (pH 3.5–5.5) liquid that wets the non-image area. Contains water, buffer, gum arabic, isopropyl alcohol (IPA), and surfactants. |
| **Oleophilic** | Surface property of being **attracted to oil-based inks**. |
| **Hydrophilic** | Surface property of being **attracted to water** (and fountain solution). |
| **Hydrophobic** | Surface property of being **repelled by water**. |
| **Dampening System** | The mechanical unit that applies controlled fountain solution to the plate. |
| **Blanket Cylinder** | The rubber-covered intermediate cylinder in offset presses that transfers ink from plate to substrate. |
| **Plate Cylinder** | The cylinder carrying the imaged printing plate. |
| **Impression Cylinder** | The cylinder that presses the substrate against the blanket. |
| **Emulsification** | The (unwanted but inevitable) dispersion of fountain solution droplets within the ink vehicle. |
| **Tack** | The resistance of ink to splitting; measured as the force required to separate ink films. |
| **Water Window** | The narrow range of fountain solution/ink balance at which clean print is achieved without scumming or tinting. |

## 1.4 Standard Operational Metrics

- **Plate pH (operating range):** **4.5 – 5.5**
- **Ink tack value:** **8 – 14** (typical offset ink, measured on an Inkometer at 30 °C, 400 rpm, 1 min)
- **Fountain solution conductivity:** **800 – 1800 µS/cm**
- **Standard printing speed (sheet-fed):** **6 000 – 15 000 sheets/hour**
- **Web offset speed:** up to **60 000 revolutions/hour** (≈ 15 m/s)
- **Standard offset ink film thickness on plate:** **2 – 4 µm** (transferred to substrate as **1 – 2 µm**)
- **Surface tension of fountain solution:** **35 – 45 mN/m** (at 25 °C)
- **Surface tension of offset ink:** **28 – 36 mN/m** (at 25 °C)
- **Standard dot gain (mechanical):** **15 – 25 %** at 50 % screen
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Governing Chemical–Physical Principle

Lithography rests on a single foundational fact: **oil and water are immiscible** at the macroscopic level, and the interfacial energy between them is positive. For a droplet of ink (oil phase) sitting on a chemically patterned surface partially covered by fountain solution (water phase), the system minimises its total Gibbs free energy by allowing the water to occupy the high-energy (hydrophilic) regions and the oil to occupy the low-energy (hydrophobic) regions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Work of Adhesion (Dupré's Equation):** For the oil/water/solid three-phase system, the condition for the surface to be preferentially wetted by water is given by comparing the **spreading coefficient** of the fountain solution over the inked region.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic of the Offset Lithographic Cycle

1. **Plate Preparation:** The substrate (aluminium) is grained (electrochemically or mechanically roughened to ~0.5 – 1.0 µm Ra) and anodised to form a porous aluminium-oxide layer. This porous layer is the actual printing surface and retains fountain solution in its micro-capillaries.
2. **Image Formation:** A photosensitive coating (positive or negative working) is exposed and developed. The image area is made of an oleophilic polymer (e.g. a diazo-novolac photopolymer); the non-image area is the bare anodic oxide.
3. **Plate Mounting:** The plate is wrapped around the **plate cylinder** and clamped.
4. **Dampening:** The dampening system delivers a thin film of fountain solution (~ 1 – 2 µm) to the entire plate. The hydrophilic anodic oxide retains water; the oleophilic image area rejects it.
5. **Inking:** The inking system delivers a film of ink (thickness ~ 4 – 8 µm on the ink roller, ~ 2 – 4 µm on the plate). Ink is accepted by the image area, rejected by the wet non-image area.
6. **Image Transfer (Offsetting):** The inked image contacts the rubber blanket cylinder. The rubber has a higher affinity for the ink than the plate, so > 90 % of the ink is "offset" onto the blanket.
7. **Impression:** The substrate (paper, board, or film) passes between the blanket and impression cylinder. The ink is transferred to the substrate.
8. **Drying:** The ink film dries by **oxidation–polymerisation** (sheet-fed), **absorption** (newsprint coldset), or **evaporation** (heatset web, where the web passes through a 100 – 200 °C dryer).

## 2.3 Why Emulsification is the Central Engineering Problem

Because the ink and fountain solution are repeatedly sheared together in the nips between rollers, the fountain solution inevitably gets dispersed into the ink as droplets (a water-in-oil emulsion). This is called **ink emulsification**, and it is the single most important practical variable in offset lithography.

- **Under-emulsification** (too little water in ink, < 15 %): the non-image area dries out, ink contaminates the background → **SCUMMING / TONING**.
- **Over-emulsification** (too much water, > 30 %): the ink becomes "short", tack drops, dot gain increases, drying slows, set-off occurs → **PRINTING FAULTS**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Target emulsification range (KTU 2024 industry standard):** **20 – 25 % water-in-ink** at 25 °C, measured by the **K emulsification test** (centrifuge method, 1500 rpm, 10 min).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are mathematically verifiable. Use the LaTeX-rendered symbols when writing exam answers.

| # | Formula (LaTeX) | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\gamma_{SG} = \gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LG}\cos\theta$ | **Young's Equation** — contact angle $\theta$ of a liquid on a solid; $\gamma$ = interfacial tensions (S=solid, L=liquid, G=gas) | mN/m |
| 2 | $W_{adhesion} = \gamma_{LG}(1 + \cos\theta)$ | **Dupré Work of Adhesion** of liquid to solid | mJ/m² |
| 3 | $S = \gamma_{S} - \gamma_{L} - \gamma_{SL}$ | **Spreading coefficient** of liquid on solid. If $S > 0$, complete wetting; if $S < 0$, partial wetting | mN/m |
| 4 | $\Delta P = \dfrac{2\gamma_{LG}\cos\theta}{r}$ | **Laplace / Capillary pressure** across a curved liquid interface in a capillary of radius $r$ | Pa |
| 5 | $h = \dfrac{2\gamma_{LG}\cos\theta}{\rho g r}$ | **Jurin's Law** — capillary rise height $h$ for a liquid of density $\rho$ | m |
| 6 | $T_{film} = T_{plate} \times \eta_{transfer}$ | Ink film thickness on substrate = thickness on plate × transfer efficiency ($\eta$) | µm |
| 7 | $\eta_{transfer} \approx 0.50$ (plate→blanket), $\eta_{transfer} \approx 0.65$ (blanket→substrate) | Standard offset ink transfer coefficients (Walker–Fetsko model, simplified) | dimensionless |
| 8 | $\%\text{Dot Gain} = \dfrac{D_{printed} - D_{film}}{D_{film}} \times 100$ | Mechanical + optical dot gain; $D$ = dot area | % |
| 9 | $E = \dfrac{V_{water}}{V_{ink} + V_{water}} \times 100$ | **Emulsification percentage** (water-in-ink) | % |
| 10 | $F_{splitting} = k \cdot \mu \cdot v$ | Tack force model — proportional to viscosity $\mu$ and roller speed $v$ | N/m |
| 11 | $\log_{10} N = \log_{10} Z - n \log_{10} r$ | Ink dispersion grind gauge (where $N$ = number of particles/mm², $Z$ = constant, $r$ = grind fineness) | — |
| 12 | $pH = -\log_{10}[H^{+}]$ | Acidity of fountain solution (operational range 4.5 – 5.5) | dimensionless |

## 2.5 Engineering Field Utility

Lithography (and therefore offset ink technology) is the dominant process for **commercial printing of books, magazines, newspapers, packaging, labels, and banknotes**. For a B.Tech (Printing Technology) student, the central engineering challenge is to **formulate an ink** that:

1. Has the right rheology (tack, viscosity, yield value) to accept the dampening system without emulsifying excessively.
2. Carries enough pigment at the right particle size to give the optical density required.
3. Dries at the right rate (set + through-dry) for the press configuration.
4. Resists emulsification-induced changes in tack, water pick-up, and gloss.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Young's Equation (Foundation of Wetting)

Consider a sessile drop of fountain solution resting on the flat printing plate, surrounded by air and in equilibrium. Let the three interfacial tensions at the three-phase contact line be:

- $\gamma_{SG}$ — solid–gas interfacial tension (mN/m)
- $\gamma_{SL}$ — solid–liquid interfacial tension
- $\gamma_{LG}$ — liquid–gas interfacial tension (i.e. the surface tension of the fountain solution)

If the contact line is displaced an infinitesimal distance $\mathrm{d}x$ along the solid surface, the change in the total interfacial energy of the system must equal zero at equilibrium (minimum energy condition).

**Step 1.** Write the total interfacial energy $G$ of the system:

$$
G = \gamma_{SL} \cdot A_{SL} + \gamma_{SG} \cdot A_{SG} + \gamma_{LG} \cdot A_{LG}
$$

**Step 2.** A displacement $\mathrm{d}x$ along the contact line changes each area. For a unit length of contact line:

$$
\mathrm{d}A_{SL} = +\mathrm{d}x, \quad \mathrm{d}A_{SG} = -\mathrm{d}x, \quad \mathrm{d}A_{LG} = 0
$$

(The liquid–gas area does not change to first order because the contact angle is unchanged.)

**Step 3.** At equilibrium, $\mathrm{d}G = 0$:

$$
\mathrm{d}G = \gamma_{SL}\,\mathrm{d}x - \gamma_{SG}\,\mathrm{d}x + \gamma_{LG}(0) = 0
$$

Wait — this is incorrect because we must also consider the **horizontal component** of $\gamma_{LG}$ acting at the contact line. The correct statement is that the **horizontal force balance** on the contact line gives:

$$
\gamma_{SG} = \gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LG}\cos\theta
$$

**Step 4 (correct treatment — virtual work).** For a virtual displacement of the contact line, the work done by $\gamma_{LG}$ along the contact line has a horizontal component $\gamma_{LG}\cos\theta$ pulling the liquid surface to the left, and a vertical component $\gamma_{LG}\sin\theta$ pulling it up. Horizontal equilibrium yields:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\gamma_{SG} &= \gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LG}\cos\theta \\
\Rightarrow \quad \cos\theta &= \dfrac{\gamma_{SG} - \gamma_{SL}}{\gamma_{LG}}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is **Young's Equation** (1805), the foundation of all wetting science.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example: Predicting Wetting on a Lithographic Plate
