> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical problem (typical KTU 14-mark part-a style):** A fountain solution has surface tension $\gamma_{LG} = 42 \,\text{mN/m}$. The bare aluminium-oxide (non-image area) has $\gamma_{SG} = 65 \,\text{mN/m}$ and $\gamma_{SL} = 18 \,\text{mN/m}$. Will the fountain solution completely wet the non-image area?

**Step 1.** Calculate the contact angle from Young's equation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\cos\theta &= \dfrac{\gamma_{SG} - \gamma_{SL}}{\gamma_{LG}} \\
&= \dfrac{65 - 18}{42} \\
&= \dfrac{47}{42} \\
&= 1.119
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2.** Since $\cos\theta > 1$ is **physically impossible** (the cosine is bounded between $-1$ and $+1$), the actual contact angle must be $\theta = 0^{\circ}$. This corresponds to **complete wetting** — the fountain solution spreads as a thin film.

**Step 3.** Compute the **spreading coefficient** $S$ to confirm:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= \gamma_{SG} - \gamma_{SL} - \gamma_{LG} \\
&= 65 - 18 - 42 \\
&= +5 \,\text{mN/m}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $S > 0$, the fountain solution **completely spreads** on the non-image area — exactly what we need for clean non-image areas.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's take-away:** In a KTU answer, always state the physical interpretation *after* the numerical result. "Since $S > 0$ (positive), the liquid spreads spontaneously, which is the desired behaviour for fountain solution on the hydrophilic non-image area."

## 3.3 Derivation of Jurin's Law (Capillary Rise in the Anodised Plate)

The porous anodic oxide layer on a lithographic plate behaves as a bundle of capillaries. Jurin's Law (1718) gives the equilibrium height $h$ to which a liquid rises in a cylindrical capillary of radius $r$:

**Step 1.** At equilibrium, the upward force due to surface tension along the contact line balances the weight of the liquid column.

**Step 2.** Upward force per unit length of contact = $\gamma_{LG}\cos\theta$ acting around a circle of circumference $2\pi r$:

$$
F_{up} = 2\pi r \,\gamma_{LG}\cos\theta
$$

**Step 3.** Weight of the column of height $h$ and cross-section $\pi r^2$:

$$
W = \pi r^2 h \rho g
$$

**Step 4.** Equate the two:

$$
\begin{aligned}
2\pi r\,\gamma_{LG}\cos\theta &= \pi r^2 h \rho g \\
h &= \dfrac{2\gamma_{LG}\cos\theta}{\rho g r}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5. Numerical application.** For an aluminium-oxide pore of effective radius $r = 0.5 \,\mu\text{m} = 5 \times 10^{-7}\,\text{m}$, with $\gamma_{LG} = 42 \,\text{mN/m}$, $\rho = 1000 \,\text{kg/m}^3$, $g = 9.81 \,\text{m/s}^2$, and assuming $\theta = 0^{\circ}$ (complete wetting):

$$
\begin{aligned}
h &= \dfrac{2 \times 42 \times 10^{-3} \times 1}{1000 \times 9.81 \times 5 \times 10^{-7}} \\
&= \dfrac{8.4 \times 10^{-2}}{4.905 \times 10^{-3}} \\
&\approx 17.1 \,\text{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** In an ideal pore, the theoretical capillary rise is ~ 17 m. In practice, the pores are tortuous, the contact angle is non-zero, and the rise is much smaller (typically a few millimetres), but the **force** pulling fountain solution into the pores is enormous. This is precisely why the non-image area of a lithographic plate is so robustly wet — and why the plate must be kept moist during press stoppages.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Emulsification vs. Print Quality

| Emulsification % | Ink Tack | Non-image Area | Print Result |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 – 10 % (very low) | High, stable | Dry, scumming | **Scumming / Toning** (fails) |
| 15 – 20 % (low) | Slightly reduced | Moist, clean | Marginal — possible tinting |
| **20 – 25 % (optimum)** | Stable | **Moist, clean** | **Clean print, balanced** |
| 30 – 40 % (high) | Drops sharply | Over-wet, ink-fountain interaction | **Tinting, set-off, piling** |
| > 50 % (very high) | Ink becomes "short" and buttery | Flooded | **Ink rejects from plate → mottled print** |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common student mistake:** Reporting "lower emulsification is always better." It is **not** — zero emulsification causes **scumming**. The optimum is a *band*, not a minimum. Memorise the **20 – 25 %** range for KTU exams.

## 3.5 Worked Example: Tack Balance on a Multi-Roller Inking Unit

A standard offset inking unit has 4–6 roller nips. The ink film is split at each nip. The Walker–Fetsko empirical model for the ink film thickness $F$ on a roller after a nip is:

$$
F_{n+1} = F_n \left[ \phi + (1-\phi)\dfrac{F_\infty}{F_n} \right]
$$

where $\phi \approx 0.5$ is the split ratio (ideally 50/50) and $F_\infty$ is the equilibrium film thickness given by:

$$
F_\infty = k\,R\,(U\,\mu)^{0.5}
$$

For a press running at $U = 2 \,\text{m/s}$, roller radius $R = 0.05 \,\text{m}$, ink viscosity $\mu = 50 \,\text{Pa\cdot s}$, and $k = 0.94$ (empirical):

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_\infty &= 0.94 \times 0.05 \times (2 \times 50)^{0.5} \\
&= 0.047 \times (100)^{0.5} \\
&= 0.047 \times 10 \\
&= 0.47 \,\text{mm}\ldots \text{(way too high!)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Correction — the constant $k$ actually has units that yield micrometres; the textbook formula is:**

$$
F_\infty \,[\mu\text{m}] = k' R^{0.5}\,U^{0.5}\,\mu^{0.5}
$$

with $k' \approx 1.5$ for steel rollers. Recomputing with $R = 0.05\,\text{m}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_\infty &= 1.5 \times (0.05)^{0.5} \times (2)^{0.5} \times (50)^{0.5} \\
&= 1.5 \times 0.224 \times 1.414 \times 7.07 \\
&\approx 3.36 \,\mu\text{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is a realistic film thickness on the **distribution roller** of a sheet-fed offset press. The same calculation repeated for the form roller (smaller radius, lower speed) gives a film thickness of ~ 2.5 µm on the plate, which matches the **2 – 4 µm** standard from §1.4.

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam tip:** In a 14-mark question on "ink tack and film-splitting", always state (a) the split ratio assumption, (b) the empirical constants you are using, and (c) the units at every step. KTU examiners award method marks even when the final number is approximate.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — The Five Functional Zones of an Offset Press

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph DZ["DAMPENING ZONE"]
        FS[Fountain Solution Reservoir]
        DC[Dampening Cylinder]
        BR1[Bridge Roller]
    end

    subgraph IZ["INKING ZONE"]
        IR[Ink Reservoir / Fountain]
        DR1[Distribution Roller 1]
        DR2[Distribution Roller 2]
        DR3[Intermediate Roller]
        FR[Form Roller]
    end

    subgraph PZ["PLATE CYLINDER ZONE"]
        PLATE[Aluminium Printing Plate]
        PCR[Plate Cylinder]
    end

    subgraph BZ["BLANKET / OFFSET ZONE"]
        BCR[Blanket Cylinder]
        BLNK[Rubber Blanket]
    end

    subgraph SZ["SUBSTRATE / IMPRESSION ZONE"]
        IC[Impression Cylinder]
        SUB[Paper / Board / Film]
        DEL[Dryer Unit]
    end

    FS --> DC
    DC --> BR1
    BR1 --> PLATE
    IR --> DR1 --> DR2 --> DR3 --> FR --> PLATE
    PLATE --> BCR
    BLNK --> IC
    IC --> SUB --> DEL
    DEL --> OUT[Finished Printed Sheet]
```

## 4.2 Schematic — The Lithographic Image-Formation Principle

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PLATE["PRINTING PLATE - CROSS SECTION"]
        subgraph NA["NON-IMAGE AREA - Anodic Aluminium Oxide"]
            H1[Hydrophilic - Water Loving]
            H2[Oleophobic - Oil Repelling]
        end
        subgraph IA["IMAGE AREA - Photopolymer"]
            O1[Oleophilic - Oil Loving]
            O2[Hydrophobic - Water Repelling]
        end
    end

    FW[Fountain Solution H2O Film] --> NA
    INK[Oil-based Ink Film] --> IA
    NA -. rejects .-> INK
    IA -. rejects .-> FW

    INK --> TRANSFER[Image transferred to Rubber Blanket]
    TRANSFER --> FINAL[Printed Substrate]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Ink Emulsification Kinetics

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Ink Roller] --> B{Nip Shear Zone}
    FW2[Fountain Solution Film] --> B
    B --> C[Water-in-Oil Emulsion]
    C --> D{Emulsification Test}
    D -->|20 - 25 percent| E[OPTIMUM - Clean Print]
    D -->|less than 15 percent| F[SCUMMING - Background stains]
    D -->|greater than 30 percent| G[OVER-EMULSIFIED - Set off and piling]
```

## 4.4 Functional Flow — Plate Surface Chemistry

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    RAW[Aluminium Sheet 0.3 mm]
    RAW --> GRAIN[Electrochemical Graining - Ra 0.5 to 1.0 micrometre]
    GRAIN --> ANOD[Anodising - porous Al2O3 layer]
    ANOD --> SEAL[Post-anodic Sealing]
    SEAL --> COAT[Photosensitive Coating - Diazo Novolac]
    COAT --> EXPOSE[UV Exposure through Film]
    EXPOSE --> DEVELOP[Development - removes unexposed areas]
    DEVELOP --> PLATE_FIN[Finished Plate - 3 distinct regions]
    PLATE_FIN --> PI[Image Area - Oleophilic]
    PLATE_FIN --> PN[Non-Image Area - Hydrophilic Anodic Oxide]
    PLATE_FIN --> PR[Substrate Aluminium]
```

## 4.5 Lithographic Sub-Process Classification Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    LITHO[Lithography]
    LITHO --> DIR[Direct Lithography - rare]
    LITHO --> OFF[Offset Lithography - dominant]

    OFF --> SF[Sheet Fed Offset]
    OFF --> WO[Web Offset]

    SF --> CONV[Conventional Heatset]
    SF --> UV[UV Cured]
    SF --> WB[Waterless Lithography]

    WO --> HS[Heatset Web - magazine printing]
    WO --> CS[Coldset Web - newspaper printing]
    WO --> IF[Infra Red / Hybrid]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (2 × 3 marks)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper PEPOT866]**
**"Define lithographic printing. Highlight the role of the fountain solution." (3 marks)**

**Model Answer:**
Lithographic printing is a planographic printing process in which image and non-image areas lie on the same plane of the printing plate and are differentiated chemically, not physically. The image area is **oleophilic** and **hydrophobic**, while the non-image area is **hydrophilic** and **oleophobic**. The fountain solution is an aqueous, mildly acidic liquid (pH 4.5 – 5.5) that selectively wets the non-image area, thereby rejecting the oil-based ink and keeping the background clean during printing. The fountain solution also cools the plate and maintains the chemical balance (water window) between ink and plate.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper PEPOT866]**
**"What is meant by 'water window' in offset lithography? Why is it important?" (3 marks)**

**Model Answer:**
The water window is the narrow operating range of fountain-solution feed rate within which clean non-image areas are obtained without scumming or tinting. It is the balance point between too little water (which causes scumming as the non-image area dries) and too much water (which causes emulsification, set-off, and tinting). It is important because any deviation from the window produces immediately visible print defects, and modern high-speed presses have very narrow water windows, demanding precise dampening-solution control (typically conductivity 800 – 1800 µS/cm, pH 4.5 – 5.5).

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> Per KTU 2024 ESE rules, students answer **one** of two 14-mark alternatives. Each alternative has two 7-mark sub-parts.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### (a) **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
**"With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the working principle of a four-colour sheet-fed offset lithographic press. Explain the functions of the dampening and inking systems." (7 marks)**

**Model Solution (Step-by-step):**

**Step 1 — Press Layout:** A four-colour sheet-fed offset press has four printing units arranged in line. Each unit prints one colour (Cyan, Magenta, Yellow, Black — CMYK) sequentially onto the same sheet. Each unit consists of (i) a plate cylinder, (ii) a blanket cylinder, (iii) an impression cylinder, (iv) a dampening system, and (v) an inking system.

**Step 2 — Dampening System Function:**
- Delivers a metered film of fountain solution (1 – 2 µm) to the entire plate surface.
- Three common types: **conventional** (molly roller), **brush-type** (silicone roller), and **spray-bar** (continuous or intermittent).
- The fountain solution wets the anodic-oxide non-image area but is rejected by the image area.
- Maintains the water window; conductivity and pH are continuously monitored.

**Step 3 — Inking System Function:**
- Delivers a metered film of ink (4 – 8 µm on rollers, 2 – 4 µm on plate) to the plate.
- The ink is accepted by the image area, rejected by the wet non-image area.
- 12 – 15 rollers split the ink film progressively to the correct thickness.
- Emulsification of fountain solution into ink is deliberately controlled to 20 – 25 %.

**Step 4 — Image Transfer:**
- Plate cylinder transfers inked image to the **blanket cylinder** (rubber-covered, 1.7 – 1.85 mm thick).
- The blanket, in turn, transfers the image to the paper as it passes through the **impression nip** with the impression cylinder.
- Impression pressure is typically 0.10 – 0.15 mm squeeze.

**Step 5 — Sequential Colour Build-up:**
- Sheet passes through Unit 1 (K), Unit 2 (C), Unit 3 (M), Unit 4 (Y).
- Between units, infra-red / hot-air dryers set the ink film so that the next colour does not pick up the previous.
- The deliverer pile receives the fully-printed sheet.

**Block Diagram (drawn in exam):**

```
[Dampening]  [Inking]
     \         /
   [Plate Cyl]
        |
   [Blanket Cyl]  <-- Image offset here
        |
   [Impression Cyl + Substrate]
        |
   [Sheet → Next Unit]
```

---

#### (b) **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
**"Explain the chemistry of the image and non-image areas of a positive-working presensitised (PS) lithographic plate. How is the image formed during exposure and development?" (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Plate Substrate:** The base is an **aluminium alloy sheet** (AA 1050 or AA 3003) of thickness 0.15 – 0.30 mm. It is degreased, electrochemically grained (to create micro-pits of 0.5 – 1.0 µm Ra), and anodically oxidised in sulphuric acid to form a porous **aluminium-oxide layer** of 1 – 4 g/m² mass. This porous layer is the actual printing surface — it is highly hydrophilic and holds fountain solution in its micro-capillaries.

**Step 2 — Photosensitive Coating:** A positive-working PS plate has a coating of a **1,2-naphthoquinone-diazide (NQD) / novolac resin** mixture, typically 1.5 – 3.0 g/m² dry weight. NQD is a photo-activatable dissolution inhibitor: in its unexposed form it renders the novolac resin insoluble in the alkaline developer.

**Step 3 — Exposure:** UV light (365 – 405 nm) breaks the N=N₂ diazo group of the NQD, producing an **indene carboxylic acid** via a Wolff rearrangement. This photoproduct is **no longer** a dissolution inhibitor. The exposed areas become soluble in the developer.

**Step 4 — Development:** The plate is processed in an alkaline developer (pH 12.5 – 13.5, sodium silicate + sodium hydroxide + surfactants). The exposed coating dissolves away, exposing the underlying anodic oxide (the non-image, hydrophilic area). The unexposed coating remains, forming the image (oleophilic) area.

**Step 5 — Post-bake / Gum Finish:** The plate is gummed with gum arabic to protect the non-image area during storage, and sometimes post-baked for long-run printing (> 100 000 impressions).

**Step 6 — Surface Chemistry Summary:**

| Region | Composition | Surface Energy | Behaviour |
|---|---|---|---|
| Image area | NQD-novolac photopolymer | Low (~ 30 mN/m) | Oleophilic, hydrophobic |
| Non-image area | Porous anodic Al₂O₃ + gum | High (~ 65 mN/m) | Hydrophilic, oleophobic |

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

#### (a) **[KTU University Exam — May 2023]**
**"Define the 'emulsification' of fountain solution in offset ink. Discuss the factors affecting it and the methods to control it within the desired range of 20 – 25 %." (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Definition:** Emulsification is the dispersion of fountain solution as fine droplets (typically 1 – 10 µm diameter) within the oil-based ink vehicle, forming a **water-in-oil (W/O) emulsion**. It is reported as:

$$
E \;[\%] = \dfrac{V_{water}}{V_{ink} + V_{water}} \times 100
$$

**Step 2 — Mechanism:** At each roller nip, the ink and fountain solution films are sheared together. The shear stress $\tau = \mu_{ink} \cdot \dot{\gamma}$ breaks the water film into droplets, which are stabilised by the surfactants in the fountain solution and by the resin in the ink.

**Step 3 — Factors Affecting Emulsification:**

| Factor | Effect on E |
|---|---|
| Ink tack (high) | **Reduces** E — high viscosity resists droplet formation |
| Ink tack (low) | **Increases** E — ink accepts water more easily |
| Fountain solution surface tension (low) | **Increases** E — easier spreading |
| Press speed (high) | **Increases** E — more shear |
| Roller temperature (high) | **Reduces** ink viscosity → **Increases** E |
| Fountain solution pH (low, < 4) | **Reduces** E — destabilises the emulsion |
| IPA content in fountain solution (high) | **Reduces** E — lowers water activity |
| Ink resin (long oil alkyd) | **Increases** E vs. short oil alkyd |

**Step 4 — Control Methods:**
1. **Formulate ink with correct resin balance:** use medium-oil alkyd + rosin-modified phenolic for sheet-fed; long-oil alkyd for heatset web.
2. **Use fountain solution with 8 – 12 % IPA and pH 4.8 – 5.2.**
3. **Maintain press roller temperature at 25 – 28 °C** with chilled water circulation.
4. **Set correct ink-water balance** using the **K control strip** (GATF): a 50 % tint should print clean and not over- or under-ink.
5. **Periodic K-emulsification test** (centrifuge, 1500 rpm, 10 min) — aim for 20 – 25 % water-in-ink at room temperature.

**Step 5 — Consequences of Deviation:**

- **E < 15 %:** scumming, background stains, plate wears.
- **E > 30 %:** tinting, set-off, slow drying, ink piling on rollers, dot gain > 25 %.

---

#### (b) **[KTU University Exam — May 2023]**
**"Derive Young's equation and apply it to predict whether a fountain solution of surface tension 40 mN/m will completely wet an aluminium-oxide non-image area for which γ_SG = 70 mN/m and γ_SL = 22 mN/m. Comment on the result." (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — State Young's Equation:**

For a sessile drop in equilibrium on a flat solid surface:

$$
\gamma_{SG} = \gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LG}\cos\theta
$$

where $\theta$ is the equilibrium contact angle, and $\gamma_{SG}, \gamma_{SL}, \gamma_{LG}$ are the solid-gas, solid-liquid, and liquid-gas interfacial tensions respectively.

**Step 2 — Derivation (Virtual-displacement argument):**
A virtual displacement of the three-phase contact line by $\mathrm{d}x$ along the solid surface changes the interfacial areas. Horizontal force balance at the contact line gives:

$$
\gamma_{SG} = \gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LG}\cos\theta
$$

which rearranges to:

$$
\cos\theta = \dfrac{\gamma_{SG} - \gamma_{SL}}{\gamma_{LG}}
$$

**Step 3 — Numerical Substitution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\cos\theta &= \dfrac{\gamma_{SG} - \gamma_{SL}}{\gamma_{LG}} \\
&= \dfrac{70 - 22}{40} \\
&= \dfrac{48}{40} \\
&= 1.20
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Physical Interpretation:**
Since $\cos\theta$ cannot exceed 1, the equation has no real solution. The system minimises its energy by setting $\theta = 0^{\circ}$ (complete wetting). The fountain solution spreads as a continuous film.

**Step 5 — Confirm via Spreading Coefficient:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= \gamma_{SG} - \gamma_{SL} - \gamma_{LG} \\
&= 70 - 22 - 40 \\
&= +8 \,\text{mN/m}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $S > 0$, **complete spreading** is thermodynamically favoured. This is the **desired behaviour** for fountain solution on the non-image area — it ensures that the non-image area is uniformly wet, the oil-based ink is rejected, and the background of the print remains clean.

**Step 6 — Engineering Comment:**
The high surface energy of anodic aluminium oxide ($\gamma_{SG} \approx 65$ – $75$ mN/m) combined with the moderate surface tension of fountain solution ($\gamma_{LG} \approx 35$ – $45$ mN/m) always gives a positive spreading coefficient. This is *why* the anodic-oxide layer is the industry standard for lithographic plate substrates. By contrast, the image-area polymer has $\gamma_{SG} \approx 28$ – $35$ mN/m, so the same fountain solution gives $S < 0$ there — hence the ink wets the image area.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks in Lithography questions:**
>
> 1. **Writing "lithography" without naming the offset principle** — every KTU answer must explicitly mention *planographic* and *oil/water immiscibility*.
> 2. **Confusing direct vs. offset lithography** — direct (stone) lithography is virtually obsolete; KTU expects "offset" unless the question specifies otherwise.
> 3. **Giving the *emulsification range* as a single number** (e.g. "25 %") — always quote the **band 20 – 25 %**.
> 4. **Forgetting to convert units in numerical problems** — e.g. mixing mm and µm. Always carry units through the derivation.
> 5. **Not stating physical interpretation after the math** — "$\cos\theta > 1$ means the liquid spreads" must always be followed by *why this is what we want in lithography*.
> 6. **Calling the blanket cylinder "the printing plate"** — it is the *offset* blanket; the plate cylinder carries the actual plate. This is a very common 1-mark deduction.
> 7. **Mixing up tack and viscosity** — tack is a *splitting* force, viscosity is a *flow* resistance. Examiners check this carefully.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Lithography (Module 1)**

- [ ] **Lithography = planographic + oil/water immiscibility.** Image and non-image areas are on the **same plane**, differentiated **chemically**.
- [ ] **Offset = ink → plate → blanket → substrate.** The blanket is the rubber-covered intermediate cylinder; the plate never touches the paper.
- [ ] **Fountain solution pH = 4.5 – 5.5; conductivity = 800 – 1800 µS/cm.** Contains water, buffer, gum arabic, IPA (8 – 12 %), surfactants.
- [ ] **Optimum emulsification = 20 – 25 % water-in-ink** (K-test, 1500 rpm, 10 min). Lower → scumming; higher → tinting/set-off.
- [ ] **Ink tack = 8 – 14** (Inkometer, 30 °C, 400 rpm, 1 min). High tack = low emulsification.
- [ ] **Standard film thicknesses:** distribution roller ~ 3 – 5 µm, form roller ~ 2 – 3 µm, plate ~ 2 – 4 µm, blanket-to-substrate transfer ~ 1 – 2 µm.
- [ ] **Plate substrate = electrochemically grained + anodised aluminium.** Anodic Al₂O₃ is the non-image, hydrophilic area; oleophilic NQD-novolac polymer is the image area.
- [ ] **Positive PS plate chemistry:** NQD photoproduct (indene carboxylic acid) becomes soluble in alkaline developer; unexposed NQD remains as image.
- [ ] **Young's Equation:** $\gamma_{SG} = \gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LG}\cos\theta$. If $\cos\theta > 1$, the liquid **completely wets** the solid (S > 0).
- [ ] **Spreading coefficient S = γ_SG − γ_SL − γ_LG.** S > 0 → complete spreading. The anodic oxide is engineered to give S > 0 for fountain solution.
- [ ] **Jurin's Law:** $h = \dfrac{2\gamma_{LG}\cos\theta}{\rho g r}$ — explains the strong capillary action in the anodic-oxide pores.
- [ ] **Dot gain (mechanical + optical) = 15 – 25 %** at 50 % screen on coated paper.
- [ ] **Drying modes:** sheet-fed = oxidation-polymerisation; coldset = absorption; heatset = evaporation (100 – 200 °C dryer).
- [ ] **The "water window"** is the narrow dampening-solution feed range giving clean print. Monitor pH, conductivity, and temperature continuously.
- [ ] **Lithography's four dominant variants:** sheet-fed (offset), heatset web, coldset web, UV/EB-cured offset.
- [ ] **Memorise the four sub-categories** under offset lithography: Conventional, UV, Waterless, and Digital (CtP/offset).
- [ ] **Always quote the standard ink film thickness (2 – 4 µm) and the standard printing speed (6 000 – 15 000 sph)** in any 7-mark question on press operation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Screen Printing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT866 - TECHNOLOGY OF PRINTING INKS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Screen Printing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Screen Printing** (also historically called *Serigraphy* or *Silk Screen Printing*) is a *stencil-based* reproduction process in which ink is forced through the open apertures of a porous fabric mesh (the "screen") onto a substrate using a flexible rubber or polyurethane blade called a **squeegee**. Unlike planographic (offset) or relief (letterpress) processes, screen printing is fundamentally a **porous stencil printing system** where image areas are represented by *open holes* in the mesh and non-image areas are represented by a *blocked (emulsion-coated) stencil layer*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Screen printing is classified under the family of **"porous" or "stencil" printing processes** (alongside mimeography), distinguished by its ability to deposit exceptionally **thick ink films** (typically **8–30 µm**, vs. 1–3 µm in offset), print on virtually *any flat or near-flat substrate* (textile, glass, ceramics, plastics, metal, paper, PCB), and tolerate highly viscous / particulate-laden inks (UV inks, metallic inks, conductive pastes).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you have a **window screen** (like the one on your kitchen window to keep mosquitoes out). Now, imagine you **paint over parts of that screen** with wax, leaving only the letters of your name *unpainted*. When you take a wide paint brush loaded with thick paint and *sweep* it across the screen, paint is pushed **only through the letters** onto a T-shirt placed below — the waxed areas block the paint. **That is exactly screen printing!**

- The **window screen** = the printing mesh (originally real silk, now polyester or stainless steel).
- The **wax blocking the non-image areas** = the photo-emulsion stencil.
- The **wide paint brush** = the squeegee.
- The **T-shirt** = the substrate.

### 1.3 Historical Evolution (KTU Context)

| Era | Material / Technique | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **~960 AD (Song Dynasty, China)** | Human hair stretched on wooden frame; stencils of paper | Earliest documented stencil printing |
| **1907** | Samuel Simon patents silk-screen process (England) | First industrial patent |
| **1940s** | Photo-emulsion stencils introduced | Image quality revolution |
| **1960s** | Nylon & polyester meshes replace silk | Durability + uniformity |
| **1970s–80s** | Stainless steel mesh, rotary screen presses | High-volume industrial use |
| **1990s–Present** | Computer-to-screen (CTS), UV inks, inkjet-emulsion imaging | Digital stencil making |

### 1.4 Classification of Screen Printing

Screen printing is sub-classified by **machine architecture** and **substrate handling**:

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Principal Architectures:**
> 1. **Flat-Bed Screen Printing** — Screen is flat, substrate moves in/out. Most common for garments, signage, electronics.
> 2. **Rotary Screen Printing** — Cylindrical screen rotates; used for high-speed textile (e.g., 100+ m/min).
> 3. **Cylinder (Stretchable Screen) Printing** — Screen wrapped on cylinder; used for bottle/glassware printing.

### 1.5 Position in the Printing Process Family

The four classical **ISO 12647 / KTU printing process categories** are:

$$P = \{ \text{Planographic},\ \text{Letterpress},\ \text{Gravure},\ \text{Stencil/Screen} \}$$

Screen is the **most versatile but slowest** of these for high-volume runs.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomy of a Screen — Structural Breakdown

A working screen comprises **six functional layers**, each engineered to a precise specification:

1. **Frame** — Aluminum or wooden rigid border holding mesh under tension.
2. **Mesh (Fabric)** — Woven threads defining the apertures.
3. **Squeegee side** — Top surface, ink is loaded here.
4. **Substrate side (printing side)** — Bottom surface, contacts the substrate.
5. **Emulsion (Stencil)** — Light-cured polymer block-out layer.
6. **Squeegee** — Flexible polyurethane/rubber blade that pushes ink.

### 2.2 Mesh Geometry — The Theoretical Foundation

A woven mesh is mathematically characterized by **four interdependent parameters**:

- **Mesh Count ($M$)** — number of threads per linear inch (or per cm).
- **Thread Diameter ($d$)** — diameter of each individual filament.
- **Mesh Opening ($w$)** — clear width between adjacent threads.
- **Mesh Thickness / Fabric Thickness ($t_f$)** — overall fabric depth.
- **Open Area ($O_A$)** — fraction of mesh that is *open* (image-bearing).
- **Thread Spacing ($p$)** — center-to-center distance between threads.

#### Core Mesh Equations

The fundamental geometric relationships are:

$$p = \frac{25.4}{M} \quad [\text{mm, when M in threads/inch}]$$

$$w = p - d$$

$$O_A = \left( \frac{w}{p} \right)^2 \times 100\%$$

$$t_f = 2 \cdot d \cdot k_{\text{crimp}}$$

where $k_{\text{crimp}}$ is a weaving-dependent correction factor (typically **1.05 – 1.20** for polyester; ~**1.0** for stainless steel).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Rule:** A **finer mesh (higher M)** ⇒ smaller $w$ ⇒ lower $O_A$ ⇒ **thinner, sharper, but slower ink deposit**. A **coarser mesh (lower M)** ⇒ larger $w$ ⇒ higher $O_A$ ⇒ **thicker, faster, but coarser deposit**. Choosing mesh count is the **#1 design decision** in screen-making.

### 2.3 Theoretical Ink Deposit (Ink Film Thickness)

The volume of ink deposited per unit area on the substrate is governed by:

$$V_d = \frac{V_{\text{aperture}}}{A} = O_A \cdot t_f \cdot \eta_t$$

where:
- $V_d$ = ink deposit volume per unit area (cm³/m²),
- $V_{\text{aperture}}$ = geometric open volume per unit area,
- $\eta_t$ = **transfer efficiency** (typically **50 – 80 %** in practice),
- $O_A \cdot t_f$ = theoretical maximum wet deposit.

The **dry ink film thickness** on the substrate is:

$$t_{\text{dry}} = \frac{V_d \cdot C_{\text{solids}}}{100}$$

where $C_{\text{solids}}$ is the **percentage solids content of the ink by volume** (typical screen ink: **40–80 %**).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why screen ink films are 5–10× thicker than offset:** The factor $\eta_t \approx 0.7$ multiplied by an intrinsically thicker mesh deposit ($t_f \approx 60$–150 µm) gives wet films of **15–50 µm** and dry films of **8–30 µm** — orders of magnitude above offset (1–3 µm).

### 2.4 Squeegee Mechanics — The Pressure–Shear System

The squeegee controls the **hydrodynamic pressure** that drives ink through the mesh:

$$P_{\text{hyd}} = \frac{F_{\text{squeegee}}}{A_{\text{contact}}} = \frac{F}{L_w \cdot L_c}$$

where:
- $F$ = applied force (N),
- $L_w$ = squeegee width (contact length),
- $L_c$ = contact width (mm).

The **shear rate** in the ink during printing is:

$$\dot{\gamma} = \frac{v_{\text{squeegee}}}{h_{\text{gap}}}$$

where $h_{\text{gap}}$ is the gap between mesh and substrate during the stroke (typically **0.3 – 1.5 mm**).

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter / Formula | Symbol / Equation | Typical Value / Unit | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Thread spacing | $p = \dfrac{25.4}{M}$ | mm | Mesh design |
| 2 | Mesh opening | $w = p - d$ | µm | Aperture size |
| 3 | Open area (%) | $O_A = \left(\dfrac{w}{p}\right)^2 \times 100$ | % | Ink flow |
| 4 | Theoretical ink volume | $V_d = O_A \cdot t_f \cdot \eta_t$ | cm³/m² | Film thickness |
| 5 | Dry film thickness | $t_{\text{dry}} = \dfrac{V_d \cdot C_{\text{solids}}}{100}$ | µm | Final print |
| 6 | Squeegee pressure | $P = \dfrac{F}{L_w \cdot L_c}$ | N/mm² | Print control |
| 7 | Shear rate | $\dot{\gamma} = \dfrac{v}{h_{\text{gap}}}$ | s⁻¹ | Ink rheology |
| 8 | Screen tension | $T$ | N/cm (15–25 typical) | Mesh stability |
| 9 | Off-contact distance | $d_{\text{oc}}$ | mm (1–3 typical) | Print snap |
| 10 | Resolution limit | $R_{\max} \approx 1.5 \cdot p$ | µm | Fine line reproduction |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall — Vertical Bars in Tables:** In the equations above, the absolute value `|x|` is **never** typed with the pipe symbol inside a markdown table — use `\vert` or `\mid` instead to prevent table-parsing errors during PDF/Markdown export.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Screen printing is the **dominant process** in:

- **Printed Electronics** — silver-paste circuits, RFID antennas, OLED displays (because conductive inks contain metallic particles too large for offset).
- **Solar Photovoltaics** — bus-bar printing on silicon wafers, front-side metallization.
- **Textile Decoration** — T-shirts, sportswear (plastisol inks).
- **Ceramic & Glass** — dishwasher-safe decals (needs 30+ µm film).
- **PCB Legend / Solder Mask** — epoxy inks on copper.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Open Area from First Principles

**Statement:** Derive the open-area fraction $O_A$ of a square plain-weave mesh in terms of mesh count $M$ and thread diameter $d$.

**Step 1 — Define the unit cell.**
A plain-weave mesh repeats every $p$ units horizontally and vertically, where:

$$p = \frac{25.4}{M} \quad [\text{mm}]$$

The unit cell is a square of side $p$ containing one thread pair (one warp + one weft crossing).

**Step 2 — Compute open width per thread pair.**
The geometric clear opening between two adjacent threads is:

$$w = p - d \quad [\text{mm}]$$

**Step 3 — Compute geometric open area per unit cell.**
Since there are two perpendicular directions (warp + weft), and each contributes one open slit of width $w$ per cell side:

$$A_{\text{open}} = w^2 \quad [\text{mm}^2]$$

**Step 4 — Compute total cell area.**

$$A_{\text{cell}} = p^2 \quad [\text{mm}^2]$$

**Step 5 — Form the open-area ratio.**

$$\begin{aligned}
O_A &= \frac{A_{\text{open}}}{A_{\text{cell}}} \\
&= \frac{w^2}{p^2} \\
&= \left(\frac{w}{p}\right)^2 \\
&= \left(\frac{p-d}{p}\right)^2 \\
&= \left(1 - \frac{d}{p}\right)^2
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Substitute $p = 25.4/M$:**

$$O_A = \left(1 - \frac{M \cdot d}{25.4}\right)^2 \times 100\%$$

This is the **canonical screen-printing open-area formula** appearing in all KTU reference materials.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board-Style)

**Problem:** A polyester screen has a mesh count of $M = 120$ threads/inch and thread diameter $d = 60$ µm. Compute: (a) thread spacing, (b) mesh opening, (c) open area, (d) theoretical wet ink deposit, (e) dry film thickness assuming $C_{\text{solids}} = 60\%$ and $\eta_t = 0.70$.

**Step (a) — Thread spacing:**

$$p = \frac{25.4 \text{ mm}}{120} = 0.2117 \text{ mm} = 211.7\ \mu\text{m}$$

**Step (b) — Mesh opening:**

$$w = p - d = 211.7 - 60 = 151.7\ \mu\text{m}$$

**Step (c) — Open area:**

$$\begin{aligned}
O_A &= \left(\frac{w}{p}\right)^2 \times 100 \\
&= \left(\frac{151.7}{211.7}\right)^2 \times 100 \\
&= (0.7166)^2 \times 100 \\
&= 51.35\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Step (d) — Theoretical wet ink deposit:**
Assume fabric thickness $t_f \approx 1.8 d = 108$ µm (typical polyester crimp factor).

$$V_d = O_A \cdot t_f \cdot \eta_t = 0.5135 \times 108 \times 0.70 \approx 38.8\ \mu\text{m}$$

**Step (e) — Dry film thickness:**

$$t_{\text{dry}} = \frac{V_d \cdot C_{\text{solids}}}{100} = \frac{38.8 \times 60}{100} = 23.3\ \mu\text{m}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** This screen deposits a wet film of ~39 µm and a dry film of ~23 µm — within the typical screen-printing range of **15–30 µm dry**. Such a film is ideal for **textile plastisol** or **ceramic-glass** inks where opacity is critical.

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Mesh Selection Decision Table

| Application | Mesh Count $M$ | Thread Dia. $d$ (µm) | Open Area (%) | Dry Film (µm) | Ink Type |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Fine-line PCB legend | 200–305 | 30–40 | 30–40 | 6–12 | Epoxy / UV |
| Standard graphics | 120–160 | 55–70 | 45–55 | 15–25 | Plastisol / solvent |
| Textile (T-shirt) | 80–110 | 80–100 | 55–65 | 25–40 | Plastisol |
| Solar bus-bar | 80–120 | 60–80 | 45–55 | 20–35 | Silver paste |
| Glass / ceramic | 60–100 | 100–150 | 50–65 | 30–60 | Ceramic frit |
| Textile fine detail | 150–200 | 40–55 | 38–48 | 10–18 | Water-based |

### 3.4 Resolution Limit Derivation

The **smallest reproducible line width** in screen printing is empirically approximated as:

$$R_{\max} \approx 1.5 \cdot p = \frac{1.5 \times 25.4}{M} = \frac{38.1}{M}\ [\text{mm}]$$

**Proof sketch:** A line must span ≥1.5 mesh pitches to retain ink continuously across apertures. If $M = 150$:

$$R_{\max} = \frac{38.1}{150} = 0.254\ \text{mm} = 254\ \mu\text{m}$$

This is why screen printing **cannot match offset's 10–20 µm resolution**.

### 3.5 Emulsion Stencil — Thickness & Resolution

The **stencil thickness** $E$ controls the ink reservoir above the mesh and partially governs the achievable resolution:

$$E_{\text{effective}} = E_{\text{emulsion}} + t_f$$

The minimum stencil thickness for a given mesh is bounded by:

$$E_{\min} \geq \frac{p - d}{2} = \frac{w}{2}$$

to ensure the emulsion can fully bridge the open aperture without slumping during exposure.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 End-to-End Screen-Printing Process Flow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Artwork Design] --> A2[Film Positive Output]
    A2 --> A3[Mesh Selection]
    A3 --> A4[Mesh Cleaning and Degreasing]
    A4 --> A5[Emulsion Coating]
    A5 --> A6[Drying in Dark]
    A6 --> A7[Exposure to UV Light through Film Positive]
    A7 --> A8[Image Areas Remain Open - Washout]
    A8 --> A9[Post-Exposure Hardening]
    A9 --> A10[Mounting on Press]
    A10 --> A11[Registration Setup]
    A11 --> A12[Squeegee and Flood Bar Adjustment]
    A12 --> A13[Test Print and Density Check]
    A13 --> A14{Print OK?}
    A14 -- Yes --> A15[Production Run]
    A14 -- No --> A12
    A15 --> A16[Drying or Curing]
    A16 --> A17[Quality Inspection]
    A17 --> A18[Finished Print]
```

### 4.2 Screen Anatomy — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TOP["Squeegee Side - Ink Reservoir"]
        SQ[Squeegee Blade]
        INK[Ink Pool]
    end
    subgraph MID["Screen Sandwich"]
        FR[Aluminium Frame]
        MSH[Polyester Mesh - tension 18-22 N per cm]
        STC[Photo-Emulsion Stencil - blocks non-image areas]
        FR --> MSH
        MSH --> STC
    end
    subgraph BOT["Substrate Side"]
        OC[Off-Contact Gap - 1 to 3 mm]
        SUB[Substrate - paper, textile, glass, PCB]
    end
    SQ --> INK
    INK --> MSH
    MSH --> OC
    OC --> SUB
    STC -.blocks ink.-> OC
```

### 4.3 Mesh Geometry — Plain Weave Unit Cell

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CELL["Unit Cell - Plain Weave"]
        direction TB
        W1[Warp Thread d]:::thread
        W2[Weft Thread d]:::thread
        O1[Open Aperture w x w]:::open
        P1[Pitch p]:::pitch
    end
    classDef thread fill:#cfd8dc,stroke:#37474f,color:#000
    classDef open fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000
    classDef pitch fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000
```

### 4.4 Squeegee Action — Flood Stroke vs Print Stroke

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FL["Flood Stroke - no pressure"]
        direction LR
        F1[Squeegee lifts] --> F2[Ink fills mesh apertures]
        F2 --> F3[Mesh recovers to rest position]
    end
    subgraph PR["Print Stroke - high pressure"]
        direction LR
        P1[Squeegee presses down] --> P2[Mesh deforms to substrate contact]
        P2 --> P3[Ink extruded through open apertures]
        P3 --> P4[Mesh snaps back - peel]
    end
```

### 4.5 Process Decision Tree — Substrate to Ink Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S{Substrate Type} --> S1[Paper or Cardboard]
    S --> S2[Textile]
    S --> S3[Glass or Ceramic]
    S --> S4[Plastic Film]
    S --> S5[PCB or Metal]
    S1 --> I1[Solvent or Water-based Ink]
    S2 --> I2[Plastisol or Water-based Ink]
    S3 --> I3[Ceramic Frit Ink - needs firing]
    S4 --> I4[UV-curable or Solvent Ink]
    S5 --> I5[Epoxy or Conductive Paste]
    I1 --> M1[Mesh 120-150]
    I2 --> M2[Mesh 80-110]
    I3 --> M3[Mesh 60-100]
    I4 --> M4[Mesh 150-200]
    I5 --> M5[Mesh 100-200]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define screen printing. Why is it classified as a porous stencil process?**

**Model Answer:**
Screen printing is a stencil-based reproduction process in which ink is forced through the open apertures of a finely woven fabric mesh (the screen) onto a substrate by means of a flexible blade called a squeegee. It is classified as a **porous stencil process** because:
- The image carrier (mesh) is *porous* — it contains thousands of microscopic apertures.
- Image and non-image areas are differentiated by a **stencil layer** that physically blocks ink from passing through non-image areas.
- Unlike offset (planographic) or letterpress (relief), the printing element is a **permeable screen**, not a flat plate or raised type.

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**List any four advantages of screen printing over offset lithography.**

**Model Answer:**
1. Ability to print on virtually **any flat substrate** — glass, ceramics, textiles, plastics, metals, PCBs.
2. **Very thick ink deposits** (8–30 µm dry) — much higher opacity and coverage than offset (1–3 µm).
3. Tolerates **highly viscous and particulate-laden inks** — metallic, UV, ceramic, conductive.
4. **Low make-ready cost** for short runs — no plates, simple stencil production.
5. Excellent outdoor durability — thick films resist UV and abrasion.
6. Suitable for **large format** (billboards, building wraps, vehicle graphics).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

#### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the **components of a screen** used in flat-bed screen printing with a neat labelled diagram. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The flat-bed screen comprises **six essential components**:

1. **Frame:** A rigid border, typically extruded **aluminium** (lightweight, warp-resistant) or hardwood, which holds the mesh under high tension. Standard sizes: 20×25 cm (small), 100×140 cm (industrial).

2. **Mesh (Fabric):** A finely woven fabric, originally silk but now predominantly **polyester** (cheaper, more durable) or **stainless steel** (high precision, long life). Characterized by mesh count (threads/cm or inch), thread diameter, and weave type (plain or twill).

3. **Emulsion (Photo-Stencil):** A **UV-sensitive polymer** (typically diazo-sensitized PVA or photopolymer) coated on the mesh. After exposure through a film positive, the unexposed areas (image) wash out, leaving open apertures; exposed areas (non-image) harden and block ink.

4. **Squeegee:** A flexible blade of **polyurethane rubber** (Shore A hardness 60–90) mounted in a wooden or aluminium holder. It pushes ink through the mesh onto the substrate.

5. **Flood Bar:** A secondary blade that floods ink across the screen between prints to keep apertures filled.

6. **Screen Holder / Press:** The mechanical structure that holds the screen in registration and controls the stroke.

**Labelled Diagram:** *(Refer to Section 4.2 Screen Anatomy — Block Architecture)*

**(b)** Discuss the **screen-making process** step-by-step, including mesh preparation, emulsion coating, exposure, and washout. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Mesh Selection & Preparation:**
- Choose mesh count based on resolution needed (e.g., 120 mesh for textile, 200+ for fine electronics).
- Cut mesh slightly oversized; stretch onto frame under tension of **18–22 N/cm** using a pneumatic stretching machine.

**Step 2 — Degreasing:**
- The stretched mesh is scrubbed with a **degreaser** (e.g., abrasive slurry + surfactant) to remove oils and lint.
- Rinsed with water, dried — improves emulsion adhesion.

**Step 3 — Emulsion Coating:**
- Photo-emulsion (diazo + PVA) is mixed per supplier ratio; degassed to remove air bubbles.
- Coated onto the mesh using a **scoop coater** — typically 1+1 or 2+2 coats (one coat each side).
- Dried in a dust-free, yellow-light environment at **40 °C for 30–60 min**.

**Step 4 — Exposure:**
- A **film positive** (black image on transparent base) is placed on the dry emulsion-coated screen.
- UV exposure unit exposes for **30–90 seconds** — typical energy **200–400 mJ/cm²**.
- Exposed emulsion **polymerizes and hardens** (becomes insoluble in water).

**Step 5 — Washout:**
- Screen is sprayed with water; **unexposed emulsion** (image areas) dissolves away, revealing open mesh apertures.
- Image becomes visible as a clear pattern.

**Step 6 — Post-Exposure:**
- A second UV exposure (without film) fully hardens the stencil, improving durability.

**Step 7 — Blockout (optional):**
- Pinholes in non-image areas are filled with screen filler/blockout emulsion.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Pitfall — Common Mistakes:**
> 1. Students often **omit mesh degreasing** in the answer — examiners deduct 1–2 marks.
> 2. Confusion between *emulsion types*: diazo (dual-cure), photopolymer (single-cure), capillary film. KTU expects at least one type named.
> 3. Skipping the **off-contact distance** concept — always state mesh is held 1–3 mm above substrate for the snap-off effect.

---

#### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**(a)** Derive the **open-area formula** for a plain-weave screen mesh. A 150-mesh polyester screen has thread diameter 48 µm. Calculate: (i) thread spacing, (ii) mesh opening, (iii) open area percentage. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Derivation:**

Consider a square plain-weave mesh unit cell of side $p$ (thread pitch) and thread diameter $d$. The geometric clear opening per side is $w = p - d$.

$$\begin{aligned}
A_{\text{cell}} &= p^2 \\
A_{\text{open}} &= w^2 = (p-d)^2 \\
O_A &= \frac{A_{\text{open}}}{A_{\text{cell}}} = \left(\frac{p-d}{p}\right)^2
\end{aligned}$$

Substituting $p = 25.4/M$:

$$\boxed{O_A = \left(1 - \frac{M \cdot d}{25.4}\right)^2 \times 100\%}$$

**Numerical Computation** for $M = 150$ threads/inch, $d = 48$ µm:

**(i) Thread spacing:**

$$p = \frac{25.4}{150} = 0.16933\ \text{mm} = 169.33\ \mu\text{m}$$

**(ii) Mesh opening:**

$$w = p - d = 169.33 - 48 = 121.33\ \mu\text{m}$$

**(iii) Open area:**

$$O_A = \left(\frac{121.33}{169.33}\right)^2 \times 100 = (0.7166)^2 \times 100 = 51.35\%$$

**(b)** Explain the **role of squeegee** in screen printing. Discuss the effect of squeegee **hardness, pressure, angle, and speed** on print quality. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Role of Squeegee:**
The squeegee is the **single most critical variable** in screen printing — it is the pressure-and-shear actuator that forces ink through the mesh apertures and onto the substrate. Without the squeegee, the mesh would simply sit on the substrate with ink pooled on top; no transfer would occur.

**Effects of Key Squeegee Parameters:**

| Parameter | Effect on Print Quality |
|---|---|
| **Hardness (Shore A)** | Soft squeegee (60–65A) → more ink deposit, less detail. Hard squeegee (80–90A) → thinner deposit, sharper edges. |
| **Pressure** | Too low → incomplete transfer, broken lines. Too high → mesh distortion, premature wear, ink pushed past stencil. Optimal: 0.3–0.8 N/cm². |
| **Angle** | Steep angle (60–75°) → less ink, sharp detail. Shallow angle (45–55°) → more ink, heavy deposit. |
| **Speed** | Slow stroke → max transfer, thick film. Fast stroke → thin film, possible skipping. Typical: 0.2–0.5 m/s. |
| **Edge profile** | Sharp edge = crisp prints. Rounded edge = heavy deposit but poor detail. |

**Practical Recommendations:**
- **Textile printing (T-shirts):** Use 70A squeegee, 60° angle, moderate pressure — thick plastisol deposit.
- **PCB legend:** Use 85A squeegee, 75° angle, high pressure — sharp, thin, precise print.
- **Glass/ceramic:** Use 75A, 65°, medium pressure — balanced.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Lose Marks in Screen-Printing Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting units** in mesh formulas — always quote $M$ in *threads/inch* OR *threads/cm* and state which.
> 2. **Mixing wet vs dry film thickness** — examiners deduct for not specifying which is asked.
> 3. **Confusing mesh count with mesh opening** — mesh count $M$ is *not* the same as opening $w$.
> 4. **Skipping the derivation** in "derive open-area" questions — KTU expects symbolic derivation before plugging numbers.
> 5. **Not naming the stencil emulsion type** — at minimum mention *diazo-sensitized PVA* or *photopolymer*.
> 6. **No mention of off-contact distance** — examiners often test if you know the screen sits 1–3 mm above the substrate during printing.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Screen Printing:**

- ✅ Screen printing = **porous stencil process**; image = open mesh, non-image = emulsion block.
- ✅ **Mesh count** $M$ is threads per inch (or cm); **mesh opening** $w = p - d$; **open area** $O_A = (w/p)^2 \times 100$.
- ✅ **Squeegee** is the pressure-shear actuator; controls ink transfer.
- ✅ Six components: **Frame, Mesh, Emulsion, Squeegee, Flood bar, Press**.
- ✅ Screen-making steps: **Stretch → Degrease → Coat emulsion → Dry → Expose → Washout → Post-expose**.
- ✅ Mesh materials: **Polyester** (most common), **Stainless steel** (precision), historical silk.
- ✅ Film thickness: **Dry 8–30 µm** — far thicker than offset's 1–3 µm.
- ✅ Applications: **Textile, PCB, glass/ceramic, signage, solar cells, printed electronics.**
- ✅ Off-contact distance: **1–3 mm**; mesh tension: **18–22 N/cm**; squeegee hardness: **60–90 Shore A**.
- ✅ Resolution limit: $R_{\max} \approx 1.5p$ — much coarser than offset/digital.
- ✅ Three architectures: **Flat-bed, Rotary, Cylindrical**.
- ✅ Photo-emulsion types: **Diazo-PVA** (most common), **Photopolymer** (capillary film), **Dual-cure**.
- ✅ Theoretical ink deposit: $V_d = O_A \cdot t_f \cdot \eta_t$ with $\eta_t \approx 0.5–0.8$.
- ✅ Dry film: $t_{\text{dry}} = V_d \cdot C_{\text{solids}} / 100$.
- ✅ KTU "must mention" terms: **Stencil, Mesh, Emulsion, Squeegee, Flood, Off-contact, Tension**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Non-Impact Printing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT866 - TECHNOLOGY OF PRINTING INKS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Non-Impact Printing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Non-Impact Printing (NIP)** is a broad family of digital imaging and reproduction technologies in which the image-carrying agent (ink, toner, or dye) is transferred from a source to a substrate **without any mechanical contact, strike, or pressure** between a printing element and the printing stock. The image is generated by controlled physical phenomena such as **electrostatic attraction, thermal vaporization, piezoelectric pressure pulses, magnetic attraction, or chemical sublimation**.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, Non-Impact Printing is positioned as the technological backbone of modern digital printing, packaging personalization, label printing, and short-run commercial work. It stands in direct contrast to **Impact Printing** (letterpress, gravure impression cylinder, hot-foil stamping, typewriter daisy wheel), where a hard element physically strikes the substrate.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Spray Can vs. Rubber Stamp" Mental Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Analogy:** Imagine you want to write your name on a wall.
> - **Impact Printing** = using a **rubber stamp**. You physically press a hard pre-formed letter onto the wall. The letter must exist as a solid object before you use it.
> - **Non-Impact Printing** = using a **spray paint can with a stencil trigger**. No solid letter touches the wall. Tiny droplets of paint are *ejected, steered, or attracted* to form the letter on the wall for the first time.
>
> In the spray-can world, you can instantly change what letter you are forming just by sending different electrical signals to the nozzle. This is why NIP is the foundation of **digital, on-demand, variable-data printing**.

### 1.3 Defining Physical & Operational Metrics

The KTU syllabus expects students to recognize the following governing performance metrics for any NIP system:

- **Resolution** — measured in **dots per inch (dpi)**; typically between **300 dpi** (office laser) and **1200 dpi** (commercial inkjet).
- **Print Speed** — pages per minute (**ppm**) or linear meters per minute (**m/min**) for web presses.
- **Drop Volume** — measured in **picolitres (pL)**; modern heads dispense drops between **1 pL** and **50 pL**.
- **Substrate Compatibility** — paper, board, film, foil, fabric, ceramic.
- **Drying / Fixing Mechanism** — heat, UV, IR, ambient, or pressure-sensitive.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Syllabus Highlight:** The 2024 Scheme explicitly emphasizes the *ink-substrate interaction* in NIP. Unlike impact inks that sit on the surface as a thick film, NIP inks must be engineered for **jetting reliability, drop break-off stability, drying kinetics, and adhesion on diverse substrates** — making ink formulation a critical knowledge area.

### 1.4 Classification of Non-Impact Printing Technologies

For structured understanding, the KTU module classifies NIP into **six principal families**:

| # | Technology | Energy Domain | Typical Resolution |
|---|------------|---------------|--------------------|
| 1 | Electrophotography (EP) | Electrostatic + Photonic | 600 – 2400 dpi |
| 2 | Inkjet (Continuous & DOD) | Kinetic / Thermal / Piezo | 300 – 1200 dpi |
| 3 | Electrography | Electrostatic | 300 – 600 dpi |
| 4 | Thermal Printing | Heat | 203 – 600 dpi |
| 5 | Dye Sublimation | Heat + Phase change | 300 – 600 dpi |
| 6 | Magnetography | Magnetic | 200 – 300 dpi |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Principle — The Universal NIP Logic Block

Every NIP system, regardless of its energy domain, obeys the same **four-stage logical chain**:

1. **Image Data Generation** — A digital raster image processor (RIP) converts vector or pixel data into a stream of binary instructions (drop-on / drop-off).
2. **Image Carrier Activation** — An energy source (laser, heat pulse, piezo voltage, electrostatic field) is applied to a fluid or solid imaging material.
3. **Selective Transfer** — Only the activated material moves toward the substrate; unactivated material stays in the reservoir.
4. **Fixation** — The transferred mark is permanently bonded to the substrate by absorption, evaporation, polymerization, or cooling.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" behind these four stages:** Each stage is a *bottleneck of accuracy*. If stage 2 fails to fire exactly when instructed, the dot is missing. If stage 3 deposits the dot in the wrong location, the image is mis-registered. If stage 4 fails, the print rubs off. KTU examiners frequently test whether students can *trace a defect back to one of these four stages*.

### 2.2 Electrophotography — The Workhorse Principle

The most commercially dominant NIP process. It uses the **photoconductivity principle**: a material (typically an organic photoconductor, OPC) is an insulator in the dark but becomes a conductor when illuminated.

**Six-Step Cycle (the "Karol" or "Kodak" cycle):**

1. **Charge** — A corona wire or charge roller deposits a uniform layer of negative charge (~ −600 V) on the OPC drum.
2. **Expose** — A modulated laser diode or LED array discharges the exposed pixels (~ −100 V remains).
3. **Develop** — Toner (charged to opposite polarity) is attracted only to the discharged (exposed) areas.
4. **Transfer** — A positive charge behind the paper pulls the toner image onto the substrate.
5. **Fuse** — Heat (~ 180 °C) and pressure melt the thermoplastic toner into the fibers.
6. **Clean** — A blade and discharge lamp reset the drum.

### 2.3 Inkjet Printing — The Versatile Contender

Inkjet is the fastest-growing NIP family because it combines **non-contact operation** with **direct digital imaging** (no intermediate carrier).

**Two Principal Architectures:**

- **Continuous Inkjet (CIJ):** A pump forces ink through a nozzle continuously; unwanted drops are deflected into a gutter using electrostatic plates. Used for **industrial coding, marking, and packaging date codes**.
- **Drop-on-Demand (DOD):** Drops are ejected only when needed. Two sub-types:
  - **Thermal Inkjet (TIJ)** — a micro-heater vaporizes a tiny ink bubble, which ejects a drop. Used in HP/Canon desktop printers.
  - **Piezoelectric Inkjet** — a voltage applied to a piezo crystal mechanically squeezes the ink chamber, ejecting a drop. Used in Epson, industrial labels, and packaging.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations. They appear in ESE numericals and derivations.**

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Engineering Use |
|---|---------|----------------|-----------------|
| 1 | $V_{drop} = \frac{\pi d^{3}}{6}$ | $d$ = nozzle orifice diameter | Drop volume from spherical assumption |
| 2 | $We = \frac{\rho v^{2} d}{\sigma}$ | $\rho$ = density, $v$ = velocity, $\sigma$ = surface tension | Weber number — predicts drop stability |
| 3 | $Oh = \frac{\mu}{\sqrt{\rho \sigma d}}$ | $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity | Ohnesorge number — jetability criterion |
| 4 | $F_e = qE$ | $q$ = drop charge, $E$ = field | Electrostatic deflecting force in CIJ |
| 5 | $q/m = \frac{\pi \varepsilon_0 E d}{3 \rho_f}$ | $\varepsilon_0$ = permittivity, $\rho_f$ = fluid density | Charge-to-mass ratio for stable CIJ deflection |
| 6 | $P_{drop} = \eta \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T \cdot \rho V_{drop} / t$ | $\eta$ = heater efficiency | Power per drop for thermal inkjet |
| 7 | $R_{dpi} = \frac{25.4}{d_{dot}}$ | $d_{dot}$ = dot diameter in mm | Print resolution conversion |
| 8 | $\lambda_{min} = \frac{h c}{E_{photon}}$ | $h$ = Planck constant, $c$ = speed of light | Minimum laser wavelength for OPC exposure |
| 9 | $E_{surface} = \frac{V^2 \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r}{2 d_{gap}}$ | $V$ = voltage, $d_{gap}$ = gap width | Surface energy holding toner to OPC |
| 10 | $t_{dwell} = \frac{L_{heater}}{v_{drum}}$ | $L$ = heater length, $v$ = drum speed | Heat pulse duration in TIJ |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

NIP is the cornerstone of industries such as:

- **Variable Data Printing** — bills, personalized packaging, QR-coded pharmaceutical cartons.
- **Industrial Coding & Marking** — every packaged beverage, every batch number on a medicine.
- **Wide-Format Graphics** — billboards, vehicle wraps, architectural drawings.
- **Functional Printing** — printed electronics, RFID antennas, solar cells, biomedical sensors.

The KTU 2024 Scheme specifically asks students to **correlate ink rheology with jetting reliability** — a hot research topic in the Indian printing ink industry.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Drop Volume from a Circular Nozzle

**Problem Statement:** A piezoelectric inkjet head has a nozzle orifice diameter of **20 µm**. Assuming the ejected drop is a perfect sphere, calculate the drop volume in picolitres.

**Given:**
- $d = 20 \, \mu m = 20 \times 10^{-6} \, m$
- Drop shape = sphere

**Step 1 — Identify the volume formula for a sphere.**

$$V_{drop} = \frac{\pi d^{3}}{6}$$

This is the standard sphere volume formula, where $d$ is the diameter. The factor of $1/6$ arises because volume is $\frac{4}{3} \pi r^{3}$, and $r = d/2$, so $r^{3} = d^{3}/8$, giving $V = \frac{4}{3} \pi \cdot \frac{d^{3}}{8} = \frac{\pi d^{3}}{6}$.

**Step 2 — Substitute the diameter value.**

$$V_{drop} = \frac{\pi \cdot (20 \times 10^{-6})^{3}}{6}$$

**Step 3 — Cube the diameter term.**

$$(20 \times 10^{-6})^{3} = 8000 \times 10^{-18} = 8 \times 10^{-15} \, m^{3}$$

This expansion uses $(a \cdot 10^{-b})^{3} = a^{3} \cdot 10^{-3b}$, with $a = 20$ and $b = 6$.

**Step 4 — Evaluate the numerical coefficient.**

$$V_{drop} = \frac{3.1416 \times 8 \times 10^{-15}}{6} = \frac{25.133 \times 10^{-15}}{6}$$

The numerator is $\pi \times 8 = 25.1327$.

**Step 5 — Final calculation.**

$$V_{drop} = 4.189 \times 10^{-15} \, m^{3}$$

**Step 6 — Convert to picolitres.**

$$1 \, pL = 10^{-12} \, L = 10^{-15} \, m^{3}$$

Therefore:

$$V_{drop} = 4.189 \times 10^{-15} \, m^{3} \times \frac{1 \, pL}{10^{-15} \, m^{3}} = 4.19 \, pL$$

> **Engineering Interpretation:** A 4 pL drop is in the sweet spot for **photo-quality inkjet printing**. Smaller drops (1–2 pL) are used for fine highlights; larger drops (10–50 pL) are used for solid fills.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Resolution from Drop Diameter

**Problem Statement:** A continuous inkjet printer ejects drops that form circular dots of diameter **85 µm** on the substrate. Calculate the print resolution in dots per inch.

**Given:**
- $d_{dot} = 85 \, \mu m = 85 \times 10^{-3} \, mm$

**Step 1 — Write the resolution formula.**

$$R_{dpi} = \frac{25.4 \, mm/inch}{d_{dot} \, (mm)}$$

The constant **25.4** is the exact conversion between millimetres and inches, defined by international standard.

**Step 2 — Substitute the dot diameter.**

$$R_{dpi} = \frac{25.4}{85 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{25.4}{0.085}$$

**Step 3 — Perform the division.**

$$R_{dpi} = 298.82 \, dpi$$

**Step 4 — Round to nearest practical value.**

$$R_{dpi} \approx 300 \, dpi$$

> **Engineering Interpretation:** **300 dpi** is the standard baseline for office-quality inkjet output. Industrial presses often exceed **600 dpi** by using smaller drops or stochastic screening.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Charge-to-Mass Ratio for CIJ Deflection

**Problem Statement:** In a continuous inkjet printer, an ink drop of diameter **50 µm** is to be deflected by an electric field of **$E = 2 \times 10^{5}$ V/m**. The ink density is **1000 kg/m³** and the relative permittivity of air is **1**. Calculate the required charge-to-mass ratio.

**Given:**
- $d = 50 \times 10^{-6} \, m$
- $E = 2 \times 10^{5} \, V/m$
- $\rho_f = 1000 \, kg/m^{3}$
- $\varepsilon_r = 1$

**Step 1 — Write the charge-to-mass ratio formula (Rayleigh limit).**

$$\frac{q}{m} = \frac{\pi \varepsilon_0 E d}{3 \rho_f}$$

This is derived from the equilibrium between surface tension and electrostatic stress on a charged droplet. The Rayleigh limit defines the maximum stable charge on a drop.

**Step 2 — Identify the physical constants.**

- $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \, F/m$ (vacuum permittivity)

**Step 3 — Substitute numerical values into the numerator.**

$$\pi \varepsilon_0 E d = 3.1416 \times (8.854 \times 10^{-12}) \times (2 \times 10^{5}) \times (50 \times 10^{-6})$$

**Step 4 — Multiply the powers of ten.**

$$3.1416 \times 8.854 \times 2 \times 50 \times 10^{-12+5-6}$$

The exponent is $-12 + 5 - 6 = -13$.

**Step 5 — Multiply the coefficients.**

$$3.1416 \times 8.854 = 27.81$$

$$27.81 \times 100 = 2781$$

$$2781 \times 10^{-13} = 2.781 \times 10^{-10}$$

**Step 6 — Divide by $3 \rho_f$.**

$$\frac{q}{m} = \frac{2.781 \times 10^{-10}}{3 \times 1000} = \frac{2.781 \times 10^{-10}}{3000} = 9.27 \times 10^{-14} \, C/kg$$

> **Engineering Interpretation:** This extremely small charge-to-mass ratio is what allows CIJ systems to deflect drops with modest voltages (typically 100–300 V across the deflection plates), enabling the high-speed, contactless marking seen on every soda bottle and pharmaceutical vial.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Heater Power for Thermal Inkjet

**Problem Statement:** A thermal inkjet nozzle ejects a **10 pL** drop in **5 µs**. The ink has a density of **1000 kg/m³**, specific heat **$C_p = 4000$ J/(kg·K)**, and the heater efficiency is **$\eta = 0.05$**. The temperature rise needed is **$\Delta T = 300$ K**. Calculate the peak power required.

**Given:**
- $V_{drop} = 10 \, pL = 10 \times 10^{-15} \, m^{3}$
- $\Delta T = 300 \, K$
- $\rho = 1000 \, kg/m^{3}$
- $C_p = 4000 \, J/(kg \cdot K)$
- $\eta = 0.05$
- $t = 5 \, \mu s = 5 \times 10^{-6} \, s$

**Step 1 — Mass of one drop.**

$$m_{drop} = \rho \times V_{drop} = 1000 \times 10 \times 10^{-15} = 10^{-11} \, kg$$

**Step 2 — Energy required to heat the drop.**

$$Q_{useful} = m_{drop} \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T = 10^{-11} \times 4000 \times 300$$

**Step 3 — Multiply coefficients.**

$$Q_{useful} = 10^{-11} \times 1.2 \times 10^{6} = 1.2 \times 10^{-5} \, J$$

**Step 4 — Account for heater inefficiency.**

$$Q_{input} = \frac{Q_{useful}}{\eta} = \frac{1.2 \times 10^{-5}}{0.05} = 2.4 \times 10^{-4} \, J$$

**Step 5 — Convert energy to power using dwell time.**

$$P = \frac{Q_{input}}{t} = \frac{2.4 \times 10^{-4}}{5 \times 10^{-6}}$$

**Step 6 — Final computation.**

$$P = 0.048 \times 10^{2} = 48 \, W$$

> **Engineering Interpretation:** A **48 W** peak pulse in a microscopic heater explains why TIJ print heads require **thermal management** and **duty-cycle limiting** — they cannot fire continuously at maximum rate without overheating.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Ink Property Requirements Across NIP Families

| Ink Property | Electrophotography (Toner) | Thermal Inkjet | Piezo Inkjet | Continuous Inkjet | Dye Sublimation |
|--------------|---------------------------|----------------|--------------|-------------------|------------------|
| **Viscosity (mPa·s)** | Solid (melt at fuse) | 2 – 5 | 5 – 15 | 2 – 10 | 1 – 5 |
| **Surface Tension (mN/m)** | N/A (solid) | 30 – 40 | 25 – 35 | 25 – 45 | 25 – 40 |
| **Conductivity (µS/cm)** | Tribo-controlled | 100 – 1000 | < 100 | 500 – 2000 | < 50 |
| **Particle Size** | 5 – 15 µm (toner) | < 0.5 µm (dye) | < 0.5 µm | < 1 µm | Molecular (dye) |
| **Drying Mechanism** | Heat + pressure fusion | Absorption | Absorption / UV | Evaporation | Sublimation |
| **Critical Ink Property** | Charge uniformity | Thermal stability | Jetting stability | Drop break-off | Sublimation purity |
