## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Question 1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]`
> **Define Non-Impact Printing. List any four major non-impact printing technologies.**

**Model Answer (for 3 marks):**

Non-Impact Printing (NIP) is a category of digital printing technologies in which the image-forming agent is transferred to the substrate without any physical contact, strike, or pressure between a printing element and the printing stock. *(1 mark for definition)*

The four major NIP technologies are: *(2 marks for listing with brief description)*

1. **Electrophotography** — uses a photoconductive drum, laser exposure, and dry toner.
2. **Inkjet Printing** — ejects liquid ink droplets through nozzles, either continuously or on demand.
3. **Thermal Printing** — uses a heated print head to activate a thermally sensitive coating or ribbon.
4. **Dye Sublimation** — uses heat to vaporize solid dyes that diffuse into a polymer receiver.

*(Acceptable alternative: Electrography, Magnetography)*

---

> **Question 2.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model QP]`
> **Distinguish between Continuous Inkjet (CIJ) and Drop-on-Demand (DOD) inkjet printing.**

**Model Answer (for 3 marks):**

| Parameter | Continuous Inkjet (CIJ) | Drop-on-Demand (DOD) |
|-----------|--------------------------|------------------------|
| Drop generation | Continuous stream from pressurized nozzle | Drops only when signal is applied |
| Drop control | Electrostatic deflection | No deflection; positioned by head motion |
| Unused drops | Deflected to gutter and recirculated | None generated |
| Typical use | Industrial coding, marking, expiry dates | Office printing, photo printing, packaging |
| Ink properties | Conductive, low viscosity, fast drying | Wider viscosity range, dye or pigment |
| Speed | Very high (up to 100 m/s drops) | Moderate (limited by drop frequency) |

*(2 marks for the table, 1 mark for any additional distinguishing point such as the role of the gutter in CIJ or the absence of recirculation in DOD)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **Question A.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP — Part B Module 1]`
> **(a)** Explain with a neat block diagram the **six-step electrophotography process** used in laser printers. State the function of each stage clearly. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** Discuss the **classification of electrophotographic toners** based on the manufacturing method, charging mechanism, and fix mechanism. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution for Question A(a) — Electrophotography Process (7 marks)

**Introduction (1 mark):**

Electrophotography, commonly called the xerographic process (from Greek *xeros* = dry, *graphos* = writing), is the dominant non-impact printing technology for office and commercial applications. It operates on the principle of **photoconductivity** — the property by which a material's electrical conductivity changes when exposed to light.

**Six-Step Process (with 1 mark each for explaining any six of the following stages, or 6 marks for all stages + 1 mark for a clean diagram):**

| Step | Name | Function |
|------|------|----------|
| 1 | **Charge** | A corona wire or charge roller applies a uniform negative electrostatic charge (~ −600 V) onto the surface of the OPC drum. |
| 2 | **Expose** | A laser diode or LED array scans across the drum, discharging the illuminated pixels to ~ −100 V, forming a **latent electrostatic image**. |
| 3 | **Develop** | Toner particles, carrying opposite polarity charge, are cascaded or brushed across the drum and are electrostatically attracted only to the discharged (image) areas, converting the latent image into a **visible toner image**. |
| 4 | **Transfer** | The paper is passed over the drum; a positive charge behind the paper pulls the toner image from the drum onto the paper. |
| 5 | **Fuse (Fix)** | The paper passes through a fuser assembly consisting of a hot roller (~180 °C) and a pressure roller. The heat melts the thermoplastic toner and the pressure bonds it into the paper fibers. |
| 6 | **Clean & Reset** | A wiper blade scrapes off any residual toner into a waste reservoir, and a discharge lamp neutralizes any residual charge, preparing the drum for the next cycle. |

**Conclusion (if needed):**
The process is cyclic and continuous, allowing high-volume printing. Modern digital presses repeat this cycle at speeds exceeding 200 ppm.

---

#### Model Solution for Question A(b) — Toner Classification (7 marks)

**1. Classification by Manufacturing Method (2 marks):**

- **Conventional (Pulverized) Toner** — Produced by melt-mixing polymers, pigments, and additives, followed by mechanical grinding and air classification. Limited to particle sizes around 8–12 µm.
- **Chemically Prepared Toner (CPT)** — Produced by emulsion aggregation, suspension polymerization, or polyester dispersion. Allows precise control of particle size (down to 4–6 µm), shape, and surface properties. Used in modern high-resolution digital presses.

**2. Classification by Charging Mechanism (2 marks):**

- **Two-Component (Developer)** — Uses a separate carrier bead (steel or ferrite core with polymer coating) and toner. Triboelectric charging occurs between toner and carrier. Stable but requires periodic replenishment.
- **Single-Component (Monocomponent)** — Toner itself has the magnetic or charging property. Either **magnetic** (contains magnetic pigment for transport) or **non-magnetic** (jumped development, used in modern printers).

**3. Classification by Fix Mechanism (2 marks):**

- **Heat-Pressure Fusing** — Standard hot roller fusing at 160–200 °C.
- **Cold Fusing / Flash Fusing** — Uses a xenon flash lamp; suitable for heat-sensitive substrates.
- **Oil-Less Fusing** — Uses special silicone oils or oil-less fuser surfaces to prevent toner offset.

**Diagrammatic representation of toner particle structure (1 mark):**

A toner particle consists of:
- **Core** — styrene-acrylate or polyester binder resin
- **Pigment** — carbon black or organic colorants
- **Charge Control Agent (CCA)** — for triboelectric stability
- **Wax / Release Agent** — for clean release from fuser
- **Surface Additives** — silica, titania for flow and charge uniformity

---

> **Question B.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model QP — Part B Module 1 — Internal Choice]`
> **(a)** With the help of a block diagram, explain the working principle of **thermal inkjet (TIJ)** printing. Derive the equation for drop volume from a nozzle. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** Compare **piezoelectric inkjet** and **thermal inkjet** technologies in terms of working principle, ink compatibility, drop size control, and lifetime. State two industrial applications of each. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution for Question B(a) — Thermal Inkjet (7 marks)

**Working Principle (4 marks):**

The thermal inkjet (TIJ) head contains a micro-fabricated silicon substrate with an array of microscopic chambers. Each chamber has three essential elements:

1. **Inlet channel** — supplies ink from a reservoir.
2. **Micro-resistor heater** — a thin-film resistive element (typically tantalum-aluminum or tungsten) approximately 30 µm wide.
3. **Nozzle orifice** — typically 10–50 µm in diameter.

**Working Cycle (per drop):**

- **Pulse phase** — A current pulse (~ 100 mA, 5 µs duration) is passed through the resistor. The temperature rises rapidly to ~ 300–400 °C in a few microseconds.
- **Nucleate boiling phase** — A thin layer of ink adjacent to the heater reaches superheat and a vapor bubble nucleates and grows explosively.
- **Ejection phase** — The expanding bubble pushes a volume of ink out of the nozzle at velocities of 5–10 m/s, forming a droplet.
- **Collapse and refill phase** — The bubble collapses as the pulse ends, creating a partial vacuum that draws fresh ink into the chamber from the reservoir.

This cycle can repeat at frequencies of 10–30 kHz per nozzle.

**Derivation of Drop Volume (3 marks):**

For a nozzle of diameter $d$, the ejected drop is approximately spherical. The volume of a sphere of diameter $d$ is:

$$V_{drop} = \frac{4}{3} \pi r^{3} = \frac{4}{3} \pi \left(\frac{d}{2}\right)^{3}$$

Substituting $r = d/2$:

$$V_{drop} = \frac{4}{3} \pi \cdot \frac{d^{3}}{8} = \frac{\pi d^{3}}{6}$$

**Numerical example:** For $d = 30 \, \mu m = 3 \times 10^{-5} \, m$:

$$V_{drop} = \frac{\pi (3 \times 10^{-5})^{3}}{6} = \frac{\pi \cdot 2.7 \times 10^{-14}}{6} = 1.41 \times 10^{-14} \, m^{3} = 14.1 \, pL$$

This is consistent with typical TIJ drop volumes.

---

#### Model Solution for Question B(b) — Comparison (7 marks)

| Parameter | Thermal Inkjet (TIJ) | Piezoelectric Inkjet |
|-----------|----------------------|-----------------------|
| **Working Principle** | Uses heat to vaporize ink and eject a drop by bubble expansion | Uses voltage applied to a piezo crystal that mechanically squeezes the ink chamber |
| **Actuation Energy** | Thermal (~ 300 °C localized) | Mechanical (piezo deformation ~ 1 µm) |
| **Ink Compatibility** | Water-based or dye-based only; pigments can clog due to thermal stress | Wide range: aqueous, solvent, UV, hot-melt, oil-based, pigmented |
| **Drop Size Control** | Limited (fixed by heater geometry and bubble dynamics) | Precise (drop volume proportional to voltage waveform) |
| **Drop Volume Range** | Typically 3–20 pL | Typically 1–500 pL (industrial heads can do pL to nL) |
| **Nozzle Lifetime** | Shorter (~ 10⁹ drops per nozzle) due to thermal fatigue | Longer (~ 10¹⁰ drops) since no thermal stress |
| **Ink Temperature Sensitivity** | Critical — wrong ink viscosity causes misfires | Less critical — viscosity window is wider |
| **Frequency** | Up to ~ 30 kHz per nozzle | Up to ~ 100 kHz per nozzle (industrial heads) |

**Industrial Applications (2 marks):**

- **TIJ applications** — Desktop printers (HP, Canon), office inkjet faxes, small-format photo printing, ticket and receipt printers.
- **Piezo applications** — Wide-format graphics (Epson, Roland), ceramic tile decoration, PCB legend printing, label printing, 3D printing of biomedical scaffolds.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in Non-Impact Printing Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Thermal Inkjet with Thermal Printing.** They are *different*. TIJ is an *inkjet* technology that uses heat to fire liquid ink drops. Thermal printing uses heat to darken a *leuco dye coating on paper*. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks if these are interchanged.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the recirculation in CIJ.** Students often describe CIJ as a "wasteful" system. The undeflected drops are *recycled to the reservoir*; this is one of CIJ's key engineering advantages.
>
> 3. **Writing "laser printer uses toner" without specifying the *charge polarity*.** Always mention that toner is charged oppositely to the latent image area. Examiners expect this precision.
>
> 4. **Mixing up "resolution" and "drop volume".** Resolution is in dpi (dots per inch) and depends on *dot spacing on the substrate*. Drop volume is in pL (picolitres) and depends on *nozzle geometry*. They are related but not identical.
>
> 5. **Skipping the derivation steps.** In 7-mark derivations, students often write only the final formula. KTU valuation expects *each algebraic step* with a one-line explanation. Skipping steps costs 2–3 marks.
>
> 6. **Ignoring ink rheology.** Modern KTU questions increasingly test whether students know that NIP inks must satisfy the **Ohnesorge number criterion** ($0.1 < Oh < 1$) for stable jetting. Do not ignore this.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist:**

- **NIP** = any printing technology that transfers image material **without physical contact** with the substrate.
- **Electrophotography** is the **most commercially deployed** NIP for office printing. Memorize the **six-step cycle**: Charge → Expose → Develop → Transfer → Fuse → Clean.
- **Inkjet** is the **fastest-growing** NIP family. Know the two main branches: **CIJ** (industrial marking) and **DOD** (office and packaging).
- **Drop volume equation** $V_{drop} = \frac{\pi d^{3}}{6}$ — universal for spherical drops.
- **Resolution equation** $R_{dpi} = \frac{25.4}{d_{dot}}$ — convert mm to dpi.
- **Weber number** $We = \frac{\rho v^{2} d}{\sigma}$ governs drop stability in flight.
- **Ohnesorge number** $Oh = \frac{\mu}{\sqrt{\rho \sigma d}}$ governs jetability; printable range is **$0.1 < Oh < 1$**.
- **Rayleigh limit** sets the maximum charge a drop can hold before disintegrating: $q_{max} = 8\pi \sqrt{\varepsilon_0 \sigma d^{3}}$.
- **Thermal inkjet** cannot use **pigmented** inks effectively due to thermal stress; **piezo inkjet** can.
- **Dye sublimation** is the only NIP technology that produces **continuous-tone photographic** images.
- **Fuser temperature** in EP is ~ **180 °C**; OPF (oil-less fusing) systems use silicone release layers.
- **OPC drum** is amorphous selenium or organic photoconductor (phthalocyanine, titanyl phthalocyanine).
- **Piezo materials** — lead zirconate titanate (PZT) is the industry standard.
- **Substrate** in NIP must be **flat, dimensionally stable, and receptive** to the ink — unlike impact printing where the substrate can be rough or uneven.
- **Ink formulation in NIP** is more demanding than in impact printing because ink must be **rheologically optimized for jetting**, not just for color and adhesion.
- **Charge-to-mass ratio** in CIJ must be tightly controlled; otherwise drops deflect erratically.
- **Drying time** in NIP is the **bottleneck** of throughput; UV-curable inks are increasingly used to eliminate this bottleneck.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Substrate Selection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT866 - TECHNOLOGY OF PRINTING INKS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Substrate Selection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1 — CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition — Substrate (KTU PEPOT866 / Module 1)**
> A **substrate** in printing technology is defined as the *receptive base material* (also termed the **stock** or **print-receiving surface**) upon which ink, varnish, or other functional coatings are deposited to form a permanent image, decoration, or functional layer. It is the foundational layer of every printed product and directly governs ink-substrate interaction, print fidelity, drying behavior, adhesion, and end-use performance.

The substrate is **not** a passive element — it is an *active participant* in the ink-transfer and ink-fixing process. Its surface chemistry, topography, porosity, polarity, and mechanical properties dictate whether a printed article will succeed or fail in the field.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 1 Highlight**
> Substrate selection is the **first design decision** in any print job. Selecting the wrong substrate is the leading cause of print defects (mottling, set-off, poor rub resistance, ink-lift, blocking, and adhesion failure). All subsequent ink formulation choices are made *with respect to* the chosen substrate.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain-English Intuition

Imagine you are **painting a portrait on a wall**:

- The **paint** → corresponds to the **ink**.
- The **brush** → corresponds to the **printing press / application system**.
- The **wall** → corresponds to the **substrate**.

If the wall is rough, chalky, damp, or peeling — even the best paint and brush cannot produce a durable portrait. The wall *governs* the final outcome.

Similarly, in printing:

- A **smooth, non-porous plastic film** demands a *solvent-based or UV-curing ink* with strong adhesion chemistry.
- A **porous newsprint** demands a *penetrative, fast-setting oil-based ink* (because absorbency is the dominant fixation mechanism).
- A **glossy coated paper** demands a *fast-drying oxidative or radiation-curing ink* (because absorbency is absent, so chemical drying must take over).

> **Mnemonic Anchor:** *"The substrate is the silent partner — it neither speaks in the press, but it controls every word on the page."*

---

## 1.3 Primary Categories of Substrates (Classification Overview)

> [!NOTE]
> The **KTU 2024 syllabus** recognizes the following substrate families for printing-ink technology:

| S.No. | Substrate Family | Typical Examples | Dominant Drying Mechanism |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Paper & Paperboard** | Newsprint, WFC, LWC, Art paper, Kraft, FBB, SBS | Absorption + Oxidation |
| 2 | **Plastic Films & Foils** | BOPP, PET, PE, PVC, PE-coated board, Aluminium foil | Evaporation / Cure |
| 3 | **Metallic Substrates** | Tinplate, Aluminium sheets, Anodised Al | Cure / Lamination |
| 4 | **Glass & Ceramics** | Bottles, Tiles, Decorative panels | High-temp cure / UV |
| 5 | **Textiles & Fabrics** | Cotton, Polyester, Non-wovens | Cure / Heat-set |
| 6 | **Specialty Substrates** | Corrugated board, Wallpaper, Labels, Wall decals | Process-dependent |

---

## 1.4 Key Properties That Define a Substrate

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A substrate is characterized by **seven primary engineering properties**. Mastery of these is mandatory for the KTU Module 1 examination.

1. **Surface Roughness / Smoothness** — measured in μm (PPS, Bendtsen, Sheffield units).
2. **Porosity & Absorbency** — measured as Cobb value (g/m²) or K&N ink absorbency.
3. **Surface Energy (γ_S)** — measured in **mN/m (dynes/cm)** — critical for wetting.
4. **Wettability (Contact Angle, θ)** — measured in degrees (°).
5. **Mechanical Properties** — tensile strength, stiffness, tear resistance.
6. **Chemical Composition / Polarity** — determines compatibility with ink binders.
7. **Dimensional Stability** — response to humidity, heat, and tension.

> **Bold Physical Constants / Standard Metrics:**
> - **Surface tension of water at 20 °C = 72.8 mN/m**
> - **Surface tension of typical offset ink oil = 30–35 mN/m**
> - **Critical surface tension of BOPP film = 29–31 mN/m**
> - **Critical surface tension of PET film = 41–43 mN/m**
> - **Ideal contact angle for ink wetting: 0° ≤ θ < 90°**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2 — DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Six Pillars of Substrate Selection

The selection of an appropriate substrate is governed by a structured six-pillar framework. Each pillar must be evaluated before ink formulation begins.

### Pillar 1 — Surface Energy & Wetting
The ink must **wet** the substrate spontaneously. Wetting is governed by the thermodynamic balance between the surface tensions of the solid, liquid ink, and the solid–ink interface.

### Pillar 2 — Porosity & Absorption
Substrates absorb ink either **internally** (paper fibres) or **externally** (coating layer). Excessive absorption causes strike-through and mottle; insufficient absorption causes set-off and slow drying.

### Pillar 3 — Surface Roughness
Rough surfaces increase the **mechanical adhesion** (interlocking) but reduce **print gloss** and dot fidelity.

### Pillar 4 — Chemical Compatibility
The substrate's polar functional groups (–OH, –COOH, –CONH–) must be chemically compatible with the ink binder (e.g., nitrocellulose, polyurethane, acrylic).

### Pillar 5 — Mechanical & Dimensional Stability
The substrate must survive press tensions, drying temperatures, and post-print converting (cutting, folding, laminating).

### Pillar 6 — End-Use Requirements
Final use — food packaging, outdoor signage, currency, magazine, label — imposes requirements of lightfastness, rub resistance, chemical resistance, and migration safety.

---

## 2.2 Adhesion Mechanisms on Substrates

> [!NOTE]
> **Four Recognized Adhesion Mechanisms (KTU Module 1):**

1. **Mechanical Interlocking** — Ink flows into substrate pores/crevices and anchors physically (dominant on paper, textiles, rough films).
2. **Chemical (Specific) Adhesion** — Covalent, ionic, or hydrogen bonding between ink binder and substrate functional groups.
3. **Thermodynamic (Wetting) Adhesion** — Driven by minimization of interfacial free energy; requires γ_S > γ_L.
4. **Electrostatic Adhesion** — Charge transfer across the ink–substrate interface (notable in polymer films).

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations are **exam-critical**. Master each one before attempting Part B questions.

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Variables & Units | Physical Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Young's Equation** | $\gamma_{SV} = \gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta$ | $\gamma$ = surface tension (mN/m), $\theta$ = contact angle (°) | Fundamental wetting balance at the three-phase contact line |
| 2 | **Work of Adhesion (Dupré)** | $W_A = \gamma_{SV} + \gamma_{LV} - \gamma_{SL}$ | All terms in mN/m | Energy gained when unit area of two interfaces form adhesive contact |
| 3 | **Work of Adhesion (Young–Dupré)** | $W_A = \gamma_{LV}(1 + \cos\theta)$ | $\gamma_{LV}$ in mN/m, $\theta$ in ° | Directly measurable form using contact angle |
| 4 | **Critical Surface Tension (Zisman)** | $\cos\theta = 1 - \dfrac{\gamma_c}{\gamma_{LV}}$ | $\gamma_c$ = critical surface tension (mN/m) | Threshold value above which complete wetting occurs |
| 5 | **Owens–Wendt (Polar–Dispersive)** | $\gamma_S = \gamma_S^d + \gamma_S^p$ | $d$ = dispersive, $p$ = polar (mN/m) | Total surface energy decomposed into components |
| 6 | **Interfacial Tension (Fowkes–geometric)** | $\gamma_{SL} = \gamma_{SV} + \gamma_{LV} - 2\sqrt{\gamma_S^d \gamma_L^d}$ | All mN/m | Interfacial tension from dispersive interactions |
| 7 | **Spreading Coefficient (S)** | $S = \gamma_{SV} - (\gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LV})$ | mN/m | Positive $S$ → spontaneous spreading |
| 8 | **Cobb Value Conversion** | $Cobb_{60} = \dfrac{(m_{wet} - m_{dry})}{A}$ | g/m² | Water absorbency in 60 s |

> **Mnemonic for wetting rule:** *"Surface energy must SURVIVE the liquid"* — i.e., $\gamma_{SV} > \gamma_{LV}$ for complete wetting.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering Practice

| Application Domain | Substrate Selection Logic |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Food Packaging (FSSAI / FDA)** | Must use migration-safe inks on PET/PP/PE with proper surface treatment (corona, flame) |
| **Newspaper Printing** | High-Cobb, high-porosity newsprint + cold-set oil ink |
| **Magazine (Heat-set)** | LWC/SC paper + heat-set ink (60–180 °C) |
| **Flexible Packaging** | Treated BOPP/PET + polyurethane-based laminating inks |
| **Metal Decoration** | Tinplate + UV/EB-cure ink + white base |
| **Label Printing** | Pressure-sensitive face stock (paper/PE/PET) + acrylic-based ink |

---

## 2.5 The "Why" Behind Each Pillar — Accessible Explanation

- **Why surface energy?** Because ink molecules must *stick* the instant they land. Low surface energy = ink beads up = poor print.
- **Why porosity?** Because absorbency fixes the ink and prevents set-off on the back of the next sheet.
- **Why roughness?** Because smooth surfaces yield sharp dots (good for halftones); rough surfaces yield diffused dots (good for coverage).
- **Why chemical compatibility?** Because acid/alkali incompatibility destroys the ink film within days.
- **Why mechanical stability?** Because web-tension failures cause breaks costing ₹ lakhs per hour.
- **Why end-use?** Because a shampoo sachet needs different ink chemistry than a wedding card.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3 — STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of Young's Equation (Thermodynamic Wetting)

We start from **first principles** — the mechanical force balance at the three-phase contact line of a sessile ink drop resting on a substrate.

### Step 1 — System Definition
Consider a small droplet of liquid ink (L) placed on a solid substrate (S) in equilibrium with its vapour (V). Three interfacial tensions act along the contact line:

- $\gamma_{SV}$ — solid–vapour interface tension (along the substrate).
- $\gamma_{SL}$ — solid–liquid interface tension (along the drop base).
- $\gamma_{LV}$ — liquid–vapour interface tension (along the drop's free surface).

### Step 2 — Virtual Displacement Principle
Imagine the contact line is displaced by an infinitesimal virtual distance $dx$ in a direction that **increases** the solid–liquid contact area. The system is in **thermodynamic equilibrium** at constant $T$, $P$, so the net change in total free energy must be zero:

$$\Delta G = \gamma_{SL} \, dA_{SL} - \gamma_{SV} \, dA_{SV} + \gamma_{LV} \, dA_{LV} = 0$$

Because the geometry is constrained, the three area changes are related by:

$$dA_{SL} = +dA \qquad dA_{SV} = -dA \qquad dA_{LV} = +dA \cdot \cos\theta$$

Substituting:

$$\gamma_{SL} \, dA - \gamma_{SV} \, dA + \gamma_{LV} \, dA \cdot \cos\theta = 0$$

### Step 3 — Division and Rearrangement

Dividing through by $dA$ (which is non-zero):

$$\gamma_{SL} - \gamma_{SV} + \gamma_{LV} \cos\theta = 0$$

### Step 4 — Final Form (Young's Equation, 1805)

$$\boxed{\;\gamma_{SV} = \gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta\;}$$

**Interpretation:**
- If $\theta = 0°$ → $\gamma_{SV} = \gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LV}$ → complete spreading.
- If $\theta = 90°$ → $\gamma_{SV} = \gamma_{SL}$ → neutral wetting.
- If $\theta > 90°$ → $\gamma_{SL} > \gamma_{SV}$ → non-wetting (ink beads up).

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Young–Dupré Equation (Work of Adhesion)

### Step 1 — Define Work of Adhesion
The **reversible work of adhesion** $W_A$ is the energy required to separate unit area of the solid–liquid interface into separate solid–vapour and liquid–vapour interfaces:

$$W_A = \gamma_{SV} + \gamma_{LV} - \gamma_{SL}$$

*(This is the Dupré equation, 1869.)*

### Step 2 — Substitute Young's Equation
Rearranging Young's equation to express $\gamma_{SL}$:

$$\gamma_{SL} = \gamma_{SV} - \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta$$

Substitute into Dupré's equation:

$$W_A = \gamma_{SV} + \gamma_{LV} - (\gamma_{SV} - \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta)$$

### Step 3 — Simplification

$$W_A = \gamma_{LV} + \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta$$

$$\boxed{\;W_A = \gamma_{LV}(1 + \cos\theta)\;}$$

**Interpretation:**
- $W_A$ is **maximum** when $\theta = 0°$ → $W_A = 2\gamma_{LV}$ (cohesive failure limit).
- $W_A = 0$ when $\theta = 180°$ (theoretical non-adhesion).
- $W_A$ in **mN/m**; typical good ink–substrate value: **45–80 mN/m**.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Examination Style)

> **Problem:** A solvent-based flexographic ink has a surface tension $\gamma_{LV} = 28.5$ mN/m at 23 °C. When dropped onto an **untreated BOPP film**, the equilibrium contact angle is **$\theta = 67°$**. Determine:
> (a) The work of adhesion $W_A$.
> (b) Whether this value is acceptable for flexographic printing.

### Part (a) — Calculation of $W_A$

Given:
- $\gamma_{LV} = 28.5$ mN/m
- $\theta = 67°$

Step 1 — Compute $\cos 67°$:

$$\cos 67° = 0.3907$$

Step 2 — Substitute into Young–Dupré:

$$W_A = 28.5 \times (1 + 0.3907)$$

$$W_A = 28.5 \times 1.3907$$

$$\boxed{W_A = 39.63 \text{ mN/m}}$$

### Part (b) — Acceptance Decision

Reference table:

| $W_A$ Range (mN/m) | Print Quality Verdict |
| :--- | :--- |
| $< 30$ | **Poor** — pinholes, ink retraction |
| 30 – 45 | **Marginal** — requires corona treatment |
| 45 – 70 | **Good** — standard flexo acceptable |
| 70 – 100 | **Excellent** — high bond strength |
| $> 100$ | Theoretical upper bound |

**Verdict:** $W_A = 39.63$ mN/m falls in the **marginal** zone. **Recommendation:** Apply corona treatment to raise $\gamma_S$ of BOPP from ~29 mN/m to ≥ 38 mN/m so that $\theta$ drops below 50° and $W_A > 45$ mN/m.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Zisman Critical Surface Tension

> **Problem:** Three test liquids of known surface tension are placed on a polymer film, and the contact angles are measured. Data:
> - Liquid A: $\gamma_L = 25.0$ mN/m, $\cos\theta = 0.52$
> - Liquid B: $\gamma_L = 35.0$ mN/m, $\cos\theta = 0.28$
> - Liquid C: $\gamma_L = 45.0$ mN/m, $\cos\theta = 0.04$
>
> Determine the **critical surface tension** $\gamma_c$ of the polymer film using Zisman's method.

### Step 1 — Apply Zisman's Empirical Equation

$$\cos\theta = 1 - \frac{\gamma_L}{\gamma_c}$$

Rearranging for $\gamma_c$:

$$\gamma_c = \frac{\gamma_L}{1 - \cos\theta}$$

### Step 2 — Compute for Each Liquid

For Liquid A:

$$\gamma_c^{(A)} = \frac{25.0}{1 - 0.52} = \frac{25.0}{0.48} = 52.08 \text{ mN/m}$$

For Liquid B:

$$\gamma_c^{(B)} = \frac{35.0}{1 - 0.28} = \frac{35.0}{0.72} = 48.61 \text{ mN/m}$$

For Liquid C:

$$\gamma_c^{(C)} = \frac{45.0}{1 - 0.04} = \frac{45.0}{0.96} = 46.88 \text{ mN/m}$$

### Step 3 — Zisman Plot Extrapolation

When $\cos\theta = 1$ (i.e., $\theta = 0°$, complete wetting), $\gamma_L = \gamma_c$. A plot of $\cos\theta$ vs. $\gamma_L$ is linear; extrapolating to $\cos\theta = 1$ yields $\gamma_c$.

Averaging the three values (as a first-order estimate):

$$\overline{\gamma_c} = \frac{52.08 + 48.61 + 46.88}{3} = 49.19 \text{ mN/m}$$

$$\boxed{\gamma_c \approx 49 \text{ mN/m}}$$

**Practical Conclusion:** Any ink with $\gamma_L < 49$ mN/m will spontaneously wet this film. Standard flexo inks at 28–35 mN/m easily satisfy this — **provided the film is clean**.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Substrate Families vs. Process Compatibility

| Substrate | Offset | Gravure | Flexo | Screen | Digital (Inkjet) |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Newsprint | ✓✓✓ | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ |
| Coated Paper (WFC, LWC) | ✓✓✓ | ✓✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓✓ |
| Uncoated Woodfree | ✓✓ | ✓ | ✓✓ | ✓ | ✓✓ |
| Folding Boxboard (FBB/SBS) | ✓✓✓ | ✓✓ | ✓✓ | ✓ | ✓ |
| BOPP Film (treated) | ✗ | ✓✓✓ | ✓✓✓ | ✓ | ✓✓ |
| PET Film | ✗ | ✓✓ | ✓✓ | ✓ | ✓✓✓ |
| PE Film | ✗ | ✓✓ | ✓✓✓ | ✗ | ✓ |
| PVC Film | ✗ | ✓✓ | ✓✓ | ✓✓ | ✓✓ |
| Aluminium Foil | ✓ | ✓✓✓ | ✓✓ | ✓ | ✓ |
| Tinplate (Metal) | ✓✓ | ✓ | ✗ | ✓✓✓ | ✓ |
| Glass | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | ✓✓✓ | ✓✓ |
| Textile (Fabric) | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | ✓✓✓ | ✓✓ |

> **Legend:** ✓✓✓ = Excellent / Industry-standard, ✓✓ = Good / Acceptable, ✓ = Limited, ✗ = Not used.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4 — STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 The Six-Pillar Substrate Selection Framework (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["START: Print Job Requirement"] --> B["Pillar 1: Identify End-Use Application"]
    B --> C["Pillar 2: Evaluate Surface Energy gamma_S"]
    C --> D{"Is gamma_S greater than gamma_L of ink?"}
    D -- No --> E["Apply Corona or Flame Treatment"]
    E --> F["Re-measure gamma_S"]
    F --> D
    D -- Yes --> G["Pillar 3: Evaluate Porosity and Cobb Value"]
    G --> H{"Does Cobb Match Process Drying?"}
    H -- No --> I["Change Substrate or Adjust Ink Viscosity"]
    I --> J["Return to Pillar 3"]
    H -- Yes --> K["Pillar 4: Evaluate Surface Roughness PPS"]
    K --> L["Pillar 5: Verify Chemical Compatibility"]
    L --> M["Pillar 6: Confirm Mechanical and Dimensional Stability"]
    M --> N["FINAL SUBSTRATE APPROVED"]
    N --> O["Proceed to Ink Formulation Stage"]
```

## 4.2 Substrate-to-Process Decision Flowchart

```mermaid
    Q2 --> R
    Q3 --> R
    Q4 --> R

    S --> T["Step 6: Bulk Production Release"]
```

## 4.3 Wetting Behaviour Schematic — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["Substrate Surface gamma_S high"] --> nodeB["Ink Drop Lands"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Young Equation Activated"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Contact Angle theta measured"]
    nodeD --> nodeE{"theta less than 90 degrees?"}
    nodeE -- Yes --> nodeF["Good Wetting — Acceptable Print"]
    nodeE -- No --> nodeG["Poor Wetting — Reject Substrate"]
    nodeG --> nodeH["Surface Treatment Required"]
    nodeH --> nodeI["Corona or Flame or Primer Coat"]
    nodeI --> nodeD
```

## 4.4 Ink–Substrate Compatibility Interaction Map

