
> **Reading Note:** The diagrams above are functional block-level representations. In professional settings, these are accompanied by SEM micrographs of substrate topography and contact-angle goniometer images, which the KTU examination may ask you to *describe* but not to *reproduce*.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 (Similar)]**
> Define the term **"substrate"** in the context of printing-ink technology. List **four** major families of substrates commonly encountered in commercial printing.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A substrate is the receptive base material or stock upon which ink is deposited to form a permanent image in any printing process. It actively participates in ink fixation through its surface chemistry, porosity, and energy.

Four major families of substrates are:

1. **Paper and paperboard** — newsprint, coated paper, folding boxboard.
2. **Plastic films and foils** — BOPP, PET, PE, PVC, aluminium foil.
3. **Metallic substrates** — tinplate, anodised aluminium sheets.
4. **Specialty substrates** — glass, textiles, corrugated board, wallpapers.

*(1 mark for definition + 0.5 each for the four families = 3 marks)*

---

### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 (Similar)]**
> State **Young's equation** and explain the significance of the **contact angle (θ)** in determining print quality.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

$$\gamma_{SV} = \gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta$$

Where $\gamma_{SV}$, $\gamma_{SL}$, and $\gamma_{LV}$ are the interfacial tensions at the solid-vapour, solid-liquid, and liquid-vapour interfaces, respectively, and $\theta$ is the equilibrium contact angle.

**Significance of θ:**

- $\theta < 90°$ → ink wets the substrate → acceptable print.
- $\theta \to 0°$ → complete spreading → excellent wetting.
- $\theta > 90°$ → ink beads up → print failure (pinholes, retraction).

A low contact angle ensures strong ink-substrate adhesion, sharp dot reproduction, and uniform gloss. The contact angle is therefore the **single most important diagnostic parameter** in substrate-ink compatibility testing. *(1 mark for equation + 2 marks for explanation = 3 marks)*

---

## PART B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Note:** Module 1 carries **14 marks** in the End-Semester Examination. Students must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION A — (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 (Expected Pattern)]**

> **(a)** Discuss in detail the **seven primary engineering properties** that govern substrate selection for a printing process. Mention the standard test methods used to quantify each. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** A water-based gravure ink has surface tension $\gamma_{LV} = 42.0$ mN/m at 25 °C. The equilibrium contact angle on a coated paper substrate is found to be **$\theta = 38°$**. Determine: (i) the work of adhesion $W_A$, (ii) the interfacial tension $\gamma_{SL}$ given $\gamma_{SV} = 48.5$ mN/m, and (iii) whether this ink is acceptable for commercial gravure printing on this stock. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION — Part A(a) — Seven Engineering Properties of Substrates

**Property 1 — Surface Roughness / Smoothness**
- Controls dot fidelity, gloss, and ink film uniformity.
- Measured by **PPS** (Parker Print-Surf) in μm, **Bendtsen** in ml/min, and **Sheffield** in Sheffield units.

**Property 2 — Porosity & Absorbency**
- Determines depth of ink penetration.
- Measured by **Cobb value** (g/m²) and **K&N ink absorbency** (% reflectance drop).

**Property 3 — Surface Energy ($\gamma_S$)**
- Governs wetting by the ink.
- Measured by **Owens-Wendt** or **Zisman** methods using test liquids.
- Standard unit: **mN/m** (equivalent to dynes/cm).

**Property 4 — Wettability / Contact Angle ($\theta$)**
- Direct indicator of wetting.
- Measured by **goniometer** in degrees.

**Property 5 — Mechanical Properties**
- Tensile strength, tear resistance, stiffness, burst strength.
- Measured by **Instron-type tensile testers**, **Elmendorf tear**, and **Taber stiffness**.

**Property 6 — Chemical Compatibility**
- Surface functional groups (–OH, –COOH, –CONH–).
- Assessed by **FTIR spectroscopy**, **XPS**, and contact-angle titration.

**Property 7 — Dimensional Stability**
- Response to humidity and heat.
- Measured by **expansion/contraction tests** under controlled RH and temperature.

*(1 mark per property explained + 0 mark for minor omissions; total 7 marks)*

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION — Part A(b) — Numerical Problem

**Given:**
- $\gamma_{LV} = 42.0$ mN/m
- $\theta = 38°$
- $\gamma_{SV} = 48.5$ mN/m

**(i) Work of Adhesion $W_A$:**

Step 1: Compute $\cos 38°$:

$$\cos 38° = 0.7880$$

Step 2: Apply Young–Dupré:

$$W_A = \gamma_{LV}(1 + \cos\theta) = 42.0 \times (1 + 0.7880)$$

$$W_A = 42.0 \times 1.7880$$

$$\boxed{W_A = 75.10 \text{ mN/m}}$$

**(ii) Interfacial Tension $\gamma_{SL}$:**

From Young's equation:

$$\gamma_{SL} = \gamma_{SV} - \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta$$

$$\gamma_{SL} = 48.5 - (42.0 \times 0.7880)$$

$$\gamma_{SL} = 48.5 - 33.10$$

$$\boxed{\gamma_{SL} = 15.40 \text{ mN/m}}$$

**(iii) Acceptance Verdict:**

| Parameter | Computed | Threshold | Verdict |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| $W_A$ | 75.10 mN/m | $\geq 45$ mN/m | **Excellent** ✓ |
| $\theta$ | 38° | $< 50°$ | **Excellent** ✓ |
| $\gamma_{SL}$ | 15.40 mN/m | $< 25$ mN/m | **Excellent** ✓ |

**Conclusion:** The ink is **highly acceptable** for commercial gravure printing on this coated paper stock. No surface treatment is required. *(3 + 2 + 2 = 7 marks distribution)*

---

### ⭐ QUESTION B — (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 (Expected Pattern)]**

> **(a)** Explain with neat sketches the concept of **contact angle and wettability**. Derive the **Young–Dupré equation** for the work of adhesion starting from Young's equation. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** A flexographic ink with $\gamma_L = 32$ mN/m is to be printed on three candidate substrates. The contact angles are: (i) Treated BOPP — $\theta = 22°$, (ii) PET film — $\theta = 48°$, (iii) Coated PE — $\theta = 71°$. Compute $W_A$ in each case and recommend the best substrate with justification. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION — Part B(a) — Concept and Derivation

**Concept of Contact Angle:**

When a liquid ink drop is placed on a solid substrate, it forms a characteristic angle with the surface at the three-phase contact line. This is the **contact angle $\theta$**, measured inside the liquid drop through the denser phase.

- $\theta < 90°$ → liquid is *wetting* (good for printing).
- $\theta = 0°$ → *complete spreading*.
- $\theta > 90°$ → *non-wetting* (ink beads up — failure).

Three interfacial tensions act at the contact line: $\gamma_{SV}$ (substrate–air), $\gamma_{SL}$ (substrate–ink), and $\gamma_{LV}$ (ink–air). Their mechanical balance gives Young's equation:

$$\gamma_{SV} = \gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta$$

**Derivation of Young–Dupré Equation:**

The **Dupré work of adhesion** $W_A$ is defined as the reversible work required to separate unit area of the SL interface into SV and LV interfaces:

$$W_A = \gamma_{SV} + \gamma_{LV} - \gamma_{SL} \quad \text{...(1)}$$

From Young's equation:

$$\gamma_{SL} = \gamma_{SV} - \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta \quad \text{...(2)}$$

Substituting (2) into (1):

$$W_A = \gamma_{SV} + \gamma_{LV} - (\gamma_{SV} - \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta)$$

$$W_A = \cancel{\gamma_{SV}} + \gamma_{LV} - \cancel{\gamma_{SV}} + \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta$$

$$\boxed{\,W_A = \gamma_{LV}(1 + \cos\theta)\,}$$

This is the **Young–Dupré equation** (valid for smooth, homogeneous, rigid, non-reactive substrates). It allows direct computation of $W_A$ from one easily measured parameter ($\theta$). *(3 marks for explanation + 4 marks for derivation = 7 marks)*

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION — Part B(b) — Numerical Selection

**Given:** $\gamma_L = 32$ mN/m for all three cases.

**Case 1 — Treated BOPP, $\theta = 22°$:**

$$\cos 22° = 0.9272$$

$$W_A^{(1)} = 32 \times (1 + 0.9272) = 32 \times 1.9272$$

$$\boxed{W_A^{(1)} = 61.67 \text{ mN/m}}$$

**Case 2 — PET Film, $\theta = 48°$:**

$$\cos 48° = 0.6691$$

$$W_A^{(2)} = 32 \times (1 + 0.6691) = 32 \times 1.6691$$

$$\boxed{W_A^{(2)} = 53.41 \text{ mN/m}}$$

**Case 3 — Coated PE, $\theta = 71°$:**

$$\cos 71° = 0.3256$$

$$W_A^{(3)} = 32 \times (1 + 0.3256) = 32 \times 1.3256$$

$$\boxed{W_A^{(3)} = 42.42 \text{ mN/m}}$$

**Comparison Table:**

| Substrate | $\theta$ | $W_A$ (mN/m) | Verdict |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| Treated BOPP | 22° | **61.67** | Excellent — best choice |
| PET Film | 48° | 53.41 | Good |
| Coated PE | 71° | 42.42 | Marginal |

**Recommendation:** **Treated BOPP** is the best substrate — it offers the highest work of adhesion (61.67 mN/m) and the lowest contact angle (22°). Both metrics are well above the flexographic-printing acceptability thresholds ($W_A \geq 45$ mN/m, $\theta < 50°$). *(2 marks per case + 1 mark for final recommendation = 7 marks)*

---

## ⛔ KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING / PITFALL CALLOUT

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Areas in Substrate Selection Questions**
>
> 1. **Unit Confusion (–2 marks typical):** Students frequently forget that $\gamma$ is in **mN/m**, not in N/m. Always state the unit explicitly.
> 2. **Contact Angle in Degrees vs. Radians (–1 to –3 marks):** Scientific calculators must be set to **DEG**, not RAD. The cosine of 67° ≠ cos of 67 radians. Triple-check before submission.
> 3. **Omitting the Polarity Condition:** For wetting to occur, $\gamma_{SV} > \gamma_{LV}$. Many students write the formula but forget to verify this prerequisite in numerical answers.
> 4. **Confusing $W_A$ with $W_C$ (Work of Cohesion):** $W_A = \gamma_{LV}(1+\cos\theta)$; $W_C = 2\gamma_{LV}$. These are different quantities and are frequently interchanged by careless students.
> 5. **Skipping the Verdict:** Even with correct numbers, students lose 1–2 marks by not stating *whether the ink is acceptable* for the given process.
> 6. **Ignoring Surface Treatment:** When $W_A$ is borderline (e.g., 39 mN/m for BOPP), the correct engineering answer must mention **corona or flame treatment** as a remediation step.
> 7. **Misclassifying Substrate Families:** In Part A definitions, students sometimes confuse "paperboard" with "plastic films" or misplace "aluminium foil" under metals instead of films. Read the question carefully.

---

## ✅ TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Substrate Selection (KTU Module 1)**

- ⭐ A **substrate** is the print-receiving base material; it is *active*, not passive.
- ⭐ Substrate **selection precedes ink formulation** in any print job design.
- ⭐ **Six primary pillars:** end-use → surface energy → porosity → roughness → chemistry → mechanical stability.
- ⭐ **Young's Equation:** $\gamma_{SV} = \gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta$ — must be memorised in symbol form.
- ⭐ **Young–Dupré:** $W_A = \gamma_{LV}(1+\cos\theta)$ — directly measurable via contact angle.
- ⭐ **Wetting condition:** $\gamma_{SV} > \gamma_{LV}$ for spontaneous ink spreading.
- ⭐ **Acceptance threshold:** $W_A \geq 45$ mN/m and $\theta < 50°$ for typical commercial printing.
- ⭐ **BOPP corona treatment** raises $\gamma_S$ from ~29 mN/m to ≥ 38 mN/m — enables flexo printing.
- ⭐ **Critical surface tension $\gamma_c$** (Zisman) = highest $\gamma_L$ for $\theta = 0°$.
- ⭐ **Owens-Wendt decomposition:** $\gamma_S = \gamma_S^d + \gamma_S^p$ — explains why polar inks prefer polar substrates.
- ⭐ **Cobb60** measures water absorbency; standard range 20–30 g/m² for coated paper.
- ⭐ **Four adhesion mechanisms:** mechanical interlocking, chemical bonding, thermodynamic wetting, electrostatic.
- ⭐ **Always state units** in numerical answers: mN/m for surface tension, ° for contact angle, g/m² for Cobb value.
- ⭐ **Always conclude with a verdict** in numerical problems — engineering judgment is part of the answer.
- ⭐ **Plastics need treatment; paper does not** — this is the single most frequent board-exam contrast question.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Print Recognition
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT866 - TECHNOLOGY OF PRINTING INKS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Print Recognition

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Print Recognition** (also termed *Print Identification* or *Print Process Identification*) is the systematic scientific discipline within Printing Technology that involves the **identification, classification, and differentiation of the printing process used to produce a given printed substrate**, by analysing the visible and microscopic characteristics of the printed image, ink layer, dot structure, and substrate interaction.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEPOT866 – Technology of Printing Inks)**, Print Recognition forms the foundational cognitive bridge between **substrate science** and **ink-substrate interaction mechanisms**. It is the procedure by which a print technologist determines whether a sample was produced via:

- **Relief Printing** (Letterpress, Flexography)
- **Planographic Printing** (Offset Lithography)
- **Intaglio Printing** (Gravure, Engraving)
- **Stencil/Screen Printing** (Silk Screen, Serigraphy)
- **Digital Printing** (Inkjet, Electrophotography, Toner-based)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** Print Recognition is the **opening cognitive anchor** of Module 1. It establishes the analytical lens through which all subsequent ink–substrate–process interrelationships are interpreted. Mastery of this topic is **prerequisite** for understanding ink formulation rationale.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine walking into a room full of cakes made by five different chefs. Even without asking, you can often tell *how* a cake was made just by looking at the **texture, frosting pattern, edge sharpness, and ingredient distribution** on top.

- A **knife-frosted cake** (sharp edges, scraped lines) → analogous to **Offset Lithography** (sharp dot edges, uniform ink film).
- A **piped cake** (raised, dimensional swirls) → analogous to **Relief/Flexo printing** (raised ink deposit).
- A **sprayed cake** (misty, soft gradient) → analogous to **Inkjet/Digital printing** (random dot dispersion).
- A **carved chocolate cake** (ink seated *inside* grooves) → analogous to **Intaglio/Gravure** (ink held in cells).
- A **stencil-decorated cake** (sharp, paint-on patterns) → analogous to **Screen printing** (thick ink deposit).

**Print Recognition is the "detective science" of the print technologist** — reading physical evidence from the printed substrate to reconstruct the manufacturing history.

## 1.3 Why Print Recognition Matters in Ink Technology

| Reason | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|
| **Ink–Process Compatibility** | Each printing process demands ink with specific rheology, viscosity, drying mechanism |
| **Quality Forensics** | Detects misprints, counterfeits, or process drift in production |
| **Substrate Verification** | Validates whether ink chemistry matches the declared process |
| **Reverse Engineering** | Reproducing or matching inks for legacy print samples |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Metric:** The **Print Contrast Ratio (PCR)** is a primary quantitative tool in print recognition, where $PCR = \frac{R_s - R_b}{R_s}$, with $R_s$ = solid reflectance and $R_b$ = background reflectance.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five-Principle Classification (The I.S.P.D. Framework)

The **ISO 12647 / KTU 2024 reference framework** classifies every printing process under one of **five physical principles**, based on how the **image carrier (plate, screen, or digital buffer)** holds or transfers ink.

### Principle I — Relief (Typographic / Flexographic)

- **Image carrier geometry:** The image areas are *raised* above the non-image areas.
- **Mechanism:** Ink is applied to the raised surfaces; pressure transfers ink to substrate.
- **Identifying Signatures:**
  - Slight **embossing/depression** on the reverse side of substrate
  - **Sharp, often square-cornered dot edges**
  - Ink deposit sits *on top* of the substrate
  - Typical ink film thickness: **2 – 8 µm**
- **Examples:** Letterpress, Flexography, Aniline printing.

### Principle II — Planographic (Offset / Lithographic)

- **Image carrier geometry:** Image and non-image areas lie in the **same plane**; differentiated by **chemical properties** (oleophilic vs. hydrophilic).
- **Mechanism:** Water/fountain solution repels ink from non-image areas; ink adheres to image areas.
- **Identifying Signatures:**
  - **Round/elliptical dot shape** (because ink spreads from the chemical boundary)
  - **Halo effect** around dots (scumming / ink-water interference)
  - Thin, uniform ink film: **0.5 – 2 µm**
  - Slight dot gain due to ink spreading on substrate
- **Examples:** Sheet-fed offset, Web offset, Waterless lithography.

### Principle III — Intaglio (Gravure / Engraved)

- **Image carrier geometry:** Image areas are *recessed* (cells, lines, pits) below the surface.
- **Mechanism:** Ink fills the recesses; doctor blade wipes excess; capillary/pressure releases ink to substrate.
- **Identifying Signatures:**
  - **Square or lozenge-shaped cells** visible under magnification (10×–50×)
  - Heavy ink deposit with **distinct relief texture**
  - Ink film thickness: **5 – 15 µm**
  - Ink often appears to be **embedded** in the substrate
- **Examples:** Rotogravure, Sheet-fed gravure, Security engraving.

### Principle IV — Stencil / Porous (Screen / Serigraphy)

- **Image carrier geometry:** Ink passes through **open mesh apertures**; non-image areas are blocked.
- **Mechanism:** Squeegee forces ink through stencil openings onto substrate.
- **Identifying Signatures:**
  - **Thickest ink deposit** of all conventional processes: **10 – 100 µm**
  - Visible **mesh pattern texture** under magnification
  - Slight surface relief; ink sits as a discrete **layer**
  - Edges may show **squeegee stroke marks**
- **Examples:** Flat-bed screen, Rotary screen, Textile printing.

### Principle V — Digital (Non-Impact / Imaging)

- **Image carrier geometry:** No physical plate — image exists as a **digital data file**.
- **Mechanism:** Ink/toner is jetted, fused, or deposited pixel-by-pixel.
- **Identifying Signatures:**
  - **Random dot placement** (no regular halftone screen)
  - Microscopic **splatter or satellite dots** (in inkjet)
  - **Toner edges may show fusing boundary** (in electrophotography)
  - Variable dot sizes from **single-pass modulation**
- **Examples:** Inkjet (continuous & drop-on-demand), Electrophotography, Dye-sublimation.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet — Print Recognition Parameters

| Parameter | Symbol / Unit | Formula / Standard | Engineering Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Print Contrast Ratio** | $PCR$ (dimensionless) | $\dfrac{R_s - R_b}{R_s}$ | Quantifies optical ink density vs. substrate |
| **Solid Optical Density** | $OD$ (log) | $OD = -\log_{10}\left(\dfrac{I_t}{I_0}\right) = -\log_{10} R$ | Measures ink coverage strength |
| **Dot Area (Geometric)** | $A_d$ (mm²) | $A_d = \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4}$ for round dots | Calculates theoretical tone value |
| **Halftone Tone Value** | $TV$ (%) | Murray–Davies: $TV = \dfrac{1 - 10^{-(D_t - D_0)}}{1 - 10^{-(D_s - D_0)}}$ | Determines % ink coverage of a halftone |
| **Yule–Nielsen (Corrected)** | $TV_{YN}$ (%) | $TV = \dfrac{1 - 10^{-(D_t - D_0)/n}}{1 - 10^{-(D_s - D_0)/n}}$ | Accounts for light scattering in paper |
| **Screen Ruling** | $L$ (lpi = lines/inch) | $L = \dfrac{1}{p}$ where $p$ = pitch | Defines halftone frequency |
| **Mechanical Dot Gain** | $\Delta M$ (%) | $\Delta M = TV_{print} - TV_{film}$ | Identifies ink spreading on substrate |
| **Ink Film Thickness** | $IFT$ (µm) | $IFT = \dfrac{m}{A \cdot \rho}$ | Critical for ink–substrate recognition |
| **Reflectance** | $R$ (dimensionless) | $R = \dfrac{I_r}{I_i}$ | Used in PCR & OD calculations |

> [!NOTE]
> **Board Examiner's Tip:** In KTU valuation, the term *"dot enlargement"* is often the deciding marker between **offset** and **flexo**. Offset dots swell on the substrate due to ink-water chemistry; flexo dots appear squeezed due to impression pressure. Both cause **mechanical dot gain**, but the geometry differs.

## 2.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Speed of light in vacuum:** $c = 3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$ (referenced for spectrophotometric calibration)
- **Standard viewing geometry for densitometry:** **45°/0°** (incident 45°, measured 0°)
- **Standard illuminant for print evaluation:** **D50** (5000 K, CIE standard)
- **Reference white in densitometry:** Barium sulfate ($BaSO_4$) with absolute reflectance ≈ 0.98

## 2.4 Why This Matters in Engineering

Print Recognition is the **diagnostic backbone** of:

1. **Forensic printing investigations** (counterfeit detection)
2. **Pre-press compatibility verification**
3. **Quality assurance in contract printing**
4. **Ink–substrate–process triangle** — the three pillars of every ink formulation decision

Without correct identification, an ink formulated for **offset** may be wrongly applied to a **flexo** press, causing catastrophic print failure.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Murray–Davies Equation (Tone Value Calculation)

The **Murray–Davies equation** is the cornerstone for determining the **percentage tone value** of a halftone print — the single most diagnostic quantitative measurement in Print Recognition.

### Step 1: Define the Concept of Tone Value

The **Tone Value ($TV$)** is defined as the ratio of the inked area on the substrate to the total geometric area of the halftone cell, expressed as a percentage.

$$TV = \frac{A_{ink}}{A_{total}} \times 100\%$$

### Step 2: Apply Beer's Law (Lambert–Beer) to the Solid Ink Layer

For a fully solid (100%) inked area, the optical density $D_s$ follows:

$$D_s = -\log_{10}(R_s) = -\log_{10}\left(\frac{I_t}{I_0}\right)$$

where $I_t$ is transmitted intensity and $I_0$ is incident intensity.

### Step 3: Express Reflectance of a Halftone

A halftone area is a **mixture** of:
- Inked area fraction ($f$) reflecting with solid reflectance $R_s$
- Un-inked area fraction ($1-f$) reflecting with paper reflectance $R_0$

Assuming **additive light mixing (no optical scattering / penetration)**:

$$R_t = f \cdot R_s + (1-f) \cdot R_0$$

### Step 4: Solve for the Tone Value Fraction $f$

Rearranging the above equation:

$$f = \frac{R_0 - R_t}{R_0 - R_s}$$

### Step 5: Convert to Density Form

Since $R = 10^{-D}$, substitute $R_0 = 10^{-D_0}$, $R_s = 10^{-D_s}$, $R_t = 10^{-D_t}$:

$$f = \frac{10^{-D_0} - 10^{-D_t}}{10^{-D_0} - 10^{-D_s}}$$

### Step 6: Final Form (Murray–Davies Equation)

$$\boxed{\;TV = \frac{1 - 10^{-(D_t - D_0)}}{1 - 10^{-(D_s - D_0)}} \times 100\%\;}$$

> **Textual interpretation:** The numerator is the *extra darkness added by the halftone over paper*; the denominator is the *maximum extra darkness achievable by a solid ink film*. The ratio yields the effective ink coverage percentage.

## 3.2 Derivation of Print Contrast Ratio (PCR)

### Step 1: Define the Components

Let:
- $R_s$ = reflectance over a solid (100%) ink patch
- $R_b$ = reflectance over the bare substrate (background)

### Step 2: Express Print Contrast as a Ratio

$$PCR = \frac{R_b - R_s}{R_b}$$

This is the *normalized difference* in reflectance between substrate and solid ink.

> **Equivalent form:** $PCR = 1 - \dfrac{R_s}{R_b}$, often preferred when $R_b$ is used as the normalizing reference.

### Step 3: Interpretation in Print Recognition

- **High PCR (≥ 0.80):** Strong ink–substrate contrast → indicates **good ink transfer & coverage**, typical of **offset, gravure**.
- **Low PCR (< 0.50):** Weak contrast → suggests **ink starvation, poor impression, or wrong ink–process match**.

## 3.3 Derivation of Mechanical Dot Gain

### Step 1: Define Film vs. Print Tone Value

- $TV_{film}$ = tone value measured on the **original film/script** (ideal)
- $TV_{print}$ = tone value measured on the **final printed substrate** (actual)

### Step 2: Apply the Difference

$$\Delta M = TV_{print} - TV_{film}$$

### Step 3: Interpretation

A **positive $\Delta M$** indicates the dots have grown — diagnostic of:

| Dot-Gain Source | Typical Magnitude | Process Indicator |
|---|---|---|
| **Optical dot gain** (light scatter inside paper) | 5–15% | All processes (substrate-dependent) |
| **Mechanical dot gain** (dot flattening on press) | 5–20% | Offset, Letterpress |
| **Edge spread / ink setting** | 3–10% | Flexo, Gravure |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examination Tip:** A dot-gain signature chart is a high-yield answer asset. A student who can list *which* gain type dominates *which* process earns easy partial credit.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Print Recognition Decision Tree (Quantitative)

| Observation | Quantitative Threshold | Most Likely Process |
|---|---|---|
| Dot shape under microscope | Square / lozenge cells, regular grid | **Gravure / Intaglio** |
| Dot shape under microscope | Round, soft-edged with halo | **Offset / Planographic** |
| Dot shape under microscope | Round, sharp, possibly squeezed | **Flexo / Relief** |
| Ink film thickness (cross-section) | 0.5 – 2 µm | **Offset** |
| Ink film thickness (cross-section) | 2 – 8 µm | **Flexo / Letterpress** |
| Ink film thickness (cross-section) | 5 – 15 µm | **Gravure** |
| Ink film thickness (cross-section) | 10 – 100 µm | **Screen** |
| Dot placement pattern | Regular grid (screen ruling) | **Conventional (Offset/Gravure/Flexo)** |
| Dot placement pattern | Random (no grid) | **Digital (Inkjet/Electrophotography)** |
| PCR value | ≥ 0.85 | Offset, Gravure (high ink density) |
| PCR value | 0.60 – 0.80 | Flexo, Screen |
| PCR value | ≤ 0.60 | Low-coverage digital or starved press |

## 3.5 Numerical Example (Model Calculation)

> **Problem:** A printed sample has the following densitometer readings:
> - Background density: $D_0 = 0.08$
> - Solid ink density: $D_s = 1.42$
> - Halftone density (50% screen): $D_t = 0.62$
>
> **Find:** (a) Tone Value using Murray–Davies, (b) Mechanical dot gain if the original film tone was 50%.

### Solution (a):

$$TV = \frac{1 - 10^{-(D_t - D_0)}}{1 - 10^{-(D_s - D_0)}} \times 100\%$$

$$TV = \frac{1 - 10^{-(0.62 - 0.08)}}{1 - 10^{-(1.42 - 0.08)}} \times 100\%$$

$$TV = \frac{1 - 10^{-0.54}}{1 - 10^{-1.34}} \times 100\%$$

$$TV = \frac{1 - 0.2884}{1 - 0.0457} \times 100\%$$

$$TV = \frac{0.7116}{0.9543} \times 100\%$$

$$TV \approx 74.57\%$$

### Solution (b):

$$\Delta M = TV_{print} - TV_{film} = 74.57\% - 50\% = +24.57\%$$

> **Diagnostic interpretation:** A 24.57% dot gain is **high**, suggesting either a **soft substrate, excessive impression pressure, or an offset/letterpress process** where mechanical flattening dominates.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Recognition Flowchart (Mermaid Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Print Sample Received] --> B{Visual Naked-Eye Inspection}
    B --> C{Substrate Type Identified}
    C --> D{Paper or Film?}
    D --> E{Examine under 10x-50x Microscope}
    E --> F{Dot Shape Pattern}
    F --> G[Round Dot, Soft Edges, Halo]
    F --> H[Square or Lozenge Cells, Regular Grid]
    F --> I[Round Dot, Sharp Edges, Embossed Reverse]
    F --> J[No Regular Grid, Random Placement]
    F --> K[Mesh Texture, Thick Deposit]
    G --> G1[Offset Lithography]
    G1 --> G2[Planographic: Ink Water Chemistry]
    H --> H1[Gravure Intaglio]
    H1 --> H2[Ink in Cells, Doctor Blade Wipe]
    I --> I1[Flexography or Letterpress]
    I1 --> I2[Relief: Raised Image Carrier]
    J --> J1[Digital Inkjet or Electrophotography]
    J1 --> J2[Non-Impact: Pixel by Pixel]
    K --> K1[Screen Serigraphy]
    K1 --> K2[Stencil: Mesh Aperture Transfer]
    G2 --> L[Quantitative Verification]
    H2 --> L
    I2 --> L
    J2 --> L
    K2 --> L
    L --> M{Measure Ink Film Thickness}
    L --> N{Measure PCR and OD}
    L --> O{Measure Tone Value Murray Davies}
    M --> P[Cross Section Microscopy]
    N --> Q[Densitometer Reading]
    O --> R[Densitometer Curve Analysis]
    P --> S[Confirm Process Match]
    Q --> S
    R --> S
    S --> T[Final Print Process Identified]
```

## 4.2 Ink Film Thickness Comparison Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Offset Lithography] --> A1[IFT: 0.5 to 2 micrometer]
    B[Flexo Relief] --> B1[IFT: 2 to 8 micrometer]
    C[Gravure Intaglio] --> C1[IFT: 5 to 15 micrometer]
    D[Screen Stencil] --> D1[IFT: 10 to 100 micrometer]
    E[Digital Inkjet] --> E1[IFT: 1 to 10 micrometer variable]
    F[Electrophotography] --> F1[IFT: 5 to 20 micrometer toner]
    A1 --> X[Print Recognition IFT Master Scale]
    B1 --> X
    C1 --> X
    D1 --> X
    E1 --> X
    F1 --> X
```

## 4.3 Dot Geometry Recognition Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Halftone Dot Under Microscope] --> Q1{Is dot grid regular or random?}
    Q1 -->|Regular grid| Q2{Dot edge character?}
    Q1 -->|Random scatter| Q3[Digital Process Suspected]
    Q2 -->|Sharp square| R1[Gravure Cell Architecture]
    Q2 -->|Round with halo| R2[Offset Planographic]
    Q2 -->|Round sharp squeezed| R3[Flexo Relief]
    Q3 --> Q4{Satellite dots visible?}
    Q4 -->|Yes| R4[Inkjet Continuous or DOD]
    Q4 -->|No fused boundary| R5[Electrophotography Toner]
```

## 4.4 Dot Gain Diagnostic Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Measure TV on Film] --> B[Measure TV on Print]
    B --> C{Compute Delta M}
    C -->|Delta M less than 10 percent| D[Low Gain: Good Control]
    C -->|Delta M 10 to 20 percent| E[Moderate: Optical plus Mechanical]
    C -->|Delta M greater than 20 percent| F[High: Offset Soft Substrate]
    D --> G[Process Stable]
    E --> H[Investigate Substrate]
    F --> I[Likely Offset or Letterpress]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]`

**Q: Define Print Recognition. List the five major printing principles used as the basis of process identification.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Print Recognition is the systematic identification of the printing process used to produce a printed substrate, based on the analysis of visual features, microscopic dot geometry, ink film thickness, and optical characteristics.

**Five Principles (2 marks):**

1. **Relief** — image raised; e.g., Flexography, Letterpress.
2. **Planographic** — image and non-image on same plane; e.g., Offset.
3. **Intaglio** — image recessed; e.g., Gravure.
4. **Stencil/Porous** — image forced through mesh; e.g., Screen.
5. **Digital/Non-impact** — image from data file; e.g., Inkjet, Electrophotography.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`

**Q: What is Print Contrast Ratio (PCR)? Mention its significance in Print Recognition.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Print Contrast Ratio is the normalized difference between the reflectance of the substrate (background) and the reflectance over the solid ink patch, given by:

$$PCR = \frac{R_b - R_s}{R_b}$$

where $R_b$ = background reflectance and $R_s$ = solid ink reflectance.

**Significance (1.5 marks):**
- PCR quantifies the optical strength of ink coverage.
- A high PCR (≥ 0.80) indicates good ink transfer and full coverage.
- Low PCR values help identify poorly calibrated presses or process mismatches.
- It serves as a numerical fingerprint of the ink–substrate–process combination during print recognition.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** and typically contains two sub-parts of **7 marks each**.

### Question A `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Q: With the help of neat sketches, explain the characteristic dot patterns and ink film thickness ranges that distinguish Offset, Flexo, Gravure, and Screen printing processes.**

**Model Answer:**

**Offset (1.75 marks):**
- Dot shape: **round, soft-edged**, with characteristic **halo** due to ink-water interface.
- Ink film thickness: **0.5 – 2 µm**.
- Dot arrangement: regular halftone grid, dots may swell by 15–25% on press.

**Flexo (1.75 marks):**
- Dot shape: **round but sharp-edged**, often appearing slightly **squeezed** due to impression pressure.
- Ink film thickness: **2 – 8 µm**.
- May show **embossing** on reverse of substrate if thin stock is used.

**Gravure (1.75 marks):**
- Dot shape: **square or lozenge cells**, etched or engraved into cylinder.
- Ink film thickness: **5 – 15 µm**.
- Heavy ink deposit; cells are visible at 10×–50× magnification.

**Screen (1.75 marks):**
- Dot shape: irregular, with visible **mesh-pattern texture** superimposed.
- Ink film thickness: **10 – 100 µm** (thickest of all conventional processes).
- Surface relief is detectable by touch; edges may show squeegee marks.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Q: Derive the Murray–Davies equation. State its limitation and explain how Yule–Nielsen equation addresses it.**

**Model Answer:**

**Murray–Davies Derivation (5 marks):**
- Start with Beer–Lambert law: $D = -\log_{10}R$.
- Define tone value $f$ as area fraction of ink: $R_t = f R_s + (1-f) R_0$.
- Rearrange: $f = (R_0 - R_t) / (R_0 - R_s)$.
- Convert to densities using $R = 10^{-D}$:

$$f = \frac{10^{-D_0} - 10^{-D_t}}{10^{-D_0} - 10^{-D_s}}$$

- Final form (in %):

$$TV = \frac{1 - 10^{-(D_t - D_0)}}{1 - 10^{-(D_s - D_0)}} \times 100\%$$

**Limitation (1 mark):** The Murray–Davies equation assumes no **light penetration or scattering** into the paper — valid only for perfectly opaque substrates.

**Yule–Nielsen Modification (1 mark):** The Yule–Nielsen equation introduces the **correction factor $n$** (typically $1.0$ to $2.0$) to account for internal light scattering within the paper substrate:

$$TV_{YN} = \frac{1 - 10^{-(D_t - D_0)/n}}{1 - 10^{-(D_s - D_0)/n}} \times 100\%$$

This correction is essential for **uncoated and porous substrates** commonly used in offset printing.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` — Alternative Choice

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Q: Explain in detail the systematic procedure to identify the printing process of an unknown printed sample. Include the role of microscopy, densitometry, and ink film thickness measurement.**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 – Visual Inspection (1 mark):** Examine substrate type (coated/uncoated, paper/film), print appearance, embossing, surface texture, and gloss.

**Step 2 – Microscopy (2 marks):** Use 10×–50× magnification to observe:
- Dot shape (round, square, lozenge)
- Dot arrangement (grid vs. random)
- Edge sharpness
- Mesh texture (for screen prints)

**Step 3 – Densitometry (2 marks):** Measure $D_0$, $D_s$, and $D_t$ using a reflection densitometer at standard 45°/0° geometry. Compute:
- Optical Density (OD)
- Print Contrast Ratio (PCR)
- Tone Value (Murray–Davies)

**Step 4 – Ink Film Thickness (1 mark):** Cross-sectional microscopy or gravimetric method gives $IFT = m / (A \rho)$, where $m$ = mass of ink, $A$ = area, $\rho$ = ink density.

**Step 5 – Cross-Referencing (1 mark):** Match all observations against the IFT master scale and dot geometry matrix to confirm the printing process.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Q: Discuss the Print Contrast Ratio (PCR) and Mechanical Dot Gain as quantitative tools in Print Recognition. A printed sample shows $R_b = 0.85$, $R_s = 0.12$, and the halftone density readings are $D_0 = 0.07$, $D_s = 1.40$, $D_t = 0.55$ for a 40% screen. Calculate (i) PCR, (ii) Tone Value, (iii) Mechanical Dot Gain.**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) PCR (2 marks):**

$$PCR = \frac{R_b - R_s}{R_b} = \frac{0.85 - 0.12}{0.85} = \frac{0.73}{0.85} \approx 0.859$$

**(ii) Tone Value (3 marks):**

$$TV = \frac{1 - 10^{-(D_t - D_0)}}{1 - 10^{-(D_s - D_0)}} \times 100\%$$

$$TV = \frac{1 - 10^{-(0.55 - 0.07)}}{1 - 10^{-(1.40 - 0.07)}} \times 100\%$$

$$TV = \frac{1 - 10^{-0.48}}{1 - 10^{-1.33}} \times 100\%$$

$$TV = \frac{1 - 0.3311}{1 - 0.0468} \times 100\%$$

$$TV = \frac{0.6689}{0.9532} \times 100\% \approx 70.18\%$$

**(iii) Mechanical Dot Gain (2 marks):**

$$\Delta M = TV_{print} - TV_{film} = 70.18\% - 40\% = +30.18\%$$

> **Diagnostic Comment:** A PCR of 0.86 confirms good ink transfer. A dot gain of 30.18% is **very high**, strongly suggesting **offset lithography on soft/uncoated paper** — the dots are mechanically flattening and optically spreading.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Print Recognition Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Optical Density with Reflectance** — students often write $D = R$ instead of $D = -\log_{10}(R)$. Examiner deducts **1 mark** per occurrence.
> 2. **Wrong units for tone value** — must express as **percentage (%)**, not as a decimal. A TV of 0.70 should be written as **70%**, not **0.70**.
> 3. **Forgetting the substrate in Murray–Davies** — using $D_t$ alone without subtracting $D_0$ gives wrong results.
> 4. **Mislabelling processes** — students often call all offset prints "litho" without distinguishing **web offset** vs. **sheet-fed**. Examiner expects **principle-based classification**, not trade names.
> 5. **No cross-section reference for IFT** — stating IFT ranges without linking to a **measurement method** (gravimetric or cross-sectional) loses **1 mark** under KTU's *method-justification* rubric.
> 6. **Ignoring random-dot patterns of digital prints** — students often force digital prints into halftone categories; this is a **conceptual error**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Module 1 — Print Recognition)**

- ✅ **Print Recognition** = identifying the printing process from the printed substrate using visual, microscopic, and densitometric evidence.
- ✅ **Five principles:** Relief (Flexo), Planographic (Offset), Intaglio (Gravure), Stencil (Screen), Digital (Inkjet/Electrophotography).
- ✅ **Key diagnostic features:**
  - Dot shape (round / square / lozenge / random)
  - Dot edge (sharp / haloed / squeezed)
  - Ink film thickness (0.5 µm to 100 µm depending on process)
  - Surface texture (mesh, embossing, gloss)
- ✅ **Murray–Davies equation:**

$$TV = \frac{1 - 10^{-(D_t - D_0)}}{1 - 10^{-(D_s - D_0)}} \times 100\%$$

- ✅ **Yule–Nielsen correction:** introduces exponent $n$ for substrate scattering — essential for uncoated paper.
- ✅ **Print Contrast Ratio:**

$$PCR = \frac{R_b - R_s}{R_b} \quad \text{(range: 0 to 1, ideal} \geq 0.80)$$

- ✅ **Mechanical Dot Gain:**

$$\Delta M = TV_{print} - TV_{film} \quad \text{(positive = dots grew)}$$

- ✅ **Standard measurement geometry:** 45°/0°, illuminant **D50**.
- ✅ **IFT master scale (must memorize):**
  - Offset: 0.5–2 µm
  - Flexo: 2–8 µm
  - Gravure: 5–15 µm
  - Screen: 10–100 µm
  - Digital inkjet: 1–10 µm (variable)
- ✅ **Digital print signature:** random (non-grid) dot placement; possible satellite dots in inkjet; fused boundaries in electrophotography.
- ✅ **Always pair a qualitative observation with a quantitative verification** — this is the KTU examiner's expectation for full marks.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Mnemonic — "R-P-I-S-D":** **R**elief, **P**lanographic, **I**ntaglio, **S**tencil, **D**igital — the five print principles, in increasing order of typical ink film thickness.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Raw Materials for Printing Inks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT866 - TECHNOLOGY OF PRINTING INKS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Raw Materials for Printing Inks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the discipline of **Printing Ink Technology**, the term **"Raw Materials"** refers to the complete spectrum of chemically and functionally distinct chemical substances that, when combined in precisely formulated proportions, yield a usable printing ink capable of transferring a controlled image onto a substrate (paper, board, plastic, metal, or foil) via any of the conventional or modern print reproduction processes.

According to KTU 2024 Scheme (PEPOT866) Module 2 nomenclature, the raw materials of printing inks are systematically classified into **three principal functional families**:

| Functional Family | Functional Role in the Ink Film |
|---|---|
| **Colorants** | Impart hue, chroma, tinting strength, and opacity/transparency |
| **Vehicles (Binders/Carriers)** | Bind pigment, confer adhesion, gloss, flexibility, drying, and rheology |
| **Additives** | Modify specific performance attributes (drying, slip, dispersion, defoaming) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU examiner's expectation is that students must clearly distinguish between a **pigment** (insoluble, particulate colorant) and a **dye** (soluble molecular colorant). This distinction alone accounts for ~3 marks in every board examination on this topic.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a painting artist's palette:

- The **pigments** (color powders) are like the artist's colored powders — they sit on top of the canvas and create the visual impression.
- The **medium/oil** (linseed oil, gum, or acrylic binder) is like the carrier — it is the liquid that lets the artist spread the powder evenly and makes it stick permanently to the canvas after drying.
- The **additives** are like the artist's small "secret ingredients" — a drop of retarder to slow drying, a dab of matting agent to kill gloss, or a drop of drier to speed curing.

A printing ink is essentially an **engineered industrial version** of this palette, formulated to behave predictably on a high-speed press running at hundreds of meters per minute.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway:** A printing ink is NOT just a colored liquid. It is a **heterogeneous, multi-phase colloidal dispersion** — solid pigment particles suspended in a liquid/resinous continuous phase, often with surfactants and auxiliary agents stabilizing the system.

## 1.3 The "Big Picture" Classification Map

```
RAW MATERIALS FOR PRINTING INKS
        │
        ├── 1. COLORANTS (10–25% by mass)
        │       ├── Pigments (Organic / Inorganic)
        │       └── Dyes (Acid / Basic / Direct / Solvent)
        │
        ├── 2. VEHICLES (50–85% by mass)
        │       ├── Oils (Drying / Semi-drying / Non-drying)
        │       ├── Resins (Natural / Modified / Synthetic)
        │       └── Solvents (Aqueous / Non-aqueous)
        │
        └── 3. ADDITIVES (0.1–5% by mass)
                ├── Driers / Catalysts
                ├── Waxes / Slip agents
                ├── Plasticizers
                ├── Surfactants / Dispersants
                ├── Antifoams / Defoamers
                └── Antioxidants / UV Stabilizers
```
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Functional Pillars — Detailed Breakdown

### 🔹 Pillar 1: COLORANTS (The "Identity" of the Ink)

A **colorant** is any substance that absorbs selective wavelengths of visible light (380–750 nm) and reflects/transmits the remainder, producing the visual sensation of color. Colorants are subdivided into:

#### A. PIGMENTS (Insoluble Particulate Colorants)
Pigments do **not dissolve** in the vehicle; they exist as discrete crystalline particles (typically **0.01–10 µm**) dispersed mechanically.

**I. Inorganic Pigments**
- *Examples:* Titanium Dioxide (TiO₂ — white), Carbon Black (black), Iron Oxide (red/yellow/brown), Chrome Yellow (PbCrO₄), Ultramarine Blue, Chromium Oxide Green.
- *Characteristics:* Excellent opacity, superior lightfastness, high chemical resistance, lower tinting strength, **denser specific gravity (typically 3.5–5.0 g/cm³)**.

**II. Organic Pigments**
- *Examples:* Azo pigments (Hansa Yellow, Lithol Rubine), Phthalocyanine Blue/Green, Quinacridone (magenta/violet), Carbazole Violet, Benzimidazolone, Dioxazine.
- *Characteristics:* Higher chroma, transparency (preferred for process printing), **lower specific gravity (~1.4–1.8 g/cm³)**, broader color gamut.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pigment Sub-Categorization by Chemical Class (KTU Favorite):**
> - **Azo pigments** — largest family, contain the –N=N– chromophore.
> - **Phthalocyanines** — tetra-aza macrocyclic Cu/Ni complexes; benchmark for blue/green.
> - **Polycyclic pigments** — quinacridones, perylenes, isoindolinones; high-performance.

#### B. DYES (Soluble Molecular Colorants)
Dyes dissolve completely in the vehicle to form a true molecular solution. They are predominantly used in **flexographic** and **gravure** inks where the printing plate demands very low-viscosity, highly fluid inks.

- *Examples:* Methyl Violet, Rhodamine B, Victoria Blue, Auramine O.
- *Drawbacks:* Poor lightfastness, inferior bleed resistance, no opacity.

### 🔹 Pillar 2: VEHICLES (The "Backbone" of the Ink)

The vehicle is the **continuous phase** that transports the pigment, adheres it to the substrate, and imparts flow, drying, gloss, and film properties. Vehicles are blends of **oils, resins, and solvents** in carefully engineered ratios.

#### A. OILS
Oils provide film-forming capability through oxidative polymerization.

| Oil Type | Iodine Value | Drying Mechanism | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Drying oils** (Linseed, Tung, Dehydrated Castor) | > 140 | Oxidative polymerization | Offset (sheet-fed), letterpress |
| **Semi-drying oils** (Soya, Sunflower) | 100–140 | Slow oxidation | Heat-set, news ink |
| **Non-drying oils** (Mineral oil, Castor) | < 100 | No polymerization (evaporation) | News ink, solvent inks |

> [!NOTE]
> **Iodine Value (IV)** is the mass of iodine (in grams) absorbed by 100 g of oil/fat. Higher IV = greater unsaturation = faster oxidative drying.

#### B. RESINS
Resins are the "**structural glue**" of the ink film — they determine hardness, gloss, adhesion, solvent release, and rub resistance.

- **Natural Resins:** Rosin (gum rosin, wood rosin, tall oil rosin), Shellac, Copal, Damar.
- **Modified Natural Resins:** Rosin esters (Pentaerythritol ester, Glycerol ester), Maleic-modified rosin, Phenolic-modified rosin.
- **Synthetic Resins:** Phenolic resins, Alkyd resins, Acrylic resins, Polyamide resins, Polyurethane resins, Ketone resins, Maleic resins, Vinyl resins (PVC/Polyvinyl acetate copolymer).

#### C. SOLVENTS
Solvents dissolve/resinize the resin system and adjust viscosity/drying rate.

- **Aqueous:** Water, water-glycol blends.
- **Non-aqueous:** Aliphatic hydrocarbons (mineral spirits, heptane), Aromatic hydrocarbons (toluene, xylene), Alcohols (ethanol, IPA), Esters (ethyl acetate), Ketones (MEK, MIBK).

### 🔹 Pillar 3: ADDITIVES (The "Performance Tuners")

| Additive | Function | Typical Loading |
|---|---|---|
| **Driers (Siccatives)** | Catalyze oxidative drying (Co, Mn, Pb naphthenates/octates) | 0.5–3% |
| **Waxes** (PE, PTFE, Carnauba) | Slip, rub/abrasion resistance | 1–3% |
| **Plasticizers** (DOP, DOA) | Flexibility of dry film | 1–5% |
| **Surfactants/Dispersants** | Wet pigment, prevent flocculation | 0.5–2% |
| **Antifoams** (silicone, mineral oil based) | Suppress foam in aqueous systems | 0.1–0.5% |
| **Antioxidants** | Inhibit skinning in can | 0.1–1% |
| **UV Stabilizers / HALS** | Prevent photodegradation | 0.5–2% |
| **pH Stabilizers (amines)** | Maintain alkaline pH in water-based inks | 0.2–1% |

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Parameter / Formula | Expression | Units | Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Pigment Volume Concentration (PVC)** | $PVC = \dfrac{V_{pigment}}{V_{pigment} + V_{vehicle}} \times 100$ | % | Determines gloss/matte; critical PVC is when vehicle just fills pigment voids |
| **Oil Absorption (OA)** | $OA = \dfrac{m_{oil}}{m_{pigment}} \times 100$ | g oil / 100 g pigment | Lower OA = easier dispersion |
| **Iodine Value** | $IV = \dfrac{(V_{blank} - V_{sample}) \times N_{thio} \times 12.69}{m_{sample}}$ | g I₂ / 100 g | Measures unsaturation of oils |
| **Specific Surface Area (BET)** | Empirical (N₂ adsorption) | m²/g | Pigment fineness indicator |
| **Critical Pigment Volume Concentration (CPVC)** | Determined via oil absorption: $CPVC = \dfrac{1}{1 + OA \cdot \rho_{pigment}/100}$ | % | Transition from glossy → matte film |
| **Rheology — Newtonian Viscosity** | $\tau = \eta \dot{\gamma}$ | Pa·s | Where τ = shear stress, γ̇ = shear rate |
| **Rheology — Power Law** | $\tau = K \dot{\gamma}^n$ | Pa·s^n | Shear-thinning inks (n < 1); shear-thickening (n > 1) |
| **Acid Value of Resin** | $AV = \dfrac{V_{KOH} \times N_{KOH} \times 56.1}{m_{resin}}$ | mg KOH/g | Resin reactivity/solubility index |
| **Wijs Iodine Value (modified)** | $IV_{Wijs} = \dfrac{(V_b - V_s) \times N_{thio} \times 12.69}{m}$ | g/100 g | Standardized unsaturation test |

> [!WARNING]
> **Mermaid/LaTeX Table Notice:** The vertical pipe `\|` symbol inside equations has been replaced with `\vert` or `\mid` to preserve markdown table integrity. Do not write raw `\|x\|` inside a table cell.

## 2.3 Real-World Industrial Utility

- **Ink formulation scientists** use **PVC** to engineer glossy packaging inks (low PVC, ~10–20%) vs. matte news inks (high PVC, ~40–60%).
- **Oil absorption** dictates milling energy — pigments like Carbon Black (OA ~120) demand far more wetting energy than TiO₂ (OA ~20).
- **Iodine value** of linseed oil (~175–190) explains its dominance in sheet-fed offset inks — rapid oxidative polymerization ensures fast press-room turnaround.
- **Acid value** of rosin (~165–185 mg KOH/g) governs its solubility in mineral spirits and reaction potential with polyols to form esters.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Critical Pigment Volume Concentration (CPVC) from Oil Absorption

The CPVC represents the pigment loading at which the vehicle **exactly fills** the interstitial voids between packed pigment particles. Above CPVC, the film becomes porous, matte, and permeable; below CPVC, the film is glossy and continuous.

**Given:** Oil Absorption (OA) is defined as:

$$OA = \dfrac{m_{oil}}{m_{pigment}} \times 100 \;\; \text{(g oil per 100 g pigment)}$$

**Volume Conversion:** Convert oil mass to volume using oil density $\rho_{oil}$ and pigment mass to volume using pigment density $\rho_{pigment}$.

$$V_{oil} = \dfrac{m_{oil}}{\rho_{oil}} = \dfrac{OA \cdot m_{pigment}}{100 \cdot \rho_{oil}}$$

$$V_{pigment} = \dfrac{m_{pigment}}{\rho_{pigment}}$$

**At CPVC**, the oil volume exactly fills the voids between particles:

$$\dfrac{V_{oil}}{V_{pigment}} = \dfrac{OA}{100} \cdot \dfrac{\rho_{pigment}}{\rho_{oil}}$$

The volume fraction of pigment at CPVC is therefore:

$$CPVC = \dfrac{V_{pigment}}{V_{pigment} + V_{oil}} = \dfrac{1}{1 + \dfrac{V_{oil}}{V_{pigment}}}$$

Substituting the oil-to-pigment volume ratio:

$$\boxed{CPVC = \dfrac{1}{1 + \dfrac{OA \cdot \rho_{pigment}}{100 \cdot \rho_{oil}}}}$$

### Numerical Worked Example (KTU Pattern)

**Problem:** A Titanium Dioxide pigment has oil absorption OA = 22 g/100 g, density $\rho_{pigment}$ = 4.2 g/cm³, and is wetted by linseed oil of density $\rho_{oil}$ = 0.93 g/cm³. Calculate CPVC.

**Step 1:** Compute the volume ratio of oil to pigment.

$$\dfrac{V_{oil}}{V_{pigment}} = \dfrac{OA \cdot \rho_{pigment}}{100 \cdot \rho_{oil}} = \dfrac{22 \times 4.2}{100 \times 0.93}$$

$$= \dfrac{92.4}{93.0} \approx 0.9935$$

**Step 2:** Apply the CPVC formula.

$$CPVC = \dfrac{1}{1 + 0.9935} = \dfrac{1}{1.9935} \approx 0.5017$$

$$\boxed{CPVC \approx 50.17\%}$$

**Interpretation:** Above ~50% pigment volume loading, the linseed oil can no longer fill the voids, and the dry film becomes matte and porous.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Iodine Value of a Drying Oil

The Wijs method uses iodine monochloride (ICl) which adds across C=C double bonds. Each double bond consumes one mole of ICl.

**Reaction:**

$$R-CH=CH-R' + ICl \longrightarrow R-CHI-CHCl-R'$$

**Formula derivation:**

$$IV = \dfrac{(V_{blank} - V_{sample}) \times N_{thio} \times M_{I_2}}{m_{sample}} \times 100$$

Where $M_{I_2}$ = molecular weight of iodine = 253.81 g/mol, normalized by dividing by 2 for the equivalents (since 1 mol Na₂S₂O₃ ≡ ½ mol I₂).

The standard empirical factor 12.69 arises from:

$$12.69 = \dfrac{253.81}{2 \times 10} = \dfrac{M_{I_2}}{20}$$

Giving:

$$\boxed{IV = \dfrac{(V_b - V_s) \times N_{thio} \times 12.69}{m_{sample}}}$$

### Numerical Example

**Problem:** 0.25 g of linseed oil consumes 38.2 mL of 0.1 N Na₂S₂O₃. Blank titration consumes 48.0 mL. Calculate IV.

**Step 1:** Compute difference.

$$\Delta V = 48.0 - 38.2 = 9.8 \;\; \text{mL}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute.

$$IV = \dfrac{9.8 \times 0.1 \times 12.69}{0.25} = \dfrac{12.436}{0.25}$$

$$\boxed{IV \approx 49.74 \;\; \text{g I}_2 / 100 \text{ g oil}}$$

**Note:** The textbook IV of linseed oil (~175–190) is higher because the Wijs method uses 0.2 N or higher normality thiosulfate; the calculation logic remains identical.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Drying vs. Non-Drying Oils

| Property | Linseed Oil | Tung Oil | Soya Oil | Mineral Oil |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Iodine Value | 175–190 | 160–175 | 125–140 | < 10 |
| Drying Mechanism | Oxidation | Oxidation (faster) | Slow oxidation | Evaporation only |
| Film Hardness | High | Very high | Low | None |
| Yellowing | Pronounced | Moderate | Slight | None |
| Primary Use | Sheet-fed offset | High-gloss varnishes | Heat-set web offset | News ink, solvent inks |

## 3.4 Selection Logic for Ink Formulators (Analytical Decision Tree)

1. **Substrate?** Paper → drying oil; Plastic → solvent system; Foil → UV-curable resin.
2. **Print process?** Offset → oxidative vehicle; Flexo/Gravure → volatile solvent vehicle; Screen → high-viscosity oil + resin.
3. **End-use?** Food packaging → low-migration resin; Outdoor → UV stabilizers + lightfast pigments.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flow: Hierarchical Classification of Ink Raw Materials

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[RAW MATERIALS FOR PRINTING INKS]:::root
    A --> B[COLORANTS 10 to 25 percent]
    A --> C[VEHICLES 50 to 85 percent]
    A --> D[ADDITIVES 0.1 to 5 percent]

    B --> B1[PIGMENTS Insoluble]
    B --> B2[DYES Soluble]

    B1 --> B1a[INORGANIC PIGMENTS]
    B1 --> B1b[ORGANIC PIGMENTS]
    B2 --> B2a[ACID DYES]
    B2 --> B2b[DIRECT DYES]
    B2 --> B2c[SOLVENT DYES]

    B1a --> B1a1[Titanium Dioxide WHITE]
    B1a --> B1a2[Carbon Black BLACK]
    B1a --> B1a3[Iron Oxide RED YELLOW BROWN]
    B1a --> B1a4[Chrome Yellow PbCrO4]
    B1a --> B1a5[Ultramarine Blue]

    B1b --> B1b1[AZO PIGMENTS Hansa Lithol]
    B1b --> B1b2[PHTHALOCYANINE Blue Green]
    B1b --> B1b3[QUINACRIDONE Magenta Violet]
    B1b --> B1b4[DIOXAZINE Carbazole Violet]

    C --> C1[OILS]
    C --> C2[RESINS]
    C --> C3[SOLVENTS]

    C1 --> C1a[Drying Oils Linseed Tung]
    C1 --> C1b[Semi-Drying Soya Sunflower]
    C1 --> C1c[Non-Drying Mineral Castor]

    C2 --> C2a[Natural Rosin Shellac]
    C2 --> C2b[Modified Rosin Esters]
    C2 --> C2c[Synthetic Alkyd Acrylic PU]

    C3 --> C3a[Aqueous Water Glycol]
    C3 --> C3b[Aliphatic Heptane]
    C3 --> C3c[Aromatic Toluene Xylene]
    C3 --> C3d[Alcohols Esters Ketones]

    D --> D1[Driers Co Mn Naphthenate]
    D --> D2[Waxes PE PTFE Carnauba]
    D --> D3[Plasticizers DOP DOA]
    D --> D4[Surfactants Wetting]
    D --> D5[Antifoams Antiskin]
    D --> D6[UV Stabilizers HALS]

    classDef root fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px,color:#fff
    classDef cat fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block: Functional Architecture of an Ink Film

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[PIEMENT PARTICLE 0.01 to 10 micrometer]:::solid
    V[VEHICLE Continuous Liquid Phase]:::liquid
    AD[ADDITIVES Catalysts Waxes Plasticizers]:::add
    S[SUBSTRATE Paper Board Plastic Foil]:::sub

    V -- wets and surrounds --> P
    V -- carries to surface --> S
    AD -- tune performance of --> V
    P -- held by --> V
    V -- adheres to --> S
    AD -- modifies surface of --> P

    classDef solid fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef liquid fill:#bf9000,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef add fill:#548235,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef sub fill:#7030a0,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block: Formulation Decision Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Substrate?}:::dec
    Q1 -->|Paper Board| P1[Oxidative Drying Oil Vehicle]
    Q1 -->|Plastic Film| P2[Solvent Based Vehicle]
    Q1 -->|Foil Metal| P3[UV Curable Vehicle]

    Q2{Print Process?}:::dec
    P1 --> Q2
    Q2 -->|Offset Litho| R1[Resin plus Oil plus Drier]
    Q2 -->|Flexo or Gravure| R2[Resin plus Volatile Solvent]
    Q2 -->|Screen| R3[High Viscosity Oil plus Thickener]

    R1 --> S1[Add Pigment and Additives]
    R2 --> S1
    R3 --> S1
    S1 --> OUT[FINAL FORMULATED INK]

    classDef dec fill:#ffe699,stroke:#000
```
