# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**Q: Differentiate between a pigment and a dye as colorants used in printing inks.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

| Property | Pigment | Dye |
|---|---|---|
| Solubility in vehicle | Insoluble (dispersed) | Soluble (true solution) |
| Physical form | Discrete particles (0.01–10 µm) | Molecular dispersion |
| Transparency | Often transparent (organic) | Generally transparent |
| Lightfastness | Superior | Generally poor |
| Bleed resistance | Excellent | Poor |
| Tinting strength | High (organic) | Variable |
| Typical application | Offset, screen, most inks | Flexo, gravure, ink-jet |

Pigments are preferred in printing inks because of their **superior bleed resistance and lightfastness**, whereas dyes are restricted to fluid low-viscosity ink systems.

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**Q: What are driers in printing inks? Name two examples.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**
Driers (or siccatives) are **metallic soap catalysts** added to oil-based printing inks to **accelerate the oxidative polymerization** of drying oils such as linseed oil. They function by catalyzing the breakdown of hydroperoxides, propagating the free-radical chain reaction that converts the liquid oil into a dry solid film.

**Examples:**
- Cobalt naphthenate (surface drier — fast tack-free)
- Manganese naphthenate (through-drier)
- Lead octoate (historically used, now restricted for toxicity)
- Zirconium naphthenate (modern, low-toxicity alternative)

Typical loading is **0.5–3% by mass** of the finished ink.

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**(a)** Classify the raw materials of printing inks into three major groups with suitable examples. (7 Marks)
**(b)** Explain in detail the role of **oils, resins, and solvents** as components of the vehicle system. (7 Marks)

`[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2]`

### **Model Answer (a) — 7 Marks**

The raw materials of printing inks are classified into three principal groups:

**1. Colorants (10–25%):** Pigments (organic and inorganic) and dyes — provide hue, chroma, opacity.

**2. Vehicles (50–85%):** Oils, resins, and solvents — carry pigment, provide film formation, adhesion, and rheology.

**3. Additives (0.1–5%):** Driers, waxes, plasticizers, surfactants, antifoams, UV stabilizers — tune specific performance parameters.

(Each subgroup elaborated with 2–3 examples and a one-line function statement.)

### **Model Answer (b) — 7 Marks**

The vehicle system is a carefully engineered blend of **oils, resins, and solvents**, each performing a distinct function:

- **Oils:** Provide **film-forming capability** through oxidative polymerization of unsaturated fatty acid chains. Drying oils like **linseed oil** (IV ~175–190) polymerize rapidly, forming tough cross-linked films. Non-drying oils (mineral oil) function purely as carrier and evaporate.

- **Resins:** Act as the **structural binder** of the ink film. They confer **gloss, hardness, adhesion, and solvent-release properties**. Common resins include **rosin esters, phenolic resins, alkyds, and acrylics**. Acid value (mg KOH/g) is the key quality parameter.

- **Solvents:** **Dissolve the resin system** and reduce ink viscosity to the press-operable range. Solvents may be **aqueous (water, glycol)** or **non-aqueous (mineral spirits, toluene, alcohols, esters, ketones)**. Solvent volatility governs drying rate — fast-evaporating solvents for heat-set web, slow-evaporating ones for gravure.

The vehicle **carries the pigment onto the substrate**, **anchors it via resin adhesion**, and **develops final film properties** through drying/curing mechanisms.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**(a)** Describe the **classification of pigments** used in printing inks with examples. Compare organic and inorganic pigments. (7 Marks)
**(b)** What are **additives**? Discuss the function of driers, waxes, plasticizers, and surfactants in printing inks. (7 Marks)

`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2]`

### **Model Answer (a) — 7 Marks**

**Classification of Pigments:**
Pigments are classified by **origin** into:

**Inorganic Pigments:** Titanium dioxide (white), Carbon black (black), Iron oxides (red/yellow/brown), Chrome yellow, Ultramarine blue, Chromium oxide green.

**Organic Pigments:** Azo pigments (Hansa yellow, Lithol rubine), Phthalocyanine (blue/green), Quinacridone (magenta/violet), Dioxazine (violet), Carbazole violet, Benzimidazolone.

**Comparison Table (drawn by student in exam):**

| Property | Inorganic | Organic |
|---|---|---|
| Color strength | Lower | Higher |
| Opacity | High | Variable (often transparent) |
| Lightfastness | Excellent | Variable (excellent for polycyclic) |
| Chemical resistance | Excellent | Good to moderate |
| Specific gravity | High (3.5–5.0) | Low (1.4–1.8) |
| Cost | Lower (except cadmiums) | Higher |

### **Model Answer (b) — 7 Marks**

**Definition:** Additives are minor constituents (0.1–5%) added to printing inks to **modify specific functional properties** without altering the primary color or body of the ink.

- **Driers:** Metallic soaps (Co, Mn, Pb, Zr naphthenates) catalyze oxidative polymerization of drying oils. Cobalt = surface drier, Manganese = through drier.

- **Waxes:** Polyethylene (PE), PTFE, or carnauba wax provide **slip, scratch resistance, and rub resistance**. They migrate to the surface during drying, forming a low-friction layer.

- **Plasticizers:** DOP (dioctyl phthalate), DOA (dioctyl adipate) impart **flexibility** to the dry film, preventing cracking on flexible substrates.

- **Surfactants/Dispersants:** Reduce interfacial tension, **wet the pigment** during milling, and **prevent flocculation/re-agglomeration** of pigment particles, ensuring stable dispersion and maximum color development.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🔴 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Mixing up "pigment" with "dye" definitions** — most common error; loses 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Stating Iodine Value units incorrectly** — it is *g I₂ per 100 g sample*, NOT *g per 100 mL*.
> 3. **Omitting chemical examples** — the examiner expects at least 2 named examples per category (e.g., Phthalocyanine Blue, Lithol Rubine, Linseed oil).
> 4. **Forgetting the role of vehicles vs. binders vs. additives** — many students incorrectly call additives "binders." Additives are *modifiers*; resins are the *binders*.
> 5. **In numerical problems, forgetting to convert mass to volume** for CPVC calculations — direct use of oil absorption without density conversion yields wrong answers.

---

## ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- 🎯 **Three families:** Colorants + Vehicles + Additives.
- 🎯 **Pigment vs. Dye:** Pigment = insoluble particulate; Dye = soluble molecular colorant.
- 🎯 **Inorganic pigments:** TiO₂ (white), Carbon Black (black), Iron Oxide, Chrome Yellow, Ultramarine — high opacity, low chroma.
- 🎯 **Organic pigments:** Azo, Phthalocyanine, Quinacridone, Dioxazine — high chroma, transparent, higher cost.
- 🎯 **Drying oils** have Iodine Value > 140; **non-drying oils** IV < 100.
- 🎯 **Linseed oil** (IV ~175–190) is the workhorse of sheet-fed offset ink vehicles.
- 🎯 **Rosin + phenolic/alkyd resins** dominate the binder market for offset inks.
- 🎯 **PVC formula:** $PVC = V_{pigment} / (V_{pigment} + V_{vehicle}) \times 100$.
- 🎯 **CPVC** = transition from glossy → matte film; function of oil absorption and pigment/oil densities.
- 🎯 **Driers** = metallic soap catalysts (Co, Mn, Zr); **Waxes** = slip/rub resistance; **Plasticizers** = film flexibility; **Surfactants** = pigment wetting/dispersion.
- 🎯 **Typical composition:** 10–25% pigment, 50–85% vehicle, 0.1–5% additives.
- 🎯 **Solvent selection** governs drying rate — fast solvents for heat-set, slow for gravure.
- 🎯 **Acid value of rosin** (~165–185 mg KOH/g) is a critical resin quality parameter for KTU exam derivations.
- 🎯 **KTU 14-mark questions** ALWAYS expect a *comparison table* and at least *three chemical examples* per answer.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pigments
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT866 - TECHNOLOGY OF PRINTING INKS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Pigments

---

# 🎨 PIGMENTS — The Soul of Printing Inks

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> A **pigment** is a finely divided, insoluble solid particulate material that is dispersed in a vehicle (binder/vehicle) to impart **colour, opacity, body, and other functional properties** to a printing ink. Unlike dyes, pigments do not dissolve in the medium but remain as discrete particles held in suspension.

In the language of KTU 2024 Scheme, pigments are classified as the **chromatic (colour-bearing) and functional solid phase** of a printing ink formulation. They are the reason why ink has visual identity on paper, plastic, metal, or any substrate.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:**
> In the 2024 Scheme PEPOT866 syllabus, the term *Pigments* is positioned as the **fundamental building block** of all ink systems — letterpress, offset, flexo, gravure, and screen. The examiner expects you to know the *chemistry, classification, properties, and selection criteria* of pigments.

### 🔍 Conceptual Analogy — The "Confetti in Soup" Intuition

Imagine you drop a fistful of **brightly coloured, sand-sized confetti pieces** into a transparent bowl of honey. The honey acts as the vehicle (binder), and the confetti pieces are the pigments. The honey carries the confetti onto the paper, where it dries and locks the coloured pieces in place.

- If the confetti pieces are **bright red**, the dried film looks red.
- If they are **black**, the film looks black.
- If the confetti pieces are **very small and well-dispersed**, the colour looks uniform and rich.
- If they are **large or clumped**, the film looks streaky and dull.

This is **exactly** what happens in a printing ink. The pigment is the *colour payload*, and the vehicle is the *transporter*.

### 🎯 Why Pigments Are Indispensable in Printing Inks

A printing ink needs pigments because they deliver four critical engineering functions:

1. **Colour** (hue, saturation, brightness) — visual communication.
2. **Opacity / Transparency** — control of how much substrate shows through.
3. **Bulk and Body** — rheology control, prevents ink from being too thin.
4. **Functional Properties** — corrosion resistance, UV protection, conductivity, magnetic response, etc.

### 📊 Fundamental Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Industry Metrics to Memorize:**
> - **Refractive Index (RI)** of common pigments: **1.5 – 2.7** (TiO₂ ≈ 2.7 — highest)
> - **Particle Size** range for printing ink pigments: **0.01 µm – 10 µm** (typical: 0.1 – 5 µm)
> - **Density** of pigments: **1.5 – 5.5 g/cm³**
> - **Surface Area**: **5 – 100 m²/g**
> - **Oil Absorption (OA)**: **10 – 100 g oil / 100 g pigment**
> - **pH (aqueous slurry)**: **4 – 9** (stability window)
> - **Thermal Stability required**: up to **> 200 °C** (for heatset inks)

### Pigment vs Dye — The Critical Distinction

| Property | Pigment | Dye |
|---|---|---|
| Solubility in vehicle | **Insoluble** | **Soluble** |
| State in ink | Dispersed particles | Molecular solution |
| Lightfastness | High | Variable |
| Chemical resistance | Excellent | Often poor |
| Transparency range | Wide | Wide |
| Cost | Moderate–High | Variable |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Examiners love the "Differentiate between Pigment and Dye" question. Memorize this table — it is a guaranteed 3-mark short-answer hit.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Pigments (KTU Most-Tested Topic)

Pigments are classified along **two major axes**: (a) **Chemical Origin** and (b) **Colour/Optical Behaviour**.

### A. Classification by Chemical Origin

#### I. Inorganic Pigments
- **Oxides:** Titanium Dioxide (TiO₂) — white; Iron Oxide (Fe₂O₃) — red, yellow, brown; Zinc Oxide (ZnO) — white.
- **Sulphides:** Cadmium Sulphide (CdS) — yellow; Cadmium Selenide (CdSe) — red; Mercuric Sulphide (HgS) — vermilion.
- **Chromates:** Lead Chromate (PbCrO₄) — yellow; Molybdate Orange.
- **Carbon-based:** Carbon Black, Lampblack.
- **Metallic:** Aluminium (silver), Bronze (gold), Zinc dust.
- **Ultramarine:** Complex aluminosilicate — blue.

#### II. Organic Pigments
- **Azo Pigments** (largest class): Monoazo yellows, disazo oranges/reds, β-naphthol reds, Naphthol AS, BON reds/maroons, Disazo condensation pigments.
- **Phthalocyanine Blues** (P.B. 15) and **Phthalocyanine Greens** (P.G. 7) — *workhorses* of printing ink.
- **Quinacridone** — violets, magentas, reds (excellent lightfastness).
- **Anthraquinone** — reds and violets.
- **Indigo & Thioindigo** — denim blues, reds.
- **Dioxazine** — strong violets.
- **Isoindolinone & Isoindoline** — yellows, oranges, reds.
- **Diketo-pyrrolo-pyrrole (DPP)** — bright reds (newer class).
- **Benzimidazolone** — yellows, oranges, reds.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters in KTU exams:** Examiners frequently ask students to **"Classify organic pigments with examples"** or **"Discuss the role of phthalocyanine pigments in offset inks."** Memorize at least 2–3 examples per chemical family.

### B. Classification by Optical/Functional Behaviour

| Category | Behaviour | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Colour (Chromatic) Pigments** | Provide hue | Phthalocyanine Blue, Azo Yellow |
| **White Pigments** | Scatter all wavelengths | TiO₂, ZnO, Lithopone |
| **Black Pigments** | Absorb all wavelengths | Carbon Black |
| **Extender Pigments** | Cheap fillers, modify body, gloss | China clay, calcium carbonate, talc, barytes |
| **Metallic Pigments** | Reflective flake | Al, Bronze |
| **Pearlescent / Interference** | Multi-angle iridescence | Mica coated with TiO₂ |
| **Fluorescent & Phosphorescent** | Emit visible light | Dyes in resin matrix |
| **Functional Pigments** | Magnetic, conductive, anti-corrosive | Iron oxide, carbon black |

## 2.2 Key Properties of Pigments (Engineering Parameters)

For a pigment to be useful in printing ink, it must satisfy a stringent set of properties. These are the **"9 Pillars of Pigment Quality"** — every KTU examiner expects you to know them.

1. **Colour Strength / Tinting Strength** — the ability to impart colour.
2. **Hue** — the specific shade (red, blue, green).
3. **Purity / Saturation (Chroma)** — vividness vs dullness.
4. **Opacity or Transparency** — covering power vs see-through.
5. **Particle Size & Distribution** — affects gloss, rheology, and strength.
6. **Chemical Resistance** — to acids, alkalis, solvents, soap.
7. **Lightfastness** — resistance to fading under light.
8. **Heat Stability** — resistance to temperature during processing.
9. **Bleeding / Migration Resistance** — pigment must not bleed into vehicle or substrate.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this table. Carry it to your exam revision. These are the high-yield equations.**

| Property | Formula / Definition | Units | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Oil Absorption (OA)** | $\text{OA} = \dfrac{m_{\text{oil}}}{m_{\text{pigment}}} \times 100$ | g oil / 100 g pigment | Indicates binder demand; affects rheology |
| **Specific Surface Area (SSA)** | $\text{SSA} = \dfrac{A}{m}$ | m²/g | Smaller particles → higher SSA |
| **Hiding Power (HP)** | $\text{HP} = \dfrac{m_{\text{pigment}}}{A_{\text{covered}}}$ at contrast ratio 0.98 | m²/g or m²/L | Opacity of a film |
| **Tinting Strength (TS)** | $\text{TS} \propto \dfrac{1}{c_{\text{pigment}}}$ for matched depth | % vs standard | Efficiency of colour delivery |
| **Refractive Index Ratio (R)** | $R = \dfrac{n_{\text{pigment}}}{n_{\text{vehicle}}}$ | dimensionless | If $R \gg 1$ → opaque; if $R \approx 1$ → transparent |
| **Mean Particle Diameter (d)** | From PSD by volume distribution | µm | Affects gloss, strength, viscosity |
| **Surface Coverage (per particle)** | $A_p = \pi d^2$ | µm² | Geometric coverage |
| **Volume of Pigment in Ink (φ)** | $\phi = \dfrac{V_{\text{pigment}}}{V_{\text{ink}}}$ | dimensionless | Loading; affects rheology |
| **Beer–Lambert (transmittance)** | $T = 10^{-\varepsilon c \ell}$ | dimensionless | Tinting strength proxy |
| **Cubic Structure Hiding** | $\text{Scattering} \propto R = \dfrac{(n_p^2 - n_v^2)^2}{(n_p^2 + 2n_v^2)^2}$ | — | Higher $R$ → more opaque (Fresnel relation) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical reminder:** When writing inline math, use $\vert x \vert$ or $\lvert x \rvert$ — never the bare pipe `|` — to keep markdown tables intact.

## 2.4 Why These Properties Matter in Real Ink Engineering

- **High Oil Absorption** = high binder demand = sticky, tacky ink = poor press performance. Formulators prefer **low-OA pigments** for sharp offset printing.
- **Small Particle Size** (sub-micron) = higher tinting strength, glossier films, but harder to disperse and more prone to flocculation.
- **High Refractive Index Ratio** ($n_p / n_v \gg 1$) = opaque (hiding) pigment, used for **cover inks on dark substrates**.
- **Lightfast Organic Pigments** (Phthalocyanine, Quinacridone) are mandatory for **outdoor packaging, posters, and signage**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Relating Particle Size to Surface Area

The surface area of a single spherical pigment particle of diameter $d$ is:

$$
A_p = \pi d^2
$$

The volume of that same particle is:

$$
V_p = \frac{1}{6} \pi d^3
$$

If we have a mass $m$ of pigment with density $\rho_p$, the total number of particles is:

$$
N = \frac{m}{\rho_p \cdot V_p}
$$

Substituting the volume expression:

$$
N = \frac{m}{\rho_p} \cdot \frac{6}{\pi d^3} = \frac{6m}{\pi \rho_p d^3}
$$

Total surface area is therefore:

$$
A_{\text{total}} = N \cdot A_p = \frac{6m}{\pi \rho_p d^3} \cdot \pi d^2
$$

Cancelling $\pi$ and one $d$:

$$
A_{\text{total}} = \frac{6m}{\rho_p d}
$$

Specific surface area (SSA) per unit mass:

$$
\text{SSA} = \frac{A_{\text{total}}}{m} = \frac{6}{\rho_p d}
$$

> **Engineering Insight:** SSA is **inversely proportional to particle diameter**. Halving particle size doubles the surface area, which means doubling the binder demand and doubling the viscosity. This is why **nano-pigments** are powerful but rheologically aggressive.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Hiding Power from Contrast Ratio

The **contrast ratio** $C_R$ of an ink film of thickness $\ell$ over a black substrate versus a white substrate is:

$$
C_R = \frac{R_{\text{black}}}{R_{\text{white}}}
$$

For a pigment with refractive index $n_p$ in a vehicle with refractive index $n_v$, the **Fresnel reflection factor** at each interface is:

$$
R_{\text{Fresnel}} = \left( \frac{n_p - n_v}{n_p + n_v} \right)^2
$$

The hiding power is defined such that $C_R = 0.98$ (ASTM D2805). The minimum film thickness $\ell_{\min}$ required to hide the substrate is given by:

$$
\ell_{\min} = \frac{-\ln(0.02)}{K \cdot \phi}
$$

where $K$ is the scattering coefficient and $\phi$ is the pigment volume fraction. The **hiding power per unit mass** is then:

$$
\text{HP} = \frac{1}{\rho_p \cdot \ell_{\min}} = \frac{K \phi}{-\rho_p \ln(0.02)} = \frac{K \phi}{3.91 \rho_p}
$$

> **Real-world use:** A pigment with $K = 5 \times 10^4$ m⁻¹, $\phi = 0.15$, $\rho_p = 4.2$ g/cm³ (TiO₂) gives $\text{HP} \approx 4.6$ m²/g — matching the **literature value for rutile TiO₂**.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Tinting Strength by Kubelka–Munk

For a pigmented film, the **scattering coefficient $S$** and **absorption coefficient $K$** are combined into the **Kubelka–Munk function**:

$$
\frac{K}{S} = \frac{(1 - R_\infty)^2}{2 R_\infty}
$$

where $R_\infty$ is the reflectance of a film so thick that further thickness has no effect. Tinting strength compares the $K/S$ ratio of a sample to a standard:

$$
\text{TS} (\%) = \frac{(K/S)_{\text{sample}}}{(K/S)_{\text{standard}}} \times 100
$$

> **Practical Use:** When a supplier says their pigment is "110% tinting strength" vs a competitor, they mean their $K/S$ is 1.10× higher, so you can use **9% less pigment** for the same colour depth.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Oil Absorption

> **Problem:** A pigment weighing 20 g absorbs 8 g of linseed oil to reach the saturation point. Calculate its **oil absorption number (OA)** in g/100 g.

**Step 1 — Identify the ratio:**

$$
\text{OA} = \frac{m_{\text{oil}}}{m_{\text{pigment}}} \times 100
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute:**

$$
\text{OA} = \frac{8}{20} \times 100
$$

**Step 3 — Compute:**

$$
\text{OA} = 40 \text{ g oil} / 100 \text{ g pigment}
$$

> **Interpretation:** This is a *medium-OA* pigment. For comparison, carbon black typically shows OA = 100–300, while TiO₂ shows OA = 20–25.

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — Tinting Strength Ratio

> **Problem:** A test ink made with 5 g of sample pigment produces a $K/S$ of 0.85. The standard ink with 5 g of reference pigment produces $K/S$ of 0.78. Calculate the **tinting strength of the sample**.

**Step 1 — Apply the formula:**

$$
\text{TS}_{\text{sample}} = \frac{(K/S)_{\text{sample}}}{(K/S)_{\text{standard}}} \times 100
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute:**

$$
\text{TS}_{\text{sample}} = \frac{0.85}{0.78} \times 100
$$

**Step 3 — Compute:**

$$
\text{TS}_{\text{sample}} = 108.97 \% \approx 109\%
$$

> **Interpretation:** The sample is **9% stronger** than the reference — you can use 8.3% less pigment for the same colour.

## 3.6 Comparative Analytical Matrix — Common Ink Pigments

| Pigment | Class | Colour | RI (approx.) | Typical OA | Lightfastness |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Phthalocyanine Blue (P.B. 15) | Organic | Deep blue | 1.55 | 40–60 | 8 (excellent) |
| Carbon Black (P.B. 7) | Inorganic | Black | 1.95 (anatase-like) | 100–300 | 8 (excellent) |
| TiO₂ Rutile (P.W. 6) | Inorganic oxide | White | 2.70 | 20–25 | 8 (excellent) |
| Arylide Yellow (P.Y. 12) | Azo organic | Yellow | 1.55 | 40–55 | 5–6 (moderate) |
| Quinacridone Red (P.R. 122) | Organic | Magenta-red | 1.60 | 45–60 | 8 (excellent) |
| Iron Oxide Red (P.R. 101) | Inorganic | Red-brown | 2.95 | 20–30 | 8 (excellent) |
| Lithol Rubine (P.R. 57:1) | Azo organic | Rubine red | 1.55 | 50–70 | 4–5 (limited) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of Pigment Behaviour in Ink

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Pigment Powder] --> B[Pre-Dispersion Stage]
    B --> C[High-Speed Mixer / Bead Mill]
    C --> D[Wetting by Vehicle]
    D --> E[De-agglomeration]
    E --> F[Stabilization in Vehicle]
    F --> G[Final Printing Ink]
    
    H[Pigment Properties] --> I[Particle Size]
    H --> J[Surface Chemistry]
    H --> K[Refractive Index]
    H --> L[Oil Absorption]
    
    I --> M[Rheology of Ink]
    J --> M
    K --> N[Optical Performance]
    L --> M
    I --> N
    
    M --> O[Press Runnability]
    N --> P[Colour Strength Gloss Opacity]
    
    O --> Q[Print Quality]
    P --> Q
    
    Q --> R[End Use Application]
    R --> S[Offset]
    R --> T[Flexo]
    R --> U[Gravure]
    R --> V[Screen]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Tree Diagram — Classification of Pigments

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    PIG[Pigments]
    PIG --> INORG[Inorganic]
    PIG --> ORG[Organic]
    
    INORG --> INOX[Oxides]
    INORG --> INSUL[Sulphides and Selenides]
    INORG --> INCHR[Chromates and Molybdates]
    INORG --> INCARB[Carbon Black]
    INORG --> INMET[Metallic Flakes]
    
    INOX --> TIO2[Titanium Dioxide]
    INOX --> FEO[Iron Oxide]
    INOX --> ZNO[Zinc Oxide]
    
    INSUL --> CDS[Cadmium Sulphide]
    INSUL --> CDSE[Cadmium Selenide]
    
    INCHR --> PBCH[Lead Chromate]
    INCHR --> MOLB[Molybdate Orange]
    
    INCARB --> CB[Carbon Black Furnace]
    INCARB --> LB[Lampblack]
    
    INMET --> AL[Aluminium]
    INMET --> BR[Bronze]
    
    ORG --> AZO[Azo Pigments]
    ORG --> PTH[Phthalocyanines]
    ORG --> QUN[Quinacridones]
    ORG --> ANT[Anthraquinones]
    ORG --> DIO[Dioxazines]
    ORG --> DPP[DPP Pigments]
    ORG --> BZI[Benzimidazolones]
    
    AZO --> AZY[Arylide Yellow]
    AZO --> BNR[BON Red Maroon]
    AZO --> NPH[Naphthol AS]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Flowchart — Selection Logic for a Printing Ink Pigment

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Select Pigment for Ink] --> Q1{Substrate?}
    Q1 --> PAPER[Paper or Board]
    Q1 --> FILM[Plastic Film]
    Q1 --> METAL[Metal Foil]
    
    PAPER --> Q2{Process?}
    FILM --> Q3{End Use?}
    METAL --> Q4{End Use?}
    
    Q2 --> OFFSET[Offset]
    Q2 --> GRAV[Gravure]
    Q2 --> FLEXO[Flexo]
    
    Q3 --> FOOD[Food Packaging]
    Q3 --> OUT[Outdoor]
    
    Q4 --> DECO[Decorative]
    Q4 --> FUNC[Functional]
    
    OFFSET --> P1[Use organic pigments high transparency for process inks]
    GRAV --> P2[Use soluble low particle size pigments]
    FLEXO --> P3[Use pigments compatible with water or solvent]
    
    FOOD --> P4[Use low migration pigments]
    OUT --> P5[Use high lightfastness pigments]
    
    DECO --> P6[Use metallic pearlescent pigments]
    FUNC --> P7[Use functional pigments]
    
    P1 --> END[Ink Formulation Ready]
    P2 --> END
    P3 --> END
    P4 --> END
    P5 --> END
    P6 --> END
    P7 --> END
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Define a pigment. How is it different from a dye in the context of printing inks?

> **Model Answer:**
> A **pigment** is a finely divided, **insoluble** solid particulate material that is dispersed (not dissolved) in the vehicle of a printing ink to provide **colour, opacity, and functional properties**.
> 
> **Difference:**
> - Pigments are **insoluble** in the vehicle; dyes are **soluble**.
> - Pigments remain as **discrete particles**; dyes form **molecular solutions**.
> - Pigments offer **superior lightfastness and chemical resistance**; dyes are usually brighter but less durable.
> - Pigments are used in inks, paints, and plastics; dyes are used in textile and liquid inks.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024] List any four essential properties required for a pigment to be suitable for use in printing inks.

> **Model Answer:**
> 1. **High Tinting Strength** — to deliver maximum colour per unit mass.
> 2. **Good Dispersion Stability** — to prevent flocculation or settling.
> 3. **Lightfastness** — resistance to fading under light (rating 6–8 on Blue Wool Scale).
> 4. **Chemical Resistance** — non-reactive with vehicle, solvents, acids, or alkalis.
> 
> *(Optional add-ons: heat stability, low oil absorption, low bleed, hue purity.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Question (14 Marks) — With KTU Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> - Students often **omit the difference between organic and inorganic pigments** when asked about "classification". Examiner deducts **2–3 marks** immediately.
> - Many write **"pigment gives colour"** and stop. Examiner expects **"how"** — refractive index, particle size, scattering. You lose **2 marks** for missing the mechanism.
> - **Avoid confusing extender pigments with colour pigments.** Extenders (CaCO₃, talc, china clay) have no chromophore and only contribute to body.
> - **Do not write generic definitions copied from chemistry textbooks.** KTU examiners want ink-specific answers (vehicle interaction, dispersion, hiding power in print film).

---

### **Part B – Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]

> **(a)** Classify pigments used in printing inks with suitable examples. Discuss the role of **phthalocyanine pigments** in offset ink formulations. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Explain the concept of **hiding power** and **tinting strength** of a pigment. How are these properties measured, and why are they important in ink formulation? **(7 Marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a)

Pigments are classified along **chemical origin** and **optical behaviour**.

**1. Inorganic Pigments**
- Oxides: **TiO₂** (white), Fe₂O₃ (red/yellow)
- Sulphides: **CdS** (yellow)
- Chromates: **PbCrO₄** (yellow)
- Carbon: **Carbon Black** (black)
- Metallic: **Aluminium, Bronze**

**2. Organic Pigments**
- **Azo** (largest class): Arylide Yellow (P.Y. 12), Lithol Rubine (P.R. 57:1)
- **Phthalocyanine**: P.B. 15 (blue), P.G. 7 (green)
- **Quinacridone**: P.R. 122, P.V. 19
- **Dioxazine**: P.V. 23
- **DPP**: P.R. 254

**Role of Phthalocyanine Pigments in Offset Inks:**
- Copper Phthalocyanine Blue (P.B. 15:3) and Green (P.G. 7) are the **dominant chromatic pigments** in process colour printing.
- They offer **exceptional lightfastness (8)**, **chemical resistance**, and **heat stability**.
- Their **crystal form** (α, β, ε) controls hue and stability — β-form is greener and more stable.
- They are **transparent** in offset ink films, ideal for **overprint and trapping** of process colours.
- They have **low oil absorption** → low tack → good press runnability.

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b)

**Hiding Power (HP):**
- Defined as the **area of substrate** that a unit mass of pigment can hide at a given contrast ratio (typically 0.98 per ASTM D2805).
- **Mathematical Form:** $\text{HP} = \dfrac{K \phi}{3.91 \rho_p}$ where $K$ is the scattering coefficient, $\phi$ is the volume fraction, and $\rho_p$ is the pigment density.
- **Measurement:** Drawn-down films of varying thickness on a Leneta card, measured with a spectrophotometer to determine the film thickness that gives $C_R = 0.98$.
- **Importance:** Determines whether a pigment is suitable for **cover inks, overprint bases, or transparent process inks**.

**Tinting Strength (TS):**
- Defined as the **ability of a pigment to impart colour** when mixed with a white pigment or vehicle.
- **Mathematical Form (Kubelka–Munk):** $\text{TS} = \dfrac{(K/S)_{\text{sample}}}{(K/S)_{\text{standard}}} \times 100$
- **Measurement:** Pigment is mixed with a fixed amount of TiO₂, drawn down, and reflectance measured.
- **Importance:** A pigment with high TS is **economical** — less pigment gives the same depth of colour.

---

### **Part B – Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice

> **(a)** With neat diagrams, explain the **general method of manufacture of organic pigments** (e.g., azo pigments). **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Discuss the role of **particle size, refractive index, and oil absorption** in determining the performance of pigments in printing inks. **(7 Marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a) — Manufacture of Azo Pigments

1. **Diazotization:** An aromatic amine (e.g., 3,3'-dichlorobenzidine) is reacted with sodium nitrite (NaNO₂) and hydrochloric acid (HCl) at 0–5 °C to form a **diazonium salt**.

$$
\text{Ar-NH}_2 + \text{NaNO}_2 + 2\text{HCl} \longrightarrow \text{Ar-N}_2^+\text{Cl}^- + \text{NaCl} + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}
$$

2. **Coupling:** The diazonium salt is reacted with a coupling component (e.g., acetoacetanilide, naphthol, pyrazolone) in alkaline or acidic medium to form the **azo pigment** as an insoluble precipitate.

$$
\text{Ar-N}_2^+\text{Cl}^- + \text{H-Coupling} \longrightarrow \text{Ar-N=N-Coupling} + \text{HCl}
$$

3. **Filtration, Washing, Drying.**
4. **Conditioning (Grinding/Synthesis Treatment):** The crude pigment is heated in solvents (e.g., DMF, xylene) or with surfactants to grow the **correct crystal phase**, improve **dispersibility, lightfastness, and rheology**.

> Optional block diagram of the process.

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b) — Key Performance Parameters

**Particle Size:**
- Smaller particles → **higher tinting strength** (more surface area).
- Smaller particles → **higher gloss** (smoother film).
- But: too small → **higher viscosity, harder to disperse, prone to flocculation**.
- Sweet spot: 0.1–5 µm for offset inks; sub-micron for high-gloss liquid inks.

**Refractive Index (RI):**
- $n_{\text{TiO}_2} = 2.70$ → high opacity (hiding).
- $n_{\text{organic}} = 1.5$ → transparent (process colour printing).
- The **RI ratio $n_p / n_v$** determines scattering and therefore hiding.

**Oil Absorption (OA):**
- $\text{OA} = \dfrac{m_{\text{oil}}}{m_{\text{pigment}}} \times 100$
- High OA → high binder demand → tacky ink → poor press performance.
- Low OA → fluid ink → good transfer → sharper print.
- Carbon black has the highest OA; TiO₂ has the lowest among colour pigments.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **📝 High-Density Revision Notes — Read This Night Before Exam**

- **Pigment = insoluble colourant dispersed in vehicle.** Dye = soluble colourant. Memorize this distinction.
- **Two master classes: Inorganic & Organic.** Inorganic = oxides, sulphides, chromates, carbon black. Organic = azo, phthalocyanine, quinacridone, anthraquinone, DPP.
- **Phthalocyanine Blue (P.B. 15) & Green (P.G. 7)** = kings of offset ink. β-form is the most stable.
- **TiO₂ Rutile** = highest hiding power (RI = 2.70). Used in whites.
- **Carbon Black** = highest oil absorption (100–300 g/100g). Most adsorbent surface.
- **Quinacridone (P.R. 122, P.V. 19)** = premium magenta/violet. Best for outdoor.
- **9 Pillars of Pigment Quality:** Tinting Strength, Hue, Purity, Opacity/Transparency, Particle Size, Chemical Resistance, Lightfastness, Heat Stability, Bleed Resistance.
- **Formulas you MUST memorize:**
  - $\text{OA} = \dfrac{m_{\text{oil}}}{m_{\text{pigment}}} \times 100$
  - $\text{SSA} = \dfrac{6}{\rho_p d}$
  - $K/S = \dfrac{(1-R_\infty)^2}{2R_\infty}$
  - $\text{TS} = \dfrac{(K/S)_{\text{sample}}}{(K/S)_{\text{standard}}} \times 100$
- **Smaller particle size = more strength, more gloss, more viscosity.** There is a trade-off.
- **Refractive index ratio $n_p / n_v \gg 1$ = opaque; $\approx 1$ = transparent.**
- **Extender pigments (CaCO₃, talc, china clay, barytes)** are NOT colour pigments — they are bodying agents.
- **Lightfastness is rated 1–8 (Blue Wool Scale)** — process colours need ≥ 6; outdoor needs ≥ 7.
- **Food packaging inks** require **low-migration pigments** and **heavy-metal-free** chemistry (no PbCrO₄, no CdS).
- **Metallic pigments (Al, Bronze)** are leafing or non-leafing; used for decorative inks.
- **Pearlescent pigments** = mica coated with TiO₂; produce angle-dependent iridescence.

> 🎯 **Final KTU 2024 Tip:** When asked *"Discuss the role of pigments in printing inks"*, structure your answer in **four sections: (i) Definition & distinction from dyes, (ii) Classification with examples, (iii) Properties with quantitative parameters, (iv) Selection for specific processes.** A structured answer with subheadings and at least 2 examples per class fetches **full 14 marks** in Part B.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dyestuffs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT866 - TECHNOLOGY OF PRINTING INKS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Dyestuffs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Dyestuff?

A **dyestuff** is a coloured organic compound that, when dissolved in a suitable medium (solvent, water, oil, or a resin solution), produces a coloured solution that can be applied to a substrate to impart colour. The word "dye" originates from the Anglo-Saxon word *daeg* or *daehl*, meaning "to dye" or "to colour". A dye must be **substantive to the substrate** (i.e., it must have a natural affinity for the fibres or surface being coloured) and must produce **fast, durable colour** when used appropriately.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Textbook Standard):**
> A **dyestuff** is a soluble colouring matter (organic in nature) that has an affinity for the substrate on which it is applied and which, by undergoing a process of exhaustion from the medium in which it is applied, becomes fixed to the substrate.

In the context of **printing ink technology**, dyestuffs are the colourants that are *fully dissolved* in the ink vehicle (carrier fluid/resin), unlike **pigments** which are *insoluble* particles dispersed in the vehicle. This single distinction — solubility — governs almost every practical difference between dye-based and pigment-based printing inks.

## 1.2 Dyestuffs vs. Pigments — A Foundational Comparison

| Property | Dyestuff | Pigment |
|---|---|---|
| Solubility in vehicle | **Soluble** | **Insoluble** (dispersed) |
| Particle size | Molecular (~1–2 nm) | Particulate (0.01–50 µm) |
| Optical strength | Very high (transparent) | Moderate to high |
| Lightfastness | Generally **lower** | Generally **higher** |
| Chemical resistance | Variable, often lower | Generally excellent |
| Heat stability | Often limited | Usually high |
| Bleeding / migration | Prone to bleeding | Minimal bleeding |
| Cost | Often lower | Can be higher |
| Typical ink systems | Flexo, gravure, inkjet | Offset, screen, packaging |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in printing:** Because dyestuffs are molecularly dissolved, the ink vehicle itself becomes the colour. There is **no particle** to scatter light, so dye-based prints appear exceptionally **transparent and brilliant** — ideal for packaging laminations and photographic-quality inkjet prints. Pigment-based inks, in contrast, sit on the surface and offer **opacity and durability**.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Sugar in Tea" Model

Imagine a **clear glass of hot water**. Now:

- If you add **granulated sugar** and stir, the crystals **disappear** and the water becomes a uniformly coloured, transparent syrup. The sugar is **molecularly dissolved** — this is exactly what a **dyestuff** does in an ink vehicle. The colour is in the liquid itself.
- If you add **sand or chalk powder**, the particles remain visible, settle, and make the water appear cloudy and opaque. The colour comes from a **dispersed phase** — this is what a **pigment** does in an ink.

This "sugar vs. sand" analogy is the cleanest mental model for understanding dyestuffs in printing inks. Every property downstream — transparency, bleed, lightfastness, viscosity, filtration — flows from this single solubility distinction.

## 1.4 The Chromophore–Auxochrome Concept

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Chromophore:** The part of a dye molecule responsible for the colour. It is a conjugated π-electron system (alternating double and single bonds) that absorbs specific wavelengths of visible light (~400–700 nm).
>
> **Auxochrome:** A substituent group attached to the chromophore that **intensifies** the colour and **provides substantivity** (affinity) to the substrate. Common auxochromes: **–OH, –NH₂, –NHR, –NR₂, –SO₃H, –COOH**.

The classical chromophore groups in dyestuff chemistry include:
- **Azo group** (–N=N–)
- **Nitro group** (–NO₂)
- **Carbonyl group** (C=O)
- **Quinoid structure**
- **Phthalocyanine macrocycle**

## 1.5 Key Metrics Used to Characterise Dyestuffs

- **Molecular weight (M):** typically **200 – 1500 g/mol** for organic dyestuffs
- **Wavelength of maximum absorption (λ_max):** measured in **nanometres (nm)**
- **Molar extinction coefficient (ε):** measured in **L·mol⁻¹·cm⁻¹**; high ε = strong colourant
- **Lightfastness (Blue Wool Scale 1–8):** higher number = better
- **Solubility (g/100 g solvent):** critical for ink formulation
- **Hue, saturation, chroma** (CIE L*a*b* coordinates)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Dyestuffs — Multiple Schemes

Dyestuffs are classified by KTU 2024 syllabus along three orthogonal axes:

### 2.1.1 Chemical Structure Classification
1. **Nitro & nitroso dyes** (e.g., Naphthol Yellow S)
2. **Azo dyes** (monoazo, disazo, polyazo) — *the largest class, ~60–70% of all dyes*
3. **Anthraquinone dyes** — excellent lightfastness
4. **Indigoid dyes** (Indigo, Tyrian Purple)
5. **Phthalocyanine dyes** (Cu-phthalocyanine tetrasulphonic acid) — bright blues/greens
6. **Triarylmethane dyes** (Malachite Green, Crystal Violet)
7. **Methine, cyanine, and styryl dyes**
8. **Quinoline, xanthene, acridine, azine, oxazine, thiazine dyes**

### 2.1.2 Application Classification (Most Important for Printing Inks)
- **Acid dyes** — contain –SO₃H or –COOH; applied from acidic solution
- **Direct dyes** — substantive to cellulose (paper, textiles)
- **Basic (cationic) dyes** — carry a positive charge; brilliant hues
- **Disperse dyes** — non-ionic, used for hydrophobic substrates
- **Solvent dyes** — soluble in organic solvents; *the workhorse class for inkjet, flexo, gravure*
- **Reactive dyes** — form covalent bond with substrate
- **Vat dyes** — applied as leuco form, oxidised on fibre
- **Mordant dyes** — require a metal salt (e.g., chrome) to fix
- **Food, drug & cosmetic dyes** — strictly purified

### 2.1.3 Dyeing Method Classification
- **Exhaustion dyes** (move from bath to substrate)
- **Substantive dyes** (affinity-driven)
- **Ingrain dyes** (formed in situ on the substrate)

## 2.2 Solvent Dyes — The Printer's Workhorse

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Highlight:** In printing ink technology, the most relevant class is the **solvent dye**. They are organic, non-ionic (or weakly ionic), and dissolve in organic vehicles like alcohols, esters, ketones, glycol ethers, and mineral spirits. They are the standard colourants for **flexographic, gravure, and inkjet inks**.

| Solvent Dye Family | Common Examples | Typical Hue |
|---|---|---|
| Azo metal complexes | Solvent Yellow 82, Solvent Red 119 | Yellows, Reds, Oranges |
| Anthraquinone | Solvent Blue 36, Solvent Red 52 | Reds, Blues, Violets |
| Phthalocyanine | Solvent Blue 70, Solvent Green 5 | Bright Blues, Greens |
| Quinophthalone | Solvent Yellow 33 | Bright Yellows |
| Methine | Solvent Orange 60 | Oranges |
| Triarylmethane | Basic Violet 1 (if solubilised) | Magentas, Violets |

## 2.3 Properties Required of a Dyestuff in Printing Inks

For a dyestuff to function in a printing ink, it must satisfy **eight critical criteria** — these are the "must-pass" tests in any commercial dyestuff specification:

1. **High solubility** in the chosen vehicle (no crystallisation on storage)
2. **Chemical compatibility** with resins, solvents, and additives
3. **Lightfastness** adequate for the end-use (indoor vs. outdoor)
4. **Heat stability** (especially for heatset gravure, inkjet, drying tunnels)
5. **Resistance to bleeding** with water, alcohol, oils, and plasticisers
6. **Substantivity** to the target substrate
7. **Brilliance and saturation** of hue
8. **Toxicological & regulatory compliance** (e.g., REACH, FDA, RoHS, ISEGA food-contact approval)

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations are the **mathematical backbone** of dyestuff–light interaction, central to understanding colour and fading in printing inks.

| # | Concept | Formula / Equation | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Beer–Lambert Law | $A = \varepsilon \cdot c \cdot \ell$ | $A$ = absorbance (no unit), $\varepsilon$ = molar extinction coefficient (L·mol⁻¹·cm⁻¹), $c$ = concentration (mol/L), $\ell$ = path length (cm) |
| 2 | Transmittance | $T = \dfrac{I}{I_0} = 10^{-A}$ | $I$ = transmitted intensity, $I_0$ = incident intensity |
| 3 | Absorbance from transmittance | $A = -\log_{10} T$ | dimensionless |
| 4 | Energy of absorbed photon | $E = h\nu = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda}$ | $h$ = Planck's constant = **6.626 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s**, $c$ = 3 × 10⁸ m/s, $\lambda$ = wavelength (m) |
| 5 | Wavenumber | $\tilde{\nu} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | measured in cm⁻¹ |
| 6 | CIE Tristimulus values | $X = \int S(\lambda)\,\bar{x}(\lambda)\,d\lambda$, etc. | $S(\lambda)$ = spectrum, $\bar{x}, \bar{y}, \bar{z}$ = matching functions |
| 7 | Hue (CIELab) | $h_{ab} = \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{b^*}{a^*}\right)$ | $a^*, b^*$ = chroma coordinates |
| 8 | Chroma (CIELab) | $C^*_{ab} = \sqrt{a^{*2} + b^{*2}}$ | dimensionless |
| 9 | Total colour difference | $\Delta E^*_{ab} = \sqrt{(\Delta L^*)^2 + (\Delta a^*)^2 + (\Delta b^*)^2}$ | dimensionless |
| 10 | Lightfastness fade rate (simplified) | $\dfrac{dC}{dt} = -k \cdot C^n$ | $C$ = concentration, $k$ = rate constant, $n$ = reaction order (≈1 for dyes) |
| 11 | Quantum yield of fading | $\Phi = \dfrac{\text{moles dye faded}}{\text{moles photons absorbed}}$ | dimensionless |
| 12 | Stokes' solubility equation | $\log S = \dfrac{-\Delta H_{\text{sol}}}{2.303\,R\,T} + \text{const.}$ | $S$ = solubility, $R$ = 8.314 J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹, $T$ = K |
| 13 | Molar mass calculation | $M = \dfrac{m}{n}$ | $m$ = mass (g), $n$ = moles |

> **Note on KTU 2024 expectation:** Equations 1, 3, 8, 9, 11 are the most frequently tested. Equation 4 is essential for understanding why dyes absorb in the visible region (~400–700 nm ⇒ E ≈ 2.8 × 10⁻¹⁹ to 5.0 × 10⁻¹⁹ J).

## 2.5 Why Dyestuffs Matter in Real-World Ink Engineering

- **Inkjet printing** (the highest-volume application): demands **sub-micron filtration**, ultra-pure dyes free of insolubles, and exceptional **thermal and light stability**. Reactive and direct dyes dominate textile inkjet.
- **Flexo & gravure printing** for **packaging**: solvent dyes (especially azo metal complexes) give the depth and brilliance required for **laminated films**.
- **Security printing** (banknotes, passports): specialty dyes with **specific Raman / IR / UV signatures** are formulated into inks.
- **Food packaging**: only ISEGA / FDA-cleared dyes may be used, with strict migration limits (e.g., **< 10 µg/kg** food simulant under EU Regulation 10/2011).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Beer–Lambert Law for a Dye Solution

**Aim:** Derive the relationship between absorbance, concentration, and path length, then apply it to predict the absorbance of a known dye solution used in a flexo ink.

**Step 1 — Setup.** Consider monochromatic light of intensity $I_0$ incident on a dye solution of thickness $dx$ at depth $x$. The fractional decrease in intensity, $dI/I$, is proportional to:
- the **concentration** $c$ of dye molecules,
- the **path element** $dx$,
- a **proportionality constant** $\varepsilon(\lambda)$ — the molar extinction coefficient (wavelength-dependent).

$$
\dfrac{dI}{I} = -\varepsilon(\lambda) \cdot c \cdot dx
$$

**Step 2 — Integrate from 0 to $\ell$.**

$$
\int_{I_0}^{I} \dfrac{dI}{I} = -\varepsilon\,c \int_{0}^{\ell} dx
$$

$$
\ln\!\left(\dfrac{I}{I_0}\right) = -\varepsilon\,c\,\ell
$$

**Step 3 — Convert natural log to base 10.** Since $A = -\log_{10}(I/I_0)$ and $\ln x = 2.303 \log_{10} x$:

$$
A = \varepsilon\,c\,\ell
$$

This is the **Beer–Lambert Law** in its canonical form, the fundamental equation linking dye concentration to optical absorption.

**Step 4 — Worked numerical example (KTU exam style).**

> A flexographic ink contains **0.020 g/L** of Solvent Red 119 (molar mass **488 g/mol**) in ethanol. The ink film path length in the densitometer is **1.0 cm**. The molar extinction coefficient of the dye at λ_max = 520 nm is **23,000 L·mol⁻¹·cm⁻¹**. Calculate the absorbance at λ_max.

**Solution:**

$$
c = \dfrac{0.020 \text{ g/L}}{488 \text{ g/mol}} = 4.10 \times 10^{-5} \text{ mol/L}
$$

$$
A = \varepsilon\,c\,\ell = (23{,}000)(4.10 \times 10^{-5})(1.0)
$$

$$
A = 0.943 \text{ (dimensionless)}
$$

**Interpretation:** Transmittance $T = 10^{-0.943} = 0.114$, i.e., only **~11.4 %** of the incident green light is transmitted — a strong, saturated red ink, exactly as required for flexo packaging.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Photon Energy and Why Dyes Absorb in the Visible

**Step 1 — Energy of a single photon.**

$$
E = h\nu = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda}
$$

**Step 2 — Insert the constants** $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s and $c = 3.00 \times 10^{8}$ m/s.

$$
E = \dfrac{6.626 \times 10^{-34} \times 3.00 \times 10^{8}}{\lambda}
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate at the extremes of visible light.**

For $\lambda = 400$ nm (violet):

$$
E = \dfrac{1.9878 \times 10^{-25}}{4.00 \times 10^{-7}} = 4.97 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J} \approx 3.10 \text{ eV}
$$

For $\lambda = 700$ nm (red):

$$
E = \dfrac{1.9878 \times 10^{-25}}{7.00 \times 10^{-7}} = 2.84 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J} \approx 1.77 \text{ eV}
$$

**Conclusion:** Dyes are coloured because their **π–π*** (or **n–π***) electronic transitions have energies in the range **1.77 – 3.10 eV** — exactly matching the visible photon energy band. Any molecule with extended conjugation absorbing in this range appears coloured.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Choosing a Dyestuff for a Given Printing Process

The KTU 2024 board often tests the ability to **select a dyestuff class for a given ink system**. The following decision matrix is a board-exam goldmine.

| Printing Process | Typical Vehicle | Required Properties | Recommended Dye Class |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Offset lithography** | Mineral oil + resin | Particle-bearing systems (pigments dominate); dyes used in fountain solutions | Acid dyes (in fountain solution) |
| **Flexography** | Alcohol, water, or acetate | Solubility, low bleed, lamination bond | Solvent dyes, azo metal complexes |
| **Gravure** | Toluene, esters, ketones | Deep shade, low residue | Solvent dyes, anthraquinone reds |
| **Inkjet (dye-based)** | Water, glycol, glycol ether | Ultra-pure, thermal stability, no crystallisation | Acid, direct, reactive dyes |
| **Inkjet (pigment-based)** | Water + dispersant | — (dyes not used) | — |
| **Screen printing** | Plastisol or solvent | High build, durability | Pigments (occasional solvent dyes) |
| **Security printing** | Specialty vehicles | Specific spectral signatures | Specialty azo, anthraquinone, polymethine |
| **Textile printing** | Water + thickener | Wash-fastness, fibre affinity | Reactive, direct, acid, disperse dyes |
| **Food packaging** | Specific solvent regulations | ISEGA / FDA approval, low migration | High-purity food-grade dyes |

## 3.4 Worked Example — Lightfastness Comparison

**Problem:** A print made with a dye of lightfastness **Blue Wool 3** loses **40 %** of its optical density in 100 hours of Xenon-arc exposure. A second print with a Blue Wool 6 dye of identical initial density loses only **5 %** in the same test. Comment on the practical implication.

**Step 1 — Convert to log scale.** Blue Wool rating doubles roughly every 2 levels on a fade-rate basis. So Blue Wool 6 ≈ **8×** the lightfastness of Blue Wool 3.

**Step 2 — Calculate fade rates.**

$$
k_3 = \dfrac{-\ln(0.60)}{100} = 5.11 \times 10^{-3} \text{ h}^{-1}
$$

$$
k_6 = \dfrac{-\ln(0.95)}{100} = 5.13 \times 10^{-4} \text{ h}^{-1}
$$

**Step 3 — Ratio.**

$$
\dfrac{k_3}{k_6} = 9.96 \approx 10
$$

**Conclusion:** The Blue Wool 6 dye fades ~**10× slower** than the Blue Wool 3 dye. For outdoor signage or UV-exposed packaging, Blue Wool 6 or higher is mandatory.

## 3.5 Step-by-Step: Resolving the Colour of a Dye in CIELab Space

**Step 1 — Obtain measured tristimulus values** $X, Y, Z$ (e.g., from a spectrophotometer using the D50 illuminant and 2° observer).

**Step 2 — Convert to CIE L*a*b* coordinates.**

$$
L^* = 116\left(\dfrac{Y}{Y_n}\right)^{1/3} - 16
$$

$$
a^* = 500\left[\left(\dfrac{X}{X_n}\right)^{1/3} - \left(\dfrac{Y}{Y_n}\right)^{1/3}\right]
$$

$$
b^* = 200\left[\left(\dfrac{Y}{Y_n}\right)^{1/3} - \left(\dfrac{Z}{Z_n}\right)^{1/3}\right]
$$

where $X_n, Y_n, Z_n$ are tristimulus values of the illuminant (D50: 96.42, 100.00, 82.49).

**Step 3 — Compute chroma and hue.**

$$
C^*_{ab} = \sqrt{a^{*2} + b^{*2}}
$$

$$
h_{ab} = \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{b^*}{a^*}\right)
$$

**Step 4 — Interpret.** A positive $a^*$ and positive $b^*$ ⇒ **orange-red**; both negative ⇒ **blue-green**; positive $a^*$, negative $b^*$ ⇒ **magenta**; negative $a^*$, positive $b^*$ ⇒ **yellow-green**.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Workflow of a Dyestuff in a Printing Ink

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the journey of a dyestuff from chemical synthesis to its function inside a printed substrate. This is a common board-question structure for "explain the role of a dyestuff in printing ink".

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw aromatic precursors: benzene, naphthalene, anthracene] --> B[Chemical synthesis: nitration, sulphonation, diazotisation, coupling]
    B --> C[Purification: recrystallisation, dialysis, ion-exchange]
    C --> D[Quality control: UV-Vis, HPLC, CIElab, lightfastness test]
    D --> E[Dyestuff in pure crystalline or powdered form]
    E --> F[Dissolution in ink vehicle: alcohol, ester, water, glycol]
    F --> G[Ink formulation: dye + resin + additives + solvent]
    G --> H[Printing process: flexo / gravure / inkjet / screen]
    H --> I[Substrate penetration and fixation]
    I --> J[Final printed product: coloured, transparent, fast]
    D -.->|Reject if fails| K[Re-dissolution or waste stream]
    J -.->|Fading / bleed over time| L[End of functional life]
```

## 4.2 Schematic — Classification Hierarchy of Dyestuffs

```mermaid
graph TD
    Root[Dyestuffs in Printing Ink Technology] --> S1[By chemical structure]
    Root --> S2[By application method]
    Root --> S3[By solubility in vehicle]
    S1 --> S11[Azo - 60-70 percent of all dyes]
    S1 --> S12[Anthraquinone]
    S1 --> S13[Phthalocyanine]
    S1 --> S14[Indigoid]
    S1 --> S15[Triarylmethane]
    S1 --> S16[Methine and cyanine]
    S2 --> S21[Acid dyes - SO3H / COOH]
    S2 --> S22[Direct dyes - substantive to cellulose]
    S2 --> S23[Basic dyes - cationic]
    S2 --> S24[Disperse dyes - non-ionic]
    S2 --> S25[Solvent dyes - organic-soluble]
    S2 --> S26[Reactive dyes - covalent bond]
    S3 --> S31[Water-soluble dyes - for aqueous flexo and inkjet]
    S3 --> S32[Solvent-soluble dyes - for gravure, packaging, inkjet]
    S3 --> S33[Oil-soluble dyes - for offset and news inks]
```

## 4.3 Functional Flow — Light Absorption, Transmission, and Print Appearance

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Light as White Light Source
    participant Dye as Dyestuff in Vehicle
    participant Sub as Substrate (paper / film)
    participant Eye as Observer
    Light->>Dye: Incandescent beam I0 strikes dye film
    Dye->>Dye: Dye chromophore absorbs in specific lambda region
    Dye->>Sub: Unabsorbed wavelength components transmit
    Sub->>Sub: Diffuse reflection from substrate backing
    Sub->>Eye: Reflected spectral mix reaches eye
    Eye->>Eye: Brain perceives complementary colour
    Note over Dye: lambda_max determines hue
    Note over Dye: epsilon determines intensity
    Note over Dye: C*ab determines saturation
```

## 4.4 Conceptual Schematic — Chromophore and Auxochrome Interaction

```mermaid
graph LR
    Chrom[Chromophore: conjugated pi-system, e.g. -N=N-] --> Absorb[Absorbs in visible region]
    Auxo[Auxochrome: -OH, -NH2, -NR2, -SO3H, -COOH] --> Intensify[Intensifies colour]
    Auxo --> Substant[Provides substantivity to substrate]
    Chrom --> Batho[Bathochromic shift - longer wavelength]
    Auxo --> Batho
    Chrom --> Hypso[Hypsochromic shift - shorter wavelength]
    Auxo --> Solub[Increases aqueous solubility]
```

## 4.5 Decision Tree — Selecting a Dyestuff Class for an Ink

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Choose dyestuff for ink] --> Q1{Is substrate cellulose paper?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Is water-based vehicle?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> Q3[Use direct or acid dye]
    Q2 -- No --> Q4[Use solvent dye with alcohol vehicle]
    Q1 -- No --> Q5{Is substrate plastic film?}
    Q5 -- Yes --> Q6{Is print lamination required?}
    Q6 -- Yes --> Q7[Use azo metal-complex solvent dye]
    Q6 -- No --> Q8[Use anthraquinone or phthalocyanine solvent dye]
    Q5 -- No --> Q9{Is substrate textile?}
    Q9 -- Cotton --> Q10[Use reactive dye]
    Q9 -- Polyester --> Q11[Use disperse dye]
    Q9 -- Nylon or wool --> Q12[Use acid dye]
    Start --> Reg{Regulatory approval needed?}
    Reg -- Food contact --> Q13[Use only ISEGA or FDA-listed high-purity dye]
    Reg -- Toy or child contact --> Q14[Use EN 71-3 compliant dye]
```

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Differentiate between a dyestuff and a pigment with respect to solubility, particle size, and lightfastness.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Solubility:** A dyestuff is **soluble** in the ink vehicle and forms a true molecular solution, whereas a pigment is **insoluble** and exists as discrete dispersed particles. *(1 mark)*
- **Particle size:** Dyestuff molecules are of the order of **1–2 nm** (single molecule), while pigment particles range from **0.01 µm to 50 µm**. *(1 mark)*
- **Lightfastness:** Pigments generally exhibit **superior lightfastness** (Blue Wool 6–8) compared to dyestuffs (typically Blue Wool 1–5), because pigments do not have accessible molecular excited states that lead to photochemical degradation in the same way. *(1 mark)*

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Define chromophore and auxochrome. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Chromophore:** The part of a dye molecule containing a **conjugated π-electron system** that absorbs visible light and is responsible for the colour. Examples: the **azo group (–N=N–)** in methyl orange, the **carbonyl group (C=O)** in anthraquinone dyes. *(1.5 marks)*
- **Auxochrome:** A substituent group that, when attached to a chromophore, **intensifies the colour** and improves **substantivity** to the substrate. Examples: **–OH, –NH₂, –NHR, –NR₂, –SO₃H, –COOH**. The auxochrome in methyl orange is the **–N(CH₃)₂** group. *(1.5 marks)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each — Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** split as **7 + 7** across two sub-parts, with **internal choice** between (a) and (b) OR between the two main questions.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 2 variant]**

**(a) Classify dyestuffs on the basis of (i) chemical structure and (ii) application method. Give at least two examples in each category. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Classification by chemical structure** *(3.5 marks)*

| Class | Structural Feature | Example Dye | Hue |
|---|---|---|---|
| Azo | One or more –N=N– groups | Methyl Orange, Solvent Red 119 | Yellow–Red |
| Anthraquinone | Anthraquinone nucleus | Solvent Red 52, Alizarin | Red–Violet |
| Phthalocyanine | Macrocyclic Cu complex | Solvent Blue 70, PB 15 derivative | Bright blue–green |
| Indigoid | Indigoid ring system | Indigo, Tyrian Purple | Deep blue / purple |
| Triarylmethane | Three aryl groups on central C | Crystal Violet, Malachite Green | Magenta, green |
| Nitro / Nitroso | –NO₂ / –N=O group | Naphthol Yellow S | Yellow |
| Methine / Cyanine | –CH= conjugated bridge | Solvent Orange 60 | Orange–red |

**(ii) Classification by application method** *(3.5 marks)*

| Class | Application Mechanism | Substrate | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| Acid dye | Anionic, fixes via H-bonds / ionic bonds to amino groups | Wool, silk, nylon, ink | Acid Red 52 |
| Direct dye | Substantive to cellulose via H-bonds / van der Waals | Cotton, paper | Direct Black 22 |
| Basic dye | Cationic, fixes to acidic groups | Acrylic, paper | Basic Violet 1 |
| Solvent dye | Dissolves in organic vehicle | Inks, coatings, plastics | Solvent Yellow 33 |
| Disperse dye | Non-ionic, partitions into hydrophobic fibre | Polyester | Disperse Blue 183 |
| Reactive dye | Covalent bond with –OH / –NH of substrate | Cotton, wool | Reactive Black 5 |
| Vat dye | Reduced to leuco form, then oxidised on fibre | Cotton | Indigo, Vat Blue 1 |

**(b) A flexographic ink contains 0.015 g/L of Solvent Red 119 (M = 488 g/mol) in ethanol. Using a 1.0 cm path length and ε = 23,000 L·mol⁻¹·cm⁻¹, calculate (i) the absorbance at λ_max, (ii) the percent transmittance, and (iii) the chroma C*ab if a\* = 55.0 and b\* = 38.2. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Absorbance** *(2.5 marks)*

$$
c = \dfrac{m}{M \cdot V} = \dfrac{0.015 \text{ g}}{488 \text{ g/mol} \times 1 \text{ L}} = 3.07 \times 10^{-5} \text{ mol/L}
$$

$$
A = \varepsilon \, c \, \ell = (23{,}000)(3.07 \times 10^{-5})(1.0) = 0.707
$$

**(ii) Percent transmittance** *(2 marks)*

$$
T = 10^{-A} = 10^{-0.707} = 0.1963
$$

$$
\%T = 19.63 \text{ %}
$$

**(iii) Chroma C\*ab** *(2.5 marks)*

$$
C^*_{ab} = \sqrt{a^{*2} + b^{*2}} = \sqrt{55.0^{2} + 38.2^{2}} = \sqrt{3025 + 1459.24} = \sqrt{4484.24}
$$

$$
C^*_{ab} = 66.96 \text{ (dimensionless, highly saturated red)}
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2 variant]**

**(a) Explain the chromophore–auxochrome theory with suitable examples. Discuss how the structure of a dye affects its hue, substantivity, and lightfastness. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

- **Chromophore:** The chromophore is a covalently unsaturated group (e.g., azo –N=N–, nitro –NO₂, carbonyl C=O, or quinoid) that is responsible for the selective absorption of visible light. For example, the azo group in methyl orange absorbs in the blue-green region (~460 nm), making the dye appear **orange-red**. *(1.5 marks)*

- **Auxochrome:** Auxochromes are colour-enhancing groups such as –OH, –NH₂, –NR₂, –SO₃H, –COOH. They do not impart colour on their own but **deepen the colour** of a chromophore by donating lone-pair electrons into the π-system (extending conjugation) and they **provide substantivity** to the substrate. For example, the dimethylamino group in methyl orange shifts the absorption bathochromically and provides substantivity to protein fibres. *(1.5 marks)*

- **Effect on hue:** Extending conjugation (more double bonds, fused rings) shifts λ_max to longer wavelengths — a **bathochromic (red) shift**. Electron-donating auxochromes produce bathochromic shifts, electron-withdrawing groups may cause **hypsochromic (blue) shifts**. Example: adding –N(CH₃)₂ to an azo system moves the hue from yellow → red. *(1.5 marks)*

- **Effect on substantivity:** Groups like –SO₃H (anionic) and –NR₂ (cationic) allow ionic interaction with charged sites on the substrate, conferring substantivity. H-bonding groups (–OH, –NH₂) improve affinity to cellulose. *(1 mark)*

- **Effect on lightfastness:** Stronger intramolecular H-bonding, larger aromatic systems, and **metal complexation** (e.g., Cu, Cr, Co) raise lightfastness by deactivating the excited state and dissipating energy as heat. Azo metal complexes (e.g., Solvent Yellow 82) are far more lightfast than uncomplexed azo dyes. *(1.5 marks)*

**(b) (i) State and explain the Beer–Lambert law. (ii) A gravure ink uses 0.050 g/L of a dye with ε = 18,500 L·mol⁻¹·cm⁻¹ at λ_max = 640 nm, M = 612 g/mol, path length 0.5 cm. Compute the absorbance and the photon energy absorbed at this λ_max. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Beer–Lambert Law statement** *(3 marks)*

The Beer–Lambert law states that the absorbance $A$ of a coloured solution is **directly proportional** to the **concentration** $c$ of the absorbing species and to the **path length** $\ell$ of the light through the solution:

$$
A = \varepsilon \, c \, \ell
$$

where $\varepsilon$ is the molar extinction coefficient (L·mol⁻¹·cm⁻¹), a property of the dye at a given wavelength. The law holds for **dilute, non-scattering, molecularly dissolved** colourants — exactly the condition for a dyestuff in a gravure ink vehicle. Deviations occur at high concentrations due to dye aggregation.

**(ii) Calculation** *(4 marks)*

$$
c = \dfrac{0.050 \text{ g/L}}{612 \text{ g/mol}} = 8.17 \times 10^{-5} \text{ mol/L}
$$

$$
A = \varepsilon \, c \, \ell = (18{,}500)(8.17 \times 10^{-5})(0.5) = 0.756
$$

$$
E = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda} = \dfrac{(6.626 \times 10^{-34})(3.00 \times 10^{8})}{640 \times 10^{-9}}
$$

$$
E = \dfrac{1.9878 \times 10^{-25}}{6.40 \times 10^{-7}} = 3.11 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J} = 1.94 \text{ eV}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing dyestuff and dye** — both are acceptable, but mixing "pigment" terminology (e.g., "pigment particle size") in a dyestuff answer costs marks. Use **"molecular"** for dyestuffs and **"particulate"** for pigments.
> 2. **Forgetting units in Beer–Lambert calculations** — always state $c$ in **mol/L** and $\ell$ in **cm**, so that $\varepsilon$ in L·mol⁻¹·cm⁻¹ gives a **dimensionless** absorbance.
> 3. **Writing "solvent dye" instead of "solvent-soluble dye"** — these are accepted as synonyms, but write the full form once to be safe.
> 4. **Skipping the application example** — for "azo metal complexes," KTU examiners expect at least one named example (e.g., **Solvent Yellow 82** or **Solvent Red 119**).
> 5. **Using the vertical bar in tables** — in handwritten scripts, mark "as needed" with simple arrows, not $\vert$ symbols, in tables (this is a LaTeX-vs-handwritten cue).
> 6. **Not distinguishing λ from λ_max** — λ_max is the wavelength of **maximum** absorption; any other wavelength gives a lower ε.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> **Important things to remember for KTU PEPOT866 — Module 2 — Dyestuffs**

- **Dyestuff** = soluble colourant (molecular dispersion, ~1–2 nm), **Pigment** = insoluble colourant (particulate, 0.01–50 µm).
- The two defining properties of a dyestuff are: (1) **solubility in the vehicle**, and (2) **affinity (substantivity) to the substrate**.
- **Chromophore** = colour-bearing group (azo, anthraquinone, phthalocyanine, indigoid, nitro, carbonyl).
- **Auxochrome** = –OH, –NH₂, –NR₂, –SO₃H, –COOH; **intensifies** colour, **adds substantivity**.
- **Bathochromic shift** = absorption moves to longer λ (red shift); **Hypsochromic shift** = shorter λ (blue shift).
- **Solvent dyes** are the **workhorse** class for flexo, gravure, and inkjet inks.
- The **largest class of dyes** is the **azo class** (~60–70 % of all commercial dyes).
- **Phthalocyanine dyes** give the brightest blues and greens; their Cu complex is lightfast.
- **Anthraquinone dyes** are preferred for **deep reds and violets** with high lightfastness.
- **Reactive dyes** form a **covalent bond** with the substrate — the most wash-fast class.
- **Acid dyes** carry –SO₃H / –COOH; **Basic dyes** are cationic; **Direct dyes** are substantive to cellulose.
- **Beer–Lambert Law**: $A = \varepsilon c \ell$ — foundation of quantitative dye analysis.
- **Visible photon energy range**: **1.77 eV (red, 700 nm) to 3.10 eV (violet, 400 nm)**.
- **CIELab coordinates** $(L^*, a^*, b^*)$ describe the **lightness, green–red, and blue–yellow** components of a colour.
- **Chroma** $C^*_{ab} = \sqrt{a^{*2} + b^{*2}}$ — higher chroma = more saturated.
- **Total colour difference** $\Delta E^*_{ab}$ — values <1 are imperceptible, <3 acceptable for most printing.
- **Blue Wool Scale 1–8**: higher number = better lightfastness; outdoor prints need **≥ 6**.
- **Regulatory bodies** for ink dyes: **FDA, EFSA, ISEGA, REACH, RoHS, EN 71-3** (toys).
- For **food packaging inks**, only high-purity dyes with documented **migration < 10 µg/kg** are permitted under EU 10/2011.
- **C.I. (Colour Index)** Generic Names uniquely identify a dyestuff by class and hue, e.g., **Solvent Red 119**, **Acid Yellow 23**, **Direct Blue 86**.
- A dyestuff's **lightfastness is ~10× higher for every two Blue Wool scale steps**.
- In **inkjet inks**, dyestuff must be **sub-micron filtered** (typically 0.2 µm) to prevent nozzle clogging.
- The **quantum yield of fading** $\Phi$ is a key metric for predicting print longevity.

---

**End of Module 2 — Dyestuffs Notes | KTU 2024 Scheme | PEPOT866**
