topics.title: Oils
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPOT866 - TECHNOLOGY OF PRINTING INKS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Oils # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the discipline of **Printing Ink Technology**, **Oils** constitute the foundational liquid vehicle (binder carrier) for traditional paste inks—particularly **oil-based lithographic (offset), letterpress, and gravure inks**. Chemically, drying oils are **triglycerides** (tri-esters of glycerol with long-chain fatty acids) derived from vegetable seeds, nuts, or modified petroleum sources, which undergo oxidative polymerization to form a tough, dry, solid ink film on the substrate. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Definition (Syllabus-Aligned):** > *Oils used in printing inks are primarily drying and semi-drying vegetable oils, refined and processed to specific viscosities and molecular weights, whose function is to (a) disperse and wet pigments, (b) provide flow and transfer properties on the press, and (c) dry to a hard, glossy, flexible film via oxidative cross-linking.* ## 1.2 Intuitive Overview (Real-World Analogy) Imagine you are painting a wooden fence with a brush: - The **pigment** is the coloured powder that gives the paint its appearance (the visible identity). - The **oil** is the wet liquid that lets you spread the pigment smoothly across the wood, holds it in place while wet, and then slowly hardens into a permanent skin that resists rain and sunlight. Without the oil, the pigment would be mere dust. Without the pigment, the oil would be a useless transparent film. Together, they form an **ink**—and the oil's job is to be a *temporary liquid worker* that *sacrifices its fluidity* to become a *permanent solid protector* of the colour. > [!IMPORTANT] > **The "Personality Flip" of an Oil:** > An oil's central miracle is that it begins life as a *viscous liquid* (allowing it to be printed at high speed) and ends life as a *rigid solid film* (giving permanence to the print). This transformation is driven by **oxygen from the air**, which is why such oils are called **drying oils**—not because water evaporates, but because oxygen is chemically absorbed and incorporated into the oil film. ## 1.3 Classification of Oils Used in Printing Inks | Category | Examples | Drying Behavior | Typical Use in Inks | |----------|----------|-----------------|---------------------| | **Drying Oils** | Linseed oil, Tung oil (China wood oil), Dehydrated Castor Oil (DCO), Oiticica oil | Absorb oxygen, polymerize, form hard films | Lithographic, letterpress, oil-based varnishes | | **Semi-Drying Oils** | Soybean oil, Safflower oil, Sunflower oil | Slowly polymerize; remain soft/tacky | Heat-set web offset, modifying varnishes | | **Non-Drying Oils** | Mineral oil (white spirit), Coconut oil, Palm oil | Do not polymerize by oxidation | News ink vehicles, paste ink plasticizers | | **Modified/Processed Oils** | Stand oil, Boiled oil, Blown oil, Lithographic varnish | Pre-polymerized to target viscosity | Direct ink vehicles (rarely used alone) | > [!NOTE] > **Standard Industry Metric (KTU Board-Relevant Constants):** > - **Iodine Value (IV) – Linseed oil:** 170–190 g I₂/100 g > - **Iodine Value – Tung oil:** 160–170 g I₂/100 g > - **Iodine Value – Soybean oil:** 125–140 g I₂/100 g > - **Acid Value (AV) – Refined oil:** < 5 mg KOH/g > - **Saponification Value (SV) – Linseed oil:** 188–196 mg KOH/g # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Chemical Architecture of a Drying Oil A drying oil is a **triglyceride**: one glycerol molecule esterified with **three fatty acid chains** (R₁, R₂, R₃). The unsaturation (C=C double bonds) in these fatty acid chains is what enables drying. **General triglyceride structure:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Triglyceride} &= \text{Glycerol} + 3 \times \text{Fatty Acid (FA)} \\[4pt] \text{Structure} &= \text{CH}_2\text{OOC–R}_1 \\ &\quad \text{CHOOC–R}_2 \\ &\quad \text{CH}_2\text{OOC–R}_3 \end{aligned} $$ **Key unsaturated fatty acids in printing oils:** | Fatty Acid | Symbol | Double Bonds | Source Oil | |------------|--------|--------------|------------| | Linolenic acid | C18:3 (α) | 3 conjugated/isolated | Linseed | | Linoleic acid | C18:2 | 2 | Soybean, Safflower | | Eleostearic acid | C18:3 (α-eleo) | 3 **conjugated** | Tung | | Licanic acid | C18:3 keto | 3 conjugated | Oiticica | | Ricinoleic acid | C18:1 (OH) | 1 (with –OH) | Castor | | Oleic acid | C18:1 | 1 | Olive, all oils | > [!IMPORTANT] > **The "Conjugation Rule" (Exam Favorite):** > *Conjugated* double bonds (alternating C=C–C=C, as in tung oil's eleostearic acid) dry **10–20 times faster** than *isolated* double bonds (separated by –CH₂–, as in linseed oil's linolenic acid). This is why tung oil is the "fastest drying" vegetable oil in the ink industry. ## 2.2 The Drying Mechanism — Stepwise Logic Drying of an oil is **NOT** evaporation. It is **oxidative polymerization** in three stages: 1. **Induction Period:** Oxygen dissolves into the oil film; antioxidants (natural tocopherols) are consumed. The film remains fluid. 2. **Peroxide Formation:** O₂ attacks the α-carbon (adjacent to C=C) creating **hydroperoxides** (–OOH). 3. **Cross-linking:** Hydroperoxides decompose into free radicals (R•, RO•), which initiate **radical polymerization**, linking fatty acid chains into a 3-D network — the dry film. > [!NOTE] > **Why Driers (Siccatives) Are Added:** > Cobalt, Manganese, and Lead salts of naphthenic/linoleic acids act as **catalysts** that shorten the induction period and accelerate peroxide decomposition. Without driers, linseed oil may take 4–7 days to dry; with 0.5% cobalt, it dries in **6–12 hours**. ## 2.3 Processing of Oils (KTU Board-Relevant) | Process | Purpose | Operating Conditions | |---------|---------|----------------------| | **Alkali Refining** | Remove free fatty acids, gums, phospholipids | NaOH treatment, 60–80 °C | | **Bleaching** | Remove pigments, oxidation products | Acid-activated earth (Fuller's earth), 90–110 °C, vacuum | | **Deodorization** | Remove volatile odoriferous compounds | Steam stripping, 200–250 °C, high vacuum | | **Heat-Bodying (Stand Oil)** | Increase viscosity, MW, drying rate | 280–320 °C under inert gas (CO₂/N₂) for several hours | | **Air-Blowing** | Oxidatively polymerize to high viscosity | 100–150 °C with air sparging → blown oil | | **Cooking with Resin** | Produce oleoresinous varnish | Oil + phenolic/rosin/alkyd resin, 250–290 °C | ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | Parameter | Formula | Units | Significance | |-----------|---------|-------|--------------| | **Acid Value (AV)** | $\text{AV} = \dfrac{V_{\text{KOH}} \times N_{\text{KOH}} \times 56.1}{m_{\text{oil}}}$ | mg KOH/g oil | Free fatty acid content; refining efficiency | | **Saponification Value (SV)** | $\text{SV} = \dfrac{V_{\text{KOH}} \times N_{\text{KOH}} \times 56.1}{m_{\text{oil}}}$ | mg KOH/g oil | Average molecular weight of oil | | **Iodine Value (IV)** | $\text{IV} = \dfrac{(V_b - V_s) \times N_{\text{Na}_2\text{S}_2\text{O}_3} \times 12.69}{m_{\text{oil}}}$ | g I₂ / 100 g oil | Degree of unsaturation → drying tendency | | **Average Molecular Weight of Oil** | $M_{\text{oil}} = \dfrac{3 \times 56.1 \times 1000}{\text{SV}}$ | g/mol | Useful in resin-oil stoichiometry | | **Drying Index (qualitative)** | Higher IV + Conjugation → Faster drying | dimensionless | Empirical oil selection rule | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Pitfall:** Acid Value and Saponification Value use the *same numerical formula* but different sample treatments—**AV** uses direct titration of oil in neutral solvent; **SV** uses **back-titration after KOH reflux** with HCl. Examiners frequently award partial marks here. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked-Out Derivation 1: Calculating Iodine Value **Problem Statement:** 0.500 g of refined linseed oil is treated with 25.00 mL of 0.1 N Wijs solution (ICl in acetic acid). After 1 hour in the dark, 10.00 mL of 0.1 N Na₂S₂O₃ is required for back-titration (starch indicator). A blank titration (no oil) consumes 25.00 mL of 0.1 N Na₂S₂O₃. Calculate the Iodine Value. ### Step 1: Write the Reaction Scheme $$ \begin{aligned} \text{C=C (in oil)} + \text{ICl} &\rightarrow \text{C–I} \; \text{C–Cl} \quad \text{(addition reaction)} \\[4pt] \text{ICl} + 2 \, \text{Na}_2\text{S}_2\text{O}_3 &\rightarrow \text{NaCl} + \text{NaI} + \text{Na}_2\text{S}_4\text{O}_6 \quad \text{(back-titration)} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 2: Establish the Titration Logic The **excess ICl** (unreacted with oil) reacts with Na₂S₂O₃. Therefore: $$ V_{\text{blank}} - V_{\text{sample}} = V_{\text{ICl consumed by oil}} $$ ### Step 3: Substitute the Numerical Values $$ \begin{aligned} V_{\text{blank}} &= 25.00 \; \text{mL} \\ V_{\text{sample}} &= 10.00 \; \text{mL} \\ V_{\text{consumed by oil}} &= 25.00 - 10.00 = 15.00 \; \text{mL} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 4: Apply the Iodine Value Formula $$ \text{IV} = \dfrac{(V_b - V_s) \times N \times 12.69}{m_{\text{oil}}} $$ $$ \text{IV} = \dfrac{(25.00 - 10.00) \times 0.1 \times 12.69}{0.500} $$ ### Step 5: Compute the Final Result $$ \begin{aligned} \text{IV} &= \dfrac{15.00 \times 0.1 \times 12.69}{0.500} \\[4pt] &= \dfrac{15.00 \times 1.269}{0.500} \\[4pt] &= \dfrac{19.035}{0.500} \\[4pt] &= 38.07 \; \text{g I}_2 / 100 \; \text{g oil} \end{aligned} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Numerical Sanity Check:** Real linseed oil has IV ≈ 170–190. A value of 38 indicates the **sample weight / normality assumption** in the problem is illustrative; in KTU board problems, the *formula and unit handling* carry full credit, not the absolute numerical magnitude. Always show the 12.69 factor (= 126.9/10, where 126.9 is the atomic weight of iodine). ## 3.2 Worked-Out Derivation 2: Average Molecular Weight from Saponification Value **Given:** SV of an oil = 190 mg KOH/g oil. **Logic:** 1 mole of triglyceride (M grams) requires 3 moles of KOH (= 3 × 56.1 g). So 1 g of KOH corresponds to M/(3 × 56.1) grams of oil. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{SV} &= \dfrac{3 \times 56.1 \times 1000}{M_{\text{oil}}} \\[4pt] M_{\text{oil}} &= \dfrac{3 \times 56.1 \times 1000}{\text{SV}} \\[4pt] M_{\text{oil}} &= \dfrac{3 \times 56.1 \times 1000}{190} \\[4pt] M_{\text{oil}} &= \dfrac{168300}{190} \\[4pt] M_{\text{oil}} &\approx 885.8 \; \text{g/mol} \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > **KTU Board Insight:** Pure trilinolein has M = 885.4 g/mol, which matches a SV of ~190. This is why linseed oil (rich in trilinolenin/linolein) shows SV in the 188–196 range. ## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Selecting an Oil for an Ink | Property Required | Best Oil Choice | Justification | |-------------------|-----------------|---------------| | Fastest drying | **Tung oil** | 3 conjugated double bonds (eleostearic acid) | | Pale, non-yellowing film | **Soybean / Safflower oil** | Low linolenic acid, slower yellowing | | Maximum hardness | **Linseed + Tung blend** | Linseed for body, Tung for crosslink density | | Heat-set web offset | **High IV soybean oil + petroleum resin** | Controlled MW, good solvency for resin | | Plasticizing (no drying) | **Mineral oil / Coconut oil** | Non-drying, stays flexible | | Letterpress news ink | **Mineral oil + carbon black** | Penetrates newsprint, no oxidation needed | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram: Classification & Flow of Oils in Ink Manufacturing ```mermaid flowchart TD rawMat["Raw Oil Source
Vegetable Seeds Nuts Petroleum"]:::raw refine["Refining Stage
Degumming Alkali Bleaching"]:::proc mod["Modification Stage
Heat Bodying Air Blowing"]:::proc blend["Oil Selection
Drying Semi Drying Non Drying"]:::branch tung["Tung Oil
Eleostearic Acid 3 Conjugated DB"]:::dryOil lin["Linseed Oil
Linolenic Acid 3 Isolated DB"]:::dryOil soy["Soybean Oil
Linoleic Acid 2 DB"]:::semiOil min["Mineral Oil
Paraffinic Naphthenic"]:::nonOil var["Varnish Cooking
Oil plus Resin 250 to 290 C"]:::proc ink["Final Printing Ink
Oil plus Pigment plus Additives"]:::final film["Dry Ink Film
Oxidative Polymerization"]:::final rawMat --> refine refine --> mod mod --> blend blend --> tung blend --> lin blend --> soy blend --> min tung --> var lin --> var soy --> var min --> ink var --> ink ink --> film classDef raw fill:#fff4e6,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000 classDef proc fill:#e6f3ff,stroke:#0066cc,color:#000 classDef branch fill:#f0f0f0,stroke:#666666,color:#000 classDef dryOil fill:#ffe0e0,stroke:#cc0000,color:#000 classDef semiOil fill:#fffacc,stroke:#999900,color:#000 classDef nonOil fill:#e0ffe0,stroke:#006600,color:#000 classDef final fill:#d9d2e9,stroke:#330099,color:#000 ``` ## 4.2 Schematic: Oxidative Drying Mechanism of Linseed Oil ```mermaid flowchart LR liq["Liquid Oil Film
Triglyceride on Paper"]:::liq ind["Induction Period
O2 Dissolves Antioxidants Consumed"]:::step hyd["Hydroperoxide Formation
RH plus O2 to ROOH"]:::step rad["Radical Generation
ROOH to RO plus OH radicals"]:::step cross["Cross linking
C C bonds form 3D Network"]:::step dry["Solid Dry Film
Glossy Tough Flexible"]:::dry liq --> ind --> hyd --> rad --> cross --> dry classDef liq fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000 classDef step fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#cc9900,color:#000 classDef dry fill:#d5a6bd,stroke:#660033,color:#000 ``` ## 4.3 Block Diagram: Oil Processing Routes (Functional Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart TD cr["Crude Vegetable Oil"]:::input dG["Degumming
Water or Acid Treatment"]:::unit dN["Deacidification
Alkali Neutralization"]:::unit dB["Bleaching
Activated Earth"]:::unit dD["Deodorization
Steam Stripping"]:::unit rb["Refined Oil
Low AV Pale Color"]:::output hB["Heat Bodying
280 to 320 C N2 Atmosphere"]:::unit aB["Air Blowing
100 to 150 C O2 Sparging"]:::unit cook["Varnish Cooking
Oil plus Resin at 290 C"]:::unit sO["Stand Oil
High Viscosity"]:::product bO["Blown Oil
High MW Oxidized"]:::product vR["Oleoresinous Varnish
Binder for Ink"]:::product cr --> dG --> dN --> dB --> dD --> rb rb --> hB --> sO rb --> aB --> bO rb --> cook --> vR classDef input fill:#fce4d6,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000 classDef unit fill:#deebf7,stroke:#2e75b6,color:#000 classDef output fill:#e2efda,stroke:#548235,color:#000 classDef product fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#bf9000,color:#000 ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper Pattern] **"Distinguish between drying oils, semi-drying oils and non-drying oils. Give two examples of each, with reference to their use in printing inks."** **Model Answer (3 marks allocation):** - **Drying oils (1 mark):** Oils that absorb oxygen from air and polymerize to form a hard, tough, dry film. Examples: *Linseed oil, Tung oil*. Used in lithographic and letterpress inks as primary film-forming vehicles. - **Semi-drying oils (1 mark):** Oils that polymerize slowly, giving soft, tacky films. Examples: *Soybean oil, Safflower oil*. Used in heat-set web offset inks. - **Non-drying oils (1 mark):** Oils that do not polymerize by oxidation and remain permanently fluid. Examples: *Mineral oil, Coconut oil*. Used in news inks and as plasticizers in paste inks. ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper Pattern] **"Define Acid Value and Saponification Value. Why is the determination of Acid Value important for printing ink oils?"** **Model Answer (3 marks allocation):** - **Acid Value (1 mark):** The number of milligrams of KOH required to neutralize the free fatty acids present in 1 gram of oil. Formula: $\text{AV} = \dfrac{V \times N \times 56.1}{m}$. - **Saponification Value (1 mark):** The number of milligrams of KOH required to saponify the triglycerides (esters) in 1 gram of oil. - **Importance of AV (1 mark):** High acid value indicates *poor refining* and presence of free fatty acids, which (a) react with basic pigments causing **saponification & viscosity drift**, (b) reduce gloss & water resistance in lithographic inks, and (c) impair pigment wetting. Therefore, refined printing ink oils must have **AV < 5 mg KOH/g**. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) — Option for Module 2 Topic **(a)** Explain in detail the **chemical composition of drying oils** used in printing inks, with emphasis on triglyceride structure and the role of unsaturated fatty acids. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Describe the **oxidative polymerization mechanism** by which drying oils harden into a dry ink film. Discuss the role of **driers (siccatives)** in accelerating this process. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution (a): 1. **Triglyceride Backbone (2 marks):** Drying oils are tri-esters of glycerol (a trihydric alcohol) with three long-chain (C16–C18) fatty acids. The general formula is: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{CH}_2\text{OOC–R}_1 \\ \text{CHOOC–R}_2} \\ \text{CH}_2\text{OOC–R}_3 \end{aligned} $$ 2. **Unsaturated Fatty Acids (3 marks):** The drying ability is governed by the **degree and position of unsaturation (C=C)**. Common drying-oil fatty acids include: - Linolenic acid (C18:3, 3 isolated DB) — Linseed oil - Eleostearic acid (C18:3, 3 *conjugated* DB) — Tung oil - Linoleic acid (C18:2, 2 DB) — Soybean oil - Ricinoleic acid (C18:1, with –OH) — Castor oil 3. **Role of Conjugation (2 marks):** Conjugated systems react with oxygen **much faster** than isolated systems because the resulting radical is stabilized by resonance over alternating double bonds. #### Model Solution (b): 1. **Three-Stage Drying Mechanism (4 marks):** - **Induction:** O₂ diffuses; natural antioxidants consumed. - **Peroxide formation:** O₂ attacks α-methylene → hydroperoxide (–OOH). - **Cross-linking:** Hydroperoxides decompose → free radicals → radical polymerization → 3-D network. 2. **Role of Driers / Siccatives (3 marks):** - Driers are **metal soaps** — Co, Mn, Pb naphthenates or octoates. - Cobalt is a powerful **surface drier** (acts at film-air interface). - Manganese and Lead are **through-driers** (act in the bulk). - Typical dosage: **0.02–0.5% metal by weight of oil**. - They shorten the induction period by catalyzing hydroperoxide decomposition. ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **(a)** Compare the properties and ink applications of **Linseed oil, Tung oil, and Soybean oil**. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Describe the **heat-bodying and air-blowing processes** for modifying oils. How does modification improve ink vehicle performance? **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution (a): | Property | Linseed Oil | Tung Oil | Soybean Oil | |----------|------------|----------|-------------| | Main fatty acid | Linolenic (C18:3 isolated) | Eleostearic (C18:3 conjugated) | Linoleic (C18:2) | | Iodine value | 170–190 | 160–170 | 125–140 | | Drying speed | Medium–Fast | Very Fast (forms gel if heated alone) | Slow | | Yellowing | High (darkens on aging) | Low–Medium | Lowest | | Film hardness | Hard, glossy | Extremely hard, water-resistant | Soft, flexible | | Application | Lithographic inks, varnishes, alkyd resins | Fast-drying varnishes, overprint varnishes | Heat-set web offset, pale inks | *(7 marks: tabular data + 2–3 sentences explanation)* #### Model Solution (b): 1. **Heat-Bodying (Stand Oil Process) (3.5 marks):** - Oil heated to **280–320 °C** under **inert atmosphere (CO₂ or N₂)** for 8–24 hours. - Causes **thermal polymerization** (Diels-Alder between conjugated dienes). - Viscosity rises from ~50 cP (raw) to **1000–5000 cP** (thick stand oil). - Produces **brighter, faster-drying, non-yellowing** oil of higher MW. 2. **Air-Blowing (3.5 marks):** - Oil heated to **100–150 °C** with **air sparged** through it. - Causes **oxidative polymerization** with O₂ incorporated into the chain. - Produces **blown oil** with high viscosity, short body, and pre-formed peroxides. - Blown oils dry *faster* but give darker films than stand oils. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:** > > 1. **Conflating "drying" with "evaporation":** Mineral oil evaporates from newsprint — this is **NOT** drying. Drying = oxidative polymerization. > 2. **Confusing Acid Value with Saponification Value:** AV = free FA only; SV = total ester + free FA. Many students write "AV determines drying" — it does **NOT**; **IV** determines drying tendency. > 3. **Wrong constant for IV:** The factor is **12.69** (not 126.9). Forgetting to divide iodine's atomic weight (126.9) by 10 is the most common numerical error. > 4. **Forgetting driers:** Students describe the drying mechanism but omit **catalysts** (Co/Mn/Pb). Examiners deduct 2–3 marks. > 5. **Confusing heat-bodied and blown oils:** Heat-bodied = thermal, no O₂; blown = oxidative, with O₂. They are **chemically opposite** processes. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - 🔹 **Oils are vehicles**, not pigments — their job is dispersion + film formation. - 🔹 **Drying = oxidative polymerization**, NOT evaporation. - 🔹 **Three oil classes:** Drying (linseed, tung), Semi-drying (soybean), Non-drying (mineral). - 🔹 **Conjugation accelerates drying** — tung > linseed > soybean. - 🔹 **Iodine Value (IV)** measures unsaturation; **higher IV → faster drying**. - 🔹 **Acid Value (AV)** measures free fatty acids; **must be < 5** for ink-grade oil. - 🔹 **Saponification Value (SV)** = 168300 / M_oil; inversely related to molecular weight. - 🔹 **Standard IV constants:** Linseed 170–190, Tung 160–170, Soybean 125–140. - 🔹 **Heat-bodying** = thermal polymerization in N₂ → **stand oil** (pale, fast drying). - 🔹 **Air-blowing** = oxidative polymerization with O₂ → **blown oil** (dark, fast drying). - 🔹 **Driers (siccatives):** Co (surface) + Mn/Pb (through) metal soaps at 0.02–0.5%. - 🔹 **Drying stages:** Induction → Hydroperoxide → Radical → Cross-link → Solid film. - 🔹 **Triglyceride structure:** Glycerol + 3 fatty acids = the universal oil backbone. - 🔹 **Linolenic (C18:3 isolated)** in linseed; **Eleostearic (C18:3 conjugated)** in tung. - 🔹 **Oleoresinous varnish** = oil cooked with resin (phenolic/rosin/alkyd) at ~290 °C — the principal binder of offset ink. - 🔹 **KTU numerical factor to memorize:** $\text{IV factor} = 12.69$ (= 126.9/10). - 🔹 **Mineral oil in news ink** dries by absorption into newsprint — a *physical* not *chemical* drying.

topics.title: Solvents
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT866 - TECHNOLOGY OF PRINTING INKS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Solvents

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Solvents** in printing ink technology are defined as **volatile organic or aqueous liquids** whose primary function is to **dissolve or disperse the resin binder**, reduce the viscosity of the ink system to a printable consistency, and subsequently **evaporate during the drying stage** of the printing process, leaving behind the solid ink film on the substrate.

In the context of printing inks, a solvent is not merely a "thinner" — it is an **active rheological and film-forming participant**. It must:
1. Solubilize the chosen resin (binder) completely or partially.
2. Wet and disperse the pigment uniformly.
3. Provide correct viscosity and tack for the chosen press.
4. Evaporate at a rate compatible with press speed and drying tunnel capacity.
5. Be chemically inert toward pigments, additives, and the substrate.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy — The Water in Paint:**
> Think of a solvent like the **water in a wet sandcastle**. While the sandcastle is being built (ink being printed on the substrate), water holds the grains of sand (pigment + resin particles) together in a workable, flowing form. Once the tide recedes (the drying oven or air-drying stage), the water evaporates, and only the bound sand structure (the dried ink film) remains. Change the water to oil or honey, and your building experience changes dramatically — the same principle applies when ink chemists swap one solvent for another.

## 1.3 Classification Overview (Syllabus Highlight)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Module 2 — Solvent Classification Map**

| Family | Example Solvents | Typical Ink System |
|---|---|---|
| **Aliphatic hydrocarbons** | Mineral spirit (white spirit), heptane | News ink, heatset web offset |
| **Aromatic hydrocarbons** | Toluene, xylene | Publication gravure |
| **Alcohols** | Ethanol, isopropanol (IPA), n-butanol | Flexo, packaging gravure |
| **Esters** | Ethyl acetate (EtOAc), butyl acetate | Flexo, lamination inks |
| **Ketones** | Methyl ethyl ketone (MEK), acetone | Industrial gravure, vinyl inks |
| **Glycol ethers** | Butyl glycol, methoxy propanol | Screen inks, water-based flexo co-solvent |
| **Glycol esters** | Ethylene glycol monoacetate | Slow-evaporating flexo |
| **Water** | Deionized water | Water-based flexo, inkjet |

## 1.4 Key Physical Constants & Metrics (Highlighted)

- **Standard Evaporation Reference**: **n-Butyl acetate = 1.0** (or in some references, **diethyl ether = 1.0**).
- **Kauri-Butanol (Kb) Value**: Numerical measure of **solvency power** — higher Kb ⇒ stronger solvent.
- **Aniline Point (°C)**: Lower aniline point ⇒ higher aromaticity and solvency.
- **Boiling Range (°C)**: Determines volatility class.
- **Flash Point (°C)**: Critical for **transport, storage, and press-room safety**.
- **Hildebrand Solubility Parameter (δ, MPa^½)**: Predicts resin–solvent compatibility.
- **Surface Tension (mN/m)**: Governs **wetting** of substrate and pigment.
- **Vapor Pressure (mmHg at 20 °C)**: Governs **evaporation tendency**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Role of a Solvent in an Ink System — Step-by-Step Logic

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Five-Stage Journey" of a Solvent in Printing**

1. **Stage 1 — Dissolution / Dispersion Phase**:
   The solvent molecules surround resin chains, breaking intermolecular forces (van der Waals, hydrogen bonds). This produces a homogeneous resin solution. **Why it matters:** Without this, the ink will be heterogeneous, leading to gloss mottle and poor print fidelity.

2. **Stage 2 — Pigment Wetting Phase**:
   Solvent reduces the interfacial tension between pigment agglomerates and the binder solution. **How:** Lower surface tension of the solvent allows capillary penetration into pigment agglomerates.

3. **Stage 3 — Viscosity / Rheology Control Phase**:
   By dissolving more or less resin, and by plasticizing action, the solvent controls **viscosity, tack, and flow**. Ink transfer from the fountain to the substrate depends critically on this.

4. **Stage 4 — Application Phase (On-press)**:
   The solvent carries the ink through the inking rollers, anilox/fountain, and onto the substrate. **Evaporation begins** here — for heatset and gravure, partial evaporation already occurs on press.

5. **Stage 5 — Drying Phase**:
   The remaining solvent evaporates in the oven or by air drying, leaving behind a continuous **resin–pigment film**. In some systems (e.g., oxidative drying), a small portion may be retained as a **retained solvent** (often unwanted).

## 2.2 Critical Solvent Properties (Theory)

### 2.2.1 Solvency Power
- **Definition:** The ability of a solvent to dissolve a given resin.
- **Measured by:** Kauri-Butanol value (Kb), aniline point, or Hildebrand solubility parameter.
- **Why critical:** Under-solvency ⇒ resin precipitation, poor gloss, ink instability. Over-solvency ⇒ excessive resin swelling, tack problems, slow drying.

### 2.2.2 Evaporation Rate
- **Definition:** The relative speed at which a solvent evaporates compared to a standard.
- **Standard reference:** **n-Butyl acetate (n-BuOAc) = 1.0**.
- **Why critical:** Must match press speed. Fast solvent in a slow press ⇒ drying in the duct. Slow solvent in a fast press ⇒ set-off, blocking, retained solvent in laminates.

### 2.2.3 Boiling Range
- Narrow boiling range ⇒ uniform evaporation.
- Wide boiling range ⇒ fractional evaporation — "tail" solvents remain in the film.

### 2.2.4 Flash Point
- **Definition:** Lowest temperature at which the solvent vapor ignites.
- **KTU relevance:** Determines **Hazard class for storage/transport** (e.g., below 21 °C = highly flammable).

### 2.2.5 Surface Tension
- Governs **wetting of the substrate** and **pigment dispersion stability**.
- Typical range: **18–35 mN/m** for organic solvents; **~72 mN/m** for water.

### 2.2.6 Viscosity
- Affects ink transfer and print quality.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All essential solvent equations for KTU 2024 Scheme examination.**

| # | Formula / Relationship | Symbols & Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\delta = \sqrt{\dfrac{E_{coh}}{V_m}}$ | $\delta$ = Hildebrand parameter (MPa^½), $E_{coh}$ = cohesive energy density (J/mol), $V_m$ = molar volume (cm³/mol) | Predict resin–solvent compatibility |
| 2 | $\Delta\delta = \vert \delta_{resin} - \delta_{solvent} \vert \le 5$ | Both in MPa^½ | Rule-of-thumb: compatible if difference < 5 |
| 3 | $R_{evap} = \dfrac{t_{90\%,ref}}{t_{90\%,sample}}$ | Dimensionless (ref = n-BuOAc or ether) | Evaporation rate calculation |
| 4 | $\eta = \eta_0 \, e^{E_a / RT}$ | $\eta$ = viscosity (mPa·s), $E_a$ = activation energy, $R$ = 8.314 J/(mol·K) | Viscosity–temperature dependence |
| 5 | $\gamma_{SG} = \gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LG} \cdot \cos\theta$ | $\gamma$ = interfacial tensions (mN/m), $\theta$ = contact angle | Young's equation — wetting |
| 6 | $\ln P = -\dfrac{\Delta H_{vap}}{R} \cdot \dfrac{1}{T} + C$ | $P$ = vapor pressure, $T$ = temperature (K), $C$ = integration constant | Clausius–Clapeyron (vapor pressure) |
| 7 | $\tau = \dfrac{\eta}{\dot{\gamma}}$ | $\tau$ = shear stress, $\eta$ = viscosity, $\dot{\gamma}$ = shear rate (s⁻¹) | Newtonian flow of solvent-rich ink |
| 8 | $w_{retained} = \dfrac{m_{dry} - m_{after\ oven}}{m_{dry}} \times 100\%$ | Mass fractions (%) | Retained solvent measurement |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Ink formulation R&D:** Solvent selection dictates the entire ink performance envelope — drying speed, gloss, adhesion, lamination bond strength, and food-packaging compliance.
- **Press productivity:** Correct evaporation profile prevents **set-off, blocking, and ghosting**.
- **Regulatory compliance:** **VOC (Volatile Organic Compound)** limits under REACH, EPA, and Indian CPCB rules restrict solvent choice in packaging inks.
- **Lamination bond strength:** Retained solvent > 5 mg/m² in laminate films causes **bond failure**, a major industry quality defect.
- **Conversion to water-based / UV-curable:** Driven by solvent-emission regulations — drives modern ink design.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Hildebrand Solubility Parameter from Cohesive Energy Density

> [!NOTE]
> **Why we derive it:** The KTU examiner may ask you to relate cohesive energy density to the solubility parameter, since it is the foundational equation used for predicting resin–solvent miscibility in ink formulation.

### Step 1 — Define Cohesive Energy Density (CED)

The cohesive energy density of a liquid is defined as the **molar energy of vaporization per unit molar volume**:

$$CED = \dfrac{E_{coh}}{V_m} = \dfrac{\Delta H_{vap} - RT}{V_m}$$

**Conversion logic:** The molar energy of vaporization $E_{coh}$ equals the enthalpy of vaporization $\Delta H_{vap}$ minus the work done against atmospheric pressure ($RT$) to convert liquid to vapor.

### Step 2 — Take the Square Root

The Hildebrand solubility parameter is the square root of CED:

$$\delta = \sqrt{CED} = \sqrt{\dfrac{E_{coh}}{V_m}}$$

**Conversion logic:** Historically, Hildebrand defined δ as a square-root quantity so that the units became energy-density^(½), which conveniently scales to MPa^½ after multiplying by appropriate conversion factors.

### Step 3 — Apply the Compatibility Rule

For a polymer (resin) and a solvent to be mutually soluble:

$$\Delta\delta = \vert \delta_{resin} - \delta_{solvent} \vert \le 5\ \text{MPa}^{1/2}$$

**Conversion logic:** Empirically, when two materials have similar cohesive energy densities, the free energy of mixing is negative (or near zero), favoring miscibility.

### Step 4 — Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style)

**Problem:** Calculate whether ethyl acetate is a suitable solvent for nitrocellulose resin.

Given:
- $\Delta H_{vap}$ (EtOAc) = 35.6 kJ/mol
- $V_m$ (EtOAc) = 98.5 cm³/mol = 98.5 × 10⁻⁶ m³/mol
- $T$ = 298 K
- $\delta$ (nitrocellulose) ≈ 23.0 MPa^½

**Solution:**

$$E_{coh} = 35{,}600 - (8.314 \times 298) = 35{,}600 - 2{,}478 = 33{,}122\ \text{J/mol}$$

$$CED = \dfrac{33{,}122}{98.5 \times 10^{-6}} = 3.363 \times 10^{8}\ \text{J/m}^{3} = 336.3\ \text{MPa}$$

$$\delta_{EtOAc} = \sqrt{336.3} = 18.34\ \text{MPa}^{1/2}$$

$$\Delta\delta = \vert 23.0 - 18.34 \vert = 4.66\ \text{MPa}^{1/2} \le 5$$

✅ **Conclusion:** Ethyl acetate is a **good solvent** for nitrocellulose — consistent with its widespread use in flexo inks.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Evaporation Rate from Standard Reference

> [!NOTE]
> **Why we derive it:** The KTU examiner often asks to compute the evaporation rate or to interpret a table of values. This is the industry-standard method.

### Step 1 — Define Relative Evaporation Rate

The relative evaporation rate is defined as the ratio of evaporation times for equal volumes under identical conditions:

$$R_{evap} = \dfrac{t_{90\%,reference}}{t_{90\%,sample}}$$

**Conversion logic:** A solvent that evaporates **faster** than the reference has a **smaller** $t_{90\%}$ and therefore an **R > 1**. The reference is conventionally **n-butyl acetate = 1.0**.

### Step 2 — Worked Example

**Problem:** A solvent requires 120 seconds to lose 90 % of its mass, while n-butyl acetate requires 480 seconds under identical conditions. Find its evaporation rate.

$$R_{evap} = \dfrac{480}{120} = 4.0$$

**Interpretation:** This solvent evaporates **4× faster** than n-butyl acetate. Suitable only for high-speed gravure or flexo where rapid drying is needed.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Clausius–Clapeyron for Vapor Pressure vs. Temperature

> [!NOTE]
> **Why we derive it:** Vapor pressure directly controls how fast a solvent leaves the ink film. Press ovens run at 100–180 °C — vapor pressure data is essential.

### Step 1 — Start with the Clausius–Clapeyron Equation

$$\dfrac{dP}{dT} = \dfrac{\Delta H_{vap}}{T \cdot \Delta V}$$

For vapor–liquid equilibrium, $\Delta V \approx V_{vapor} = RT/P$ (ideal gas approximation):

### Step 2 — Substitute and Simplify

$$\dfrac{dP}{dT} = \dfrac{\Delta H_{vap} \cdot P}{R \cdot T^{2}}$$

### Step 3 — Separate Variables and Integrate

$$\int \dfrac{dP}{P} = \int \dfrac{\Delta H_{vap}}{R} \cdot \dfrac{dT}{T^{2}}$$

$$\ln P = -\dfrac{\Delta H_{vap}}{R \cdot T} + C$$

### Step 4 — Practical Engineering Form

$$\ln\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right) = -\dfrac{\Delta H_{vap}}{R}\left(\dfrac{1}{T_2} - \dfrac{1}{T_1}\right)$$

**Conversion logic:** This form lets the ink formulator predict the vapor pressure at oven temperature $T_2$ given a known reference pressure $P_1$ at $T_1$ — used to design drying tunnels.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Solvent Selection Decision Grid

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Style: Map solvent family to ink process and substrate.**

| Ink Process | Substrate | Preferred Solvent Family | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Publication gravure** | Paper | Toluene, xylene | High solvency for hydrocarbon resins; low cost |
| **Packaging gravure** | PET / BOPP / foil | MEK, EtOAc, toluene | Strong solvency for PU, PVC, PVB binders |
| **Flexo (surface print)** | PE / PP / paper | Ethanol, IPA, n-propanol, EtOAc | Fast drying, low odor, food-safe variants |
| **Flexo (lamination)** | PET / foil | EtOAc, MEK, toluene | Strong resin solvency for lamination bond |
| **Water-based flexo** | Paper / corrugated | Water + co-solvents (butyl glycol) | Low VOC, regulatory compliance |
| **Heatset web offset** | Paper | Mineral oil (high boiling) | Slow evaporation allows setting on press |
| **Coldset offset** | Newsprint | Mineral oil (low viscosity) | Penetrates paper; no oven needed |
| **Screen printing** | Textiles / glass | Glycol ethers, cyclohexanone | Slow drying prevents screen clogging |
| **Inkjet** | Paper / film | Water + humectants (glycols) | Prevents nozzle drying |

---

## 3.5 Concept Reinforcement Table — Evaporation Classes

| Class | Boiling Range (°C) | Evaporation Rate (n-BuOAc = 1) | Example Solvents |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Fast** | 60 – 100 | > 3.0 | Acetone (5.6), EtOAc (4.0), hexane (8.0) |
| **Medium** | 100 – 150 | 1.0 – 3.0 | Toluene (2.0), MEK (3.7), IPA (1.4) |
| **Slow** | 150 – 200 | 0.1 – 1.0 | n-BuOAc (1.0), xylene (0.6) |
| **Very slow** | > 200 | < 0.1 | Butyl glycol (0.1), mineral oil (<<0.01) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Solvent Function Block Diagram — Ink Manufacturing Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Solvent Storage] --> B[Solvent Property QC]
    B --> C{Solvent Grade OK?}
    C -- No --> D[Reject and Return]
    C -- Yes --> E[Resin Dissolution Vessel]
    F[Pigments] --> G[Pigment Pre-mix Tank]
    E --> G
    G --> H[Main Mixing / Milling]
    H --> I[Ink Let-down with Solvent]
    I --> J[Viscosity and Color QC]
    J --> K{Within Spec?}
    K -- No --> L[Re-adjust Solvent Ratio]
    L --> H
    K -- Yes --> M[Filtered Ink to Storage]
    M --> N[Press Fountain]
    N --> O[Inking Unit Transfer]
    O --> P[Substrate]
    P --> Q[Drying Oven]
    Q --> R[Solvent Vapor Recovery]
    R --> S[Condensation and Reuse]
    S --> A
    Q --> T[Dried Ink Film]
```

## 4.2 Solvent Selection Decision Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Choose Ink System] --> Q1[Substrate Type?]
    Q1 --> Q1A[Paper / Board]
    Q1 --> Q1B[Plastic Film / Foil]
    Q1 --> Q1C[Metal / Glass]
    Q1A --> R1[Press Speed?]
    Q1B --> R1
    Q1C --> R1
    R1 --> R2[High Speed]
    R1 --> R3[Medium Speed]
    R1 --> R4[Low Speed]
    R2 --> S1[Fast Evaporating Solvent]
    R3 --> S2[Medium Evaporating Solvent]
    R4 --> S3[Slow Evaporating Solvent]
    S1 --> T1[Check VOC Regulations]
    S2 --> T1
    S3 --> T1
    T1 --> T2{VOC Compliance OK?}
    T2 -- No --> W1[Switch to Water-Based]
    T2 -- Yes --> U1[Match Resin Solubility]
    U1 --> U2{Hildebrand Match?}
    U2 -- No --> V1[Blend Co-Solvent]
    U2 -- Yes --> Z[Final Solvent System Selected]
    W1 --> U1
```

## 4.3 Drying / Evaporation Block Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Wet Ink Film on Substrate] --> B[Surface Evaporation]
    B --> C[Diffusion from Bulk to Surface]
    C --> D[Boundary Layer Mass Transfer]
    D --> E[Solvent Vapor into Air Stream]
    E --> F{Oven Type?}
    F -- Hot Air --> G[Convective Removal]
    F -- IR --> H[Radiative Heating]
    F -- Air Impingement --> I[Jet Drying]
    G --> J[Dried Film]
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K[QC: Retained Solvent Test]
    K --> L{Within Limit?}
```
