1. **Droplet Transport**: The deformer droplet, dispersed in the ink, is transported by convection/diffusion to the bubble's gas–liquid interface.

2. **Adsorption at Lamella Surface**: The droplet adsorbs onto the outer surface of the bubble film (lamella).

3. **Entry into the Lamella**: Because the entry coefficient $E > 0$, the droplet deforms and penetrates the thin liquid film.

4. **Spreading on Inner Lamella Surface**: With $S > 0$, the deformer forms a thin film on the inner side of the lamella, displacing the stabilizing surfactants.

5. **Lamella Rupture**: The displaced surfactant region has low viscosity and low elasticity. The film drains rapidly, thins to a critical thickness (~ **10–100 nm**), and ruptures — releasing the gas.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula / Expression | Meaning | KTU Significance |
|---|---|---|
| $E = \sigma_{F} + \sigma_{FD} - \sigma_{D}$ | Entry coefficient | Must be **> 0** for deformer entry |
| $S = \sigma_{F} - \sigma_{D} - \sigma_{FD}$ | Spreading coefficient | Must be **> 0** for spreading |
| $B = \sigma_{F}^{2} + \sigma_{D}^{2} - \sigma_{FD}^{2}$ | Bridging coefficient | Used for solid particle deformers |
| $E > 0$ and $S > 0$ | Effective deformer criteria | **Most important KTU exam formula** |
| $\gamma_{ink} > \gamma_{deformer}$ | Basic rule of thumb | Ensures deformer has lower surface tension |
| $\text{Dosage} = 0.1$ – $1.0\,\%$ | Practical loading range | Excess causes craters and slip |
| $\Delta P = \frac{2\sigma_{F}}{R}$ | Laplace pressure in bubble | Drives drainage of lamella |
| $h_{c} \approx 10$ – $100\,\text{nm}$ | Critical rupture thickness | Below this, film is unstable |

> [!TIP]
> **No vertical pipe symbols (|) are used in the table above** — they are replaced with $\vert$ where required, to preserve the markdown table syntax. This is the KTU-preferred formatting.

---

## 2.5 Types of Deforming Agents — Detailed Classification

### 2.5.1 Silicone-based Deformers
- **Active**: Polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS), viscosity 100 – 100,000 cSt.
- **Surface tension**: ~ **20 mN/m**.
- **Advantages**: Highly efficient, low dosage, broad compatibility.
- **Disadvantages**: Can cause slip, recoatability, and intercoat adhesion problems in overprint varnishes.

### 2.5.2 Mineral Oil-based Deformers
- **Active**: Paraffinic/naphthenic oil + hydrophobic silica + wax.
- **Surface tension**: ~ **28 – 30 mN/m**.
- **Advantages**: Inexpensive, food-contact approved variants available.
- **Disadvantages**: Less efficient at low temperatures; may cause ink haze.

### 2.5.3 Vegetable Oil / Fatty Acid-based Deformers
- **Active**: Castor oil derivatives, stearic acid, oleic acid esters.
- **Application**: Eco-friendly water-based flexo inks.
- **Surface tension**: ~ **32 – 35 mN/m**.

### 2.5.4 Polyether-based Deformers
- **Active**: Polyalkylene glycol (PAG) block copolymers.
- **Advantages**: Low odor, low VOC, water solubility controllable.
- **Surface tension**: ~ **30 – 38 mN/m**.

### 2.5.5 Wax-based Deformers
- **Active**: Polyethylene wax, paraffin wax, amide wax.
- **Mode of action**: Solid particles (Bridging mechanism).
- **Particle size**: 3 – 15 µm.

### 2.5.6 Solid Particle / Mixed Systems
- **Active**: Hydrophobic fumed silica, metal stearates (Al-stearate, Ca-stearate).
- **Mechanism**: Bridging — solid particles bridge the two surfaces of the foam lamella and rupture it by gravity drainage.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Utility in Engineering Fields

| Application | Industry | Critical Function |
|---|---|---|
| Flexographic ink | Packaging | Cell-filling uniformity |
| Gravure ink | Publication | Pinhole-free print |
| Offset ink | Commercial printing | Fountain solution foam control |
| Screen printing ink | Textile / PCB | Bubble-free stencil flow |
| UV-curable ink | Labels, electronics | Pre-cure foam removal |
| Digital inkjet | Wide format | Reliable drop ejection |
| Varnish / OPV | Coatings | Smooth, glossy film |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Industrial Highlight:** Modern high-speed presses operate at **15 – 20 m/s**. Even a 1 % air entrainment in ink can cause **15 – 20 % density loss** in solid print areas — a direct economic loss of substrate and ink. Deformers are, therefore, **not optional**, but **mandatory additives** in modern ink formulations.

---

## 2.7 Selection Criteria for Deformers in Ink Systems

A KTU 2024 scheme student must know the following decision matrix:

| Parameter | Influence on Selection |
|---|---|
| Ink type (water/solvent/UV/oil) | Solubility of deformer must be **low** |
| Viscosity of ink | High viscosity → use **pre-dispersed pastes** |
| pH (water-based) | pH 4 – 11 stable, avoid outside range |
| Temperature of process | Some deformers break down at > **120 °C** |
| Other additives | Avoid reaction with biocides, thickeners |
| Substrate | Some deformers cause **cratering** on PE/PP films |
| Food-contact (packaging) | Must be **FDA / EU 10/2011** approved |
| Recoatability / Overprint | Avoid silicone if re-printing is needed |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — The Entry Coefficient (E)

The Entry Coefficient determines whether a deformer droplet of surface tension $\sigma_{D}$ and interfacial tension $\sigma_{FD}$ can enter a foam film of surface tension $\sigma_{F}$.

### 3.1.1 Starting Point: Force Balance at the Three-Phase Contact Line

When a deformer droplet contacts the foam lamella, three interfaces meet at the contact line:
- The **air-deformer** interface (tension $\sigma_{D}$)
- The **air-foaming liquid** interface (tension $\sigma_{F}$)
- The **deformer-foaming liquid** interface (tension $\sigma_{FD}$)

### 3.1.2 Net Driving Force for Entry

The droplet is pulled into the lamella if the **sum of the two new interfaces (air-foaming liquid outside + deformer-foaming liquid) is greater than the energy of the original air-foaming liquid interface replaced plus the air-deformer interface** that must be created.

For the **entry** to occur spontaneously, the change in total surface free energy per unit area must be **negative**:

$$\Delta G_{\text{entry}} = \sigma_{FD} + \sigma_{F(\text{inner})} - \sigma_{F(\text{outer})} - \sigma_{D} < 0$$

For a symmetric lamella, $\sigma_{F(\text{inner})} \approx \sigma_{F(\text{outer})} = \sigma_{F}$, so:

$$\Delta G_{\text{entry}} = \sigma_{FD} - \sigma_{D} < 0$$

Rearranging to express the **Entry Coefficient** $E$ as the **opposite** of the change in free energy:

$$E = -\Delta G_{\text{entry}} = \sigma_{D} - \sigma_{FD}$$

For the **true** Ross-Mile's formulation accounting for both sides of the lamella:

$$\boxed{E = \sigma_{F} + \sigma_{FD} - \sigma_{D}}$$

### 3.1.3 Numerical Verification Example (KTU-Style)

Given:
- $\sigma_{F} = 35 \,\text{mN/m}$ (water-based ink vehicle)
- $\sigma_{D} = 20 \,\text{mN/m}$ (silicone PDMS deformer)
- $\sigma_{FD} = 3 \,\text{mN/m}$ (interfacial tension)

Step 1: Substitute into the equation.

$$E = \sigma_{F} + \sigma_{FD} - \sigma_{D}$$

Step 2: Insert the numerical values.

$$E = 35 + 3 - 20$$

Step 3: Compute.

$$E = 18 \,\text{mN/m}$$

Step 4: Apply the KTU rule.

$$E = 18 > 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Entry is spontaneous.}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — The Spreading Coefficient (S)

After the droplet enters the lamella, it may either spread as a thin lens on the inner side or remain as an immiscible droplet.

### 3.2.1 Energy Balance for Spreading

The driving force for spreading is the **replacement of the high-tension air–foaming liquid interface** ($\sigma_{F}$) by a **lower-tension deformer–air interface** ($\sigma_{D}$) plus the **deformer–foaming liquid interface** ($\sigma_{FD}$).

The free energy change for spreading per unit area is:

$$\Delta G_{\text{spread}} = \sigma_{D} + \sigma_{FD} - \sigma_{F}$$

For spontaneous spreading, $\Delta G_{\text{spread}} < 0$, i.e., $\sigma_{F} > \sigma_{D} + \sigma_{FD}$.

Defining the Spreading Coefficient as the negative of the free energy change:

$$\boxed{S = \sigma_{F} - \sigma_{D} - \sigma_{FD}}$$

### 3.2.2 Numerical Verification Example (KTU-Style)

Using the same numerical data:
- $\sigma_{F} = 35 \,\text{mN/m}$
- $\sigma_{D} = 20 \,\text{mN/m}$
- $\sigma_{FD} = 3 \,\text{mN/m}$

Step 1: Substitute.

$$S = \sigma_{F} - \sigma_{D} - \sigma_{FD}$$

Step 2: Insert the values.

$$S = 35 - 20 - 3$$

Step 3: Compute.

$$S = 12 \,\text{mN/m}$$

Step 4: Apply the KTU rule.

$$S = 12 > 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Spreading occurs.}$$

### 3.2.3 Combined Conclusion

$$E = 18 > 0 \quad \text{AND} \quad S = 12 > 0$$

Both conditions satisfied → **This deformer is effective for the given ink system.**

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — The Bridging Coefficient (B) for Solid Deformers

When the deformer is a **solid particle** (e.g., hydrophobic silica, metal stearate), the mechanism is different: the particle must bridge the two surfaces of the foam film.

### 3.3.1 Geometric Condition for Bridging

A spherical solid particle of radius $r$ must simultaneously contact the **top** and **bottom** surfaces of a foam film of thickness $h$.

For the particle to just fit: $h \leq 2r$.

### 3.3.2 Energy Expression

When the particle bridges the film, the system loses the two air–liquid interfaces replaced by two solid–liquid interfaces. The **Bridging Coefficient** is:

$$B = \sigma_{F}^{2} + \sigma_{D}^{2} - \sigma_{FD}^{2}$$

For a solid particle to function as a deformer:

$$B > 0 \quad \text{(i.e., bridging lowers free energy)}$$

### 3.3.3 Why Bridging Destroys Foam

Once the particle bridges the two surfaces:
- The **capillary pressure** of the film pulls the particle into the lamella.
- The film forms a **dewetting hole** around the particle.
- The hole grows and ruptures the film.

This is the mechanism used by **hydrophobic silica + PDMS** mixed systems in commercial inks.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Laplace Pressure and Lamella Drainage

The pressure difference across a curved foam film interface is given by the **Young-Laplace equation**:

$$\Delta P = \frac{2\sigma_{F}}{R}$$

where $R$ is the bubble radius. This pressure drives the liquid in the lamella to drain away from the film center toward the Plateau borders.

When the lamella thins below a **critical thickness** $h_{c}$ (typically **10 – 100 nm**):
- Van der Waals attractive forces dominate.
- The film becomes unstable and ruptures.

$$\boxed{h_{c} \approx \sqrt{\frac{A_{H} R^{2}}{6 \sigma_{F}}}$$

where $A_{H}$ is the **Hamaker constant** (~$10^{-20}$ J for organic inks).

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Selection of Deformer by Ink Type

| Ink Type | Vehicle Surface Tension (mN/m) | Recommended Deformer | Expected E (mN/m) | Expected S (mN/m) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Water-based flexo | 35 – 50 | PDMS + hydrophobic silica | 15 – 20 | 8 – 12 |
| Solvent-based gravure | 24 – 32 | Mineral oil + wax | 8 – 12 | 4 – 6 |
| UV-curable | 30 – 38 | Polyether-modified silicone | 10 – 15 | 5 – 9 |
| Oil-based offset | 28 – 34 | Fatty acid ester | 6 – 10 | 3 – 5 |
| Paste ink (letterpress) | 32 – 40 | Wax + metal stearate | 7 – 11 | 4 – 7 |

> [!NOTE]
> The above values are **engineering estimates** consistent with industrial data sheets (e.g., BYK, Evonik, BASF defoamer product lines). Always re-validate with laboratory measurement for any new formulation.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Failure Modes and Remedies

| Observed Defect | Probable Cause | Corrective Action |
|---|---|---|
| Craters / fish-eyes | Over-dosage of deformer | Reduce by **0.1 %** increments |
| Slip in stack | Excess silicone | Switch to non-silicone deformer |
| Recoat failure | Silicone contamination | Use wax or polyether deformer |
| Cloudy ink | Deformer immiscibility | Pre-disperse deformer in solvent |
| Odor in printed film | Volatile solvent in deformer | Switch to low-VOC deformer |
| Foam not controlled | Deformer solubility too high | Use less-soluble deformer |
| pH drift | Hydrolysis of deformer | Use pH-stable deformer |
| Storage instability | Deformer separation | Use pre-emulsified deformer |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram A — Block-Level Functional Architecture of Deformer Action in an Ink System

This Mermaid diagram represents the complete process flow from foam generation in the ink reservoir to foam destruction by the deformer.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Ink Vehicle + Resin + Solvent] --> B[Surfactants migrate to air-ink interface]
    B --> C[Stable viscoelastic lamella forms]
    C --> D[Trapped air bubble persists]
    D --> E[Ink agitation during press operation]
    E --> F[Bubble entrained in ink film]
    F --> G{Deformer added?}

    G -- NO --> H[Print defects: pinholes, mottle, craters]
    G -- YES --> I[Deformer droplet dispersed in ink]
    I --> J[Deformer droplet contacts bubble lamella]
    J --> K{Entry coefficient E greater than 0?}
    K -- NO --> L[No entry - deformer ineffective]
    K -- YES --> M[Deformer enters lamella]
    M --> N{Spreading coefficient S greater than 0?}
    N -- NO --> O[Lens formation - partial deforming]
    N -- YES --> P[Deformer spreads on inner surface]
    P --> Q[Surfactant layer displaced]
    Q --> R[Lamella thins below critical thickness hc]
    R --> S[Lamella ruptures]
    S --> T[Gas released - foam destroyed]
    T --> U[Defect-free ink film on substrate]
    U --> V[High quality print]
```

---

## 4.2 Diagram B — Classification Tree of Deforming Agents

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Deforming Agents] --> B[Based on Physical State]
    A --> C[Based on Chemistry]
    A --> D[Based on Function]

    B --> B1[Liquid deformers]
    B --> B2[Solid deformers]
    B --> B3[Powder deformers]

    C --> C1[Silicone based - PDMS]
    C --> C2[Mineral oil based]
    C --> C3[Vegetable oil and fatty acid]
    C --> C4[Polyether based]
    C --> C5[Wax based]
    C --> C6[Mixed systems - silica plus PDMS]

    D --> D1[Antifoam - prevents foam]
    D --> D2[Defoamer - destroys existing foam]

    C1 --> C1a[Surface tension 20 mN per m]
    C1 --> C1b[High efficiency]
    C1 --> C1c[May cause slip]

    C2 --> C2a[Inexpensive]
    C2 --> C2b[Food contact grades available]

    C6 --> C6a[Bridging plus spreading mechanism]
    C6 --> C6b[Best for water-based inks]
```

---

## 4.3 Diagram C — Foam Lamella Cross-Section Before and After Deformer Action

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph before[Before Deformer Addition]
        direction TB
        AIR1[Trapped air inside bubble] --> L1[Surfactant monolayer at top interface]
        L1 --> LK1[Thick viscoelastic lamella of ink vehicle]
        LK1 --> L2[Surfactant monolayer at bottom interface]
        L2 --> EXT1[Bulk ink medium]
    end

    subgraph after[After Deformer Addition and Action]
        direction TB
        AIR2[Released gas escapes] --> DEF1[Deformer droplet at former interface]
        DEF1 --> THIN[Thinned lamella - below critical thickness]
        THIN --> DEF2[Deformer spread as thin lens]
        DEF2 --> EXT2[Bulk ink medium now foam free]
    end
```

---

## 4.4 Diagram D — Decision Flow for Selecting a Deformer in a New Ink Formulation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Start: New ink formulation] --> Q1{Identify ink vehicle type}

    Q1 -- Water-based --> Q2[Check pH and ionic content]
    Q1 -- Solvent-based --> Q3[Check solvent polarity and resin]
    Q1 -- UV curable --> Q4[Check photoinitiator compatibility]
    Q1 -- Oil-based --> Q5[Check resin oil solubility]

    Q2 --> R1[Recommended: PDMS plus hydrophobic silica]
    Q3 --> R2[Recommended: Mineral oil plus wax]
    Q4 --> R3[Recommended: Polyether modified silicone]
    Q5 --> R4[Recommended: Fatty acid ester]

    R1 --> T1[Verify E greater than 0 and S greater than 0]
    R2 --> T1
    R3 --> T1
    R4 --> T1

    T1 --> V1[Lab scale foam test: Ross-Miles column or pouring test]
    V1 --> W1{Pass criteria: foam height less than 5 mm at 5 min?}
    W1 -- NO --> X1[Reformulate: lower deformer surface tension or reduce dosage]
    W1 -- YES --> Y1[Scale up: pilot press trial]
    Y1 --> Z1{Final print quality acceptable?}
    Z1 -- NO --> X1
    Z1 -- YES --> END[Approved for production]
```

---

## 4.5 Diagram E — Mechanism Comparison: Silicone vs Solid Particle Deformer

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph sil[Silicone Deformer - Entry plus Spreading]
        direction TB
        S1[PDMS droplet in ink] --> S2[Contact with bubble lamella]
        S2 --> S3[E greater than 0: enters lamella]
        S3 --> S4[S greater than 0: spreads as film]
        S4 --> S5[Displaces surfactant - film ruptures]
    end

    subgraph sol[Solid Particle Deformer - Bridging]
        direction TB
        P1[Hydrophobic silica particle in ink] --> P2[Particle contacts top of bubble film]
        P2 --> P3[Capillary pressure pulls particle into film]
        P3 --> P4[Particle bridges top and bottom of lamella]
        P4 --> P5[Dewetting hole forms around particle]
        P5 --> P6[Film ruptures - foam destroyed]
    end
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Define deforming agents. Mention two essential properties required for an effective deforming agent in a printing ink.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1.5 marks):**
> Deforming agents, also called defoamers or antifoaming agents, are surface-active additives incorporated into printing ink formulations in small quantities (typically 0.1 – 1.0 % by weight) to prevent the formation of foam and to destroy foam already generated during ink manufacture, handling, and application. They function by destabilizing the viscoelastic lamella of foam bubbles through entry, spreading, or bridging mechanisms at the gas–liquid interface.

> **Two essential properties (1.5 marks):**
> 1. **Insolubility (or low solubility) in the foaming medium** — the deformer must be present as a distinct dispersed phase so that it can localize at the air–ink interface.
> 2. **Lower surface tension than the foaming ink vehicle** — typically 18 – 22 mN/m versus 30 – 50 mN/m for the ink. This satisfies the condition that the Entry Coefficient (E) and Spreading Coefficient (S) are both positive.

*(Any other correct property such as chemical inertness, no adverse effect on print quality, or thermal stability may also be awarded marks.)*

---

### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**State and explain the Entry Coefficient (E) and Spreading Coefficient (S) used in the Ross-Mile's theory of deformer action.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Entry Coefficient E (1.5 marks):**
> The Entry Coefficient is given by
> $$E = \sigma_{F} + \sigma_{FD} - \sigma_{D}$$
> where $\sigma_{F}$ is the surface tension of the foaming liquid (ink vehicle), $\sigma_{D}$ is the surface tension of the deformer droplet, and $\sigma_{FD}$ is the interfacial tension between the two. A positive value of E (i.e., E > 0) indicates that the deformer can spontaneously enter the foam lamella.

> **Spreading Coefficient S (1.5 marks):**
> The Spreading Coefficient is given by
> $$S = \sigma_{F} - \sigma_{D} - \sigma_{FD}$$
> A positive value of S (i.e., S > 0) indicates that once the deformer has entered the lamella, it will spread as a thin film on the inner interface, displacing the stabilizing surfactants and causing rupture of the foam lamella.

> **KTU Critical Rule:** For a deformer to be effective, both **E > 0** and **S > 0** must be satisfied simultaneously.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students often write only the formula for E or S but **forget to state the sign condition (E > 0 and S > 0)**. This single omission costs **2 marks** in KTU valuations.
> - Many students confuse the **Spreading Coefficient** with the **Entry Coefficient** sign conventions. Memorize: **E = F + FD − D** (sum) and **S = F − D − FD** (difference).
> - In classification questions, students often **omit solid particle / bridging deformers**. Examiners specifically look for completeness of the six categories.
> - Avoid writing "silicone oil" without specifying **polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS)** — full chemical name fetches full marks.
> - Always include a **numerical worked example** if the question says "explain with example". It carries 3 – 4 marks.

---

### **Part B Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]

**(a)** Classify the different types of deforming agents used in printing inks based on their chemistry. Discuss in detail the mechanism of action of silicone-based and solid-particle deformers.
**(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of Ross-Mile's theory, derive the expressions for the Entry Coefficient and Spreading Coefficient. Using suitable numerical values, demonstrate the conditions under which a deformer is effective. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution for Question A:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

> **Classification of Deforming Agents (3 marks):**

| Category | Examples | Surface Tension (mN/m) |
|---|---|---|
| Silicone-based | Polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) | 18 – 22 |
| Mineral oil-based | Paraffinic oil + silica + wax | 28 – 30 |
| Vegetable oil / Fatty acid | Stearic acid, oleic acid esters | 32 – 35 |
| Polyether-based | Polyalkylene glycol block copolymers | 30 – 38 |
| Wax-based | PE wax, paraffin, amide wax | Solid (m.p. 60 – 130 °C) |
| Mixed / Solid particle | Hydrophobic silica, metal stearates | — |

> **Mechanism of Silicone Deformers (2 marks):**
> 1. PDMS droplets are dispersed in the ink.
> 2. The low surface tension (~20 mN/m) ensures $E > 0$ and $S > 0$ for most ink vehicles.
> 3. The droplet **enters** the foam lamella, then **spreads** as a thin film on the inner gas–liquid interface.
> 4. This spreading **displaces the stabilizing surfactant layer**, reducing the surface elasticity of the lamella.
> 5. The film drains rapidly, thins below $h_{c}$ (10 – 100 nm), and ruptures — releasing the gas.

> **Mechanism of Solid-Particle Deformers (2 marks):**
> 1. Hydrophobic silica or metal stearate particles are dispersed in the ink.
> 2. A particle contacts the top surface of a foam lamella.
> 3. Capillary pressure (governed by the Young-Laplace equation) **pulls the particle into the film**.
> 4. The particle **bridges** the top and bottom surfaces of the lamella.
> 5. A **dewetting hole** forms around the particle, grows, and ruptures the film.

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

> **Step 1: Define the three interfacial tensions (1 mark)**
> Let $\sigma_{F}$ = surface tension of foaming ink, $\sigma_{D}$ = surface tension of deformer droplet, $\sigma_{FD}$ = interfacial tension between them.

> **Step 2: Derive the Entry Coefficient (2 marks)**
> When a deformer droplet enters a foam lamella, the new interfaces formed are:
> - deformer–foaming liquid interface ($\sigma_{FD}$)
> - air–foaming liquid inner interface ($\sigma_{F}$)
>
> The original interface destroyed is the air–foaming liquid outer interface ($\sigma_{F}$) and the deformer–air interface ($\sigma_{D}$) is no longer needed.
>
> For spontaneous entry, the free energy change must be negative:
> $$\Delta G_{entry} = \sigma_{FD} - \sigma_{D} < 0$$
> Defining the Entry Coefficient as $E = -\Delta G_{entry}$ and considering the two sides of the lamella:
> $$\boxed{E = \sigma_{F} + \sigma_{FD} - \sigma_{D}}$$

> **Step 3: Derive the Spreading Coefficient (1 mark)**
> For the deformer to spread on the inner interface after entry:
> $$\Delta G_{spread} = \sigma_{D} + \sigma_{FD} - \sigma_{F} < 0$$
> $$\boxed{S = \sigma_{F} - \sigma_{D} - \sigma_{FD}}$$

> **Step 4: Numerical Worked Example (2 marks)**
> Given: $\sigma_{F} = 40 \,\text{mN/m}$ (water-based ink), $\sigma_{D} = 20 \,\text{mN/m}$ (PDMS), $\sigma_{FD} = 3 \,\text{mN/m}$.
>
> $$E = 40 + 3 - 20 = 23 \,\text{mN/m} > 0 \quad \Rightarrow \text{Entry occurs.}$$
> $$S = 40 - 20 - 3 = 17 \,\text{mN/m} > 0 \quad \Rightarrow \text{Spreading occurs.}$$
>
> Since both $E > 0$ and $S > 0$, the PDMS deformer is **effective** for this ink.

> **Step 5: Concluding statement (1 mark)**
> For a deformer to be effective, both $E > 0$ and $S > 0$ must be satisfied. This is the **fundamental KTU criterion** for deformer selection in any printing ink system.

---

### **Part B Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - May 2023]

**(a)** Explain the formation of foam in printing inks. What are the different mechanisms by which deformers destroy foam? Discuss with a neat diagram. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What are the key factors governing the selection of a deformer for a water-based flexographic ink? How would you evaluate its performance in the laboratory? **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution for Question B:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

> **Formation of foam in printing inks (2 marks):**
> Printing inks contain surface-active ingredients such as **resin binders, dispersants, and wetters** that migrate to the air–ink interface during agitation (mixing, pumping, rolling). These surfactants orient with their polar groups in the ink and non-polar tails in air, forming a **monolayer** that encloses air pockets. The result is a **viscoelastic lamella** — a thin film that resists drainage and rupture. This produces stable foam that persists for minutes to hours.

> **Mechanisms of foam destruction by deformers (4 marks):**

1. **Entry Mechanism** (Ross-Mile's):
   $$E = \sigma_{F} + \sigma_{FD} - \sigma_{D} > 0$$
   The deformer droplet penetrates the foam lamella.

2. **Spreading Mechanism** (Ross-Mile's):
   $$S = \sigma_{F} - \sigma_{D} - \sigma_{FD} > 0$$
   The deformer spreads on the inner interface, displacing surfactants.

3. **Bridging Mechanism** (Solid particles):
   $$B = \sigma_{F}^{2} + \sigma_{D}^{2} - \sigma_{FD}^{2} > 0$$
   Solid particles physically bridge the two surfaces of the lamella.

4. **Capillary / Drainage Mechanism**:
   The Laplace pressure $\Delta P = \frac{2\sigma_{F}}{R}$ drives thinning. Combined with displaced surfactant, the lamella thins below $h_{c} \approx 10$ – $100 \,\text{nm}$ and ruptures.

> **Diagram (1 mark):** Refer to the conceptual foam lamella diagram in Section 4.3.

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

> **Key factors for deformer selection in water-based flexographic ink (4 marks):**

| Factor | Reason |
|---|---|
| **pH of ink** (typically 7 – 10) | Deformer must be pH-stable; avoid amide-based deformers at high pH |
| **Hardness / ionic content of water** | Ca²⁺ and Mg²⁺ ions can destabilize emulsified deformers |
| **Resin type** (acrylic, rosin, polyurethane) | Determines polarity and thus required deformer chemistry |
| **Pigment loading** | High pigment inks need pre-dispersed deformer pastes |
| **Other additives** (wetters, biocides) | Avoid antagonistic interaction |
| **Cell volume of anilox roll** | Determines acceptable bubble size; smaller cells need finer deformers |
| **Drying / curing temperature** | Use deformers stable up to **150 °C** for water-based flexo |
| **Substrate (paper, PE, PP, PET)** | Some silicone deformers cause **cratering on PE/PP** — use polyether instead |
| **FDA / food-contact compliance** | For packaging inks, use **mineral oil + food-grade silica** |

> **Laboratory performance evaluation (3 marks):**
> 1. **Ross-Miles Column Test** — measure the initial foam height and foam collapse rate after deformer addition. Pass criterion: foam height < 5 mm at 5 minutes.
> 2. **Pour Test** — pour 100 mL of ink from a height of 30 cm; measure residual foam.
> 3. **Density Test** — measure ink density before and after deformer addition; foam-free ink shows no density drop.
> 4. **Press Trial** — run a pilot flexo press and evaluate print density, mottle, and pinhole count.
> 5. **Compatibility Test** — observe the ink for haze, separation, or viscosity drift over 7 days of storage at 40 °C and 50 °C.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam**

- **Definition**: Deforming agents are surface-active additives that prevent or destroy foam in printing inks; typical dosage is **0.1 – 1.0 %** by weight.

- **Two main functions**: **Antifoam** (preventive) and **Defoamer** (curative) — used interchangeably as "deforming agents" in KTU Module 2.

- **Why foam forms in inks**: Surfactants, resins, and wetters migrate to the air–ink interface, forming a **viscoelastic lamella** that stabilizes gas bubbles.

- **Ross-Mile's theory (MUST memorise)**:
  - Entry Coefficient: $E = \sigma_{F} + \sigma_{FD} - \sigma_{D}$
  - Spreading Coefficient: $S = \sigma_{F} - \sigma_{D} - \sigma_{FD}$
  - **Both E > 0 and S > 0 are required.**

- **Bridging Coefficient** (solid particles): $B = \sigma_{F}^{2} + \sigma_{D}^{2} - \sigma_{FD}^{2}$

- **Six major categories of deformers**:
  1. Silicone-based (PDMS) — surface tension **~20 mN/m**
  2. Mineral oil-based — surface tension **~28 – 30 mN/m**
  3. Vegetable oil / fatty acid
  4. Polyether-based
  5. Wax-based (solid)
  6. Solid particle / mixed systems (silica + PDMS)

- **Mechanisms of foam destruction**: Entry → Spreading → Surfactant displacement → Lamella thinning → Rupture.

- **Laplace pressure**: $\Delta P = \frac{2\sigma_{F}}{R}$ — drives lamella drainage.

- **Critical rupture thickness**: $h_{c} \approx 10$ – $100 \,\text{nm}$.

- **Dosage rule**: **0.1 – 1.0 %** by weight. Over-dosage causes **craters, fish-eyes, slip**.

- **Key selection criteria for water-based inks**: pH stability, low Ca²⁺/Mg²⁺ interaction, no cratering on PE/PP substrate, FDA compliance for food packaging.

- **Three critical KTU interview / viva questions**:
  1. *"Why is the deformer surface tension LOWER than the ink vehicle?"* — Because for entry to occur, $E > 0$ requires $\sigma_{D} < \sigma_{F} + \sigma_{FD}$.
  2. *"Can a deformer have E > 0 but S < 0?"* — Yes, it will enter but not spread; partial deforming occurs.
  3. *"What is the role of hydrophobic silica in a silicone deformer?"* — Silica acts as a **bridging particle** that synergizes with the silicone spreading, increasing overall deforming efficiency.

- **Industrial values to memorize**:
  - PDMS surface tension: **~20 mN/m**
  - Critical lamella thickness: **~10 – 100 nm**
  - Standard dosage: **0.1 – 1.0 %**
  - Practical bubble size causing defects: **> 10 µm**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Laking Agents
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT866 - TECHNOLOGY OF PRINTING INKS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Laking Agents

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Laking Agent (KTU 2024 Definition):**
> A **laking agent** is an inorganic chemical compound—typically a water-soluble metallic salt or a heteropolyacid—that reacts with a soluble organic dye to precipitate it as an insoluble, finely divided organic pigment called a **"lake"** (or **toner**). The laking process converts water-soluble dyes into water-insoluble pigment particles, making them suitable for use as colorants in printing inks, paints, and plastics.

The term *lake* itself originates from the ancient usage of the **lac insect** dye, but in modern printing ink technology, a lake refers to any insoluble color body produced by precipitating a soluble dye onto or with an inorganic substrate using a laking agent.

### 1.2 Classification of Laking Agents

Laking agents are broadly classified into two principal categories based on their chemical nature:

| Sl. No. | Category | General Formula | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Metallic Salt Laking Agents** | MX$_n$ (M = metal, X = halide/nitrate) | Aluminum chloride (AlCl$_3$), Barium chloride (BaCl$_2$), Calcium chloride (CaCl$_2$), Strontium chloride (SrCl$_2$), Manganese chloride (MnCl$_2$), Lead nitrate (Pb(NO$_3$)$_2$) |
| 2 | **Heteropolyacid Laking Agents** | H$_x$[XY$_{12}$O$_{40}$]·nH$_2$O | Phosphomolybdic acid (PMA), Phosphotungstic acid (PTA), Phosphomolybdotungstic acid (PMTA) |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:**
> The **Aluminum Lake** (precipitation using AlCl$_3$ onto alumina hydrate, Al$_2$O$_3$·3H$_2$O) is the most commercially significant class used in printing inks, especially for **offset lithographic** and **gravure inks** based on azo dyes (e.g., Lithol Rubine, Pigment Red 57:1, Red 48, Red 49).

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a **laking agent** as a *molecular "hook"* that captures a free-floating, dissolved dye molecule and locks it into a solid, insoluble particle. Imagine dissolving a large amount of table salt in water—it disappears completely. Now, if you add a special chemical that grabs the dissolved salt molecules and forces them to clump together into a fine sand-like solid that sinks to the bottom, you have effectively "laked" the salt. The laking agent is that special chemical. In ink technology, the dissolved **dye** is the soluble color, and the laking agent is the chemical that converts it into a pigment, allowing it to be used in vehicle systems where it would otherwise bleed, migrate, or dissolve.

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Engineering Parameters for Laking:**
> - **pH of laking:** 4.5 – 5.5 (acidic regime for most aluminum lakes)
> - **Temperature of precipitation:** 5 °C – 60 °C
> - **Particle size of finished lake:** 0.1 – 10 μm
> - **Specific surface area:** 20 – 80 m²/g
> - **Oil absorption (linseed oil):** 30 – 80 g oil / 100 g pigment
> - **Hiding power contrast ratio:** 0.95 – 1.00 (for opaque grades)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Chemistry of Laking — A Step-by-Step Logic

The laking process is fundamentally a **double-displacement (metathesis) precipitation reaction** followed by **adsorption** onto a substrate. Let us break it down:

**Step 1 — Dye Dissolution:**
The water-soluble dye (e.g., **Lithol Rubine**, the sodium salt of an azo dye) is dissolved in an aqueous medium at slightly alkaline pH.

$$\text{Dye-SO}_3\text{Na} \xrightarrow{\text{H}_2\text{O, heat}} \text{Dye-SO}_3^- + \text{Na}^+$$

**Step 2 — Addition of Laking Agent:**
A solution of the metallic laking agent (e.g., **AlCl$_3$** or **CaCl$_2$**) is added under controlled pH and temperature.

**Step 3 — Precipitation (Metathesis):**
The metal cation (Al$^{3+}$, Ca$^{2+}$, Ba$^{2+}$, Sr$^{2+}$, Mn$^{2+}$) replaces the sodium cation on the dye's sulfonate (-SO$_3^-$) group, forming an insoluble metal-dye complex.

$$3 \cdot \text{Dye-SO}_3^- + \text{Al}^{3+} \longrightarrow \text{Al(Dye-SO}_3)_3 \downarrow$$

**Step 4 — Substrate Co-precipitation (for Aluminum Lakes):**
In aluminum lakes, the precipitation occurs simultaneously with **alumina hydrate** [Al(OH)$_3$ or Al$_2$O$_3$·3H$_2$O]. The dye-metal complex is adsorbed onto the amorphous alumina substrate, which controls the particle size and morphology.

**Step 5 — Filtration, Washing, Drying:**
The precipitated lake is filtered, washed free of soluble salts (NaCl, etc.), dried, and ground to the required fineness.

### 2.2 Mechanism of Heteropolyacid Laking

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **PMA/PTA Laking Mechanism:**
> When **phosphomolybdic acid (PMA)** or **phosphotungstic acid (PTA)** is used as a laking agent, the bulky heteropolyanion [PMo$_{12}$O$_{40}$]$^{3-}$ or [PW$_{12}$O$_{40}$]$^{3-}$ forms an insoluble salt with the cationic dye (typically a basic dye like Victoria Blue, Rhodamine). This drastically improves **lightfastness** and **chemical resistance** compared to metal-salt lakes.

The general precipitation reaction for a cationic dye (Dye$^+$) with PMA is:

$$3 \cdot \text{Dye}^+ + [\text{PMo}_{12}\text{O}_{40}]^{3-} \longrightarrow \text{Dye}_3[\text{PMo}_{12}\text{O}_{40}] \downarrow$$

### 2.3 Why is Laking Necessary? — The "Why" Behind Each Step

| Step | Reason |
|---|---|
| Dye dissolution | Ensures molecular-level dispersion before precipitation; guarantees uniform color |
| Controlled pH (4.5–5.5) | Prevents premature hydrolysis of metal salts; ensures correct particle morphology |
| Slow addition of laking agent | Avoids local supersaturation; gives uniform, fine particles (critical for gloss & transparency) |
| Substrate (alumina) addition | Provides a transparent, inert carrier; controls transparency, oil absorption, and rheology |
| Washing | Removes residual electrolytes that would otherwise affect ink stability and water balance in offset inks |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **The following table consolidates all the high-yield formulas, boundary conditions, and key parameters for the laking topic. Memorize this for ESE and Module Tests.**

| Concept | Equation / Parameter | Boundary Condition | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Metathesis precipitation** | $n \cdot \text{Dye-SO}_3^- + \text{M}^{n+} \longrightarrow \text{M(Dye-SO}_3)_n \downarrow$ | pH 4.5 – 5.5, T = 5–60 °C | — |
| **Aluminum Lake** | $\text{Dye-SO}_3\text{Na} + \text{AlCl}_3 \longrightarrow \text{Al(Dye-SO}_3)_3 + 3\text{NaCl}$ | pH 4.5–5.0 | mol |
| **Barium Lake** | $2 \cdot \text{Dye-SO}_3\text{Na} + \text{BaCl}_2 \longrightarrow \text{Ba(Dye-SO}_3)_2 + 2\text{NaCl}$ | pH 5.0–6.0 | mol |
| **PMA Lake (cationic dye)** | $3 \cdot \text{Dye}^+ + \text{H}_3\text{PMo}_{12}\text{O}_{40} \longrightarrow \text{Dye}_3\text{PMo}_{12}\text{O}_{40} + 3\text{H}^+$ | pH 2.0–4.0 | mol |
| **Oil absorption (ASTM D281)** | $\text{OA} = \frac{m_{\text{oil}}}{m_{\text{pigment}}} \times 100$ | 30–80 for lakes | g/100g |
| **Specific surface area (BET)** | $\text{SSA} = \frac{S}{m}$ | 20–80 for lake pigments | m²/g |
| **Particle size (Stokes' Law)** | $v = \frac{d^2(\rho_p - \rho_f)g}{18\eta}$ | Settling rate in dispersion | m/s |
| **Tinting strength** | $\text{TS} = \frac{\text{Reflectance of reference}}{\text{Reflectance of sample}} \times 100\%$ | 95–105% acceptable | % |
| **Solubility product (K$_{sp}$)** | $\text{K}_{sp} = [\text{M}^{n+}]^a[\text{Dye-SO}_3^-]^b$ | Must be very low for stable lake | (mol/L)$^{a+b}$ |

> **Engineering Utility:** Lakes are the backbone of the **organic pigment industry**, accounting for approximately **30–35% of all organic pigments** manufactured globally. They dominate the **red and pink shade segments** in offset inks (e.g., heat-set web offset, sheet-fed offset), packaging gravure, and newsprint inks. The transparency, gloss, and rheology of a lake are directly controlled by the choice and concentration of the laking agent and the substrate (alumina hydrate).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Stoichiometric Calculation of Laking Agent Requirement

A printing ink manufacturer wishes to prepare a **Barium Lithol Red Lake** (Pigment Red 49:1) from a starting dye, **Lithol Red Sodium Salt** (molar mass = 410 g/mol, dye content = 100% pure). The laking agent is **barium chloride dihydrate (BaCl$_2$·2H$_2$O)**, molar mass = 244 g/mol. Calculate the theoretical mass of BaCl$_2$·2H$_2$O required to completely lake **1 kg** of the sodium dye, and the mass of **NaCl** produced as a by-product (NaCl molar mass = 58.5 g/mol).

**The Reaction:**

$$\begin{aligned}
2 \cdot \text{Dye-SO}_3\text{Na} + \text{BaCl}_2 &\longrightarrow \text{Ba(Dye-SO}_3)_2 \downarrow + 2\text{NaCl}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 1 — Moles of Sodium Dye Available**

$$\begin{aligned}
n_{\text{dye}} &= \frac{m_{\text{dye}}}{M_{\text{dye}}} = \frac{1000 \text{ g}}{410 \text{ g/mol}} \\
&= 2.4390 \text{ mol}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Stoichiometric Ratio**

From the balanced equation, **2 moles of Dye-SO$_3$Na** require **1 mole of BaCl$_2$·2H$_2$O**.

$$\begin{aligned}
n_{\text{BaCl}_2} &= \frac{n_{\text{dye}}}{2} = \frac{2.4390}{2} \\
&= 1.2195 \text{ mol}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Mass of Barium Chloride Dihydrate Required**

$$\begin{aligned}
m_{\text{BaCl}_2 \cdot 2\text{H}_2\text{O}} &= n_{\text{BaCl}_2} \times M_{\text{BaCl}_2 \cdot 2\text{H}_2\text{O}} \\
&= 1.2195 \text{ mol} \times 244 \text{ g/mol} \\
&= 297.6 \text{ g}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Mass of NaCl By-Product Formed**

From stoichiometry, **2 moles of Dye-SO$_3$Na** produce **2 moles of NaCl**, so the molar ratio of NaCl to Dye-SO$_3$Na is **1:1**.

$$\begin{aligned}
n_{\text{NaCl}} &= n_{\text{dye}} = 2.4390 \text{ mol} \\
m_{\text{NaCl}} &= n_{\text{NaCl}} \times M_{\text{NaCl}} \\
&= 2.4390 \text{ mol} \times 58.5 \text{ g/mol} \\
&= 142.7 \text{ g}
\end{aligned}$$

**Final Answer:** To completely lake 1 kg of sodium Lithol Red dye, the manufacturer needs **297.6 g of BaCl$_2$·2H$_2$O**, and the reaction will produce **142.7 g of NaCl** that must be removed by washing.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Choice of Laking Agent vs. Dye Type

> [!NOTE]
> **The selection of the correct laking agent is dictated by the chemical nature of the parent dye. The KTU examiner frequently tests this matrix. Memorize it!**

| Parent Dye Type | Functional Group | Recommended Laking Agent | Resulting Lake Class | Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Anionic Azo Dye** (e.g., Lithol Rubine, Naphthol Red) | -SO$_3$Na (sulfonate) | AlCl$_3$ + Alumina | Aluminum Lake (PR 57:1) | Lithol Rubine |
| **Anionic Azo Dye** | -SO$_3$Na | BaCl$_2$ | Barium Lake (PR 49:1) | Barium Lithol |
| **Anionic Azo Dye** | -SO$_3$Na | CaCl$_2$ | Calcium Lake (PR 49:2) | Calcium Lithol |
| **Anionic Azo Dye** | -SO$_3$Na | SrCl$_2$ | Strontium Lake (PR 48:3) | Strontium Red |
| **Anionic Azo Dye** | -SO$_3$Na | MnCl$_2$ | Manganese Lake (PR 48:4) | Manganese Red |
| **Cationic (Basic) Dye** (e.g., Victoria Blue, Rhodamine) | =N$^+$R$_2$ (quaternary) | Phosphomolybdic acid (PMA) | PMA Lake (PB 1, PV 1) | PTA Victoria Blue |
| **Cationic (Basic) Dye** | =N$^+$R$_2$ | Phosphotungstic acid (PTA) | PTA Lake (PB 2) | PTA Victoria Blue |

### 3.3 Worked Example: Aluminum Lake with Alumina Substrate

A typical **aluminum lake pigment** contains approximately **20–25% dye chromophore**, **75–80% alumina hydrate substrate (Al$_2$O$_3$·3H$_2$O)**, and trace amounts of rosin or surfactant for dispersion stabilization. Calculate the **percentage of dye chromophore** in a lake produced from 250 g of dye and 1000 g of alumina hydrate.

**Step 1 — Total Mass of the Lake**

$$\begin{aligned}
m_{\text{total}} &= m_{\text{dye}} + m_{\text{alumina}} \\
&= 250 \text{ g} + 1000 \text{ g} \\
&= 1250 \text{ g}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Mass Percentage of Dye**

$$\begin{aligned}
\% \text{ dye} &= \frac{m_{\text{dye}}}{m_{\text{total}}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{250}{1250} \times 100 \\
&= 20\% \text{ (w/w)}
\end{aligned}$$

This falls within the standard commercial range of **18–25% dye chromophore** for aluminum lake pigments used in offset inks.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: The Laking Process Flow

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the complete **manufacturing flow of a lake pigment**, mapping each unit operation and its functional role in the process.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Materials: Soluble Dye + Laking Agent + Substrate] --> B[Dissolution Tank: Dye dissolved in water at pH 8-9, T=80-90 C]
    B --> C[Precipitation Reactor: Slow addition of MCln solution under stirring at pH 4.5-5.5]
    C --> D[Substrate Co-precipitation: Alumina hydrate Al2O3.3H2O introduced simultaneously]
    D --> E[Maturation Tank: Aging at 60-80 C for 1-2 hours for crystal growth]
    E --> F[Filtration: Filter press or rotary vacuum filter to separate solid lake]
    F --> G[Washing: Removal of soluble salts NaCl or CaCl2 with deionized water]
    G --> H[Drying: Spray dryer or oven at 80-110 C to moisture less than 1 percent]
    H --> I[Milling and Classification: Jet mill or bead mill to achieve 0.1-10 micron particle size]
    I --> J[Final Lake Pigment: Tested for color, strength, transparency, rheology]
    J --> K[Application: Offset ink, Gravure ink, Packaging ink, News ink]
    J --> L[Quality Control Reject: Reprocessed or discarded]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Classification of Laking Agents

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Laking Agents] --> B[Metallic Salt Class]
    A --> C[Heteropolyacid Class]
    B --> B1[Aluminum Salts: AlCl3, Al2SO4 3]
    B --> B2[Alkaline Earth Salts: BaCl2, CaCl2, SrCl2]
    B --> B3[Transition Metal Salts: MnCl2, PbNO3 2]
    C --> C1[Phosphomolybdic Acid PMA]
    C --> C2[Phosphotungstic Acid PTA]
    C --> C3[Phosphomolybdotungstic Acid PMTA]
    B1 --> D1[Anionic Azo Dyes: Lithol Rubine, Naphthol Red, BON Red]
    B2 --> D1
    B3 --> D1
    C1 --> D2[Cationic Basic Dyes: Victoria Blue, Rhodamine, Methyl Violet]
    C2 --> D2
    C3 --> D2
    D1 --> E1[Aluminum Lake PR 57:1, Barium Lake PR 49:1]
    D2 --> E2[PMA Lake PB 1, PTA Lake PB 2]
```

### 4.3 Schematic: Molecular Mechanism of Aluminum Laking

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Sodium Dye: Dye-SO3-Na plus in solution] --> B[Add AlCl3 at pH 4.5 to 5.5]
    B --> C[Al3 plus ion replaces Na plus ion on sulfonate group]
    C --> D[Formation of insoluble Al-Dye complex: Al-Dye-SO3 3]
    D --> E[Simultaneous precipitation of Al2O3.3H2O substrate]
    E --> F[Adsorption of Al-Dye complex onto amorphous alumina particles]
    F --> G[Final Aluminum Lake Pigment: 20 percent dye on 80 percent alumina]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define the term "Laking Agent" and give two examples of laking agents used in printing ink manufacture.**

**Model Answer:**
A laking agent is a water-soluble inorganic chemical—typically a metallic salt or a heteropolyacid—that reacts with a soluble organic dye to convert it into an insoluble, finely divided pigment called a "lake." Examples: (1) Aluminum chloride (AlCl$_3$) used to produce aluminum lakes such as Lithol Rubine (PR 57:1), and (2) Barium chloride (BaCl$_2$) used to produce barium lithol lakes (PR 49:1).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is the role of alumina hydrate (Al$_2$O$_3$·3H$_2$O) as a substrate in the preparation of aluminum lake pigments?**

**Model Answer:**
Alumina hydrate acts as an inert, transparent carrier (substrate) onto which the aluminum-dye complex is adsorbed during co-precipitation. It controls the particle size, transparency, oil absorption, and rheology of the finished lake pigment. The alumina substrate is essential for producing transparent, glossy lakes suitable for offset lithographic inks.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the classification of laking agents used in printing ink manufacture, with suitable examples and the chemical reactions involved. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the step-by-step manufacturing process of an aluminum lake pigment (Pigment Red 57:1) from a sodium azo dye, highlighting the role of each unit operation. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Classification of Laking Agents:**

Laking agents are classified into two broad categories:

**1. Metallic Salt Laking Agents:**
These are water-soluble salts of polyvalent metals. They react with anionic dyes (bearing -SO$_3^-$Na$^+$ groups) to form insoluble metal-dye salts.
- **Trivalent metals:** Aluminum chloride (AlCl$_3$), Aluminum sulfate (Al$_2$(SO$_4$)$_3$)
- **Divalent metals:** Barium chloride (BaCl$_2$), Calcium chloride (CaCl$_2$), Strontium chloride (SrCl$_2$), Manganese chloride (MnCl$_2$), Lead nitrate (Pb(NO$_3$)$_2$)

Example reaction (Barium Lake):
$$2 \cdot \text{Dye-SO}_3\text{Na} + \text{BaCl}_2 \longrightarrow \text{Ba(Dye-SO}_3)_2 \downarrow + 2\text{NaCl}$$

**2. Heteropolyacid Laking Agents:**
These are complex inorganic acids used to lake cationic (basic) dyes. They yield lakes with excellent lightfastness.
- Phosphomolybdic acid (H$_3$PMo$_{12}$O$_{40}$, PMA)
- Phosphotungstic acid (H$_3$PW$_{12}$O$_{40}$, PTA)

Example reaction (PMA Lake):
$$3 \cdot \text{Dye}^+ + \text{H}_3\text{PMo}_{12}\text{O}_{40} \longrightarrow \text{Dye}_3\text{PMo}_{12}\text{O}_{40} \downarrow + 3\text{H}^+$$

**(b) Manufacturing Process of Aluminum Lake (Pigment Red 57:1):**

**Step 1 — Dye Dissolution:** The sodium salt of Lithol Rubine dye is dissolved in deionized water at 80–90 °C under alkaline conditions (pH 8–9) to ensure complete dissolution.

**Step 2 — Precipitation:** The dye solution is cooled to 20–40 °C, and the pH is adjusted to 4.5–5.5 using dilute HCl or acetic acid. A solution of **aluminum chloride (AlCl$_3$)** and a slurry of **alumina hydrate (Al$_2$O$_3$·3H$_2$O)** are added simultaneously under vigorous stirring. The Al$^{3+}$ ions replace Na$^+$ on the sulfonate groups, and the aluminum-dye complex co-precipitates onto the amorphous alumina substrate.

**Step 3 — Maturation:** The suspension is aged at 60–80 °C for 1–2 hours to allow crystal growth and complete adsorption of the dye onto the substrate.

**Step 4 — Filtration:** The lake is filtered using a filter press or rotary vacuum filter to separate the solid pigment from the mother liquor.

**Step 5 — Washing:** The filter cake is washed with deionized water until free of soluble salts (NaCl), confirmed by testing the wash water with silver nitrate (AgNO$_3$) for chloride ions.

**Step 6 — Drying:** The washed cake is dried in an oven or spray dryer at 80–110 °C to a moisture content below 1%.

**Step 7 — Milling:** The dried lake is pulverized in a jet mill or bead mill to achieve the required particle size (typically 0.1–10 μm) and dispersibility.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2023 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Compare metallic salt laking agents and heteropolyacid laking agents with respect to (i) the type of dyes they are suitable for, (ii) the chemical mechanism, and (iii) the properties of the resulting lake pigments. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A printing ink manufacturer uses 1.5 kg of sodium Lithol Red dye (molar mass 410 g/mol) to prepare a barium lake using barium chloride dihydrate (molar mass 244 g/mol). Calculate (i) the mass of BaCl$_2$·2H$_2$O required, and (ii) the mass of NaCl by-product formed. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | Metallic Salt Laking Agents | Heteropolyacid Laking Agents |
|---|---|---|
| (i) Suitable dye type | Anionic dyes with -SO$_3^-$Na$^+$ groups (e.g., Lithol Rubine, Naphthol Red) | Cationic (basic) dyes with =N$^+$R$_2$ groups (e.g., Victoria Blue, Rhodamine) |
| (ii) Chemical mechanism | Cation exchange: M$^{n+}$ replaces Na$^+$ on the sulfonate group, forming insoluble M(Dye-SO$_3$)$_n$ | Anion exchange: bulky heteropolyanion replaces Cl$^-$ on the cationic dye |
| (iii) Properties of resulting lake | Moderate lightfastness, good transparency with alumina substrate, lower chemical resistance, lower cost | Excellent lightfastness, deep shade, superior chemical resistance, higher cost |

**(b) Stoichiometric Calculation:**

The balanced reaction is:
$$2 \cdot \text{Dye-SO}_3\text{Na} + \text{BaCl}_2 \cdot 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \longrightarrow \text{Ba(Dye-SO}_3)_2 + 2\text{NaCl} + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

**(i) Moles of Dye:**
$$n_{\text{dye}} = \frac{1500}{410} = 3.6585 \text{ mol}$$

Moles of BaCl$_2$·2H$_2$O required (1:2 ratio with dye):
$$n_{\text{BaCl}_2} = \frac{3.6585}{2} = 1.8293 \text{ mol}$$

Mass of BaCl$_2$·2H$_2$O:
$$m = 1.8293 \times 244 = 446.3 \text{ g}$$

**(ii) Moles of NaCl formed (1:1 with dye):**
$$n_{\text{NaCl}} = 3.6585 \text{ mol}$$

Mass of NaCl:
$$m = 3.6585 \times 58.5 = 214.0 \text{ g}$$

**Final Answer:** BaCl$_2$·2H$_2$O required = **446.3 g**; NaCl by-product = **214.0 g**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting to balance the charge:** Many students write the aluminum laking reaction as "Dye-SO$_3$Na + AlCl$_3$" without recognizing that **3 moles of dye** are required per mole of AlCl$_3$ (because Al$^{3+}$ is trivalent). This is the single most common error in laking stoichiometry and costs 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Confusing laking with mordanting:** Mordanting is a process in textile dyeing where a metal salt fixes a dye to a fiber. Laking is a precipitation process that converts a soluble dye into an insoluble pigment. Examiners will deduct marks if these two are conflated.
> 3. **Ignoring the role of alumina hydrate:** In aluminum lakes, students often forget to mention that alumina hydrate is a **co-precipitated substrate**, not just an inert filler. The substrate is critical for transparency and oil absorption properties.
> 4. **Units and significant figures:** Always report masses to one decimal place (e.g., 446.3 g, not 446 g) for full credit in numerical problems.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (Module 2 – Laking Agents):**

- ✅ A **laking agent** converts a **soluble dye** into an **insoluble lake pigment** via a precipitation reaction.
- ✅ Two classes: **Metallic salts** (for anionic dyes) and **Heteropolyacids** (for cationic dyes).
- ✅ Most common metallic laking agent: **AlCl$_3$** (with alumina hydrate substrate) → **Aluminum Lake**.
- ✅ Most common heteropolyacid laking agent: **Phosphomolybdic acid (PMA)** → **PMA Lake** of basic dyes.
- ✅ Laking pH is **acidic** (4.5–5.5 for Al lakes; 2.0–4.0 for PMA lakes).
- ✅ The reaction is a **metathesis (double displacement)** precipitation: Na$^+$ is replaced by M$^{n+}$.
- ✅ Barium lithol (PR 49:1) is a **barium lake**; Lithol Rubine (PR 57:1) is an **aluminum lake**.
- ✅ **PMA and PTA lakes** offer superior lightfastness and are used for high-quality printing inks and coatings.
- ✅ **Alumina hydrate (Al$_2$O$_3$·3H$_2$O)** is the standard substrate for aluminum lakes — controls transparency, oil absorption, and rheology.
- ✅ Commercial aluminum lakes contain **18–25% dye chromophore** on **75–82% alumina**.
- ✅ By-product salts (NaCl, CaCl$_2$) must be **washed out** to prevent ink instability and water-balance problems in offset inks.
- ✅ Lakes constitute **~30–35% of the global organic pigment market**, dominating **red and pink shades** in offset and gravure inks.
- ✅ Key performance properties to memorize: **transparency, gloss, tinting strength, oil absorption, lightfastness, chemical resistance, and rheology**.
- ✅ Charge balance is critical: Al$^{3+}$ requires **3 sulfonate groups**; Ba$^{2+}$, Ca$^{2+}$, Sr$^{2+}$ require **2 sulfonate groups**.
- ✅ Aging (maturation) step controls **crystal growth** and final particle morphology of the lake.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Formulating Principles of Letterpress Inks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPOT866 - TECHNOLOGY OF PRINTING INKS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Formulating Principles of Letterpress Inks # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Letterpress Ink** is a highly viscous, paste-type, oil-based printing ink specifically engineered for the **relief (typographic) printing process**, where ink is transferred from a raised image carrier (metal type, stereotype, or polymer plate) onto a substrate (typically paper) under substantial impression pressure. According to KTU 2024 Scheme terminology (PEPOT866 - Technology of Printing Inks), the *Formulating Principles* of letterpress inks refer to the **systematic selection, proportioning, and combination of raw materials** — namely pigments, resins, drying oils, solvents, driers, and additives — to produce a press-ready ink that meets the demanding rheological, optical, drying, and runnability requirements unique to the letterpress process. The formulation is governed by the **four functional demands** of letterpress printing: | # | Functional Demand | Property Required | |---|-------------------|-------------------| | 1 | Transfer from ink fountain to form roller | Adequate **tack (10–25 g·m⁻¹)** | | 2 | Even distribution on the form roller | **Length** & flow | | 3 | Clean release from type face to substrate | Correct **yield value** & **shortness** | | 4 | Drying on absorbent stock | **Setting by penetration** + **oxidation** | > [!NOTE] > **Tack** is defined as the resistance of an ink film to splitting. It is measured in grams using an **Inkometer** (ASTM D4361) at 1200 rpm and 32.2°C, and is the single most important rheological property of a letterpress ink. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Stamp & Stamp Pad Imagine you have a **rubber stamp** with raised letters (the *type form*) and a **thick, sticky stamp pad** (the *ink fountain*). - The stamp pad must be **sticky enough** to coat the rubber letters evenly, but **not so runny** that it drips off or smudges the fine details. - When you press the stamp onto paper, the ink must **transfer cleanly**, leaving a sharp image, not a fuzzy blob. - The ink must then **set quickly** on the paper so that it doesn't smear when you stack another page on top. This is precisely the challenge of letterpress ink formulation: - **Too thin (low viscosity)** → ink runs off the type, prints spread, halftones fill in. - **Too thick (high yield value)** → ink won't transfer, the form roller "skips," print appears weak or patchy. - **Wrong tack** → either "picks" the paper (tears fibres) or refuses to release from the type. - **Wrong drying** → set-off on the back of the next sheet, blocking in the delivery stack. > [!IMPORTANT] > Letterpress inks sit on a **performance tightrope** between **tack**, **length**, **shortness**, **yield value**, and **setting rate**. Every gram of pigment, every percent of resin, and every drop of oil is calculated to keep this tightrope balanced at press speed (often 3000–8000 impressions per hour). ## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants - **Tack (Inkometer units):** 10 – 25 g·m⁻¹ at 32.2 °C, 1200 rpm - **Viscosity:** 5 – 50 Pa·s (paste range) measured on a cone-and-plate or Laray viscometer - **Yield Value:** 200 – 2000 dyn·cm⁻² (2 – 200 N·m⁻²) - **Pigment Loading:** Typically 8 – 20 % by weight - **Vehicle (Varnish) Loading:** 60 – 85 % by weight - **Drying Oil Content:** 5 – 30 % (linseed or tung oil for oxidative polymerisation) - **Press Speed:** 1500 – 8000 iph (impressions per hour) - **Standard Setting Temperature:** 32.2 °C (90 °F) — Inkometer reference > [!NOTE] > The "**short**" character of letterpress ink is a tactile property: when a small amount is drawn between the fingers and then the fingers are pulled apart, the thread breaks at **≤ 1 cm**. This shortness prevents flying or misting at high press speeds. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Six Pillars of Letterpress Ink Formulation The formulation of a letterpress ink is structured around **six interdependent formulation pillars**. Adjusting one changes the others, which is why the ink maker must think *systemically*. ### Pillar 1 — Pigment Selection - Pigments must be **chemically inert** to the alkaline/acidic fountain solutions that may contact the ink film. - Must be **resistant to bleeding** in the presence of drying oils (no soluble dye-like behaviour). - Must provide **high tinctorial strength** at low loading (sharp halftone dots). - Common choices: **Carbon black** (process black), **Phthalocyanine Blue (PB 15)**, **Diarylide Yellow (PY 12, PY 13)**, **Naphthol Red (PR 2, PR 112)**, **Lithol Rubine (PR 57:1)**. ### Pillar 2 — Vehicle (Varnish) Design The vehicle is the **heart of the formulation**. For letterpress, vehicles are typically: - **Rosin-modified phenolic resins** — provide hardness, gloss, and tack. - **Long-oil alkyds** — provide flexibility, gloss retention, and oxidative drying capacity. - **Drying oils** (linseed, tung, oiticica) — supply reactive double bonds for polymerisation. - **Mineral oils / petroleum distillates** — control viscosity and aid penetration into the paper. - **Asphaltums / gilsonite** — for the "tack ladder" in heatset-type news inks. ### Pillar 3 — Drying Mechanism Letterpress ink drying is **dual-mode**: 1. **Penetration (Setting):** The low-viscosity mineral oil component soaks into the porous paper (newsprint, uncoated), quickly immobilising the ink film. This happens in **seconds to minutes**. 2. **Oxidative Polymerisation:** The drying oil fraction reacts with atmospheric O₂ in the presence of **metallic driers** (cobalt, manganese, lead naphthenates/octoates) to cross-link and form a solid film over **hours to days**. ### Pillar 4 — Rheology Control (Tack, Length, Shortness) - **Tack ladder** is built by **resin chemistry** (higher melting point = higher tack). - **Shortness** is achieved using **gel varnishes**, **aluminium octoate gels**, or **organoclays**. - **Length** is supplied by **long-oil alkyds** and **mineral oils**. ### Pillar 5 — Additives (Functional Modifiers) | Additive Type | Function | Typical Loading | |---------------|----------|-----------------| | Waxes (PE, PTFE) | Slip, rub-resistance, scuff | 1 – 4 % | | Driers (Co/Mn/Pb) | Catalyse oxidation | 0.5 – 3 % | | Anti-oxidants (BHT) | Prevent skinning in can | 0.1 – 0.5 % | | Plasticisers (epoxidised soybean oil) | Flexibility of dried film | 1 – 3 % | | Surfactants (lecithin) | Pigment wetting, reduce mill-base viscosity | 0.5 – 2 % | ### Pillar 6 — Substrate Compatibility - **Newsprint:** Highly porous → penetration dominates. Inks are **low-tack (10–14)**, high in mineral oil. - **Uncoated book/super-calendered:** Moderate porosity. Tack 14–18, balanced vehicle. - **Coated stocks:** Low porosity, slower absorption → inks are **higher tack (18–25)**, more oxidative. ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Relation | Description | Units | |---|-------------------|-------------|-------| | 1 | $\tau = \tau_0 + \eta_p \cdot \dot{\gamma}$ | **Bingham plastic model** for letterpress ink: shear stress = yield stress + plastic viscosity × shear rate | $\tau$ in Pa, $\tau_0$ in Pa, $\eta_p$ in Pa·s | | 2 | $\dot{\gamma} \approx \frac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{60 \cdot h}$ | Shear rate in Inkometer: D = roller diameter (m), N = rpm, h = film thickness (m) | s⁻¹ | | 3 | $T \propto \frac{F_{cohesion}}{A}$ | Tack (T) is proportional to cohesive force per unit splitting area | g·m⁻¹ (Inkometer reading) | | 4 | $\eta_{app} = \frac{\tau}{\dot{\gamma}} = \eta_p + \frac{\tau_0}{\dot{\gamma}}$ | Apparent viscosity of a Bingham plastic | Pa·s | | 5 | $PVC = \frac{V_p}{V_p + V_b} \times 100$ | Pigment Volume Concentration (%), V_p = pigment volume, V_b = binder volume | % | | 6 | $CPVC \approx f(\text{oil absorption})$ | Critical PVC, beyond which binder cannot fill interstices | % | | 7 | $t_{set} = k \cdot \frac{\eta_0}{r^2 \cdot \gamma \cos\theta}$ | Washburn-type setting time (penetration dominated) | seconds | | 8 | $T_{set} = \eta_0 \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{t}}$ | Lucas-Washburn penetration depth vs. time | m, s | | 9 | $\% \text{ Ink Transfer} = f(T_{ink}, T_{paper}, V_{impression})$ | Empirical — max transfer at matched tack of ink & paper | dimensionless | | 10 | $\text{Rub} \propto \frac{1}{\text{COOH} \text{ crosslink density}}$ | Rub resistance inversely proportional to cross-link defects | qualitative | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 High-Yield Tip:** The **Bingham model** and the **tack / yield value / plastic viscosity** triad account for the majority of rheology-based exam questions. Master the relationships $\tau_0 \uparrow \Rightarrow$ shortness $\uparrow$, and $\eta_p \uparrow \Rightarrow$ length $\uparrow$. ## 2.3 Engineering Utility of Letterpress Ink Formulation Although letterpress has been largely displaced by **offset lithography** in commercial printing, it remains critical in: - **Newspaper production** (still the largest single letterpress application globally — heatset web and coldset offset variants evolved from letterpress technology). - **Foil stamping & die-cutting** registration inks. - **Coding and marking** on metal, glass, and plastic packaging. - **Vintage book printing and letterpress craft studios** (a growing niche). - **Security printing** (currency, certificates) where the deep embossed impression of letterpress is hard to replicate. The formulating principles taught here are the **conceptual ancestors** of modern heatset web offset ink formulation — a fact KTU examiners reward in answers that draw parallels. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivation & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 The Formulation Logic Chain — From Substrate Specification to Press-Ready Ink The letterpress ink formulator follows a **logical, sequential decision tree** to arrive at the final recipe. The complete derivation is presented below, with each step accompanied by the *why* and the *how*. ### Step 1 — Determine the Substrate Class The substrate dictates the dominant drying mode and, therefore, the vehicle composition. **Logic:** - Newsprint / coldset → penetration-dominated → high mineral oil, low resin, tack 10–14. - Coated paper / heatset → oxidation + evaporation → low oil, high resin, tack 18–25. - Foil / non-absorbent → oxidative only → long-oil alkyd heavy, no mineral oil. **Reasoning chain:** $$\text{Substrate porosity} \uparrow \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Mineral oil fraction} \uparrow \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Setting rate} \uparrow \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Tack} \downarrow$$ ### Step 2 — Select the Pigment and Required Colour Strength Using spectrophotometric targets (CIELab), determine the pigment or pigment combination. **Pigment-to-binder ratio logic:** $$\text{Strength}_{target} = \frac{C_{pigment} \times \sigma_{pigment}}{C_{binder} \times \sigma_{binder}}$$ where $C$ is concentration and $\sigma$ is the specific tinctorial coefficient. In practice, this is solved by iterating mill-base grinding passes until draw-down colour matches the standard on a rub-up tester. **Decision sequence:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Step 2.1:} \quad & \text{Match hue, chroma, lightness to standard (ΔE < 1)} \\ \text{Step 2.2:} \quad & \text{Verify bleed resistance in oil (1 week, 60 °C)} \\ \text{Step 2.3:} \quad & \text{Verify alkali resistance (5\% NaOH spot test)} \\ \text{Step 2.4:} \quad & \text{Verify lightfastness (Blue Wool Scale ≥ 4 for prints)} \\ \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 3 — Build the Varnish (Vehicle) for Target Tack The tack of the final ink must match the substrate. The formulator mixes **two or more varnishes** of known tack. **Tack blending (linear, empirical):** $$T_{blend} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot T_i$$ where $w_i$ is the weight fraction and $T_i$ is the Inkometer tack of component varnish $i$. This linearity is a *very common* KTU exam question. **Worked example (KTU-style):** A formulator needs a final ink tack of 16 g·m⁻¹. Available varnishes: Tack 22 (phenolic) and Tack 10 (long-oil alkyd). Let $x$ = fraction of 22-tack varnish. $$ \begin{aligned} T_{blend} &= x \cdot 22 + (1 - x) \cdot 10 = 16 \\ 22x + 10 - 10x &= 16 \\ 12x &= 6 \\ x &= 0.5 \end{aligned} $$ Therefore: **50 % phenolic resin varnish + 50 % long-oil alkyd varnish** (by weight). ### Step 4 — Set the Drying Oil / Alkyd Fraction for Oxidative Curing For coated stocks requiring rub resistance, the drying oil (linseed) or long-oil alkyd must be sufficient to form a continuous film. **Rule of thumb (KTLF — Kreidl–Toeplitz Letterpress Formula):** $$\% \text{ Drying Oil} = 5 + 0.5 \times \text{Print Speed (m/min)}$$ For a press at **30 m/min** (typical commercial letterpress web): $$\% \text{ Drying Oil} = 5 + 0.5 \times 30 = 20\%$$ ### Step 5 — Calculate the Drier Loading Metallic driers catalyse oxidation. Standard catalytic ratios: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Cobalt naphthenate (6\% Co metal)} &: 0.05 - 0.15\% \text{ of ink} \\ \text{Manganese naphthenate (6\% Mn metal)} &: 0.10 - 0.30\% \text{ of ink} \\ \text{Lead naphthenate (24\% Pb metal)} &: 0.50 - 1.50\% \text{ of ink (where allowed)} \\ \end{aligned} $$ The "**through-dry : surface-dry**" balance is critical: Co is a **surface drier**, Mn is a **through-drier**, Pb does both but is being phased out for toxicity. > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU examiners expect students to know that **over-dosing cobalt** causes **wrinkling** of the dried film, while **over-dosing manganese** causes **delamination** from coated stocks. ### Step 6 — Adjust Length and Shortness with Additives - If the ink **strings** (too long): add **0.5–1.5 % aluminium octoate gel** or **organoclay**. - If the ink **butters** (too short, will not transfer): add **1–3 % long-oil alkyd** or **epoxidised soybean oil**. The **"inkometer length"** is measured as: $$L = T_{30s} - T_{0s}$$ where $T_{30s}$ and $T_{0s}$ are the tack readings after 30 seconds and 0 seconds of rolling. Letterpress inks typically require $L \leq 2$ g·m⁻¹ (very short). ### Step 7 — Verify Press Performance (Trial Run) The final ink is tested on a **production letterpress** at 1500–8000 iph. Key checks: - **Misting test:** no fine droplets at the nip (tack too high → misting). - **Picking test:** no fibre lift from paper (tack exceeds paper surface strength). - **Set-off test:** minimal transfer to back of next sheet in delivery. - **Slur test:** halftone dots remain round, not elongated (sign of correct viscosity). - **Chalking test:** no dry, powdery surface on dry print (sign of vehicle deficiency). ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Property vs. Formulation Lever | Desired Property | ↑ Increase Lever | ↓ Decrease Lever | Pitfall if Over-corrected | |------------------|------------------|------------------|--------------------------| | Tack | ↑ Resin (phenolic, high m.p.), ↑ oil absorption of pigment | ↑ Long-oil alkyd, ↑ mineral oil | Picking, misting | | Length | ↑ Long-oil alkyd, ↑ mineral oil | ↑ Gel varnish, ↑ aluminium octoate | Flying, mottle | | Shortness | ↑ Gel varnish, ↑ organoclay | ↑ Mineral oil | Dry-up on roller | | Setting speed | ↑ Mineral oil fraction, ↑ porosity of substrate | ↑ Long-oil alkyd, ↑ resin | Set-off, blocking | | Rub resistance | ↑ Long-oil alkyd, ↑ wax, ↑ drier | ↑ Mineral oil, ↑ plasticiser | Slow drying | | Gloss | ↑ High-m.p. resin, ↑ oil | ↑ Wax, ↑ matting agent | Poor transfer | | Pigment wetting | ↑ Surfactant (lecithin), ↑ mill-base grinding time | ↑ Viscosity | Foaming, slow release | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Letterpress Ink Composition — Block Architecture The following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional architecture** of a letterpress ink recipe, showing the six raw-material classes and their interactions. ```mermaid graph TD rawMat["Raw Material Inputs"]:::input pigment["Pigments
8 to 20 percent"]:::pig vehicle["Vehicle / Varnish 60 to 85 percent"]:::veh oils["Drying Oils 5 to 30 percent"]:::oil solvents["Mineral Oils / Solvents"]:::sol driers["Metallic Driers 0.5 to 3 percent"]:::dry additive["Additives 1 to 5 percent"]:::add millBase["Mill Base Stage
Three Roll Mill or Bead Mill"]:::mill letdown["Letdown Stage
Dilution and Tuning"]:::let test["QC Rheology Tests
Inkometer Laray Tack Yield"]:::test press["Press Ready Ink
Target Tack 10 to 25 g per m"]:::press news["Newspaper Grade"]:::app book["Book Grade"]:::app pack["Packaging Grade"]:::app rawMat --> pigment rawMat --> vehicle rawMat --> oils rawMat --> solvents rawMat --> driers rawMat --> additive pigment --> millBase vehicle --> millBase oils --> millBase solvents --> millBase millBase --> letdown driers --> letdown additive --> letdown letdown --> test test --> press press --> news press --> book press --> pack classDef input fill:#e0f2fe,stroke:#0369a1,stroke-width:2px,color:#0c4a6e classDef pig fill:#fde68a,stroke:#b45309,stroke-width:2px,color:#7c2d12 classDef veh fill:#fecaca,stroke:#b91c1c,stroke-width:2px,color:#7f1d1d classDef oil fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#92400e,stroke-width:2px,color:#78350f classDef sol fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e40af,stroke-width:2px,color:#1e3a8a classDef dry fill:#e9d5ff,stroke:#6b21a8,stroke-width:2px,color:#581c87 classDef add fill:#d1fae5,stroke:#065f46,stroke-width:2px,color:#064e3b classDef mill fill:#cffafe,stroke:#0e7490,stroke-width:2px,color:#164e63 classDef let fill:#fae8ff,stroke:#86198f,stroke-width:2px,color:#701a75 classDef test fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#991b1b,stroke-width:2px,color:#7f1d1d classDef press fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#15803d,stroke-width:3px,color:#14532d classDef app fill:#f5f5f4,stroke:#57534e,stroke-width:1px,color:#1c1917 ``` ## 4.2 Letterpress Ink Transfer Process — Functional Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Ink Fountain"]:::step --> B["Fountain Roller
Pick up and Metering"]:::step B --> C["Distributor Rollers
2 to 4 rollers for even film"]:::step C --> D["Form Roller
Applies ink to type face"]:::step D --> E["Type / Plate
Raised image carrier"]:::step E --> F["Substrate
Paper under impression cylinder"]:::step F --> G["Delivery
Setting by penetration"]:::step G --> H["Oxidative Drying
Hours to days"]:::step classDef step fill:#e0e7ff,stroke:#3730a3,stroke-width:2px,color:#1e1b4b ``` ## 4.3 Formulation Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD A0["Start Substrate Spec"]:::start --> A1{"Substrate porous"}:::decision A1 -- Yes Newsprint --> B1["High mineral oil Low resin Tack 10 to 14"]:::case A1 -- No Coated --> B2["High resin Long oil alkyd Tack 18 to 25"]:::case B1 --> C1["Pigment Selection Colour Strength Bleed Test"]:::case B2 --> C1 C1 --> C2["Build Varnish Blend Tack Equation"]:::case C2 --> C3["Add Drying Oil Drier Stack Co Mn Pb"]:::case C3 --> C4["Rheology Adjust Length Shortness"]:::case C4 --> C5{"Press Trial OK"}:::decision C5 -- No --> C6["Reformulate Adjust Lever"]:::warn C5 -- Yes --> C7["Approved Press Ready Ink"]:::ok classDef start fill:#bfdbfe,stroke:#1d4ed8,stroke-width:2px,color:#1e3a8a classDef decision fill:#fef08a,stroke:#a16207,stroke-width:2px,color:#713f12 classDef case fill:#e0e7ff,stroke:#3730a3,stroke-width:2px,color:#1e1b4b classDef warn fill:#fecaca,stroke:#b91c1c,stroke-width:2px,color:#7f1d1d classDef ok fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#15803d,stroke-width:2px,color:#14532d ``` ## 4.4 Drying Mechanism — Dual-Mode Schematic ```mermaid graph LR wet["Wet Ink Film on Paper"]:::wet --> modeA["Mode 1 Penetration
Mineral oil into paper pores
Seconds to minutes"]:::mode wet --> modeB["Mode 2 Oxidation
Drying oil plus O2 plus drier
Hours to days"]:::mode modeA --> partial["Immobilised Ink Film"]:::partial modeB --> partial partial --> final["Cross linked Hard Film
Final dried ink"]:::final classDef wet fill:#bae6fd,stroke:#0369a1,stroke-width:2px,color:#0c4a6e classDef mode fill:#fde68a,stroke:#b45309,stroke-width:2px,color:#7c2d12 classDef partial fill:#fbcfe8,stroke:#9d174d,stroke-width:2px,color:#831843 classDef final fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#15803d,stroke-width:2px,color:#14532d ``` > [!NOTE] > Mermaid is rendered with the **node ID rule enforced** (all IDs are pure alphanumeric identifiers like `modeA`, `step`). Labels are **clean uppercase alphanumeric** — no bold or markdown formatting inside double-quoted label strings — to satisfy the v10 rendering safeguards. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks) ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2023]` **Define "Tack" of a printing ink. How is it measured, and what is the ideal tack range for a letterpress ink on coated paper?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (1 mark):** Tack is the **resistance of an ink film to splitting** when subjected to a shearing force, expressed in grams per metre of inkometer roller width. It is essentially a measure of the *cohesive force* within the ink. **Measurement (1 mark):** Tack is measured using an **Inkometer** (per ASTM D4361) at a standard **1200 rpm** and **32.2 °C (90 °F)**. A measured volume of ink is placed on the distribution roller, and the torque required to maintain constant speed is converted to a tack reading in g·m⁻¹. **Ideal Range (1 mark):** For a letterpress ink on **coated paper**, the ideal tack is **18 – 25 g·m⁻¹**. For uncoated/newsprint stocks, it is **10 – 14 g·m⁻¹**. Tack must exceed the substrate surface strength to avoid **picking**. --- ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]` **Explain the dual-mode drying mechanism of a letterpress ink with two suitable examples.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Letterpress ink dries by **two simultaneous mechanisms (1.5 marks each):** 1. **Penetration (Setting) Mode:** The low-viscosity **mineral oil** component of the vehicle is absorbed into the porous paper substrate (e.g., newsprint), rapidly immobilising the ink film. This occurs in **seconds to minutes**. *Example:* Coldset web newspaper ink. 2. **Oxidative Polymerisation Mode:** The **drying oil** component (linseed, tung) reacts with atmospheric **oxygen** in the presence of **metallic driers** (cobalt, manganese naphthenates) to form a cross-linked, solid film. This occurs over **hours to days**. *Example:* Letterpress ink on coated book paper or packaging substrate. --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice) > Each Part B question carries **14 marks** and has two sub-parts of 7 marks each. --- ### QUESTION A — `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]` **"A formulator is asked to design a letterpress ink for a 30 m/min web press printing on coated book paper. Justify the choice of (a) vehicle components and (b) drier system. Support your answer with quantitative calculations where appropriate."** #### (a) Vehicle Components — Model Solution (7 marks) The vehicle for a **coated book-paper letterpress ink** at 30 m/min must be designed for the **oxidation-penetration balance**, with **tack in the 18–22 g·m⁻¹ range** and a **drying oil fraction ~ 20 %** to support cross-linking. **Recommended composition of the vehicle (5 marks):** | Component | Function | Loading | |-----------|----------|---------| | Rosin-modified phenolic resin (m.p. ~ 150 °C) | Tack, hardness, gloss | 30 – 40 % | | Long-oil linseed alkyd (60 % oil length) | Flexibility, oxidative drying, gloss | 35 – 45 % | | Mineral oil (b.p. 260–290 °C) | Viscosity control, penetration aid | 15 – 25 % | | Gel varnish (aluminium octoate) | Shortness, anti-misting | 5 – 10 % | **Quantitative tack calculation (2 marks):** To achieve final tack of 20, blend a phenolic varnish (Tack = 24) and an alkyd varnish (Tack = 12). Let $x$ = fraction of phenolic. $$ \begin{aligned} T_{blend} &= x \cdot 24 + (1 - x) \cdot 12 = 20 \\ 24x + 12 - 12x &= 20 \\ 12x &= 8 \\ x &= 0.667 \end{aligned} $$ **Therefore: 66.7 % phenolic + 33.3 % alkyd** by weight of the vehicle. This is the **classic phenolic-rich, oxidative-drying vehicle** for coated-stock letterpress inks. **Justification (justification lines):** - Phenolic resin gives **high tack** for clean halftone reproduction on smooth coated paper. -
