Long-oil alkyd provides the **drying oil functional groups** for cross-linking. - Mineral oil provides **initial setting** and **roller-stripping** efficiency. - Gel varnish enforces **shortness**, preventing flying and misting at 30 m/min press speed. #### (b) Drier System — Model Solution (7 marks) **Recommended drier stack (5 marks):** | Drier | Role | Loading (% of ink) | |-------|------|--------------------| | Cobalt naphthenate (6 % Co) | Surface drier — initiates oxidation | 0.10 – 0.15 % | | Manganese naphthenate (6 % Mn) | Through-drier — propagates cross-linking | 0.20 – 0.30 % | | Lead naphthenate (24 % Pb) | Synergist — combines surface & through action | 0.50 – 1.00 % (or replace with Zr / Ca) | | Anti-oxidant (BHT) | Prevents in-can skinning | 0.10 – 0.20 % | **Quantitative calculation — total metal content (2 marks):** For a 1000 kg batch with 0.12 % Co + 0.25 % Mn + 0.80 % Pb drier: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Mass of Co} &= 1000 \times 0.0012 \times 0.06 = 0.072 \text{ kg} \\ \text{Mass of Mn} &= 1000 \times 0.0025 \times 0.06 = 0.150 \text{ kg} \\ \text{Mass of Pb} &= 1000 \times 0.0080 \times 0.24 = 1.920 \text{ kg} \end{aligned} $$ Total active metal = **2.142 kg** in 1000 kg ink = **0.21 % active metal**, well within the safe range to avoid wrinkling, brittleness, or retardation. **Justification:** - Cobalt is the **primary surface drier** — without it, the surface stays tacky and set-off occurs. - Manganese is the **through-drier** — ensures full film hardness in 6–24 hours. - Lead acts as a **synergist** (or is replaced by Ca/Zr octoates for low-toxicity formulations). - BHT is critical — it scavenges oxygen in the can to prevent surface skinning during storage, but is consumed before the ink reaches the substrate. --- ### QUESTION B — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]` *(Internal Choice Alternative)* **"Discuss the formulating principles of letterpress ink in terms of (a) rheology control and (b) substrate compatibility. Use specific raw materials and present the tack equation for a 50:50 blend of two varnishes of tack 26 and 14."** #### (a) Rheology Control — Model Solution (7 marks) **Rheology of letterpress ink (3 marks):** Letterpress ink is a **non-Newtonian, shear-thinning, Bingham-plastic fluid**. It exhibits: - A **yield value** ($\tau_0$): the minimum stress required for the ink to flow. - A **plastic viscosity** ($\eta_p$): the resistance to flow once the yield value is exceeded. The Bingham model: $$\tau = \tau_0 + \eta_p \cdot \dot{\gamma}$$ **Tack (1 mark):** The most critical rheological parameter, equal to the **cohesion of the ink film** under the splitting action of the rollers. Higher tack = greater cohesion = greater resistance to splitting. **Length and Shortness (1 mark):** These are **tactile descriptors** of how the ink behaves when a thread is pulled between two surfaces. *Length* = the distance the thread can be extended before breaking. *Shortness* = the ability to break cleanly with little stringing. Letterpress inks must be **short** to prevent flying at high press speeds. **Formulation levers (2 marks):** | Rheological Property | ↑ Increase by | ↓ Decrease by | |----------------------|---------------|---------------| | Tack | ↑ Resin (phenolic, high m.p.), ↑ Pigment oil absorption | ↑ Long-oil alkyd, ↑ Mineral oil | | Yield value | ↑ Aluminium octoate gel, ↑ Carbon black loading | ↑ Long-oil alkyd | | Plastic viscosity | ↑ Pigment loading, ↑ Resin concentration | ↑ Mineral oil, ↑ Heat | | Shortness | ↑ Gel varnish, ↑ Organoclay | ↑ Mineral oil | | Length | ↑ Long-oil alkyd, ↑ Mineral oil | ↑ Gel varnish | #### (b) Substrate Compatibility — Model Solution (7 marks) **Three principal substrate classes (5 marks):** 1. **Newsprint (Coldset Web):** Highly porous, weak surface. Inks are formulated with **low tack (10–14)**, high mineral oil (30–45 %), and rely **almost entirely on penetration drying**. Pigment loading is moderate (8–12 % carbon black). Anti-offset spray powder is used in delivery. 2. **Uncoated / Super-calendered Book Paper:** Moderate porosity. Inks have **intermediate tack (14–18)**, balanced mineral oil and alkyd, and dual drying (penetration + oxidation). Gloss is moderate. 3. **Coated Book / Cover Paper:** Low porosity, smooth, strong surface. Inks are **high-tack (18–25)**, low mineral oil (≤ 15 %), high phenolic resin and long-oil alkyd. Drying is **predominantly oxidative**, requiring a full drier stack (Co/Mn/Pb). Gloss is high; rub resistance is critical. **Tack equation for the requested 50:50 blend (2 marks):** Given: Varnish A (tack = 26), Varnish B (tack = 14), equal weight fractions (w_A = 0.5, w_B = 0.5). $$ \begin{aligned} T_{blend} &= w_A \cdot T_A + w_B \cdot T_B \\ T_{blend} &= (0.5)(26) + (0.5)(14) \\ T_{blend} &= 13 + 7 = \mathbf{20 \text{ g·m}^{-1}} \end{aligned} $$ This 50:50 blend yields a tack of **20 g·m⁻¹**, which is the **ideal range for coated-paper letterpress** — a perfect illustration of the tack-blending principle. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > - **Confusing tack with viscosity.** Tack is *cohesion*; viscosity is *resistance to flow*. Examiners deduct 1 mark for interchange. > - **Forgetting units** on tack readings (must state g·m⁻¹ and reference ASTM D4361 at 32.2 °C / 1200 rpm). > - **Writing "letterpress ink dries by evaporation"** — this is the most common error. Letterpress ink does **not** primarily dry by evaporation; it dries by **penetration + oxidation**. Evaporation is a heatset-web offset concept. > - **Omitting the anti-oxidant** in the drier system discussion — examiners expect this to balance the in-can stability argument. > - **Using the wrong shear rate equation** in the Inkometer — students often forget the 60 in the denominator for rpm→rps conversion. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - **Letterpress ink** is a paste-type, high-tack ink for relief printing under mechanical impression. - **Tack** is the *single most important* rheological property, measured on an **Inkometer** at **1200 rpm, 32.2 °C**. - Ideal tack: **10–14** (newsprint), **14–18** (uncoated), **18–25** (coated). - The **Bingham plastic model** describes letterpress ink rheology: $\tau = \tau_0 + \eta_p \cdot \dot{\gamma}$. - **Tack blending is linear:** $T_{blend} = \sum w_i T_i$ — use this for any formulation calculation. - **Dual-mode drying:** (1) Penetration of mineral oil into paper + (2) Oxidative polymerisation of drying oils. - **Vehicle composition:** rosin-modified phenolic resin + long-oil alkyd + mineral oil + gel varnish. - **Drier stack:** Cobalt (surface) + Manganese (through) + Lead (synergist); anti-oxidant (BHT) in-can. - **Pigment loading:** 8 – 20 % by weight; pigments must resist bleeding in oil and alkali. - **Shortness** prevents misting and flying; achieved via gel varnish and organoclays. - **Length** is controlled by long-oil alkyd and mineral oil; required for ink distribution. - **Additives:** Waxes (rub, slip), surfactants (wetting), plasticisers (flexibility), anti-oxidants (in-can stability). - **Substrate dictates formulation:** porous → penetration drying; non-porous → oxidative drying. - **Picking** = tack too high relative to paper surface strength; **Misting** = tack too high + press speed too high; **Set-off** = drying too slow. - **CPVC (Critical Pigment Volume Concentration)** is the threshold above which the binder cannot fill pigment interstices; ink is usually formulated at or slightly above CPVC for letterpress. - The **Washburn equation** ($t_{set} \propto \eta_0 / r^2$) governs penetration setting kinetics. - **Cobalt over-dosing** → wrinkling; **Manganese over-dosing** → delamination; **Lead over-dosing** → embrittlement. - The **tack-blending linear equation** is the most frequently asked quantitative question in KTU exams for this module. - Letterpress technology is the **conceptual ancestor** of heatset web offset — drawing parallels earns appreciation marks.

topics.title: Lithographic Inks
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT866 - TECHNOLOGY OF PRINTING INKS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Lithographic Inks

---

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Lithographic Ink** is a highly specialised, paste-like, oil-based (or oleoresinous) ink formulation engineered specifically for the **planographic (lithographic) offset printing process**, where the image and non-image areas lie on the same plane of the printing plate, and differentiation relies on the **physico-chemical contrast** between hydrophobic (oleophilic) image areas and hydrophilic (oleophobic) non-image areas.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Exact Terminology):**
> *"Lithographic inks are viscous, paste-type inks formulated with selected pigments, oleoresinous vehicles, drying oils, resins, solvents, and additives, designed to operate in the presence of an aqueous fountain solution, exhibit controlled emulsification, transfer via split-film mechanism, and set/dry rapidly through oxidative polymerisation, penetration, and/or evaporation."*

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **kitchen grease-coated pan vs. a water-soaked sponge** sitting side by side on the same flat countertop:

- The **greasy pan** repels water but attracts oil.
- The **sponge** loves water but rejects oil.

Now, when you splash a **mixture of oil + pigment (the ink) and water (fountain solution)** across this countertop:
- The oil-and-pigment mixture clings *only* to the greasy spots (forming the image).
- The water clings *only* to the sponge spots (protecting the non-image).

A lithographic ink is essentially this "greasy paint" — it must **simultaneously repel fountain solution water**, **adhere firmly to the image area**, **transfer cleanly through the rubber blanket**, and **dry quickly** once on paper without smearing.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Think of a lithographic ink as a *selectively-sociable friend* at a party — it befriends oil and the image plate, but politely refuses to mingle with the water-based fountain solution crowd.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics in Lithographic Inks

The following parameters are critical and **must be memorised** for KTU valuation:

- **Tack value:** Typically **8 to 14 tack units** (measured on a **Inkometer** at 1200 rpm, 90 °F, after 1 minute).
- **Viscosity range:** **10 to 50 Pa·s** (10,000 to 50,000 cP).
- **Yield value (yield stress):** **200 to 1500 dyn/cm²** (0.02 to 0.15 N/cm²).
- **Water pick-up (emulsification):** Maximum **30%** by weight for sheet-fed; up to **50%** for heat-set web.
- **Film thickness on press:** **0.5 to 2.5 μm** wet ink film.
- **Pigment loading:** Typically **10% to 25%** by weight.
- **Vehicle (varnish) content:** **50% to 70%** by weight.
- **Drying temperature (heat-set web):** **120 °C to 160 °C**.
- **Standard drying time:** ≤ **24 hours** for sheet-fed oxidative setting.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Favourite:** Any exam answer involving lithographic ink properties **must quote numerical ranges** — students who provide only qualitative descriptions typically lose 1–2 marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

---

## 2.1 Operational Architecture — The "Anatomy" of a Lithographic Ink

A modern lithographic ink is a **multi-phase colloidal system** consisting of four distinct functional phases. The harmonious interaction of these phases defines print quality.

### Phase A — Pigment (The "Colour Carrier")
- Provides **chromatic identity** (hue), **opacity**, and **chemical resistance**.
- Must be **chemically inert** to fountain solution and resistant to water bleed.
- Common pigments: **Phthalocyanine Blue (PB 15)**, **Carbon Black (PBk 7)**, **Arylide Yellows (PY 12, PY 13)**, **Lithol Rubine (PR 57:1)**.
- Particle size: **0.1 to 10 μm** (ideally < 2 μm for offset).
- Dispersion is achieved via **three-roll mill, bead mill, or attrition mill**.

### Phase B — Vehicle / Varnish (The "Binder + Solvent Carrier")
- Comprises **drying oils, resins, and high-boiling petroleum solvents**.
- **Functions:** wets the pigment, carries it to the substrate, and binds it permanently.
- Components:
  - **Drying oils:** Linseed oil (oxidative), tung oil (fast polymerisation).
  - **Hard resins:** Rosin-modified phenolic, alkyd, hydrocarbon (C9/C5).
  - **Solvents:** Mineral oils (boiling range **240 °C – 290 °C** for heat-set; **260 °C – 320 °C** for sheet-fed).

### Phase C — Additives (The "Performance Modifiers")
- **Waxes** (PE, PTFE, paraffin): improve rub resistance (1–3%).
- **Driers / Siccatives** (Co, Mn, Pb naphthenates): catalyse oxidative drying.
- **Anti-set-off compounds:** starch granules to prevent block stacking.
- **Surfactants:** tune ink-water balance.
- **Plasticisers:** improve flexibility.
- **Anti-oxidants:** extend shelf life.

### Phase D — The Invisible Phase: Fountain Solution Interaction
- Not part of the ink itself, but a **co-reactant** that creates the **water-in-ink emulsion**.
- Typical fountain solution pH: **4.0 – 5.5** (acidic for conventional) or **7.0 – 9.5** (for fountain-less / waterless systems).

---

## 2.2 Why Lithographic Inks Behave Differently — The Core "Why"

Lithographic inks differ fundamentally from letterpress or gravure inks due to **three unforgiving demands**:

1. **The Ink-Water Balance Problem:** The ink must tolerate up to 30% water emulsification without losing rheological integrity.
2. **The Split-Film Transfer Constraint:** The ink film splits at two nip points — *plate-to-blanket* and *blanket-to-substrate* — so it must flow, split, and re-flow perfectly.
3. **The Drying Speed Imperative:** Must set fast enough to prevent set-off yet slow enough to remain stable on the press for hours.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a regular oil paint fails:** A regular artist's oil paint absorbs water unpredictably, has uncontrolled viscosity, and never "sets" — making it useless for offset. Lithographic inks are precision-engineered to manage all three constraints simultaneously.

---

## 2.3 Drying / Setting Mechanisms — Four Pathways

| **Mechanism** | **Process** | **Typical Use** |
|---|---|---|
| **Oxidative Polymerisation** | Drying oils absorb O₂, cross-link via peroxide bridges. | Sheet-fed conventional |
| **Penetration (Quick-set)** | Solvent fraction penetrates paper pores; resin gels. | Cold-set / business forms |
| **Evaporation (Heat-set)** | Solvents driven off at 120–160 °C in a dryer. | Heat-set web offset (magazines) |
| **UV / EB Curing** | Photoinitiators trigger free-radical chain polymerisation. | UV offset (labels, packaging) |

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is a board-exam essential — memorise before every KTU exam.**

| **Quantity** | **Formula / Expression** | **Units / Notes** |
|---|---|---|
| **Tack Reduction Equation** | $\Delta T \;=\; T_{0} \;-\; T_{w}$ where $T_{0}$ = initial tack, $T_{w}$ = tack after water pickup | Tack units |
| **Water Pick-up %** | $W\% \;=\; \dfrac{M_{emul} \;-\; M_{ink}}{M_{ink}} \times 100$ | % by mass |
| **Emulsion Ratio (E)** | $E \;=\; \dfrac{V_{w}}{V_{i}}$ where $V_{w}$ = water volume, $V_{i}$ = ink volume | Dimensionless |
| **Ink Film Thickness on Substrate** | $t_{sub} \;=\; \dfrac{t_{plate}}{2}$ (under equal split) | μm |
| **Split Film Ratio** | $t_{1}/t_{2} \;=\; \dfrac{\eta_{2}}{\eta_{1}}$ (viscosity-inverse) | Dimensionless |
| **Yield Stress (Bingham Plastic)** | $\tau \;=\; \tau_{y} \;+\; \eta_{p} \cdot \dot{\gamma}$ | Pa (shear stress) |
| **Newtonian Limit (low shear)** | $\tau \;=\; \eta \cdot \dot{\gamma}$ | Pa·s |
| **Power-Law / Ostwald Model** | $\tau \;=\; K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{\,n}$ where $n < 1$ = pseudoplastic | Pa, dimensionless $n$ |
| **Drying Rate (heat-set)** | $R_{d} \;=\; \dfrac{h}{t_{d}}$ where $h$ = solvent mass, $t_{d}$ = dwell time | kg/s |
| **Pigment Volume Concentration** | $PVC \;=\; \dfrac{V_{pig}}{V_{pig} \;+\; V_{binder}} \times 100$ | % by volume |
| **Critical PVC (CPVC)** | Property transition point where binder just fills pigment voids | % by volume |
| **Setting Time (Quick-set)** | $t_{s} \;\propto\; \dfrac{\eta_{0}}{\eta_{\infty}}$ (initial/equilibrium viscosity ratio) | seconds |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Notation Rule:** In the above formulas, **never write absolute values using vertical bars** like $\vert x \vert$ inside markdown tables — use $\lvert x \rvert$ or describe qualitatively. This is a mandatory KTU Markdown rendering rule.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Packaging Industry:** Lithographic inks dominate folding cartons, labels, and flexible packaging due to excellent image reproduction.
- **Newspaper & Magazine Printing:** Heat-set web offset uses lithographic inks at speeds exceeding **15 m/s** (≈ 50,000 rpm equivalent web speed).
- **Security Printing:** Banknotes, stamps, and certificates use specialised lithographic inks (e.g., **OVI — Optically Variable Inks**) to prevent counterfeiting.
- **Food Packaging:** UV-curable and low-migration lithographic inks ensure regulatory compliance with **FDA / EU Regulation 1935/2004**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

---

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation 1 — The Tack Reduction by Water Pick-up

The empirical relationship between ink tack and water emulsification can be modelled linearly for small water fractions:

### Step 1: Define the initial tack
At the moment of mixing with fountain solution, the ink has a measurable initial tack $T_{0}$, measured on the Inkometer.

### Step 2: Introduce the water fraction
Let $f_{w}$ be the mass fraction of water emulsified in the ink, defined as:

$$
f_{w} \;=\; \dfrac{m_{w}}{m_{ink} \;+\; m_{w}}
$$

### Step 3: Write the linear tack decay model
For low water pick-up (≤ 30%), tack reduces approximately linearly:

$$
T \;=\; T_{0} \;-\; k \cdot f_{w}
$$

where $k$ is the **tack sensitivity coefficient** (a property of the vehicle system; typically 8–15 tack units per unit water fraction).

### Step 4: Solve for the allowable water fraction
Given a target minimum tack $T_{min}$ (e.g., 6 tack units), rearrange:

$$
f_{w,\max} \;=\; \dfrac{T_{0} \;-\; T_{min}}{k}
$$

### Step 5: Convert to water pick-up percentage
Multiplying by 100:

$$
W_{\max}\,\% \;=\; \dfrac{T_{0} \;-\; T_{min}}{k} \times 100
$$

### Step 6: Numerical example for KTU
Let $T_{0} = 11$, $T_{min} = 6$, $k = 10$:

$$
W_{\max}\,\% \;=\; \dfrac{11 \;-\; 6}{10} \times 100 \;=\; 50\,\%
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** This ink can tolerate up to 50% water pick-up before its tack falls below the printable minimum — typical of heat-set web inks.

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation 2 — Split-Film Thickness Distribution

When ink transfers from plate → blanket → substrate, it splits at each nip. Assuming **equal viscosity splits**, the film is divided in inverse proportion to the receiving surface's absorbency.

### Step 1: Define total film thickness on plate
$$
t_{plate} \;=\; 2.5\;\mu m \quad \text{(standard offset film)}
$$

### Step 2: Apply the Scott Equation (simplified)
$$
t_{blanket} \;=\; t_{plate} \times \phi_{b}
$$

where $\phi_{b}$ is the **transfer coefficient** between plate and blanket, empirically **0.45 to 0.55**.

### Step 3: Compute blanket film
Using $\phi_{b} = 0.50$:

$$
t_{blanket} \;=\; 2.5 \times 0.50 \;=\; 1.25\;\mu m
$$

### Step 4: Compute substrate film
Using $\phi_{s} = 0.55$ (paper absorbs more than blanket):

$$
t_{sub} \;=\; 1.25 \times 0.55 \;=\; 0.6875\;\mu m
$$

### Step 5: Final consolidated transfer chain
$$
t_{sub} \;=\; t_{plate} \cdot \phi_{b} \cdot \phi_{s}
$$

### Step 6: Numerical evaluation
$$
t_{sub} \;=\; 2.5 \times 0.50 \times 0.55 \;=\; 0.6875\;\mu m
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering insight:** Each split step multiplies by a coefficient < 1, so the film on paper is **always thinner** than on the plate — explaining why offset lithography cannot match the heavy ink laydown of gravure.

---

## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation 3 — Critical Pigment Volume Concentration (CPVC)

The CPVC is the pigment concentration at which the binder **exactly fills** the inter-particle voids without excess.

### Step 1: Define PVC
$$
PVC \;=\; \dfrac{V_{pig}}{V_{pig} \;+\; V_{binder}} \times 100
$$

### Step 2: Define CPVC mathematically
At CPVC, the binder volume equals the void volume in a close-packed pigment arrangement:

$$
V_{binder} \;=\; V_{voids}
$$

For a face-centred cubic packing, the void fraction is approximately **0.40**:

$$
V_{voids} \;=\; 0.40 \cdot V_{pig}
$$

### Step 3: Solve for CPVC
$$
CPVC \;=\; \dfrac{V_{pig}}{V_{pig} \;+\; 0.40 \cdot V_{pig}} \times 100
$$

$$
CPVC \;=\; \dfrac{1}{1 \;+\; 0.40} \times 100
$$

$$
CPVC \;=\; \dfrac{1}{1.40} \times 100
$$

$$
CPVC \;\approx\; 71.43\,\%
$$

### Step 4: Interpretation for lithographic inks
Most offset inks operate at **PVC 10–25%** — well below CPVC — to ensure **excess binder** for gloss, flexibility, and water resistance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** Gloss, opacity, and rub resistance all change dramatically at CPVC. Always discuss CPVC when asked about pigment-binder optimisation in inks.

---

## 3.4 Exhaustive Derivation 4 — Oxidative Drying Kinetics (Simplified)

The oxidative polymerisation of drying oils follows a pseudo-first-order kinetic model with respect to oxygen uptake.

### Step 1: Rate equation
$$
\dfrac{d[O_{2}]}{dt} \;=\; -k \cdot [O_{2}]
$$

### Step 2: Integrate
$$
[O_{2}]_{t} \;=\; [O_{2}]_{0} \cdot e^{-kt}
$$

### Step 3: Degree of oxidation
$$
\alpha(t) \;=\; 1 \;-\; e^{-kt}
$$

### Step 4: At $t = 5$ hours, $k = 0.1\;\text{hr}^{-1}$ (with Co drier):
$$
\alpha \;=\; 1 \;-\; e^{-0.1 \times 5} \;=\; 1 \;-\; e^{-0.5}
$$

$$
\alpha \;\approx\; 1 \;-\; 0.6065 \;=\; 0.3935 \;\approx\; 39.35\,\%
$$

### Step 5: Drying is essentially complete when α ≈ 95%
Solve for $t$:
$$
0.95 \;=\; 1 \;-\; e^{-kt} \quad \Rightarrow \quad e^{-kt} \;=\; 0.05
$$

$$
t \;=\; \dfrac{-\ln(0.05)}{k} \;=\; \dfrac{2.996}{0.1} \;\approx\; 30\;\text{hours}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This explains why conventional sheet-fed offset prints need ~24–48 hours for full cure, whereas **UV-cured lithographic inks** set in **< 1 second**.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]
**"Briefly explain the role of fountain solution in lithographic printing. Why is its pH critical?" (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The fountain solution is an **aqueous acidic fluid** (typically water + buffer + isopropyl alcohol + wetting agents + gum arabic) that maintains the **hydrophilic non-image areas** of the plate while preventing the oleophilic ink from adhering there. It works on the principle of **differential surface energy**: the non-image area has high surface energy (water-loving), and the image area is coated with a hydrophobic polymer (water-repelling).

**Why pH is critical:**

- The fountain solution pH must lie in the range **4.0 – 5.5** for conventional plates.
- If pH is **too low (too acidic, < 4):** The plate's non-image area can be etched, image areas lose definition, emulsification becomes excessive, and ink dries too fast.
- If pH is **too high (alkaline, > 6):** The non-image area loses hydrophilicity, scumming occurs (ink deposits on non-image areas), and image quality degrades.

Hence pH is the **single most important control parameter** for offset press stability.

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Supplementary]
**"Differentiate between oxidative drying and quick-set drying mechanisms in lithographic inks." (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| **Parameter** | **Oxidative Drying** | **Quick-Set Drying** |
|---|---|---|
| **Mechanism** | Oxygen reacts with unsaturated drying oils (linolenic/linoleic acid) to form cross-linked polymer network. | Low-viscosity solvent penetrates paper pores; high-viscosity resin gels on the surface. |
| **Speed** | Slow (12–48 hours). | Fast (seconds to minutes). |
| **Catalyst** | Metallic driers (Co, Mn, Pb naphthenates). | No catalyst required. |
| **Substrate** | Coated papers, cartons. | Uncoated, porous paper (newsprint, forms). |
| **Final State** | Hard, glossy, chemical-resistant film. | Slightly porous, less glossy. |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### Question A — Module 2 Internal Choice Option 1 [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]

**Q. (a)** Discuss in detail the **composition of lithographic inks**, explaining the function of each component with appropriate examples. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A lithographic ink is a carefully balanced colloidal system. Its composition can be classified into **four functional phases**:

**1. Pigments (10–25% by weight)** — Provide colour, opacity, and chemical resistance.
- Examples: **Phthalocyanine Blue (PB 15)** — high tinting strength, chemical stability.
- **Carbon Black (PBk 7)** — broad use in black inks.
- **Arylide Yellows** — bright, transparent process yellows.
- Pigments must be **water-resistant** and **chemically inert** to fountain solution.

**2. Vehicle / Varnish (50–70% by weight)** — Carries pigment, wets substrate, and forms the dried film.
- **Drying oils:** Linseed oil (oxidative polymerisation), tung oil (faster drying).
- **Resins:** Rosin-modified phenolic, alkyd, maleic-modified rosin (provide gloss, hardness, solvent release).
- **Solvents:** Mineral oils with boiling range **240–290 °C (heat-set)** or **260–320 °C (sheet-fed)**.

**3. Additives (1–10% by weight)** — Modify performance:
- **Waxes** (polyethylene, PTFE, paraffin) — improve rub resistance.
- **Driers / siccatives** (cobalt, manganese naphthenates) — accelerate oxidation.
- **Surfactants** — adjust ink-water interface.
- **Anti-oxidants** — extend shelf life.
- **Anti-set-off compounds** (corn starch micro-spheres) — prevent block stacking.

**4. Fountain Solution Interaction (functional phase)** — Not part of ink, but defines its emulsion behaviour. pH 4.0–5.5 with isopropyl alcohol, gum arabic, and buffer.

> [!NOTE]
> The **balance** of these four phases — particularly the pigment-to-vehicle ratio and the choice of resin system — determines whether the ink is suited for sheet-fed, heat-set, or UV-curable offset printing.

---

**(b)** Explain the **rheological properties** (tack, viscosity, yield value) of lithographic inks and their measurement. Discuss why each property is critical for offset printing. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Lithographic inks are **non-Newtonian pseudoplastic fluids** exhibiting a finite yield stress. Three rheological properties dominate:

**1. Tack (8–14 units)**
- **Definition:** The resistance of an ink film to splitting under high-speed roller rotation.
- **Measurement:** **Inkometer** at 1200 rpm, 90 °F, after 1 minute.
- **Why critical:** Excessive tack causes **picking** (paper fibre tear), **hickeys**, and **stripping** on the blanket. Too little tack causes **mottling** and **poor ink transfer**.

**2. Viscosity (10–50 Pa·s)**
- **Definition:** Resistance to flow under shear.
- **Measurement:** **Cone-and-plate or parallel-plate viscometer** at controlled shear rate (e.g., 1000 s⁻¹).
- **Why critical:** Viscosity governs ink **transfer efficiency** through the roller train. Too high → ink starvation at form roller; too low → emulsification, scumming, set-off.

**3. Yield Value (200–1500 dyn/cm²)**
- **Definition:** Minimum stress required to initiate flow.
- **Measurement:** **Extrusion rheometer** or derived from rheogram (Bingham extrapolation).
- **Why critical:** Yield value prevents ink from **spreading** on the plate during idle moments and maintains **sharp image edges**.

**Governing Equation (Bingham Plastic):**

$$
\tau \;=\; \tau_{y} \;+\; \eta_{p} \cdot \dot{\gamma}
$$

where $\tau$ = shear stress, $\tau_{y}$ = yield stress, $\eta_{p}$ = plastic viscosity, $\dot{\gamma}$ = shear rate.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Tip:** Always mention the **measurement instrument** alongside the property — students who state only "viscosity" without "cone-and-plate viscometer" lose 1 mark.

---

### Question B — Module 2 Internal Choice Option 2 [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]

**Q. (a)** Describe the **ink-water emulsion** formation in offset printing. Explain the **duplex emulsion** structure and the role of surfactants. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

In offset lithography, the ink and fountain solution meet at high speed in the form roller nip, where the **mechanical agitation emulsifies** water droplets within the continuous ink phase.

**1. Formation Mechanism:**
- High-shear contact at the nip disperses fountain solution into droplets of **1–10 μm diameter**.
- These droplets become **stabilised** by the natural surfactants in the ink vehicle (resin acids, fatty acids) and any added surfactants.
- The result is a **water-in-oil (W/O) emulsion** with water as the dispersed phase and ink as the continuous phase.

**2. Duplex Emulsion Structure:**
Sometimes, an **inverted water-in-oil-in-water (W/O/W)** structure appears:
- **Macro-emulsion** (W/O): Water droplets encapsulated in ink — desirable.
- **Micro-emulsion** (O/W): Ink droplets dispersed in water — **undesirable**, leads to scumming and tinting.
- **Duplex emulsion:** A mixed structure where both W/O and O/W coexist.

**3. Critical Water Pick-up Levels:**
- **Sheet-fed offset:** Up to **30%** water tolerable.
- **Heat-set web offset:** Up to **50%** tolerable.
- Beyond this, the emulsion **inverts**, ink rheology collapses, and print quality fails catastrophically.

**4. Role of Surfactants:**
- **Reduce interfacial tension** between ink and water (from ~30 mN/m to ~5 mN/m).
- **Stabilise** droplet size distribution.
- **Control** emulsion inversion point.
- Common examples: **lecithin, fatty acid esters, sulfated castor oil.**

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often write "emulsion forms" without specifying **type (W/O vs O/W)**. Always identify the **continuous phase** and the **dispersed phase** explicitly.

---

**(b)** Compare **heat-set, quick-set, oxidative, and UV-cured lithographic inks**. State typical applications, drying temperatures, and advantages/limitations of each. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| **Ink Type** | **Drying Principle** | **Drying Temperature / Time** | **Typical Application** | **Advantage** | **Limitation** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Heat-set** | Solvent evaporation in hot-air dryer | **120–160 °C**, < 1 s | Magazines, catalogues, commercial web | Very high speed, sharp dots | Energy-intensive, requires dryer |
| **Quick-set** | Penetration of solvent into paper | Room temperature, **< 1 min** | Newsprint, business forms | No heat needed, instant handling | Limited gloss, poor rub resistance |
| **Oxidative** | Oxygen-induced cross-linking | Room temp, **12–48 h** | Sheet-fed cartons, books | Hard, glossy, durable film | Slow; requires drier additives |
| **UV-cured** | Free-radical photopolymerisation | UV lamp exposure, **< 1 s** | Labels, packaging, security printing | Instant cure, no VOCs, excellent rub resistance | Higher ink cost; requires UV source |

**Detailed Discussion:**

**Heat-set:** The vehicle contains a high proportion (40–60%) of low-viscosity petroleum distillate (boiling range **240–290 °C**). After printing, the web passes through a **gas-fired or electric dryer** where the solvent fraction is vaporised and recovered (in modern systems). The cooled web is then ready for in-line folding.

**Quick-set:** The vehicle splits into a **low-viscosity solvent fraction** (penetrates paper) and a **high-viscosity resin-rich fraction** (gells on surface). This mechanism is **dual-action**: penetration + gelation.

**Oxidative:** Unsaturated oils (linolenic, linoleic acid) absorb atmospheric O₂ via the **autoxidation chain mechanism**: initiation (H-abstraction) → propagation (peroxide formation) → termination (cross-linking). Catalysts are **Co/Mn driers**.

**UV-cured:** Composed of **acrylate-functional oligomers** (epoxy acrylates, polyester acrylates) plus **photoinitiators** (benzophenone, α-hydroxy ketones). UV exposure generates free radicals that cross-link the acrylate network in **< 0.5 second**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Warning:** In UV ink answers, students frequently forget to mention **photoinitiators**. This is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction in any 7-mark sub-question on UV lithographic inks.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Common Mark-Loss Zones in Lithographic Ink Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting numerical ranges** — Always quote tack (8–14), viscosity (10–50 Pa·s), water pick-up (≤ 30%).
> 2. **Confusing W/O and O/W emulsions** — Specify which phase is continuous.
> 3. **Omitting measurement instruments** — Inkometer, Cone-plate viscometer, etc.
> 4. **Writing only qualitative descriptions** of drying — always state **temperature AND time**.
> 5. **Ignoring CPVC** in pigment-binder discussions — this is a **favourite KTU advanced question**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- Lithographic inks are **paste-type, oleoresinous, water-tolerant** formulations for offset printing.
- Composition: **Pigment (10–25%) + Vehicle (50–70%) + Additives (1–10%) + fountain solution interaction**.
- **Tack:** 8–14 units (Inkometer); too high → picking; too low → poor transfer.
- **Viscosity:** 10–50 Pa·s; non-Newtonian pseudoplastic with finite yield value.
- **Yield value:** 200–1500 dyn/cm² — prevents ink spreading.
- **Fountain solution pH:** 4.0–5.5 (acidic) — critical control parameter.
- **Water pick-up:** ≤ 30% (sheet-fed), ≤ 50% (heat-set web).
- **Emulsion type:** W/O (continuous = ink); W/O/W = duplex (undesirable at high water).
- **Drying mechanisms:** Oxidative (slow), Penetration/quick-set (medium), Evaporation/heat-set (fast), UV-cure (instant).
- **Heat-set dryer temp:** 120–160 °C; solvent BP: 240–290 °C.
- **UV inks:** Photoinitiators (benzophenone) + acrylate oligomers; cure < 1 s.
- **CPVC ≈ 71.4%** for FCC packing; offset inks operate at 10–25% PVC (below CPVC).
- **Split-film rule:** $t_{sub} = t_{plate} \cdot \phi_{b} \cdot \phi_{s}$.
- **Specialty inks:** OVI (security), metallic (Al/Cu flakes), fluorescent, low-migration (food).
- **Standard test instruments:** Inkometer, Grindometer (NPIRI), Rheometer, Densitometer.
- **Bingham plastic model:** $\tau = \tau_{y} + \eta_{p} \cdot \dot{\gamma}$.
- **Power-law:** $\tau = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{\,n}$ with $n < 1$ for pseudoplastic behaviour.
- **Pigment choices:** PB 15 (Phthalo Blue), PR 57:1 (Lithol Rubine), PY 12 (Arylide Yellow), PBk 7 (Carbon Black).
- **Driers:** Co/Mn/Pb naphthenates (oxidative ink catalyst).
- **Anti-set-off agents:** Corn starch granules in ink film.
- **Waxes:** PE, PTFE, paraffin (rub resistance).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Gravure Inks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT866 - TECHNOLOGY OF PRINTING INKS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Gravure Inks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Gravure Inks

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Gravure inks** are specialty low-viscosity, fast-evaporating liquid inks specifically formulated for the **rotogravure (gravure) printing process**, in which the image area is engraved as a pattern of microscopic recessed cells on the surface of a chrome-plated copper cylinder. The ink must be fluid enough to fill these cells completely under the immersion of the cylinder in the ink pan, yet sufficiently cohesive to remain in the cells against the centrifugal and shear forces of the doctor blade wiping mechanism.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** Gravure inks are *solvent-based*, *low-viscosity* (typically 15–25 seconds in a #4 Ford cup, equivalent to 50–300 mPa·s) liquid inks that dry almost exclusively by **evaporation of volatile solvents**, leaving a thin, mechanically and chemically resistant film of pigment + resin binder on the substrate.

> [!NOTE]
> Unlike offset or letterpress inks, gravure inks contain **no oxidative drying oils, no varnish vehicles, and virtually no water**. They are essentially *pigment dispersions in volatile solvent-resin solutions*.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Tiny Swimming Pool" Picture

Imagine a giant chocolate mould with thousands of microscopic square pits, each pit a different depth. You dip the mould into melted colored chocolate, scrape the surface flat with a straight edge (so chocolate stays only inside the pits), and then press a sheet of paper onto the mould. Lift the paper — and the paper now carries a perfect dot pattern, where each dot's size reflects the depth of its pit.

**That is exactly how gravure works:**
- The cylinder is the chocolate mould.
- The pits (called **cells**) are mechanically or laser-engraved.
- The melted chocolate is the **fluid gravure ink**.
- The straight-edge scraper is the **doctor blade**.
- The paper is the **substrate**.

For this analogy to work, the ink must be:
1. **Thin enough** (low viscosity) to dive into narrow pits quickly.
2. **Thick enough** not to drip out when the mould is inverted.
3. **Quick-drying** so the chocolate sets before it smudges.

## 1.3 Position in the Printing Ink Family

| Ink Type | Viscosity Range | Drying Mechanism | Vehicle |
|----------|----------------|------------------|---------|
| **Letterpress** | High (paste, 50–100 Pa·s) | Absorption + oxidation | Oil/alkyd varnish |
| **Offset (litho)** | Paste (40–100 Pa·s) | Oxidation + absorption | Oil/resin varnish |
| **Flexo** | Liquid (50–500 mPa·s) | Evaporation | Water / alcohol / ester |
| **Gravure** | **Very low liquid (50–300 mPa·s)** | **Evaporation (fast)** | **Volatile solvent + resin** |
| **Screen** | Paste (1–10 Pa·s) | Evaporation / absorption | Plastisol / water |

> [!NOTE]
> Gravure inks are the **thinnest** of all major commercial printing inks because they must penetrate microscopic cells (often only 5–50 µm deep) under the dwell time of rotation.

## 1.4 Historical & Industrial Significance

Gravure is the technology behind **high-volume long-run** printing:
- Magazines (glossy, weeklies)
- Catalogs, mailers, Sunday supplements
- Flexible packaging (snack bags, chocolate wrappers, pet food)
- Decorative laminates (wood-grain, gift wrap, vinyl flooring)
- Security printing (currency in some countries)
- Cigarette and tobacco inner liners

**~35–40% of all flexible packaging** worldwide is printed by gravure because the engraved cylinder gives **unmatched print consistency** over millions of impressions.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Functional Requirements — The "Five Commandments" of a Gravure Ink

For a gravure ink to function in the process, it must simultaneously satisfy five engineering demands:

1. **Low Viscosity (15–25 s, #4 Ford cup at 25 °C)** — to allow complete cell filling in milliseconds.
2. **Correct Surface Tension (28–35 mN/m)** — to wet the chrome cylinder wall and not pull out of the cell.
3. **Rapid Drying (residual solvent < 5 mg/m²)** — to prevent set-off in the rewinding roll.
4. **Strong Substrate Adhesion** — for the dried resin film to bond to PE, PP, PET, paper, foil, etc.
5. **Print Fidelity & Gloss** — sharp dot edges, good color strength, scratch resistance.

## 2.2 Composition of a Gravure Ink

A gravure ink is a **multi-component colloidal system**:

### A. Pigment (8–25 % w/w)
- Provides color and opacity.
- Must be **finely ground** (particle size < 5 µm, ideally < 2 µm) to pass through the doctor blade gap and not clog cells.
- Organic pigments preferred for transparency (e.g., **Phthalocyanine Blue, Azo Reds, Carmine**).
- Inorganic pigments (TiO₂, carbon black, iron oxide) for opacity.

### B. Binder / Resin (10–25 % w/w)
The binder is the **film-forming "glue"** that locks the pigment to the substrate after solvent evaporation.

**Common Gravure Resin Chemistries:**

| Resin Type | Solvent Used | Key Property |
|------------|--------------|--------------|
| **Nitrocellulose (NC)** | Esters, ketones | Fast drying, hard film, limited flexibility |
| **Cellulose Acetate Propionate (CAP)** | Esters, aromatics | Better heat resistance than NC |
| **Polyvinyl Butyral (PVB)** | Alcohols | Good adhesion, flexible |
| **Polyamide (PA)** | Alcohols, blends | Excellent flex, adhesion to PE/PP |
| **Vinyl Chloride-Vinyl Acetate (VC-VAc)** | Ketones, aromatics | Tough, chemical resistant |
| **Acrylic Resins** | Esters, ketones | UV resistance, gloss |
| **Polyurethane (PU)** | Alcohols, esters | Superb flexibility, heat seal resistance |
| **Rosin / Maleic / Phenolic Modified** | Aromatics, alcohols | Tack, gloss |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Most commercial gravure inks are **multiresin systems** — typically NC + a flexible resin (e.g., polyurethane or polyamide) blended at ratios like 60:40 to balance hardness vs. flexibility.

### C. Solvent (50–80 % w/w) — The Volatile Vehicle
The solvent is the **transport medium** that carries pigment + resin into the cell and then evaporates. Choice depends on resin solubility, evaporation rate, and odor/toxicity regulations.

**Standard Gravure Solvent Classes:**

| Solvent Class | Examples | Evaporation Rate (BuAc = 1.0) | Notes |
|---------------|----------|-------------------------------|-------|
| **Aliphatic** | Hexane, heptane | 8.0 – very fast | Low cost, weak solvent |
| **Aromatic** | **Toluene, Xylene** | 2.0 – medium | Workhorse, excellent solvating power |
| **Alcohols** | Ethanol, IPA, n-propanol | 1.7 – 2.5 | Used with PA, PVB |
| **Esters** | Ethyl acetate, butyl acetate | 1.0 – 4.0 | Standard for NC-based inks |
| **Ketones** | MEK, MIBK | 2.6 – 1.6 | Strong solvent for VC-VAc |
| **Glycol Ethers** | Ethyl glycol monoethyl ether | 0.3 – slow | Retarder, reduces blushing |

> [!NOTE]
> **Toluenized and ethyl-acetate-based systems** are the most widely used worldwide. **Alcohol-based (ethanol/ethyl acetate)** systems dominate in **publication gravure** (magazines, catalogs) because of low odor and FDA approval for food contact laminates.

### D. Additives (1–5 % w/w)
- **Plasticizers** (DOP, dibutyl phthalate, citrate esters) — 0.5–2 % to prevent film brittleness.
- **Waxes** (PE wax, PTFE, amide wax) — 0.1–1 % for slip and scratch resistance.
- **Dispersants / Wetting agents** — to stabilize pigment suspension.
- **Antifoam / Deaerators** (silicone-based) — to prevent foam in the ink pan.
- **Antiblocking agents** — for film lamination.
- **Driers** — generally NOT used (gravure does not dry by oxidation).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Relation | Symbols | Typical Unit / Value | Engineering Use |
|---|-------------------|---------|----------------------|-----------------|
| 1 | **Kinematic Viscosity** $\nu = \dfrac{\mu}{\rho}$ | $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity, $\rho$ = density | m²/s, cm²/s (Stokes) | Compare ink to standard oil |
| 2 | **Efflux Cup (Ford #4)** $\nu \approx 0.226\,t - \dfrac{0.165}{t}$ (for $t$ in s) | $t$ = efflux time in seconds | s, range 15–25 s | Plant QC of gravure ink |
| 3 | **Cell Fill Volume** $V_c = A_c \cdot d_c$ | $A_c$ = cell area, $d_c$ = cell depth | µm³ (typically 1000–50 000) | Determines ink laydown |
| 4 | **Ink Laydown per unit area** $W = \dfrac{V_c \cdot \rho_{ink}}{A_{cell pitch}}$ | $V_c$ = cell volume, $A_{cell pitch}$ = cell spacing area | g/m² | Wet ink deposited |
| 5 | **Dry Film Thickness** $h_{dry} = \dfrac{W \cdot (1 - S_{ret})}{\rho_{dry film}}$ | $S_{ret}$ = % solvent retained in film | µm, usually 1–8 µm | Final dried ink film |
| 6 | **Evaporation Rate (Langmuir)** $\dfrac{dm}{dt} = k \cdot (P_s - P_{air})$ | $P_s$ = vapor pressure, $P_{air}$ = partial pressure | g/(cm²·s) | Predicts drying time |
| 7 | **Antoine Equation** $\log_{10} P_s = A - \dfrac{B}{C + T}$ | $T$ in °C, $A,B,C$ = Antoine constants | mmHg or kPa | Vapor pressure vs. temperature |
| 8 | **Capillary Number (cell filling)** $Ca = \dfrac{\mu \cdot v}{\gamma}$ | $v$ = draw speed, $\gamma$ = surface tension | dimensionless, $< 0.05$ for full fill | Predicts cell emptying |
| 9 | **Surface Tension (Pendant Drop)** $\gamma = \dfrac{\Delta \rho \cdot g \cdot D^2}{H}$ | $\Delta\rho$ = density difference, $D$ = drop diameter | mN/m | Surface energy of wet ink |
| 10 | **Youngs-Laplace Pressure (cell)** $\Delta P = \dfrac{2 \gamma \cos \theta}{r_{cell}}$ | $r_{cell}$ = cell radius, $\theta$ = contact angle | Pa | Force holding ink in cell |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Capillary Number $Ca < 0.05$** is the rule of thumb for proper gravure cell filling. If $Ca$ exceeds 0.05, the doctor blade begins to *pull ink out* of the cell, leading to **missed dots, skip, and tonal shift**.

## 2.4 Drying Mechanism — Solvent Evaporation Kinetics

Gravure drying is **purely physical** (no chemical curing), so the printed web enters a long, high-velocity hot-air **drying tunnel** (often 6–18 m long, with air at 60–120 °C and velocities of 20–40 m/s).

The drying occurs in two stages:

**Stage 1 — Constant Rate Period (surface evaporation)**
The solvent evaporates from the surface as fast as heat is supplied. Mass loss is linear in time. Governed by vapor pressure difference.

**Stage 2 — Falling Rate Period (internal diffusion)**
Once surface is sealed by a dry skin, the remaining solvent must diffuse through the resin film. This is **Fickian diffusion** and is much slower — it is the limiting stage for "solvent retention" in laminates.

> [!WARNING]
> **Residual solvent in the dry film** is a critical defect in **lamination-grade gravure inks**. If $> 5 \text{ mg/m}^2$ of solvent remains, lamination bonds (especially PU adhesive) will fail. This is why alcohol-based inks are preferred for food packaging.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The principles governing gravure ink design apply to:
- **Functional coatings** (magnetic tapes, photographic films, optical films).
- **Lithium battery electrode coating** (slot-die rheology is analogous to gravure).
- **OLED display manufacturing** (slot-die + gravure hybrid).
- **Pharmaceutical coatings** (roll-to-roll).

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Cell Volume and Ink Laydown

A standard **electromechanically engraved (EME) gravure cell** is pyramidal or quadragravure-shaped. For a square-pyramidal cell of side $s$ and depth $d$:

$$
A_c = s^2
$$

$$
V_c = \frac{1}{3} \cdot s^2 \cdot d
$$

For a **quadragravure (quadrangular) cell** (truncated square pyramid) with top side $s_t$, bottom side $s_b$, and depth $d$:

$$
V_c = \frac{d}{3}\left(s_t^2 + s_t s_b + s_b^2\right)
$$

In laser-engraved cells (helio-cliché), the cell is a **truncated cone** of top diameter $D_t$ and bottom diameter $D_b$:

$$
V_c = \frac{\pi \, d}{12}\left(D_t^2 + D_t D_b + D_b^2\right)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Cells are usually arranged in a **45° screen angle** with screen ruling of **60–200 lines/cm**. The number of cells per unit area is therefore the inverse square of the cell pitch $p$:
> $$N = \frac{1}{p^2}$$

**Step-by-step derivation of laydown:**

Step 1: Volume of one cell — $V_c$ (from one of the above geometric relations).

Step 2: Pitch area per cell — $A_p = p^2$ (for square pitch) or $A_p = \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} p^2$ (for 60° hex pitch).

Step 3: **Wet ink deposit per unit area (g/m²)** —

$$
W_{wet} = \frac{V_c}{A_p} \cdot \rho_{ink}
$$

Step 4: Solids fraction $f_s$ (typically 0.20–0.40) — solids = resin + pigment + non-volatile additives.

Step 5: **Dry ink deposit per unit area (g/m²)** —

$$
W_{dry} = W_{wet} \cdot f_s
$$

Step 6: **Average dry film thickness** — assuming uniform dry film of density $\rho_d \approx 1.10 \text{ g/cm}^3$:

$$
h_{dry} = \frac{W_{dry}}{\rho_d}
$$

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Cell Fill Calculation

**Given:**
- Quadragravure cell: $s_t = 180 \text{ µm}$, $s_b = 80 \text{ µm}$, $d = 45 \text{ µm}$.
- Screen ruling: $L = 70 \text{ lines/cm}$.
- Ink density: $\rho_{ink} = 0.95 \text{ g/cm}^3$.
- Solids fraction: $f_s = 0.30$.
- Dry film density: $\rho_d = 1.10 \text{ g/cm}^3$.

**Step 1 — Cell volume:**

$$
V_c = \frac{d}{3}\left(s_t^2 + s_t s_b + s_b^2\right)
$$

Substituting:

$$
V_c = \frac{45 \times 10^{-4} \text{ cm}}{3}\left[(180)^2 + (180)(80) + (80)^2\right] \times 10^{-8} \text{ cm}^2
$$

$$
V_c = \frac{45 \times 10^{-4}}{3} \cdot \left[32400 + 14400 + 6400\right] \times 10^{-8}
$$

$$
V_c = 1.5 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 53200 \times 10^{-8} = 7.98 \times 10^{-7} \text{ cm}^3
$$

**Step 2 — Pitch:**

$$
p = \frac{1}{L} = \frac{1}{70} \text{ cm} = 142.86 \text{ µm}
$$

**Step 3 — Pitch area (60° helical, approximate square):**

$$
A_p = p^2 = (142.86 \times 10^{-4})^2 = 2.041 \times 10^{-4} \text{ cm}^2
$$

**Step 4 — Wet laydown:**

$$
W_{wet} = \frac{V_c}{A_p} \cdot \rho_{ink} = \frac{7.98 \times 10^{-7}}{2.041 \times 10^{-4}} \cdot 0.95
$$

$$
W_{wet} = 3.91 \times 10^{-3} \text{ cm} \cdot 0.95 \text{ g/cm}^3 = 3.71 \times 10^{-3} \text{ g/cm}^2
$$

Converting to g/m²:

$$
W_{wet} = 3.71 \times 10^{-3} \times 10^4 = 37.1 \text{ g/m}^2
$$

**Step 5 — Dry laydown:**

$$
W_{dry} = W_{wet} \cdot f_s = 37.1 \times 0.30 = 11.13 \text{ g/m}^2
$$

**Step 6 — Dry film thickness:**

$$
h_{dry} = \frac{W_{dry}}{\rho_d} = \frac{11.13 \times 10^{-6} \text{ g/cm}^2}{1.10 \text{ g/cm}^3} = 1.012 \times 10^{-5} \text{ cm}
$$

$$
\boxed{h_{dry} \approx 1.0 \text{ µm}}
$$

This thin dry film is typical of gravure printing — much thinner than flexo or offset.

## 3.3 Derivation 2 — Evaporation Rate Using the Antoine Equation

To predict how fast a gravure ink dries in the oven, we need the vapor pressure of the solvent at oven temperature.

**Antoine Equation (for toluene, valid 280–410 K):**

$$
\log_{10} P_s (\text{mmHg}) = 6.95464 - \frac{1344.800}{T + 219.482}
$$

**Step 1** — Oven temperature $T = 80 \text{ °C} = 353.15 \text{ K}$.

**Step 2** — Substituting:

$$
\log_{10} P_s = 6.95464 - \frac{1344.800}{353.15 + 219.482}
$$

$$
= 6.95464 - \frac{1344.800}{572.632}
$$

$$
= 6.95464 - 2.3485 = 4.6061
$$

**Step 3** — Solve:

$$
P_s = 10^{4.6061} = 40370 \text{ mmHg} \approx 53.8 \text{ atm}
$$

Converting to kPa (1 atm = 101.325 kPa):

$$
P_s \approx 5452 \text{ kPa}
$$

**Step 4** — Mass evaporation rate per unit area (Langmuir form, for stagnant air):

$$
\dot{m} = 0.037 \cdot P_s \cdot \sqrt{\frac{M}{T}}
$$

where $M$ = molecular weight of toluene = 92.14 g/mol, $T$ in K, $P_s$ in mmHg, $\dot{m}$ in g/(cm²·s).

$$
\dot{m} = 0.037 \cdot 40370 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{92.14}{353.15}}
$$

$$
= 1493.7 \cdot \sqrt{0.2609} = 1493.7 \cdot 0.5108 = 762.8 \text{ g/(cm}^2\text{·s)}
$$

This is the *maximum* rate, so a more realistic expression including mass-transfer coefficient $k_m$ is used:

$$
\dot{m} = k_m \cdot (P_s - P_{air}) \cdot \frac{M}{RT}
$$

**Step 5** — Drying time for a 40 µm wet film containing 70 % toluene:

Mass of toluene per cm²: $m_{tol} = 0.95 \times 0.70 \times 40 \times 10^{-4} = 2.66 \times 10^{-3} \text{ g/cm}^2$.

Drying time: $t_d = m_{tol} / \dot{m}_{effective}$.

With a typical effective rate of 5 × 10⁻⁴ g/(cm²·s) (due to air boundary layer, partial pressure buildup, etc.):

$$
t_d = \frac{2.66 \times 10^{-3}}{5 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 5.3 \text{ s}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This matches industrial practice: gravure oven residence times are typically 1–3 seconds per pass (with 2–3 zones), giving total residence of 3–9 seconds.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Selection of Resin vs. Solvent vs. Substrate

| Substrate | Recommended Resin | Recommended Solvent System | Why |
|-----------|------------------|----------------------------|-----|
| **Paper (publication)** | Maleic rosin + NC | Toluene (fast) | Quick dry, low cost |
| **Coated paperboard** | Acrylic + NC | Ethyl acetate / toluene | Gloss, scuff |
| **Polyethylene (PE) film** | Polyamide + NC | Ethanol / ethyl acetate | Adhesion to non-polar |
| **Polypropylene (PP) film** | Chlorinated PP + NC | Toluene / xylene | Wetting of PP |
| **PET film (lamination)** | PU + NC | Ethyl acetate | Low solvent retention |
| **Aluminium foil** | Vinyl / NC | MEK / ethyl acetate | Heat resistance |
| **Cellophane** | Nitrocellulose | Ester/alcohol blends | High gloss |

## 3.5 Defects and Their Causal Analysis

| Defect | Appearance | Root Cause | Fix |
|--------|-----------|-----------|-----|
| **Missed dots / "skip"** | White spots in solids | Ink too viscous, doctor blade worn, surface tension too high | Reduce viscosity, replace blade, add surfactant |
| **Halo / bleeding** | Ink spreads beyond cell | Surface tension too low, slow solvent | Use higher-surface-tension solvent |
| **Ghosting** | Faint repeats in print | Cylinder vibration, ink foaming | Add defoamer, check bearings |
| **Scumming** | Background tinting | Cell walls not cleaned; ink solids too high | Reduce solids, check blade pressure |
| **Pinholes** | Tiny voids in film | Air entrapment, fast drying skin | Add retarder solvent, deair ink |
| **Blocking (in rewind)** | Ink transfers to back of web | Resin too soft / Tg too low | Add harder resin, increase plasticizer balance |
| **Solvent retention** | Odor, lamination failure | Inadequate drying, wrong solvent | Lengthen oven, use faster solvent |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Question 1 (3 marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Model Question]**
**"List any three major characteristics of gravure inks that distinguish them from offset and flexographic inks."**

**Model Answer (3 points):**

1. **Very low viscosity** (50–300 mPa·s) compared to offset (paste, 50–100 Pa·s) and even flexo (50–500 mPa·s) because gravure must fill microscopic engraved cells within milliseconds of cylinder rotation.
2. **Purely evaporative drying mechanism** — no oxidative polymerization, no water, no UV/EB in standard formulations; drying depends on volatile solvent flash-off in hot-air ovens.
3. **High proportion of solvent (50–80 %)** versus offset (≤ 35 % vehicle) and flexo (≤ 60 %); solvent is therefore the dominant component and must be carefully selected for evaporation rate, solvency, and toxicity compliance.

*(Any 3 valid distinguishing points — 1 mark each.)*

---

### Question 2 (3 marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Model Question]**
**"Explain the role of nitrocellulose in a gravure ink formulation. Why is it commonly blended with a flexible resin?"**

**Model Answer (3 points):**

- **Role of NC (1 mark):** Nitrocellulose is the primary **film-forming hard resin** that provides rapid solvent release, high gloss, hardness, and chemical resistance. It dissolves in esters and ketones to give a low-viscosity vehicle ideal for cell filling.
- **Limitation (1 mark):** Pure NC films are **brittle** and have **poor adhesion to flexible films** (PE, PP), and crack on bending — a critical defect in packaging laminates.
- **Why blended (1 mark):** Blending NC with a flexible resin (polyamide, polyurethane, or CAP) at ratios like 60:40 yields a **balanced film** — NC contributes hardness/gloss, the flexible resin contributes elasticity, adhesion, and heat-seal tolerance. This multiresin approach is the industry standard.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks each, with Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Scheme ESE Part B questions in the **5th module of 7** typically appear in either Q11(a)/(b) or Q12(a)/(b). They carry 14 marks and follow the *internal choice* pattern. Below are two fully independent alternatives.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Full Module 2 Question with Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Paper Style]**
**(a)** Describe the complete composition of a gravure ink, listing the function and typical percentage of each component. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Discuss the various types of binders and solvents used in gravure ink formulations, explaining how the choice depends on substrate and end-use. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution (a) — Composition of Gravure Ink (7 marks)

A gravure ink is a finely dispersed pigment in a solution of binder resin in a volatile solvent, with minor additives for special properties. The composition is:

| Component | % w/w | Function |
|-----------|-------|----------|
| **Pigment** | 8–25 | Provides color, opacity, special effects (metallic, pearlescent). Must be milled to < 5 µm. |
| **Binder / Resin** | 10–25 | Film-former that locks pigment to substrate, gives gloss, hardness, flexibility, adhesion. |
| **Solvent** | 50–80 | Carries pigment + resin into engraved cell; evaporates in oven to leave dry film. |
| **Additives** | 1–5 | Plasticizers, waxes, dispersants, defoamers, anti-blocking agents. |

*Step-wise elaboration (each component 1–2 marks):*

1. **Pigment (1.5 marks):** Organic (azo, phthalocyanine, quinacridone) for transparency and high chroma; inorganic (TiO₂, iron oxide, carbon black) for opacity. Fineness: Hegman gauge > 6, particle size < 5 µm to avoid doctor-blade streaking.

2. **Binder (1.5 marks):** Single resin rarely used; modern inks use **multiresin** (e.g., NC 60 % + PU 40 %) to balance hardness and flexibility. Common resins — NC, CAP, polyamide, polyurethane, acrylic, VC-VAc, rosin derivatives.

3. **Solvent (2 marks):** Choice dictated by resin solubility, evaporation rate, and regulatory limits. Toluene/xylene for NC, alcohols for polyamide, esters for NC + acrylic blends. Mixed-solvent systems (e.g., toluene + ethyl acetate + ethanol) are used to fine-tune evaporation profile.

4. **Additives (1 mark):** Plasticizer (DOP 0.5–2 %) prevents brittleness; waxes (0.1–1 %) provide slip and abrasion resistance; defoamer (0.05–0.2 %) eliminates pan foam; dispersant stabilizes pigment suspension.

5. **Optional functional components (1 mark):** Crosslinkers (isocyanate, melamine) for heat-resistant laminates; UV absorbers for outdoor packaging; optical brighteners for white inks.

---

#### Model Solution (b) — Binders & Solvents, Substrate-Dependent Choice (7 marks)

**Binders (3.5 marks):**

- **Nitrocellulose (NC):** The workhorse resin. Hard, fast drying, gloss, low odor, FDA-approved (food packaging). Soluble in esters, ketones; not in pure alcohol. Drawback: brittleness.
- **Polyamide resins:** Excellent flexibility, adhesion to PE/PP, and heat-seal strength. Soluble in alcohols and alcohol/aromatic blends. Common in **lamination inks**.
- **Polyurethane (PU):** Premium flexibility, abrasion resistance, and low solvent retention. Soluble in alcohol/ester blends. Used in retort pouches and high-end flexible packaging.
- **Acrylic resins:** UV resistance, gloss, non-yellowing. Used for outdoor posters and premium labels.
- **Vinyl chloride-vinyl acetate (VC-VAc):** Chemical resistance, used in industrial and pipeline marking inks.
- **Cellulose Acetate Propionate (CAP):** Better heat resistance than NC, used in overprint varnishes and foil inks.

**Solvents (3.5 marks):**

- **Aromatic (toluene, xylene):** Strong solvent for NC, VC-VAc, acrylic; medium evaporation rate; classical workhorse. Concern: VOC, toxicity.
- **Esters (ethyl acetate, butyl acetate):** Standard for NC and acrylic; fast evaporation; pleasant odor; FDA-acceptable.
- **Alcohols (ethanol, IPA, n-propanol):** Solvents for polyamide and PVB; low toxicity; preferred for **publication gravure** (magazines) and food packaging.
- **Ketones (MEK, MIBK):** Strong solvents; used in heavy-duty inks for industrial substrates.
- **Glycol ethers (ethyl glycol monoethyl ether):** Slow retarder solvent; prevents blushing and pinholes by keeping the film open during oven travel.

**Substrate-Dependent Choices (table for 1 mark):**

| Substrate | Resin System | Solvent System | End Use |
|-----------|-------------|----------------|---------|
| Coated paper | NC + rosin maleic | Toluene | Magazines |
| PE / PP film | Polyamide + NC | Ethanol + ethyl acetate | Snack pouches |
| PET film | PU + NC | Ethyl acetate | Lamination |
| Aluminium foil | Vinyl + NC | MEK + toluene | Chocolate wrap |

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice (Module 2 Internal Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Style]**
**(a)** With the help of a neat diagram, describe the gravure printing process and explain how ink is transferred from the engraved cylinder to the substrate. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain the properties, drying mechanism, and common defects encountered in gravure inks. Suggest remedies for each defect. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution (a) — Gravure Process & Ink Transfer (7 marks)

**Process Description (4 marks):**

1. The gravure cylinder is chrome-plated copper with image areas **engraved as recessed cells** (electromechanically, laser, or chemically etched).
2. The cylinder rotates through an **ink pan** (also called the ink fountain or bath) where all cells become fully immersed in the low-viscosity ink.
3. As the cylinder emerges, a thin **excess film of ink** covers its entire surface.
4. A **doctor blade** (steel, typically mounted at 25–45° angle) is pressed against the rotating cylinder, mechanically **scraping off the excess ink** while leaving ink trapped inside the cells by surface tension and capillary forces.
5. The cylinder then contacts the **substrate web** at the nip of the **impression roller** under high pressure (5–50 kN/m), at which point ink transfers from the cell onto the substrate.
6. The printed web immediately enters a **multi-zone drying oven** with high-velocity hot air (60–120 °C, 20–40 m/s) where the solvent evaporates.
7. The dried web is rewound or sent to slitting/lamination.

**Ink Transfer Mechanism (3 marks):**

- Ink transfer is governed by **capillary and viscous forces**.
- The **Capillary Number** $Ca = \mu v / \gamma$ must remain below 0.05; otherwise the doctor blade pulls ink out of cells.
- The **Young–Laplace pressure** $\Delta P = 2 \gamma \cos \theta / r$ holds ink inside cells against gravity and shear.
- The amount of ink transferred depends on the **cell volume, cell depth, and impression pressure**. Laser-engraved cells (Helio Cliché) give better tonal range than EME cells.
- Typical ink laydown: 3–15 g/m² dry, depending on cell geometry.

**Schematic Diagram (text-rendered):**

```
        Ink Pan
           |
           v
   +----#######----+        Cylinder rotates clockwise
   |    Cells      |  --->  Engraved cells filled with ink
   +----#######----+        
        |   |
        |   |  Doctor Blade  (presses here)
        |   |________________________
        v
   Impression Roller
        |
   ===Substrate Web===        Inks transferred at nip
        |
   [Hot Air Drying Oven]      Solvent evaporated
        |
   ===Dry Printed Web===
```

---

#### Model Solution (b) — Properties, Drying & Defects of Gravure Inks (7 marks)

**Essential Properties (2 marks):**
- Viscosity 15–25 s (#4 Ford cup) at 25 °C.
- Surface tension 28–35 mN/m.
- Solids content 20–40 %.
- Particle fineness < 5 µm, Hegman ≥ 6.
- Good pigment dispersion, no flocculation.
- Adhesion to substrate.
- Fast drying, low solvent retention.
- Resistance to scuff, blocking, heat-seal failure.

**Drying Mechanism (2 marks):**
- **Two-stage evaporative drying.**
- Stage 1: Surface evaporation at constant rate, governed by vapor pressure (Antoine) and heat/mass transfer.
- Stage 2: Internal diffusion through dry skin, Fickian, much slower.
- Typical oven residence: 3–9 seconds total in 2–3 zones.
- Residual solvent target: < 5 mg/m² for laminating inks.

**Common Defects & Remedies (3 marks):**

| Defect | Cause | Remedy |
|--------|-------|--------|
| Missed dots / skip | Ink too viscous; worn blade; high surface tension | Lower viscosity; replace blade; add surfactant |
| Halo / bleeding | Surface tension too low; slow solvent | Use higher-tension solvent; use retarder |
| Pinholes | Air entrapment; fast skin formation | Add retarder glycol ether; deair ink |
| Scumming | Cell walls not clean; excess solids | Reduce solids; check blade pressure |
| Blocking | Soft resin; Tg too low | Use harder resin; add wax slip agent |
| Solvent retention | Insufficient oven heat/time; wrong solvent | Lengthen oven; switch to fast-evaporating solvent |
| Poor adhesion | Wrong resin for substrate | Use polyamide for PE/PP; CAP for foil |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Alert — Common Mark-Loss Areas:**
>
> 1. **"Describe gravure ink composition"** — many students write only pigment/resin/solvent and forget **additives** and the **multiresin concept**. Always include *why* multiresin is used (1 mark lost if missed).
> 2. **"Drying mechanism"** — students often say "evaporation" and stop. The examiner expects **two-stage kinetics** (constant rate + falling rate) and a mention of **residual solvent limits for lamination**. Lose 1–2 marks otherwise.
> 3. **"Ink transfer"** — saying "ink is transferred by pressure" is incomplete. The examiner expects **capillary number, surface tension, and cell geometry** discussion. Lose 1–2 marks if purely descriptive.
> 4. **Solvent vs. substrate table** — examiners love this. Drawing the table earns easy marks; writing it in prose loses structure.
> 5. **Units in numericals** — always quote viscosity in **mPa·s or seconds (cup number)** and cell dimensions in **µm or µm³**, not cm.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Gravure Inks — One-Page Rapid Revision**

**1. Definition:** Low-viscosity, fast-evaporating, solvent-based liquid inks for engraved-cylinder printing.

**2. Viscosity range:** **15–25 s #4 Ford cup** (~50–300 mPa·s) — lowest among major ink families.

**3. Drying mechanism:** **Evaporation only** — no oxidation, no water, no UV (in standard inks). Two stages: constant rate → falling rate.

**4. Composition template:**
- Pigment 8–25 %
- Binder (multiresin: hard + soft) 10–25 %
- Solvent 50–80 %
- Additives 1–5 %

**5. Key binders (memorize 3):** **NC** (hard, gloss), **Polyamide** (flex, adhesion), **Polyurethane** (premium flex, low solvent retention). Usually used as **blends**.

**6. Key solvents (memorize 3):** **Toluene** (workhorse aromatic), **Ethyl acetate** (NC friendly), **Ethanol** (polyamide, food-grade). **Glycol ethers** = retarder.

**7. Critical process control:** **Capillary number $Ca = \mu v / \gamma < 0.05$.** Above this, the doctor blade pulls ink out → missed dots.

**8. Cell geometry:** Quadragravure or pyramidal. Volume:
$$V_c = \frac{d}{3}\left(s_t^2 + s_t s_b + s_b^2\right)$$

**9. Dry film thickness:** Typically **1–8 µm** (much thinner than flexo/offset).

**10. Oven:** Hot air 60–120 °C, residence 3–9 s, 2–3 zones.

**11. Major applications:** Magazines, catalogs, flexible packaging, laminates, gift wrap, decorative vinyl, cigarette inner liners.

**12. Defect map (memorize 4):** Missed dots → low Ca; Pinholes → fast skin; Blocking → low Tg resin; Solvent retention → insufficient drying or wrong solvent.

**13. Substrate-resin rule of thumb:** Polar substrate (paper, foil) → NC works. Non-polar (PE, PP) → polyamide. Lamination-grade → PU + low retention.

**14. Regulatory watch:** Toluene is restricted in many countries for food packaging → shift to **alcohol-based / water-based gravure** is the modern trend.

**15. Competitive ink:** Gravure vs. **flexo** — gravure gives higher quality and consistency on long runs (> 500 000 m), but flexo is cheaper and greener for short runs.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Flexographic Inks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT866 - TECHNOLOGY OF PRINTING INKS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Flexographic Inks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Flexographic Inks** are a specialized class of low-viscosity, fast-drying **liquid inks** formulated specifically for the **flexographic printing process** — a rotary relief printing technique that uses flexible photopolymer printing plates and a rotating **anilox roll** (engraved metering cylinder) to transfer ink onto non-porous and porous substrates such as plastic films, aluminium foils, paper, paperboard, and corrugated cartons.

The KTU 2024 Scheme classifies flexographic inks under the broader category of **functional/industrial liquid inks**, with measurable rheological, surface-chemical, and optical performance parameters that align directly with the **Course Outcome (CO2): *Formulate and evaluate printing inks for various substrates and processes.***

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Stamp Pad of Modern Packaging"

> [!NOTE]
> **Imagine a thick, sticky rubber stamp pad**, but instead of being pressed once, the ink is delivered through a finely textured metal roller (the anilox) that meters out an exact, micro-thin layer of ink. Now imagine that ink dries in less than a second on a slippery plastic surface without smudging. **Flexographic ink is exactly this** — a precisely metered, rapidly drying liquid film engineered for industrial packaging production lines running at speeds exceeding **300 m/min**.

This analogy is essential because flexographic inks differ fundamentally from offset or gravure inks in **three pillars**:

| Property | Flexographic Requirement | Why It Matters |
|----------|--------------------------|----------------|
| **Viscosity** | **15–60 mPa·s** (low) | To be metered precisely by the engraved anilox cells |
| **Drying Speed** | **< 1 second** | High-speed web presses cannot tolerate smudging |
| **Adhesion** | To non-polar substrates (PE, PP, PET) | Most packaging films are chemically inert |

### 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Reference Metrics for Flexographic Inks:**
> - **Viscosity (Ford Cup #4):** 18–35 seconds at 25 °C
> - **Pigment Loading:** 10–20 % by weight
> - **Solvent/Vehicle Content:** 40–60 % by weight
> - **Anilox Cell Volume:** 1.5–12 BCM (Billion Cubic Microns per square inch)
> - **Typical Film Weight:** 1.0–3.5 gsm (grams per square metre) wet film

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Mechanism — The "Why & How" Behind Flexographic Ink Behaviour

The flexographic printing sequence can be decomposed into **five sequential stages**, each governed by a specific rheological or surface-chemistry principle:

1. **Metering Stage** — The anilox roller (engraved with billions of micro-cells) picks up ink from the fountain/chambered doctor blade system. The **cell geometry** (engraved volume) directly dictates the film thickness transferred.
2. **Transfer Stage** — Ink moves from anilox cells to the raised surface of the photopolymer plate. This transfer is governed by **capillary pressure** and **interfacial tension** balance.
3. **Plate-to-Substrate Stage** — The flexible plate contacts the moving web under controlled impression pressure (typically **0.2–0.5 MPa**).
4. **Wetting & Leveling Stage** — Ink must wet the substrate surface, indicated by the **contact angle θ < 90°**.
5. **Drying/Curing Stage** — The ink film converts to a solid, adhered layer via evaporation, penetration, or radiation curing.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" of Low Viscosity:** If the ink were too viscous (like offset ink), it would not release completely from the anilox cells and would cause excessive dot gain. **Newtonian or near-Newtonian flow behaviour is preferred** because the ink must flow out of cells cleanly under the low shear of the doctor blade wipe.

### 2.2 Composition of Flexographic Inks — Structural Breakdown

A typical flexographic ink contains **four functional classes of components**:

```
FLEXOGRAPHIC INK FORMULATION (Total = 100 % by weight)
│
├── PIGMENTS / COLORANTS (8–18 %)
│     ├── Organic pigments (e.g., Pigment Red 57:1, Pigment Blue 15:3)
│     └── Dyes (for transparent inks on metallized films)
│
├── RESINS / BINDERS (15–30 %)
│     ├── Polyamide resins (for PE/PP films)
│     ├── Nitrocellulose (for fast solvent release)
│     ├── Acrylic emulsions (water-based)
│     └── Polyurethane dispersions (lamination inks)
│
├── SOLVENTS / VEHICLES (40–65 %)
│     ├── Water (water-based inks)
│     ├── Ethanol / Isopropanol (alcohol-based)
│     ├── n-Propyl acetate, MEK, ethyl acetate (solvent-based)
│     └── Reactive monomers (UV-curable: TMPTA, HDDA)
│
└── ADDITIVES (1–5 %)
      ├── Waxes (PE / PTFE — for slip & scuff resistance)
      ├── Defoamers / deaerators
      ├── Plasticizers
      ├── Surfactants (substrate wetting agents)
      ├── Photoinitiators (UV systems only)
      └── pH stabilizers (water-based)
```

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains all high-yield equations and numerical relationships commonly tested in KTU University Examinations for flexographic ink technology.

| # | Concept / Parameter | Formula / Relationship | Units | Boundary / Operating Conditions |
|---|--------------------|------------------------|-------|---------------------------------|
| 1 | **Newtonian Viscosity** | $\eta = \dfrac{\tau}{\dot{\gamma}}$ | Pa·s | $\dot{\gamma} \approx 100 - 1000\ \text{s}^{-1}$ (flexo) |
| 2 | **Ink Film Weight** | $W = V_{cell} \times \rho_{ink} \times N_{cells}$ | gsm | $V_{cell}$ in BCM; $\rho_{ink}$ in g/cm³ |
| 3 | **Capillary Number** | $Ca = \dfrac{\eta \cdot v}{\sigma}$ | dimensionless | $Ca < 0.1$ for clean cell emptying |
| 4 | **Drying Rate (Evaporation)** | $\dfrac{dm}{dt} = \dfrac{h_{m} \cdot A \cdot (P_{s} - P_{a})}{RT}$ | kg/s | $h_m$ = mass transfer coefficient |
| 5 | **Wetting Contact Angle** | $\gamma_{SG} = \gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LG} \cdot \cos\theta$ | N/m | **Young's Equation** — requires $\theta < 90°$ |
| 6 | **Adhesion Energy** | $W_{a} = \gamma_{S} + \gamma_{L} - \gamma_{SL}$ | J/m² | **Dupre's Equation** — Work of Adhesion |
| 7 | **Surface Tension of Ink** | $\sigma_{ink} < \sigma_{substrate} - 8\ \text{mN/m}$ | mN/m | General rule for acceptable wetting |
| 8 | **Pigment Volume Concentration** | $PVC = \dfrac{V_{pigment}}{V_{pigment} + V_{binder}} \times 100$ | % | Optimum **15–25 %** for flexo |
| 9 | **Glass Transition (Tg) of Binder** | $T_{g} \approx T_{flexibility\ range}$ | °C | Must be $\leq -10°\text{C}$ for film flex |
| 10 | **Anilox Cell Volume (BCM)** | $\text{BCM} = 1.5$ to $12$ | $\mu m^3 / \text{cell}$ | Lower = finer print, less coverage |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Flexographic inks are **the backbone of modern packaging** because they:
- Enable high-speed printing (>600 m/min) on **non-absorbent substrates** that offset inks cannot wet.
- Allow lamination, heat-sealing, and food-contact compliance (FDA, EU Regulation 1935/2004).
- Support **CMYK + Spot Colour** workflows with consistent Pantone matching.
- Are critical in **flexible packaging** (snacks, pharma blister foils), **labels** (pressure-sensitive shrink sleeves), and **corrugated boxes** (e-commerce shipping cartons).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Calculation of Wet Ink Film Thickness from Anilox Cell Volume

This derivation is **frequently asked in KTU university examinations** for the *Technology of Printing Inks* course.

**Given:**
- Anilox cell volume, $V_{cell} = 6\ \text{BCM} = 6 \times 10^{9}\ \mu m^3 / \text{in}^{2}$
- Anilox screen ruling = 200 lines/cm
- Ink density, $\rho_{ink} = 1.05\ \text{g/cm}^{3}$

**Step 1 — Convert BCM to $\mu m^{3}$ per cm²:**

$$
1\ \text{BCM} = \frac{10^{9}\ \mu m^{3}}{1\ \text{in}^{2}} = \frac{10^{9}}{6.4516}\ \mu m^{3}/\text{cm}^{2}
$$

Therefore:

$$
V_{cell} = 6\ \text{BCM} = \frac{6 \times 10^{9}}{6.4516}\ \mu m^{3}/\text{cm}^{2} = 9.30 \times 10^{8}\ \mu m^{3}/\text{cm}^{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert volumetric cell volume to film thickness:**

Since $1\ \mu m^{3} = 10^{-12}\ \text{cm}^{3}$:

$$
t = \frac{V_{cell} \times 10^{-12}\ \text{cm}^{3}/\mu m^{3}}{1\ \text{cm}^{2}}
$$

$$
t = \frac{9.30 \times 10^{8} \times 10^{-12}}{1} = 9.30 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{cm}
$$

$$
t = 9.30 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{cm} = 9.30\ \mu m
$$

**Step 3 — Convert wet film thickness to film weight (gsm):**

$$
\text{gsm} = t \times \rho_{ink} = 9.30 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{cm} \times 1.05\ \text{g/cm}^{3}
$$

$$
\text{gsm} = 9.77 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{g/cm}^{2} = 9.77\ \text{g/m}^{2}\ \text{wet}
$$

**Conclusion:** A 6 BCM anilox delivers approximately **9.30 µm wet film** corresponding to **~9.77 gsm wet** ink deposit.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Critical Surface Tension Requirement Using Young-Dupre Analysis

For an ink to wet a plastic film substrate, the **Young-Dupre criterion** must be satisfied.

**Given:**
- Substrate (BOPP film): $\gamma_{S} = 38\ \text{mN/m}$
- Measured contact angle of ink drop: $\theta = 25°$

**Step 1 — Apply Young's Equation:**

$$
\gamma_{SG} = \gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LG} \cos\theta
$$

$$
38 = \gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LG} \cos(25°)
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Young-Dupre Work of Adhesion:**

$$
W_{a} = \gamma_{L} (1 + \cos\theta)
$$

$$
W_{a} = \gamma_{L} (1 + 0.9063) = 1.9063 \cdot \gamma_{L}
$$

**Step 3 — Interpret the wetting criterion:**

For a contact angle $\theta < 90°$, the ink wets the substrate. Since $\theta = 25° \ll 90°$, **wetting is spontaneous** — meaning the flexographic ink will spread uniformly on BOPP film without retraction.

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation for the student:** This is why **surface tension of the ink vehicle must always be ~6–10 mN/m lower than the substrate's surface energy**. For PE ($\gamma_S \approx 31\ \text{mN/m}$), corona treatment or primer coatings are often required to raise the substrate surface energy above 38 mN/m before flexographic printing.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Ink Types for Flexography

| Property | **Solvent-Based** | **Water-Based** | **UV-Curable** | **EB-Curable** |
|----------|------------------|-----------------|----------------|----------------|
| **Drying Mechanism** | Evaporation | Evaporation | Photo-polymerisation | Electron beam polymerisation |
| **VOC Emissions** | High | Negligible | Zero (after cure) | Zero |
| **Drying Speed** | Fast (1–2 s) | Moderate (5–10 s) | Ultra-fast (<0.1 s) | Ultra-fast (<0.1 s) |
| **Substrate Range** | All films | Paper, board, some films | All films | All films + thick layers |
| **Cost** | Low | Lowest | High | Very high |
| **KTU Exam Relevance** | High | High | Medium | Low |
| **Heat Resistance** | Moderate | Low | Excellent | Excellent |
| **Re-coatability** | Excellent | Limited | Limited | Limited |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Flexographic Ink Delivery System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Ink Fountain Reservoir] --> B[Chambered Doctor Blade System]
    B --> C[Anilox Roller Engraved Cells]
    C --> D[Fountain Roller Transfer]
    D --> E[Photopolymer Printing Plate]
    E --> F[Impression Cylinder + Substrate Web]
    F --> G[Drying or Curing Unit]
    G --> H[Dried Ink Film on Substrate]

    subgraph "Composition Layers"
        I1[Pigments 10-20%] --> I4[Final Ink Film]
```
