```mermaid
        I2[Resins 15-30%] --> I4
        I3[Solvents 40-60%] --> I4
        I5[Additives 1-5%] --> I4
    end

    subgraph "Quality Control"
        Q1[Viscosity Check Ford Cup 4] --> Q2[Print Density Spectro]
        Q2 --> Q3[Adhesion Cross Hatch]
        Q3 --> Q4[Scuff Resistance Rub Test]
    end
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Architecture Flow — Drying Mechanism Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Flexographic Ink Type Selection] --> B{Substrate Type?}
    B -- Non-porous film --> C{Speed Required?}
    B -- Porous paper --> D[Water-Based Penetration Drying]
    B -- Foil or metallized --> E{Heat Sensitive?}
    E -- No --> F[Solvent-Based Evaporation]
    E -- Yes --> G[UV Curable with Photoinitiators]

    C -- Less than 100 m/min --> H[Water-Based Forced Air Dryer]
    C -- Greater than 200 m/min --> I[UV or EB Curing]
    C -- Moderate speed --> J[Solvent-Based with Recovery]

    A --> K[Final QC: Color Density Adhesion Scuff]
    H --> K
    I --> K
    J --> K
    F --> K
    G --> K
    D --> K
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Process Map — UV-Flexographic Ink Cure Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[UV Flexographic Ink Film on Substrate] --> B[UV Lamp Exposure 365 nm]
    B --> C[Photoinitiator Absorbs UV Photon]
    C --> D[Free Radical Generation R dot]
    D --> E[Radical Attacks Acrylate Double Bond C=C]
    E --> F[Chain Growth Polymerization]
    F --> G[Crosslinked Polymer Network Formation]
    G --> H[Instant Solid Film with Full Cure]
    H --> I[Lamination or Finishing Ready]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Q1]**
> **Q1.** Define flexographic ink and list **any three** key properties required for it to function in a high-speed packaging press.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Flexographic ink is a **low-viscosity, fast-drying liquid ink** designed for flexography — a rotary relief process that uses flexible photopolymer plates and an engraved anilox roller to print on packaging substrates. The three essential properties are:
1. **Low viscosity** (15–60 mPa·s) — to allow clean release from anilox cells under low shear.
2. **Rapid drying/curing** — to prevent smudging on non-absorbent substrates at speeds >200 m/min.
3. **Good adhesion to diverse substrates** — including PE, PP, PET, aluminium foil, and paperboard, often requiring surface tension matching through corona treatment.

*(Marking split: 1 mark for definition + 2 marks for the three properties.)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Q2]**
> **Q2.** Differentiate between **water-based** and **UV-curable** flexographic inks in terms of drying mechanism, VOC emissions, and substrate suitability.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Water-Based | UV-Curable |
|-----------|-------------|------------|
| **Drying Mechanism** | Water evaporation + partial binder penetration | Photo-initiated free-radical polymerisation |
| **VOC Emissions** | Negligible | Zero (post-cure) |
| **Substrate Range** | Paper, board, some films | All films, foils, laminates |

*(Marking split: 2 marks for table + 1 mark for concluding note.)*

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU ESE — Model Question Paper, Module 2]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Complete Answer Choice*

**(a)** Explain the **complete composition of a solvent-based flexographic ink**, detailing the function of each component with typical percentage ranges. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Discuss in detail the **drying mechanisms** employed in flexographic inks with suitable examples. Compare solvent evaporation drying with UV-curing. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Composition of Solvent-Based Flexographic Ink (7 marks):**

A high-quality solvent-based flexographic ink is engineered with four functional classes of materials:

1. **Pigments (8–18 %):** Provide colour and opacity. Organic pigments such as *Pigment Red 57:1* (rubine toner) or *Pigment Blue 15:3* (Phthalo Blue) are most common due to their high chromaticity and chemical stability.

2. **Resins/Binders (15–30 %):** The "skeleton" of the dried film. Typical choices:
   - **Polyamide resins** — flexible, alcohol-soluble, excellent adhesion to PE/PP films.
   - **Nitrocellulose** — promotes fast solvent release ("solvent release agent").
   - **Maleic/rosin-modified resins** — improved gloss and pigment wetting.

3. **Solvents/Vehicles (40–60 %):** Volatile carriers that control drying rate. Common flexographic solvents:
   - **Ethanol / isopropanol** — fast evaporation, low toxicity.
   - **n-Propyl acetate** — controlled evaporation.
   - **Methyl ethyl ketone (MEK)** — strong solvency for many resins.
   - The **solvent blend ratio** is tuned to balance press stability with drying speed.

4. **Additives (1–5 %):** Function-specific chemicals:
   - **Waxes (PE, PTFE)** — slip and scuff resistance.
   - **Plasticizers** — film flexibility.
   - **Surfactants** — wetting of difficult substrates.
   - **Defoamers** — eliminate microfoam in the fountain.
   - **Anti-oxidants** — prevent pigment/resin degradation.

**(b) Drying Mechanisms in Flexographic Inks (7 marks):**

Three principal drying mechanisms are used:

1. **Evaporation Drying** — Solvent (water or organic) evaporates from the printed film, leaving behind a binder-rich solid film. Governed by Fick's law and partial pressure differentials. Typical for solvent-based and water-based inks.

2. **Penetration Drying** — Ink vehicle migrates into porous substrates (paper, board), leaving binder and pigment near the surface. Used for water-based inks on corrugated board.

3. **Radiation Curing** — UV or EB radiation triggers crosslinking of monomers/oligomers via free-radical polymerisation. Used for UV-curable inks; delivers instant cure and zero VOC.

**Comparison Table:**

| Aspect | Solvent Evaporation | UV Curing |
|--------|---------------------|-----------|
| Energy required | Heat | UV photons |
| Speed | 0.5–2 s | <0.1 s |
| VOC | High | Zero |
| Capital cost | Low | High (UV lamp) |
| Substrate heat exposure | High (ovens) | None |

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Internal Choice*

**(a)** With the help of **Young-Dupre equation**, explain the wetting behaviour of flexographic ink on plastic films. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Discuss the role of **anilox roller cell volume (BCM)** in determining ink film thickness and print quality. Include a numerical example. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Young-Dupre Wetting Analysis (7 marks):**

The ability of flexographic ink to wet a substrate is governed by the balance of interfacial tensions. **Young's Equation** states:

$$
\gamma_{SG} = \gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LG}\cos\theta
$$

where:
- $\gamma_{SG}$ = substrate surface free energy (mN/m),
- $\gamma_{SL}$ = solid-liquid interfacial tension,
- $\gamma_{LG}$ = liquid (ink) surface tension,
- $\theta$ = equilibrium contact angle.

The **Young-Dupre equation** for the work of adhesion:

$$
W_{a} = \gamma_{L}(1 + \cos\theta)
$$

**Wetting Condition:** For spontaneous spreading, $\theta < 90°$ and ideally $\theta < 30°$. This requires $\sigma_{ink} < \sigma_{substrate} - 8\ \text{mN/m}$.

**Practical Application:**
- Polyethylene (PE) has $\gamma_{S} \approx 31$ mN/m. To wet it, ink surface tension must be below **23 mN/m**. This is achieved by adding fluorosurfactants.
- Most plastic films are therefore **corona-treated** to raise $\gamma_{S}$ above **38 mN/m** before flexo printing.

**(b) Anilox Cell Volume — Numerical Example (7 marks):**

**Given:** Anilox specification = **8 BCM**, screen = **200 lines/cm**, ink density = **1.10 g/cm³**.

**Step 1 — Convert BCM to $\mu m^{3}/cm^{2}$:**

$$
V = \frac{8 \times 10^{9}}{6.4516} = 1.24 \times 10^{9}\ \mu m^{3}/\text{cm}^{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert to wet film thickness:**

$$
t = 1.24 \times 10^{9} \times 10^{-4}\ \mu m / cm = 12.4\ \mu m
$$

*(Wait — recalculating carefully:)*

$$
t = 1.24 \times 10^{9}\ \mu m^{3} \div 10^{8}\ \mu m^{2}/\text{cm}^{2} = 12.4\ \mu m
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to gsm:**

$$
\text{gsm}_{wet} = 12.4 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{cm} \times 1.10\ \text{g/cm}^{3} = 13.64\ \text{g/m}^{2}
$$

**Interpretation:** An 8 BCM anilox is suitable for **fine-line printing and process colour** (CMYK process printing). For solid coverage (e.g., kraft liner), 12–14 BCM anilox is preferred to deliver heavier ink laydown.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing flexo with offset inks:** Many students incorrectly cite "tack" as a primary property — flexo inks are characterized by **viscosity and drying speed**, not tack.
> 2. **Wrong BCM conversion:** Forgetting that 1 BCM = $10^{9}\ \mu m^{3}$/in² (not per cm²). Always divide by 6.4516 to convert to cm² units.
> 3. **Missing corona treatment mention:** Writing about ink adhesion on PE/PP without mentioning **corona discharge / surface treatment** as a prerequisite will lose 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Skipping the role of additives:** Examiners specifically test whether students can name *at least* 2 additives with their function (waxes → slip, defoamers → microfoam removal).
> 5. **Ignoring wash-up:** Solvent-based flexo inks require proper wash-up with **acetate/MEK blends** — a practical aspect often asked for 2 marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **📋 Rapid Revision Checklist — Flexographic Inks**

**Core Concept:**
- Flexographic inks are **low-viscosity, fast-drying liquid inks** for **flexography**.
- Process uses **anilox roller + photopolymer plate** to print on **non-absorbent** substrates.

**Critical Composition Numbers (must memorize):**
- Pigments: **10–20 %**
- Resins/Binders: **15–30 %**
- Solvents: **40–60 %**
- Additives: **1–5 %**
- Viscosity: **15–60 mPa·s**
- Ford Cup #4: **18–35 s**

**Key Equations:**
- Young's Equation: $\gamma_{SG} = \gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LG}\cos\theta$
- Young-Dupre Work of Adhesion: $W_{a} = \gamma_{L}(1 + \cos\theta)$
- BCM to gsm: $\text{gsm} = \dfrac{\text{BCM} \times 10^{9} \times \rho}{6.4516 \times 10^{8}}$
- Wetting Criterion: $\sigma_{ink} < \sigma_{substrate} - 8\ \text{mN/m}$

**Drying Mechanisms:**
- Solvent evaporation, Penetration, UV curing, EB curing.

**Ink Types:**
- Solvent-based, Water-based, UV-curable, EB-curable.

**Common Binders:**
- Polyamide (PE/PP films), Nitrocellulose (fast release), Acrylic emulsion (water-based), Polyurethane (lamination).

**Substrates Most Commonly Printed:**
- PE, PP, PET films; aluminium foil; paper; paperboard; corrugated board; metallized films.

**Quality Tests:**
- **Viscosity** (Ford Cup #4), **Density** (Spectro-densitometer), **Adhesion** (Cross-hatch / T-peel), **Scuff** (Taber / Sutherland Rub), **Gloss** (60° gloss meter).

**Critical Realisation:** Flexo inks are a **process-engineered product** — the formulation must match the anilox, plate, substrate, and press speed **simultaneously**. Change any one variable and the ink recipe must be re-tuned.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Radiation Curable Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT866 - TECHNOLOGY OF PRINTING INKS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Radiation Curable Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Radiation Curable Systems (RCS)** in printing ink technology refer to a class of **100% solids or low-VOC liquid ink formulations** that undergo rapid, *in-situ* polymerization and crosslinking when exposed to high-energy electromagnetic radiation — specifically **Ultraviolet (UV) light (100–400 nm)** or **Electron Beam (EB) radiation (typically 80–300 keV)**. The cure is initiated by the generation of reactive species (free radicals or cations) from a **photoinitiator (PI)**, which trigger chain-growth polymerization of the **oligomers and monomers** present in the vehicle.

The curing process is **almost instantaneous** (milliseconds to seconds), converting the liquid ink film into a hard, chemically resistant, three-dimensional polymer network. This is the foundation of modern **energy-curable inks** used in offset, flexo, gravure, screen, and inkjet printing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Radiation Curable Systems fall under the broader category of **"Non-Traditional/Alternative Curing Technologies"** that replace oxidative drying, evaporative drying, and catalytic systems. They are commercially critical for **food packaging, labels, and narrow-web printing**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a tray of liquid resin sitting in sunlight. If you leave it for hours, nothing happens — it just slowly evaporates. Now imagine you add a special "magic dust" (the **photoinitiator**) to the liquid, and then you flash a very bright UV torch at it.

- The magic dust **absorbs the UV light** and "explodes" into tiny reactive sparks (free radicals).
- These sparks grab molecules of the liquid resin and link them together like a chain of paperclips.
- Within **less than a second**, the liquid becomes a **solid, glossy, scratch-resistant film** — just like a superhero's armor instantly hardening in combat.

That instant, on-demand hardening is exactly what **radiation curable ink** does on a press.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value / Unit | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| UV-A wavelength | **315–400 nm** | Long-wave; surface cure |
| UV-B wavelength | **280–315 nm** | Mid-wave; bulk cure |
| UV-C wavelength | **100–280 nm** | Short-wave; high energy |
| UV-C / UV-V dosage (Hg lamp) | **80–250 mJ/cm²** | Energy delivered per unit area |
| Peak intensity (Hg lamp) | **200–600 W/cm** | Lamp output power density |
| EB energy range | **80–300 keV** | Penetration & dose control |
| Line speed (UV press) | **50–400 m/min** | Press throughput |
| Photoinitiator loading | **1–10 wt%** | Typical in formulation |
| VOC content | **< 5 wt%** | Near-zero emission |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Metric:** The *UV dose* (energy per unit area) is the single most important control parameter on a UV press. It is defined as:
> $$\text{UV Dose} \left( \frac{\text{mJ}}{\text{cm}^2} \right) = \text{Intensity} \left( \frac{\text{mW}}{\text{cm}^2} \right) \times \text{Exposure Time (s)}$$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Radiation Curable Systems

Radiation curable ink systems are classified along **two orthogonal axes**: (a) the **type of radiation** used, and (b) the **polymerization mechanism**.

#### 2.1.1 By Radiation Type

**A. UV Curable Inks (Most common)**
- Require a **photoinitiator** in the formulation.
- Use **mercury (Hg)**, **metal halide**, or **UV-LED** lamps as the radiation source.
- Cure in air, but surface cure can be **inhibited by oxygen** (oxygen inhibition effect).
- Cheaper equipment than EB; widely adopted in commercial printing.

**B. EB (Electron Beam) Curable Inks**
- **Do NOT require a photoinitiator** — high-energy electrons directly ionize the resin.
- Cure in **thick films** and **pigmented systems** with no oxygen inhibition.
- Require **inert nitrogen atmosphere** (typically < 50 ppm O₂) to prevent ozone generation and to control free-radical lifetime.
- Higher capital cost; used for **food packaging and high-end labels**.

#### 2.1.2 By Polymerization Mechanism

**A. Free-Radical Polymerization (FRP)**
- Initiated by **Type I (cleavage) or Type II (H-abstraction) photoinitiators**.
- Inhibited by **atmospheric oxygen** at the surface.
- Fast cure speed; **acrylate-based chemistry** dominant.
- Post-cure (dark reaction) can continue for hours.

**B. Cationic Polymerization (CP)**
- Initiated by **onium salts** (e.g., diaryliodonium, triarylsulfonium hexafluoroantimonate).
- **Not inhibited by oxygen** — major advantage for surface cure.
- **Post-cure continues indefinitely** (living polymerization).
- Slower initiation, but excellent adhesion and low shrinkage.
- Epoxide and vinyl ether resins are typical.

### 2.2 Formulation Architecture (Composition of a UV Ink)

A radiation curable ink is a four-component system:

- **Oligomers (30–60 wt%)** — the "backbone" providing the final film properties.
  - *Epoxy acrylates* — fast cure, high gloss, chemical resistance.
  - *Polyurethane acrylates (PUA)* — flexibility, abrasion resistance.
  - *Polyester acrylates* — low viscosity, pigment wetting.
  - *Silicone acrylates* — release properties.

- **Monomers / Reactive Diluents (10–40 wt%)** — viscosity reducers that become part of the film.
  - *Monofunctional*: isobornyl acrylate (IBOA), 2-phenoxyethyl acrylate (2-PEA).
  - *Difunctional*: 1,6-hexanediol diacrylate (HDDA), tripropylene glycol diacrylate (TPGDA).
  - *Polyfunctional*: trimethylolpropane triacrylate (TMPTA), pentaerythritol triacrylate (PETIA).

- **Photoinitiators (1–10 wt%)** — the radiation "trigger".
  - *Type I (α-cleavage)*: benzoin ethers, benzil ketals, α-hydroxy ketones (e.g., Irgacure 184, Irgacure 651), acylphosphine oxides (TPO, BAPO).
  - *Type II (H-abstraction)*: benzophenone + amine synergist (e.g., Michler's ketone, 2-ITX).
  - *Cationic PIs*: triarylsulfonium hexafluoroantimonate salts.

- **Additives (1–5 wt%)** — performance enhancers.
  - *Pigments*: carbon black (problematic — absorbs UV!), phthalocyanines (UV-transparent).
  - *Sensitizers*: thioxanthones, anthracenes — extend absorption to longer wavelengths.
  - *Stabilizers*: HALS (Hindered Amine Light Stabilizers), UV absorbers (Benzotriazoles).
  - *Wetting/dispersing agents, defoamers, slip additives*.

### 2.3 The Cure Mechanism (Free Radical — Step-by-Step)

**Step 1 — Photoinitiation (photolysis):**
The photoinitiator (PI) absorbs a UV photon matching its absorption band, exciting it from the singlet ground state (S₀) to a reactive excited state (S₁), which then undergoes **α-cleavage** (Norrish Type I) to yield two free radicals.

$$\text{PI} \xrightarrow{h\nu} [\text{PI}]^{*} \rightarrow \text{R}^{\bullet} + \text{R'}^{\bullet}$$

**Step 2 — Chain Initiation:**
A primary radical attacks the C=C double bond of an acrylate monomer/oligomer, creating a new carbon-centered radical on the chain end.

$$\text{R}^{\bullet} + \text{CH}_2{=}\text{CH}{-}\text{X} \rightarrow \text{R}{-}\text{CH}_2{-}^{\bullet}\text{CH}{-}\text{X}$$

**Step 3 — Chain Propagation:**
The chain radical adds successive monomer units in a head-to-tail manner, propagating the radical center down the chain.

**Step 4 — Chain Termination:**
Two growing chains combine (coupling or disproportionation) or react with an inhibitor, ending chain growth.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why oxygen inhibits free-radical cure:** Atmospheric O₂ is a diradical that rapidly adds to the growing chain radical, forming a stable **peroxy radical (ROO•)** that cannot propagate. The solution: increase PI loading, use Type II PIs, flood the surface with N₂, or add amine synergists.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $D = I \times t$ | UV dose = Intensity × Time | $\text{mJ/cm}^2$ |
| 2 | $I(z) = I_0 \, e^{-\alpha z}$ | Beer-Lambert intensity attenuation through film | $\text{W/cm}^2$ |
| 3 | $D_p = \dfrac{1}{2.303 \,\varepsilon \, c}$ | Penetration depth (37% transmission) | cm |
| 4 | $\alpha = 2.303 \,\varepsilon \, c$ | Absorption coefficient (decadic) | $\text{cm}^{-1}$ |
| 5 | $R_p = k_p [\text{M}] \sqrt{\dfrac{2 f k_d [\text{I}]}{k_t}}$ | Steady-state rate of polymerization | $\text{mol/(L·s)}$ |
| 6 | $\text{DC}(\%) = \dfrac{C_0 - C_t}{C_0} \times 100$ | Degree of C=C double bond conversion | dimensionless % |
| 7 | $\Phi_{\text{cure}} = \dfrac{n_{\text{chains}}}{n_{\text{photons absorbed}}}$ | Quantum yield of initiation | dimensionless |
| 8 | $E_g = \dfrac{h c}{\lambda}$ | Photon energy | J or eV |
| 9 | $T_g \uparrow \text{ with crosslink density}$ | $T_g$ increases with crosslinking | °C |
| 10 | $\eta \propto M_w^{3.4}$ | Viscosity scales with molecular weight | cP |

> **Where:** $\varepsilon$ = molar absorptivity (L·mol⁻¹·cm⁻¹); $c$ = concentration (mol/L); $\alpha$ = linear absorption coefficient (cm⁻¹); $k_p$ = propagation rate constant; $k_d$ = initiator decomposition rate constant; $k_t$ = termination rate constant; $f$ = initiator efficiency (0 < f ≤ 1); $E_g$ = photon energy; $h$ = Planck's constant (**6.626 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s**); $c$ = speed of light (**3 × 10⁸ m/s**); $\lambda$ = wavelength (m).

> **Real-world utility:** The Beer-Lambert relationship dictates the **maximum printable film thickness** for a UV ink. If a black pigment absorbs strongly, only the top few microns cure — leading to *through-cure failure*. The solution is to use **transparent pigments** (Phthalocyanine Blue, PY-83) and **surface-functionalized carbon black** with enhanced PI loading.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: UV Dose as a Function of Lamp Geometry and Substrate Speed

A UV lamp is typically a linear source of length $L$ (cm), emitting total radiant power $P_0$ (W) into a focal length $F$ (cm) above the substrate. The irradiance $I$ on the substrate at any point is approximated as the lamp power divided by the focal spot area.

**Given:**
- Lamp power: $P_0$ (W)
- Focal length (lamp to substrate): $F$ (cm)
- Lamp arc length: $L$ (cm)
- Substrate line speed: $v$ (cm/s)

**Step 1 — Focal spot area on the substrate:**

The lamp image is focused to a rectangle of width $F$ and length $L$ on the substrate.

$$A_{\text{spot}} = F \times L \;\; \text{(cm}^2\text{)}$$

**Step 2 — Irradiance (Intensity) on substrate:**

Intensity is power per unit area:

$$I = \frac{P_0}{A_{\text{spot}}} = \frac{P_0}{F \cdot L} \;\; \left( \frac{\text{W}}{\text{cm}^2} \right)$$

**Step 3 — Exposure time for a point on the substrate:**

As the substrate moves at velocity $v$ under the lamp of length $L$, a point spends a transit time $t$ beneath the focal zone:

$$t = \frac{L}{v} \;\; \text{(s)}$$

**Step 4 — UV Dose delivered:**

Combining the above, the integrated energy per unit area (dose) is:

$$\boxed{D = I \times t = \frac{P_0}{F \cdot L} \times \frac{L}{v} = \frac{P_0}{F \cdot v} \;\; \left( \frac{\text{J}}{\text{cm}^2} \right)}$$

The lamp arc length $L$ **cancels out** — only the focal length and line speed govern the dose! This is a classic KTU result.

**Numerical Example:**

Given: $P_0 = 200$ W, $F = 5$ cm, $v = 100$ cm/s.

$$D = \frac{200}{5 \times 100} = \frac{200}{500} = 0.4 \; \frac{\text{J}}{\text{cm}^2} = 400 \; \frac{\text{mJ}}{\text{cm}^2}$$

This is sufficient dose for a typical UV offset ink (requires 200–500 mJ/cm²). ✓

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Penetration Depth in a Pigmented UV Ink

For a UV ink containing pigment, the depth to which curing occurs is governed by the Beer-Lambert law. Light intensity at depth $z$ is:

$$I(z) = I_0 \, e^{-\alpha z}$$

For an ink to cure, the local intensity must exceed a **threshold intensity** $I_{\text{th}}$ (typically 5–20 mW/cm²). Setting $I(z) = I_{\text{th}}$:

$$I_{\text{th}} = I_0 \, e^{-\alpha \, z_{\max}}$$

Solving for the maximum cure depth $z_{\max}$:

$$e^{\alpha \, z_{\max}} = \frac{I_0}{I_{\text{th}}}$$

$$\ln\left(\frac{I_0}{I_{\text{th}}}\right) = \alpha \, z_{\max}$$

$$\boxed{z_{\max} = \frac{1}{\alpha} \ln\left(\frac{I_0}{I_{\text{th}}}\right) = \frac{1}{2.303 \, \varepsilon \, c} \log_{10}\left(\frac{I_0}{I_{\text{th}}}\right)}$$

**Interpretation:** Doubling the molar absorptivity (e.g., by adding more carbon black) **halves the cure depth** — directly explaining why black UV inks require special formulation.

**Numerical Example:**

Given: $\varepsilon = 5000 \; \text{L} \cdot \text{mol}^{-1} \cdot \text{cm}^{-1}$, $c = 0.05 \; \text{mol/L}$, $I_0 = 200 \; \text{mW/cm}^2$, $I_{\text{th}} = 10 \; \text{mW/cm}^2$.

**Step 1 — Compute absorption coefficient:**

$$\alpha = 2.303 \times 5000 \times 0.05 = 575.75 \; \text{cm}^{-1}$$

**Step 2 — Compute log ratio:**

$$\log_{10}\left(\frac{200}{10}\right) = \log_{10}(20) = 1.301$$

**Step 3 — Compute maximum cure depth:**

$$z_{\max} = \frac{1.301}{575.75} = 2.26 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{cm} = 22.6 \; \mu\text{m}$$

This explains why a **2 µm ink film** fully cures, but a **50 µm thick UV flexo ink** may not. → Use **transparent pigments** or **multiple PI packages** for thick films.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Degree of Conversion in Free-Radical Polymerization

For an acrylate system, the **degree of conversion (DC)** is the fraction of C=C double bonds that have reacted. Using the kinetic model from Section 2.3 (Equation 5), the rate of monomer consumption is:

$$\frac{d[\text{M}]}{dt} = -k_p [\text{M}] [\text{R}^{\bullet}]$$

In the steady state, the radical concentration is:

$$[\text{R}^{\bullet}]_{\text{ss}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 f k_d [\text{I}]}{k_t}}$$

Therefore:

$$-\frac{d[\text{M}]}{dt} = k_p [\text{M}] \sqrt{\frac{2 f k_d [\text{I}]}{k_t}}$$

**Integrating from $t = 0$ to $t$:**

$$-\int_{[\text{M}]_0}^{[\text{M}]} \frac{d[\text{M}]}{[\text{M}]} = k_p \sqrt{\frac{2 f k_d [\text{I}]}{k_t}} \int_0^t dt$$

$$\ln\left(\frac{[\text{M}]_0}{[\text{M}]}\right) = k_p \sqrt{\frac{2 f k_d [\text{I}]}{k_t}} \; t$$

**The degree of conversion is:**

$$\text{DC}(t) = 1 - \frac{[\text{M}]}{[\text{M}]_0} = 1 - \exp\left(-k_p \sqrt{\frac{2 f k_d [\text{I}]}{k_t}} \; t\right)$$

$$\boxed{\text{DC}(t) = 1 - \exp\left(-k_p \sqrt{\frac{2 f k_d [\text{I}]}{k_t}} \; t\right)}$$

**Key Insight:** DC approaches 1 asymptotically, and is **never 100%** in practice. Real UV acrylate films reach **DC = 70–95%** depending on atmosphere and PI loading. The unreacted C=C bonds can cause *odor, extractables, and migration* — a critical concern in **food packaging inks** regulated by EU Regulation 1935/2004 and Swiss Ordinance 817.023.21.

---

### 3.4 Photon Energy Calculation

UV photons carry energy inversely proportional to wavelength:

$$E_g = \frac{h c}{\lambda}$$

For a UV-A photon at $\lambda = 365$ nm (typical LED wavelength):

$$E_g = \frac{(6.626 \times 10^{-34}) \times (3 \times 10^8)}{365 \times 10^{-9}}$$

$$E_g = \frac{1.988 \times 10^{-25}}{3.65 \times 10^{-7}} = 5.446 \times 10^{-19} \; \text{J/photon}$$

Converting to electron-volts (1 eV = $1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ J):

$$E_g = \frac{5.446 \times 10^{-19}}{1.602 \times 10^{-19}} = 3.40 \; \text{eV/photon}$$

This is **sufficient to cleave a C–C bond** (~3.5 eV) — explaining why UV photons can trigger the α-cleavage of a Type I photoinitiator. For comparison, an **EB electron at 100 keV** carries ~100,000 eV — vastly more than UV, but the energy is deposited differently (penetrating, ionizing, not bound to specific chromophores).

---

### 3.5 Comparative Analytical Matrix (KTU 2024 High-Yield)

| Property | UV Free-Radical | UV Cationic | EB Curing |
|---|---|---|---|
| Photoinitiator required? | **Yes (1–10%)** | Yes (onium salts) | **No** |
| Oxygen inhibition | Severe | **None** | None |
| Cure speed | Very fast (ms–s) | Slower (1–60 s) | Very fast (ms) |
| Post-cure | Minor | **Significant (living)** | None |
| Pigment compatibility | Moderate | Good | **Excellent** |
| Equipment cost | Low | Low | **High** |
| Operating cost | Low (lamps) | Low | High (shielding, N₂) |
| VOC content | < 5% | < 5% | **~0%** |
| Film shrinkage | High (~5–10%) | **Low (~1–2%)** | Moderate |
| Migration risk | Higher | **Lower** | Lower |
| Best for | Coatings, commercial print | Food packaging, adhesives | Thick films, laminates |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Schematic 1: UV Ink Cure Process (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **functional architecture of a UV curing process** on a printing press. It traces the energy flow from electrical input to the final crosslinked polymer film, highlighting each subsystem.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Electrical Power Supply 380V 3Phase] --> B[UV Lamp Ballast and Inverter]
    B --> C[UV Lamp Mercury or Metal Halide or LED]
    C --> D[UV Radiation 100 to 400 nm]
    D --> E[Ink Film Surface Photoinitiator Absorbs Photons]
    E --> F[Photolysis Generates Free Radicals R dot]
    F --> G[Radical Attacks Acrylate C double bond C]
    G --> H[Chain Propagation Oligomer and Monomer]
    H --> I[Chain Termination Coupling or Disproportionation]
    I --> J[Crosslinked Polymer Network Solid Film]
    J --> K[Substrate Paper Film Foil Board]
    L[Atmospheric Oxygen Inhibitor] -.->|competes with| F
    M[N2 Blanket Optional EB type] -.->|eliminates| L
    C -.->|emits| N[IR Heat Side Effect]
    N -.-> J
    O[Pigment Absorbs UV] -.->|reduces| D
    O -.-> P[Carbon Black Cures Poorly]
```

### 4.2 Schematic 2: Decision Tree for Choosing a Radiation Curable System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[New Ink Formulation Project] --> B{Substrate Type}
    B -->|Absorbent| C[Paper or Board]
    B -->|Non Absorbent| D[Film or Foil]
    C --> E{Press Type}
    D --> E
    E -->|Offset| F[Low Viscosity Ink]
    E -->|Flexo| G[Very Low Viscosity]
    E -->|Screen| H[High Viscosity Paste]
    F --> I{Cure Speed Required}
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I -->|>100 m per min| J[Free Radical Acrylate]
    I -->|< 50 m per min| K{Cationic Acceptable}
    K -->|Yes| L[Epoxy Vinyl Ether Onium PI]
    K -->|No| M[Boost PI Loading or Multi PI Package]
    J --> N{Food Contact}
    L --> N
    M --> N
    N -->|Yes| O[EB or LED UV with Low Migration]
    N -->|No| P[Standard UV Hg Lamp]
    O --> Q[Use Acrylate with High Functionality and Low Extractables]
    P --> R[Optimize Cost with BAPO TPO Blend]
```

### 4.3 Schematic 3: Photoinitiator Classification (Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Photoinitiators] --> B[Type I Cleavage]
    A --> C[Type II H Abstraction]
    A --> D[Cationic Onium Salts]
    B --> B1[Benzoin Ether]
    B --> B2[Alpha Hydroxy Ketone Irgacure 184]
    B --> B3[Acylphosphine Oxide TPO BAPO]
    C --> C1[Benzophenone plus Amine]
    C --> C2[Thioxanthone plus Amine]
    C --> C3[2 ITX plus MDEA]
    D --> D1[Diaryliodonium PF6 or SbF6]
    D --> D2[Triarylsulfonium SbF6]
    B2 --> E[Free Radical Cure]
    B3 --> E
    C1 --> E
    D1 --> F[Cationic Cure]
    D2 --> F
    E --> G[Acrylate System]
    F --> H[Epoxy or Vinyl Ether System]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** In Mermaid, single dots and special characters inside node labels are stripped for safety. The diagrams above use plain uppercase alphanumeric text to remain compatible with the KTU 2024 rendering engine. The dotted arrows (e.g., `-.->`) indicate **side-effects or competing pathways** (oxygen inhibition, IR heat emission, pigment absorption) — not part of the main flow.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Define **photoinitiator**. Classify photoinitiators used in UV-curable printing inks with one example each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **photoinitiator (PI)** is a compound that absorbs UV radiation (typically 200–400 nm) and generates reactive species — free radicals or cations — that initiate the polymerization of monomers and oligomers in a radiation-curable ink.

**Classification:**

| Type | Mechanism | Example | Cure Type |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Type I (α-cleavage)** | Unimolecular bond cleavage upon excitation | Irgacure 184 (1-hydroxycyclohexyl phenyl ketone), TPO | Free radical |
| **Type II (H-abstraction)** | Bimolecular H-abstraction from a co-initiator (synergist) | Benzophenone + MDEA (triethanolamine or amine) | Free radical |
| **Cationic (Onium salts)** | Photolysis → superacid | Triarylsulfonium hexafluoroantimonate | Cationic |

*(Award 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for classification table, 1 mark for example + cure type.)* ✓

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Differentiate between **UV curable** and **EB curable** printing ink systems. Mention two advantages of EB over UV.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Property | UV Curable | EB Curable |
|---|---|---|
| Radiation source | Mercury / LED lamps (UV photons) | Electron beam (high-energy electrons, 80–300 keV) |
| Photoinitiator | Required (1–10 wt%) | **Not required** |
| Atmosphere | Air (oxygen inhibits surface cure) | Inert N₂ blanket (< 50 ppm O₂) |
| Capital cost | Moderate | High |
| Pigment compatibility | Moderate (UV-absorbing pigments problematic) | **Excellent** |
| Through-cure in thick films | Limited | Excellent |

**Two advantages of EB over UV:**
1. **No photoinitiator needed** → no extractables, no odor, no PI migration — ideal for food-contact packaging.
2. **Excellent through-cure** of thick, pigmented films → enables heavy ink laydowns (e.g., metallics, whites, laminating inks).

*(Award 1 mark for differentiation, 1 mark each for the two advantages.)* ✓

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **Question A (14 marks):** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
> **(a)** Explain the **free-radical polymerization mechanism** of a UV-curable acrylate printing ink with a neat sketch of the four steps. Discuss the role of each formulation component. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Derive the **maximum cure depth equation** in a pigmented UV ink. A black UV flexo ink contains a pigment with $\varepsilon = 8000 \; \text{L mol}^{-1} \text{cm}^{-1}$ at $c = 0.04 \; \text{mol/L}$. If $I_0 = 250 \; \text{mW/cm}^2$ and $I_{\text{th}} = 20 \; \text{mW/cm}^2$, compute the maximum cure depth in µm. Comment on the result. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — Free Radical Polymerization Mechanism (7 marks)

The UV cure proceeds in four well-defined kinetic steps:

**Step 1 — Initiation (Photolysis):**
The photoinitiator (e.g., Irgacure 184) absorbs a UV photon, is excited to its S₁ state, and undergoes α-cleavage to yield two free radicals. A primary radical $R^{\bullet}$ attacks the C=C of an acrylate monomer:

$$\text{PI} \xrightarrow{h\nu} \text{PI}^{*} \rightarrow 2\,\text{R}^{\bullet} \qquad \text{(3-marks breakdown: 1 mark for PI cleavage)}$$

**Step 2 — Propagation:**
The new chain radical adds successive acrylate molecules in head-to-tail fashion, propagating the radical center. The growing chain retains its radical character until termination.

**Step 3 — Termination:**
Two chain radicals combine by **coupling** (forming a single dead polymer chain) or **disproportionation** (forming two chains, one saturated, one unsaturated).

**Step 4 — Chain Transfer (side reaction):**
A growing radical abstracts a hydrogen from another species (additive, oligomer, or solvent), terminating the chain but creating a new radical elsewhere. This controls molecular weight.

**Role of each formulation component (4 marks total — distribute as needed):**

| Component | Role |
|---|---|
| **Oligomer** (e.g., epoxy acrylate) | Provides backbone, hardness, chemical resistance, gloss |
| **Monomer** (e.g., HDDA, TMPTA) | Dilutes viscosity; crosslinks for hardness and solvent resistance |
| **Photoinitiator** | Absorbs UV, generates radicals that start polymerization |
| **Pigment** | Provides color, but absorbs UV (problem for cure) |
| **Additives** | Slip, defoam, wetting, stabilization |

*Total part (a): 7 marks (1+1+1+1 = 4 marks for mechanism steps, 3 marks for component table, mark distribution as the examiner fits.)*

#### Part (b) — Derivation of Maximum Cure Depth (7 marks)

**Step 1 — State the governing equation (Beer-Lambert):**
The UV intensity decays exponentially with depth $z$ in the ink film:
$$I(z) = I_0 \, e^{-\alpha z} \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$

**Step 2 — Define the cure threshold condition:**
The ink cures only where the local intensity exceeds $I_{\text{th}}$:
$$I(z_{\max}) = I_{\text{th}} \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $z_{\max}$:**
$$I_{\text{th}} = I_0 \, e^{-\alpha z_{\max}} \implies e^{\alpha z_{\max}} = \frac{I_0}{I_{\text{th}}}$$
$$\alpha \, z_{\max} = \ln\left(\frac{I_0}{I_{\text{th}}}\right) \quad \text{(2 marks)}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to molar absorptivity form:**
With $\alpha = 2.303 \, \varepsilon \, c$:
$$\boxed{z_{\max} = \frac{1}{2.303 \, \varepsilon \, c} \log_{10}\left(\frac{I_0}{I_{\text{th}}}\right)} \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$

**Step 5 — Numerical computation:**
Given: $\varepsilon = 8000 \; \text{L mol}^{-1} \text{cm}^{-1}$, $c = 0.04 \; \text{mol/L}$, $I_0 = 250$, $I_{\text{th}} = 20$.

- $\alpha = 2.303 \times 8000 \times 0.04 = 736.96 \; \text{cm}^{-1}$
- $\log_{10}(250/20) = \log_{10}(12.5) = 1.0969$
- $z_{\max} = 1.0969 / 736.96 = 1.488 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{cm} = \mathbf{14.88 \; \mu m}$ *(2 marks — 1 mark for substituting, 1 mark for final answer)*

**Step 6 — Comment (1 mark):**
The black UV flexo ink only cures through **~15 µm** of the film thickness. If the wet ink laydown is 20–30 µm, **through-cure failure** will occur — leading to soft, tacky, poorly resistant films. **Solution:** Use **surface-treated carbon black** with enhanced photoinitiator loading, **multiple PI packages** (Type I + Type II), or switch to **EB curing** for the black inks.

*Total part (b): 7 marks.*

---

> **Question B (14 marks, Alternative Choice):** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
> **(a)** Discuss in detail the **components of a radiation-curable ink** — oligomers, monomers, photoinitiators, and additives — with suitable examples. Compare the properties of epoxy acrylate, polyurethane acrylate, and polyester acrylate oligomers. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** What is **oxygen inhibition** in free-radical UV curing? Explain four practical methods to overcome it. Calculate the **UV dose** for a press running with a 200 W lamp at 5 cm focal length and a substrate speed of 80 cm/s. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — Ink Components (7 marks)

**Oligomers (3 marks — write as a comparison table):**

| Property | Epoxy Acrylate | Polyurethane Acrylate | Polyester Acrylate |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cure speed | **Very fast** | Moderate | Moderate |
| Viscosity | Moderate–high | **High** | **Low** |
| Flexibility | Brittle | **Excellent** | Good |
| Abrasion resistance | Excellent | Excellent | Moderate |
| Chemical resistance | **Excellent** | Good | Moderate |
| Pigment wetting | Moderate | Moderate | **Excellent** |
| Cost | Low | **High** | Moderate |
| Typical use | Hard coatings, overprint varnishes | Flexible films, laminating inks | Litho/offset color inks |

**Monomers (1.5 marks):** Function as reactive diluents to control viscosity and participate in crosslinking. Examples: HDDA (difunctional, low shrinkage), TMPTA (trifunctional, high crosslink density), IBOA (monofunctional, low shrinkage, good adhesion).

**Photoinitiators (1.5 marks):** Benzophenone + amine (Type II, low cost); Irgacure 184, TPO, BAPO (Type I, surface + through-cure). For food-contact: use **BAPO** (low extractables) or **TPO-L** (liquid, easy blending).

**Additives (1 mark):** Slip agents (silicone acrylates), defoamers, wetting agents, HALS stabilizers (prevent post-cure yellowing), UV absorbers (Benzotriazoles), and sensitizers (thioxanthones for pigmented systems).

#### Part (b) — Oxygen Inhibition & UV Dose Calculation (7 marks)

**Oxygen inhibition (3 marks):**
Atmospheric O₂ is a diradical (triplet ground state) that rapidly quenches the excited PI and adds to growing chain radicals:

$$\text{R}^{\bullet} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{ROO}^{\bullet} \quad \text{(stable peroxy radical, non-propagating)}$$

This results in **tacky, under-cured surface layers** — a major issue in air-cured UV inks.

**Four practical methods to overcome oxygen inhibition (3 marks):**

1. **Increase PI loading** (typically 1.5–2× the optimized level) to consume the dissolved O₂.
2. **Use Type II PIs** with amine synergists — the amine is preferentially oxidized by O₂, sparing the chain radical.
3. **N₂ inertion blanket** over the substrate before the lamp — reduces O₂ concentration to < 50 ppm.
4. **Wax/slip additives** that migrate to the surface, forming a barrier to O₂.
5. *(Bonus)* Use **lamps with higher peak intensity** so the cure rate exceeds the O₂ inhibition rate.

**UV Dose calculation (1 mark):**

$$D = \frac{P_0}{F \cdot v} = \frac{200 \; \text{W}}{5 \; \text{cm} \times 80 \; \text{cm/s}} = \frac{200}{400} = 0.5 \; \frac{\text{J}}{\text{cm}^2} = \mathbf{500 \; mJ/cm^2}$$

This dose is sufficient for **UV offset and screen inks** (typical requirement: 250–600 mJ/cm²). ✓

*Total: 7 marks (1+1+1 = 3 marks for explanation, 1 mark each for 4 methods (best 3 of 4 counted) = 3 marks, 1 mark for dose calc.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing Type I and Type II PIs:** Type I undergoes unimolecular α-cleavage; Type II requires a co-initiator (amine) for H-abstraction. Examiners frequently test this distinction.
> 2. **Mixing units in the dose calculation:** Always use $P_0$ in **watts**, $F$ in **cm**, $v$ in **cm/s** to get $D$ in J/cm². Converting to mJ/cm² requires a factor of 1000.
> 3. **Forgetting the $2.303$ factor in Beer-Lambert:** This is the natural-to-decadic logarithm conversion. Writing $z_{\max} = \frac{1}{\varepsilon c} \ln(\cdots)$ instead of $\frac{1}{2.303 \,\varepsilon c} \log_{10}(\cdots)$ will lose 1 mark.
> 4. **Stating that EB inks also need photoinitiators:** This is a classic KTU trap question. EB inks are uniquely photoinitiator-free.
> 5. **Confusing "through-cure" and "surface cure":** These are not the same. Surface cure = gelation; through-cure = full network formation throughout the film depth.
> 6. **Ignoring migration in food-contact UV inks:** Always mention EU Regulation 1935/2004 and the **"Nestlé guidance on packaging inks"** when discussing curing of inks for food packaging.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Radiation Curable Systems — One-Page Revision Guide for KTU 2024**

- **Definition:** Inks cured by UV (100–400 nm) or EB (80–300 keV) via **free-radical or cationic polymerization**.
- **No photoinitiator in EB** — high-energy electrons directly ionize the resin.
- **Free-radical cure is faster but oxygen-inhibited**; cationic cure is slower but oxygen-insensitive.
- **Formulation:** Oligomer (30–60%) + Monomer (10–40%) + Photoinitiator (1–10%) + Additives (1–5%).
- **Epoxy acrylate** = fast, hard, brittle; **PUA** = flexible, expensive; **Polyester acrylate** = low viscosity, good pigment wetting.
- **TMPTA and PETIA** are trifunctional monomers — they boost crosslink density and hardness.
- **Type I PI** = unimolecular cleavage (Irgacure 184, TPO, BAPO). **Type II PI** = bimolecular H-abstraction (Benzophenone + MDEA).
- **Oxygen inhibition** can be defeated by: ↑ PI loading, Type II PIs with amines, N₂ inertion, wax barriers.
- **Key equation:** $D = I \times t = \dfrac{P_0}{F \cdot v}$ — independent of lamp arc length!
- **Penetration depth:** $z_{\max} = \dfrac{1}{2.303 \,\varepsilon \, c} \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{I_0}{I_{\text{th}}}\right)$ — black pigments limit cure depth.
- **Degree of conversion** never reaches 100% in free-radical systems — typically 70–95%. Unreacted C=C is the source of odor and migration.
- **Cationic PIs** (onium salts) — used with epoxies and vinyl ethers — give **living polymerization** with extended post-cure.
- **Carbon black is a strong UV absorber** — always use surface-treated grades for UV inks.
- **Phthalocyanine blue and yellow 83** are UV-transparent — preferred for UV ink pigments.
- **LED-UV (365/385/395 nm)** is replacing Hg lamps — narrower emission, lower heat, instant on/off, no mercury.
- **Migration & food safety:** EB > cationic UV > free-radical UV, in order of increasing extractables.
- **BAPO (phenylbis(2,4,6-trimethylbenzoyl)phosphine oxide)** is the gold standard for low-migration UV food packaging.
- **For KTU numerical problems:** Always include units, state the formula before substituting, and round to 2–3 significant figures.
- **High-yield exam keywords to memorize:** "α-cleavage," "H-abstraction," "Beer-Lambert," "quantum yield," "oxygen inhibition," "post-cure," "degree of conversion," "Norrish Type I," "onium salt," "living polymerization."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Processes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT866 - TECHNOLOGY OF PRINTING INKS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Processes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Printing Ink Manufacturing Processes

In the context of **Technology of Printing Inks (PEPOT866)**, *Processes* refers to the complete sequence of **unit operations** employed to transform raw materials—pigments, resins, solvents, and additives—into a homogeneous, stable, and rheologically optimized printing ink. The KTU 2024 Scheme defines this as the *engineered conversion pathway* where solid pigment aggregates are progressively broken down, wetted, dispersed within a vehicle, and finished to meet strict printability specifications.

The principal unit operations in ink manufacturing are:

- **Pre-mixing (Varnish Preparation / Pigment Wetting)**
- **Milling / Dispersion (Size Reduction)**
- **Let-down (Adjustment)**
- **Filtration & Straining**
- **Quality Control & Standardization**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The 2024 Scheme emphasizes the *cause–effect relationship* between process parameters (shear rate, residence time, temperature) and end-product properties (fineness of grind, viscosity, color strength). Students must be able to map *process → property → printability*.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine making a perfect cup of filter coffee in a South Indian kitchen. You don't just dump coffee powder into hot water and stir once. Instead, you:

1. **Roast and grind** the beans to a fine powder (analogous to pigment milling).
2. **Boil water with decoction** prepared through slow extraction (analogous to varnish pre-mixing).
3. **Filter** to remove coarse particles (analogous to straining).
4. **Add milk and sugar** to taste and consistency (analogous to let-down).

Similarly, ink making is **not just mixing**—it is a controlled, multi-step engineered process where each stage progressively refines the product. The *paints* and *inks* industries share 80% of their manufacturing DNA: **dispersion is the heart**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** In *paint* making, the final film is relatively thick (>25 µm), so some flocculation is tolerable. In *ink* making, the printed film is extremely thin (1–5 µm), so the dispersion must be **near-colloidal** in fineness. This is why ink mills operate at far higher shear than paint mills.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics in Ink Processing

The following metrics are universally cited in KTU reference material and ISO 2846 standards:

- **Fineness of Grind (FOG):** Measured in **µm**, typically **≤ 5 µm** for offset inks and **≤ 2 µm** for gravure/flexo inks.
- **Viscosity:** Expressed in **poise (P)** or **pascal-seconds (Pa·s)**; 1 P = 0.1 Pa·s.
- **Tack:** Measured on an **Inkometer** at 90 °C, 400 rpm, reported in tack units.
- **Yield Value:** Threshold stress (dyn/cm²) below which the ink behaves as a solid.
- **Pigment Volume Concentration (PVC):** Critical ratio governing gloss and flexibility.
- **Dispersion Energy:** Expressed in **kWh per ton of ink** (typical range: **50–250 kWh/ton**).

> [!NOTE]
> These bolded metrics form the **terminology backbone** of KTU 2024 Scheme Part A questions. Memorize the units precisely.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Stage 1 — Varnish Preparation (Pre-mix)

The **varnish** (or *vehicle*) is the binder–solvent phase that will later suspend the pigment. Pre-mixing involves:

- Heating the resin to its **softening point** (typically **120–180 °C** for hydrocarbon resins).
- Dissolving it in the solvent under controlled agitation.
- Adding **wetting agents** (fatty acids, lecithin, or surface-active dispersants) to displace air/moisture from the pigment surface.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why pre-mix?** A pigment arriving from the manufacturer has *air voids* and *agglomerates*. Direct milling of dry pigment with resin leads to poor dispersion because air pockets prevent resin–pigment contact. The pre-mix stage creates an *ink base* or *mill base*—a high-viscosity paste that traps no air.

### 2.1.1 Critical Pre-mix Parameters

| Parameter | Typical Range | Effect on Process |
|---|---|---|
| Varnish viscosity at mixing | **5–50 poise** | Too low → poor wetting; too high → cavitation |
| Mixing temperature | **40–80 °C** | Reduces resin viscosity, aids air release |
| Mixing time | **15–45 min** | Insufficient → entrapped air; excessive → pre-gelation |
| Wetting agent dosage | **0.5–3 % by weight** | Improves pigment–vehicle interfacial adhesion |

## 2.2 Stage 2 — Milling / Dispersion (Size Reduction)

This is the **heart of ink manufacturing**. The mill base is subjected to intense **shear forces** that break pigment agglomerates into primary particles (typically 0.1–10 µm) and distribute them uniformly.

### 2.2.1 The Three Sub-mechanisms of Dispersion

1. **Wetting** — Replacement of pigment surface air/water by vehicle.
2. **Mechanical De-agglomeration** — Breaking down clusters via shear.
3. **Stabilization** — Preventing re-flocculation through steric or charge repulsion.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Stoke's Law** governs sedimentation stability post-dispersion:
>
> $$v = \frac{2 r^{2} \, (\rho_{p} - \rho_{v}) \, g}{9 \eta}$$
>
> where $v$ = settling velocity, $r$ = particle radius, $\rho_{p}$ = pigment density, $\rho_{v}$ = vehicle density, $g$ = gravitational acceleration, $\eta$ = vehicle viscosity. To minimize settling, $r$ must be small and $\eta$ must be high during storage.

### 2.2.2 Types of Mills Used in Ink Manufacturing

The KTU 2024 syllabus gives high weightage to identifying mill types. The major categories are:

**A) Three-Roll Mill (Triple Roller Mill)**
- Used for **paste inks** (offset, letterpress, news inks).
- Operates on **compression + shear** between three corotating rolls.
- Roll speeds: **n₁ : n₂ : n₃ = 1 : 3 : 9** (typical).
- Achieves FOG of **≤ 5 µm**.
- Disadvantages: Batch process, high operator skill, slow.

**B) Bead Mill (Sand Mill / Attritor)**
- Used for **liquid inks** (gravure, flexo).
- Grinding media: **zirconium silicate or glass beads** of **0.6–2.5 mm** diameter.
- Operates on **high-speed impingement + shear**.
- Achieves FOG of **≤ 2 µm**.
- Continuous process, easy to scale.

**C) Ball Mill**
- Used historically; still used for **news ink** and **carbon black dispersion**.
- Tumbling action; low energy density.
- Long residence time (8–24 hours).
- Now largely replaced by bead mills for quality reasons.

**D) High-Speed Disperser (HSD) / Cowles Blade**
- Pre-mix equipment, not a true mill.
- Used **before** bead/ball milling to make a coarse mill base.

**E) Horizontal vs Vertical Bead Mill**
- Horizontal: better media fill, higher energy input.
- Vertical: easier to clean, lower residence time.

## 2.3 Stage 3 — Let-down

The mill base is a *concentrate* (typically **30–50 % pigment**). It is diluted with additional varnish, solvent, and additives to reach final specifications:

- Final pigment loading: **5–25 %** (depending on ink type).
- Final viscosity: **0.5–50 poise** (ink-type dependent).
- Additives added: driers, antioxidants, anti-set-off compounds, waxes.

## 2.4 Stage 4 — Filtration & Quality Control

- **Filtration** is done through **perlon cloth, nylon mesh, or sintered metal filters** (typically 10–25 µm pore size for offset; 5–10 µm for liquid inks).
- **FOG test:** Hegman gauge reading (0–8 scale, where 8 = 0 µm, 0 = 100 µm).
- **Viscosity** checked on Brookfield/Cup viscometers.
- **Tack** measured on Inkometer.
- **Color strength** via drawdown against a standard.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are KTU examiner favorites. Memorize units.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $v = \frac{2 r^{2} \Delta\rho g}{9 \eta}$ | Stoke's settling velocity | m/s |
| 2 | $\dot{\gamma} = \frac{\pi D N}{60 \, h}$ | Shear rate in roll mill (N = rpm, D = roll dia, h = gap) | s⁻¹ |
| 3 | $N_{1} : N_{2} : N_{3} = 1 : 3 : 9$ | Three-roll mill speed ratio | — |
| 4 | $PVC = \frac{V_{p}}{V_{p} + V_{b}} \times 100$ | Pigment Volume Concentration | % |
| 5 | $\tau = \eta \cdot \dot{\gamma}$ | Newtonian viscosity definition | Pa |
| 6 | $E_{d} = P \cdot t / m$ | Specific dispersion energy (P = power, t = time, m = mass) | kWh/ton |
| 7 | $\eta_{rel} = \eta / \eta_{0}$ | Relative viscosity | dimensionless |
| 8 | $D_{s} = \frac{6}{\rho_{p} \cdot A}$ | Sauter mean diameter from surface area A | m |

> [!WARNING]
> In KTU answer sheets, examiners **deduct marks** if units are missing or wrong. Always state: $v$ in m/s, $r$ in m, $\eta$ in Pa·s.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Understanding these processes is critical for:

- **Print quality troubleshooting:** Press problems (hickeys, piling, misting) are usually traced back to a *processing defect* (poor wetting, FOG too high, wrong viscosity).
- **Cost optimization:** Bead mills use 5× more energy than HSDs but produce 10× better dispersion—deciding the mill type is an economic trade-off.
- **Scale-up:** Lab bead mills (1 L) do not scale linearly to production (1000 L); geometric similarity, $P/V$ ratio, and residence time distribution must be matched.
- **Sustainability:** Modern processes minimize VOC emissions and use water-based vehicles, demanding *newer* mills (e.g., stirred media detritors) capable of handling water.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Shear Rate in a Three-Roll Mill

The KTU 2024 board regularly asks students to derive the shear rate expression for a three-roll mill nip.

### 3.1.1 Setup

Consider two adjacent rolls of radii $R_{1}$ and $R_{2}$ rotating at angular velocities $\omega_{1}$ and $\omega_{2}$ respectively, with the gap between them being $h$ (typically 5–25 µm for ink milling). The mill base enters the nip and exits with the surfaces of both rolls.

### 3.1.2 Geometric Derivation

The peripheral velocity of roll 1 is:

$$V_{1} = \omega_{1} R_{1} = \frac{2 \pi N_{1} R_{1}}{60}$$

Similarly, for roll 2:

$$V_{2} = \frac{2 \pi N_{2} R_{2}}{60}$$

The relative velocity difference across the gap is:

$$\Delta V = V_{2} - V_{1} = \frac{2 \pi}{60}(N_{2} R_{2} - N_{1} R_{1})$$

For equal-radius rolls ($R_{1} = R_{2} = R$):

$$\Delta V = \frac{2 \pi R}{60}(N_{2} - N_{1})$$

The **average gap** $h$ is the distance between the rolls at the nip. Hence, the **average shear rate** in the nip region is:

$$\dot{\gamma} = \frac{\Delta V}{h} = \frac{2 \pi R (N_{2} - N_{1})}{60 \, h}$$

### 3.1.3 Conversion to Standard Form

By defining the average roll speed $N = (N_{1} + N_{2})/2$, and using $N_{2} - N_{1} \approx N$ for large speed ratios (as in a three-roll mill, where the front roll is slowest and the apron roll is fastest), we obtain the KTU reference expression:

$$\dot{\gamma} = \frac{\pi D N}{60 \, h}$$

where $D = 2R$ is the roll diameter and $N$ is the speed of the faster roll.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** A typical offset ink mill has $D = 0.4$ m, $N = 200$ rpm, and $h = 10$ µm. Plugging in:
>
> $$\dot{\gamma} = \frac{\pi \times 0.4 \times 200}{60 \times 10 \times 10^{-6}} = 4.19 \times 10^{5} \text{ s}^{-1}$$
>
> This **ultra-high shear** is what breaks down pigment agglomerates. For comparison, a kitchen mixer produces only 10²–10³ s⁻¹.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Critical Pigment Volume Concentration (CPVC)

CPVC is the PVC at which the binder just fills all inter-particle voids. It is a **fundamental KTU concept**.

### 3.2.1 Starting Point

Let $V_{p}$ = pigment volume, $V_{b}$ = binder volume, and let the pigment's packing fraction (volume of solids per unit volume of powder) be $\phi_{p}$. The void volume in a dry pigment pack of volume $V_{p}/\phi_{p}$ is:

$$V_{void} = \frac{V_{p}}{\phi_{p}} - V_{p} = V_{p} \left( \frac{1 - \phi_{p}}{\phi_{p}} \right)$$

At CPVC, the binder **just fills** these voids, so $V_{b} = V_{void}$:

$$V_{b} = V_{p} \left( \frac{1 - \phi_{p}}{\phi_{p}} \right)$$

### 3.2.2 Solving for CPVC

By definition, $PVC = \frac{V_{p}}{V_{p} + V_{b}}$. Substituting $V_{b}$:

$$CPVC = \frac{V_{p}}{V_{p} + V_{p} \left(\frac{1 - \phi_{p}}{\phi_{p}}\right)} = \frac{1}{1 + \frac{1 - \phi_{p}}{\phi_{p}}}$$

After algebraic simplification (multiply numerator and denominator by $\phi_{p}$):

$$CPVC = \frac{\phi_{p}}{1} = \phi_{p}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key result:** $CPVC$ is numerically equal to the pigment's dry-packing fraction. For spherical pigments in random close packing, $\phi_{p} \approx 0.64$. For irregular ink pigments, $\phi_{p}$ typically ranges from **0.30 to 0.55**.

### 3.2.3 Engineering Significance

| Condition | Result |
|---|---|
| $PVC < CPVC$ | Binder-rich → glossy, flexible, good adhesion |
| $PVC = CPVC$ | Critical point — sharp property transitions |
| $PVC > CPVC$ | Binder-starved → matte, porous, poor barrier |

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Specific Energy of Dispersion

The energy required to disperse a pigment is computed per unit mass of ink:

$$E_{d} = \frac{P \cdot t}{m}$$

where $P$ = power consumed by the mill (kW), $t$ = residence time (h), $m$ = mass of ink processed (tons).

For a **bead mill** of volume $V$ (L) operating at power density $P/V$ (kW/L) and processing flow rate $Q$ (L/h):

$$t = \frac{V}{Q}$$

Hence:

$$E_{d} = \frac{(P/V) \cdot V^{2}}{Q \cdot m} = \frac{(P/V) \cdot V}{Q \cdot \rho_{ink}}$$

This shows that **higher power density and longer residence time both increase $E_{d}$** linearly, but both also improve dispersion quality.

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Mill Selection

| Ink Type | Viscosity | Required FOG | Recommended Mill | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| News ink | Low–Med | ≤ 10 µm | Ball mill / Bead mill | High volume, low cost, tolerant |
| Offset ink | High | ≤ 5 µm | Three-roll mill | Paste viscosity, high shear |
| Gravure ink | Very low | ≤ 2 µm | Bead mill | Liquid, fine dispersion |
| Flexo ink | Low | ≤ 2 µm | Bead mill | Water/solvent based |
| UV ink | Med | ≤ 5 µm | Three-roll or bead | High viscosity, monomer-rich |
| Inkjet ink | Ultra-low | ≤ 0.5 µm | Bead + filtration | Nano-dispersion, low viscosity |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Flow Diagram: Complete Ink Manufacturing Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Material Receiving] --> B[Quality Inspection of Pigments and Resins]
    B --> C[Varnish Preparation in Reactor]
    C --> D[Pre-mix with Pigment in High Speed Disperser]
    D --> E[Mill Base Formed Coarse Dispersion]
    E --> F{Mill Selection}
    F -->|Paste Ink| G[Three Roll Mill]
    F -->|Liquid Ink| H[Bead Mill]
    F -->|Low Cost High Volume| I[Ball Mill]
    G --> J[Fine Mill Base FOG below 5 micron]
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K[Let down with Varnish Solvents Additives]
    K --> L[Filtration through Mesh]
    L --> M[Final Quality Control]
    M --> N{Standards Met}
    N -->|Yes| O[Packing and Dispatch]
    N -->|No| P[Rework or Reject]
    P --> D
```

## 4.2 Three-Roll Mill Internal Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TM[Three Roll Mill]
        R1[Feed Roll Slow Speed N1] --> R2[Center Roll Medium Speed N2]
        R2 --> R3[Apron Roll Fastest Speed N3]
    end
    A[Hopper Feeds Mill Base] --> R1
    R1 -->|Shear at Gap 1| R2
    R2 -->|Shear at Gap 2| R3
    R3 -->|Scraper Blade| P[Finished Ink Collector]
```

## 4.3 Bead Mill Cross-Section

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph BM[Bead Mill Chamber]
        I[Inlet for Mill Base] --> CH[Grinding Chamber Filled with Zirconia Beads]
        CH --> IM[High Speed Impeller Shaft]
        IM --> M[Motor Drive 50 to 500 rpm Variable]
        CH --> SC[Separating Screen at Outlet]
        SC --> O[Outlet for Dispersed Ink]
    end
```

## 4.4 Process Parameter Influence Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Shear Rate] --> Q1[Fineness of Grind]
    P2[Residence Time] --> Q1
    P3[Temperature] --> Q1
    P4[Wetting Agent Type] --> Q2[Color Strength]
    P5[Mill Type] --> Q2
    P1 --> Q3[Viscosity Stability]
    P2 --> Q3
    P3 --> Q4[Gloss]
    P1 --> Q4
    Q1 --> R1[Print Quality: Sharpness]
    Q2 --> R2[Print Quality: Vibrancy]
    Q3 --> R3[Press Performance: Misting Piling]
    Q4 --> R4[Print Quality: Reflectance]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading tip:** When the examiner asks *"How does a process parameter affect print quality?"* start your answer with this influence map. It guarantees a **structured 7-mark response**.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**
**Define "fineness of grind" (FOG) and state its typical range for (a) offset ink and (b) gravure ink. Name the instrument used to measure it.**

**Model Answer:**
Fineness of grind (FOG) is the maximum size of the largest pigment particle or agglomerate present in a finished ink, expressed in micrometres (µm). It is a direct indicator of dispersion quality.

- For **offset ink:** FOG ≤ **5 µm** (typically 0–10 µm).
- For **gravure ink:** FOG ≤ **2 µm** (typically 0–5 µm).

The instrument used is the **Hegman Gauge** (also called a grindometer), which is a stainless-steel block with a calibrated tapered groove. The ink is placed at the deep end and scraped toward the shallow end with a straight-edged scraper. The point at which a continuous streak of particles becomes visible is read off the scale (0 = 100 µm, 8 = 0 µm).

*(Examiner awards 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for ranges, 1 mark for instrument.)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
**What is the function of a wetting agent in ink manufacturing? Give two examples.**

**Model Answer:**
A **wetting agent** is a surface-active additive that reduces the interfacial tension between the pigment surface and the vehicle, thereby displacing air and moisture from pigment agglomerates and allowing the vehicle to penetrate and wet each particle uniformly. This is essential in the **pre-mix stage** to avoid entrapped air, which would otherwise cause *hickeys* and *piling* on the press.

**Two examples:**
1. **Fatty acids** (e.g., oleic acid, stearic acid) — used in oil-based offset inks.
2. **Lecithin** (a phospholipid) — used in both oil-based and some water-based inks.
3. *(Acceptable also: surface-active dispersants like Solsperse, Efka, or BYK additives.)*

*(Examiner awards 1 mark for function, 2 marks for examples.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Style]**

**(a)** Describe with a neat sketch the construction and working of a **three-roll mill** used in printing ink manufacturing. Explain why a speed ratio of 1:3:9 is maintained between the rolls. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A three-roll mill has rolls of diameter **0.35 m**, with the fastest roll rotating at **180 rpm** and the gap between the middle and apron roll being **8 µm**. Calculate the average shear rate in the nip. If the mill base has a viscosity of **8 poise**, what is the shear stress developed? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a)

**Construction:**
A three-roll mill consists of three parallel horizontal rolls—**feed roll** (slowest), **center roll** (intermediate), and **apron roll** (fastest)—made of hardened steel or ceramic-coated steel. They are mounted in a heavy cast-iron frame. The rolls are pressed against each other pneumatically or by spring-loaded mechanisms. A hopper feeds the mill base onto the feed roll. A scraper blade is fitted against the apron roll to collect the finished ink.

**Working:**
1. The mill base from the hopper is fed onto the **feed roll**, which rotates at the lowest speed.
2. Adhesion carries the mill base into the **nip** (small gap) between the feed and center rolls, where intense **shear and compression** break down agglomerates.
3. The partially dispersed ink is then carried into the **second nip** between the center and apron rolls, where even higher shear is applied.
4. The fully dispersed ink is finally **scraped off** the apron roll by the doctor blade and collected in a tray.

**Why speed ratio 1:3:9:**
The progressively increasing roll speed:
- Ensures that the ink is **transported forward** (no backflow) at each nip.
- Creates a **velocity gradient** across the nip that generates the shear required to break agglomerates.
- The highest speed at the apron roll allows the thin, finished ink film to be cleanly **scraped off** without dripping back.
- Mathematical requirement: For steady-state transport, the surface velocity of each downstream roll must exceed the upstream roll by a factor sufficient to overcome the back-pressure in the nip.

*(Examiner awards 2 marks for sketch, 3 marks for working, 2 marks for speed-ratio reasoning.)*

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b)

**Given:**
- Roll diameter $D = 0.35$ m
- Speed of fastest roll $N = 180$ rpm
- Gap $h = 8$ µm = $8 \times 10^{-6}$ m
- Viscosity $\eta = 8$ poise = $0.8$ Pa·s (since 1 P = 0.1 Pa·s)

**Step 1 — Shear rate calculation:**

Using the formula:

$$\dot{\gamma} = \frac{\pi D N}{60 \, h}$$

Substituting:

$$\dot{\gamma} = \frac{\pi \times 0.35 \times 180}{60 \times 8 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$\dot{\gamma} = \frac{197.92}{4.8 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$\dot{\gamma} = 4.12 \times 10^{5} \text{ s}^{-1}$$

**Step 2 — Shear stress calculation:**

Using Newton's law of viscosity:

$$\tau = \eta \cdot \dot{\gamma}$$

$$\tau = 0.8 \times 4.12 \times 10^{5}$$

$$\tau = 3.30 \times 10^{5} \text{ Pa} = 330 \text{ kPa}$$

**Final Answer:** $\dot{\gamma} = 4.12 \times 10^{5}$ s⁻¹ and $\tau = 3.30 \times 10^{5}$ Pa.

*(Examiner awards 3 marks for formula, 2 marks for substitution, 2 marks for final numerical answer with units.)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]**

**(a)** Explain the **stages of ink manufacturing** with a flow diagram. Discuss the role of the **pre-mix stage** in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is **Pigment Volume Concentration (PVC)**? A printing ink has pigment volume 12 cm³ and binder volume 28 cm³. Calculate the PVC. If the critical PVC (CPVC) of the pigment is 48 %, determine whether the ink is **glossy or matte** and justify. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a)

**Stages of ink manufacturing (as per the flow diagram in Section 4.1):**

1. **Raw material inspection** — Pigments checked for purity, particle size; resins for softening point; solvents for purity.
2. **Varnish preparation** — Resin dissolved in solvent at elevated temperature with stirring.
3. **Pre-mix** — Pigment slowly added to varnish in a high-speed disperser (HSD) with a Cowles blade. Wetting agents are added to displace air and break down large agglomerates. A coarse mill base is produced.
4. **Milling** — Mill base is passed through a three-roll mill (for pastes) or bead mill (for liquids) for size reduction to FOG ≤ 5 µm.
5. **Let-down** — Mill base is diluted with additional varnish, solvents, and additives (driers, waxes, antioxidants) to reach target viscosity and printing properties.
6. **Filtration** — The finished ink is filtered through 10–25 µm mesh to remove stray coarse particles.
7. **Quality control** — FOG, viscosity, tack, color strength, drying time, and gloss are tested against standards.

**Role of pre-mix stage (in detail):**
- **Wetting:** Wetting agents lower the solid–liquid interfacial tension, allowing the vehicle to penetrate the porous pigment agglomerates and push out air. Without proper wetting, the mill would have to do *all* the de-agglomeration work, increasing energy cost 5–10 fold.
- **De-agglomeration (partial):** The HSD's high tip velocity (15–25 m/s) creates turbulent flow (Reynolds number > 10⁴) that breaks large clusters into smaller ones.
- **Air release:** Vacuum-assisted pre-mixers or slow-stirring under vacuum can be used to remove entrained air.
- **Homogenization:** Ensures uniform pigment loading before milling — critical for consistent color strength batch-to-batch.

*(Examiner awards 3 marks for stages, 4 marks for pre-mix role.)*

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b)

**Definition:**
Pigment Volume Concentration (PVC) is the ratio of the volume of pigment to the total volume of pigment plus binder (non-volatile vehicle) in an ink or coating, expressed as a percentage.

$$PVC = \frac{V_{p}}{V_{p} + V_{b}} \times 100$$

**Calculation:**

Given: $V_{p} = 12$ cm³, $V_{b} = 28$ cm³.

$$PVC = \frac{12}{12 + 28} \times 100 = \frac{12}{40} \times 100 = 30 \text{ \%}$$

**Comparison with CPVC:**
- Calculated PVC = **30 %**
- Given CPVC = **48 %**
- Since **PVC < CPVC** (30 % < 48 %), the binder is in **excess** of what is needed to fill the pigment interstices.

**Conclusion:**
The ink will be **glossy** (and flexible, with good adhesion and a continuous binder film at the surface). The excess binder forms a smooth over-layer that reflects light specularly, producing high gloss. If the PVC were greater than CPVC, the ink would be **matte** and porous, with poor barrier and mechanical properties.

*(Examiner awards 2 marks for definition, 2 marks for calculation, 3 marks for comparison + glossy/matte justification.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in "Processes" Questions:**
> 1. **Missing units in shear-rate/stress problems.** The examiner *will* deduct 0.5–1 mark if you write $4.12 \times 10^5$ without "s⁻¹" or "Pa".
> 2. **Confusing CPVC with PVC.** Many students write "PVC = CPVC means glossy" — this is **wrong**. Gloss requires **PVC < CPVC**.
> 3. **Forgetting to convert poise to Pa·s.** 1 poise = 0.1 Pa·s. Several students lose 1 full mark here.
> 4. **Skipping the sketch in mill questions.** A neat labelled diagram of the three-roll mill carries **2 marks minimum** and is non-negotiable.
> 5. **Naming the wrong instrument.** "Viscometer" is **not** the FOG instrument. The correct answer is **Hegman gauge / grindometer**.
> 6. **In Q.A(b), assuming a Newtonian fluid.** Most mill bases are *shear-thinning*, so using the low-shear viscosity (8 poise) overestimates $\tau$. KTU accepts the Newtonian assumption for full marks, but a sharp student can add a remark: *"In practice, mill base is pseudoplastic, so actual $\tau$ is lower."* This often earns an **extra appreciation mark** from the examiner.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (read this 30 minutes before the exam):**

- ☐ **Five stages of ink manufacturing:** Pre-mix → Milling → Let-down → Filtration → QC.
- ☐ **Three-roll mill** for paste inks (offset, letterpress, news); **bead mill** for liquid inks (gravure, flexo).
- ☐ **Speed ratio** 1:3:9 in three-roll mill — remember this number, it is asked almost every year.
- ☐ **FOG limits:** Offset ≤ 5 µm, Gravure/Flexo ≤ 2 µm, Inkjet ≤ 0.5 µm.
- ☐ **Hegman gauge** is the FOG instrument (NOT a viscometer).
- ☐ **Shear-rate formula:** $\dot{\gamma} = \pi D N / (60 h)$.
- ☐ **Stoke's law:** $v = 2 r^{2} \Delta\rho g / (9 \eta)$ — used to argue dispersion stability.
- ☐ **PVC < CPVC → glossy**, **PVC > CPVC → matte** (this single relation is worth 2–3 marks in many questions).
- ☐ **Wetting agents** displace air from pigments; examples: fatty acids, lecithin.
- ☐ **CPVC ≈ dry-packing fraction** of the pigment; for spheres ~0.64, for irregulars 0.30–0.55.
- ☐ **High-shear rheology:** Mill base in three-roll mill experiences $\dot{\gamma} \sim 10^{5}$ s⁻¹ — orders of magnitude above kitchen-mixer shear.
- ☐ **Filtration** is the last defence against hickeys and press piling.
- ☐ **Always state units** in numerical answers; KTU examiners are strict.
- ☐ **No programming code** is required in this paper — it is a pure chemistry/process engineering subject.
- ☐ **Diagrams fetch easy marks** — always draw a labelled three-roll mill or process flow diagram wherever the question allows.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mixing Equipment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT866 - TECHNOLOGY OF PRINTING INKS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Mixing Equipment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mixing** is a fundamental **unit operation in printing ink manufacturing** that involves the uniform distribution and homogenization of two or more immiscible or partially miscible phases (pigment, vehicle/binder, solvents, additives) into a single, physically and chemically uniform mass, with the application of **mechanical shear, convection, and diffusion forces**.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEPOT866 - Technology of Printing Inks)**, the term **"Mixing Equipment"** refers to the engineered mechanical devices used to achieve this homogenization of the ink formulation. This is distinct from, but often coupled with, **dispersion equipment** (which is concerned primarily with breaking down pigment agglomerates).

### Classification of Mixing Operations (KTU Syllabus Framework)

The KTU syllabus categorizes mixing equipment for printing inks primarily on the basis of:
1. **Intensity of Shear** imparted
2. **Type of Flow Pattern** generated (axial / radial / tangential)
3. **Phase Behaviour** of the system (liquid-liquid, solid-liquid, paste-paste)
4. **Viscosity Range** handled (low / medium / high)

| Mixing Regime | Typical Viscosity (Pa·s) | Equipment Examples |
|:---|:---:|:---|
| **Low Viscosity Agitation** | 0.001 – 1 | Propeller, Paddle, Turbine |
| **Medium Viscosity Mixing** | 1 – 50 | Anchor, Helical, Planetary |
| **High Viscosity / Paste Mixing** | 50 – 1000+ | Sigma Blade, Z-Blade, Double Planetary |
| **High Shear / Dispersing Mixers** | 0.1 – 100 | Cowles Dissolver, Sawtooth Impeller, Rotor-Stator |

> [!NOTE]
> **Distinction Key for Examiners:** *Mixing* aims at **homogenization** (uniformity of composition), while *Dispersion* aims at **de-agglomeration** (breaking of primary particles). However, in practice, high-shear mixers perform both functions simultaneously and are often classified together as "mixing equipment" in ink plants.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Real-World Intuition

**The "Chai Masala Analogy" – How Mixing Works in Everyday Life**

Imagine you are preparing a cup of **masala chai**. You have:
- **Water** (continuous phase / vehicle)
- **Milk** (second liquid phase / co-solvent)
- **Tea powder** (fine solid / pigment)
- **Sugar** (dissolving solid / additive)
- **Ginger + Cardamom** (coarse additives / extenders)

If you simply leave them all in a cup, the tea powder floats, ginger sinks, and sugar forms clumps. You, being the "engineer," pick up a **spoon** and stir it.

- The **spoon** is the **impeller**.
- The **stirring action** imparts **velocity gradients** (shear).
- The resulting **vortex** creates **convective currents** that pull dense particles down and light particles up.
- Over time, **molecular diffusion** finishes the last bit of uniformity.

In printing ink manufacturing, the same principle is scaled up — but instead of a teaspoon, we use multi-kilowatt motors turning impellers at speeds from **30 RPM** (gentle folding of high-viscosity varnishes) to **6000 RPM** (high-shear pigment wetting). The **goal is identical**: every drop of the final ink must have the **exact same color strength, viscosity, and density** as any other drop.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Standard atmospheric pressure:** **101.325 kPa**
- **Density of typical ink vehicle (linseed oil-based):** **≈ 930 kg/m³**
- **Standard gravitational acceleration:** **g = 9.81 m/s²**
- **Typical power density for high-speed dispersers:** **3 – 6 kW per 1000 L**
- **Industrial mixing time benchmark:** **15 – 45 minutes per batch**

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner's Tip:** Always quote viscosity in **Pa·s** (SI) or **cP** (1 cP = 10⁻³ Pa·s). Many students lose marks by writing "viscosity = 10" without units.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework: The Science Behind Mixing

Mixing is governed by three simultaneous transport mechanisms. Understanding these is essential for selecting the right equipment:

### A. **Convective Bulk Transport (Macromixing)**
- Movement of large pockets of material from one location to another due to the impeller's induced flow.
- Generated primarily by **axial-flow impellers** (propellers, pitched-blade turbines) and **radial-flow impellers** (flat-blade disc turbines).
- This is the "macro" level — it brings unmixed regions into contact.

### B. **Turbulent Eddy Diffusion (Mesomixing)**
- Inertial forces in turbulent flow create **eddies** (mini whirlpools) of size comparable to the impeller.
- These eddies stretch and fold the fluid, dramatically accelerating mixing at intermediate scales.
- Governed by the **Reynolds Number** regime: Re > 10⁴ is fully turbulent.

### C. **Molecular Diffusion (Micromixing)**
- At the smallest scale, random Brownian motion equalizes concentration gradients.
- This is the slowest process and is usually the **rate-limiting step** in liquid-liquid mixing.

## 2.2 Selection Logic: Step-by-Step Equipment Choice

The KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to demonstrate a **decision-tree approach** to selecting mixing equipment. The operational logic is:

1. **Determine the viscosity of the system.**
   - < 1 Pa·s → Low-viscosity propeller / turbine mixer
   - 1 – 50 Pa·s → Anchor / helical ribbon / planetary
   - > 50 Pa·s → Sigma / Z-blade / double-arm kneader

2. **Determine the volume / batch size.**
   - Pilot scale (< 100 L) → Laboratory high-speed disperser
   - Production scale (100 – 10,000 L) → Industrial dissolver + bead mill combination
   - Bulk scale (> 10,000 L) → Multi-impeller in-tank mixers

3. **Determine whether the system is shear-sensitive.**
   - Yes (e.g., polymer-bound pigments) → Low-shear planetary
   - No (e.g., inorganic pigments) → High-shear Cowles or rotor-stator

4. **Determine the mixing objective.**
   - Simple blending → Paddle / propeller
   - Heat transfer during mixing → Anchor / helical with scrapers
   - Wetting of pigment powder → High-speed sawtooth / Cowles blade
   - De-agglomeration of pigment → Bead mill (technically "dispersion," but considered under mixing in KTU Module 3)

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table summarizes **every high-yield formula** for Mixing Equipment that the KTU 2024 examiner can ask in either University (ESE) or Internal (ISA) assessments.

| # | Formula / Relation | Description | Units / Notes |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | $N_p = \dfrac{P}{\rho \cdot N^{3} \cdot D^{5}}$ | **Power Number** (Newton's Number); relates power draw to impeller geometry and operating conditions | Dimensionless |
