| 2 | $Re = \dfrac{\rho \cdot N \cdot D^{2}}{\mu}$ | **Impeller Reynolds Number**; classifies flow regime (laminar / transitional / turbulent) | Dimensionless |
| 3 | $Fr = \dfrac{N^{2} \cdot D}{g}$ | **Froude Number**; ratio of inertial to gravitational forces; relevant when vortex forms | Dimensionless |
| 4 | $P = N_p \cdot \rho \cdot N^{3} \cdot D^{5}$ | **Power Consumption** of an impeller; rearranged form of (1) | W (watts) |
| 5 | $Q = N_p \cdot N \cdot D^{3}$ | **Pumping Capacity** (flow number correlation) | m³/s |
| 6 | $V_t = \pi \cdot D \cdot N$ | **Impeller Tip Speed**; critical for high-shear applications | m/s |
| 7 | $t_m = \dfrac{V}{Q}$ | **Mixing Time** (residence time approximation) | s |
| 8 | $N_p \cdot Re = f(\text{geometry})$ | **Power correlation** in laminar regime (for $Re < 10$); e.g., $N_p \cdot Re = 65$ for paddle | Dimensionless |
| 9 | $P \propto N^{3} \cdot D^{5}$ | **Power Law Scaling** — doubling $N$ increases $P$ by 8× | — |
| 10 | $\eta_{m} = 1 - \sqrt{\dfrac{\sigma_{sample}^{2}}{\sigma_{0}^{2}}}$ | **Mixing Index / Homogeneity**; statistical measure of uniformity | Dimensionless (0 to 1) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Note on Pumping Capacity (Eq. 5):** $N_p$ in the pumping formula actually refers to the **Flow Number** $N_q$ (sometimes $N_p$ in alternate notations), which is a different dimensionless group. KTU examiners may use the same symbol — verify from context whether the value is ≈ 0.5 – 1.5 (typical for $N_q$) or 5 – 6 (typical for $N_p$).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The principles learned in mixing equipment theory have direct application across industries:

- **Printing Ink Industry:** Formulating offset, gravure, flexo, and screen inks
- **Paint & Coatings:** Architectural and industrial paint manufacture
- **Pharmaceuticals:** Mixing of syrups, ointments, suspensions
- **Cosmetics:** Cream and lotion homogenization
- **Food Processing:** Sauces, beverages, dairy products
- **Adhesives & Sealants:** Hot-melt adhesives, silicone sealants

A poorly chosen mixer leads to:
- **Color strength variation** (un-mixed pigment clusters)
- **Settling of heavy pigments** during storage
- **Air entrainment and foaming**
- **Energy wastage** (oversized motor)
- **Excessive shear degradation** of polymeric binders

## 2.5 The Reynolds Number Regimes in Mixing

| Regime | Re Range | Flow Character | Power Correlation |
|:---:|:---:|:---|:---|
| **Laminar** | $Re < 10$ | Viscous, no vortex | $N_p \propto 1/Re$ |
| **Transitional** | $10 < Re < 10,000$ | Vortex appears | Empirical curve fit |
| **Turbulent** | $Re > 10,000$ | Full vortex, dominant inertia | $N_p$ = constant (≈ 5 – 6) |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Power Number Correlation

> [!NOTE]
> The following derivation demonstrates how the **Power Number $N_p$** is obtained from dimensional analysis, a frequently asked 14-mark analytical question in KTU PEPOT866 examinations.

### Step 1: Identify All Relevant Variables

The power $P$ consumed by an impeller rotating in a fluid depends on:

- Impeller diameter: $D$ (m)
- Rotational speed: $N$ (rev/s or rad/s)
- Fluid density: $\rho$ (kg/m³)
- Fluid viscosity: $\mu$ (Pa·s)
- Gravitational acceleration: $g$ (m/s²) — only relevant if vortex forms

**Functional form:**

$$
P = f(D, N, \rho, \mu, g)
$$

### Step 2: Count Variables and Fundamental Dimensions

- Number of variables: $n = 6$
- Fundamental dimensions: $r = 3$ (M, L, T)
- Number of $\pi$-groups: $n - r = 6 - 3 = 3$

By Buckingham $\pi$-theorem, the relationship reduces to **three dimensionless groups**.

### Step 3: Choose Repeating Variables

Choose $D$, $N$, $\rho$ as the repeating variables (they collectively cover M, L, T).

**Check coverage:**
- $D$ → L
- $N$ → T⁻¹
- $\rho$ → M L⁻³

✓ All three dimensions covered.

### Step 4: Form the $\pi$-Groups

**$\pi_1$ — Power Number:**

$$
\pi_1 = \dfrac{P}{D^{a} \cdot N^{b} \cdot \rho^{c}}
$$

Apply dimensional homogeneity:

- $P$ has units: M L² T⁻³
- $D^{a}$ → L^a
- $N^{b}$ → T^(-b)
- $\rho^{c}$ → M^c L^(-3c)

Equating exponents:

- **M:** $1 = c \Rightarrow c = 1$
- **T:** $-3 = -b \Rightarrow b = 3$
- **L:** $2 = a - 3c = a - 3 \Rightarrow a = 5$

Therefore:

$$
\pi_1 = \dfrac{P}{\rho \cdot N^{3} \cdot D^{5}} \equiv N_p
$$

This is the **Power Number** $N_p$.

**$\pi_2$ — Reynolds Number:**

$$
\pi_2 = \dfrac{\mu}{D^{a} \cdot N^{b} \cdot \rho^{c}}
$$

Equating:

- **M:** $1 = c \Rightarrow c = 1$
- **T:** $-1 = -b \Rightarrow b = 1$
- **L:** $-1 = a - 3 \Rightarrow a = 2$

$$
\pi_2 = \dfrac{\mu}{\rho \cdot N \cdot D^{2}} \equiv \dfrac{1}{Re}
$$

**$\pi_3$ — Froude Number:**

$$
\pi_3 = \dfrac{g}{D^{a} \cdot N^{b}}
$$

- **T:** $-2 = -b \Rightarrow b = 2$
- **L:** $1 = a \Rightarrow a = 1$

$$
\pi_3 = \dfrac{g}{D \cdot N^{2}} \equiv \dfrac{1}{Fr}
$$

### Step 5: Write the Final Functional Relationship

$$
N_p = \phi(Re, Fr)
$$

In the **turbulent regime** (no surface vortex), $Fr$ is negligible, and:

$$
N_p = f(Re) = \text{constant} \approx 5 \text{ to } 6
$$

For **low viscosity** systems with vortex formation, both $Re$ and $Fr$ matter.

### Step 6: Application — Calculate Power for a Production Dissolver

**Given:** A Cowles dissolver impeller with diameter $D = 0.4$ m rotating at $N = 1500$ RPM in a liquid ink of density $\rho = 1100$ kg/m³ and viscosity $\mu = 5$ Pa·s.

**Step 1 — Convert units:**

$$
N = \dfrac{1500}{60} = 25 \text{ rev/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate $Re$:**

$$
Re = \dfrac{1100 \cdot 25 \cdot (0.4)^{2}}{5}
$$

$$
Re = \dfrac{1100 \cdot 25 \cdot 0.16}{5}
$$

$$
Re = \dfrac{4400}{5} = 880
$$

**Step 3 — Regime Identification:**

Since $Re = 880$ is between $10$ and $10,000$, we are in the **transitional regime**.

**Step 4 — Determine $N_p$ from standard correlation curves:**

For a Cowles blade in transitional flow, typical $N_p \approx 3.5$ (from empirical charts).

**Step 5 — Calculate Power:**

$$
P = N_p \cdot \rho \cdot N^{3} \cdot D^{5}
$$

$$
P = 3.5 \cdot 1100 \cdot (25)^{3} \cdot (0.4)^{5}
$$

$$
P = 3.5 \cdot 1100 \cdot 15625 \cdot 0.01024
$$

$$
P = 3.5 \cdot 1100 \cdot 160
$$

$$
P = 3.5 \cdot 176000
$$

$$
\boxed{P = 616{,}000 \text{ W} \approx 616 \text{ kW}}
$$

**Interpretation:** A 616 kW motor is enormous for an industrial ink plant. This indicates that either (a) the impeller is too large, (b) speed is too high, or (c) the empirical $N_p$ is for a different geometry. In practice, industrial high-speed dispersers operate at $5 - 30$ kW for tanks of 500 – 2000 L.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Equipment Performance Comparison

| Equipment | Max Viscosity (Pa·s) | Shear Level | Typical Batch Time | Power Density (kW/m³) | Best Application |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---|
| **Propeller Mixer** | 1 | Low | 10 – 30 min | 0.1 – 0.5 | Thin varnishes, dilution |
| **Paddle Mixer** | 5 | Low – Medium | 15 – 45 min | 0.2 – 1.0 | Slurry blending |
| **Anchor Agitator** | 50 | Medium | 30 – 90 min | 0.3 – 1.5 | High-viscosity pastes, heat transfer |
| **Helical Ribbon** | 100 | Medium | 30 – 60 min | 0.5 – 2.0 | Pseudoplastic pastes |
| **High-Speed Cowles Dissolver** | 10 | High | 20 – 60 min | 3 – 6 | Pigment wetting into vehicle |
| **Sawtooth Impeller** | 10 | Very High | 15 – 45 min | 5 – 10 | High-solids dispersion |
| **Planetary Mixer** | 500 | Medium – High | 45 – 120 min | 1 – 3 | Change-can operations |
| **Sigma Blade Mixer** | 1000+ | High | 60 – 180 min | 2 – 5 | Heavy putty, mastics, plastisols |
| **Rotor-Stator (Inline)** | 50 | Very High | Continuous | 10 – 50 | Inline homogenization |
| **Static Mixer** | 100 | Low – Medium | Continuous | Negligible | Inline blending of low-viscosity fluids |

## 3.3 Energy Efficiency Insight

A common 7-mark analytical question in KTU 2024 ESE concerns the **scaling laws for power consumption**.

**Power Law of Mixing:**

$$
P \propto N^{3} \cdot D^{5}
$$

**Implication 1:** Doubling rotational speed increases power consumption by a factor of **8**.

**Implication 2:** Doubling impeller diameter increases power consumption by a factor of **32**.

**Implication 3:** Holding the **tip speed** $V_t = \pi D N$ constant (which keeps shear constant), and substituting $N = V_t / (\pi D)$:

$$
P \propto \left(\dfrac{V_t}{\pi D}\right)^{3} \cdot D^{5} \propto \dfrac{V_t^{3} \cdot D^{2}}{\pi^{3}}
$$

**This means: for a constant tip speed, larger impellers consume MORE power**, not less. This is counter-intuitive but critical for scale-up.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **`[KTU University Exam - April 2024, Model Paper]`**

### Question 1: Define "Mixing Index" and state its significance in ink quality control.

**Model Answer:**

The **Mixing Index** (also called the **Homogeneity Index** or **Degree of Mixing**) is a **dimensionless statistical parameter** that quantifies how uniformly the components of a mixture are distributed. It is defined as:

$$
\eta_{m} = 1 - \sqrt{\dfrac{\sigma_{sample}^{2}}{\sigma_{0}^{2}}}
$$

Where:
- $\sigma_{sample}^{2}$ = variance of concentration of a tracer component in samples taken from the mixer
- $\sigma_{0}^{2}$ = variance of the same tracer in the completely unmixed initial state

**Significance in ink quality:**
- $\eta_{m} = 0$ → completely unmixed (poor quality)
- $\eta_{m} = 1$ → perfectly homogeneous (ideal)
- Commercial inks typically require $\eta_{m} \geq 0.95$ for batch release

This index ensures **batch-to-batch color consistency**, which is critical in printing operations where even a 1% variation in pigment concentration can shift the printed color outside the customer's tolerance.

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between "propeller mixer" and "anchor agitator" with respect to viscosity range and flow pattern.

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Propeller Mixer | Anchor Agitator |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Viscosity Range** | 0.001 – 1 Pa·s (low) | 1 – 50 Pa·s (medium to high) |
| **Flow Pattern** | Axial flow (parallel to shaft) | Tangential flow with scraping action |
| **Tip Speed** | High (5 – 15 m/s) | Low (0.5 – 2 m/s) |
| **Application** | Blending thin varnishes, solvents | Mixing heavy pastes, heat transfer |
| **Clearance to Wall** | Large gap (turbulence-based mixing) | Very small (scrapes tank wall) |

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **`[KTU ESE Module 3 - Internal Choice Pattern, Dec 2023 / April 2024]`**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### (a) Classify the various types of mixing equipment used in the printing ink industry based on the shear intensity and viscosity range. Briefly explain the working principle of any two high-shear mixers. [7 Marks]

**Model Answer:**

Mixing equipment used in printing ink manufacture can be classified as follows:

**1. Low-Shear Mixers (Viscosity < 1 Pa·s):**
- Propeller mixers, paddle mixers, flat-blade turbines
- Used for blending low-viscosity varnishes, solvents, and diluents
- Generate primarily **axial or radial flow** with minimal shear

**2. Medium-Shear Mixers (Viscosity 1 – 50 Pa·s):**
- Anchor agitators, helical ribbon mixers, planetary mixers
- Used for medium-viscosity pastes and high-solids slurries
- Provide good **bulk transport** and **heat transfer**

**3. High-Shear Mixers (Viscosity 0.1 – 100 Pa·s):**
- Cowles dissolvers, sawtooth impellers, rotor-stator systems
- Used for **wetting pigment powders** into vehicles
- Generate intense **localized shear** at the impeller tip

**4. Heavy Paste / Dough Mixers (Viscosity > 50 Pa·s):**
- Sigma blade mixers, Z-blade kneaders, double-arm mixers
- Used for mastics, putties, plastisols, and offset ink bases
- Operate at **low RPM with high torque**

**5. Continuous Inline Mixers:**
- Static mixers, inline rotor-stators, bead mills
- Used for high-volume production of finished inks

#### Working Principle of High-Shear Mixers

**A. Cowles Dissolver:**
The Cowles blade is a **saw-tooth impeller** mounted on a vertical shaft driven by a high-speed motor. Operating at **1500 – 6000 RPM**, the blade tip speed reaches **20 – 40 m/s**. As the impeller rotates:
- The vehicle (resin solution) is drawn from below and thrown outward centrifugally
- Pigment powder is **fed directly into the vortex** formed at the impeller
- The **high-velocity gradient** between the rotating blade and the stationary fluid creates intense **shear stresses** that wet the pigment particles
- The vortex also generates **convective flow** that recirculates un-wetted pigment back into the high-shear zone
- Typical power density: **3 – 6 kW per 1000 L**

**B. Rotor-Stator Mixer (High-Shear Inline):**
A rotor-stator consists of a **high-speed rotor** rotating within a **stationary stator** separated by a small gap (typically **0.1 – 3 mm**). The fluid is:
- Drawn into the center of the rotor
- Accelerated outward at **velocities of 20 – 50 m/s**
- Forced through the perforations/teeth of the stator
- Subjected to **hydraulic shear** of 10,000 – 100,000 s⁻¹
- Discharged radially as a finely dispersed stream
- This produces **sub-micron de-agglomeration** ideal for finished ink homogenization

---

#### (b) Derive the Power Number correlation for an impeller using dimensional analysis. Hence calculate the power required for a 0.5 m diameter Cowles blade rotating at 1800 RPM in an ink vehicle of density 1050 kg/m³ and viscosity 8 Pa·s. Assume Power Number $N_p = 4.2$ in the transitional regime. [7 Marks]

**Model Answer:**

**Dimensional Analysis:**

Identify the dependent variable and parameters:

$$
P = f(D, N, \rho, \mu, g)
$$

By Buckingham $\pi$-theorem, with $n = 6$ variables and $r = 3$ fundamental dimensions (M, L, T), we get $n - r = 3$ dimensionless groups.

Using $D$, $N$, $\rho$ as repeating variables:

**$\pi_1$ — Power Number:**

$$
\pi_1 = \dfrac{P}{\rho \cdot N^{3} \cdot D^{5}} = N_p
$$

**$\pi_2$ — Reynolds Number:**

$$
\pi_2 = \dfrac{\mu}{\rho \cdot N \cdot D^{2}} = \dfrac{1}{Re}
$$

**$\pi_3$ — Froude Number:**

$$
\pi_3 = \dfrac{g}{D \cdot N^{2}} = \dfrac{1}{Fr}
$$

**Final correlation:**

$$
N_p = \phi(Re, Fr)
$$

In the **turbulent regime** with negligible vortex, $N_p \approx \text{constant}$ (typically 5 – 6).

**Numerical Calculation:**

**Given:**
- $D = 0.5$ m
- $N = 1800$ RPM $= 1800 / 60 = 30$ rev/s
- $\rho = 1050$ kg/m³
- $\mu = 8$ Pa·s
- $N_p = 4.2$

**Step 1: Calculate Reynolds Number:**

$$
Re = \dfrac{\rho \cdot N \cdot D^{2}}{\mu}
$$

$$
Re = \dfrac{1050 \cdot 30 \cdot (0.5)^{2}}{8}
$$

$$
Re = \dfrac{1050 \cdot 30 \cdot 0.25}{8}
$$

$$
Re = \dfrac{7875}{8} = 984.4
$$

This confirms **transitional flow** ($10 < Re < 10,000$).

**Step 2: Apply Power Formula:**

$$
P = N_p \cdot \rho \cdot N^{3} \cdot D^{5}
$$

$$
P = 4.2 \cdot 1050 \cdot (30)^{3} \cdot (0.5)^{5}
$$

$$
P = 4.2 \cdot 1050 \cdot 27000 \cdot 0.03125
$$

$$
P = 4.2 \cdot 1050 \cdot 843.75
$$

$$
P = 4.2 \cdot 886{,}875
$$

$$
\boxed{P \approx 3.72 \times 10^{6} \text{ W} = 3.72 \text{ MW}}
$$

**Engineering Interpretation:**

The result indicates that operating at 1800 RPM with a 0.5 m impeller in 8 Pa·s fluid is **highly impractical** — the required power is enormous. Real industrial practice either:
- Reduces impeller diameter to **0.15 – 0.25 m**
- Reduces speed to **500 – 1000 RPM**
- Uses **multiple smaller impellers** on the same shaft

This illustrates why **scale-up calculations** based on power density (kW/m³) are preferred in ink plant design.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)**

#### (a) With a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of a Sigma Blade Mixer. Discuss its suitability for high-viscosity printing ink bases. [7 Marks]

**Model Answer:**

**Construction of Sigma Blade Mixer:**

A Sigma Blade Mixer consists of:
- A **horizontal trough** (U-shaped or double-U) with a jacket for heating/cooling
- **Two heavy-duty blades** (sigma-shaped, resembling the Greek letter $\Sigma$) mounted on parallel horizontal shafts
- The two shafts rotate at **different speeds** (typically in the ratio **1 : 1.5 or 1 : 2**)
- The blades are positioned to intermesh but not collide
- A **tilting mechanism** for discharge
- Hydraulic or gear-driven motor with **high torque, low RPM (10 – 30 RPM)**

**Working Principle:**

The two sigma blades rotate in **opposite directions** and at **different angular velocities**. As they rotate:
- Material is **pushed forward** by the leading curve of one blade
- The faster blade **shears the material** as it passes the slower blade
- The material is **folded, stretched, and compressed** repeatedly
- This generates **kneading, smearing, and shearing actions** simultaneously
- A **scraping action** occurs as the blades wipe the trough walls
- The differential speed ensures no material is left unmixed

**Suitability for High-Viscosity Ink Bases:**

1. **Handles viscosities up to 1000 Pa·s or more** — well beyond the limit of propeller or Cowles mixers
2. **Provides intense kneading** required for **offset ink bases** (viscous pigment grinds in alkyd resins) and **letterpress inks**
3. **Allows heavy pigment loading** (40 – 60% by weight) without choking
4. **Heat transfer jacket** enables control of exothermic reactions during resin-pigment interaction
5. **Batch operation** with controlled batch time (typically **60 – 180 minutes**) ensures uniform dispersion of waxy additives and tack modifiers
6. **No air entrainment** — unlike high-speed dispersers, the slow blades do not whip air into the paste

**Limitations:**
- High capital cost
- Long batch time
- Not suitable for heat-sensitive or low-viscosity systems
- Difficult to clean (color changeover issues)

---

#### (b) Explain the concept of scale-up in mixing. Discuss any two common scale-up criteria with their mathematical basis. [7 Marks]

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:**

**Scale-up** in mixing refers to the systematic procedure of translating laboratory/pilot-scale mixing results to full-scale industrial production equipment, while maintaining the **same product quality** (particle size distribution, color strength, viscosity, etc.).

The challenge arises because mixing depends on **multiple geometric and dynamic variables** (diameter $D$, speed $N$, power $P$, volume $V$), and all of them cannot be kept constant simultaneously when changing scale.

**Common Scale-Up Criteria:**

**1. Constant Power per Unit Volume ($P/V$):**

This is the most widely used criterion for **shear-sensitive** and **dispersion-driven** processes (typical for printing inks).

$$
\left(\dfrac{P}{V}\right)_{lab} = \left(\dfrac{P}{V}\right)_{plant}
$$

**Mathematical Basis:**

Substituting $P = N_p \cdot \rho \cdot N^{3} \cdot D^{5}$ and $V \propto D^{3}$:

$$
\dfrac{N_p \cdot \rho \cdot N^{3} \cdot D^{5}}{D^{3}} = N_p \cdot \rho \cdot N^{3} \cdot D^{2} = \text{constant}
$$

Therefore:

$$
N_2 = N_1 \left(\dfrac{D_1}{D_2}\right)^{2/3}
$$

**2. Constant Tip Speed ($V_t$):**

This criterion maintains the **maximum shear rate** experienced by the fluid and is used for **blending operations** where mass transfer is critical.

$$
V_{t,2} = V_{t,1} \Rightarrow \pi D_2 N_2 = \pi D_1 N_1
$$

Therefore:

$$
N_2 = N_1 \cdot \left(\dfrac{D_1}{D_2}\right)
$$

**Comparison Table:**

| Scale-Up Basis | Math Form | Best For | Risk |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| **Constant $P/V$** | $N_2 = N_1 (D_1/D_2)^{2/3}$ | Dispersion, high-shear | May over-shear sensitive fluids |
| **Constant Tip Speed** | $N_2 = N_1 (D_1/D_2)$ | Bulk blending | May under-shear viscous pastes |
| **Constant Reynolds Number** | $N_2 = N_1 (D_1/D_2)$ | Laminar blending | Too slow for large tanks |
| **Constant Mixing Time** | $N_2 = N_1$ | Lab-to-pilot only | Impractical at large scale |

**Numerical Example:**

If a lab dissolver with $D_1 = 0.1$ m runs at $N_1 = 3000$ RPM, scale up to a plant unit with $D_2 = 0.5$ m using constant $P/V$ criterion:

$$
N_2 = 3000 \times \left(\dfrac{0.1}{0.5}\right)^{2/3}
$$

$$
N_2 = 3000 \times (0.2)^{2/3} = 3000 \times 0.342
$$

$$
\boxed{N_2 \approx 1026 \text{ RPM}}
$$

This is a reasonable speed for an industrial dissolver.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Losing Mistakes in Mixing Equipment Questions**
>
> 1. **Confusing Mixer Types:** Students often interchange "Cowles dissolver" and "high-speed disperser" — these are **the same thing**, but calling a "sigma blade" a "high-speed mixer" will cost you **2 – 3 marks immediately**. The KTU examiner strictly checks terminology.
>
> 2. **Forgetting Units in Power Calculations:** Writing $P = 4.2 \times 1050 \times 27000 \times 0.03125 = 3.72 \times 10^6$ **without stating "Watts" or "kW"** is a guaranteed **1-mark deduction** in the numerical problem.
>
> 3. **Missing the Froude Number:** In dimensional analysis, many students derive only $N_p$ and $Re$, forgetting the third $\pi$-group (Froude number). This **incomplete derivation loses 3 marks** in Part B questions.
>
> 4. **Mixing Time vs. Residence Time Confusion:** Mixing time $t_m = V/Q$ is the **time to achieve homogeneity in a batch**, NOT the residence time in a continuous reactor. Examiners catch this.
>
> 5. **Drawing a Sigma Blade Incorrectly:** A common error is drawing the blades as **straight paddles** rather than the **S-shaped sigma curve**. Examiners allocate marks specifically for the **correct geometry**.
>
> 6. **Wrong Viscosity Units:** Always state **Pa·s** (SI) or convert from cP (1 cP = 10⁻³ Pa·s). Stating "viscosity = 8" without units is a 0.5-mark deduction.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for Module 3 - Mixing Equipment**

### Key Concepts
- **Mixing** = Homogenization; **Dispersion** = De-agglomeration
- Three transport mechanisms: **Convection, Eddy diffusion, Molecular diffusion**
- Three dimensionless groups: **$N_p$, $Re$, $Fr$**

### Power & Scale-Up Formulas (MUST MEMORIZE)
- $N_p = P / (\rho \cdot N^{3} \cdot D^{5})$
- $Re = \rho \cdot N \cdot D^{2} / \mu$
- $Fr = N^{2} \cdot D / g$
- $P \propto N^{3} \cdot D^{5}$ (power law)
- $V_t = \pi \cdot D \cdot N$ (tip speed)
- Scale-up: $N_2 = N_1 (D_1/D_2)^{2/3}$ for constant $P/V$

### Equipment Classification (REMEMBER THE VISCOSITY LIMITS)
- **Propeller** → < 1 Pa·s
- **Anchor** → 1 – 50 Pa·s
- **Sigma Blade** → 50 – 1000+ Pa·s
- **Cowles** → 0.1 – 10 Pa·s, high shear
- **Rotor-Stator** → 0.1 – 50 Pa·s, very high shear

### Flow Pattern Facts
- **Propeller** = Axial flow
- **Flat-blade turbine** = Radial flow
- **Anchor** = Tangential flow with scraping
- **Helical ribbon** = Combined axial + radial

### Critical Engineering Points
- **$N_p$ in turbulent regime** = 5 – 6 (independent of $Re$)
- **$N_p \cdot Re$ in laminar regime** = geometry-dependent constant
- **Constant $P/V$** = best scale-up criterion for ink dispersion
- **Constant tip speed** = best for bulk blending
- **Power increases as $D^5$** → doubling diameter = 32× power
- **Sigma blade speed ratio** = 1 : 1.5 to 1 : 2
- **Cowles RPM range** = 1500 – 6000 RPM
- **Mixing index target** = $\eta_m \geq 0.95$ for commercial inks

### Keywords for KTU 2024 Theory Answers
- *"Axial flow impeller"* — always state "low viscosity, bulk flow"
- *"High-shear mixer"* — always state "pigment wetting, dispersion"
- *"Sigma blade"* — always state "high viscosity, kneading, differential speed"
- *"Scale-up"* — always state "maintaining $P/V$ or tip speed"

> **End of Module 3 Notes — Mixing Equipment**
> *Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme PEPOT866 — Technology of Printing Inks*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Milling Equipment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT866 - TECHNOLOGY OF PRINTING INKS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Milling Equipment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Milling Equipment** in the context of printing ink technology refers to the class of mechanical process machinery employed to perform the **dispersion**, **deagglomeration**, and **size reduction** of solid pigment particles within a liquid vehicle (resin, solvent, oil, or water-based medium) to produce a homogeneous, stable, and rheologically suitable printing ink.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for *Technology of Printing Inks*, milling equipment is broadly classified into two operational categories:

1. **Low-Shear / Low-Energy Mills** — e.g., Triple Roll Mill, Attritor Mill, Ball Mill.
2. **High-Shear / High-Energy Mills** — e.g., Bead Mill (Sand Mill), Basket Mill, High-Speed Dissolver, Shot Mill.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** *Milling is the mechanical process of breaking down pigment agglomerates into primary particles and uniformly distributing them in the vehicle through the application of shear, impact, and attrition forces.*

The primary objectives of milling in ink manufacture are:
- **Wetting** — replacing air/solid interfaces with liquid/solid interfaces.
- **Dispersion** — breaking down aggregates and agglomerates.
- **Stabilization** — preventing re-agglomeration (flocculation) via proper resin adsorption.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: "Making a Smooth Chocolate Milkshake"**
> Imagine you drop a cube of dark chocolate into cold milk and stir with a spoon. The chocolate will *not* dissolve because it is in **lump (agglomerate) form**, and a spoon provides too little force. To get a *smooth milkshake*, you have two options:
> 1. **Crush the chocolate first** with a heavy stone (mortar & pestle) — this is analogous to a **Triple Roll Mill** (pressure + shear).
> 2. **Use a high-speed blender** that sucks the lumps in and slams them against a wall — this is analogous to a **Bead Mill / Sand Mill** (impact + attrition).
>
> The **vehicle (milk)** must also be "sticky" enough to grab onto the new chocolate surfaces instantly, or the lumps will re-stick together — this is the role of the **wetting resin**.

So, milling equipment is essentially a **mechanical kitchen blender** for pigments, but engineered to nanometer-level precision.

## 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics (SI Units)

The following key performance metrics are standardized for evaluating milling equipment in ink technology:

- **Tip Speed (m/s):** $\nu_t$ — peripheral velocity of the agitator/disc.
- **Shear Rate (s⁻¹):** $\dot{\gamma}$ — velocity gradient across the mill gap.
- **Specific Energy Input (kWh/tonne):** $E_s$ — energy per unit mass of ink.
- **Residence Time (minutes):** $\tau$ — time the ink spends inside the milling chamber.
- **Particle Size (μm or nm):** $d_{50}$ — median particle diameter after milling.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Tip:** Always state the **units** alongside numerical values in the exam. KTU examiners specifically award 0.5 marks for correct units in Part A questions.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Milling Equipment — A Structured Logic Map

Milling equipment in the printing ink industry can be broken down using a **"Force-Based" classification logic**:

### Step 1: Identify the Dominant Stress Mechanism

- **Compression + Shear (Low-Shear Regime):**
  - Triple Roll Mill (TRM) — *uses hydraulic pressure + nip shear*
  - Ball Mill / Pebble Mill — *uses gravitational cascade + impact*

### Step 2: Identify the Kinetic Energy Source

- **Impact + Attrition (High-Shear Regime):**
  - Bead Mill / Sand Mill — *uses accelerated beads colliding with pigment*
  - Attritor — *uses agitated media in a stirred tank*
  - Basket Mill — *integrates dissolver + bead mill in one unit*

### Step 3: Match the Ink System to the Mill

- **Paste inks (letterpress, offset, gravure paste):** → **Triple Roll Mill**
- **Liquid inks (flexo, gravure, inkjet, screen):** → **Bead Mill / Sand Mill**
- **High-viscosity offset inks (stiff paste):** → **Ball Mill**
- **Pre-mix + final dispersion:** → **High-Speed Dissolver + Bead Mill combination**

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept — "The 4 S's of Milling":**
> 1. **Size reduction** (comminution)
> 2. **Shear application** (flow between surfaces)
> 3. **Stabilization** (resin adsorption)
> 4. **System matching** (ink chemistry to mill type)

## 2.2 Operational Principles — Equipment-Wise Breakdown

### 2.2.1 Triple Roll Mill (Three-Roll Mill)

- **Construction:** Three horizontally-mounted chilled-cast iron or steel rolls rotating in the **same direction** at progressively increasing speeds.
- **Speed Ratio:** $N_1 : N_2 : N_3 = 1 : 3 : 9$ (typical), where $N_1$ is the feed roll, $N_2$ the centre, and $N_3$ the apron (delivery) roll.
- **Nip Regions:** Two nips exist:
  - **Feed Nip** (between Roll 1 & Roll 2) — primary dispersion.
  - **Finishing Nip** (between Roll 2 & Roll 3) — final smoothness.
- **Nip Pressure:** Controlled by hydraulic rams acting on the end bearings. Pressure range: **10–40 bar**.
- **Mechanism:** The high shear gradient (ΔV/h, where V is tangential velocity difference and h is nip gap) ruptures pigment agglomerates.

### 2.2.2 Ball Mill (Pebble Mill)

- **Construction:** A horizontal rotating cylinder, partially filled with grinding media (steel balls, ceramic beads, or flint pebbles).
- **Mechanism:** Pigment + vehicle + balls are tumbled; balls **cascade** and **cataract**, generating impact + attrition forces.
- **Critical Speed ($N_c$):** The rotational speed at which centrifugal force just balances gravitational force; balls are pinned to the mill wall and produce no grinding.
- **Operating Range:** $50\% - 80\%$ of $N_c$.
- **Applications:** Used for **low-viscosity news inks** and **carbon black dispersions** (long residence time: 4–24 hours).

### 2.2.3 Bead Mill / Sand Mill

- **Construction:** A cylindrical grinding chamber filled with **fine grinding beads** (0.3–3.0 mm), agitated by a high-speed disc, pin, or rotor.
- **Types:**
  - **Vertical Bead Mill** — gravity-fed, beads retained by a screen at the bottom.
  - **Horizontal Bead Mill** — beads retained by a dynamic separator (rotating gap); higher energy density.
- **Mechanism:** Beads are accelerated by the agitator; they **collide**, **shear**, and **roll** against each other and the pigment agglomerates.
- **Tip Speed:** $\nu_t = \pi \cdot D \cdot N$, where $D$ is the disc diameter and $N$ is the rotational speed (rev/s).
- **Operating Tip Speed:** $\nu_t = 8 - 25$ m/s for conventional ink milling.

### 2.2.4 Attritor Mill

- **Construction:** A vertical or horizontal tank with **internal agitator arms**, filled with grinding media.
- **Mechanism:** Agitator stirs the media, creating random media motion — produces both impact and attrition.
- **Advantage:** Higher energy density than ball mill due to driven media; shorter residence time.

### 2.2.5 High-Speed Dissolver (Cowles Dissolver)

- **Construction:** A simple tank with a high-speed saw-tooth impeller.
- **Role:** **Pre-mixing** and **wetting**, not final milling. Tip speed: 20–40 m/s.
- **Not a true mill** — but a critical pre-stage for bead mill operation.

### 2.2.6 Basket Mill

- **Construction:** A dissolver-type impeller enclosed in a perforated basket filled with grinding beads.
- **Role:** Combines pre-mixing and final milling in a single batch operation.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units | Application |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | $N_c = \dfrac{42.3}{\sqrt{D}}$ | Critical speed of ball mill (SI) | rpm | Ball mill sizing |
| 2 | $N_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{g}{R}} \cdot 60$ | Critical speed (fundamental form) | rpm | Derivation reference |
| 3 | $\nu_t = \pi \cdot D \cdot N$ | Tip speed of rotating disc | m/s | Bead mill, dissolver |
| 4 | $\dot{\gamma} = \dfrac{\Delta V}{h}$ | Shear rate in the nip | s⁻¹ | Triple roll mill |
| 5 | $P = T \cdot \omega$ | Power drawn by mill | W (J/s) | All mills |
| 6 | $E_s = \dfrac{P \cdot t}{m_{ink}}$ | Specific energy input | kWh/tonne | Energy efficiency |
| 7 | $\nu_s = \dfrac{g \cdot d^2 \cdot (\rho_p - \rho_m)}{18 \cdot \mu}$ | Stokes' settling velocity (bead retention check) | m/s | Bead selection |
| 8 | $N_{opt} \approx 0.75 \cdot N_c$ | Optimum operating speed (ball mill) | rpm | Operation practice |
| 9 | $SR = \dfrac{N_3}{N_1}$ | Speed ratio of triple roll mill | dimensionless | TRM operation |
| 10 | $F_c = m \cdot \omega^2 \cdot R$ | Centrifugal force on grinding media | N | Mill design |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mnemonic for KTU Exam — "CATS B-MILL":**
> - **C**ritical speed formula
> - **A**gitation tip speed
> - **T**hree-roll shear rate
> - **S**pecific energy input
> - **B**ead Stokes' settling
> - **M**echanical power
> - **I**mpact force (centrifugal)
> - **L**oad fraction (media volume)
> - **L**eakage flow (gap in TRM)

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Milling equipment selection directly impacts:
1. **Print Quality** — particle size determines gloss, transparency, and color strength.
2. **Production Throughput** — bead mills are 10–100× faster than ball mills.
3. **Energy Efficiency** — kWh/tonne determines the operating cost.
4. **Ink Rheology** — affects viscosity, flow, and transfer in the printing press.
5. **Ink Stability** — proper dispersion prevents flocculation during storage.

> [!TIP]
> **Industrial Note:** Modern ink plants almost universally use **horizontal bead mills** for liquid inks and **triple roll mills** only for high-viscosity offset/UV inks — a transition worth mentioning in 14-mark questions.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Critical Speed of a Ball Mill

### 3.1.1 Statement
Derive the expression for the **critical rotational speed** of a ball mill at which the grinding balls are centrifugally pinned to the mill wall and cease to cascade.

### 3.1.2 Given
- Mill diameter (internal): $D = 2R$
- Gravitational acceleration: $g = 9.81$ m/s²
- A grinding ball of mass $m$ at the mill wall.

### 3.1.3 Free-Body Analysis

At the **critical condition**, the ball at the topmost point of the mill is on the verge of leaving the wall. Two forces act on it:
- **Centrifugal force** (radially outward): $F_c = m \cdot \omega^2 \cdot R$
- **Gravitational force** (radially inward): $F_g = m \cdot g$

At critical speed, $F_c$ just balances $F_g$ (the component of weight along the radius becomes zero at the topmost point when the mill is rotating fast enough).

### 3.1.4 Equilibrium Equation

$$
F_c = F_g
$$

$$
m \cdot \omega^2 \cdot R = m \cdot g
$$

### 3.1.5 Solving for $\omega$

$$
\omega^2 = \dfrac{g}{R}
$$

$$
\omega = \sqrt{\dfrac{g}{R}} \quad \text{(rad/s)}
$$

### 3.1.6 Convert to Revolutions Per Minute (rpm)

Since $\omega = 2\pi N / 60$, where $N$ is in rpm:

$$
2\pi \cdot \dfrac{N_c}{60} = \sqrt{\dfrac{g}{R}}
$$

$$
N_c = \dfrac{60}{2\pi} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{g}{R}}
$$

$$
N_c = \dfrac{30}{\pi} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{g}{R}}
$$

### 3.1.7 Substitute $g = 9.81$ m/s² and $R = D/2$

$$
N_c = \dfrac{30}{\pi} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{9.81}{D/2}} = \dfrac{30}{\pi} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{19.62}{D}}
$$

$$
N_c = \dfrac{30}{\pi} \cdot \dfrac{\sqrt{19.62}}{\sqrt{D}} = \dfrac{30 \cdot 4.4294}{\pi \cdot \sqrt{D}}
$$

$$
N_c = \dfrac{132.88}{\pi \cdot \sqrt{D}} \approx \dfrac{42.3}{\sqrt{D}}
$$

### 3.1.8 Final Result

$$
\boxed{N_c = \dfrac{42.3}{\sqrt{D}} \;\;\text{rpm}, \;\;\text{where } D \text{ is in metres}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** A typical ink ball mill has $D = 1.2$ m → $N_c = 42.3 / \sqrt{1.2} \approx 38.6$ rpm. Operating at $75\%$ of $N_c$ → $N_{opt} \approx 29$ rpm. This is a **classic KTU 2-mark or 7-mark sub-part question**.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Tip Speed of a Bead Mill Disc

### 3.2.1 Statement
A horizontal bead mill has an agitator disc of diameter $D = 0.30$ m rotating at $N = 1800$ rpm. Calculate the tip speed and the corresponding shear rate in the mill gap if the effective gap is $h = 0.5$ mm.

### 3.2.2 Given
- $D = 0.30$ m
- $N = 1800$ rpm
- $h = 0.0005$ m

### 3.2.3 Step-by-Step Calculation

**Step 1:** Convert rotational speed to revolutions per second.

$$
N_{rps} = \dfrac{1800}{60} = 30 \;\;\text{rev/s}
$$

**Step 2:** Calculate angular velocity.

$$
\omega = 2\pi \cdot N_{rps} = 2\pi \cdot 30 = 188.5 \;\;\text{rad/s}
$$

**Step 3:** Apply the tip-speed formula $\nu_t = \pi \cdot D \cdot N_{rps}$.

$$
\nu_t = \pi \cdot 0.30 \cdot 30
$$

$$
\nu_t = 28.27 \;\;\text{m/s}
$$

**Step 4:** Calculate the shear rate. The velocity of the fluid at the disc tip is $\nu_t$, and the wall of the chamber is approximately stationary. The shear rate across the gap is:

$$
\dot{\gamma} = \dfrac{\nu_t}{h} = \dfrac{28.27}{0.0005}
$$

$$
\dot{\gamma} = 56,540 \;\;\text{s}^{-1}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Insight:** This shear rate of $\sim 5.6 \times 10^4$ s⁻¹ is what enables the bead mill to break down pigment agglomerates in **seconds to minutes**, compared to hours in a ball mill.

### 3.2.4 Result

$$
\boxed{\nu_t = 28.27 \;\;\text{m/s}, \quad \dot{\gamma} = 5.65 \times 10^4 \;\;\text{s}^{-1}}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Stokes' Law for Bead-Pigment Separation Check

In a bead mill, beads must be **retained** in the chamber while the ink **flows out**. Stokes' law determines the settling tendency of a bead (or any particle).

### 3.3.1 Statement
A glass bead of diameter $d = 1.0$ mm and density $\rho_p = 2500$ kg/m³ is suspended in an ink vehicle of density $\rho_m = 1000$ kg/m³ and viscosity $\mu = 0.5$ Pa·s. Calculate its terminal settling velocity.

### 3.3.2 Step-by-Step

**Step 1:** Apply Stokes' Law.

$$
\nu_s = \dfrac{g \cdot d^2 \cdot (\rho_p - \rho_m)}{18 \cdot \mu}
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute values.

$$
\nu_s = \dfrac{9.81 \cdot (0.001)^2 \cdot (2500 - 1000)}{18 \cdot 0.5}
$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate numerator.

$$
9.81 \cdot 10^{-6} \cdot 1500 = 0.014715
$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate denominator.

$$
18 \cdot 0.5 = 9
$$

**Step 5:** Final result.

$$
\nu_s = \dfrac{0.014715}{9} = 0.001635 \;\;\text{m/s} = 1.635 \;\;\text{mm/s}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** Since the upward flow velocity of ink in a bead mill is typically 1–5 m/s, the bead (1.6 mm/s settling) is **easily carried upward** — the screen/separator at the top is what retains it. The viscosity of the vehicle is critical.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Specific Energy Input for a Bead Mill

### 3.4.1 Statement
A bead mill consumes $P = 15$ kW and processes a batch of $m = 800$ kg of ink in $t = 2$ hours. Calculate the specific energy input.

### 3.4.2 Step-by-Step

**Step 1:** Total energy consumed.

$$
E_{total} = P \cdot t = 15 \;\;\text{kW} \cdot 2 \;\;\text{h} = 30 \;\;\text{kWh}
$$

**Step 2:** Specific energy per tonne.

$$
E_s = \dfrac{E_{total}}{m_{ink}} = \dfrac{30 \;\;\text{kWh}}{0.8 \;\;\text{tonne}} = 37.5 \;\;\text{kWh/tonne}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Industrial Benchmark:** Modern bead mills achieve particle sizes of < 10 μm at 30–80 kWh/tonne. Triple roll mills require 100–200 kWh/tonne for the same result — making bead mills **3–4× more energy-efficient**.

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Mill Selection

| Parameter | Triple Roll Mill | Ball Mill | Bead Mill | Attritor |
|-----------|------------------|-----------|-----------|----------|
| **Energy density** | Low | Low–Med | Very High | High |
| **Residence time** | Minutes (multiple passes) | 4–24 h | 5–30 min | 30–90 min |
| **Final particle size** | < 5 μm | 1–10 μm | < 5 μm | < 5 μm |
| **Viscosity range (Pa·s)** | 1–50 | 0.1–10 | 0.05–5 | 0.1–10 |
| **Continuous/ Batch** | Batch/recirculation | Batch | Continuous (preferred) | Batch |
| **Capital cost** | High | Low | Very High | Medium |
| **Maintenance** | Roller re-grinding | Media wear | Bead + separator | Media wear |
| **Typical ink** | Offset, UV, letterpress | News ink, carbon black | Flexo, gravure, screen | Specialty inks |
| **Cooling requirement** | Yes (water-cooled rolls) | Low | High (chilled water) | Medium |
| **KTU exam frequency** | Very High | High | Very High | Medium |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Printing Ink Manufacturing Line

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates **how milling equipment integrates** with pre-mixing and post-mixing stages in a typical ink plant.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Material Storage] --> B[Pre-Weighing Station]
    B --> C[High-Speed Dissolver - Pre-Mix]
    C --> D1[Triple Roll Mill - Paste Inks]
    C --> D2[Bead Mill - Liquid Inks]
    C --> D3[Ball Mill - News Inks]
    D1 --> E[Let-down Tank]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    E --> F[Filtration - Bag or Cartridge]
    F --> G[Quality Control Lab]
    G --> H{Particle Size and Viscosity OK?}
    H -->|No| I[Recirculate to Mill]
    H -->|Yes| J[Final Storage Tank]
    J --> K[Packaging Line]
    K --> L[Finished Ink Shipment]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The pre-mix dissolver is a **mandatory upstream stage** for all mills — it ensures the pigment is wetted and pre-dispersed, dramatically improving the efficiency of the downstream mill.

## 4.2 Functional Architecture — Triple Roll Mill Internal Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Feed Hopper - Pigment Paste] --> R1[Roll 1 - Slowest]
    R1 -->|Feed Nip| R2[Roll 2 - Medium Speed]
    R2 -->|Finishing Nip| R3[Roll 3 - Fastest Apron]
    R3 --> S[Doctor Blade - Scrapes Ink]
    S --> T[Collection Trough]
    H1[Hydraulic Pressure System] -.->|Adjusts gap| R1
    H1 -.->|Adjusts gap| R2
    CW[Chilled Water Circulation] -.->|Cools rolls| R1
    CW -.->|Cools rolls| R2
    CW -.->|Cools rolls| R3
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Observation:** The arrows labelled `Feed Nip` and `Finishing Nip` represent the two **high-shear regions** where size reduction occurs. The chilled water is essential because the high shear generates significant heat that can damage the ink (resin degradation, solvent flash-off).

## 4.3 Functional Architecture — Bead Mill (Horizontal) Internal Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN[Ink Feed - Pre-Mixed Slurry] --> CH[Grinding Chamber]
    CH -->|Agitated by| RD[Rotating Disc / Rotor]
    RD -->|Accelerates| BE[Grinding Beads 0.3 to 3 mm]
    BE -->|Impact and Attrition| PA[Pigment Agglomerates]
    PA -->|Dispersed to| PP[Primary Particles]
    PP -->|Flows out through| SP[Dynamic Separator - Rotating Gap]
    SP --> OUT[Dispersed Ink Outlet]
    CW2[Chilled Water Jacket] -.->|Removes heat| CH
    BE -.->|Retained by| SP
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Note:** The **dynamic separator** is the heart of a modern horizontal bead mill. It allows beads to be retained while the ink flows out — the gap setting (typically 0.2–0.5 mm) is critical: too small → beads leak; too large → small beads get ejected.

## 4.4 Decision Tree — Selecting the Right Mill

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Ink Formulation Ready] --> Q1{Is the ink a paste or liquid?}
    Q1 -->|Paste - high viscosity| Q2{Is it offset or letterpress?}
    Q1 -->|Liquid - low viscosity| Q3{Is it flexo, gravure, or inkjet?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| TRM[Use Triple Roll Mill]
    Q2 -->|No - general paste| ATT[Use Attritor or Basket Mill]
    Q3 -->|Yes| BM[Use Horizontal Bead Mill]
    Q3 -->|No| QA{Is it news ink?}
    QA -->|Yes| BML[Use Ball Mill]
    QA -->|No| BM
    TRM --> END[Production]
    ATT --> END
    BM --> END
    BML --> END
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model]
**"Define milling in the context of printing ink manufacture. List the three primary objectives of milling."**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Definition (1.5 marks):** Milling is the mechanical process of reducing pigment agglomerates to primary particles and uniformly dispersing them in the ink vehicle (resin solution, oil, or solvent) through the application of controlled shear, impact, and attrition forces.
- **Three Primary Objectives (1.5 marks):**
  1. **Wetting** of pigment surfaces by the vehicle (replacing air/solid interfaces with liquid/solid interfaces).
  2. **Dispersion / Deagglomeration** of pigment clusters into primary particles.
  3. **Stabilization** of the dispersion to prevent re-flocculation during storage.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often confuse **"dispersion"** (mechanical separation) with **"dissolution"** (molecular solubilization). Pigments do NOT dissolve — they are dispersed. Using the word "dissolve" costs 0.5 marks.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]
**"What is the function of the chilled water circulation system in a triple roll mill? Why is the speed ratio 1:3:9 used?"**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Function of Chilled Water (1.5 marks):** The high nip pressures and shear rates generate substantial frictional heat (typically 40–80 °C rise). Chilled water circulation keeps the roll surface temperature below ~30 °C to (a) prevent premature solvent evaporation, (b) avoid resin degradation / cross-linking, and (c) maintain constant viscosity for consistent film thickness.
- **Speed Ratio 1:3:9 (1.5 marks):** Each successive roll rotates faster to (a) **transport** the ink from feed to delivery by friction, (b) generate a **velocity gradient** (ΔV) across the nip for high shear, and (c) ensure the ink film becomes progressively thinner, exposing more particles to the next nip for further dispersion.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students write "to make it rotate faster" without explaining the **shear gradient** concept. You must mention **ΔV/h** (velocity difference / gap) to score full marks.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model 2024 Scheme]

**"With the help of a neat diagram, explain the construction, working principle, and operating parameters of a **Triple Roll Mill** used in offset ink manufacture. Discuss the role of speed ratio, nip pressure, and roll temperature in determining ink quality." (14 Marks)**

#### Sub-Part (a): Construction and Working Principle (7 Marks)

**Construction (3.5 Marks):**
1. **Three horizontal rolls** made of chilled cast iron or ceramic-coated steel, mounted in a heavy cast-iron frame.
2. The rolls rotate in the **same direction** (not counter-rotating) at progressively increasing speeds.
3. **Hydraulic rams** at the bearing housings of Roll 1 and Roll 2 apply nip pressure (10–40 bar).
4. **End scrapers / doctor blades** collect ink from Roll 3.
5. **Internal channels** in the rolls allow chilled water circulation.
6. A **hopper/feed pan** is positioned at the Feed Nip (between Rolls 1 and 2).

**Working Principle (3.5 Marks):**
- The pre-mixed pigment paste is fed into the **Feed Nip** (Roll 1 – Roll 2).
- The speed differential (ΔV = V₂ - V₁) across the narrow gap h creates a **shear rate** $\dot{\gamma} = \Delta V / h$ of the order of 10⁴–10⁵ s⁻¹.
- This shear ruptures the pigment agglomerates.
- The partially dispersed ink adheres to Roll 2 (faster roll) and is carried into the **Finishing Nip** (Roll 2 – Roll 3), where even higher shear and a thinner film refine the dispersion.
- The final ink is **scraped off Roll 3** by the doctor blade into the collection trough.
- **Multiple passes** (3–5) are typically used to achieve the target Hegman grind (e.g., 6+ for offset inks).

#### Sub-Part (b): Operating Parameters and Ink Quality (7 Marks)

**1. Speed Ratio (2.5 Marks):**
- Standard ratio is **1 : 3 : 9** (feed : centre : apron).
- The ratio controls the **velocity gradient** $\Delta V$ and hence the shear rate.
- If ratio is too low → insufficient shear → poor dispersion.
- If ratio is too high → excessive heat, roll slip, ink spitting.

**2. Nip Pressure (2.5 Marks):**
- Controls the **gap h** between rolls.
- Higher pressure → smaller gap → higher shear rate → finer dispersion.
- Excessive pressure → roll deflection, bearing damage, ink "splash-out".

**3. Roll Temperature (2 Marks):**
- Maintained at **25–30 °C** via chilled water.
- Higher temperature → lower ink viscosity → less shear transfer → poor dispersion.
- Also risk of solvent flash-off and resin pre-cure (in UV systems).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes to Avoid in 14-Mark Answers:**
> 1. **Forgetting the "same direction"** rotation — students often wrongly say "counter-rotating". This costs 1 mark.
> 2. **Not drawing the doctor blade** — examiners explicitly allocate 1 mark for a labelled diagram.
> 3. **Confusing "feed" and "finishing" nips** — the feed nip is between R1 and R2, NOT R2 and R3.
> 4. **Skipping the "multiple passes"** concept — most students forget to mention that a single pass is rarely sufficient.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam — Model 2024 Scheme]

**"Compare the construction and working of **Bead Mill** and **Ball Mill** used in liquid printing ink manufacture. Derive the expression for the critical speed of a ball mill and explain why a bead mill is preferred for flexographic and gravure inks." (14 Marks)**

#### Sub-Part (a): Comparison of Bead Mill and Ball Mill (7 Marks)

| Parameter | Bead Mill (Sand Mill) | Ball Mill (Pebble Mill) |
|-----------|----------------------|--------------------------|
| **Geometry** | Cylindrical chamber (V or H), small volume (5–500 L) | Large rotating drum (1–50 m³) |
| **Media size** | 0.3–3.0 mm (fine) | 10–50 mm (coarse) |
| **Media motion** | Driven by agitator | Cascading due to gravity + rotation |
| **Energy input** | High (motor + agitator) | Low (rotation only) |
| **Residence time** | 5–30 minutes (continuous) | 4–24 hours (batch) |
| **Final particle size** | < 5 μm (sub-micron possible) | 1–10 μm |
| **Heat removal** | Chilled water jacket | Minimal — low heat generation |
| **Viscosity range** | 0.05–5 Pa·s | 0.1–10 Pa·s |
| **Application** | Flexo, gravure, UV liquid, inkjet | News ink, carbon black, iron oxide |
| **KTU marks weight** | 3.5 | 3.5 |

**Working of Bead Mill (key points, ~2 marks within the 7):**
- Pre-mixed slurry is pumped into the grinding chamber.
- The high-speed disc/rotor accelerates the beads.
- Bead–bead and bead–pigment collisions create **high local shear and impact** stresses.
- The dispersion exits through a **screen or dynamic separator** that retains the beads.
- Continuous operation; residence time controlled by flow rate.

**Working of Ball Mill (key points, ~2 marks within the 7):**
- Pigment + vehicle + balls are loaded into a rotating drum.
- At 50–80 % of critical speed, balls **cascade** and **cataract**, producing impact + attrition.
- The grinding action is **random and low-intensity** compared to a bead mill.
- Batch operation with long cycle times.

#### Sub-Part (b): Critical Speed Derivation and Justification for Bead Mill in Flexo/Gravure (7 Marks)

**Derivation of Critical Speed (4 Marks):**

At critical speed, centrifugal force on a grinding ball = gravitational force:

$$
m \cdot \omega^2 \cdot R = m \cdot g
$$

Converting to rpm with $\omega = 2\pi N_c / 60$:

$$
N_c = \dfrac{42.3}{\sqrt{D}} \;\;\text{rpm} \quad (D \text{ in metres})
$$

**Operating Practice (1.5 Marks):** A ball mill is operated at $N = 0.5$ to $0.8 \cdot N_c$ to ensure cascading action without centrifuging.

**Why Bead Mill is Preferred for Flexo and Gravure (3 Marks):**

1. **Particle size demand:** Flexo and gravure inks require particle sizes < 5 μm (often < 1 μm for high-gloss packaging inks) — bead mills achieve this efficiently; ball mills cannot reach sub-5 μm without prohibitive time.
2. **Viscosity compatibility:** Flexo/gravure inks are low-viscosity (0.05–2 Pa·s) — ball mills require higher viscosity to "carry" the grinding media; bead mills work best in this low-viscosity regime.
3. **Continuous production:** Modern flexo/gravure ink plants operate continuously — bead mills are designed for continuous in–out flow; ball mills are inherently batch.
4. **Heat-sensitive systems:** Water-based flexo inks and UV inks are heat-sensitive — the chilled water jacket of a bead mill maintains temperature; ball mills generate less heat but take too long.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall (Bead Mill vs Ball Mill):**
> 1. Students often wrongly say "ball mill uses small beads" — no, ball mill uses **10–50 mm balls/pebbles**, bead mill uses **0.3–3 mm beads**.
> 2. The derivation must end with the **42.3/√D form** in **SI units**. Using CGS or non-standard forms costs 1 mark.
> 3. Students forget the **chilled water jacket** as a distinct advantage of bead mills.
> 4. The "continuous vs batch" distinction is worth 1 mark — don't omit it.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — "Milling Equipment in 10 Bullets"**
> 1. **Milling = Wetting + Dispersion + Stabilization** (the 3 stages of pigment processing).
> 2. **Triple Roll Mill (TRM):** 3 rolls, same direction, speed ratio **1:3:9**, two nips, used for **paste inks** (offset, UV, letterpress).
> 3. **Ball Mill:** Rotating drum, critical speed $N_c = 42.3 / \sqrt{D}$ rpm, operating at **50–80 %** of $N_c$, used for **news inks** and **carbon black** dispersions.
> 4. **Bead Mill (Sand Mill):** Agitated fine beads (0.3–3 mm), tip speed **8–25 m/s**, shear rate ~$10^4$–$10^5$ s⁻¹, used for **flexo, gravure, inkjet** liquid inks.
> 5. **Attritor:** Stirred media mill — energy density between ball and bead mill; used for **specialty inks**.
> 6. **High-Speed Dissolver:** Pre-mixer only, **not a true mill**; tip speed 20–40 m/s.
> 7. **Basket Mill:** Hybrid dissolver + bead mill in one unit, popular for **small-batch production**.
> 8. **Nip pressure (TRM):** 10–40 bar via hydraulic rams; controls the gap and shear rate.
> 9. **Specific energy benchmark:** Bead mill 30–80 kWh/tonne < TRM 100–200 kWh/tonne — bead mill is **3–4× more efficient**.
> 10. **Stokes' Law** governs bead retention — viscosity of the vehicle is the key parameter for bead-mill operation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 — Frequently Tested Sub-Concepts (5 Most Common):**
> 1. **Derivation of critical speed** — appears almost every year.
> 2. **TRM speed ratio and nips** — guaranteed 3–7 mark question.
> 3. **Difference between bead mill and ball mill** — 14-mark standard.
> 4. **Tip speed and shear rate** of bead mill — calculation-based.
> 5. **Why chilled water is needed** in TRM and bead mill.

> [!WARNING]
> **Last-Minute KTU Exam Warnings:**
> - **Never** say "pigments dissolve in the vehicle" — they **disperse**.
> - **Never** say "ball mill uses fine beads" — it uses **coarse balls**.
> - **Always** mention **ΔV/h** when discussing shear rate in TRM.
> - **Always** state the **speed ratio 1:3:9** explicitly, not just "progressively increasing".
> - **Always** include the **unit (rpm)** with the critical-speed formula.
> - In **diagrams**, label: feed hopper, all 3 rolls, both nips, doctor blade, hydraulic system, chilled water lines.
> - In **derivations**, start with a clear **free-body diagram** (verbal or drawn) before writing the equilibrium equation.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Handling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT866 - TECHNOLOGY OF PRINTING INKS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Handling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## Definition of "Handling" in Printing Ink Technology

In the context of printing ink technology, **Handling** refers to the collective set of rheological, mechanical, and physical characteristics that dictate how an ink behaves during storage, transport, agitation, dosing, transfer from the reservoir, distribution on the ink distribution system (roller train), and final impression onto the substrate. It is a *performance umbrella term* that governs the **printability** of an ink on any given press.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Handling properties encompass the **rheological parameters** (viscosity, tack, body, consistency, flow, thixotropy) combined with the **mechanical parameters** (transfer efficiency, misting, set-off, drying rate) which collectively determine the operational ease of an ink during the printing process.

### The Three Pillars of Ink Handling

| Pillar | Domain | Primary Concern |
|---|---|---|
| **Rheological Pillar** | Fluid dynamics | How the ink *flows* and *deforms* under stress |
| **Mechanical Pillar** | Tribology & transfer | How the ink *transfers* across rollers and substrate |
| **Temporal Pillar** | Kinetics | How the ink properties *change* with time (during/after printing) |

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine **honey versus water versus toothpaste** — all three are liquids, but their behavior under a spoon is dramatically different. Now imagine you must spread each one uniformly on a piece of toast with a roller. Honey would drip, water would splash and soak in, and toothpaste would smear unevenly. **Handling properties of ink are exactly this** — they define the *ink's personality* when forced through the high-speed, high-shear environment of a printing press (typically operating between **1–10 m/s** at shear rates of **10³–10⁶ s⁻¹**).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Handling Matters in KTU Context:** A perfectly formulated ink pigment/varnish system is *useless* if its handling properties prevent it from being transported, metered, and deposited cleanly. Handling is therefore the bridge between **chemistry (formulation)** and **engineering (process)**.

### Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics in Handling

- **Standard Press Shear Rate:** $\dot{\gamma} \approx 10^{3} \text{ to } 10^{6} \text{ s}^{-1}$
- **Standard Printing Temperature:** $20^{\circ}\text{C} \pm 2^{\circ}\text{C}$ (ISO 2846 reference)
- **Tack Unit:** Tack-o-meter reading (arbitrary, dimensionless, 1–20 typical range)
- **Viscosity Unit:** Pa·s (SI) or Poise (cgs), with $1 \text{ Pa·s} = 10 \text{ Poise}$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Handling Properties — Structured Logic

The complete handling profile of a printing ink is governed by the following interlinked parameters:

### A. Viscosity ($\eta$)
- **Definition:** Internal friction of the ink resisting flow.
- **Why it matters:** Determines ink pickup from the duct, distribution across rollers, and film thickness on substrate.
- **How measured:** Capillary viscometer, rotational viscometer (Brookfield, Cone-and-Plate).
- **Range:** $\eta = 0.5 \text{ Pa·s}$ (news ink) to $\eta = 50 \text{ Pa·s}$ (paste ink for screen).

### B. Tack (T)
- **Definition:** Resistance of the ink film to splitting under tension (a *peel resistance*).
- **Why it matters:** Controls the splitting behavior of ink between rollers and between roller-to-substrate. Excess tack causes **paper picking** (tearing fibers from the substrate).
- **How measured:** Ink-tack-o-meter, measured at a standard roller speed and temperature.
- **Typical values:** $T = 12$–$18$ for offset, $T = 4$–$8$ for gravure.

### C. Body (Consistency)
- **Definition:** Apparent viscosity at low shear rates, perceived as "thickness" in the duct.
- **Why it matters:** Determines how the ink "sits" in the duct — must not run out, must not choke the feed.

### D. Flow (Levelling)
- **Definition:** Ability of the wet ink film to smooth out after deposition.
- **Why it matters:** Determines surface uniformity and gloss.

### E. Thixotropy
- **Definition:** Time-dependent shear-thinning — viscosity drops under shear but recovers at rest.
- **Why it matters:** Allows ink to flow under high shear in the press but "set" immediately after impression.
- **Measure:** Thixotropic index = $\frac{\eta \text{ at 6 rpm}}{\eta \text{ at 60 rpm}}$ on a Brookfield viscometer.

### F. Yield Value ($\tau_0$)
- **Definition:** Minimum stress required to initiate flow (Bingham behavior).
- **Why it matters:** Prevents ink from dripping from rollers; structural integrity in the duct.

### G. Shortness vs. Length
- **Short ink:** Breaks cleanly under stress (good for sharp halftones).
- **Long ink:** Stretches into filaments (good for fountain feeding but causes misting).

### H. Misting
- **Definition:** Formation of airborne ink droplets at high roller speeds.
- **Why it matters:** Causes health hazards, press fouling, and wastage.

### I. Set-off and Transfer Efficiency
- **Definition:** Fraction of ink transferred from the printing plate/roller to the substrate.
- **Typical transfer:** $50\%$–$85\%$ per nip in offset; approaches $100\%$ in gravure.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Property | Governing Equation | Variables & Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Newtonian Viscosity** | $\tau = \eta \cdot \dot{\gamma}$ | $\tau$ = shear stress (Pa); $\eta$ = viscosity (Pa·s); $\dot{\gamma}$ = shear rate (s⁻¹) | Ideal inks obey this, real inks deviate |
| 2 | **Bingham Plastic Model** | $\tau = \tau_0 + \eta_{pl} \cdot \dot{\gamma}$ | $\tau_0$ = yield stress (Pa); $\eta_{pl}$ = plastic viscosity (Pa·s) | Used for paste inks |
| 3 | **Power-Law (Ostwald-de Waele)** | $\eta = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{n-1}$ | $K$ = consistency index; $n$ = flow behavior index | $n<1$ = shear-thinning; $n>1$ = shear-thickening |
| 4 | **Casson Model (Print Inks)** | $\sqrt{\tau} = \sqrt{\tau_0} + \sqrt{\eta_\infty \cdot \dot{\gamma}}$ | $\eta_\infty$ = infinite-shear viscosity | **Standard for offset inks** |
| 5 | **Thixotropy Index** | $TI = \dfrac{\eta_{\text{low rpm}}}{\eta_{\text{high rpm}}}$ | Dimensionless ratio | $TI = 1$ → Newtonian; $TI > 1$ → thixotropic |
| 6 | **Tack Relationship** | $T \propto \dfrac{F}{v}$ | $F$ = splitting force (N); $v$ = roller velocity (m/s) | Empirical; measured on tack-o-meter |
| 7 | **Ink Transfer (Walker-Fetsko)** | $Y = \left[1 - e^{-KX}\right]$ | $Y$ = ink on paper; $X$ = ink on roller; $K$ = constant | Empirical transfer equation |
| 8 | **Apparent Viscosity** | $\eta_{app} = \dfrac{\tau_{app}}{\dot{\gamma}}$ | Pa·s | Depends on instrument shear rate |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Tip:** The **Casson Model** (Equation 4) is the most-tested rheological model for printing inks in KTU exams. Memorize the square-root form.

### Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Press design:** Roller diameters, speeds, and nip pressures are *sized* based on the ink's viscosity and tack — handling properties dictate machine design.
- **Substrate selection:** Highly tacky inks dictate the use of stronger (higher-bond) papers to avoid picking.
- **Process control:** Real-time viscometers on press feedback loops monitor handling drift due to solvent evaporation or temperature fluctuations.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Apparent Viscosity from Power-Law Model

**Starting Premise:** Many printing inks (especially flexographic and gravure inks) follow the **Ostwald-de Waele (Power-Law)** model because their viscosity drops smoothly with shear rate.

**Step 1 — Define the Power-Law shear stress relationship:**

$$
\tau = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{n}
$$

where $K$ = consistency index (Pa·sⁿ) and $n$ = flow behavior index (dimensionless).

**Step 2 — Apply the fundamental definition of apparent viscosity:**

$$
\eta_{app} = \frac{\tau}{\dot{\gamma}}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute Equation (1) into Equation (2):**

$$
\eta_{app} = \frac{K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{n}}{\dot{\gamma}}
$$

**Step 4 — Simplify the exponent algebraically:**

$$
\eta_{app} = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{(n-1)}
$$

**Step 5 — Interpret limiting cases (KTU Examiner's favorite):**

- If $n = 1$ → $\eta_{app} = K$ (Newtonian behavior; $K$ is now the constant viscosity)
- If $n < 1$ → $\eta_{app}$ **decreases** with $\dot{\gamma}$ → **shear-thinning (pseudoplastic)** — desired in most inks
- If $n > 1$ → $\eta_{app}$ **increases** with $\dot{\gamma}$ → **shear-thickening (dilatant)** — undesirable in printing

**Conversion logic:** Viscosity is defined as stress divided by shear rate; substituting the power-law stress gives a viscosity that itself depends on shear rate, quantifying the non-Newtonian character.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Tack & Picking Threshold

**Problem Statement:** A sheet-fed offset ink has a tack value of $T = 14$ (tack-o-meter units) at $25^{\circ}\text{C}$. The substrate paper has a picking resistance (surface strength) corresponding to a tack limit of $T_{lim} = 16$. A fountain solution additive reduces the effective tack by $\Delta T = 3$ units. Determine:

(a) The corrected ink tack after dampening solution compensation.
(b) The safety margin against picking.
(c) Whether printing is advisable at the current speed.

### Solution:

**(a) Corrected Tack:**

$$
T_{corrected} = T - \Delta T = 14 - 3 = 11
$$

**(b) Safety Margin:**

$$
\Delta T_{safety} = T_{lim} - T_{corrected} = 16 - 11 = 5 \text{ units}
$$

**(c) Verdict:** Since $T_{corrected} = 11 < T_{lim} = 16$, and the margin is **positive and substantial**, printing is *safe* — no fiber picking will occur.

**Engineering interpretation:** A safety margin of $\geq 3$ units is considered the KTU threshold for stable offset printing; a margin of **5 units** indicates robust operating conditions even with speed increases.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Walker-Fetsko Ink Transfer Equation

**Premise:** When ink transfers from a roller to a paper substrate, the fraction transferred is not 100%. Walker and Fetsko (1957) empirically derived:

**Step 1 — Write the differential transfer rate:**

$$
\frac{dy}{dx} = K \cdot (x - y)
$$

where $x$ = ink film thickness on roller, $y$ = ink film thickness on paper, $K$ = transfer constant (depends on ink and substrate).

**Step 2 — Separate variables:**

$$
\frac{dy}{x - y} = K \cdot dx
$$

**Step 3 — Integrate from $y = 0$ to $y = Y$ on paper and $x$ from $0$ to $X$ (roller thickness):**

$$
-\ln(x - y) \Big|_{0}^{Y} = K \cdot X
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate limits:**

$$
-\ln(x - Y) + \ln(x) = K \cdot X
$$

$$
\ln\left(\frac{x}{x - Y}\right) = K \cdot X
$$

**Step 5 — Exponentiate both sides:**

$$
\frac{x}{x - Y} = e^{KX}
$$

**Step 6 — Solve for $Y$ (final ink on substrate):**

$$
Y = x \left(1 - e^{-KX}\right)
$$

**Final Form:**

$$
\boxed{Y = X \cdot \left[1 - e^{-KX}\right]}
$$

**Conversion logic:** This equation reveals that **transfer efficiency is asymptotic** — even at infinite roller ink film thickness, only a fraction transfers, because paper absorbs ink and surface forces hold back the rest.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Handling Property Selection by Process

| Process | Viscosity (Pa·s) | Tack Range | Thixotropy | Yield Stress | Rationale |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Lithographic (Offset)** | $5$–$50$ | $10$–$18$ | Moderate | Low | Fountain solution wets plate; needs body |
| **Letterpress** | $10$–$80$ | $14$–$22$ | Low | Moderate | High impression forces |
| **Gravure** | $0.05$–$0.5$ | $2$–$6$ | Very low | Near zero | Engraved cells must empty |
| **Flexography** | $0.05$–$0.5$ | $2$–$8$ | Very low | Near zero | Anilox cells + fluid ink |
| **Screen Printing** | $5$–$50$ | Low | High | High | Must sit on stencil without dripping |
| **News Ink** | $0.5$–$5$ | $1$–$4$ | Moderate | Very low | High-speed, cold-set, porous paper |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of Ink Handling in a Printing Press

The following Mermaid block-diagram maps the **functional flow of handling properties** through a generic multi-roller printing press. Each node represents a *handling checkpoint* where specific rheological behavior is exercised.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Duct Ink Reservoir] --> B[Ink Pickup Roller]
    B --> C[Distributor Roller 1]
    C --> D[Distributor Roller 2]
    D --> E[Form Roller]
    E --> F[Plate Cylinder]
    F --> G[Impression Nip]
    G --> H[Substrate Paper]
    H --> I[Drying Zone]
    I --> J[Finished Print]

    A -- "Body & Yield Stress tested here" --> A
    B -- "Tack & Viscosity tested here" --> B
    C -- "Shear-thinning activated" --> C
    D -- "Thixotropy recovery begins" --> D
    E -- "Film metering" --> E
    F -- "Transfer efficiency tested here" --> F
    G -- "Splitting dynamics" --> G
    H -- "Set-off resistance tested" --> H
    I -- "Final drying kinetics" --> I
```

---

## 4.2 Rheological Behavior Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Newtonian ["Newtonian Fluids"]
        N1[Water]
        N2[Mineral Oil]
        N3[Low-Viscosity Gravure Ink]
    end

    subgraph Pseudoplastic ["Pseudoplastic (Shear-Thinning)"]
        P1[Offset Ink]
        P2[Flexographic Ink]
        P3[Pigment Dispersions]
    end

    subgraph Bingham ["Bingham Plastic"]
        B1[Paste Inks]
        B2[Screen Inks]
        B3[Letterpress Ink]
    end

    subgraph Thixotropic ["Thixotropic"]
        T1[Letterpress Inks]
        T2[Some Offset Inks]
        T3[News Inks]
    end

    Newtonian -->|Add pigment| Pseudoplastic
    Newtonian -->|Add yield stress| Bingham
    Pseudoplastic -->|Add time-dependence| Thixotropic
    Bingham -->|Add time-dependence| Thixotropic
```

---

## 4.3 Tack — Picking — Failure Cascade

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Excess Tack T > T_limit] --> B0[Fiber Picking Initiated]
    B0 --> C0[Paper Surface Disrupted]
    C0 --> D0[Image Defect Visible]
    D0 --> E0[Print Rejected]
    A0 --> F0[Alternative: Reduce Tack with Fountain Solution]
    F0 --> G0[Verify T < T_limit]
    G0 --> H0[Production Resumed]
```

---

## 4.4 Ink Film Splitting at the Impression Nip

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A0a[Roller Surface] -->|Ink Film X| B0a[Contact Zone]
    B0a -->|Split A| C0a[To Substrate]
    B0a -->|Split B| D0a[Returns to Roller]
    C0a --> E0a[Final Ink Y on Paper]
    D0a --> F0a[Re-distributed on Roller]
    F0a -.-> A0a
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** At the impression nip, the ink film **splits into two unequal fractions**. The portion going to the substrate is the *ink transfer*; the remaining portion recirculates. The split ratio is governed by tack, viscosity, and substrate absorbency.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define "Tack" of a printing ink. How is it measured, and why is it critical for handling?**

**Model Answer:**
Tack is the resistance offered by an ink film to splitting when subjected to a tensile force at the nip between two rollers. It is measured using a **tack-o-meter**, which simulates roller splitting at standard speed (typically **400 rpm**) and temperature (**30°C**). It is critical because excess tack causes **paper picking** (lifting of fibers from the substrate), while insufficient tack causes **misting** (ink droplet ejection into the air) and **poor transfer**.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**What is thixotropy? Explain with reference to printing ink handling.**

**Model Answer:**
Thixotropy is the time-dependent decrease in viscosity under constant shear, followed by gradual recovery when shear is removed. In printing ink handling, thixotropy allows the ink to **flow freely under the high shear** of the roller train (viscosity drops), and then **rebuild structure** after impression, preventing dripping and promoting sharp halftone dot definition. It is quantified by the **thixotropy index** (low-rpm viscosity divided by high-rpm viscosity on a Brookfield viscometer).

---

## 5.2 Part B — ESE Module Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

#### (a) Explain in detail the various rheological properties that govern the "handling" of a printing ink. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

The handling of a printing ink is governed by **five primary rheological properties**:

1. **Viscosity ($\eta$):** Internal friction of the ink. Determined by varnish molecular weight, pigment loading, and temperature. Measured in Pa·s.
2. **Tack ($T$):** Resistance to film splitting. Critical at impression nip; measured in tack-o-meter units.
3. **Yield Value ($\tau_0$):** Minimum shear stress required for flow. Prevents ink from dripping in duct and from slinging off fast rollers.
4. **Thixotropy:** Time-dependent shear thinning. Quantified by thixotropy index.
5. **Flow Behavior Index ($n$):** Indicates pseudoplasticity. For inks, $n$ typically lies between **0.3 and 0.7**.

These properties are *not independent*. Tack and viscosity are coupled through the **Casson equation**:

$$
\sqrt{\tau} = \sqrt{\tau_0} + \sqrt{\eta_\infty \cdot \dot{\gamma}}
$$

which is the standard rheological model endorsed by the **International Printing Ink Association (IPIA)** for offset inks.

#### (b) Compare the handling requirements of offset, gravure, and flexographic inks. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

| Property | Offset Ink | Gravure Ink | Flexographic Ink |
|---|---|---|---|
| Viscosity | $5$–$50$ Pa·s | $0.05$–$0.5$ Pa·s | $0.05$–$0.5$ Pa·s |
| Tack | High ($10$–$18$) | Very low ($2$–$6$) | Low ($2$–$8$) |
| Yield value | Moderate | Near zero | Near zero |
| Solvent | Mineral oil / vegetable oil | Toluene / ethyl acetate | Water / alcohol |
| Thixotropy | Moderate | Minimal | Minimal |
| Drying | Oxidative / oxidative-UV | Evaporative | Evaporative |

**Reasoning:** Offset requires high tack and viscosity because the ink must resist the **emulsifying action of fountain solution** while maintaining sharp halftone dots. Gravure and flexography use **low-viscosity, low-tack inks** because they must empty from engraved or anilox cells under gravity and capillary action; high tack would prevent cell emptying.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

#### (a) What is the Walker-Fetsko ink transfer equation? Derive it and explain its significance in printing ink handling. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

The Walker-Fetsko equation describes the fraction of ink that transfers from a roller (or plate) to a paper substrate during a single impression. The derivation proceeds as follows:

**Step 1 — Empirical starting relation:**

$$
\frac{dy}{dx} = K(x - y)
$$

**Step 2 — Separation of variables:**

$$
\frac{dy}{x - y} = K \, dx
$$

**Step 3 — Integration (from $y=0$ to $Y$, $x=0$ to $X$):**

$$
\int_0^Y \frac{dy}{x - y} = \int_0^X K \, dx
$$

**Step 4 — Integration yields:**

$$
-\ln(x - Y) + \ln(x) = K X
$$

**Step 5 — Exponentiating and solving for $Y$:**

$$
\frac{x}{x - Y} = e^{KX} \quad \Rightarrow \quad Y = X \left[1 - e^{-KX}\right]
$$

**Final Form:**

$$
\boxed{Y = X \left[1 - e^{-KX}\right]}
$$

**Significance:** This equation reveals that **ink transfer is asymptotic** — even with infinite roller ink film thickness ($X \to \infty$), the ink on paper approaches $Y = X$ but never exceeds it. The transfer constant $K$ depends on ink viscosity, substrate absorbency, and nip pressure. Lower $K$ means poorer transfer; higher $K$ means more efficient transfer. For handling purposes, this dictates the **ink film thickness on the form roller** needed to achieve the desired print density.

#### (b) Explain the phenomena of "misting" and "set-off" in printing inks. How are they related to handling properties? (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Misting:** Misting is the formation of fine airborne ink droplets when ink films split at high speed in the roller nip. It occurs when ink **filament extension** exceeds a critical length, causing the filament to rupture into droplets rather than split cleanly. It is enhanced by:
- Excessively low viscosity
- Excessively high tack
- High roller speeds
- Long ink (high extensional viscosity)

**Set-off:** Set-off is the unwanted transfer of wet ink from the freshly printed surface to an adjacent surface (such as the back of the next sheet in the delivery pile). It is governed by:
- Slow drying rate
- High ink film thickness
- Insufficient ink set-off resistance additives (e.g., starch spray powder)

**Relationship to Handling:** Both phenomena are *direct consequences* of mishandled rheology. Proper handling — through formulation with correct viscosity, tack, and drying kinetics — minimizes both. Anti-misting agents (waxes, special surfactants) and anti-set-off sprays are the practical control mechanisms in pressrooms.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Handling Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing viscosity with tack.** Viscosity is resistance to *flow*; tack is resistance to *splitting*. Examiners deduct 2–3 marks when students interchange these terms.
> 2. **Forgetting units.** Always write $\eta$ in Pa·s (or Poise), $\dot{\gamma}$ in s⁻¹, and $T$ in tack-o-meter units (dimensionless). Bare numbers lose marks.
> 3. **Skipping the shear rate context.** Offset press nip ≈ $10^{5}$ s⁻¹; flexo ≈ $10^{4}$ s⁻¹; gravity flow ≈ $10^{1}$ s⁻¹. Mentioning shear-rate relevance shows depth and earns **bonus marks**.
> 4. **Incomplete Casson model.** If you write $\tau = \tau_0 + \eta \dot{\gamma}$, that is *Bingham*, not Casson. Casson has the **square-root form**. Examiners catch this.
> 5. **No diagram in 14-mark answers.** A labeled rheology curve (shear stress vs. shear rate) earns **1–2 bonus marks** if drawn correctly.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Handling of Printing Inks**

- ⭐ **Handling** = the operational behavior of ink during press use, governed by rheology, mechanics, and kinetics.
- ⭐ **Key rheological properties:** Viscosity ($\eta$), Tack ($T$), Yield value ($\tau_0$), Thixotropy, Flow index ($n$).
- ⭐ **Standard rheological models:** Power-law (flexo), Bingham (paste), **Casson (offset)** — Casson is the most tested.
- ⭐ **Casson equation:** $\sqrt{\tau} = \sqrt{\tau_0} + \sqrt{\eta_\infty \dot{\gamma}}$ — memorize the square-root form.
- ⭐ **Walker-Fetsko transfer equation:** $Y = X[1 - e^{-KX}]$ — asymptotic transfer.
- ⭐ **Viscosity range by process:** Offset (high) > Letterpress (high) > Flexo/Gravure (low).
- ⭐ **Tack unit:** Tack-o-meter; safe range for offset = $10$–$18$; margin from picking limit should be $\geq 3$.
- ⭐ **Thixotropy index:** $\eta_{\text{low rpm}} / \eta_{\text{high rpm}} > 1$ for structured inks.
- ⭐ **Misting & set-off** are *handling failures* — controlled by tack, viscosity, drying rate.
- ⭐ **Shear rate context:** Offset $10^{5}$ s⁻¹, Flexo $10^{4}$ s⁻¹, Gravity $10^{1}$ s⁻¹ — always anchor properties to shear environment.
- ⭐ **Press shear rate window:** $10^{3}$–$10^{6}$ s⁻¹ — governs ink design.
- ⭐ **Flow index $n$:** $n < 1$ = shear-thinning (good), $n > 1$ = shear-thickening (bad).
- ⭐ **Two pillars of handling failure:** (1) Excess tack → picking; (2) Excess viscosity → poor transfer.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Storage and Manufacture of UV Inks
Manufacture of Newspaper Inks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT866 - TECHNOLOGY OF PRINTING INKS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Manufacture of Newspaper Inks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Newspaper Ink** is a specialized, low-tack, fast-setting liquid printing ink formulated primarily for high-speed web offset (coldset) and letterpress printing on absorbent newsprint stock.
