As per KTU 2024 Scheme (PEPOT866) Module 3 conventions, the *manufacture of newspaper inks* refers to the engineered sequence of unit operations — pre-mixing, milling/dispersion, let-down, filtration, and quality certification — by which carbon black pigment is uniformly dispersed into a mineral-oil-based vehicle to produce a homogeneous, rheologically stable black ink optimized for press speeds of **15,000 – 60,000 impressions/hour**.

The finished ink is a **non-drying, absorption-drying colloidal system** (Newtonian or near-Newtonian) characterized by:
- Pigment loading: **10 – 18 %** (predominantly Carbon Black, C.I. Pigment Black 7)
- Vehicle: **70 – 85 %** mineral oil (IP 15 – IP 30 grade, viscosity 3 – 10 mPa·s at 40 °C)
- Resin/binder: **5 – 15 %** (hydrocarbon resin, phenolic-modified, or gilsonite)
- Additives: **1 – 3 %** (dispersants, anti-oxidants, rheology modifiers)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Newspaper inks are unique because they do **NOT** dry by oxidation or evaporation in the conventional sense. They set almost exclusively by **capillary absorption** into the porous newsprint substrate. This single fact dictates the entire manufacturing logic — low viscosity, low tack, and absence of driers.

## 1.2 Intuitive Conceptual Analogy

Think of newspaper ink manufacturing like making **filter coffee at industrial scale** ☕:

| Coffee Analogy | Newspaper Ink Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Coffee powder (soluble solids) | Carbon black pigment (insoluble solids) |
| Hot water (extracts flavor) | Mineral oil vehicle (carries pigment into paper) |
| Sugar/milk additives | Resins & surfactants (modify flow, adhesion) |
| Decoction filter (separates grounds) | Bead mill / 3-roll mill (breaks pigment agglomerates) |
| Final decoction strength check | Viscosity & color strength QC |

Just as you must grind coffee beans finely and extract uniformly to get a strong, smooth decoction, ink manufacturers must **break down carbon-black aggregates** (primary particles are 10–100 nm but clumps reach 50–500 µm) into a stable colloidal dispersion. Incomplete dispersion = streaky, weak, press-fouling ink. Over-grinding = wasted energy and altered rheology.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why so much emphasis on carbon black?** Because **> 95 %** of all newspapers globally are printed in black-and-white using carbon-black–based ink. The optical density demanded (typically 1.30 – 1.60 on newsprint) requires intense pigment loading balanced against printability.

---

# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Raw Materials — The "ABC" of Newspaper Ink

### A. Pigment: Carbon Black
- **Function:** Provides the black color and optical density.
- **Grade used:** **Furnace Black** (ASTM N550, N660, N762) — semi-reinforcing, structured, with high oil absorption.
- **Properties:** Particle size 30–80 nm; surface area 30–100 m²/g (BET); volatile content 1.0–1.5 %; tinting strength 80–110 %.
- **Why this grade?** Furnace black offers the optimum balance of blackness, dispersibility, and economy for newsprint.

### B. Vehicle: Mineral Oil
- **Function:** Carrier fluid — transports pigment into paper capillaries.
- **Grades:** IP 15 (low viscosity, ~3 mPa·s) to IP 30 (high viscosity, ~10 mPa·s).
- **Aniline Point:** **65 – 90 °C** (higher = better solvency for resins; for coldset inks, a moderate aniline point of 70–80 °C is ideal).
- **Key specification:** **Low aromatic content** (< 2 % by mass) to minimize newspaper odor and skin irritation for press operators.

### C. Resin/Binder
- **Function:** Provides body, reduces penetration, and binds pigment to paper fibers.
- **Types used:**
  * **Hydrocarbon resins** (C5/C9 petroleum resins) — most common
  * **Gilsonite** (natural asphaltite) — historically used, still in some formulations
  * **Phenolic-modified rosin esters** — for better gloss in heatset variants
- **Softening point:** **80 – 120 °C** (Ring & Ball).

### D. Additives
- **Dispersants / Wetting agents:** Fatty acid esters, lecithin (0.5–1.0 %).
- **Anti-oxidants:** Hindered phenols (e.g., BHT) — 0.1–0.3 %.
- **Rheology modifiers:** Organoclays (rare in coldset inks).
- **Driers:** **Generally ABSENT** in coldset newspaper inks (no metallic driers).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Memory Trick — "CAMP":** **C**arbon black, **A**borrption oil, **M**odifier resin, **P**lus additives. Every newspaper ink formulation follows the CAMP ratio.

## 2.2 The Manufacturing Unit Operations — Step-by-Step Logic

The manufacture is a **four-stage sequential process**:

### Stage 1 — Pre-mix (Wetting Stage)
- All raw materials are charged into a **high-speed disperser** (cowles-type blade, 1500–3000 rpm).
- Purpose: To wet out the carbon black agglomerates with the mineral oil vehicle.
- **Logic (Why):** Dry carbon black is extremely dusty and has air-filled voids. The oil must displace this air to enable subsequent milling. Wetting is governed by the **Washburn equation**:

$$h^2 = \frac{\gamma \cdot r \cdot \cos\theta \cdot t}{2\eta}$$

Where:
- $h$ = depth of liquid penetration into capillary
- $\gamma$ = surface tension of oil (N/m)
- $r$ = capillary radius of pigment agglomerate (m)
- $\theta$ = contact angle
- $t$ = time (s)
- $\eta$ = oil viscosity (Pa·s)

### Stage 2 — Milling / Dispersion (The Critical Stage)
- The pre-mix is passed through a **bead mill** (modern) or **three-roll mill** (legacy).
- Function: To mechanically break agglomerates down to **< 10 µm** (typically 5–15 µm Hegman gauge reading: 6.0–7.5).
- **Mechanism:** Shear + impact forces from beads (zirconium oxide, 0.6–1.2 mm diameter).
- Energy input: **50 – 150 kWh per tonne of ink** depending on desired fineness.

### Stage 3 — Let-down & Adjustment
- Mill base is diluted with remaining vehicle and additives to target viscosity and color strength.
- Viscosity target: **8 – 25 Pa·s** at 25 °C (Brookfield, spindle 6, 100 rpm).
- Tack: **3 – 8 g·m** (Tack-o-scope at 400 rpm, 30 °C).

### Stage 4 — Filtration & Quality Certification
- Straining through **100–200 µm** bag filters to remove oversized particles.
- QC tests: viscosity, tack, color strength, fineness of grind, specific gravity, drying/setting rate.
- Then packaged in **200 L drums, 20 L pails, or IBC totes (1000 L)**.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Parameter | Formula | Units | Typical Range (Newspaper Ink) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Pigment Volume Concentration (PVC) | $$ PVC = \frac{V_{\text{pigment}}}{V_{\text{pigment}} + V_{\text{binder}}} \times 100 $$ | % | 5 – 18 |
| Oil Absorption (OA) | $$ OA = \frac{m_{\text{linseed oil}}}{m_{\text{pigment}}} \times 100 $$ | g/100g | 100 – 180 (for furnace black) |
| Hegman Fineness | $$ H = 8 - \frac{d_{\max}}{12.7\,\mu m} \cdot \frac{1}{1} $$ (approx.) | Hegman units | 6.0 – 7.5 |
| Viscosity (kinematic) | $$ \nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho} $$ | mm²/s | 30 – 100 |
| Yield Value (Bingham) | $$\tau = \tau_0 + \eta_p \cdot \dot{\gamma}$$ | Pa | $\tau_0 < 5$ Pa (near-Newtonian) |
| Specific Gravity | $$ SG = \frac{\rho_{\text{ink}}}{\rho_{\text{water}}} $$ | – | 1.00 – 1.10 |
| Mass Balance (overall yield) | $$ Y = \frac{m_{\text{finished ink}}}{m_{\text{raw materials}}} \times 100 $$ | % | 98 – 99.5 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical reminder from Section II:** When writing $\tau_0$ or absolute values like $\vert \tau_0 \vert$, use **\vert** or **\mid** in markdown tables. The vertical pipe symbol $\vert$ breaks the table parser.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The exact manufacturing know-how of newspaper ink underpins:
- **Press uptime economics:** A well-dispersed ink can run 8+ hours without wash-up.
- **Set-off (offset) reduction:** Critical for newspaper stacking.
- **Rub resistance:** Smudge-free reading matter.
- **Print contrast ratio:** Determines newsprint readability under fluorescent light.

---

# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Worked Calculation 1 — Formulation Mass Balance

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement:** A batch of 1,000 kg of coldset newspaper ink is to be manufactured with the following composition: Carbon Black 14 %, Mineral Oil 78 %, Hydrocarbon Resin 7 %, Additives 1 %. The actual raw-material losses during filtration and handling are 1.2 % of the carbon black input and 0.4 % of the vehicle input. Determine the raw material quantities required.

### Step 1 — Define target finished mass
$$m_{\text{finished}} = 1000 \text{ kg}$$

### Step 2 — Express target composition as absolute masses
$$m_{\text{CB, in ink}} = 0.14 \times 1000 = 140 \text{ kg}$$
$$m_{\text{oil, in ink}} = 0.78 \times 1000 = 780 \text{ kg}$$
$$m_{\text{resin, in ink}} = 0.07 \times 1000 = 70 \text{ kg}$$
$$m_{\text{add, in ink}} = 0.01 \times 1000 = 10 \text{ kg}$$

### Step 3 — Apply loss factors
Carbon black loss:
$$L_{\text{CB}} = 0.012 \times m_{\text{CB, charged}}$$
Since loss occurs on charged quantity:
$$m_{\text{CB, charged}} - 0.012 \cdot m_{\text{CB, charged}} = 140 \text{ kg}$$
$$m_{\text{CB, charged}} \times (1 - 0.012) = 140$$
$$m_{\text{CB, charged}} = \frac{140}{0.988} = 141.7 \text{ kg}$$

Mineral oil loss:
$$m_{\text{oil, charged}} = \frac{780}{1 - 0.004} = \frac{780}{0.996} = 783.13 \text{ kg}$$

Resin and additives (assume negligible loss):
$$m_{\text{resin, charged}} = 70 \text{ kg}, \quad m_{\text{add, charged}} = 10 \text{ kg}$$

### Step 4 — Total raw material required
$$m_{\text{total charged}} = 141.7 + 783.13 + 70 + 10 = 1004.83 \text{ kg}$$

### Step 5 — Verification of overall yield
$$Y = \frac{1000}{1004.83} \times 100 = 99.52\,\%$$

This matches the KTU-acceptable industrial range of 98–99.5 %.

## 3.2 Worked Calculation 2 — Hegman Gauge Interpretation

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A finished ink is tested on a Hegman gauge and reads **6.5 Hegman**. Convert this to (a) maximum particle size in micrometres and (b) classify the dispersion quality per KTU bench standards.

### Step 1 — Apply the standard ASTM D1210 conversion
The Hegman scale runs 0–8 (Hegman), with the groove depth linearly increasing from 100 µm at 0H to 0 µm at 8H over a length of 140 mm. The empirical formula:
$$d_{\max}\,(\mu m) = 100 - 12.5 \cdot H$$

For $H = 6.5$:
$$d_{\max} = 100 - 12.5 \times 6.5 = 100 - 81.25 = 18.75\,\mu m$$

### Step 2 — Classify per KTU/industry standards

| Hegman Reading | Max Particle Size | KTU Classification |
|---|---|---|
| 0 – 2 | 100 – 75 µm | Unacceptable — re-mill |
| 2 – 5 | 75 – 37.5 µm | Poor — coarse dispersion |
| 5 – 6 | 37.5 – 25 µm | Acceptable for low-grade newsprint |
| 6 – 7 | 25 – 12.5 µm | **Good — meets standard newspaper spec** |
| 7 – 8 | 12.5 – 0 µm | Excellent — premium heatset / offset |

Therefore, **6.5 Hegman = 18.75 µm = "Good" classification, fully compliant for coldset web offset newsprint.**

## 3.3 Worked Calculation 3 — PVC and CPVC Conceptual Check

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A newspaper ink has the following volumetric composition: Carbon black = 18 % (density 1.85 g/cm³), Resin binder = 14 % (density 1.05 g/cm³), Oil = 68 % (density 0.88 g/cm³). Calculate the PVC. Given CPVC of carbon black = 35 %, is the formulation under-CPVC or over-CPVC?

### Step 1 — Compute specific volumes
$$V_{\text{CB}} = \frac{18}{1.85} = 9.73 \text{ cm}^3 \text{ (per 100 g ink)}$$
$$V_{\text{resin}} = \frac{14}{1.05} = 13.33 \text{ cm}^3$$
$$V_{\text{oil}} = \frac{68}{0.88} = 77.27 \text{ cm}^3$$

### Step 2 — Apply PVC formula
$$PVC = \frac{V_{\text{CB}}}{V_{\text{CB}} + V_{\text{resin}}} \times 100$$
$$PVC = \frac{9.73}{9.73 + 13.33} \times 100 = \frac{9.73}{23.06} \times 100 = 42.19\,\%$$

### Step 3 — Compare to CPVC
Since $PVC = 42.19\% > CPVC = 35\%$, the formulation is **over-CPVC**.

### Step 4 — Engineering interpretation
Over-CPVC means **excess vehicle** is present — oil fills both binder spaces AND inter-particle voids. This gives:
- Lower viscosity (good for press)
- High capillary absorption (good for coldset drying)
- But: **higher set-off tendency** (ink transfers to the back of the overlapping sheet in the reel).

This is exactly why newspaper inks are deliberately formulated slightly over-CPVC — fast absorption is paramount.

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Equipment Selection

| Equipment | Throughput (kg/h) | Max Fineness (Hegman) | Energy (kWh/t) | Suitability for Newspaper Ink |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| High-speed disperser | 2000 – 8000 | 2 – 3 | 5 – 10 | Pre-mix only |
| Three-roll mill | 50 – 500 | 7.5 – 8 | 80 – 150 | Legacy / small batch |
| Horizontal bead mill | 500 – 5000 | 7.0 – 7.5 | 50 – 120 | **Industry standard today** |
| Vertical bead mill | 100 – 1500 | 7.0 – 7.5 | 60 – 130 | Mid-scale plants |
| Basket mill | 200 – 2000 | 6.5 – 7.0 | 40 – 100 | Batch operations |

---

# 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Process Flow Diagram — Newspaper Ink Manufacture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Material Receiving] --> B[Stage 1: Pre-mix Tank]
    A1[Carbon Black Silos] --> B
    A2[Mineral Oil Day Tank] --> B
    A3[Resin Melt Tank] --> B
    A4[Additive Dosing Unit] --> B
    B --> C[High Speed Cowles Disperser 1500-3000 rpm]
    C --> D[Stage 2: Holding Tank for Pre-mix]
    D --> E[Stage 3: Bead Mill Horizontal Type]
    E --> F[Stage 4: Let-down Tank with Slow Stirrer]
    A2 --> F
    A4 --> F
    F --> G[Stage 5: Bag Filter 100-200 micron]
    G --> H[Stage 6: QC Sampling Station]
    H --> I{Conformance Check}
    I -->|Pass| J[Stage 7: Storage Tank 5000-20000 L]
    I -->|Fail| K[Rework Loop back to Bead Mill]
    J --> L[Stage 8: Drum or IBC Filling Line]
    L --> M[Finished Goods Warehouse]
    K --> E
```

## 4.2 Raw Material–Process–Quality Interaction Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    RM1[Carbon Black N550 Furnace] --> PM[Pre-mix Stage]
    RM2[Mineral Oil IP 20] --> PM
    RM3[Hydrocarbon Resin] --> PM
    RM4[Additives Package] --> PM
    PM --> ML[Bead Mill Dispersion]
    ML --> LD[Let-down Adjustment]
    LD --> FT[Filtration]
    FT --> QC[Quality Control Tests]
    QC --> Q1[Viscosity 8-25 Pa.s]
    QC --> Q2[Tack 3-8 g.m]
    QC --> Q3[Hegman 6.0-7.5]
    QC --> Q4[Color Strength 95-105 percent]
    QC --> Q5[Specific Gravity 1.00-1.10]
    QC --> PKG[Packaging]
    PKG --> DIS[Dispatch to Newspaper Press]
```

## 4.3 Functional Block Architecture — Coldset vs Heatset Newspaper Ink Line

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph COLD[Coldset Newspaper Ink Line]
        C1[Low Viscosity Oil IP 15-20] --> C2[No Drier Added]
        C2 --> C3[Drying by Capillary Absorption]
        C3 --> C4[Press Speed 45000-60000 IPH]
    end
    subgraph HEAT[Heatset Newspaper Ink Line]
        H1[High Viscosity Oil IP 25-30] --> H2[Resin Enriched 10-15 percent]
        H2 --> H3[Drying by Hot Air Oven 200-250 degree C]
        H3 --> H4[Press Speed 30000-50000 IPH]
        H4 --> H5[Higher Gloss and Rub Resistance]
    end
    COLD -.Contrasts with.-> HEAT
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Safety Applied:** All node IDs use alphanumeric prefixes (`A1`, `PM`, `ML`, `H1` etc.) and labels contain only raw uppercase alphanumeric text — no bold, italic, or markdown formatting tags inside double-quoted labels, ensuring Mermaid compiler safety.

---

# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]
**"List the major raw materials used in the manufacture of coldset newspaper ink. Why are metallic driers deliberately omitted from these formulations?"** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**
1. **Raw materials (1.5 marks):** Carbon black (furnace grade, 10–18 %); mineral oil (IP 15–30 grade, 70–85 %); hydrocarbon resin or gilsonite (5–15 %); and small amounts of dispersant + antioxidant (1–3 %).
2. **Driers absent because (1.5 marks):** Coldset newspaper inks set by **capillary absorption into newsprint**, not by oxidative polymerization. Metallic driers (Co, Mn, Pb naphthenates) are designed to catalyze oxidation — their inclusion would (a) cause skin-formation in the can, (b) foul rollers and blankets, and (c) offer no functional benefit since the substrate is non-coated and the press cycle is seconds.

### Question 2 — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]
**"Define Hegman gauge reading. State the typical Hegman range specified for finished newspaper ink."** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**
- **Definition (2 marks):** The Hegman gauge (ASTM D1210) is a tapered groove precision steel bar used to measure the **fineness of grind** (maximum particle size) of a printing ink. The reading is taken as the point (0–8 scale) at which a continuous streak of particles first becomes visible when the ink is drawn down the groove with a scraper.
- **Typical range (1 mark):** **6.0 – 7.5 Hegman** for finished newspaper ink, corresponding to a maximum particle size of **< 25 µm**.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### Question A — [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]

> **"With the aid of a process flow diagram, describe the industrial manufacture of coldset newspaper ink. Highlight the critical control parameters at each stage."** *(14 marks)*

**Sub-part (a) — Process Description and Flow (7 marks):**

The manufacture proceeds through **four sequential unit operations**:

1. **Pre-mixing (2 marks):** Carbon black, mineral oil, and resin are charged into a high-speed disperser (Cowles blade, 1500–3000 rpm). The oil wets out pigment agglomerates, displacing entrained air (governed by the Washburn capillary equation). Pre-mix time: 30–60 minutes. Temperature: < 50 °C to avoid oil flash.

2. **Milling / Dispersion (2 marks):** Pre-mix is fed into a horizontal bead mill charged with 0.6–1.2 mm zirconium oxide beads. Shear and impact forces reduce carbon-black agglomerates from 50–500 µm down to < 25 µm. Residence time: 20–45 minutes. Mill base is recirculated until Hegman ≥ 6.0 is achieved.

3. **Let-down (1.5 marks):** Mill base is diluted with balance oil and additives in a slow-stirring let-down tank to target viscosity (8–25 Pa·s) and tack (3–8 g·m).

4. **Filtration & QC (1.5 marks):** Bag filtration (100–200 µm) removes oversized contaminants. Final QC: viscosity, tack, color strength, fineness, specific gravity.

**Sub-part (b) — Critical Control Parameters (7 marks):**

| Stage | Critical Parameter | Target Range | Deviation Consequence |
|---|---|---|---|
| Pre-mix | Blade tip speed | 20–30 m/s | Poor wetting → milling inefficiency |
| Pre-mix | Temperature | < 50 °C | Oil evaporation, fire hazard |
| Bead mill | Bead size | 0.6–1.2 mm | Larger beads → coarse grind |
| Bead mill | Residence time | 20–45 min | Under-milling → low Hegman |
| Bead mill | Energy input | 50–150 kWh/t | Over-milling → viscosity drift |
| Let-down | Viscosity | 8–25 Pa·s | High → misting; low → set-off |
| Let-down | Tack | 3–8 g·m | High → picking; low → mottle |
| Filtration | Mesh size | 100–200 µm | Larger → press roller damage |

---

### Question B — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]

> **"Compare coldset and heatset newspaper ink manufacture. Discuss the raw material differences, drying mechanism, and finished-ink properties."** *(14 marks)*

**Sub-part (a) — Raw Material Differences (7 marks):**

| Component | Coldset Ink | Heatset Ink |
|---|---|---|
| Mineral oil grade | IP 15–20 (low viscosity, 3–7 mPa·s) | IP 25–30 (higher viscosity, 8–12 mPa·s) |
| Resin content | 5–10 % | 10–15 % (phenolic-modified for gloss) |
| Resin type | Gilsonite or low-softening hydrocarbon | Phenolic/rosin-modified ester |
| Pigment load | 10–14 % | 12–18 % |
| Vehicle % | 80–85 % | 70–78 % |
| Driers | None | None (still none — both rely on physical set) |
| Waxes | Optional (0.5 %) | Compulsory (1–2 % for rub resistance) |

**Sub-part (b) — Drying Mechanism and Finished Properties (7 marks):**

1. **Coldset drying (3 marks):** Pure **capillary absorption** — the low-viscosity oil penetrates the porous newsprint substrate within 1–3 seconds. No oven. No evaporation. Set-off onto the reel is the main challenge. Surface stays dry but **inside remains wet for hours** (ink is "set, not dry").

2. **Heatset drying (2 marks):** Vehicle is partly absorbed AND partly evaporated by passing the web through a **gas-fired hot-air oven at 200–250 °C**. Web residence time in oven: ~1 second. The residual resin forms a solid film (true drying).

3. **Finished-ink property differences (2 marks):**

| Property | Coldset | Heatset |
|---|---|---|
| Gloss | Very low (matte) | Medium to high |
| Rub resistance | Poor | Good |
| Set-off | High | Low |
| Press speed | Higher (50,000 IPH) | Slightly lower (30,000 IPH) |
| Print quality | Coarser | Sharper, half-tones possible |
| Cost per kg | Lower | Higher (oven energy + waxes) |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. Students commonly lose marks by writing *"newspaper ink dries by evaporation"* — **this is WRONG for coldset**. Coldset dries by **absorption only**. Heatset uses evaporation, but the exam question must be read carefully.
> 2. Do NOT confuse **"set"** with **"dry"**. A newspaper ink is set within 1–3 seconds but is not fully dry for days.
> 3. In Hegman gauge calculations, ensure you apply the **correct conversion formula** ($d = 100 - 12.5H$), not the inverse.
> 4. Always state **units** (Pa·s, µm, g·m) explicitly — failure to do so costs 0.5–1 mark per sub-question.
> 5. Mass-balance problems must clearly distinguish **"charged mass"** from **"finished mass"** — examiners test this distinction.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Newsprint ink = Coldset = Carbon black + Mineral oil + Resin (CAMP).**
- **Drying mechanism:** Capillary absorption, NOT oxidation or evaporation (coldset).
- **Hegman fineness target:** 6.0 – 7.5 (< 25 µm).
- **Viscosity:** 8 – 25 Pa·s @ 25 °C. **Tack:** 3 – 8 g·m @ 30 °C / 400 rpm.
- **Manufacturing stages:** Pre-mix → Bead mill → Let-down → Filtration → QC → Packaging.
- **Critical equipment today:** Horizontal bead mill with 0.6–1.2 mm zirconia beads.
- **No metallic driers** are used in coldset newspaper inks.
- **Over-CPVC formulation** is intentional (drives fast absorption).
- **Aniline point** of mineral oil must be 65–90 °C.
- **Yield of overall process:** 98 – 99.5 %.
- **Particle size of carbon black:** 30–80 nm primary; **must be dispersed** down to < 25 µm.
- **Packaging sizes:** 200 L drums, 20 L pails, IBC totes (1000 L).
- **Press speeds supported:** 15,000 – 60,000 IPH.
- **Washburn equation** governs wetting physics in pre-mix.
- **PVC vs CPVC** check is a favourite KTU numerical question.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Handling and Storage of Inks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT866 - TECHNOLOGY OF PRINTING INKS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Handling and Storage of Inks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Handling and Storage of Inks** refers to the systematic set of industrial procedures, environmental controls, and material-handling practices employed at the ink manufacturing plant, the converter's premises, and the press room to preserve the **rheological, optical, and chemical integrity** of finished printing inks from the moment of manufacture until the moment of application on the substrate.

In strict KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, ink handling covers the **pre-press preparation sequence** (homogenisation, viscosity adjustment, colour matching, straining), while ink storage covers the **environmental conditioning regime** (temperature: **20 °C ± 5 °C**, relative humidity: **55 % ± 5 %**, light exclusion, FIFO rotation) that prevents premature degradation, skin formation, pigment settling, solvent loss, and oxidative thickening.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Handling and Storage is a frequently tested Module 3 topic because it directly bridges *raw material science* with *industrial print-shop economics*. Expect 3-mark definition questions and 7-mark procedural/cause-effect questions.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Think of a freshly manufactured printing ink as a **freshly prepared cup of hot chocolate with whipped cream and marshmallows sitting on a kitchen counter**:

- If you leave it uncovered → the cream forms a dry "skin" on top (analogous to **ink skinning** due to oxidation of drying oils).
- If you let it sit for hours without stirring → the cocoa settles at the bottom as a thick sludge (analogous to **pigment settling / hard packing** in a tin).
- If you put it next to a hot oven → it dries out, thickens, and the fat separates (analogous to **solvent evaporation and resin precipitation** in a hot warehouse).
- If you keep shaking or remixing it before drinking → it becomes smooth again (analogous to **mechanical agitation / re-dispersion** at the press).

Just as a barista would store chocolate in a sealed, cool, dark container and re-whisk it before serving, a press operator must store ink under controlled conditions and agitate it before use.

### 1.3 Standard Industrial Metrics

The following metrics are universally adopted in ink storage and handling protocols:

| Parameter | Standard Value | Unit |
|-----------|---------------|------|
| Storage temperature | **20 ± 5** | °C |
| Relative humidity | **55 ± 5** | % RH |
| Minimum storage temperature (water-based) | **5** | °C |
| Maximum storage temperature | **30** | °C |
| Viscosity measurement temperature | **25** | °C |
| Typical shelf life (paste inks) | **12–24** | months |
| Typical shelf life (liquid inks) | **6–12** | months |

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition — Shelf Life:** *Shelf life* is the period during which an ink, stored under manufacturer-specified conditions in its original unopened container, retains all guaranteed performance properties (viscosity, colour strength, tack, drying, gloss) within published tolerances.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Controlled Handling and Storage is Critical — The Five Failure Modes

A printing ink is a **metastable colloidal dispersion**. The binder, solvent, pigment, and additives are not in a permanent thermodynamic equilibrium — they are constantly seeking to separate. The job of proper handling and storage is to **slow down these separation kinetics** until the ink reaches the substrate.

The five primary in-storage failure modes are:

1. **Skinning** — Surface oxidation of drying oils (linseed, alkyd) forms a rubbery polymer film on top of the ink.
2. **Hard-packing / Caking** — Pigment particles aggregate irreversibly at the bottom under gravity.
3. **Viscosity drift** — Solvent loss through imperfect seals, or oxidative chain extension of the binder.
4. **Colour shift / Tone change** — Pigment flocculation, light-induced fading, or chemical attack.
5. **Gelling / Livering** — Reaction between acidic pigment and alkaline vehicle producing a stiff, liver-like mass.

### 2.2 Storage Conditions — Step-by-Step Logic

**Step 1 — Temperature Control**
- Most inks should be stored between **15 °C and 25 °C**.
- High temperatures accelerate solvent evaporation and oxidative polymerisation.
- Low temperatures (below **0 °C**) cause wax crystallisation in paste inks and *freezing* in water-based inks.

**Step 2 — Humidity Control**
- Excess humidity can rust metal containers and contaminate water-based inks.
- Very low humidity promotes static electricity and rapid skinning.

**Step 3 — Light Exclusion**
- UV light triggers free-radical reactions in drying-oil inks and fades organic pigments.
- Inks should be stored in **opaque or amber containers**, away from direct sunlight.

**Step 4 — Container Integrity**
- Containers must be tightly sealed to prevent solvent loss and skinning.
- Common container types: **tins (1 kg, 2.5 kg), pails (5 kg, 25 kg), drums (200 kg), cartridges (for flexo).**

**Step 5 — Stock Rotation (FIFO)**
- **First In, First Out** inventory principle ensures older inks are used first.
- Each container must display: *batch number, manufacture date, expiry date, and colour/shade code.*

### 2.3 Handling Procedures — Pre-Press Preparation Sequence

**Step A — Inspection**
- Check for skin formation, phase separation, or unusual odour.
- Reject inks that have exceeded shelf life or show irreversible gelling.

**Step B — Homogenisation**
- Stir gently with a **paddle/spatula** (paste inks) or **mechanical stirrer** (liquid inks).
- Avoid high-speed mechanical agitation that introduces air bubbles (causes **foaming** and **cissing** on the substrate).

**Step C — Viscosity Adjustment**
- Add the manufacturer's recommended **reducer/thinner** in small increments (typically **1–3 %** by weight at a time).
- For water-based inks → use deionised water or proprietary slow-drying retarder.
- For solvent-based inks → use the corresponding slow/fast solvent blend.

**Step D — Straining**
- Pass ink through a **60–100 mesh nylon sieve** to remove dried particles and skins.

**Step E — Colour Checking**
- Draw-down on the actual substrate and compare against the standard under **D50 standard illuminant**.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula / Data Sheet

| Concept | Expression / Parameter | Units | Application |
|---------|----------------------|-------|-------------|
| Storage temperature (paste ink) | $T_s = 20 \pm 5$ | °C | Heat-sensitive letterpress/offset inks |
| Storage temperature (water-based) | $T_s \geq 5$ | °C | Avoid freezing point of water phase |
| Relative humidity | $RH = 55 \pm 5$ | % | Container integrity + ink stability |
| Shelf life (paste ink, sealed) | $t_{shelf} = 12 - 24$ | months | Offset, letterpress |
| Shelf life (liquid ink, sealed) | $t_{shelf} = 6 - 12$ | months | Flexo, gravure |
| Viscosity adjustment cap | $\Delta\eta_{max} = \pm 10\%$ | — | Beyond this, print quality compromised |
| Reducer addition limit | $\leq 3\%$ by mass | % | Single addition |
| Anti-skin agent (e.g., MEKO) | 0.1 – 0.5 | % | Oxidative offset inks |
| Standard draw-down ink film | 1 – 2 | µm | Pre-press colour control |
| Stirring time (paste ink, 1 kg) | 2 – 3 | minutes | Mechanical homogenisation |
| Settling rate (Stokes' law) | $v = \frac{2r^2(\rho_p - \rho_v)g}{9\eta}$ | m/s | Pigment hard-pack prediction |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

These protocols are not academic exercises. In a real press room, a single drum of metallic Pantone ink worth ₹ 60,000–1,50,000 can be ruined by **two weeks of improper storage on a hot mezzanine floor** during Indian summer (ambient 40–45 °C). Proper FIFO rotation and temperature-controlled ink rooms are mandatory audit points in **ISO 12647-2** colour management and **HACCP/GMP** audits for food-packaging converters.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Stokes' Law Application — Predicting Pigment Settling During Storage

This derivation explains *why* pigment hard-packs at the bottom of a storage drum and *how long* you have before the ink becomes unusable.

**Given:** A pigment particle of radius $r$ settles through a Newtonian vehicle of viscosity $\eta$ under gravity $g$.

**Step 1 — Forces acting on the particle:**
- Downward gravitational force: $F_g = \frac{4}{3} \pi r^3 \rho_p \, g$
- Upward buoyant force: $F_b = \frac{4}{3} \pi r^3 \rho_v \, g$
- Viscous drag (Stokes regime): $F_d = 6 \pi \eta r \, v$

**Step 2 — At terminal velocity, $F_g - F_b = F_d$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{4}{3} \pi r^3 (\rho_p - \rho_v) g &= 6 \pi \eta r \, v \\
v &= \frac{2 r^2 (\rho_p - \rho_v) g}{9 \eta}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **Stokes settling velocity** $v$ of a single pigment particle.

**Step 3 — Time for the particle to settle the full height $h$ of the drum:**

$$
t_{settle} = \frac{h}{v} = \frac{9 \eta \, h}{2 r^2 (\rho_p - \rho_v) g}
$$

**Step 4 — Numerical illustration (typical offset ink):**

- $r = 5 \times 10^{-6}$ m (5 µm pigment)
- $\rho_p = 1800$ kg/m³
- $\rho_v = 950$ kg/m³ (drying oil vehicle)
- $\eta = 5$ Pa·s
- $g = 9.81$ m/s²
- $h = 0.5$ m (half-full 25 kg pail)

$$
\begin{aligned}
v &= \frac{2 (5 \times 10^{-6})^2 (1800 - 950)(9.81)}{9 \times 5} \\
&= \frac{2 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-11} \times 850 \times 9.81}{45} \\
&\approx 9.27 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m/s} \\
&\approx 0.033 \text{ mm/hour} \\
t_{settle} &= \frac{0.5}{9.27 \times 10^{-9}} \approx 5.4 \times 10^7 \text{ s} \approx 625 \text{ days}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** A 5 µm pigment in a properly formulated ink takes ~625 days to settle through a 0.5 m column. This is why well-dispersed ink *appears* stable on the shelf, but **flocculated** pigment (where effective $r$ becomes 20–50 µm) settles in days — explaining hard-pack complaints in real press rooms.

### 3.2 Anti-Skin Agent Kinetics

Drying-oil inks (oxidative offset/letterpress) skin due to **peroxide-radical chain polymerisation** with atmospheric O₂. Anti-skin agents like **methyl ethyl ketoxime (MEKO)** act as oxygen scavengers:

$$
\text{R-OOH} + \text{CH}_3\text{C(=NOH)C}_2\text{H}_5 \longrightarrow \text{R-OH} + \text{CH}_3\text{C(=NO)C}_2\text{H}_5 + \text{H}_2\text{O}
$$

The oxime is regenerated by reaction with peroxide radicals, extending the *open-time* of the ink in the can without preventing cure on the substrate (where higher temperature and catalytic driers overcome the inhibition).

### 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Defect Diagnosis from Storage

| Observed Defect | Probable Cause | Corrective Handling Action |
|----------------|---------------|----------------------------|
| Skin on surface | Oxidation, MEKO exhaustion | Remove skin, stir, add anti-skin agent if within shelf life |
| Hard cake at bottom | Long-term gravity settling / flocculation | Mechanically re-disperse; if caked irreversibly, reject |
| Increased viscosity | Solvent evaporation / oxidative thickening | Add reducer ≤ 3 %, re-check tack |
| Colour drift / duller shade | Pigment flocculation or light fading | Re-disperse; compare draw-down; reject if outside tolerance |
| Gelly / liver-like mass | Acid-base reaction (acidic pigment + basic vehicle) | Reject — irreversible |
| Foaming during stirring | Over-vigorous agitation | Allow to rest, use anti-foam (silicone) |
| Foul odour (water-based) | Microbial contamination | Reject — biological degradation |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Flow — Ink Receipt to Press Application

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Incoming Ink Drum / Tin] --> B{QC Inspection: Batch, Date, Seal}
    B -->|Pass| C[Tag and Label with Date Code]
    B -->|Fail| D[Quarantine / Reject]
    C --> E[Move to Climate-Controlled Ink Room]
    E --> F[FIFO Rack Storage: 20 C, 55 percent RH, Dark]
    F --> G[Pre-Press Pull as per FIFO]
    G --> H[Visual Check: Skinning, Phase Separation]
    H -->|Pass| I[Mechanical Agitation 2-3 min]
    H -->|Fail| J[Specialty Recovery or Reject]
    I --> K[Viscosity Adjustment with Reducer <= 3 percent]
    K --> L[Straining through 60-100 mesh sieve]
    L --> M[Draw-Down on Substrate, D50 Light Check]
    M -->|Match| N[Issue to Press]
    M -->|Mismatch| O[Re-tint or Reject]
    N --> P[Return Unused Ink to Sealed Container]
    P --> E
```

### 4.2 Storage Condition Control Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Warehouse[Climate Controlled Ink Store]
    W1[Ambient Sensor T and RH]
    W2[Thermostat 20 plus minus 5 C]
    W3[Hygrostat 55 plus minus 5 percent RH]
    W4[Ink Drums on Pallets off Floor]
    end
    W1 --> W2
    W1 --> W3
    W2 --> W4
    W3 --> W4
    W4 --> X{Within Spec}
    X -->|Yes| Y[OK - Store]
    X -->|No| Z[Activate HVAC / Dehumidifier]
```

### 4.3 Block Architecture — Ink Handling Workflow at the Press

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Ink Store] --> P2[Ink Trolley Sealed]
    P2 --> P3[Ink Tray on Press]
    P3 --> P4{Fountain or Anilox}
    P4 --> P5[Substrate]
    P5 --> P6[Drying Tunnel]
    P6 --> P7[Rewind / Sheeted Output]
    P3 --> P8[Ink Stirrer Mounted on Press]
    P8 --> P3
    P7 --> P9[Unprinted Trim Recovery]
    P9 --> P1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block-Level Diagram Note:** Because ink storage and handling is a *physical, spatial* activity rather than an electronic signal-processing chain, the Mermaid block diagrams above intentionally model the **process flow** rather than attempting to render a literal warehouse layout. This is the recommended KTU 2024 answer-sheet style for procedural topics.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model QP]**
*Define the term "shelf life" of a printing ink. State the typical shelf life of (a) paste offset inks and (b) liquid flexo/gravure inks.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Shelf life is the period during which an ink, stored under manufacturer-specified conditions in its original sealed container, retains its guaranteed properties (viscosity, colour strength, tack, drying) within published tolerances.
- (a) **Paste offset inks: 12–24 months** (sealed, 20 °C).
- (b) **Liquid flexo/gravure inks: 6–12 months** (sealed, 20 °C, away from flame).

*(Award 1 mark for definition, 1 mark each for the two numerical values.)*

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model QP]**
*List any four defects that may develop in a printing ink during improper storage.*

**Model Answer (3 marks, any four):**
1. **Skinning** — surface oxidation film.
2. **Hard packing / caking** — pigment settling into a solid cake.
3. **Viscosity drift** — thickening due to solvent loss.
4. **Colour shift** — fading or flocculation.
5. **Gelling / livering** — acid-base reaction product.
6. **Foaming** — microbial growth in water-based inks.

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

---

**Question A (14 Marks):**

**(a)** Explain the recommended storage conditions for printing inks, highlighting the significance of temperature, humidity, light, and container sealing. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe, with a flowchart, the step-by-step pre-press handling procedure for a paste offset ink from drum opening to fountain. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Recommended Storage Conditions (7 marks):**

1. **Temperature (20 ± 5 °C):** High temperatures accelerate oxidative polymerisation of drying oils (skin/liver formation) and solvent evaporation (viscosity drift). Low temperatures (below 5 °C) freeze water-based inks and crystallise waxes in paste inks. **(2 marks)**
2. **Relative Humidity (55 ± 5 %):** Prevents corrosion of tin containers, contamination of water-based inks, and static-related pigment issues. **(1.5 marks)**
3. **Light Exclusion:** UV light initiates free-radical oxidation and fades organic pigments; inks must be kept in opaque or amber containers in dark store-rooms. **(1.5 marks)**
4. **Container Sealing:** Tight seals prevent solvent evaporation (viscosity drift), atmospheric oxidation (skinning), and ingress of dust/moisture. **(1 mark)**
5. **FIFO Rotation:** Older stock is consumed first, preventing expiry-related wastage. **(1 mark)**

**(b) Pre-Press Handling Procedure (7 marks):**

```
Step 1: Receive ink drum, check batch, date, seal integrity.
Step 2: Move to ink room, allow to acclimatise to 20-25 C (1-2 h).
Step 3: Open drum, inspect for skin, phase separation, odour.
Step 4: Remove surface skin with a spatula (do not mix in).
Step 5: Mechanically agitate with paddle stirrer for 2-3 min.
Step 6: Draw small sample, measure viscosity on viscometer at 25 C.
Step 7: If viscosity high, add reducer in increments of <= 1 %, stir, re-measure.
Step 8: Strain through 60-100 mesh nylon sieve.
Step 9: Draw-down on actual substrate, check colour under D50 light.
Step 10: Pour into fountain tray, start press.
```

*(Award 1 mark per correctly explained step; allow schematic credit for the flowchart.)*

---

**Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice:**

**(a)** With the help of Stokes' law, explain the phenomenon of pigment hard-packing in stored inks. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the causes, symptoms, and corrective actions for **skinning** and **livering** in offset inks. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Stokes' Law and Hard-Packing (7 marks):**

The settling velocity of a pigment particle through a viscous vehicle is given by:

$$
v = \frac{2 r^2 (\rho_p - \rho_v) g}{9 \eta}
$$

- Larger particle radius $r$ → faster settling (hence flocculation drastically worsens hard-pack).
- Higher pigment-vehicle density difference $(\rho_p - \rho_v)$ → faster settling.
- Lower vehicle viscosity $\eta$ → faster settling.
- Higher $g$ (vibration in transport) effectively increases settling energy.

**Numerical (2 marks):** Using $r = 10^{-5}$ m, $\rho_p - \rho_v = 850$ kg/m³, $\eta = 1$ Pa·s, $h = 0.3$ m:

$$
\begin{aligned}
v &= \frac{2 (10^{-5})^2 (850)(9.81)}{9 \times 1} \approx 1.85 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m/s} \\
t_{settle} &= \frac{0.3}{1.85 \times 10^{-7}} \approx 1.62 \times 10^6 \text{ s} \approx 19 \text{ days}
\end{aligned}
$$

A 10 µm particle settles in **~19 days** — explaining why flocculated ink hard-packs within weeks of storage. **(2 marks)**

**Prevention:** Use dispersing agents, control particle size, increase vehicle viscosity, avoid vibration, and use FIFO rotation. **(1 mark)**

**(b) Skinning vs. Livering (7 marks):**

| Feature | Skinning | Livering |
|---------|---------|----------|
| Location | Top surface | Throughout the bulk |
| Cause | Oxidative polymerisation of drying oil at air interface | Acid-base reaction between acidic pigment (e.g., basic dye lake) and alkaline vehicle |
| Appearance | Thin rubbery film on top | Stiff, liver-like, thixotropic mass |
| Reversibility | Reversible — remove skin, ink still usable | **Irreversible — reject the ink** |
| Prevention | Anti-skin agents (MEKO 0.1–0.5 %), tight sealing, low-O₂ headspace | Avoid acidic pigments in alkaline vehicles; use neutralised vehicles |

*(Award 3 marks for skinning, 3 marks for livering, 1 mark for comparison/contrast.)*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> 1. Do **not** confuse *skinning* (surface, reversible) with *livering* (bulk, irreversible) — this is the single most common differentiator examiners test.
> 2. Always quote **temperature as 20 ± 5 °C**, not "room temperature" — vague answers lose 1–2 marks.
> 3. In Stokes' law questions, **state the assumptions** (spherical particle, Newtonian vehicle, laminar flow, no particle-particle interaction). Examiners award marks for these.
> 4. Always specify the **anti-skin agent name** (e.g., MEKO) rather than the generic term "anti-oxidant."
> 5. In flowchart questions, use **rectangles for processes** and **diamonds for decisions** — Mermaid-style convention is accepted and rewarded.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Storage temperature = 20 ± 5 °C**; water-based inks ≥ 5 °C (anti-freeze).
- ✅ **Relative humidity = 55 ± 5 %** — prevents container corrosion.
- ✅ **Light exclusion** — opaque/amber containers, dark store-room.
- ✅ **Container integrity** — tight sealing prevents skinning and viscosity drift.
- ✅ **FIFO rotation** — batch number, manufacture date, expiry date on every container.
- ✅ **Pre-press sequence** — Inspect → Acclimatise → De-skin → Stir 2–3 min → Adjust viscosity (reducer ≤ 3 %) → Strain (60–100 mesh) → Draw-down → Press.
- ✅ **Stokes' law:** $v = \frac{2r^2 (\rho_p - \rho_v) g}{9 \eta}$ — larger/flocculated particles settle faster.
- ✅ **Anti-skin agent:** MEKO (methyl ethyl ketoxime) at 0.1–0.5 %.
- ✅ **Skinning = surface, reversible**; **Livering = bulk, irreversible**.
- ✅ **Hard-packing prevention:** dispersing agents + high vehicle viscosity + no vibration.
- ✅ **Five failure modes:** skinning, hard-packing, viscosity drift, colour shift, gelling/livering.
- ✅ **Standard draw-down:** 1–2 µm wet ink film, viewed under **D50 illuminant**.
- ✅ **Shelf life:** 12–24 months (paste), 6–12 months (liquid).
- ✅ **ISO 12647-2 + GMP** audits specifically check ink-room climate logs.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Modern Production Trends
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT866 - TECHNOLOGY OF PRINTING INKS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Modern Production Trends

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition**
> **Modern Production Trends** in the printing ink industry refer to the contemporary shifts in manufacturing paradigms that integrate advanced process automation, digital quality control, sustainable (green) chemistry, lean manufacturing principles, and Industry 4.0 technologies to produce printing inks with higher consistency, lower environmental impact, and greater cost efficiency.

### 1.1 What Does "Modern Production Trends" Actually Mean?

In plain English, imagine an old-style *country kitchen* where grandma makes pickles in batches — one big copper pot, stirred by hand, with the recipe written on a piece of paper taped to the wall. Every batch is slightly different. Now compare this to a **modern pharmaceutical plant** where pills are produced by fully automated machines, weighed by robots, inspected by cameras, and tracked in a digital database. Every pill is identical, defects are caught instantly, and waste is recycled on the spot.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The ink industry has been making this exact leap — moving from **artisanal batch production** to **smart, data-driven, sustainable continuous manufacturing**. Understanding these trends is mandatory for KTU Module 3 under the 2024 scheme.

### 1.2 The Six Pillars of Modern Ink Production

| # | Pillar | One-Line Meaning |
|---|--------|------------------|
| 1 | **Lean Manufacturing** | Eliminate waste (*muda*), keep only value-adding steps. |
| 2 | **Six Sigma** | Drive defect rate down to **3.4 defects per million opportunities (DPMO)**. |
| 3 | **Total Quality Management (TQM)** | Quality is everyone's responsibility — from R&D to dispatch. |
| 4 | **Computer-Integrated Manufacturing (CIM)** | Design + Production + Logistics controlled by one digital brain. |
| 5 | **Just-In-Time (JIT)** | Raw materials arrive *exactly* when needed — almost zero inventory. |
| 6 | **Industry 4.0 / Smart Manufacturing** | IoT sensors, AI-driven control, digital twins on the shop floor. |

### 1.3 Real-World Intuition — The Pizza Factory Analogy

Think of a Domino's Pizza kitchen versus a street-side stall:
- **Street stall** = Old batch production. One chef, one oven, no tracking, lots of waste, inconsistent taste.
- **Domino's kitchen** = Modern production. Computer tracks each order, dough balls are pre-portioned, oven temperature is sensor-controlled, dispatch is GPS-tracked.

An **ink factory** of 2025 works the same way as the Domino's kitchen: every gram of pigment is measured by load cells, every mixing vessel is fitted with **pH probes and viscometers**, and the entire batch is logged in a **Manufacturing Execution System (MES)**.

### 1.4 Key Sustainability Metrics (Industry Standard)

- **VOC (Volatile Organic Compound) reduction**: Targets as low as **< 50 g/L** for water-based inks.
- **Carbon Footprint**: Modern plants target **≤ 1.5 kg CO₂-equivalent per kg of ink** produced.
- **Energy Intensity**: Benchmark — **≤ 2.5 kWh per kg of finished ink** for dispersion processes.

> [!TIP]
> For KTU exams, always memorize these three numbers: **VOC < 50 g/L**, **3.4 DPMO** (Six Sigma benchmark), and **OEE > 85%** (world-class manufacturing).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Trend 1 — Shift from Batch to Continuous Production

Historically, printing inks were made in **discrete batches** (e.g., 500 kg lots in a high-speed dissolver). Modern plants increasingly use **continuous in-line reactors** and **stirred-tank cascades**.

**Why the shift?**
- **Better consistency**: Continuous processes operate at steady state — no batch-to-batch variation.
- **Lower labour**: One operator can supervise an entire skid via a **DCS (Distributed Control System)**.
- **Faster throughput**: No "between-batch" cleaning downtime.

**How?**
- Inline **static mixers** replace large agitated vessels for pigment wetting.
- **Twin-screw extruders** run continuously, fed by loss-in-weight feeders.
- Product is filtered, degassed, and packaged without stopping the line.

### 2.2 Trend 2 — Lean Manufacturing (Toyota Production System Adapted)

Lean identifies **seven classical wastes (muda)** — relevant to ink production:
1. **Transport** — unnecessary movement of raw materials.
2. **Inventory** — pigments, resins, and solvents sitting in warehouses.
3. **Motion** — operator walking to fetch a tool.
4. **Waiting** — batch idle while a QC test runs.
5. **Overproduction** — making ink before the customer orders.
6. **Over-processing** — grinding finer than the print job needs.
7. **Defects** — off-shade batches rejected at QC.

### 2.3 Trend 3 — Six Sigma and Statistical Process Control (SPC)

Six Sigma uses the **DMAIC** roadmap:

| Phase | Meaning | Application in Ink Plant |
|-------|---------|--------------------------|
| **D**efine | Identify the problem | "Hue drift in offset ink batches" |
| **M**easure | Collect data | ΔE colour-difference values over 30 batches |
| **A**nalyze | Find root cause | Variation in dispersing time |
| **I**mprove | Implement fix | Install in-line rheometer feedback loop |
| **C**ontrol | Sustain fix | SPC control charts on viscosity |

### 2.4 Trend 4 — Industry 4.0 / Smart Manufacturing

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **four design principles of Industry 4.0** (per the German Plattform Industrie 4.0) — applied to ink plants:
> 1. **Interconnection** — Machines, sensors, people connected via IoT.
> 2. **Information Transparency** — Real-time dashboards for viscosity, pH, temperature.
> 3. **Technical Assistance** — Robots and AR-glasses help operators.
> 4. **Decentralized Decisions** — Cyber-physical systems self-optimize.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Metric | Equation | Units | Purpose |
|------------------|----------|-------|---------|
| **Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE)** | $OEE = A \times P \times Q$ | % (decimal) | Gold-standard KPI for plant efficiency |
| Availability ($A$) | $A = \dfrac{\text{Run Time}}{\text{Planned Production Time}}$ | fraction | Measures downtime losses |
| Performance ($P$) | $P = \dfrac{\text{Ideal Cycle Time} \times \text{Total Pieces}}{\text{Run Time}}$ | fraction | Measures speed losses |
| Quality ($Q$) | $Q = \dfrac{\text{Good Pieces}}{\text{Total Pieces}}$ | fraction | Measures defect losses |
| **Six Sigma Defect Rate** | $\text{DPMO} = \dfrac{\text{Defects} \times 10^6}{\text{Opportunities}}$ | defects/million | Process capability metric |
| **Process Capability Index** | $C_p = \dfrac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma}$ | dimensionless | Compares spec width to process spread |
| **Process Capability (shifted)** | $C_{pk} = \min\!\left(\dfrac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma},\ \dfrac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma}\right)$ | dimensionless | One-sided capability |
| **Yield (chemistry)** | $Y = \dfrac{\text{Actual Mass}}{\text{Theoretical Mass}} \times 100$ | % | Mass-based production yield |
| **Energy Intensity** | $EI = \dfrac{E_{\text{total}}}{m_{\text{ink}}}$ | kWh/kg | Sustainability KPI |
| **CO₂ Footprint** | $CF = \dfrac{m_{\text{CO}_2,\text{eq}}}{m_{\text{ink}}}$ | kg CO₂e/kg | Carbon performance |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Reminder**: World-class OEE is **≥ 85%**. Anything below **60%** is considered a "typical" plant with significant improvement potential. KTU valuation usually expects you to **state the benchmark** when you compute OEE.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- A **Cpk > 1.33** is the minimum for KTU-style ink process validation; **Cpk > 1.67** is preferred for offset ink viscosity.
- An **OEE jump from 60% to 85%** effectively **doubles the output** of the same physical plant — without buying a new reactor.
- **Energy Intensity** is now a board-level metric under the EU **CSRD (Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive)** that applies to global ink majors like Sun Chemical, Flint, and DIC.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Computing OEE for an Ink Plant

**Problem Statement:** A packaging ink plant runs an offset-ink dispersion line for **480 minutes** per shift. Planned breaks consume **60 minutes**. During the run, **12 minutes** of unplanned stoppages occurred. The line ideally should produce **100 cans per minute**, but actually produced **38,000 cans in the run**. After QC, **1,140 cans** were rejected for off-shade defects. Compute the OEE.

**Step 1 — Compute Planned Production Time**

Planned Production Time = Scheduled Time − Planned Stops

$$ \text{PPT} = 480 \text{ min} - 60 \text{ min} = 420 \text{ min} $$

**Step 2 — Compute Run Time**

Run Time = Planned Production Time − Unplanned Downtime

$$ \text{Run Time} = 420 \text{ min} - 12 \text{ min} = 408 \text{ min} $$

**Step 3 — Compute Availability (A)**

$$ A = \frac{\text{Run Time}}{\text{Planned Production Time}} = \frac{408}{420} $$

$$ A = 0.9714 = 97.14\% $$

**Step 4 — Compute Performance (P)**

Total Pieces = 38,000 cans (actual production in Run Time)
Ideal Cycle Time = 1 / 100 cans per minute = 0.01 min/can

$$ P = \frac{\text{Ideal Cycle Time} \times \text{Total Pieces}}{\text{Run Time}} $$

$$ P = \frac{0.01 \times 38{,}000}{408} = \frac{380}{408} $$

$$ P = 0.9314 = 93.14\% $$

**Step 5 — Compute Quality (Q)**

Good Pieces = Total Pieces − Defects = 38,000 − 1,140 = **36,860 cans**

$$ Q = \frac{\text{Good Pieces}}{\text{Total Pieces}} = \frac{36{,}860}{38{,}000} $$

$$ Q = 0.9700 = 97.00\% $$

**Step 6 — Compute Overall OEE**

$$ \text{OEE} = A \times P \times Q = 0.9714 \times 0.9314 \times 0.9700 $$

Computing stepwise:

$$ 0.9714 \times 0.9314 = 0.9048 $$

$$ 0.9048 \times 0.9700 = 0.8777 $$

$$ \boxed{\text{OEE} \approx 87.77\%} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation for KTU answer**: An OEE of **87.77%** classifies this ink plant as **"World Class"** (≥ 85%). The dominant loss is **Performance (P = 93.14%)** — meaning the line is running slower than its ideal cycle. **Recommendation:** investigate agitator speed, raw-material feeding rate, or viscosity drift during the run.

---

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Six Sigma DPMO Computation

**Problem Statement:** During a gravure ink batch run, **45 defects** were observed in a sample of **1,200 cans**, with each can having **25 inspection opportunities** (colour, viscosity, gloss, fineness, pH, etc.). Calculate the DPMO and the sigma level.

**Step 1 — Compute Total Opportunities**

$$ \text{Opportunities} = \text{Units Inspected} \times \text{Opportunities per Unit} $$

$$ \text{Opportunities} = 1{,}200 \times 25 = 30{,}000 $$

**Step 2 — Compute Defects per Million Opportunities (DPMO)**

$$ \text{DPMO} = \frac{\text{Defects} \times 10^6}{\text{Opportunities}} $$

$$ \text{DPMO} = \frac{45 \times 1{,}000{,}000}{30{,}000} = \frac{45{,}000{,}000}{30{,}000} $$

$$ \text{DPMO} = 1{,}500 \text{ defects per million opportunities} $$

**Step 3 — Convert DPMO to Sigma Level using standard tables**

| DPMO Range | Sigma Level |
|------------|-------------|
| ≤ 3.4 | **6 σ** |
| 233 | 5 σ |
| 6,210 | 4 σ |
| 66,807 | 3 σ |
| 308,537 | 2 σ |
| 690,000 | 1 σ |

Since **1,500 DPMO** falls between the 5 σ (233) and 4 σ (6,210) marks, the **sigma level ≈ 4.5 σ** (interpolated from Six Sigma conversion tables).

$$ \boxed{\text{DPMO} = 1{,}500 \quad ; \quad \sigma \approx 4.5} $$

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Trend-to-Application Mapping for KTU

| Trend | Ink Type Most Affected | KTU-Relevant Benefit | Implementation Cost |
|-------|------------------------|----------------------|---------------------|
| **Continuous processing** | Publication gravure, flexo | Consistent shade, lower cost | High |
| **Lean / JIT** | Packaging offset, UV inks | Inventory reduction by 40–60% | Medium |
| **Six Sigma / SPC** | All ink categories | Lower ΔE variation, fewer rejections | Low–Medium |
| **Industry 4.0 / IIoT** | High-end liquid inks | Predictive maintenance, real-time rheology | High |
| **Green chemistry** | Water-based, bio-resins | Meets REACH, EU Ecolabel | Medium |
| **Digital twins** | R&D ink development | Cuts lab trials by 50% | High |

---

### 3.4 Energy Intensity & CO₂ Footprint Mini-Derivation

**Given:** An ink plant consumed **12,000 kWh** of electricity and **800 m³** of natural gas (calorific value ≈ 9.97 kWh/m³) to produce **8,000 kg** of finished UV ink. Grid emission factor = **0.82 kg CO₂/kWh**. Gas emission factor = **0.20 kg CO₂/kWh**.

**Step 1 — Convert gas to kWh**

$$ E_{\text{gas}} = 800 \times 9.97 = 7{,}976 \text{ kWh} $$

**Step 2 — Total Energy**

$$ E_{\text{total}} = 12{,}000 + 7{,}976 = 19{,}976 \text{ kWh} $$

**Step 3 — Energy Intensity**

$$ EI = \frac{19{,}976}{8{,}000} = 2.497 \text{ kWh/kg} $$

**Step 4 — CO₂ Footprint**

$$ m_{\text{CO}_2,\text{elec}} = 12{,}000 \times 0.82 = 9{,}840 \text{ kg} $$

$$ m_{\text{CO}_2,\text{gas}} = 7{,}976 \times 0.20 = 1{,}595.2 \text{ kg} $$

$$ m_{\text{CO}_2,\text{total}} = 9{,}840 + 1{,}595.2 = 11{,}435.2 \text{ kg} $$

$$ CF = \frac{11{,}435.2}{8{,}000} = 1.429 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{e/kg ink} $$

$$ \boxed{EI = 2.50 \text{ kWh/kg}\quad ;\quad CF = 1.43 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{e/kg}} $$

> [!TIP]
> Both values are **within the world-class benchmark** stated in Section 1.4 — a perfect "good practice" answer to wrap up a KTU Part B question.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Industry 4.0 Architecture in a Modern Ink Plant

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeS1["ERP Layer - Business Planning"]

    nodeA3["pH and Temperature Probe"]
    nodeA4["Haze and Gloss Sensor"]
    nodeA5["Vision Camera - Can Inspection"]

    nodeS5 --- nodeA1
    nodeS5 --- nodeA2
    nodeS5 --- nodeA3
    nodeS5 --- nodeA4
    nodeS5 --- nodeA5

    nodeS2 --- nodeD2

    nodeS3 --- nodeOP
```

### 4.2 Lean Ink Production — Value Stream Mapping (Block Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeRM["Raw Material Receiving - Pigments Resins Solvents"]

    nodeRM --> nodeQC1
    nodeQC1 --> nodeST
    nodeST --> nodeJIT

    nodeFEED["Feedback Loop - SPC Charts on Viscosity"]

```

### 4.3 Six Sigma DMAIC Loop Applied to Hue Drift

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeD["DEFINE - Hue drift in cyan ink above dE 1.0"]
    nodeM["MEASURE - Log 50 batches dE values"]
    nodeA["ANALYZE - Pareto shows 80 percent from disperser residence time"]
    nodeI["IMPROVE - Install residence time sensor and auto feed rate"]

```

### 4.4 Decision Matrix — Batch vs Continuous vs Hybrid

```mermaid

    nodeStart --> nodeQ1
    nodeQ1 -- No --> nodeBatch
    nodeQ1 -- Yes --> nodeQ2
    nodeQ2 -- Yes --> nodeCont
    nodeQ2 -- No --> nodeHybrid
```

> [!TIP]
> These four diagrams cover **every block-level architecture** a KTU examiner can ask about under "Modern Production Trends" — from the **macro Industry 4.0 stack** down to the **micro DMAIC loop**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define the term "Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE)" and state the world-class benchmark value.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **OEE (Overall Equipment Effectiveness)** is a key performance indicator that measures the **percentage of planned production time** that is truly productive. It is the **product of three factors**: **Availability**, **Performance**, and **Quality**.
>
> $$ \text{OEE} = A \times P \times Q $$
>
> - **Availability** captures downtime losses (breakdowns, changeovers).
> - **Performance** captures speed losses (running below ideal cycle time).
> - **Quality** captures defect losses (pieces that must be reworked or scrapped).
>
> The **world-class benchmark** for OEE is **≥ 85%**, as established by the **Society of Manufacturing Engineers (SME)**. Plants operating below **60%** are classified as "typical" with significant headroom for improvement. **(3 marks — full credit)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*List any **four classical wastes (muda)** of lean manufacturing as applicable to a printing ink plant.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| # | Waste | Ink-Plant Example |
|---|-------|--------------------|
| 1 | **Waiting** | Batch idle while a fineness-grind test is run. |
| 2 | **Inventory** | Pigments stacked for months before use. |
| 3 | **Over-processing** | Grinding to 5 µm when the offset job only needs 8 µm. |
| 4 | **Defects** | Off-shade batches rejected at QC. |

*(Any four of the seven muda, with a valid ink-plant example, fetch full 3 marks.)*

---

### Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 3]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the **seven wastes (muda)** of lean manufacturing with **two examples each** in the context of a printing ink plant.

**(b) [7 marks]** A flexographic ink plant operates for **600 minutes** per shift. Planned breaks consume **45 minutes**, and **15 minutes** were lost to breakdowns. The ideal cycle time is **0.5 minutes per drum**, and the plant produced **1,050 drums** during the run, of which **30 drums** failed QC. Compute **OEE** and comment on the result.

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(a) — 7 marks:**

The seven muda, with two ink-plant examples each:

1. **Transport** — Unnecessary movement of raw materials across the plant; moving solvent drums by forklift when a piped solvent system could deliver directly.
2. **Inventory** — Stockpiling pigments beyond 60 days; finished ink cans held in warehouse awaiting dispatch.
3. **Motion** — Operator walking to fetch a viscosity cup; bending repeatedly to pick up cans from the floor instead of using conveyors.
4. **Waiting** — QC samples waiting for rheometer availability; batch held up because letdown solvent tank is empty.
5. **Overproduction** — Producing extra ink "just in case" before a confirmed order; standard SKU batches larger than the customer order.
6. **Over-processing** — Triple-roll milling when high-speed bead mill suffices; grinding below 5 µm Hegman when offset requires only 8 µm.
7. **Defects** — Off-shade batches rejected at QC; cans with leaking lids or misprinted labels.

*(Each correctly mapped waste with two valid examples = 1 mark × 7 = 7 marks.)*

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Planned Production Time**

$$ \text{PPT} = 600 - 45 = 555 \text{ min} $$

**Step 2 — Run Time**

$$ \text{Run Time} = 555 - 15 = 540 \text{ min} $$

**Step 3 — Availability**

$$ A = \frac{540}{555} = 0.9730 = 97.30\% $$

**Step 4 — Performance**

Ideal Cycle Time = 0.5 min/drum, Total Pieces = 1,050

$$ P = \frac{0.5 \times 1{,}050}{540} = \frac{525}{540} = 0.9722 = 97.22\% $$

**Step 5 — Quality**

Good Pieces = 1,050 − 30 = 1,020

$$ Q = \frac{1{,}020}{1{,}050} = 0.9714 = 97.14\% $$

**Step 6 — OEE**

$$ \text{OEE} = 0.9730 \times 0.9722 \times 0.9714 $$

Computing stepwise:

$$ 0.9730 \times 0.9722 = 0.9461 $$

$$ 0.9461 \times 0.9714 = 0.9191 $$

$$ \boxed{\text{OEE} \approx 91.91\%} $$

**Comment (1 mark):** The plant is operating in the **"World Class" band (≥ 85%)**. The dominant loss is roughly equally distributed; minor improvements in **breakdown prevention** and **defect reduction** can push OEE above 95%.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Describe the **DMAIC roadmap of Six Sigma**. Apply each phase to a real problem of **viscosity drift in a sheet-fed offset ink batch**.

**(b) [7 marks]** Explain the **four design principles of Industry 4.0** and how each is implemented in a modern ink production line.

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(a) — 7 marks:**

| DMAIC Phase | Meaning | Applied to Viscosity Drift |
|-------------|---------|----------------------------|
| **Define** | Identify the problem and customer impact | "Viscosity drifts ±5 cP from target 18 Pa·s, causing press mis-feed and customer complaints." |
| **Measure** | Collect reliable data using calibrated viscometers | Log viscosity every 15 min for 30 batches; build a baseline mean and σ. |
| **Analyze** | Use Pareto, fishbone, regression to find root causes | Fishbone shows raw-material temperature and ambient humidity contribute ~70% of variance. |
| **Improve** | Pilot solutions and confirm with hypothesis tests | Install temperature-controlled raw-material feed and HVAC in letdown bay. |
| **Control** | Sustain the gain via SPC charts and reaction plans | Implement X-bar/R chart; out-of-control rules trigger immediate operator action. |

*(Each correct phase explained + applied = 1 mark × 5 phases + 2 marks for cohesive real-world integration = 7 marks.)*

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(b) — 7 marks:**

| Principle | Meaning | Implementation in Ink Plant |
|-----------|---------|-----------------------------|
| **Interconnection** | Machines, sensors, people, and systems communicate via IoT protocols | PLCs on bead mills, fillers, and labellers all publish data to a central MES via OPC-UA. |
| **Information Transparency** | Real-time dashboards turn raw data into context-rich information | Operator HMI shows live viscosity, fineness, ΔE shade, and trends, replacing paper logbooks. |
| **Technical Assistance** | Systems aggregate and visualize information; robots help with heavy tasks | AGVs transport 200-kg pigment drums; AR-glasses show maintenance procedures overlay on equipment. |
| **Decentralized Decisions** | Cyber-physical systems make autonomous decisions | The MES auto-adjusts disperser RPM when inline rheometer detects viscosity deviating from setpoint. |

*(Each principle correctly stated + valid ink-plant example = 1.75 marks × 4 = 7 marks.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Do NOT write OEE as a single percentage without showing the three sub-factors.** Students commonly lose **2 marks** by writing only the final 91.91% — KTU's 2024 scheme key awards partial credit only when A, P, Q are separately derived.
> - **Do NOT confuse the seven muda with "lean tools."** Muda refers to **wastes**, not techniques like 5S, Kanban, or Poka-Yoke. Mixing the two loses at least **1 mark** in Part A questions.
> - **In Six Sigma answers, never write "DPMO = 3.4" as the answer without context.** The value 3.4 DPMO corresponds to **exactly 6 σ**; for any other DPMO you must state the sigma level explicitly.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **OEE = A × P × Q** — always compute the three sub-factors separately.
- **World-class OEE benchmark ≥ 85%**.
- **Six Sigma target** = **3.4 DPMO** at **6 σ**; memorize the table from Section 3.2.
- **Seven muda** of lean: Transport, Inventory, Motion, Waiting, Overproduction, Over-processing, Defects.
- **DMAIC roadmap** = Define → Measure → Analyze → Improve → Control.
- **Industry 4.0** = Interconnection + Information Transparency + Technical Assistance + Decentralized Decisions.
- **Sustainability KPIs**: VOC < 50 g/L, EI ≤ 2.5 kWh/kg, CF ≤ 1.5 kg CO₂e/kg.
- **Process capability benchmarks**: Cpk ≥ 1.33 acceptable, Cpk ≥ 1.67 preferred for offset-ink viscosity.
- **JIT / Kanban** reduces inventory carrying cost by **40–60%** in ink plants.
- **Digital twins** can cut ink R&D lab trials by approximately **50%** — relevant for KTU viva.
- **Energy Intensity (EI)** is computed per kg of finished ink — never per kg of raw material.
- **Cpk formula uses both USL and LSL** — always show both branches in the answer.
- **DMAIC is sequential** — KTU expects the cyclic arrow only at the Control phase feeding back to Define.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Rheology of Printing Inks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT866 - TECHNOLOGY OF PRINTING INKS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Rheology of Printing Inks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

> [!NOTE]
> **Rheology** is the branch of physics/science that deals with the **flow and deformation of matter** under the influence of an applied force (or stress). In the context of **Printing Inks**, rheology describes how the ink *flows* from the fountain, *transfers* through the rollers, *splits* at the nip, *wets* the substrate, and finally *levels* on the printed surface.

For printing inks, rheology is **the single most important physical property**, because every single step of the printing process (ink manufacturing → fountain → duct → rollers → plate → blanket → substrate) involves some form of fluid flow or film splitting.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key KTU Definition:** *Rheology of printing inks* is the study of the deformation and flow behavior of ink under the influence of stresses encountered during the printing process, characterized primarily by **viscosity**, **yield value**, and **thixotropy**.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Real-World Picture)

Imagine three everyday substances in your kitchen:

| Substance | Behavior | Rheological Class |
|---|---|---|
| **Water** | Pours easily, no matter how slowly or quickly you tip the glass | **Newtonian** |
| **Ketchup / Tomato Sauce** | Stays put in the bottle, but flows freely when shaken | **Pseudoplastic + Thixotropic** |
| **Toothpaste** | Does not flow under its own weight, but flows when you squeeze the tube | **Plastic (Bingham) with Yield Value** |

A **printing ink behaves like a sophisticated hybrid of ketchup and toothpaste**:
- It must **not drip** out of the duct (needs a **yield value**, like toothpaste).
- It must **flow easily** into the paper pores (needs low viscosity under pressure, like ketchup).
- It must **recover its body** quickly when stress is removed (needs **thixotropy**, like ketchup).
- It must **not splash** on the high-speed press (needs careful viscosity tuning).

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Hook for Exams:** *"Ink must be STIFF in the duct, FLUID in the nip, and STIFF again on the paper."* This is the entire essence of ink rheology.

---

## 1.3 Why Rheology is the Heart of Printing Ink Technology

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Point:** The print quality (dot sharpness, ink mileage, set-off, mottle, gloss, ink transfer uniformity) is a **direct function of ink rheology**, NOT of color or pigment chemistry. A perfect pigment dispersion in a wrongly formulated vehicle will still give poor prints.

The three primary rheological properties of a printing ink (as per KTU PEPOT866 syllabus) are:

1. **Viscosity (η)** — resistance to flow.
2. **Yield Value (τ₀ or S₀)** — minimum stress required to initiate flow.
3. **Thixotropy** — time-dependent structural recovery.

Additionally, **Tack** is a tribological (high-shear) property directly derived from rheology and is critical in offset/litho inks.

---

## 1.4 Fundamental Quantities (Must-Know for KTU Board Exam)

- **Shear Stress (τ)** — force per unit area applied tangentially. **Unit: Pa (N/m²) or dyne/cm².**
- **Shear Rate (γ̇ or D)** — rate of change of velocity with distance. **Unit: s⁻¹.**
- **Viscosity (η)** — ratio of shear stress to shear rate. **Unit: Pa·s or Poise (P).**
- **1 Pa·s = 10 Poise = 1000 cP (centipoise).**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Newton's Law of Viscosity (Foundation Stone)

For an **ideal (Newtonian) fluid**, the shear stress is **directly proportional** to the shear rate, and the proportionality constant is the viscosity.

$$\tau = \eta \cdot \dot{\gamma}$$

Where:
- $\tau$ = shear stress (Pa)
- $\eta$ = dynamic viscosity (Pa·s)
- $\dot{\gamma}$ = shear rate (s⁻¹)

**Why this matters:** Every other ink rheology equation is a *modification* or *deviation* from this single linear law. In the KTU board exam, if you remember nothing else, remember this.

---

## 2.2 Classification of Flow Behavior (The Core KTU Topic)

> [!NOTE]
> The classification is best understood by plotting **shear stress (y-axis) vs. shear rate (x-axis)**. This is called a **Flow Curve** or **Rheogram**.

### 2.2.1 Newtonian Flow
- Linear relationship: $\tau = \eta \cdot \dot{\gamma}$
- Viscosity is **constant** at all shear rates.
- Examples: Water, mineral oil, simple solvents.
- Most **vehicles** (binders) of inks approach this, but **finished inks never are Newtonian**.

### 2.2.2 Plastic Flow (Bingham Body)
- Requires a minimum stress (yield value) to start flowing.
- After yielding, behaves like a Newtonian fluid.

$$\tau - \tau_0 = \eta_{pl} \cdot \dot{\gamma} \quad \text{(for } \tau > \tau_0\text{)}$$

- $\tau_0$ = **Yield Value** (Pa)
- $\eta_{pl}$ = **Plastic Viscosity** (Pa·s)
- Examples: Toothpaste, grease, some letterpress inks.
- **Yield value** is critical — it prevents ink from dripping in the duct.

### 2.2.3 Pseudoplastic Flow (Shear-Thinning)
- Viscosity **decreases** as shear rate increases.
- **No yield value** (theoretically).
- The flow curve is **concave toward the shear rate axis**.

This is described by the **Ostwald–de Waele Power Law**:

$$\tau = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{\,n} \quad \text{where } 0 < n < 1$$

- $K$ = Consistency Index (Pa·sⁿ)
- $n$ = **Flow Behavior Index** (dimensionless)
- For $n = 1$: Newtonian. For $n < 1$: Pseudoplastic. For $n > 1$: Dilatant.
- **Examples:** Most lithographic, flexographic, and gravure inks. Polymer solutions, ketchup.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why pseudoplasticity is desirable in inks:** At **low shear** (storage, duct), the ink is *thick* (doesn't drip). At **high shear** (in the nip, doctor blade), the ink is *thin* (transfers easily).

### 2.2.4 Dilatant Flow (Shear-Thickening)
- Viscosity **increases** with shear rate.
- $n > 1$ in the power law.
- Example: Wet sand, starch suspensions, some highly pigmented inks.
- **Usually undesirable** in printing — can cause film splitting problems.

### 2.2.5 Thixotropic Flow (Time-Dependent Pseudoplastic)
- Viscosity **decreases with time** under constant shear, and **recovers** when shear is removed.
- A hysteresis loop is seen when shear rate is increased and then decreased.
- **Examples:** Ketchup, gel inks, letterpress inks, some screen inks.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Trick Question:** *"An ink shows high viscosity in the duct but low viscosity during transfer. It recovers its viscosity on the paper."* — This describes **thixotropic pseudoplastic behavior**, the most common in offset inks.

### 2.2.6 Rheopexy (Time-Dependent Dilatant)
- Viscosity **increases with time** under constant shear.
- Very rare; seen in some gypsum pastes.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Exam-Ready)

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables & Units | Application in Printing |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Newton's Law | $\tau = \eta \cdot \dot{\gamma}$ | $\tau$ (Pa), $\eta$ (Pa·s), $\dot{\gamma}$ (s⁻¹) | Foundation of all rheology |
| 2 | Apparent Viscosity | $\eta_a = \tau / \dot{\gamma}$ | Pa·s | Used for non-Newtonian fluids at a specific $\dot{\gamma}$ |
| 3 | Kinematic Viscosity | $\nu = \eta / \rho$ | m²/s, Stokes | Flow under gravity |
| 4 | Bingham Plastic Model | $\tau = \tau_0 + \eta_{pl}\dot{\gamma}$ | $\tau_0$ (Pa), $\eta_{pl}$ (Pa·s) | Letterpress inks |
| 5 | Power Law (Ostwald) | $\tau = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{\,n}$ | $K$ (Pa·sⁿ), $n$ (-) | Flexo, gravure inks |
| 6 | Casson Model | $\sqrt{\tau} = \sqrt{\tau_0} + \sqrt{\eta_\infty \cdot \dot{\gamma}}$ | $\tau_0$ (Pa), $\eta_\infty$ (Pa·s) | **Most widely used for printing inks** |
| 7 | Thixotropy Index (TI) | $TI = \eta_{\text{low speed}} / \eta_{\text{high speed}}$ | Dimensionless ratio | Practical measure of thixotropy |
| 8 | Capillary Viscometer (Hagen–Poiseuille) | $\eta = \dfrac{\pi R^4 \, P \, t}{8 L V}$ | $R,L$ (m), $P$ (Pa), $t$ (s), $V$ (m³) | Absolute viscosity measurement |
| 9 | Falling Rod Viscometer | $\eta = \dfrac{(M-\rho V g) \, t}{2 \pi L \, \ln(R_1/R_2)}$ | $M$ (kg), $V$ (m³), $R_1,R_2$ (m) | Routine ink viscosity check |
| 10 | Brookfield Viscometer | $\eta = \dfrac{\text{Scale Reading} \times \text{Factor}}{\text{Speed (rpm)}}$ | Empirical | Quality control of finished inks |
| 11 | Cone & Plate Viscometer | $\eta = \dfrac{3 T \theta}{2 \pi \omega R^3}$ | $T$ (Nm), $\theta$ (rad), $\omega$ (rad/s) | Absolute measurement at high shear |
| 12 | Tack (Inkometer) | $Tack \propto \dfrac{\text{Torque}}{\text{RPM}}$ | Arbitrary units | Offset ink transfer balance |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Exam Pitfall:** In the table above, students often write $\sqrt{\tau}$ and confuse it with $\tau^{1/2}$. They are the same. Do NOT write $n^{\frac{1}{2}}$ when you mean $\sqrt{n}$. Use `\sqrt{...}` in LaTeX.

---

## 2.4 The Casson Model (Special Focus for KTU)

The **Casson equation** is *the* model for printing inks, especially offset inks, because it captures **both yield value and shear-thinning**:

$$\sqrt{\tau} = \sqrt{\tau_0} + \sqrt{\eta_\infty \cdot \dot{\gamma}}$$

Where:
- $\tau_0$ = **Casson yield value** (Pa)
- $\eta_\infty$ = **Casson viscosity at infinite shear** (Pa·s)

**Why KTU loves this:** It gives **two useful numbers** — yield value and high-shear viscosity — that directly predict ink performance in duct and in the nip.

---

## 2.5 Concept of TACK (Offset Ink Specific)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Tack** is the **resistance of an ink film to splitting** at high shear rates (typically $10^4$ to $10^6$ s⁻¹, as in the printing nip).

- Measured using the **Inkometer** (Tack-O-Scope, or electronic equivalents).
- Units: tack units (arbitrary, based on torque required to rotate a roller at fixed RPM).
- Tacky ink = good pick resistance but bad transfer to subsequent rollers (water blocking).
- **Tack gradient** (decreasing tack across the roller train) is essential for proper ink feeding in offset.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Printing Process | Shear Rate Range (s⁻¹) | Critical Rheological Property |
|---|---|---|
| Gravure | $10^3$ – $10^5$ | Low viscosity, Newtonian-ish |
| Flexography | $10^3$ – $10^4$ | Pseudoplastic, low yield |
| Offset (Litho) | $10^4$ – $10^6$ | Tack, yield value, thixotropy |
| Letterpress | $10^3$ – $10^4$ | High yield, plastic flow |
| Screen Printing | $10^1$ – $10^2$ | Very high viscosity, thixotropic |
| Inkjet | $10^3$ – $10^5$ | Strictly Newtonian, low viscosity |

This is why **one ink cannot serve all processes** — the rheology must be tuned for the shear rate environment.
