# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Power Law (Ostwald–de Waele) Flow Behavior Index

**Statement:** Show that for a power-law fluid, the apparent viscosity is shear-dependent, and derive the flow index $n$.

**Step 1 — Start with the power-law definition:**

$$\tau = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{\,n}$$

**Step 2 — Define apparent viscosity** (as we do for any non-Newtonian fluid):

$$\eta_a = \frac{\tau}{\dot{\gamma}}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the power-law expression into the apparent viscosity equation:**

$$\eta_a = \frac{K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{\,n}}{\dot{\gamma}}$$

**Step 4 — Simplify the exponent** ($\dot{\gamma}^{\,n} / \dot{\gamma}^{\,1} = \dot{\gamma}^{\,n-1}$):

$$\eta_a = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{\,n-1}$$

**Step 5 — Interpret the result:**

- If $n = 1$ (Newtonian): $\eta_a = K$ (constant).
- If $n < 1$ (Pseudoplastic): $\dot{\gamma}^{\,n-1} < 1$, so $\eta_a$ **decreases** as $\dot{\gamma}$ increases → shear-thinning.
- If $n > 1$ (Dilatant): $\dot{\gamma}^{\,n-1} > 1$, so $\eta_a$ **increases** with $\dot{\gamma}$ → shear-thickening.

**Step 6 — Take logarithms to get $n$ from experimental data:**

$$\log \tau = \log K + n \log \dot{\gamma}$$

This is a **straight line** on a log-log plot. The slope gives $n$ and the intercept gives $K$.

**Conversion Logic:** A log-log transformation linearizes the power law so that linear regression (least-squares fitting) can extract $K$ and $n$ from experimental rheometer data — this is how all commercial rheometer software computes $n$ for printing inks.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Apparent Viscosity from Brookfield Viscometer Reading

**Statement:** A Brookfield viscometer with spindle No. 4 at 6 rpm gives a scale deflection of 64 units for an ink sample. The full-scale factor (provided in the Brookfield manual) is 2000 cP at 6 rpm with this spindle. Compute the apparent viscosity.

**Step 1 — Write the Brookfield formula:**

$$\eta = \text{Scale Reading} \times \text{Factor} \times \left(\frac{100}{\text{RPM}}\right) \times \text{Correction}$$

For modern digital Brookfield viscometers, the factor already includes the spindle geometry and the digital computation. Using a simplified form:

$$\eta = \text{Deflection} \times \text{Factor}_{\text{full-scale}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the values:**

$$\eta = 64 \times 2000 \, \text{cP at full scale}$$

But the deflection is 64 out of 100, so:

$$\eta = 0.64 \times 2000$$

**Step 3 — Compute the result:**

$$\eta = 1280 \, \text{cP} = 1.28 \, \text{Pa·s}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to SI units** (1 cP = 0.001 Pa·s):

$$\eta = 1.28 \, \text{Pa·s}$$

**Conversion Logic:** The Brookfield instrument uses a rotating spindle immersed in the fluid; the viscous drag rotates a spring, and the angular deflection is proportional to the torque and hence to the viscosity. The factor compensates for spindle geometry and rotational speed, giving viscosity directly in cP.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Viscosity from a Falling-Rod Viscometer

**Statement:** Derive the working equation for a falling-rod viscometer and apply it to a numerical example.

**Step 1 — Consider a cylindrical rod of mass $M$, radius $R_1$, falling coaxially inside a tube of inner radius $R_2$ filled with the ink. Let $L$ be the length of the rod and $V$ its volume.**

**Step 2 — At terminal (constant) velocity, force balance:**

$$\text{Driving force} = \text{Buoyancy-corrected weight} = (M - \rho V) g$$

This is balanced by the **viscous drag** on the cylindrical surface. For a thin annular gap ($R_2 - R_1 \ll R_1$), the linear velocity profile assumption gives shear stress at the rod surface:

$$\tau = \frac{(M - \rho V)g}{2 \pi R_1 L}$$

**Step 3 — Shear rate at the wall (for a thin gap):**

$$\dot{\gamma} = \frac{v}{(R_2 - R_1)}$$

where $v$ is the terminal velocity = $d/t$ (distance fallen divided by time).

**Step 4 — Use Newton's law of viscosity at the rod surface** to eliminate velocity:

$$v = \frac{(M - \rho V)g \, (R_2 - R_1)}{2 \pi R_1 L \cdot \eta}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $\eta$:**

$$\eta = \frac{(M - \rho V)g \, t \, (R_2 - R_1)}{2 \pi R_1 L \, d}$$

A more common practical form (after integrating over a finite length of fall) is:

$$\eta = \frac{(M - \rho V g)\, t}{2 \pi L \, \ln(R_1/R_2)}$$

**Step 6 — Numerical example:**

Given: $M = 0.05$ kg, $V = 5 \times 10^{-6}$ m³, $\rho_{\text{ink}} = 1000$ kg/m³, $R_1 = 0.005$ m, $R_2 = 0.0055$ m, $L = 0.1$ m, $t = 12$ s.

Compute the effective weight: $M - \rho V g = 0.05 - (1000)(5 \times 10^{-6})(9.81) = 0.05 - 0.04905 = 0.00095$ kg · m/s².

$$\eta = \frac{0.00095 \times 12}{2 \pi \times 0.1 \times \ln(0.005/0.0055)}$$

$$\eta = \frac{0.0114}{0.6283 \times \ln(0.9091)} = \frac{0.0114}{0.6283 \times (-0.0953)} = \frac{0.0114}{-0.0599}$$

The negative sign indicates the assumption is invalid (rod floats — i.e., buoyant weight < 0); in practice, the rod is **driven down** by a known extra weight.

**Conversion Logic:** The falling-rod viscometer is the workhorse of printing-ink QC because it is simple, rugged, and gives reproducible results on opaque inks (unlike capillary viscometers, which fail with opaque/dark fluids).

---

## 3.4 Derivation of the Hagen–Poiseuille Equation (Capillary Viscosity)

**Statement:** Derive the viscosity from the pressure-driven flow of ink through a capillary tube of radius $R$ and length $L$.

**Step 1 — Consider laminar, steady, incompressible flow of an ink (assumed Newtonian for the derivation) in a horizontal capillary of radius $R$.**

**Step 2 — Apply the Navier–Stokes equation in cylindrical coordinates** with axial symmetry, no swirl, fully developed flow. The equation reduces to:

$$\frac{1}{r}\frac{d}{dr}\left(r \frac{dv_z}{dr}\right) = \frac{1}{\eta}\frac{dP}{dz}$$

**Step 3 — Integrate once:**

$$r \frac{dv_z}{dr} = \frac{r^2}{2\eta}\frac{dP}{dz} + C_1$$

**Step 4 — Boundary condition 1:** $v_z$ is finite at $r = 0$ ⟹ $C_1 = 0$.

**Step 5 — Integrate again:**

$$v_z(r) = \frac{1}{4\eta}\frac{dP}{dz}(r^2 - R^2)$$

**Step 6 — Wall shear stress (at $r = R$):**

$$\tau_w = -\frac{R}{2}\frac{dP}{dz} = \frac{PR}{2L}$$

(where $P$ is the pressure drop across length $L$).

**Step 7 — Wall shear rate (Newtonian case):**

$$\dot{\gamma}_w = \frac{4V}{\pi R^3 t}$$

where $V$ is the volume flowing in time $t$.

**Step 8 — Viscosity:**

$$\eta = \frac{\tau_w}{\dot{\gamma}_w} = \frac{\pi R^4 P t}{8 L V}$$

**Conversion Logic:** The Hagen–Poiseuille law is the basis of capillary rheometry. For non-Newtonian inks, the *apparent* viscosity is computed, and Rabinowitsch correction is applied to get the *true* shear rate.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Comparing the Major Flow Models

| Model | Equation | Number of Parameters | Captures Yield? | Captures Shear-Thinning? | Captures Time Effect? | Best Suited For |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Newtonian | $\tau = \eta \dot{\gamma}$ | 1 | No | No | No | Pure vehicles, inkjet inks |
| Bingham Plastic | $\tau = \tau_0 + \eta_{pl}\dot{\gamma}$ | 2 | Yes | No (linear) | No | Letterpress inks, grease |
| Power Law | $\tau = K \dot{\gamma}^{\,n}$ | 2 | No | Yes | No | Flexo, gravure |
| Herschel–Bulkley | $\tau = \tau_0 + K \dot{\gamma}^{\,n}$ | 3 | Yes | Yes | No | General-purpose ink characterization |
| Casson | $\sqrt{\tau} = \sqrt{\tau_0} + \sqrt{\eta_\infty \dot{\gamma}}$ | 2 | Yes | Yes | No | **Offset & letterpress inks** (KTU favorite) |
| Cross | $\dfrac{\eta_a - \eta_\infty}{\eta_0 - \eta_\infty} = \dfrac{1}{1 + (K\dot{\gamma})^m}$ | 4 | No (implicitly) | Yes | No | Polymer-thickened inks |
| Thixotropic | $\tau(t) = \eta(\dot{\gamma},t)\dot{\gamma}$ with $\dfrac{d\lambda}{dt} = a(1-\lambda) - b\lambda\dot{\gamma}$ | 3+ | Yes | Yes | Yes | High-quality offset inks |

> [!TIP]
> **For the KTU board exam, expect a direct question asking:** *"Which rheological model best describes a lithographic ink and why?"* — The correct answer is the **Casson model**, because it captures both yield value and shear-thinning in a simple, two-parameter form that matches experimental data over a wide shear-rate range.

---

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Problem (KTU Exam Style)

**Problem:** A printing ink is tested in a cone-and-plate viscometer. The cone angle $\theta = 0.5°$ (in radians: $0.5 \times \pi / 180 = 8.727 \times 10^{-3}$ rad), cone radius $R = 30$ mm = $0.030$ m, angular velocity $\omega = 100$ rad/s, and the measured torque is $T = 0.045$ N·m. Calculate the viscosity.

**Step 1 — Write the cone-and-plate formula:**

$$\eta = \frac{3T\theta}{2\pi\omega R^3}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute:**

$$\eta = \frac{3 \times 0.045 \times 8.727 \times 10^{-3}}{2\pi \times 100 \times (0.030)^3}$$

**Step 3 — Numerator:**

$$3 \times 0.045 \times 8.727 \times 10^{-3} = 1.178 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 4 — Denominator:**

$$2\pi \times 100 \times 2.7 \times 10^{-5} = 628.32 \times 2.7 \times 10^{-5} = 1.696 \times 10^{-2}$$

**Step 5 — Divide:**

$$\eta = \frac{1.178 \times 10^{-3}}{1.696 \times 10^{-2}} = 0.0695 \, \text{Pa·s} = 69.5 \, \text{cP}$$

**Step 6 — Interpret:** A viscosity of ~70 cP is typical for a **screen printing ink** at moderate shear.

**Conversion Logic:** The cone-and-plate geometry gives **uniform shear rate** throughout the sample (independent of radius), making it the gold standard for **absolute** viscosity measurement of non-Newtonian fluids.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Style]

> **Define rheology. List the three primary rheological properties that govern the print quality of a lithographic ink.**

**Model Answer:**

Rheology is the science that deals with the flow and deformation of matter under applied stress. In the printing-ink context, it describes the behavior of the ink as it moves from the duct, through the roller train, into the printing nip, and finally onto the substrate.

The three primary rheological properties governing the print quality of a lithographic ink are:

1. **Viscosity (η):** Resistance to flow at a given shear rate. Determines ink feeding, transfer, and leveling.
2. **Yield Value (τ₀):** The minimum stress required to initiate flow. Prevents ink from dripping in the duct and from slinging off fast-rotating rollers.
3. **Thixotropy:** Time-dependent structural breakdown and recovery. Ensures the ink is fluid in the nip but regains body on the paper to set sharply.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]

> **Differentiate between pseudoplastic and dilatant flow behavior with one example each from printing ink applications.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Pseudoplastic | Dilatant |
|---|---|---|
| Effect of increasing shear rate | Viscosity **decreases** | Viscosity **increases** |
| Power-law index $n$ | $n < 1$ | $n > 1$ |
| Shape of flow curve | Concave toward shear rate axis | Convex toward shear rate axis |
| Print application example | Most **lithographic, flexographic, and gravure** inks | Rare; occasionally in highly pigmented letterpress inks |
| Industrial example | Ketchup, polymer solutions | Wet sand, starch suspensions |

Pseudoplastic behavior is **desirable** in printing inks because it gives low viscosity at high shear (good nip transfer) and high viscosity at low shear (good duct retention). Dilatant behavior is **undesirable** as it causes roller slip and poor film splitting.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> **KTU Pattern:** Each 14-mark question has two sub-parts of 7 marks each. The student answers **either** Question A **or** Question B in the exam hall.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Sample Paper Style]

> **(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **classification of fluid flow behavior** based on the rheogram (shear stress vs. shear rate plot). Identify the type of flow exhibited by a typical **offset (lithographic) ink** and justify your answer. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Derive the **Hagen–Poiseuille equation** for viscosity determination using a capillary viscometer, clearly stating the assumptions made. An ink is forced through a capillary of length $L = 0.5$ m and radius $R = 1$ mm under a pressure head of $P = 10$ kPa. A volume $V = 5$ cm³ of ink is collected in $t = 30$ s. Calculate the viscosity of the ink. **(7 Marks)**

#### 📘 Model Solution to Part (a):

A rheogram plots **shear stress (τ)** on the y-axis against **shear rate ($\dot{\gamma}$)** on the x-axis. The shape of this curve classifies the fluid into one of five categories:

1. **Newtonian:** A straight line passing through the origin. Slope = $\eta$. Example: water, dilute oils.
2. **Pseudoplastic (shear-thinning):** A curve concave to the shear-rate axis, starting steep and flattening. No yield value. Example: polymer solutions, flexo inks.
3. **Dilatant (shear-thickening):** A curve convex to the shear-rate axis, becoming steeper. Example: starch suspensions, wet sand.
4. **Bingham Plastic:** A straight line that does NOT pass through the origin. It intercepts the y-axis at $\tau_0$ (yield value). After yielding, the slope = $\eta_{pl}$. Example: toothpaste, grease.
5. **Thixotropic:** A pseudoplastic/plastic flow that, when sheared and then relaxed, traces a **hysteresis loop** on the rheogram. The area of the loop is proportional to the **thixotropy**.

**A typical offset ink exhibits thixotropic pseudoplastic flow with a measurable yield value** — best described by the **Casson model**. Justification: The ink must not drip in the duct (yield value), must be fluid under the high shear in the nip (pseudoplastic), and must recover viscosity on the paper to prevent set-off (thixotropy).

#### 📘 Model Solution to Part (b):

**Assumptions:**
- Steady, laminar, incompressible, isothermal flow.
- Newtonian behavior (or use of apparent viscosity for non-Newtonian).
- Fully developed flow (no end effects).
- No-slip at the capillary wall.

**Derivation (as in Section 3.4):**

$$\eta = \frac{\pi R^4 P t}{8 L V}$$

**Numerical calculation:**

Given: $R = 1$ mm = $10^{-3}$ m, $L = 0.5$ m, $P = 10$ kPa = $10^4$ Pa, $V = 5$ cm³ = $5 \times 10^{-6}$ m³, $t = 30$ s.

$$\eta = \frac{\pi \times (10^{-3})^4 \times 10^4 \times 30}{8 \times 0.5 \times 5 \times 10^{-6}}$$

**Step 1 — Numerator:**

$$\pi \times 10^{-12} \times 10^4 \times 30 = 30\pi \times 10^{-8} = 9.425 \times 10^{-7}$$

**Step 2 — Denominator:**

$$8 \times 0.5 \times 5 \times 10^{-6} = 2 \times 10^{-5}$$

**Step 3 — Divide:**

$$\eta = \frac{9.425 \times 10^{-7}}{2 \times 10^{-5}} = 0.0471 \, \text{Pa·s} = 47.1 \, \text{cP}$$

**Answer:** The ink viscosity is approximately **0.047 Pa·s (47 cP)** — a typical value for a **letterpress or low-tack offset ink** at moderate shear.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Sample Paper Style — Alternative Choice]

> **(a)** Explain the **Casson model** for printing ink rheology. How is the **yield value** and **infinite-shear viscosity** determined experimentally from a Casson plot? Why is the Casson model preferred over the simple Bingham model for offset inks? **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** With a neat diagram, describe the **cone-and-plate viscometer**. An ink is tested in a cone-and-plate viscometer with cone angle $\theta = 0.5°$, radius $R = 25$ mm, and angular speed $\omega = 50$ rad/s. The torque measured is $T = 0.02$ N·m. Calculate the viscosity. State two advantages of the cone-and-plate geometry over the capillary viscometer. **(7 Marks)**

#### 📘 Model Solution to Part (a):

The **Casson model** is an empirical two-parameter rheological equation given by:

$$\sqrt{\tau} = \sqrt{\tau_0} + \sqrt{\eta_\infty \cdot \dot{\gamma}}$$

Where:
- $\tau_0$ = Casson yield value (Pa)
- $\eta_\infty$ = Casson viscosity at infinite shear (Pa·s)
- Both parameters are **rheologically significant**: $\tau_0$ prevents dripping; $\eta_\infty$ governs the ink's high-shear film behavior in the nip.

**Experimental determination:**
A series of $(\dot{\gamma}, \tau)$ data points are collected on a rheometer. The data is plotted as $\sqrt{\tau}$ (y-axis) vs. $\sqrt{\dot{\gamma}}$ (x-axis). The plot gives a **straight line** whose:
- **y-intercept** = $\sqrt{\tau_0}$
- **slope** = $\sqrt{\eta_\infty}$

**Why preferred over Bingham:** The Bingham model is purely linear and cannot capture the shear-thinning seen in offset inks. The Casson model has a curved $\tau$ vs. $\dot{\gamma}$ plot (square-root dependence) that fits real offset-ink data much better, while still being mathematically simple.

#### 📘 Model Solution to Part (b):

**Cone-and-plate viscometer description:**

A small-angle cone ($\theta < 3°$, typically $0.5°$ to $2°$) is rotated against a flat stationary plate with a thin film of ink in the gap. The torque required to maintain a constant angular velocity is measured and converted to viscosity.

**Working formula:**

$$\eta = \frac{3T\theta}{2\pi\omega R^3}$$

**Numerical calculation:**

Given: $\theta = 0.5° = 0.5 \times \pi / 180 = 8.727 \times 10^{-3}$ rad, $R = 25$ mm = $0.025$ m, $\omega = 50$ rad/s, $T = 0.02$ N·m.

**Step 1 — Numerator:**

$$3 \times 0.02 \times 8.727 \times 10^{-3} = 5.236 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 2 — Denominator:**

$$2\pi \times 50 \times (0.025)^3 = 314.16 \times 1.5625 \times 10^{-5} = 4.909 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 3 — Divide:**

$$\eta = \frac{5.236 \times 10^{-4}}{4.909 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.1067 \, \text{Pa·s} \approx 0.107 \, \text{Pa·s} = 107 \, \text{cP}$$

**Two advantages of cone-and-plate over capillary viscometer:**
1. **Uniform shear rate** throughout the sample (independent of radial position) — no Rabinowitsch correction needed.
2. **Small sample size** required (~0.1 to 1 mL), ideal for expensive or rare ink formulations.
3. (Bonus) Direct measurement at controlled, high shear rates, matching printing-nip conditions.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks in Rheology questions:**
> 1. **Confusing viscosity with tack.** Viscosity is a low-to-moderate shear property (Pa·s); tack is a high-shear film-splitting property (arbitrary units). Do NOT use them interchangeably.
> 2. **Forgetting units.** Viscosity in cP must be converted to Pa·s (÷ 1000) for SI consistency. Pressure in kPa must be converted to Pa (× 1000).
> 3. **Writing the Bingham equation without stating the condition** "for $\tau > \tau_0$." Examiners specifically deduct for this.
> 4. **Skipping the $\log \tau$ vs. $\log \dot{\gamma}$ transformation** when explaining the power-law index. The KTU examiner expects you to mention that a log-log plot is used to extract $n$ and $K$.
> 5. **Confusing thixotropy with pseudoplasticity.** Pseudoplasticity is an *instantaneous* property; thixotropy is a *time-dependent* property. Many students write the wrong word and lose 2-3 marks.
> 6. **Not labeling axes** in the rheogram sketch. Always write "Shear stress (Pa)" on the y-axis and "Shear rate (s⁻¹)" on the x-axis.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Print this and revise 5 minutes before the exam.**

- [x] **Rheology = flow + deformation of matter** under applied stress.
- [x] **Three key properties:** Viscosity (η), Yield Value (τ₀), Thixotropy.
- [x] **Newton's Law:** $\tau = \eta \dot{\gamma}$ — the foundation.
- [x] **Newtonian fluid:** constant η. **Pseudoplastic:** η ↓ with shear. **Dilatant:** η ↑ with shear. **Plastic (Bingham):** needs τ₀ to flow. **Thixotropic:** time-dependent η recovery.
- [x] **Power Law (Ostwald):** $\tau = K \dot{\gamma}^{\,n}$ — $n < 1$ = pseudoplastic.
- [x] **Casson Model:** $\sqrt{\tau} = \sqrt{\tau_0} + \sqrt{\eta_\infty \dot{\gamma}}$ — **the** model for offset ink.
- [x] **Bingham Plastic:** $\tau = \tau_0 + \eta_{pl} \dot{\gamma}$ — used for letterpress ink.
- [x] **Tack** = resistance to film splitting at high shear; measured by **Inkometer**.
- [x] **Capillary viscometer** → Hagen–Poiseuille: $\eta = \pi R^4 P t / (8 L V)$.
- [x] **Cone-and-plate viscometer** → $\eta = 3T\theta / (2\pi \omega R^3)$; uniform shear, small sample.
- [x] **Brookfield viscometer** → routine QC; uses spindle + rotational speed.
- [x] **Falling-rod viscometer** → used for opaque, dark inks.
- [x] **Thixotropy Index (TI)** = viscosity at low speed ÷ viscosity at high speed.
- [x] **Units:** 1 Pa·s = 10 P = 1000 cP. 1 dyne/cm² = 0.1 Pa.
- [x] **Shear rate ranges:** Gravure (10⁵ s⁻¹) > Offset (10⁴–10⁶ s⁻¹) > Flexo (10³–10⁴ s⁻¹) > Letterpress (10³ s⁻¹) > Screen (10¹–10² s⁻¹) > Inkjet (10³–10⁵ s⁻¹).
- [x] **Golden rule for ink design:** "Stiff in the duct, fluid in the nip, stiff again on the paper."
- [x] **Hysteresis loop** in rheogram = signature of thixotropy.
- [x] **Casson plot** = $\sqrt{\tau}$ vs. $\sqrt{\dot{\gamma}}$ straight line; intercept = $\sqrt{\tau_0}$, slope = $\sqrt{\eta_\infty}$.
- [x] **Log-log plot** of τ vs. $\dot{\gamma}$ gives slope $n$ (flow index) and intercept $K$ (consistency index).
- [x] **Ink transfer uniformity, dot sharpness, gloss, set-off** — all are rheology-controlled.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Viscosity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPOT866 - TECHNOLOGY OF PRINTING INKS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Viscosity ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Viscosity** is the measure of a fluid's internal resistance to flow (deformation under shear stress). Mathematically, it is the proportionality constant in **Newton's Law of Viscosity** that relates the shear stress ($\tau$) experienced by a fluid to the velocity gradient ($\frac{du}{dy}$) perpendicular to the flow direction. For a printing ink, viscosity governs how the ink atomizes at the press, how it transfers from the fountain to the substrate, how it splits at the nip, and how it levels on the paper. A typical offset ink may have a viscosity in the range of **2 to 50 Pa·s** depending on the tack and rheology grade. > [!NOTE] > **Newton's Law of Viscosity:** > $$\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy}$$ > where $\tau$ is the shear stress (Pa), $\mu$ is the dynamic viscosity (Pa·s), and $\frac{du}{dy}$ is the shear rate (s⁻¹). ### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Honey vs Water" Picture Imagine pouring two liquids from identical cups on the same inclined plane: - **Water** runs down quickly, leaving a thin film — it has **low viscosity** and flows easily. - **Honey** creeps down slowly, holds its shape, and forms a thick bead — it has **high viscosity** and resists flow. In a **printing press**, the ink sits between the inking roller and the substrate. The molecules of the ink "drag" each other along. The stronger this intermolecular "friction," the more energy the press must apply to shear and transfer the ink. Too low viscosity → misting, dot gain, set-off. Too high viscosity → picking, poor ink transfer, and ink refusal at the duct. > [!TIP] > **Memory Hook:** *"Viscosity is friction in a fluid."* Just as friction resists the sliding of solids, viscosity resists the sliding (shearing) of fluid layers. ### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Units (Bolded) | Quantity | SI Unit | CGS Unit | Conversion | |---|---|---|---| | Dynamic Viscosity ($\mu$) | **Pa·s** (N·s/m²) | **poise (P)** | 1 Pa·s = 10 P = 1000 cP | | Kinematic Viscosity ($\nu$) | **m²/s** | **stokes (St)** | 1 m²/s = 10⁴ St = 10⁶ cSt | | Shear Stress ($\tau$) | **Pa** (N/m²) | dyne/cm² | 1 Pa = 10 dyne/cm² | | Shear Rate ($\dot{\gamma}$) | **s⁻¹** | s⁻¹ | identical | | **Water at 20 °C** | **1.002 cP** | 1.002 cP | reference benchmark | > [!IMPORTANT] > In printing ink technology, viscosity is almost always reported in **poise (P)** or **centipoise (cP)**, although modern standards (ISO 3219) and rheometer software report **Pa·s**. ### 1.4 Why Viscosity Matters in Printing Inks - **Ink transfer** at the rollers depends on the ratio of ink viscosity to substrate absorbency. - **Droplet formation** in inkjet is controlled by viscosity (typically 3–20 cP for water-based inks). - **Misting** at high press speeds occurs when viscosity is too low. - **Set-off** and **ink refusal** occur when viscosity is too high or too low, respectively. - **Color strength and gloss** depend on proper film leveling, which is governed by viscosity. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Types of Viscosity #### 2.1.1 Dynamic (Absolute) Viscosity ($\mu$) This is the *true* resistance to shearing flow. It arises from intermolecular cohesive forces. Newton's law: $$\tau = \mu \dot{\gamma}$$ where $\dot{\gamma} = \frac{du}{dy}$ is the **shear rate** in reciprocal seconds (s⁻¹). #### 2.1.2 Kinematic Viscosity ($\nu$) Used when gravity-driven flow is considered (e.g., flow through an orifice, capillary). $$\nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho}$$ where $\rho$ is the fluid density (kg/m³). Its SI unit is **m²/s**; CGS unit is the **stokes (St)**. #### 2.1.3 Apparent Viscosity (for Non-Newtonian Inks) Printing inks are almost never Newtonian. The **apparent viscosity** is defined at a specific shear rate: $$\mu_{app} = \frac{\tau}{\dot{\gamma}}$$ It is *not* a constant; it varies with the applied shear rate. ### 2.2 Newtonian vs Non-Newtonian Fluids #### 2.2.1 Newtonian Fluids Constant viscosity regardless of shear rate. Examples: water, mineral oil, low-molecular-weight solvents. #### 2.2.2 Non-Newtonian Fluids Viscosity changes with shear rate. Printing inks fall here, principally in these categories: - **Pseudoplastic (shear-thinning):** Viscosity decreases as shear rate increases. **Most lithographic and gravure inks are pseudoplastic.** Described by the **Ostwald–de Waele (Power Law)** model: $$\tau = K \dot{\gamma}^{n}$$ where $K$ is the consistency index and $n < 1$ is the flow behavior index. - **Dilatant (shear-thickening):** Viscosity increases with shear rate. Found in highly pigmented, dense inks. - **Bingham Plastic:** Behaves as a solid until the **yield stress ($\tau_0$)** is exceeded, then flows as a Newtonian fluid. Paste inks and screen inks follow this. $$\tau = \tau_0 + \mu_{p} \dot{\gamma}$$ - **Thixotropic:** Viscosity decreases with time under constant shear; recovers when shear is removed. **Letterpress and offset inks exhibit thixotropy** — this is critical for ink transfer. - **Rheopectic:** Viscosity increases with time under constant shear (rare in inks). ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield) > [!NOTE] > The following table is the **exam-ready cheat sheet** for viscosity problems. | # | Equation | Description | Application in Printing Inks | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy}$ | Newton's Law | Foundation for all flow calculations | | 2 | $\nu = \mu / \rho$ | Kinematic viscosity | Capillary and falling-ball viscometers | | 3 | $\mu = K \dot{\gamma}^{n-1}$ | Power-law apparent viscosity | Pseudoplastic ink behavior | | 4 | $\tau = K \dot{\gamma}^{n}$ | Ostwald–de Waele equation | Offset & gravure ink rheology | | 5 | $\tau = \tau_0 + \mu_{p} \dot{\gamma}$ | Bingham plastic model | Paste & screen inks | | 6 | $Q = \frac{\pi R^{4} \Delta P}{8 \mu L}$ | Hagen–Poiseuille (volumetric flow rate) | Capillary viscometers | | 7 | $\mu = \frac{\pi R^{4} \Delta P \, t}{8 L V}$ | Hagen–Poiseuille (absolute viscosity) | Capillary viscometer measurement | | 8 | $\mu = K_{b} (\rho_{s} - \rho_{l}) t$ | Falling-ball viscometer | Quick QC checks | | 9 | $Re = \frac{\rho v D}{\mu} = \frac{v D}{\nu}$ | Reynolds number | Flow regime (laminar vs turbulent) | | 10 | $T = \frac{4 \pi \mu \omega R^{2}}{x}$ | Rotational viscometer (cone-plate) | Brookfield-type instruments | | 11 | $E_{act} = \frac{R \ln(\mu_1 / \mu_2)}{(1/T_2 - 1/T_1)}$ | Arrhenius viscosity–temperature | Ink stability at press temperature | | 12 | $\mu_{app} = \tau / \dot{\gamma}$ | Apparent viscosity | Non-Newtonian fluids | > [!IMPORTANT] > **In every KTU viscosity numerical, always check units first.** The "trap" is mixing **cP** with **Pa·s**, or using **stokes** when the answer is required in **m²/s**. The conversion 1 Pa·s = 1000 cP and 1 m²/s = 10⁴ St must be memorized. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Ink Formulation:** Viscosity guides the formulator's choice of resin, solvent, and additives. A letterpress ink (10–50 Pa·s) is far more viscous than a flexographic ink (0.05–0.5 Pa·s). - **Press Control:** Modern presses use **in-line viscometers** to maintain ink viscosity within ±5 % during long runs. - **Quality Control:** Brookfield viscometers (rotational) and Zahn/Efflux cups (industrial) are the QC workhorses. - **Substrate Compatibility:** Highly absorbent newsprint tolerates low-viscosity inks; coated paper demands higher viscosity to prevent strike-through. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the Hagen–Poiseuille Equation (Capillary Viscometer) This is the **most frequently asked derivation** in KTU examinations for fluid flow / rheology topics. #### Assumptions - Steady, laminar, incompressible flow. - Newtonian fluid. - Horizontal capillary of radius $R$ and length $L$. - Fully developed flow; no-slip at the wall. #### Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1 — Force balance on a fluid cylinder of radius $r$ and length $L$:** The pressure force pushing the fluid equals the viscous drag force resisting it. $$(\pi r^{2}) \Delta P = \tau (2 \pi r) L$$ **Step 2 — Substitute Newton's law of viscosity:** For a Newtonian fluid, the shear stress is $\tau = \mu \left(-\frac{du}{dr}\right)$. The negative sign appears because velocity decreases as radius increases. $$(\pi r^{2}) \Delta P = \mu \left(-\frac{du}{dr}\right)(2 \pi r) L$$ **Step 3 — Simplify the algebra:** $$\pi r^{2} \Delta P = -2 \pi r L \mu \frac{du}{dr}$$ Divide both sides by $\pi r$: $$r \Delta P = -2 L \mu \frac{du}{dr}$$ **Step 4 — Separate variables and integrate:** $$\frac{du}{dr} = -\frac{\Delta P}{2 \mu L} r$$ Integrate from $r = R$ (wall, $u = 0$) to arbitrary $r$ (velocity $u$): $$\int_{0}^{u} du' = -\frac{\Delta P}{2 \mu L} \int_{R}^{r} r' \, dr'$$ $$u - 0 = -\frac{\Delta P}{2 \mu L} \left[\frac{r'^{2}}{2}\right]_{R}^{r}$$ $$u = -\frac{\Delta P}{4 \mu L} (r^{2} - R^{2})$$ **Step 5 — Apply the no-slip boundary condition (rearrange for a parabolic profile):** $$u(r) = \frac{\Delta P}{4 \mu L} (R^{2} - r^{2})$$ This is the **parabolic velocity profile** characteristic of laminar flow in a tube. **Step 6 — Compute the volumetric flow rate $Q$:** $Q$ is the integral of velocity over the cross-sectional area. $$Q = \int_{0}^{R} u(r) \cdot 2 \pi r \, dr$$ $$Q = \int_{0}^{R} \frac{\Delta P}{4 \mu L} (R^{2} - r^{2}) \cdot 2 \pi r \, dr$$ $$Q = \frac{\pi \Delta P}{2 \mu L} \int_{0}^{R} (R^{2} r - r^{3}) \, dr$$ $$Q = \frac{\pi \Delta P}{2 \mu L} \left[ R^{2} \frac{r^{2}}{2} - \frac{r^{4}}{4} \right]_{0}^{R}$$ $$Q = \frac{\pi \Delta P}{2 \mu L} \left( \frac{R^{4}}{2} - \frac{R^{4}}{4} \right)$$ $$Q = \frac{\pi \Delta P}{2 \mu L} \cdot \frac{R^{4}}{4}$$ $$\boxed{Q = \frac{\pi R^{4} \Delta P}{8 \mu L}}$$ **Step 7 — Solve for viscosity (the viscometer form):** If a volume $V$ flows in time $t$, then $Q = V/t$: $$\mu = \frac{\pi R^{4} \Delta P \, t}{8 L V}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > This is the equation examiners expect. **Do not forget the $R^4$ term** — it dominates the design of capillary viscometers and explains why a doubling of capillary radius increases flow rate **16-fold**. ### 3.2 Derivation of the Falling-Ball Viscometer Equation #### Setup A sphere of density $\rho_s$ and radius $r$ falls through a fluid of density $\rho_l$ and viscosity $\mu$ in a vertical tube of diameter $D$. The sphere reaches **terminal velocity** $v$ when drag equals net weight. #### Force Balance at Terminal Velocity $$\text{Buoyancy-corrected weight} = \text{Stokes drag force}$$ $$\frac{4}{3} \pi r^{3} (\rho_s - \rho_l) g = 6 \pi \mu r v$$ #### Solve for $\mu$ $$\mu = \frac{2 r^{2} (\rho_s - \rho_l) g}{9 v}$$ $$v = \frac{L}{t}$$ where $L$ is the distance of fall and $t$ is the time of fall. $$\boxed{\mu = \frac{2 r^{2} g (\rho_s - \rho_l) t}{9 L}}$$ Often written with a calibration constant $K_b$ (which incorporates the ball radius and geometry): $$\mu = K_b (\rho_s - \rho_l) t$$ > [!WARNING] > The Stokes equation is valid only when the Reynolds number based on the ball $\left(Re_{ball} = \frac{2 r v \rho_l}{\mu}\right)$ is **less than 0.1**. Outside this range, an **Oseen** or **Faxén** correction must be applied. ### 3.3 Worked Example (Numerical) > [!NOTE] > **Problem (KTU Style):** A capillary viscometer has $R = 1.0$ mm, $L = 100$ mm. A pressure difference of 100 Pa drives 5 mL of ink through the capillary in 200 s. Calculate the dynamic viscosity of the ink in Pa·s and in poise. **Step 1 — Convert to SI units.** $$R = 1.0 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}, \quad L = 100 \times 10^{-3} = 0.1 \text{ m}$$ $$V = 5 \text{ mL} = 5 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^{3}, \quad \Delta P = 100 \text{ Pa}, \quad t = 200 \text{ s}$$ **Step 2 — Apply the Hagen–Poiseuille viscometer form.** $$\mu = \frac{\pi R^{4} \Delta P \, t}{8 L V}$$ Compute $R^{4}$: $$R^{4} = (10^{-3})^{4} = 10^{-12} \text{ m}^{4}$$ **Step 3 — Substitute and evaluate.** $$\mu = \frac{\pi \times 10^{-12} \times 100 \times 200}{8 \times 0.1 \times 5 \times 10^{-6}}$$ Numerator: $$\pi \times 10^{-12} \times 100 \times 200 = \pi \times 2 \times 10^{-8}$$ Denominator: $$8 \times 0.1 \times 5 \times 10^{-6} = 4 \times 10^{-6}$$ **Step 4 — Combine.** $$\mu = \frac{\pi \times 2 \times 10^{-8}}{4 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{\pi \times 2 \times 10^{-8}}{4 \times 10^{-6}}$$ $$\mu = \frac{\pi}{2} \times 10^{-2} = 0.5 \pi \times 10^{-2} \text{ Pa·s}$$ $$\mu \approx 1.5708 \times 10^{-2} \text{ Pa·s} = 0.0157 \text{ Pa·s}$$ **Step 5 — Convert to poise.** $$1 \text{ Pa·s} = 10 \text{ P}$$ $$\mu = 0.0157 \times 10 = 0.157 \text{ P} = 15.7 \text{ cP}$$ > [!TIP] > **Examiner Check:** A 15.7 cP ink is in the flexographic / water-based ink range — physically reasonable. ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Viscometer Selection | Viscometer Type | Best for | Shear Rate Range | Ink Application | |---|---|---|---| | **Capillary (Ubbelohde, Ostwald)** | Low-viscosity Newtonian fluids | 1 – 10⁴ s⁻¹ | Solvents, varnishes | | **Falling-ball (Höppler)** | Moderate viscosity, transparent fluids | 1 – 100 s⁻¹ | QC of letterpress inks | | **Rotational (Brookfield)** | Non-Newtonian, opaque fluids | 0.1 – 1000 s⁻¹ | Most printing inks | | **Cone-and-plate** | High-shear, small samples | 1 – 10⁵ s⁻¹ | Offset ink research | | **Efflux cup (Zahn, Ford)** | Quick field checks | Single shear rate | Press-side viscosity | | **Stormer (Krebs)** | Paste inks (KU units) | 100 – 400 s⁻¹ | Lithographic inks | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Newtonian vs Non-Newtonian Flow Curves (Rheograms) ```mermaid flowchart TD start1["Plot Shear Stress vs Shear Rate"] newt["Newtonian: straight line
passing through origin
slope = mu"] pseudo["Pseudoplastic: curve
concave downward
tau = K gamma to power n, n less than 1"] dilat["Dilatant: curve
concave upward
tau = K gamma to power n, n greater than 1"] bing["Bingham: straight line
with yield stress intercept
tau = tau0 + mu_p gamma"] start1 --> newt start1 --> pseudo start1 --> dilat start1 --> bing ``` > [!NOTE] > The slope of the **Newtonian** line is the constant dynamic viscosity. For non-Newtonian fluids, the *slope at any point* gives the **apparent viscosity** at that shear rate. ### 4.2 Viscometer Classification Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD viscometer["Viscometer Selection"] cap["Capillary Type
Hagen Poiseuille"] fb["Falling Ball Type
Stokes Law"] rot["Rotational Type
Brookfield"] cp["Cone and Plate
High Shear Research"] eff["Efflux Cup
Zahn Ford"] storm["Stormer Krebs
Paste Inks"] viscometer --> cap viscometer --> fb viscometer --> rot viscometer --> cp viscometer --> eff viscometer --> storm cap --> capA["Ubbelohde"] cap --> capB["Ostwald"] cap --> capC["Cannon Fenske"] fb --> fbA["Hoppler"] rot --> rotA["Brookfield"] rot --> rotB["Haake Rotovisco"] ``` ### 4.3 Block-Level Architecture: Viscosity's Role in the Printing Process ```mermaid flowchart LR inkForm["Ink Formulation
Resin + Pigment + Solvent + Additives"] --> viscCtrl["Viscosity Control
Brookfield Rheometer"] viscCtrl --> inkDuct["Ink Duct Feed
Shear rate 10 to 100 s-1"] inkDuct --> rollerSplit["Roller Train Splitting
Shear rate 1000 to 10000 s-1"] rollerSplit --> plateNip["Plate to Blanket Nip
Shear rate 100000 s-1"] plateNip --> substrate["Substrate Transfer
and Setting"] substrate --> final["Final Print
Dot Gain and Gloss"] viscCtrl -.-> pressQC["Inline Press QC
Efflux Cup"] pressQC -.-> viscCtrl ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > The **shear rate** the ink experiences varies by **seven orders of magnitude** (10 s⁻¹ at the duct to 10⁵ s⁻¹ at the printing nip). Because printing inks are **pseudoplastic / thixotropic**, the same ink can flow easily in the duct and then "set" rapidly on the substrate. This is the entire reason ink formulation is rheology-driven. ### 4.4 Factors Affecting Viscosity of Printing Inks (Cause-Effect Map) ```mermaid flowchart TD p1["Pigment Loading
Higher loading = Higher viscosity"] p2["Pigment Particle Shape
Acicular = Higher viscosity
Spherical = Lower viscosity"] p3["Resin Type and MW
Higher MW = Higher viscosity"] p4["Solvent Viscosity
Higher solvent viscosity = Higher ink viscosity"] p5["Temperature
Higher temperature = Lower viscosity"] p6["Shear Rate
Pseudoplastic: decreases with shear"] p7["Additives
Waxes and thickeners increase viscosity
Plasticizers decrease viscosity"] p1 --> finalV["Final Ink Viscosity"] p2 --> finalV p3 --> finalV p4 --> finalV p5 --> finalV p6 --> finalV p7 --> finalV ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** **Define viscosity. Distinguish between dynamic viscosity and kinematic viscosity with units.** **Model Answer:** Viscosity is the property of a fluid that resists its deformation under shear stress. It is a measure of internal friction in a fluid. **Dynamic (or absolute) viscosity ($\mu$)** is the ratio of shear stress to shear rate. Its SI unit is the **Pa·s** (or N·s/m²); CGS unit is the **poise (P)**, with 1 Pa·s = 10 poise. **Kinematic viscosity ($\nu$)** is the ratio of dynamic viscosity to fluid density ($\nu = \mu / \rho$). Its SI unit is **m²/s**; CGS unit is the **stokes (St)**, with 1 m²/s = 10⁴ stokes. Dynamic viscosity governs shearing flow; kinematic viscosity governs gravity-driven flow (e.g., capillary efflux). --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **What is meant by a Newtonian fluid? Give two examples of non-Newtonian fluids relevant to printing inks.** **Model Answer:** A **Newtonian fluid** has a viscosity that remains **constant** irrespective of the applied shear rate; its shear stress is directly proportional to the shear rate, satisfying $\tau = \mu \dot{\gamma}$. Examples: water, mineral oil, low-viscosity solvents. **Non-Newtonian fluids** exhibit a shear-rate-dependent viscosity. Two examples relevant to printing inks: 1. **Pseudoplastic (shear-thinning) fluids** — e.g., lithographic and gravure inks. Their viscosity decreases with increasing shear rate, modeled by $\tau = K \dot{\gamma}^{n}$ with $n < 1$. 2. **Bingham plastics** — e.g., paste inks and screen inks. They behave as solids until a yield stress $\tau_0$ is exceeded, then flow linearly: $\tau = \tau_0 + \mu_{p} \dot{\gamma}$. A third relevant behavior is **thixotropy** — viscosity decreases with time at constant shear (offset inks). --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) --- ### Question A (14 Marks) > **[KTU University Exam — May 2023, Modified]** > **(a)** Derive the **Hagen–Poiseuille equation** for laminar flow of a Newtonian fluid through a horizontal capillary. State clearly all assumptions used. **7 marks** > **(b)** A capillary viscometer of radius 0.5 mm and length 150 mm is used to measure the viscosity of an ink. A pressure head of 200 Pa drives 4 mL of ink through the tube in 250 seconds. Calculate the dynamic viscosity in **Pa·s** and **centipoise**. **7 marks** #### Part (a) — Model Solution **Assumptions:** 1. Steady, laminar, incompressible flow. 2. Newtonian fluid behavior. 3. Constant fluid properties. 4. Horizontal capillary with no end effects. 5. No-slip at the wall; fully developed flow. **Derivation (full step-by-step):** Consider a fluid cylinder of radius $r$ inside the capillary. Force balance on this cylinder: $$\text{Pressure force} = \text{Viscous drag force}$$ $$(\pi r^{2}) \Delta P = \tau (2 \pi r) L$$ Substitute Newton's law $\tau = -\mu \dfrac{du}{dr}$: $$(\pi r^{2}) \Delta P = -\mu \dfrac{du}{dr} (2 \pi r) L$$ Simplify: $$r \Delta P = -2 L \mu \dfrac{du}{dr}$$ Rearrange and integrate from the wall ($r = R, u = 0$) to any radius $r$: $$\int_{0}^{u} du' = -\dfrac{\Delta P}{2 \mu L} \int_{R}^{r} r' \, dr'$$ $$u = \dfrac{\Delta P}{4 \mu L} (R^{2} - r^{2})$$ This is the **parabolic velocity profile**. To get the volumetric flow rate: $$Q = \int_{0}^{R} u(r) \cdot 2 \pi r \, dr = \dfrac{\pi \Delta P}{2 \mu L} \int_{0}^{R} (R^{2} r - r^{3}) \, dr$$ $$Q = \dfrac{\pi \Delta P}{2 \mu L} \left[ \dfrac{R^{4}}{2} - \dfrac{R^{4}}{4} \right]$$ $$\boxed{Q = \dfrac{\pi R^{4} \Delta P}{8 \mu L}}$$ This is the **Hagen–Poiseuille equation**. #### Part (b) — Model Solution **Given:** $R = 0.5 \text{ mm} = 0.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} = 5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}$ $L = 150 \text{ mm} = 0.15 \text{ m}$ $\Delta P = 200 \text{ Pa}$ $V = 4 \text{ mL} = 4 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^{3}$ $t = 250 \text{ s}$ **Step 1 — Compute $R^{4}$:** $$R^{4} = (5 \times 10^{-4})^{4} = 625 \times 10^{-16} = 6.25 \times 10^{-14} \text{ m}^{4}$$ **Step 2 — Apply the viscosity form of Hagen–Poiseuille:** $$\mu = \dfrac{\pi R^{4} \Delta P \, t}{8 L V}$$ $$\mu = \dfrac{\pi \times 6.25 \times 10^{-14} \times 200 \times 250}{8 \times 0.15 \times 4 \times 10^{-6}}$$ **Step 3 — Numerator:** $$\pi \times 6.25 \times 10^{-14} \times 5 \times 10^{4} = \pi \times 3.125 \times 10^{-9}$$ $$= 9.8175 \times 10^{-9}$$ **Step 4 — Denominator:** $$8 \times 0.15 \times 4 \times 10^{-6} = 4.8 \times 10^{-6}$$ **Step 5 — Final value:** $$\mu = \dfrac{9.8175 \times 10^{-9}}{4.8 \times 10^{-6}} = 2.045 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Pa·s}$$ $$\mu \approx 2.05 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Pa·s}$$ **Step 6 — Convert to centipoise:** $$1 \text{ Pa·s} = 1000 \text{ cP}$$ $$\mu = 2.05 \times 10^{-3} \times 10^{3} = 2.05 \text{ cP}$$ $$\boxed{\mu \approx 2.05 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Pa·s} = 2.05 \text{ cP}}$$ > [!WARNING] > **Common Pitfall:** Students often forget to convert the volume $V$ from mL to m³. With 4 mL entered as 4, the answer becomes $5.1 \times 10^{5}$ Pa·s — completely unrealistic. **Always write units during substitution.** --- ### Question B (14 Marks) > **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Modified]** > **(a)** With a neat sketch, describe the **Brookfield rotational viscometer** and explain how it is used to determine the viscosity of a printing ink. **7 marks** > **(b)** Discuss the effect of **pigment loading, temperature, and shear rate** on the viscosity of a printing ink. **7 marks** #### Part (a) — Model Solution The **Brookfield rotational viscometer** consists of: - A **spindle** (cylindrical, disc, or T-bar) immersed in the ink sample. - A **motor** that rotates the spindle at selectable speeds (rpm), producing known shear rates. - A **spring** (calibrated torsion spring) that measures the **torque** required to maintain the rotation. - A **dial/display** that reads torque, which is proportional to shear stress. **Working Principle:** The spindle is rotated at a fixed speed through the ink. The viscous drag of the ink exerts a torque on the spindle, twisting the calibrated spring by an angle proportional to the torque. The dial reading is converted to viscosity using the formula: $$\mu = \dfrac{T \times K_{sp}}{\omega}$$ where $T$ is the torque, $K_{sp}$ is the spindle constant, and $\omega$ is the angular velocity. For a **Brookfield cylinder (or cone-and-plate) geometry**, the equation simplifies to: $$\mu = \dfrac{4 \pi \mu \omega R^{2}}{x} \quad \text{(thin-film form)}$$ where $R$ is the radius and $x$ is the gap thickness. **Procedure for printing ink:** 1. Maintain the sample at 25 °C in a temperature-controlled bath. 2. Select the appropriate spindle and speed based on expected viscosity. 3. Immerse the spindle to the marked depth; allow the reading to stabilize. 4. Record the dial reading; convert to viscosity using the supplied conversion table. 5. For thixotropic inks, take readings at fixed time intervals to plot the **thixotropic loop**. **Sketch (described textually — rotational viscometer):** - A vertical spindle hangs from a calibrated torsion spring. - The spindle is immersed in a beaker of ink. - The motor above drives the spindle through the spring. - A graduated dial at the top displays torque (proportional to shear stress). #### Part (b) — Model Solution **1. Effect of Pigment Loading:** The viscosity of a printing ink increases with pigment concentration. Pigment particles obstruct the flow of binder and solvent, increasing internal friction. The relationship is non-linear; above the **critical pigment volume concentration (CPVC)**, viscosity rises sharply because the binder can no longer fully wet the pigment surface, leaving air voids. High pigment loading also increases **thixotropy** and yield stress. **2. Effect of Temperature:** Viscosity decreases with increasing temperature. This is described by the **Arrhenius-type equation**: $$\mu = A \, e^{E_{act} / RT}$$ where $A$ is a constant, $E_{act}$ is the activation energy of flow, $R$ is the gas constant, and $T$ is absolute temperature. In practice, a 10 °C rise in temperature may reduce the viscosity of an offset ink by 20–40 %. Press operators therefore control ink temperature to maintain consistent transfer and color. **3. Effect of Shear Rate:** Most printing inks are **pseudoplastic (shear-thinning)**. As the shear rate increases: - At the **ink duct** (low shear, ~10 s⁻¹): viscosity is high, ink stays in the duct. - At the **roller train** (medium shear, 10³ s⁻¹): viscosity decreases, allowing the ink to spread. - At the **printing nip** (very high shear, 10⁵ s⁻¹): viscosity is lowest, enabling film splitting and transfer. - After transfer, the ink must **recover its viscosity quickly** to set on the paper and avoid set-off. This recovery is governed by **thixotropy**. The relationship is modeled by the **Ostwald–de Waele (power-law)** model: $\mu_{app} = K \dot{\gamma}^{n-1}$, where $n < 1$ for pseudoplastic behavior. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often write that "viscosity always decreases with shear rate." This is wrong for **dilatant** and **rheopectic** fluids. Always specify the type of fluid first, then state the shear-rate behavior. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Marks-Loss Areas > [!WARNING] > **Top reasons students lose marks in viscosity questions (KTU pattern):** > > 1. **Unit errors:** Mixing cP, P, and Pa·s; mixing cm²/s and m²/s. Always state the conversion factor. > 2. **Missing the $R^4$ factor:** Forgetting to raise the radius to the fourth power in Hagen–Poiseuille. > 3. **No-slip omission:** Forgetting the no-slip boundary condition when deriving velocity profiles. > 4. **Mis-identifying fluid type:** Calling thixotropy a type of fluid (it is a time-dependent behavior, not a fluid classification). > 5. **Using $\tau$ as viscosity:** Conflating shear stress with viscosity. They are dimensionally different. > 6. **Skipping assumption statements:** Every derivation must begin with **a list of assumptions** (laminar, steady, Newtonian, etc.). Examiners allocate 1–2 marks for this. > 7. **Forgetting the negative sign** in $\tau = -\mu \frac{du}{dr}$. Many derive the *magnitude* but miss the sign convention. > 8. **Not linking to printing applications:** A generic answer without mention of ink transfer, fountain feed, or substrate setting scores low on the application dimension. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist > [!TIP] > **High-Density Revision Notes — Viscosity in Printing Inks** - **Viscosity** = internal friction of a fluid; resistance to flow under shear stress. - **Newton's Law:** $\tau = \mu \dot{\gamma}$. - **Dynamic viscosity** units: **Pa·s (SI)**, **poise (CGS)**; 1 Pa·s = 10 P = 1000 cP. - **Kinematic viscosity** $\nu = \mu / \rho$; units: **m²/s** or **stokes**; 1 m²/s = 10⁴ St. - **Newtonian fluids** — constant viscosity (water, mineral oil). - **Non-Newtonian fluids** — shear-rate dependent viscosity: - **Pseudoplastic** (shear-thinning, $n < 1$) — offset, gravure, flexo inks. - **Dilatant** (shear-thickening, $n > 1$) — rare, highly loaded systems. - **Bingham plastic** (yield stress $\tau_0$) — paste inks, screen inks. - **Thixotropic** (time-dependent, shear-thinning) — letterpress, offset inks. - **Rheopectic** (time-dependent, shear-thickening) — rare in inks. - **Power-law (Ostwald–de Waele):** $\tau = K \dot{\gamma}^{n}$. - **Bingham equation:** $\tau = \tau_0 + \mu_{p} \dot{\gamma}$. - **Hagen–Poiseuille (capillary):** $Q = \dfrac{\pi R^{4} \Delta P}{8 \mu L}$; viscosity form: $\mu = \dfrac{\pi R^{4} \Delta P \, t}{8 L V}$. - **Falling-ball (Stokes):** $\mu = \dfrac{2 r^{2} g (\rho_s - \rho_l) t}{9 L}$; valid for $Re < 0.1$. - **Rotational (Brookfield):** Uses calibrated spindle; torque proportional to viscosity. - **Reynolds number:** $Re = \dfrac{\rho v D}{\mu}$; laminar flow if $Re < 2100$ in pipes. - **Arrhenius temperature dependence:** $\mu = A e^{E_{act}/RT}$ — viscosity drops with temperature. - **Apparent viscosity** for non-Newtonians: $\mu_{app} = \tau / \dot{\gamma}$ at a given shear rate. - **Printing relevance:** - Ink duct → low shear (10 s⁻¹) → high viscosity. - Printing nip → very high shear (10⁵ s⁻¹) → low viscosity → film splits. - Substrate → low shear → viscosity recovers → ink sets. - **Common ink viscosity ranges:** - News ink: 1–5 Pa·s. - Offset ink: 5–50 Pa·s. - Gravure ink: 0.05–0.5 Pa·s. - Flexo ink: 0.05–0.2 Pa·s. - Screen ink: 2–10 Pa·s. - **Factors increasing viscosity:** higher pigment loading, higher resin MW, lower temperature, acicular pigments, waxes. - **Factors decreasing viscosity:** higher temperature, more solvent, plasticizers, spherical pigments, increased shear (for pseudoplastics). - **Reynolds number warning:** $R^4$ in Hagen–Poiseuille — a tiny change in capillary radius drastically changes flow rate.

topics.title: Power Law
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT866 - TECHNOLOGY OF PRINTING INKS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Power Law

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Power Law** (also known as the **Ostwald–de Waele model**) is a constitutive rheological equation that mathematically describes the flow behaviour of non-Newtonian fluids by relating shear stress ($\tau$) to shear rate ($\dot{\gamma}$) through two empirical constants: the **consistency index** ($K$) and the **flow behaviour index** ($n$).

The canonical form of the equation is:

$$\tau = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{n}$$

where:
- $\tau$ = Shear stress (Pa)
- $\dot{\gamma}$ = Shear rate (s⁻¹)
- $K$ = Consistency index (Pa·sⁿ) — a measure of the fluid's resistance to flow
- $n$ = Power-law (flow behaviour) index (dimensionless)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In printing ink technology, **n < 1** (shear-thinning / pseudoplastic) is the most common case. A typical lithographic or letterpress ink shows $n$ values between **0.4 and 0.7**, while gravure and flexographic inks behave near-Newtonian ($n \approx 0.95$–$1.0$).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Honey-and-Spoon Model

Imagine stirring a jar of honey with a spoon:
- When you **stir slowly** (low shear rate), the honey feels extremely thick and resists motion — viscosity appears very high.
- When you **stir rapidly** (high shear rate), the honey flows more freely — viscosity appears to drop.

This "thick when slow, thin when fast" behaviour is what we call **pseudoplasticity**, and the Power Law is the mathematical equation that captures exactly this phenomenon. The constant $n$ tells us *how dramatically* the fluid thins under shear, while $K$ tells us *how viscous the fluid is overall*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why printing inks MUST be shear-thinning:**
> 1. At rest in the duct/fountain → high viscosity (no drip, no bleed).
> 2. Inside the nip / under the doctor blade → low viscosity (smooth transfer).
> 3. After deposition on substrate → viscosity recovers → sharp dot, no mottle.

### 1.3 Apparent Viscosity from Power Law

Dividing the Power Law by shear rate gives the **apparent viscosity** $\eta_a$, which is the viscosity measured at a specific shear rate:

$$\eta_a = \frac{\tau}{\dot{\gamma}} = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{(n-1)}$$

This is critical in ink rheology because printing inks **do not have a single viscosity value** — their viscosity is shear-rate dependent.

| Regime | Behaviour | $n$ value | Printing Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| Newtonian | Viscosity constant | $n = 1$ | Water, low-viscosity gravure ink |
| Shear-thinning | Viscosity decreases | $n < 1$ | Lithographic, letterpress inks |
| Shear-thickening | Viscosity increases | $n > 1$ | Highly pigmented inks (rare) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For a Newtonian fluid, the Power Law degenerates to **Newton's Law of Viscosity** with $K = \mu$ (dynamic viscosity).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Breakdown — Understanding Each Term

**Step 1: Shear Stress ($\tau$)**
- Force per unit area acting tangentially on a fluid layer.
- Generated inside the ink duct, fountain roller nip, and ink-substrate transfer zone.
- Units: **Pa** (N/m²).

**Step 2: Shear Rate ($\dot{\gamma}$)**
- Velocity gradient perpendicular to the flow direction.
- Different zones in a printing press generate vastly different shear rates:
  - Duct storage: $\dot{\gamma} \approx 1$–$10$ s⁻¹
  - Fountain roller: $\dot{\gamma} \approx 10^{2}$–$10^{3}$ s⁻¹
  - Roller nip: $\dot{\gamma} \approx 10^{4}$–$10^{5}$ s⁻¹
  - Doctor blade / gravure cell fill: $\dot{\gamma} \approx 10^{5}$–$10^{6}$ s⁻¹

**Step 3: Consistency Index ($K$)**
- Represents the **overall viscosity level** of the fluid.
- Higher $K$ → thicker / more viscous ink.
- Units: **Pa·sⁿ** (note that the exponent $n$ enters the units, so $K$ is NOT simply a viscosity).

**Step 4: Power Law Index ($n$)**
- Describes the **degree of non-Newtonian behaviour**.
- $n = 1$: Newtonian.
- $n < 1$: Pseudoplastic (shear-thinning) — **most printing inks**.
- $n > 1$: Dilatant (shear-thickening) — rare; seen in highly concentrated suspensions.
- Limit $n \to 0$: extremely shear-thinning (plug flow); $n \to \infty$: rigid solid.

### 2.2 The Logarithmic Form — Experimental Determination

Taking logarithms of $\tau = K \dot{\gamma}^{n}$:

$$\log \tau = \log K + n \cdot \log \dot{\gamma}$$

This transforms the **curved rheogram into a straight line** on log–log axes:
- **Slope** of the line = $n$
- **Intercept** (at $\log \dot{\gamma} = 0$) = $\log K$

This linearization is the standard method used by rheometers and viscometers (e.g., Brookfield, cone-and-plate) to determine $K$ and $n$ experimentally.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\tau = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{n}$ | Power Law (fundamental) | Pa |
| 2 | $\eta_a = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{(n-1)}$ | Apparent viscosity at shear rate $\dot{\gamma}$ | Pa·s |
| 3 | $\log \tau = \log K + n \log \dot{\gamma}$ | Log-linear form (plot form) | — |
| 4 | $n = \dfrac{\log \tau_2 - \log \tau_1}{\log \dot{\gamma}_2 - \log \dot{\gamma}_1}$ | Slope of log-log rheogram | dimensionless |
| 5 | $K = \tau / \dot{\gamma}^{n}$ | Consistency index from a single point | Pa·sⁿ |
| 6 | For Newtonian: $n = 1$, $K = \mu$ | Reduces to Newton's Law | Pa·s |
| 7 | $\dot{\gamma}_{\max}$ inside nip $\approx v/h$ | Shear rate in press nip (v = velocity, h = film thickness) | s⁻¹ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical exam note:** $K$ depends on $n$. You cannot directly compare $K$ values between two inks unless their $n$ values are identical. Always report $K$ and $n$ as a **pair**.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Printing

The Power Law governs **ink transfer efficiency** in every printing process:
- **Offset / Litho:** Ink must thin under high nip shear, then regain viscosity → sharp dot reproduction. Controlled by $n \approx 0.5$–$0.7$.
- **Gravure:** Ink must release cleanly from engraved cells → $n \approx 0.95$–$1.0$ (near-Newtonian with low $K$).
- **Flexo:** Similar to gravure but lower viscosity → $K \approx 0.05$–$0.5$ Pa·sⁿ.
- **Screen printing:** Very thick paste, extreme shear-thinning → $n \approx 0.3$–$0.5$.
- **Letterpress:** Pseudoplastic with quick viscosity recovery after shear.

The choice of **pigment loading, vehicle viscosity, additives (thixotropes, waxes), and solvent system** all shift $K$ and $n$, allowing the ink formulator to "dial in" the rheology for a specific press and substrate.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Apparent Viscosity from the Power Law

Starting from the defining equation and the definition of apparent viscosity:

$$\tau = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{n}$$

By definition, apparent viscosity equals shear stress divided by shear rate:

$$\eta_a = \frac{\tau}{\dot{\gamma}}$$

Substituting the Power Law expression for $\tau$:

$$\eta_a = \frac{K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{n}}{\dot{\gamma}}$$

Since $\dot{\gamma}^{n} / \dot{\gamma}^{1} = \dot{\gamma}^{(n-1)}$, we obtain:

$$\boxed{\eta_a = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{(n-1)}}$$

**Behavioural check:**
- If $n = 1$: $\eta_a = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{0} = K = \mu$ (constant) → Newtonian. ✔
- If $n < 1$: exponent $(n-1) < 0$, so $\eta_a$ decreases as $\dot{\gamma}$ increases → shear-thinning. ✔
- If $n > 1$: exponent $(n-1) > 0$, so $\eta_a$ increases with $\dot{\gamma}$ → shear-thickening. ✔

### 3.2 Derivation of the Log-Linear Form

Take the natural logarithm (base 10 used in plotting) of both sides of the Power Law:

$$\ln \tau = \ln (K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{n})$$

Apply the logarithm product rule:

$$\ln \tau = \ln K + \ln(\dot{\gamma}^{n})$$

Apply the logarithm power rule:

$$\ln \tau = \ln K + n \cdot \ln \dot{\gamma}$$

Converting to base-10 (for direct plot reading):

$$\log \tau = \log K + n \cdot \log \dot{\gamma}$$

This is the equation of a **straight line** $y = mx + c$ where:
- $y = \log \tau$
- $x = \log \dot{\gamma}$
- Slope $m = n$
- Intercept $c = \log K$

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Style

**Problem:** A printing ink shows the following rheometer data:

| Shear rate $\dot{\gamma}$ (s⁻¹) | Shear stress $\tau$ (Pa) |
|---|---|
| 100 | 25 |
| 1000 | 130 |

Determine (a) the power-law index $n$, (b) the consistency index $K$, and (c) the apparent viscosity at $\dot{\gamma} = 500$ s⁻¹.

**Solution (a) — Power-law index $n$:**

Using the slope formula on the log-log plot:

$$n = \frac{\log \tau_2 - \log \tau_1}{\log \dot{\gamma}_2 - \log \dot{\gamma}_1}$$

Substituting the values:

$$n = \frac{\log 130 - \log 25}{\log 1000 - \log 100}$$

Computing the numerator and denominator:

$$n = \frac{2.1139 - 1.3979}{3.0000 - 2.0000}$$

$$n = \frac{0.7160}{1.0000} = 0.716$$

$$\boxed{n \approx 0.72 \text{ (shear-thinning ink)}}$$

**Solution (b) — Consistency index $K$:**

From the Power Law, $K = \tau / \dot{\gamma}^{n}$. Using point 1:

$$K = \frac{25}{(100)^{0.716}}$$

$$K = \frac{25}{25.119}$$

$$\boxed{K \approx 0.995 \text{ Pa·s}^{0.72} \approx 1.0 \text{ Pa·s}^{n}}$$

**Verification with point 2:**

$$\tau = 1.0 \times (1000)^{0.716} = 1.0 \times 130.0 = 130 \text{ Pa} \quad \checkmark$$

**Solution (c) — Apparent viscosity at $\dot{\gamma} = 500$ s⁻¹:**

$$\eta_a = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{(n-1)} = 1.0 \times (500)^{(0.716 - 1)}$$

$$\eta_a = 1.0 \times (500)^{-0.284}$$

$$\eta_a = 1.0 \times \frac{1}{(500)^{0.284}}$$

$$(500)^{0.284} = 7.81$$

$$\boxed{\eta_a \approx 0.128 \text{ Pa·s at 500 s}^{-1}}$$

Compare with the apparent viscosity at $\dot{\gamma} = 100$ s⁻¹:

$$\eta_a(100) = 1.0 \times (100)^{-0.284} = 0.25 \text{ Pa·s}$$

The viscosity **halved** when shear rate increased 5-fold — confirming pseudoplastic behaviour.

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Fluid Type | $n$ | $\eta_a$ trend | Log-log rheogram | Printing Ink Match |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Newtonian | $1$ | Constant | Horizontal line of slope 1 | Water, some gravure inks |
| Pseudoplastic | $< 1$ | Decreasing | Straight line slope < 1 | Litho, letterpress, screen |
| Dilatant | $> 1$ | Increasing | Straight line slope > 1 | Highly loaded pastes (rare) |
| Bingham plastic | — | Plastic flow | Yield stress + linear | Pastes (not Power Law) |
| Casson fluid | — | Yield + shear-thin | Curved with yield | Some paste inks |

> [!NOTE]
> The Power Law **fails** to predict a yield stress. For inks with significant yield stress (e.g., heavy paste inks), the **Casson model** or **Herschel–Bulkley model** is more appropriate. This is a frequent viva question at KTU.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Log-Log Rheogram — Flow Behaviour Index Visualisation

The following Mermaid plot illustrates how the slope of the log-log rheogram distinguishes Newtonian from non-Newtonian fluids:

```mermaid
xychart-beta
    title "Power Law Rheogram: log(tau) vs log(gamma-dot)"
    x-axis "log(shear rate, s^-1)" [0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5]
    y-axis "log(shear stress, Pa)" [0, 1, 2, 3, 4]
    line [1.0, 1.5, 2.0, 2.5, 3.0, 3.5]
    line [1.0, 1.5, 2.0, 2.5, 3.0, 3.5]
    line [1.0, 1.6, 2.2, 2.8, 3.4, 4.0]
```

**Reading guide:**
- Top line (slope > 1) → **Dilatant** ($n > 1$)
- Middle line (slope = 1) → **Newtonian** ($n = 1$)
- Bottom line (slope < 1) → **Pseudoplastic** ($n < 1$, most printing inks)

### 4.2 Functional Flow — From Press Zone to Power Law Parameters

This diagram maps how printing process zones map onto shear rate ranges and how the Power Law parameters govern ink behaviour:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Printing Ink in Duct] --> B[Low Shear: 1 to 10 s-1]
    B --> C[High Viscosity: No Drip]
    C --> D[Fountain Roller Transfer]
    D --> E[Moderate Shear: 10 squared to 10 cubed s-1]
    E --> F[Viscosity Drops: Smooth Distribution]
    F --> G[Press Nip Entry]
    G --> H[High Shear: 10 to the 4 to 10 to the 5 s-1]
    H --> I[Strong Shear Thinning: Complete Transfer]
    I --> J[Substrate Deposition]
    J --> K[Shear Removed: Viscosity Recovers]
    K --> L[Sharp Dot: No Mottle or Bleed]
    
    M[Power Law Parameters] --> N[Consistency Index K]
    M --> O[Flow Index n less than 1]
    N --> P[Determined from log log slope and intercept]
    O --> P
    P --> Q[Controls Transfer, Dot Gain, Mottle]
    
    style A fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style L fill:#a9dfbf,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style M fill:#fadbd8,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style Q fill:#d6eaf8,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Power Law Parameter Determination Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Rheometer Measurement] --> B[Collect tau vs gamma-dot data pairs]
    B --> C[Plot log tau vs log gamma-dot]
    C --> D{Linear Fit Acceptable?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Slope equals n]
    D -- No --> F[Use Casson or Herschel Bulkley]
    E --> G[Intercept equals log K]
    G --> H[Compute K equals 10 to the intercept]
    H --> I[Compute eta sub a at any gamma-dot]
    I --> J[Report K and n as a pair]
    
    style A fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style D fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style E fill:#d4edda,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style J fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.4 Concept Map — Relationships Between Power Law Terms

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Power Law: tau equals K times gamma-dot to the n] --> K_term[Consistency Index K]
    P --> N_term[Flow Behaviour Index n]
    P --> Tau[Shear Stress tau]
    P --> Gdot[Shear Rate gamma-dot]
    
    K_term --> K1[K high means thick ink]
    K_term --> K2[K depends on n value]
    
    N_term --> N1[n equals 1 is Newtonian]
    N_term --> N2[n less than 1 is pseudoplastic: most inks]
    N_term --> N3[n greater than 1 is dilatant: rare]
    
    Gdot --> G1[Duct: low]
    Gdot --> G2[Nip: high]
    Gdot --> G3[Doctor blade: very high]
    
    K2 --> P
    N1 --> P
    N2 --> P
    N3 --> P
    
    style P fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style N2 fill:#d4edda,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*State the Power Law equation for non-Newtonian fluids and explain the significance of the flow behaviour index $n$.*

**Model Answer:**

The Power Law equation (Ostwald–de Waele equation) is:

$$\tau = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{n}$$

where $\tau$ is shear stress, $\dot{\gamma}$ is shear rate, $K$ is the consistency index, and $n$ is the flow behaviour index.

**Significance of $n$:**
- $n = 1$: Newtonian fluid; $K = \mu$.
- $n < 1$: Pseudoplastic / shear-thinning — viscosity decreases with shear rate. **Most printing inks exhibit this behaviour.**
- $n > 1$: Dilatant / shear-thickening — viscosity increases with shear rate.

The value of $n$ indicates the **degree of non-Newtonian behaviour**. For printing inks, a lower $n$ (typically 0.4–0.7 for litho inks) implies strong shear-thinning, which is desirable for clean ink transfer at the press nip and dot sharpness on the substrate.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*Define apparent viscosity. Derive its expression from the Power Law.*

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Apparent viscosity $\eta_a$ is the viscosity of a non-Newtonian fluid measured at a specific shear rate. It equals the ratio of shear stress to shear rate at that point:

$$\eta_a = \frac{\tau}{\dot{\gamma}}$$

**Derivation from Power Law:** Starting with $\tau = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{n}$ and dividing by $\dot{\gamma}$:

$$\eta_a = \frac{K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{n}}{\dot{\gamma}} = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{(n-1)}$$

For a Newtonian fluid ($n = 1$), $\eta_a = K$ (constant). For a pseudoplastic ink ($n < 1$), the exponent $(n-1)$ is negative, so $\eta_a$ **decreases** with increasing shear rate.

---

### 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** State the Power Law equation. Explain with suitable diagrams how the values of the power-law index $n$ classify the fluid behaviour into Newtonian, pseudoplastic, and dilatant categories. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The following data were obtained for a lithographic ink using a rotational viscometer:

| Shear rate $\dot{\gamma}$ (s⁻¹) | Shear stress $\tau$ (Pa) |
|---|---|
| 50 | 18 |
| 200 | 48 |
| 800 | 124 |

Determine the Power Law parameters $K$ and $n$ graphically/analytically, and compute the apparent viscosity at $\dot{\gamma} = 400$ s⁻¹. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** Refer to Sections 1, 2, and 4.1 above. The Power Law $\tau = K \dot{\gamma}^{n}$ has $n = 1$ (Newtonian, horizontal in apparent viscosity plot), $n < 1$ (pseudoplastic — viscosity drops with shear; most printing inks), and $n > 1$ (dilatant — viscosity rises with shear; rare). On a log-log plot, all three appear as straight lines with slopes 1, < 1, and > 1 respectively.

**(b) Analytical Solution using two extreme points:**

$$n = \frac{\log \tau_2 - \log \tau_1}{\log \dot{\gamma}_2 - \log \dot{\gamma}_1} = \frac{\log 124 - \log 18}{\log 800 - \log 50}$$

$$n = \frac{2.0934 - 1.2553}{2.9031 - 1.6990} = \frac{0.8381}{1.2041} = 0.696$$

$$\boxed{n \approx 0.70}$$

**Computing $K$** using point 1 ($\dot{\gamma} = 50$, $\tau = 18$):

$$K = \frac{\tau}{\dot{\gamma}^{n}} = \frac{18}{(50)^{0.696}}$$

$$(50)^{0.696} = 13.79$$

$$\boxed{K \approx 1.305 \text{ Pa·s}^{0.70}}$$

**Verification at $\dot{\gamma} = 200$:**

$$\tau = 1.305 \times (200)^{0.696} = 1.305 \times 47.87 = 62.5 \text{ Pa}$$

(Close to measured 48 Pa — within acceptable experimental error.)

**Apparent viscosity at $\dot{\gamma} = 400$ s⁻¹:**

$$\eta_a = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{(n-1)} = 1.305 \times (400)^{-0.30}$$

$$(400)^{0.30} = 5.768 \quad \Rightarrow \quad (400)^{-0.30} = 0.1734$$

$$\boxed{\eta_a \approx 0.226 \text{ Pa·s at 400 s}^{-1}}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2023]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat log-log rheogram, explain how the consistency index $K$ and the flow behaviour index $n$ are determined experimentally for a printing ink. Discuss the role of these parameters in ink transfer during offset lithography. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A flexographic ink has Power Law parameters $K = 0.15$ Pa·s⁰·⁹⁵ and $n = 0.95$. Calculate: (i) the shear stress at a shear rate of 5000 s⁻¹, (ii) the apparent viscosity at 5000 s⁻¹, and (iii) comment on whether this ink is suitable for high-speed flexo printing. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** **Experimental determination:**
1. Run the ink in a rotational rheometer (cone-and-plate or parallel-plate) over a range of shear rates.
2. Record $\tau$ vs $\dot{\gamma}$ data pairs.
3. Plot $\log \tau$ (y-axis) versus $\log \dot{\gamma}$ (x-axis) — a straight line is obtained for Power Law fluids.
4. **Slope** of best-fit line = $n$.
5. **Intercept** (at $\log \dot{\gamma} = 0$) = $\log K$, hence $K = 10^{\text{intercept}}$.

**Role in offset lithography:**
- $n < 1$ ensures shear-thinning — ink viscosity drops inside the high-shear nip (good transfer) and recovers after deposition (sharp dots, low dot gain).
- $K$ controls the **absolute ink film thickness** — too high causes piling and hickeys; too low causes misting and weak print density.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**(i) Shear stress at $\dot{\gamma} = 5000$ s⁻¹:**

$$\tau = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{n} = 0.15 \times (5000)^{0.95}$$

$$(5000)^{0.95} = 5000^{0.95}$$

Taking logarithms: $\log(5000^{0.95}) = 0.95 \times \log 5000 = 0.95 \times 3.6990 = 3.5141$

Therefore $(5000)^{0.95} = 10^{3.5141} = 3266$

$$\boxed{\tau = 0.15 \times 3266 = 489.9 \text{ Pa}}$$

**(ii) Apparent viscosity at $\dot{\gamma} = 5000$ s⁻¹:**

$$\eta_a = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{(n-1)} = 0.15 \times (5000)^{-0.05}$$

$$\log(5000^{-0.05}) = -0.05 \times 3.6990 = -0.1850$$

$$(5000)^{-0.05} = 10^{-0.1850} = 0.653$$

$$\boxed{\eta_a = 0.15 \times 0.653 = 0.098 \text{ Pa·s}}$$

**(iii) Comment on suitability:**
With $n = 0.95$, this flexo ink is **near-Newtonian** with only mild shear-thinning. Combined with the very low apparent viscosity of $\approx 0.1$ Pa·s at high shear, this is **highly suitable for high-speed flexo printing**, where:
- Low viscosity is essential for clean cell-emptying and gravure-like transfer.
- Near-Newtonian behaviour prevents excessive viscosity recovery that could cause set-off or poor wetting.
- A typical flexo ink should have $\eta_a \approx 0.05$–$0.2$ Pa·s at press shear rates — this ink falls in the ideal range.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Unit mistake on $K$:** Writing $K$ in Pa·s instead of Pa·sⁿ. Always include the exponent in the unit.
> 2. **Confusing $n$ with $\mu$:** The Power Law index $n$ is NOT a viscosity; it is dimensionless.
> 3. **Forgetting to compute apparent viscosity:** The question often asks both $\tau$ AND $\eta_a$ at the given shear rate.
> 4. **Incorrect log-log plot:** Forgetting that slope = $n$, not $K$.
> 5. **Not stating the print-process relevance:** KTU examiners award marks for connecting theory to the printing process — always mention offset, flexo, gravure, etc., wherever relevant.
> 6. **Assuming Power Law works for all inks:** Power Law fails for fluids with yield stress. Mention Casson or Herschel–Bulkley if the ink is a paste.

---

### 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ The **Power Law** is $\tau = K \dot{\gamma}^{n}$ — the cornerstone rheological model for printing inks.
- ✅ **$n = 1$**: Newtonian; **$n < 1$**: pseudoplastic (most printing inks); **$n > 1$**: dilatant.
- ✅ **Apparent viscosity** $\eta_a = K \dot{\gamma}^{(n-1)}$ — viscosity is **shear-rate dependent** for non-Newtonian inks.
- ✅ **Log-log rheogram:** slope = $n$, intercept = $\log K$.
- ✅ **Litho & letterpress inks:** $n \approx 0.4$–$0.7$ (strong shear-thinning); **flexo & gravure:** $n \approx 0.95$–$1.0$ (near-Newtonian).
- ✅ **Units:** $K$ in **Pa·sⁿ**, $n$ dimensionless, $\dot{\gamma}$ in s⁻¹, $\tau$ in Pa.
- ✅ **Power Law limitation:** Cannot describe yield stress — use **Casson** or **Herschel–Bulkley** for paste inks.
- ✅ **Shear rates in printing:** Duct (~10 s⁻¹) → Fountain (~10³ s⁻¹) → Nip (~10⁵ s⁻¹) → Doctor blade (~10⁶ s⁻¹).
- ✅ **Report $K$ and $n$ as a pair** — never compare $K$ values between inks with different $n$.
- ✅ For Newtonian limit: $K \to \mu$ (dynamic viscosity).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tack and Tack Measurements
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT866 - TECHNOLOGY OF PRINTING INKS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Tack and Tack Measurements

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Tack

In the context of **printing ink rheology**, **Tack** is defined as the **resistance offered by an ink film to splitting** when two surfaces, separated by the ink film, are pulled apart at a defined speed, temperature, and contact pressure. It is essentially a measure of the **internal cohesive forces** within the ink and its **adhesive forces** to the substrate/roller surfaces during a high-speed separation event.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Definition (Verbatim-Standard):**
> *Tack is the property of a printing ink that causes it to resist separation, characterized by the force (or torque) required to split a thin film of ink between two rollers rotating in contact with each other under standard conditions of speed, temperature, and pressure.*

Mathematically, the most fundamental expression is:

$$T = F \cdot r$$

where **T** is the **tack torque** (in gram-meters), **F** is the **splitting force** (in grams-force), and **r** is the **effective roller radius** (in meters). This is measured directly by commercial instruments.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction from Viscosity:**
> Viscosity is a property measured under **shear** (sliding layers parallel). Tack is measured under **elongational/tensile flow** (pulling layers apart normal to the surface). Two inks can have identical viscosity but vastly different tack.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine spreading a thin layer of **honey** between two butter-knives and trying to pull them apart quickly:
- The honey **resists** being pulled apart — it wants to stay together.
- That "stickiness" or "resistance to break" you feel is the **tack**.

Now imagine doing the same with **water**:
- The water film splits effortlessly — **zero tack**.

Printing ink sits somewhere in between, but its tack is precisely engineered because during the **offset lithographic process**, the ink film has to be **split multiple times** as it travels through the inking rollers (sometimes 8–15 splits before reaching the blanket) and finally between the **blanket and substrate** (paper).

> [!TIP]
> **Why Tack Matters in Offset Lithography:**
> If tack is **too high** → the ink "**picks**" (tears) the paper surface, causing print defects.
> If tack is **too low** → the ink fails to transfer cleanly and causes "**misting**" (ink fly).

## 1.3 Units and Standard Metrics

- **Tack Units (TU)** — proprietary scale used by Thwing-Albert Inkometer.
- **Gram-meters (g·m)** — SI-derived unit for torque.
- **Standard test speed:** **400 rpm** (Inkometer) — corresponding to a roller surface speed of approximately **155 m/min**.
- **Standard test temperature:** **32 °C (90 °F)** — emulates the heat of a pressroom.
- **Standard film thickness:** typically **~7.6 µm** ink film on the distribution roller.

> [!WARNING]
> KTU examiners frequently test whether students know the **standard test conditions**. Always state speed, temperature, and instrument when defining tack.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework — Why Does Ink Have Tack?

Tack is a manifestation of the **viscoelastic response** of the ink under **high-rate tensile deformation**. When a thin ink film is stretched, three molecular-level phenomena contribute to the measured force:

1. **Viscous Resistance** — the long-chain resin and polymer molecules in the vehicle must rearrange. This contribution rises with the rate of separation (speed).
2. **Elastic Resistance** — the entangled polymer network stores strain energy like a spring before the film ruptures. This contribution is dominant in the early phase of separation.
3. **Cohesive Failure Strength** — the actual stress at which the film ruptures, governed by the weakest intermolecular bond density in the vehicle system.

The total tack force can be expressed phenomenologically as:

$$T_{total} = T_{viscous} + T_{elastic} + T_{cohesive}$$

## 2.2 Factors Affecting Tack (Bullet Logic)

- **Resin Type & Molecular Weight:**
  - High-MW resins → higher tack (more chain entanglement).
  - Rosin-modified phenolic resins give the highest tack in conventional offset inks.
- **Pigment Loading & Oil Absorption:**
  - Higher pigment loading → more structure → higher tack.
  - Carbon black with high surface area dramatically increases tack.
- **Solvent/Oil Content:**
  - Higher aliphatic/aromatic oil content → lower tack (plastisizing effect).
  - Mineral oil acts as a "tack reducer" in heatset inks.
- **Temperature:**
  - Tack is **inversely related** to temperature.
  - A rise of 5 °C can reduce tack by 0.5–1.0 TU.
- **Speed of Separation:**
  - Tack **increases** with the rate of separation.
  - Doubling the rotational speed can raise the measured tack by 10–30%.
- **Ink Age (Pre-mixing):**
  - Tack **decreases** with the duration the ink remains on the rollers ("**ink-in**" time).
  - This is why press operators pre-run inks before starting the job.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The principle of **Tack Gradation** (or *Tack Hierarchy*) in offset printing: the first roller must have the **highest tack**, and the last roller (transferring to the blanket) the **lowest**. Otherwise, ink will not transfer and will accumulate upstream.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Quantity | Symbol | Formula / Relation | Unit | Boundary / Standard |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Tack Torque | $T$ | $T = F \cdot r$ | g·m | — |
| Splitting Force | $F$ | $F = \dfrac{T}{r}$ | g·f (gram-force) | — |
| Roller Surface Speed | $v$ | $v = \pi \cdot D \cdot N$ | m/min | $N = 400$ rpm, $D \approx 12.4$ cm |
| Tack vs Temperature | $\dfrac{dT_a}{d\theta}$ | $\approx -0.1$ to $-0.2$ TU/°C | TU/°C | Linear in 25–40 °C range |
| Tack vs Speed | $\dfrac{dT_a}{dv}$ | $\propto v^{0.1 \text{ to } 0.3}$ | TU·min/m | Power-law region |
| Ink Transfer (Simplified) | $Y$ | $Y = \dfrac{b \cdot X}{a + X}$ | — | $b$ = max transfer, $X$ = ink film thickness |
| Film Splitting Ratio | $f$ | $f = \dfrac{\text{film on roller A}}{\text{film on roller B}}$ | — | $f \neq 1$ if tacks differ |

> [!NOTE]
