> The **Walker-Fetsko equation** for ink transfer (used in advanced problems) is: $Y = b(1 - e^{-kX})$. The simplified form is the **Wolkoff equation** $Y = bX/(a + X)$. Note that the variables in these equations have specific meanings; consult your module notes for the exact forms required by KTU 2024.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility

- **Press Optimization:** Adjusting tack values to match press speed, substrate absorbency, and number of roller nips.
- **Formulation:** Balancing tack against gloss, rub resistance, and setting time.
- **Quality Control:** Specifying ink tack for incoming goods inspection (typically 8–14 TU for sheetfed offset, 4–8 TU for heatset web).
- **Troubleshooting:** Diagnosing picking, misting, piling, and set-off issues on the press.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Roller Surface Speed from RPM

The standard Inkometer has a **distribution roller** of diameter **D** rotating at **N** rpm. The linear surface speed $v$ is the circumference traced per minute.

Starting from the geometric definition of circumference:

$$C = \pi \cdot D$$

For rotation at N revolutions per minute, the linear distance traveled by a point on the surface per minute is:

$$v = N \cdot C = N \cdot \pi \cdot D$$

Substituting the standard Inkometer roller diameter $D = 0.124$ m and standard test speed $N = 400$ rpm:

$$v = 400 \cdot \pi \cdot 0.124 \;\; \text{m/min}$$

$$v = 400 \cdot 0.389557 \;\; \text{m/min}$$

$$v \approx 155.82 \;\; \text{m/min}$$

This confirms the canonical KTU/industry value of **≈ 155 m/min** for the standard Inkometer test.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Relationship Between Tack and Temperature (Linear Approximation)

Experimental data over the operating window (25–40 °C) shows tack varies approximately linearly with temperature. Define:

- $T_{a,1}$ = tack measured at temperature $\theta_1$
- $T_{a,2}$ = tack measured at temperature $\theta_2$
- $\alpha$ = temperature coefficient of tack (TU/°C, negative)

The linear relationship is:

$$T_{a,2} = T_{a,1} + \alpha(\theta_2 - \theta_1)$$

Solving for the temperature coefficient (an important KTU 2024 calculation):

$$\alpha = \dfrac{T_{a,2} - T_{a,1}}{\theta_2 - \theta_1}$$

**Numerical Example (typical exam data):**
An ink reads 12.0 TU at 30 °C and 10.4 TU at 36 °C. Calculate $\alpha$.

Step 1 — Write the difference form:

$$\Delta T_a = T_{a,2} - T_{a,1} = 10.4 - 12.0 = -1.6 \;\; \text{TU}$$

Step 2 — Write the temperature difference:

$$\Delta \theta = \theta_2 - \theta_1 = 36 - 30 = 6 \;\; °C$$

Step 3 — Compute the coefficient:

$$\alpha = \dfrac{-1.6 \;\; \text{TU}}{6 \;\; °C}$$

$$\alpha \approx -0.267 \;\; \text{TU/°C}$$

Step 4 — Physical interpretation: for every 1 °C rise in roller temperature, tack drops by approximately **0.27 TU**. This negative coefficient is a key KTU fact: **printing inks have negative temperature coefficient of tack**.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Two-Roller Film Splitting — The "Soft-Packing" Equation

When two rollers rotating in the same direction contact with an ink film between them, the film splits into two parts. If the rollers have equal surface speed and contact geometry, but **different effective tack** (e.g., ink is being transferred from a "donor" to a "receiver" roller), the film splits unevenly.

Let:
- $h_1$ = film thickness on roller 1 (donor, before split)
- $h_2$ = film thickness on roller 2 (donor, before split)
- $h_1'$ = film on roller 1 after split
- $h_2'$ = film on roller 2 after split
- $T_1, T_2$ = tack of the two inks (or effective tack at the nip)

**Mass conservation (Newton's split rule):**

$$h_1 + h_2 = h_1' + h_2'$$

**Force balance at the nip** (the splitting force equals the tack of the weaker film at that instant):

$$\dfrac{h_1'}{h_2'} = \dfrac{T_2}{T_1}$$

**Solving these two equations for $h_1'$ and $h_2'$:**

$$h_1' = \dfrac{T_2 \cdot (h_1 + h_2)}{T_1 + T_2}$$

$$h_2' = \dfrac{T_1 \cdot (h_1 + h_2)}{T_1 + T_2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** If $T_1 \gg T_2$, then $h_1' \to 0$ and $h_2' \to h_1 + h_2$. This means **all the ink transfers to the lower-tack roller** — the principle behind proper **tack gradation** in the inking system.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Inkometer vs Tackoscope vs Other Instruments

| Feature | Thwing-Albert Inkometer | Electronic Tackoscope | TMI Inkometer | ROTOTHINNER |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Principle | Mechanical torque | Electronic transducer | Mechanical torque | Rotational drag |
| Readout | Pointer dial (TU) | Digital display | Digital (TU) | Viscosity-tack combo |
| Speed Range | Fixed 400 rpm | Variable 100–1200 rpm | Variable | Variable |
| Temperature Control | Water bath (32 °C std) | Peltier/electronic | Water bath | Heated block |
| Industry Acceptance | ★★★★★ (Gold Standard) | ★★★★ (Modern labs) | ★★★★ | ★★★ (Research) |
| Cost | Low | High | Medium | Medium |
| KTU 2024 Relevance | Highest | High | Medium | Low |

> [!TIP]
> For KTU 2024 theory questions, default to the **Thwing-Albert Inkometer** unless otherwise specified. It is the canonical instrument for offset ink tack testing.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — The Inkometer Operating Principle

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **functional flow** of a Thwing-Albert Inkometer from sample loading to digital readout. The instrument cannot be physically drawn in Mermaid, so the architecture flow is shown instead.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Step A1: Ink Sample Loading] --> A2[Step A2: Distribution Roller]
    A2 --> A3[Step A3: Transfer Roller at 400 rpm]
    A3 --> A4[Step A4: Heated Water Bath 32 C]
    A4 --> A5[Step A5: Ink Film Splits at Nip]
    A5 --> A6[Step A6: Reaction Torque on Driving Roller]
    A6 --> A7[Step A7: Mechanical Lever Arm Amplification]
    A7 --> A8[Step A8: Dial Indicator Reads in TU]
    A8 --> A9[Step A9: Operator Records Tack Value]
```

**Caption:** Functional flow of a single tack measurement cycle on a Thwing-Albert Inkometer.

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Tack in the Offset Inking System (Tack Gradation)

The schematic below illustrates the **tack gradation principle** across an offset press inking train. The ink flows from the duct (highest tack) to the blanket (lowest tack).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B1[Step B1: Duct Roller - Tack 14 TU] --> B2[Step B2: Distributor Roller - Tack 12 TU]
    B2 --> B3[Step B3: Vibrator Roller - Tack 10.5 TU]
    B3 --> B4[Step B4: Form Roller - Tack 9.5 TU]
    B4 --> B5[Step B5: Plate Roller - Tack 9 TU]
    B5 --> B6[Step B6: Blanket Roller - Tack 8.5 TU]
    B6 --> B7[Step B7: Substrate Paper - Final Transfer]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Each step represents a **film-splitting event**. The decreasing tack values (14 → 8.5 TU) ensure that **ink flows downstream** rather than accumulating. If you reverse the gradient, the ink will refuse to transfer and "**pile**" at the first roller with lower tack.

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Variables Affecting Tack (Causal Map)

This diagram maps the four primary input variables and their causal effect on the measured tack.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    C1[Input Variable 1: Temperature] --> D1[Direct Effect on Tack]
    C2[Input Variable 2: Speed of Separation] --> D1
    C3[Input Variable 3: Resin MW and Type] --> D1
    C4[Input Variable 4: Pigment Loading] --> D1
    C5[Input Variable 5: Solvent Content] --> D1
    D1 --> E1[Output Measured Tack in TU]
    E1 --> F1[Step F1: Compare to Specification 8 to 14 TU]
    F1 --> G1[Step G2: Accept or Reject Batch]
```

**Caption:** Causal map of inputs to measured tack and the resulting QC decision.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define tack of a printing ink. How is it different from viscosity?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1.5 marks):** Tack is the resistance of a thin ink film to splitting when subjected to a tensile force, measured as the torque required to separate the ink film between two rollers at a defined speed, temperature, and pressure. It is expressed in tack units (TU) or gram-meters.
- **Difference from viscosity (1.5 marks):** Viscosity is the **internal friction** of a fluid under **shear** (parallel sliding layers), measured in Poise or Pa·s. Tack is the **cohesion/adhesion strength** of a thin film under **tensile deformation** (perpendicular separation), measured in TU. A high-viscosity ink may have low tack (e.g., heavy paste) and vice versa.

### Question A2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Mention any three factors that affect the tack of an offset printing ink.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark each):**
1. **Resin type and molecular weight** — higher-MW resins produce higher tack.
2. **Temperature** — tack decreases as temperature rises (negative coefficient, approx. -0.1 to -0.2 TU/°C).
3. **Speed of separation** — tack increases with roller speed (power-law dependence).
   *(Equally valid: pigment loading, solvent/oil content, ink age, contact pressure.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - **Common Error 1:** Confusing tack with viscosity. Always state both **the mode of deformation** (tensile vs shear) and **the units** (TU vs Poise) when distinguishing.
> - **Common Error 2:** Forgetting to mention **standard test conditions** (32 °C, 400 rpm, ~7.6 µm film) when describing the Inkometer. Examiners deduct 1 mark for this.
> - **Common Error 3:** Writing "tack measures stickiness" — this is a colloquial description. Use the formal phrase: **"resistance of the ink film to splitting under tensile deformation."**
> - **Common Error 4:** In derivations, omitting units in the final answer. Always carry and state units.
> - **Common Error 5:** In tack gradation questions, not stating the **direction of flow** (high → low) and the **consequence of inversion** (piling/misting).

### Question B-A. `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Describe the **construction and working of the Thwing-Albert Inkometer** with a neat functional diagram. State the standard test conditions.

**(b) [7 marks]** Two inks are tested on an Inkometer. Ink X reads **13.0 TU at 28 °C** and **11.2 TU at 34 °C**. Calculate the **temperature coefficient of tack** and predict the tack of the same ink at **40 °C**.

**Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks:**

**Construction (3.5 marks):**
- A **distribution roller** (driven) and a **transfer roller** rotating in contact.
- A **water-jacketed bath** circulates temperature-controlled water to maintain the rollers at the set test temperature.
- A **speed control motor** drives the rollers, typically at **400 rpm** (≈ 155 m/min linear surface speed).
- A **bale (lever arm)** rests on the transfer roller and is deflected by the reaction torque.
- A **dial indicator / pointer** on the bale arm reads the tack in **Tack Units (TU)**.

**Working (2.5 marks):**
1. Ink is distributed evenly on the distribution roller using a pipette.
2. The motor is started; the transfer roller rotates at 400 rpm.
3. The ink film splits at the nip between the two rollers, generating a **reaction torque** on the transfer roller.
4. This torque deflects the bale, which is amplified by a lever mechanism and read on the dial.
5. The reading is the **tack of the ink** in TU.

**Standard Test Conditions (1 mark):**
- Roller speed: **400 rpm**
- Temperature: **32 °C (90 °F)**
- Film thickness: ~**7.6 µm**
- Inking time before reading: **1 minute**

**Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks:**

Given:
- $(T_a)_1 = 13.0$ TU at $\theta_1 = 28$ °C
- $(T_a)_2 = 11.2$ TU at $\theta_2 = 34$ °C
- Find: $\alpha$ and $(T_a)_3$ at $\theta_3 = 40$ °C

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Apply the linear temperature equation:

$$\alpha = \dfrac{(T_a)_2 - (T_a)_1}{\theta_2 - \theta_1}$$

$$\alpha = \dfrac{11.2 - 13.0}{34 - 28} = \dfrac{-1.8 \;\; \text{TU}}{6 \;\; °C}$$

$$\alpha = -0.30 \;\; \text{TU/°C}$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** State physical meaning — for every 1 °C rise, tack drops by 0.30 TU.

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Extrapolate to 40 °C using:

$$(T_a)_3 = (T_a)_1 + \alpha(\theta_3 - \theta_1)$$

$$(T_a)_3 = 13.0 + (-0.30)(40 - 28)$$

$$(T_a)_3 = 13.0 + (-0.30)(12)$$

$$(T_a)_3 = 13.0 - 3.6 = 9.4 \;\; \text{TU}$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Comment on the result.
- Predicted tack at 40 °C = **9.4 TU**.
- This is in the typical operating range for **heatset web offset** (4–8 TU for 4-color heatset; 9–11 TU for sheetfed). The ink would be marginally within the **sheetfed acceptable range** at 40 °C — useful QC insight.

---

### Question B-B. `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 — Alternative Choice]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the **principle of tack gradation** in an offset press. Why is it necessary, and what happens if the gradient is reversed?

**(b) [7 marks]** An ink has a tack of **10.5 TU** on a roller of effective radius **4.5 cm**. Calculate the **splitting force** in the ink film at the nip. If the press speed is increased such that the roller surface speed rises from 100 m/min to 200 m/min, by what factor does the viscous component of tack typically scale (use the power-law exponent $n = 0.2$)?

**Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks:**

**Principle (3 marks):**
- Tack gradation is the systematic **stepwise decrease in ink tack** as the ink passes from the duct roller through the inking train to the substrate.
- Typical gradient: **Duct (14 TU) → Distributor (12 TU) → Form (10 TU) → Plate (9 TU) → Blanket (8 TU)**.

**Why it is necessary (2 marks):**
- The ink film must **split at each nip** to transfer from one roller to the next.
- For a film to split, the **incoming ink must have higher tack** than the receiving surface film; otherwise, it will not transfer and will **accumulate** upstream.

**What happens if reversed (2 marks):**
- Ink will refuse to transfer from upstream roller to downstream roller.
- Results in **piling** (ink accumulation on form rollers), **misting** (ink fly), and **hickeys** on the print.
- Print density becomes uneven; first-down colors appear too light, last-down too heavy.

**Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Convert radius to meters (0.5 marks):**

$$r = 4.5 \;\; \text{cm} = 0.045 \;\; \text{m}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the torque-force relation (2.5 marks):**

$$T = F \cdot r \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; F = \dfrac{T}{r}$$

$$F = \dfrac{10.5 \;\; \text{g·m}}{0.045 \;\; \text{m}}$$

$$F \approx 233.33 \;\; \text{g·f}$$

**Step 3 — Note unit conversion to SI (1 mark):**

$$F \approx 233.33 \times 9.81 \times 10^{-3} \;\; \text{N} \approx 2.29 \;\; \text{N}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the power-law speed dependence (2 marks):**

$$T_a \propto v^n \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \dfrac{(T_a)_2}{(T_a)_1} = \left(\dfrac{v_2}{v_1}\right)^n$$

$$\text{Scaling factor} = \left(\dfrac{200}{100}\right)^{0.2} = 2^{0.2}$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate (1 mark):**

$$2^{0.2} = e^{0.2 \ln 2} = e^{0.2 \times 0.6931} = e^{0.1386} \approx 1.149$$

- The viscous tack component scales by a factor of **≈ 1.15** (a ~15% increase).
- **Interpretation:** Doubling the press speed only raises the tack by ~15% — printing ink is mildly shear-thinning/speed-sensitive, which is why **heatset web presses** (running at 600+ m/min) still need **cooled rollers** to control tack.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — "Tack and Tack Measurements"**

- ✅ **Definition:** Tack = resistance of an ink film to **tensile splitting** between two rollers. Not the same as viscosity.
- ✅ **Standard Instrument:** **Thwing-Albert Inkometer** at **400 rpm** and **32 °C (90 °F)**. Reading in **Tack Units (TU)**.
- ✅ **Tack vs Viscosity:** Tack → **tensile flow** (TU). Viscosity → **shear flow** (Poise / Pa·s).
- ✅ **Temperature Coefficient:** Negative, typically **-0.1 to -0.3 TU/°C**. Use the linear equation $T_{a,2} = T_{a,1} + \alpha(\theta_2 - \theta_1)$.
- ✅ **Speed Dependence:** Power-law, $T_a \propto v^n$ with $n \approx 0.1$–$0.3$.
- ✅ **Tack Gradation:** Must be **monotonically decreasing** from duct → substrate. Reversal causes **piling, misting, and uneven print density**.
- ✅ **Factors:** Resin MW ↑, Pigment loading ↑, Oil/solvent ↓, Temperature ↓, Speed ↑ → all raise tack.
- ✅ **Film Splitting Rule:** $h_1' / h_2' = T_2 / T_1$ — the film splits inversely to the tack of each side.
- ✅ **Numerical Defaults:** Sheetfed offset ink tack = **8–14 TU**; Heatset web = **4–8 TU**.
- ✅ **Key Equation to Memorize:** $T = F \cdot r$ and the two-roller split: $h_1' = T_2(h_1+h_2)/(T_1+T_2)$.
- ✅ **Press-Side Watch-out:** Pre-mix the ink for at least **1 minute** on the Inkometer before reading, because tack drifts downward with "ink-in" time.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dry Paint Performance Tests
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT866 - TECHNOLOGY OF PRINTING INKS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Dry Paint Performance Tests

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Dry Paint Performance Tests** are a comprehensive suite of standardized, reproducible laboratory procedures used to evaluate the **physical, mechanical, optical, and chemical properties** of a *dried/cured* printing ink film (or paint film) deposited on a substrate. The tests measure the film's ability to withstand the mechanical, chemical, and environmental stresses encountered during *post-printing handling, conversion, transport, and end-use*.

In KTU 2024 scheme terminology, these tests fall under the umbrella of **Quality Control (QC) and Quality Assurance (QA)** of finished dried ink/paint films, governed primarily by standards issued by **ASTM (American Society for Testing and Materials)**, **ISO (International Organization for Standardization)**, and **TAPPI (Technical Association of the Pulp and Paper Industry)**.

The key property classes evaluated are:
1. **Mechanical Integrity:** Adhesion, hardness, abrasion, flexibility, impact.
2. **Optical Performance:** Gloss, color, hiding power, DOI (Distinctness of Image).
3. **Chemical Resistance:** Solvent rub, alkali, acid, water, grease.
4. **Environmental Durability:** Heat, light, weathering, blocking.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Wall-Paint Chip" Analogy** 🧱
>
> Imagine you have just painted your house wall. The *wet* paint is easy to judge (color, viscosity), but the *dried* film must survive real life: kids scraping bicycles, monsoon rain, sunlight, scrubbing with detergent, accidental fingernail scratches, the doorframe bumping it, and a stack of books leaning against it. **Dry paint performance tests are exactly the controlled laboratory simulations of these real-life insults.** Each test is a "torture chamber" for the dried film, telling the formulator *how tough* their product is before it ships to the customer.

A more technical analogy: think of the dried ink film as a **polymer chain network stretched over a substrate**. The "performance tests" simply measure *which forces break that network* — be it shear (scratch), peel (adhesion), compression (hardness), or chemical attack (solvent rub).

## 1.3 Core Standard Metrics

- **Adhesion rating scale:** **ASTM 0B – 5B** (5B = best, 0B = worst).
- **Pencil hardness range:** **6B (softest) – 6H (hardest)**, with HB in the middle.
- **Gloss measurement angle:** **60° (universal)**, supplemented by 20° (high gloss) and 85° (matte).
- **Standard taber abrasion wheels:** **CS-10, CS-17, H-22** (with calibrated load **500 g / 1000 g**).
- **Cross-hatch spacing:** **1 mm, 2 mm, 3 mm** (depending on substrate & film thickness).
- **Mandrel diameters:** **¼″, ⅜″, ½″, ¾″, 1″** (set of 6 to 12 in standard kits).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 4 of *Technology of Printing Inks* (PEPOT866) explicitly lists "Dry Paint Performance Tests" as a high-weightage topic. Students are expected to know the *test name, principle, procedure, evaluation, and industrial relevance* for at least 6–8 major tests.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Dry Paint Performance Tests

The tests are broadly classified into **four functional families** based on the *type of stress* applied:

| Family | Type of Stress | Representative Tests |
|---|---|---|
| **A. Mechanical / Tribological** | Shear, scratch, abrasion, bending | Taber Abrasion, Pencil Hardness, Mandrel Bend, Impact |
| **B. Adhesive / Cohesive** | Pull, peel, shear at interface | Cross-Cut Tape Test, Pull-Off Adhesion, Scrape Adhesion |
| **C. Optical / Aesthetic** | Reflection, color, opacity | Glossmeter (60°), Hiding Power, Spectrophotometric Color |
| **D. Chemical / Environmental** | Solvent, pH, UV, heat | Solvent Rub, MEK Double-Rub, Light Fastness, Blocking |

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Test Family

### 2.2.1 Mechanical Tests
The dried ink film is a **viscoelastic polymer matrix** loaded with pigments and additives. When external shear force is applied (e.g., a fingernail or coin rubbing a printed carton), the network can fail in three ways:
- **Cohesive failure:** the film itself splits (the substrate stays clean).
- **Adhesive failure:** the film detaches from the substrate.
- **Mixed-mode failure:** partial of both.

> [!TIP]
> The **Taber Abraser** simulates *rolling friction* (e.g., a book sliding on a printed cover). The **Pencil Hardness** simulates *point shear* (e.g., a pencil tip scraping). The **Mandrel** simulates *tensile flexing* (e.g., a printed metal sheet being bent into a can).

### 2.2.2 Adhesion Tests
Adhesion is governed by three mechanisms:
1. **Mechanical interlocking** (substrate roughness anchors the film).
2. **Chemical bonding** (polar groups react with substrate).
3. **Wetting & adsorption** (van der Waals, hydrogen bonds).

> Cross-cut tests quantify the *interfacial bond strength* by attempting to lift the film with adhesive tape at a controlled angle (typically 60°) and time (60–90 s).

### 2.2.3 Optical Tests
- **Gloss (G)** is the *specular reflectance* measured at a defined angle. A perfectly mirror-like surface = 100 GU (Gloss Units); a perfectly matte surface = 0 GU.
- **DOI (Distinctness of Image)** measures how clearly a reflected image is reproduced — it correlates with surface *micro-roughness*.
- **Hiding power** = the ability to mask the substrate color, expressed in **m²/L** (spreading rate).

### 2.2.4 Chemical / Environmental Tests
- **MEK (Methyl Ethyl Ketone) Double-Rub:** the most common chemical-resistance test. A cotton pad soaked in MEK is rubbed back-and-forth on the film; the number of rubs until the film is *eroded to substrate* is recorded. **>100 rubs = excellent** for most packaging inks.
- **Blocking resistance:** two printed surfaces are placed face-to-face under a defined load (e.g., 1 psi) and temperature (e.g., 50 °C) for 24 h. Pass = no sticking.
- **Light fastness:** exposure to a controlled xenon-arc or UV source for a set number of hours; ΔE (color shift) measured.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **high-yield formula sheet** for KTU 2024 ESE numerical/analytical questions on this topic. All equations use `\vert` or `\mid` for absolute value to keep the markdown table structure valid.

| # | Test / Property | Formula | Variables & Units | Notes / Boundary Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Taber Abrasion Weight Loss | $\Delta W = W_i - W_f$ | $W_i$ = initial mass (g), $W_f$ = final mass (g) | Run for N = 100 to 1000 cycles; report $\Delta W$ per 1000 cycles. |
| 2 | Abrasion Rate (mass loss per cycle) | $R = \dfrac{\Delta W}{N \cdot t_f}$ | $R$ in g/cycle, $t_f$ = film thickness (mm) | Useful for comparing formulations. |
| 3 | Mandrel Bending Strain (outer fibre) | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{t_f}{2r} \times 100\,\%$ | $t_f$ = film thickness, $r$ = mandrel radius | Pass if $\varepsilon \le \varepsilon_{\text{critical}}$ (no cracking). |
| 4 | Pencil Hardness Conversion (Wolff formula) | $H = \dfrac{F}{\pi \cdot d^2}$ | $F$ = applied force (N), $d$ = pencil tip diameter (mm) | Empirical mapping to 6B–6H scale. |
| 5 | Gloss (Gloss Units) | $G_\theta = \dfrac{I_{s,\theta}}{I_{s,\theta,\text{ref}}} \times 100$ | $I_s$ = specular intensity; $\theta$ = 20°, 60°, 85° | Reference = polished black glass ($n = 1.567$). |
| 6 | Reflectance for Hiding Power (Kubelka–Munk) | $\dfrac{K}{S} = \dfrac{(1-R_\infty)^2}{2 R_\infty}$ | $K$ = absorption, $S$ = scattering coeff.; $R_\infty$ = reflectance over thick film | Used to compute contrast ratio. |
| 7 | Contrast Ratio (Hiding) | $C.R. = \dfrac{R_B}{R_W}$ | $R_B$ = reflectance over black, $R_W$ = over white | Pass if $C.R. \ge 0.98$. |
| 8 | Cross-Cut Adhesion Classification | 5B (0 % removed) → 0B (>65 % removed) | Area % removed by tape | ASTM D3359. |
| 9 | Blocking Load Equivalent Stress | $\sigma_b = \dfrac{F_{\text{load}}}{A_{\text{contact}}}$ | $\sigma_b$ in Pa; $F$ in N; $A$ in m² | Standard test: 1 psi ≈ 6.895 kPa. |
| 10 | Light Fastness ΔE (CIELab) | $\Delta E = \sqrt{(\Delta L^*)^2 + (\Delta a^*)^2 + (\Delta b^*)^2}$ | $L^*, a^*, b^*$ = CIELab coordinates | $\Delta E \le 1$ = not perceptible; $\ge 3$ = clearly visible. |
| 11 | MEK Double-Rub Resistance | $N_{\text{MEK}} = $ count to substrate exposure | Dimensionless integer | Typical: $N_{\text{MEK}} > 100$ for cured films. |
| 12 | Film Coverage (Spreading Rate) | $S.R. = \dfrac{V}{A} = \dfrac{m}{\rho \cdot t_f}$ | $S.R.$ in m²/L, $V$ = volume, $A$ = area, $\rho$ = density | Commercial inks: 8–15 m²/L. |
| 13 | Pull-Off Adhesion Strength | $\sigma_a = \dfrac{F_{\text{pull}}}{A_{\text{dolly}}}$ | $\sigma_a$ in MPa (or psi) | ASTM D4541. |
| 14 | Impact Resistance | $I = m \cdot g \cdot h$ | $m$ = striker mass, $h$ = drop height, $g = 9.81$ m/s² | Pass = no cracking at $I_{\text{design}}$. |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In the printing ink industry, dry paint performance tests are the **gatekeeper** between R&D and commercialization. They serve three critical functions:

1. **Customer-Spec Compliance:** Major buyers (e.g., Tetra Pak, Hindustan Unilever, Nestlé) mandate specific performance in their artwork specifications — *e.g., a milk carton must withstand 100 MEK rubs and 3B adhesion on LDPE-coated board.*
2. **Failure Diagnosis:** If a printed batch fails in the field (e.g., cartons scuff during transit), QC retraces the *exact test* that should have caught it, enabling root-cause analysis.
3. **Formulation Optimization:** Each test guides a specific formulation lever:
   - Low adhesion → add adhesion promoter (e.g., chlorinated polyolefin for film substrates).
   - Low gloss → reduce binder or add matting agent (e.g., fumed silica).
   - Low MEK rub → increase crosslink density (e.g., higher functionality resin, more isocyanate in 2K systems).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Mandrel Bending Strain on a Coated Substrate

> This is the **most exam-relevant derivation** for Module 4 of PEPOT866.

### Problem Setup
A dried ink film of thickness $t_f$ is coated on a metal substrate of thickness $t_s$. The composite is bent around a cylindrical mandrel of radius $r$. The neutral axis of bending lies within the substrate (since the film is thin and mechanically decoupled for small $t_f/t_s$ ratios). We want the **maximum tensile strain experienced by the outer fibre of the dried film**.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Geometry of bending.**
When a beam of total thickness $T = t_f + t_s$ is bent around a cylinder of radius $r$, the outermost fibre (top of the ink film) traces an arc of radius $R_{\text{outer}} = r + t_s + t_f$.

**Step 2 — Arc length of the outer fibre.**

$$
L_{\text{outer}} = (r + t_s + t_f)\cdot \theta
$$

**Step 3 — Arc length of the neutral axis (substrate mid-thickness).**
The neutral axis is approximately at $r + t_s/2$:

$$
L_{\text{neutral}} = \left(r + \dfrac{t_s}{2}\right)\cdot \theta
$$

**Step 4 — Engineering strain in the film.**

The film stretches from its rest length (along the neutral axis) to $L_{\text{outer}}$. The strain is:

$$
\varepsilon = \dfrac{L_{\text{outer}} - L_{\text{neutral}}}{L_{\text{neutral}}}
$$

Substituting:

$$
\varepsilon = \dfrac{(r + t_s + t_f) - \left(r + \dfrac{t_s}{2}\right)}{r + \dfrac{t_s}{2}}
$$

**Step 5 — Simplification (assuming $t_f \ll t_s$ and $t_s \ll r$, common in can-coating mandrel tests).**

$$
\varepsilon = \dfrac{\dfrac{t_s}{2} + t_f}{r + \dfrac{t_s}{2}}
$$

For thin films ($t_f \to 0$), this reduces to the classic flexural strain of the *substrate outer fibre*:

$$
\boxed{\;\varepsilon \;\approx\; \dfrac{t_s}{2r}\;}
$$

For the **ink film itself** (where we care about film cracking), the relevant local strain on the film layer is:

$$
\boxed{\;\varepsilon_{\text{film}} \;\approx\; \dfrac{t_f}{2r}\;}
$$

**Step 6 — Numerical example for KTU 2024 exam.**
A $t_f = 10\ \mu\text{m}$ ink film on a $t_s = 0.2\ \text{mm}$ tinplate substrate is bent on a mandrel of diameter $D = 6\ \text{mm}$ ($r = 3\ \text{mm}$). Compute the film strain.

$$
\varepsilon_{\text{film}} = \dfrac{t_f}{2r} = \dfrac{10 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{m}}{2 \times 3 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}} = \dfrac{10 \times 10^{-6}}{6 \times 10^{-3}}
$$

$$
\varepsilon_{\text{film}} = 1.667 \times 10^{-3} = 0.1667\,\%
$$

This is below the typical critical strain of ~0.5 % for well-formulated packaging inks, so the film should *pass* the mandrel test.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Kubelka–Munk Hiding Power

> This is the **second-most-asked** derivation in KTU past papers for printing ink topics.

### Step 1 — The Two-Flux Model.
Kubelka and Munk (1931) modelled light propagation in a pigmented film using two diffuse fluxes: one forward ($I$), one backward ($J$). The differential equations are:

$$
\dfrac{dI}{dx} = -(K + S)\,I + S\,J
$$

$$
\dfrac{dJ}{dx} = -(K + S)\,J + S\,I
$$

where $K$ = absorption coefficient, $S$ = scattering coefficient, $x$ = depth into film.

**Step 2 — Boundary conditions for an infinitely thick film.**
At $x \to \infty$, $I$ and $J$ reach equilibrium. The reflectance at $x = 0$ for an infinitely thick film ($R_\infty$) satisfies:

$$
R_\infty = 1 + \dfrac{K}{S} - \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{K}{S}\right)^2 + 2\,\dfrac{K}{S}}
$$

**Step 3 — Solving for $K/S$ in terms of $R_\infty$.**
Let $a = K/S$. Then:

$$
a = \dfrac{(1 - R_\infty)^2}{2 R_\infty}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\dfrac{K}{S} = \dfrac{(1 - R_\infty)^2}{2 R_\infty}\;}
$$

**Step 4 — Physical interpretation.**
- If $S \to 0$ (no scattering, e.g., a clear varnish), $K/S \to \infty$ and the film absorbs all light — *no hiding*.
- If $K \to 0$ (no absorption, e.g., a white pigment), $K/S \to 0$ and the film scatters light back — *maximum hiding*.

**Step 5 — Contrast ratio check.**
For a film of *finite thickness* $h$, the reflectance over a black substrate $R_B$ and white substrate $R_W$ gives the contrast ratio:

$$
C.R. = \dfrac{R_B}{R_W}
$$

Hiding power is considered adequate when $C.R. \ge 0.98$ for white inks; for colored inks, $C.R. \ge 0.95$ is typically accepted.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Pull-Off Adhesion Stress (ASTM D4541)

**Step 1 — Apply a tensile load $F$ perpendicular to a circular dolly of diameter $d$ glued to the dried film.**

**Step 2 — The contact area is:**

$$
A = \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4}
$$

**Step 3 — The average pull-off stress is:**

$$
\boxed{\;\sigma_a = \dfrac{F}{A} = \dfrac{4F}{\pi d^2}\;}
$$

**Step 4 — Failure mode classification.**
- *Adhesive failure* at film–substrate interface → $\sigma_a$ is the true interfacial strength.
- *Cohesive failure* in film → $\sigma_a$ is a lower bound on interfacial strength.
- *Substrate failure* → test is invalid (substrate weaker than interface).

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Test → Failure Mode → Formulation Fix

| Test | Dominant Stress | Typical Failure Mode | Formulation Lever to Improve |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cross-Cut Adhesion | Tensile peel at 60° | Adhesive (film lifts) | Add adhesion promoter (e.g., Titanate coupling agent); corona-treat PE/PP |
| Pencil Hardness | Point shear | Cohesive (film gouges) | Increase crosslink density; add harder resin (e.g., melamine-formaldehyde) |
| Taber Abrasion | Rolling friction | Surface erosion | Add waxes (PE, PTFE), hard fillers (SiO₂); raise binder $T_g$ |
| Mandrel Bend | Tensile strain on outer fibre | Cohesive (cracks) | Lower film modulus; add plasticizer; reduce $t_f$ |
| Impact | Sudden tensile + shear | Cohesive + adhesive | Toughen with elastomeric resin (e.g., polyurethane dispersion) |
| MEK Double Rub | Solvent swelling + shear | Cohesive erosion | Increase crosslink density; use higher functionality resin |
| Blocking | Compression + thermal creep | Adhesive (tack) | Reduce $T_g$ of surface; add slip agent (e.g., erucamide) |
| Gloss | Specular reflection | Surface micro-roughness | Improve substrate wetting; reduce matting agents; proper drying |
| Light Fastness | UV photo-oxidation | Chemical (chain scission) | Add HALS, UV absorbers; use light-fast pigments |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow: Dry Paint Performance Testing Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Sample Prep: Apply Ink to Standard Substrate] --> B[Controlled Drying / Curing per Spec]
    B --> C{Conditioning 23C, 50 percent RH, 24 h}
    C --> D[Mechanical Tests]
    C --> E[Adhesion Tests]
    C --> F[Optical Tests]
    C --> G[Chemical / Env Tests]
    D --> D1[Pencil Hardness ASTM D3363]
    D --> D2[Taber Abrasion ASTM D4060]
    D --> D3[Mandrel Bend ASTM D522]
    D --> D4[Impact ASTM D2794]
    E --> E1[Cross-Cut Tape ASTM D3359]
    E --> E2[Pull-Off ASTM D4541]
    F --> F1[Gloss 60 deg ASTM D523]
    F --> F2[Hiding Power ASTM D2805]
    G --> G1[MEK Double Rub ASTM D5402]
    G --> G2[Blocking ASTM D4946]
    G --> G3[Light Fastness ISO 4892]
    D1 --> H[Pass / Fail Decision]
    D2 --> H
    D3 --> H
    D4 --> H
    E1 --> H
    E2 --> H
    F1 --> H
    F2 --> H
    G1 --> H
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H
    H --> I[QC Report and Formulation Feedback]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Architecture: Cross-Cut Adhesion Test (ASTM D3359)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Dried Ink Film on Substrate] --> B[Cut Grid 6 x 6 with 1 mm Spacing using Razor]
    B --> C[Apply 75 mm Adhesive Tape Press Firmly]
    C --> D[Remove Tape at 60 deg in 0.5 to 1.0 s]
    D --> E[Inspect Under 10x Magnification]
    E --> F{Compare Removed Area vs ASTM Chart}
    F --> F1[5B: 0 percent removed Best]
    F --> F2[4B: Less than 5 percent]
    F --> F3[3B: 5 to 15 percent]
    F --> F4[2B: 15 to 35 percent]
    F --> F5[1B: 35 to 65 percent]
    F --> F6[0B: Greater than 65 percent Worst]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Architecture: Taber Abrasion Test (ASTM D4060)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Mount Specimen on Turntable] --> B[Apply Calibrated Load 500 or 1000 g]
    B --> C[Lower Abrasive Wheels CS-10 CS-17 or H-22]
    C --> D[Rotate Turntable at 60 rpm]
    D --> E[Run for N cycles typically 1000]
    E --> F[Weigh Specimen W final]
    F --> G[Compute Delta W equals W initial minus W final]
    G --> H[Report mg loss per 1000 cycles]
    H --> I{Lower Delta W equals Better Abrasion}
    I --> J[Pass if Delta W less than Specified Limit]
    I --> K[Fail: Reformulate with Wax or Hard Filler]
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Architecture: MEK Double Rub Test (ASTM D5402)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Soak Cheesecloth in MEK] --> B[Attach to Crockmeter Finger]
    B --> C[Place Specimen Flat on Crockmeter]
    C --> D[Move Finger Back and Forth 100 mm Stroke]
    D --> E[Inspect After Every 10 Rubs]
    E --> F{Film Eroded to Substrate}
    F --> F1[Record N MEK equals Rub Count]
    F1 --> G[Compare to Spec: N MEK greater than 100 typical pass]
    G --> H[Reformulate: Higher Crosslink Density]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading the diagrams:** Each block in the flow represents an *operational state* of the test. The arrows show the *sequence of operations* in a real QC lab. The decision diamonds (rhombi) correspond to the *judgment steps* where a technician makes a pass/fail call.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define Dry Paint Performance Tests. List any four major tests with the property they evaluate.
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2, Q1a]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):**
Dry paint performance tests are a suite of standardized laboratory procedures used to evaluate the **physical, mechanical, optical, and chemical properties** of a dried/cured printing ink film deposited on a substrate, so as to predict its behaviour during post-printing handling, conversion, transport, and end-use.

**Four major tests (1.5 marks — 0.375 each):**
1. **Cross-Cut Tape Test (ASTM D3359):** evaluates *adhesion* between film and substrate.
2. **Pencil Hardness Test (ASTM D3363):** evaluates *scratch resistance / surface hardness*.
3. **Taber Abrasion Test (ASTM D4060):** evaluates *abrasion resistance* under rolling friction.
4. **MEK Double Rub Test (ASTM D5402):** evaluates *chemical/solvent resistance* and degree of cure.

---

### Q2. Differentiate between **Cohesive Failure** and **Adhesive Failure** in a dried ink film. How is this differentiation useful in QC?
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper 1, Q1b]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Cohesive Failure | Adhesive Failure |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** (1 m) | The film splits *within itself*; substrate is left clean. | The film detaches *from the substrate*; the interface fails. |
| **Mechanism** (0.5 m) | Internal cohesive strength of the binder < applied stress. | Adhesive bond strength at the film–substrate interface < applied stress. |
| **Diagnosis** (0.5 m) | Reformulation lever: ↑ crosslink density, ↑ binder $T_g$, add hard filler. | Reformulation lever: add adhesion promoter, corona-treat substrate, change primer. |
| **QC utility** (1 m) | Tells the formulator whether to **strengthen the film** itself. | Tells the formulator whether to **improve the interface** (substrate pre-treatment, primer, coupling agent). |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question has sub-parts (a) for 7 marks and (b) for 7 marks. Internal choice means *Question A OR Question B* is offered to the student; both are provided here for exam preparation.

---

### ❓ Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, End Semester, Module 4, Q3a-or-Q3b type]**

**(a) Explain the Cross-Cut Adhesion Test in detail. Discuss the ASTM D3359 classification with the percentage of area removed for each grade. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Principle (1 m):** The test evaluates the *adhesion* of a dried ink film to its substrate by cutting a lattice pattern through the film, applying adhesive tape, and removing it. The percentage of film that is lifted off by the tape indicates the adhesion quality.

2. **Apparatus (1 m):** Sharp razor blade or cross-cut guide with 6 × 6 blades at 1 mm spacing (for films < 50 µm) or 2 mm spacing (for films 50–125 µm); 75 mm wide pressure-sensitive adhesive tape (e.g., 3M #250); magnifying lens (10×).

3. **Procedure (2 m):**
   - Apply the ink on a flat, rigid substrate and dry/cure as per specification.
   - Condition the specimen for 24 h at 23 °C, 50 % RH.
   - Hold the cross-cut guide flat on the film; make 6 parallel cuts, then 6 perpendicular cuts to form a 6 × 6 grid.
   - Brush off loose flakes with a soft brush.
   - Apply a piece of 75 mm adhesive tape over the grid; press firmly with a fingertip or eraser to ensure uniform contact.
   - Within 90 s, grip the free end of the tape and **pull it off at a 60° angle in 0.5–1.0 s**.
   - Inspect the grid under 10× magnification and compare to the ASTM chart.

4. **ASTM D3359 Classification (3 m):**

| Classification | Percent Area Removed | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **5B** | **0 %** | None — perfect adhesion |
| **4B** | **< 5 %** | Trace peeling along incisions |
| **3B** | **5 – 15 %** | Slight flaking along incisions |
| **2B** | **15 – 35 %** | Flaking along incisions and at intersections |
| **1B** | **35 – 65 %** | Extensive flaking |
| **0B** | **> 65 %** | Severe peeling — total adhesion failure |

   **Industrial benchmark:** A commercial packaging ink should achieve **at least 4B** on the intended substrate; **3B** is the minimum acceptable for non-critical applications.

**(b) Explain the Taber Abrasion Test with neat block diagram. Derive the formula for weight loss per 1000 cycles. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Principle (1 m):** A specimen is mounted on a rotating turntable and subjected to the *rolling abrasion* of two abrasive wheels (e.g., CS-10, CS-17) under a defined load. The mass loss after a set number of cycles is a measure of abrasion resistance.

2. **Apparatus (1 m):** Taber Abraser with turntable (60 rpm), pair of abrasive wheels (CS-10 = mild rubber-bonded; CS-17 = aggressive vitrified; H-22 = coarse), vacuum suction nozzle, calibrated loads (500 g or 1000 g), analytical balance (± 0.1 mg).

3. **Procedure (1.5 m):**
   - Cut specimen to ~100 mm disc or 100 × 100 mm square with a central hole for mounting.
   - Condition for 24 h at 23 °C, 50 % RH.
   - Weigh specimen to nearest 0.1 mg → record $W_i$.
   - Mount on turntable; apply load; lower abrasive wheels.
   - Run for 1000 cycles (or as specified); vacuum off debris.
   - Reweigh → record $W_f$.

4. **Derivation of weight-loss formula (2.5 m):**

The total mass loss after $N$ cycles is:

$$
\Delta W = W_i - W_f
$$

Normalized to **per 1000 cycles** for a common reporting basis:

$$
\boxed{\;W_L = \dfrac{\Delta W}{N} \times 1000 = \dfrac{(W_i - W_f)}{N} \times 1000\;}
$$

Units: mg per 1000 cycles (or g per 1000 cycles, depending on balance precision).

**Sample calculation:** If $W_i = 12.5432$ g, $W_f = 12.5398$ g, $N = 500$ cycles:

$$
\Delta W = 12.5432 - 12.5398 = 0.0034\ \text{g} = 3.4\ \text{mg}
$$

$$
W_L = \dfrac{3.4\ \text{mg}}{500} \times 1000 = 6.8\ \text{mg per 1000 cycles}
$$

5. **Industrial benchmark (1 m):**
   - Magazine-cover inks: $W_L < 10$ mg / 1000 cycles.
   - Carton inks: $W_L < 30$ mg / 1000 cycles.
   - Floor coatings: $W_L < 50$ mg / 1000 cycles.

---

### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper 2, Q4]**

**(a) Explain the Pencil Hardness Test. How is the result reported? Compare with the Wolff-Wilborn scale. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Principle (1 m):**
   The test determines the *scratch resistance* of a dried ink film by pushing pencil leads of progressively increasing hardness across the film at a 45° angle under a constant load (typically 500 g or 1 kg). The hardest pencil that does **not** scratch or gouge the film is the reported hardness.

2. **Apparatus (1.5 m):**
   - Set of calibrated wooden pencils ranging from **6B (softest) to 6H (hardest)**, in 14 grades: 6B, 5B, 4B, 3B, 2B, B, HB, F, H, 2H, 3H, 4H, 5H, 6H.
   - Mechanical pencil holder / cart with constant 45° angle and 500 g / 1 kg load.
   - Abrasive paper (e.g., 400-grit) to flatten the pencil tip before each test.
   - Magnifying lamp for inspection.

3. **Procedure (2 m):**
   - Condition specimen at 23 °C, 50 % RH for 24 h.
   - Starting from the **hardest** pencil (6H), flatten the tip on 400-grit sandpaper until a clean cylindrical tip is obtained (do not point it).
   - Place the pencil in the holder; push across the film for a stroke of ~6–10 mm.
   - Wipe and inspect: any visible scratch or gouge = film has failed at this hardness.
   - Move down one step in softness; repeat until **two consecutive pencils do not produce a scratch**. The harder of the two is the reported hardness.

4. **Reporting (1 m):**
   Result is reported as e.g., **"Pencil Hardness = 3H"** meaning pencils of 3H and softer did not scratch, but 4H did.

5. **Comparison with Wolff–Wilborn scale (1.5 m):**
   The Wolff–Wilborn scale (used in this test) is a *practical industrial scale* using 14 grades. It correlates *roughly* with the Mohs mineral hardness scale, but is **not a direct conversion**:
   - HB ≈ Mohs 1 (graphite / talc).
   - 6H ≈ Mohs 6–7 (quartz).
   - 6B ≈ Mohs 0.5 (very soft).
   The Wolff–Wilborn scale measures *scratch resistance of a coating*, not absolute mineral hardness.

**(b) Explain the Mandrel Bend Test. Derive the formula for bending strain in the outer fibre of the film. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Principle (1 m):**
   The test determines the *flexibility* and *adherence* of a dried film on a substrate (typically metal) by bending the specimen around a cylindrical mandrel of known diameter. The smallest mandrel diameter at which the film shows **no cracking or detachment** is the reported flexibility.

2. **Apparatus (1 m):** Cylindrical mandrel set with diameters ¼″, ⅜″, ½″, ¾″, 1″, 1¼″, 1½″, 2″ (8 mandrels); bending lever / conical mandrel (optional).

3. **Procedure (1.5 m):**
   - Coat substrate with ink; dry/cure.
   - Condition 24 h.
   - Bend specimen ~180° around the mandrel in 1 s.
   - Inspect immediately with 10× lens for cracks, lifting, or detachment.
   - Repeat with progressively smaller mandrels until failure is observed.

4. **Derivation of bending strain (3.5 m):**

Following the derivation in Section 3.1:

- Outer fibre arc length: $L_{\text{outer}} = (r + t_s + t_f)\,\theta$
- Neutral axis arc length: $L_{\text{neutral}} = \left(r + \dfrac{t_s}{2}\right)\,\theta$
- Strain in film:

$$
\varepsilon_{\text{film}} = \dfrac{L_{\text{outer}} - L_{\text{neutral}}}{L_{\text{neutral}}} = \dfrac{t_f + \dfrac{t_s}{2}}{r + \dfrac{t_s}{2}}
$$

For thin films ($t_f \ll r$, $t_s \ll r$):

$$
\boxed{\;\varepsilon_{\text{film}} \;\approx\; \dfrac{t_f}{2r} = \dfrac{t_f}{D}\;}
$$

where $D$ = mandrel diameter.

**Numerical example:** $t_f = 25\ \mu\text{m}$, mandrel $D = 6\ \text{mm}$:

$$
\varepsilon_{\text{film}} = \dfrac{25 \times 10^{-6}}{6 \times 10^{-3}} = 4.17 \times 10^{-3} = 0.417\,\%
$$

If the ink's **critical strain** (εc) is ≥ 0.5 %, the film passes. If εc = 0.3 %, the film cracks.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout** ⚠️
>
> **Common mistakes that cost marks in Mandrel / Strain derivations:**
> 1. **Confusing $r$ and $D$:** Students often write $D/2$ wrong — *diameter* vs *radius*. $D = 2r$. Always double-check.
> 2. **Forgetting unit conversion:** $t_f$ in µm and $D$ in mm give strain in *dimensionless ratio*, but you must convert to common units (e.g., both in m).
> 3. **Neglecting the $t_s/2$ term:** A common approximation error. If $t_s$ is comparable to $r$, the approximation $\varepsilon \approx t_f/(2r)$ breaks down.
> 4. **Wrong failure criterion:** It is not "no scratch" — it is "no crack or detachment of film from substrate". A common exam trap.
> 5. **Missing the practical "pass" statement:** Always finish with a *verdict* — e.g., "since ε = 0.42 % < εc = 0.5 %, the film passes the 6 mm mandrel test."

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE — Module 4**

- ✅ Dry paint performance tests evaluate the **dried/cured ink film** properties — not the wet ink.
- ✅ Four test families: **Mechanical, Adhesion, Optical, Chemical/Environmental**.
- ✅ **ASTM D3359 (Cross-Cut)** uses grades **5B (best) → 0B (worst)** based on **% area removed**.
- ✅ **ASTM D3363 (Pencil Hardness)** uses **6B (softest) → 6H (hardest)**; report the *hardest pencil that does not scratch*.
- ✅ **ASTM D4060 (Taber Abrasion)** measures **mass loss per 1000 cycles** under a defined load (500 g / 1000 g) and abrasive wheel (CS-10 / CS-17 / H-22).
- ✅ **ASTM D522 (Mandrel Bend)** uses smallest mandrel **diameter** at which film shows *no cracking*. Strain formula: $\varepsilon_{\text{film}} \approx t_f / D$.
- ✅ **ASTM D5402 (MEK Double Rub)** counts rubs to substrate exposure; **>100 rubs = excellent** for cured films.
- ✅ **ASTM D523 (Gloss)** measured at **60°** (universal); supplemented by 20° and 85°.
- ✅ **ASTM D4541 (Pull-Off Adhesion)** gives stress: $\sigma_a = 4F / (\pi d^2)$.
- ✅ **Hiding power** uses **Kubelka–Munk** relation: $K/S = (1 - R_\infty)^2 / (2R_\infty)$.
- ✅ **Contrast ratio** $C.R. = R_B / R_W \ge 0.98$ for full hiding (white inks).
- ✅ **Light fastness ΔE** is computed in **CIELab** space: $\Delta E = \sqrt{(\Delta L^*)^2 + (\Delta a^*)^2 + (\Delta b^*)^2}$.
- ✅ **Blocking** is the unwanted adhesion of two printed surfaces; tested under load + temperature.
- ✅ **Cohesive failure** = film splits; **adhesive failure** = film lifts from substrate.
- ✅ **Standard conditioning** before all tests: **23 °C, 50 % RH, 24 h** (unless otherwise specified).
- ✅ **MEK rub formulation lever:** raise crosslink density / functionality of resin.
- ✅ **Adhesion formulation lever:** add adhesion promoter (chlorinated polyolefin for film, silane for glass).
- ✅ **Gloss formulation lever:** improve substrate wetting; reduce matting agent loading.
- ✅ **Light fastness formulation lever:** add HALS + UV absorbers; use light-fast organic pigments (e.g., phthalocyanines, quinacridones) and inorganic pigments (e.g., iron oxide, rutile TiO₂).
- ✅ **Taber abrasion formulation lever:** add waxes (PE, PTFE) and hard fillers (silica, alumina).
- ✅ **Typical exam traps:** mixing $r$ and $D$; missing unit conversions; omitting pass/fail verdict; confusing ASTM and ISO numbering; ignoring film–substrate interface failure mode.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Analysis of Printing Inks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT866 - TECHNOLOGY OF PRINTING INKS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Analysis of Printing Inks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

**Analysis of Printing Inks** refers to the systematic application of physical, chemical, rheological, optical, and mechanical test methods used to characterize, quality-control, and predict the end-use performance of liquid and paste printing inks. As per KTU 2024 scheme (PEPOT866, Module 4), it encompasses the evaluation of raw materials (pigments, vehicles, additives), the finished ink product, and the resulting dried print film on the substrate.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 4 Focus:** The course outcome (CO) for this module emphasizes the ability of the student to **select appropriate analytical techniques** to evaluate ink properties and **interpret test data** to troubleshoot printing defects. Analysis is therefore the bridge between *formulation* (Module 3) and *application* (Module 5).

The three classical pillars of ink analysis recognized by **ISO 2846**, **ASTM D 5010**, and **TAPPI** standards are:

1. **Rheological Analysis** — how the ink flows.
2. **Optical/Colorimetric Analysis** — how the ink looks.
3. **Mechanical/Film Analysis** — how the ink survives on the substrate.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Medical Check-up"

Imagine a freshly manufactured ink as a **patient entering a hospital**. Before the doctor prescribes medicine, the patient goes through:

| Hospital Test | Equivalent Ink Test | What it Reveals |
|---|---|---|
| Blood test (viscosity) | Viscosity cup / Rheometer | Flow behaviour during transfer |
| Eye check-up (spectrophotometry) | Spectrophotometer / Densitometer | Color strength, hue, shade |
| Skin allergy test (adhesion) | Tape test / Cross-hatch | Bonding with substrate |
| Bone density (hardness) | Pencil hardness / Pendulum test | Surface toughness of dried film |
| Stress test (rubs) | Sutherland Rub Tester | Resistance to scuffing & abrasion |

Just as no single blood parameter tells the whole story, no single ink test is sufficient. **A battery of tests** is mandated by KTU to certify ink quality.

---

## 1.3 Why Ink Analysis is Non-Negotiable in the Printing Industry

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Metrics Used Globally:**
> - **Viscosity:** Centipoise (cP) or Pascal-seconds (Pa·s)
> - **Tack:** Tack units (0–10 scale, inkometer)
> - **Fineness of Grind:** µm (Hegman scale 0–8)
> - **Color Difference (ΔE):** CIELAB units (acceptable: ΔE < 2)
> - **Gloss:** Gloss Units (GU) at 60° geometry
> - **Density:** Densitometric units (typically 0.00 – 2.50)

These metrics are **boundary-defining numbers** — they determine whether an ink passes or fails the press trial.

---

## 1.4 Classification of Analytical Methods (KTU Framework)

For the purpose of KTU examinations, analysis is divided into **four macro-categories**:

```
A. PHYSICAL ANALYSIS
   ├── Rheological (viscosity, tack, yield value)
   ├── Fineness of grind
   ├── Specific gravity
   └── Drying / setting time

B. OPTICAL / COLORIMETRIC ANALYSIS
   ├── Spectrophotometry (CIE L*a*b*)
   ├── Densitometry (CMYK filters)
   ├── Gloss measurement
   └── Transparency / Opacity

C. CHEMICAL ANALYSIS
   ├── Pigment identification
   ├── Vehicle composition (oil length, acid value)
   └── Solvent residues

D. PERFORMANCE / FILM ANALYSIS
   ├── Adhesion (tape, cross-hatch)
   ├── Rub resistance (wet/dry)
   ├── Lightfastness
   └── Chemical resistance (soap, alkali, solvent)
```

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Rheological Analysis — The Heart of Ink Behaviour

### 2.1.1 Viscosity

**Definition:** Viscosity is the internal friction of a fluid, i.e., its resistance to flow under an applied shear stress.

**Mathematical Definition:**

$$\eta = \frac{\tau}{\dot{\gamma}}$$

Where:
- $\eta$ = dynamic viscosity (Pa·s)
- $\tau$ = shear stress (Pa)
- $\dot{\gamma}$ = shear rate (s⁻¹)

### 2.1.2 Types of Flow Behaviour in Inks

| Flow Type | Equation | Plot Shape | Example Ink |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Newtonian** | $\tau = \eta \dot{\gamma}$ | Straight line through origin | Solvent-based publication gravure |
| **Pseudoplastic (shear-thinning)** | $\tau = K \dot{\gamma}^n$, where $n < 1$ | Concave down | Letterpress, offset paste inks |
| **Dilatant (shear-thickening)** | $\tau = K \dot{\gamma}^n$, where $n > 1$ | Concave up | Highly pigmented inks (rare) |
| **Bingham Plastic** | $\tau = \tau_0 + \eta_p \dot{\gamma}$ | Straight line, intercept $\tau_0$ | Some paste inks |
| **Thixotropic** | Time-dependent shear thinning | Hysteresis loop | Lithographic inks |

### 2.1.3 The Power-Law (Ostwald–de Waele) Model — Most Tested

$$\tau = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^n$$

$$ \log \tau = \log K + n \cdot \log \dot{\gamma} $$

- $K$ = consistency index (Pa·sⁿ)
- $n$ = flow behaviour index (dimensionless)
  - $n = 1$ → Newtonian
  - $n < 1$ → Pseudoplastic
  - $n > 1$ → Dilatant

**Engineering utility:** This single two-parameter model lets a press operator predict ink behaviour at the high shear rates (~10⁵ s⁻¹) found in the nip of a modern offset press.

---

## 2.2 Tack — Offset Ink's Critical Parameter

**Tack** is the **force required to split** a thin film of ink between two rollers in contact, measured on a **Tack-o-scope or Inkometer**.

$$T = \frac{F}{A}$$

Where:
- $T$ = tack (tack units)
- $F$ = force required to split the film (N)
- $A$ = area of contact (m²)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Rule:** Tack must **decrease** down the roller train (sequence: 1st roller > 2nd > 3rd > … > forme roller) to ensure proper ink transfer and prevent **ink starvation** on the plate.

---

## 2.3 Fineness of Grind (Hegman Gauge)

Measures the **maximum particle size** of pigment dispersion. The Hegman gauge is a stainless-steel block with a tapered groove of **depth 0 → 100 µm over 150 mm length**.

Reading scale: **0 (coarsest) → 8 (finest)** corresponds to **100 µm → 0 µm**.

$$D_{particle} = 100 - \left( \frac{\text{reading on gauge}}{8} \times 100 \right) \, \mu m$$

---

## 2.4 Colorimetric Analysis — The Mathematics of Color

### 2.4.1 CIELAB Color Difference (ΔE)

The cornerstone color-difference formula in modern ink analysis:

$$\Delta E_{ab}^{*} = \sqrt{(\Delta L^{*})^2 + (\Delta a^{*})^2 + (\Delta b^{*})^2}$$

Where:
- $L^{*}$ = lightness (0 = black, 100 = white)
- $a^{*}$ = red–green axis (+ red, – green)
- $b^{*}$ = yellow–blue axis (+ yellow, – blue)

### 2.4.2 CMC Color Difference (Improved Perceptually)

For **ΔE < 2**, the simpler ΔE formula can mask perceptible differences. The **CMC (1:c)** formula corrects this:

$$\Delta E_{CMC} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{\Delta L^{*}}{l \cdot S_L}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{\Delta C^{*}}{c \cdot S_C}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{\Delta H^{*}}{S_H}\right)^2}$$

Where $l = 2$, $c = 1$ (default), and $S_L, S_C, S_H$ are weighting functions.

---

## 2.5 Densitometry — The Pressman's Tool

Densitometers use **CMY filters** to measure the **spectral reflectance density** of solid prints:

$$D = -\log_{10} R$$

Where:
- $D$ = densitometric value
- $R$ = reflectance (0 to 1)

**Acceptable KTU standard ink densities on coated paper:**

| Ink | Target Density | Tolerance |
|---|---|---|
| Cyan | 1.40 | ± 0.05 |
| Magenta | 1.50 | ± 0.05 |
| Yellow | 1.05 | ± 0.05 |
| Black | 1.80 | ± 0.05 |

---

## 2.6 Gloss Measurement

**Gloss** is the specular reflection from a surface, measured at standard geometries:

| Geometry | Application |
|---|---|
| **60°** | General purpose (most inks/paper) |
| **20°** | High-gloss surfaces |
| **85°** | Matte surfaces |

$$G = \frac{I_{specular}}{I_{incident}} \times 100 \quad \text{(in Gloss Units, GU)}$$

---

## 2.7 Adhesion Tests

### 2.7.1 Cross-Cut (Tape) Test (ASTM D 3359)

A grid of cuts is made through the ink film, adhesive tape is applied and removed. The percentage of squares remaining = **adhesion rating**.

### 2.7.2 Peel Strength (Quantitative)

$$\sigma_{peel} = \frac{F}{w}$$

Where $w$ = width of tape (mm), $F$ = peel force (N).

---

## 2.8 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Property | Formula | Standard Test | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Viscosity | $\eta = \tau / \dot{\gamma}$ | ASTM D 562 (Stormer) | Pa·s or cP |
| 2 | Power-law | $\tau = K \dot{\gamma}^n$ | Cone-and-plate | Pa, s⁻¹ |
| 3 | Tack | $T = F/A$ | ASTM D 4361 (Inkometer) | tack units |
| 4 | Yield value | $\tau_0$ | Casson plot | Pa |
| 5 | Fineness | $D = 100 - (R/8)\times 100$ | Hegman (ISO 1524) | µm |
| 6 | Density (color) | $D = -\log_{10} R$ | Densitometry | dimensionless |
| 7 | Color diff. | $\Delta E = \sqrt{\Delta L^{*2}+\Delta a^{*2}+\Delta b^{*2}}$ | CIE 1976 | CIELAB units |
| 8 | Gloss | $G = I_{spec}/I_{inc}\times 100$ | ISO 2813 | GU |
| 9 | Adhesion | \% squares retained | ASTM D 3359 | % |
| 10 | Rub resistance | rubs to failure | Sutherland tester | cycles |
| 11 | Opacity | $O = R_0/R_b$ | TAPPI T 425 | ratio |
| 12 | Lightfastness | Blue Wool Scale 1–8 | ISO 105-B02 | wool grade |
| 13 | Drying time | time to set/oxidize | Dry-o-test | minutes |
| 14 | Acid value | mg KOH/g sample | ASTM D 974 | mg/g |
| 15 | Specific gravity | $\rho_{ink} = m/V$ | Pycnometer | g/cm³ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world engineering utility:** Every press operator on a Heidelberg Speedmaster checks tack and density at 500-sheet intervals during a run. Ink chemists formulate using power-law models to predict press stability. Print buyers accept/reject lots using ΔE measurements against master standards.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Converting Stormer Readings (KU) to Absolute Viscosity

**Background:** The Stormer viscometer (ASTM D 562) measures the **time in seconds** for 200 revolutions of a paddle immersed in ink under a standard weight. The reading is reported in **Krebs Units (KU)**.

The empirical conversion to **centipoise (cP)** uses the following polynomial (standard Stormer conversion chart fit):

$$\eta \, (cP) = 10 \cdot e^{1.534 + 0.00177 \cdot t}$$

Where $t$ = time in seconds for 100 revolutions.

**Step-by-step derivation of conversion logic:**

**Step 1:** Define Stormer weight in grams as $W$, paddle geometry constant as $K_p$, rotor submerged depth as $d$.

$$\tau = \frac{K_p \cdot W}{d^2}$$

**Step 2:** Time per revolution $t_{rev}$ is inversely proportional to shear rate $\dot{\gamma}$:

$$\dot{\gamma} = \frac{C_s}{t_{rev}} = \frac{C_s \cdot N}{t}$$

Where $N$ = number of revolutions (200), $C_s$ = standard calibration constant (≈ 27 for Stormer).

**Step 3:** Substitute into Newton's law:

$$\eta = \frac{\tau}{\dot{\gamma}} = \frac{K_p \cdot W \cdot t}{d^2 \cdot C_s \cdot N}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute numerical constants and group into the **empirical exponent** form to obtain the working formula:

$$\eta \, (cP) = 10^{\,0.493 \log t - 0.474}$$

$$\eta \, (cP) = 10 \cdot e^{(1.534 + 0.00177\,t)}$$

> **Worked Example:** A paste ink takes 75 seconds on Stormer at standard load. Compute viscosity.
>
> $$\eta = 10 \cdot e^{(1.534 + 0.00177 \times 75)}$$
>
> $$\eta = 10 \cdot e^{(1.534 + 0.13275)}$$
>
> $$\eta = 10 \cdot e^{1.66675}$$
>
> $$\eta = 10 \times 5.293$$
>
> $$\boxed{\eta \approx 52.93 \text{ cP}}$$
>
> This is a typical mid-range **offset ink viscosity**, perfectly aligned with KTU standard.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Determining Power-Law Parameters K and n from Rheometer Data

**Background:** The Casson linearization is most popular for inks. We derive both the **Casson model** and **Power-law model**.

### 3.2.1 Casson Model (Industry Standard for Inks)

$$\sqrt{\tau} = \sqrt{\tau_0} + \sqrt{\eta_\infty \cdot \dot{\gamma}}$$

**Step 1 — Square both sides:**

$$\tau = \tau_0 + 2\sqrt{\tau_0 \eta_\infty} \cdot \sqrt{\dot{\gamma}} + \eta_\infty \dot{\gamma}$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange to linear form:**

$$\sqrt{\tau} = \sqrt{\tau_0} + \sqrt{\eta_\infty} \cdot \sqrt{\dot{\gamma}}$$

This is a straight line: $Y = mX + c$ where:
- $Y = \sqrt{\tau}$
- $X = \sqrt{\dot{\gamma}}$
- $c = \sqrt{\tau_0}$ (intercept → gives yield value)
- $m = \sqrt{\eta_\infty}$ (slope → gives infinite-shear viscosity)

**Step 3 — Solve for ink parameters:**

$$\tau_0 = c^2 \quad \text{and} \quad \eta_\infty = m^2$$

> **Numerical Example:** Rheometer gives the following data:
>
> | $\dot{\gamma}$ (s⁻¹) | $\tau$ (Pa) |
> |---|---|
> | 100 | 14 |
> | 500 | 32 |
> | 1000 | 50 |
> | 5000 | 110 |
> | 10000 | 170 |
>
> Plot $\sqrt{\tau}$ vs $\sqrt{\dot{\gamma}}$, perform linear regression to find slope and intercept:
>
> - Slope $m = 0.104$ Pa⁰·⁵·s⁰·⁵
> - Intercept $c = 1.05$ Pa⁰·⁵
>
> Therefore:
>
> $$\eta_\infty = (0.104)^2 = 0.0108 \text{ Pa·s}$$
>
> $$\tau_0 = (1.05)^2 = 1.1025 \text{ Pa}$$
>
> These are typical **paste ink Casson parameters**.

### 3.2.2 Power-Law Parameters via log–log Plot

From $\tau = K \dot{\gamma}^n$:

$$\log \tau = \log K + n \log \dot{\gamma}$$

So a plot of $\log \tau$ vs $\log \dot{\gamma}$ gives:
- **Slope = n** (flow index)
- **Intercept = log K** (so $K = 10^{intercept}$)

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Color Difference (ΔE) Computation from Spectrophotometer Readings

**Background:** A spectrophotometer provides $L^*$, $a^*$, $b^*$ values for both a **standard (s)** and a **sample (smp)** ink print.

**Step 1:** Calculate each delta component:

$$\Delta L^{*} = L^{*}_{smp} - L^{*}_{std}$$

$$\Delta a^{*} = a^{*}_{smp} - a^{*}_{std}$$

$$\Delta b^{*} = b^{*}_{smp} - b^{*}_{std}$$

**Step 2:** Apply Euclidean distance formula:

$$\Delta E_{ab}^{*} = \sqrt{(\Delta L^{*})^2 + (\Delta a^{*})^2 + (\Delta b^{*})^2}$$

> **Numerical Example:**
>
> | | Standard | Sample |
> |---|---|---|
> | $L^*$ | 50.0 | 51.2 |
> | $a^*$ | 25.0 | 26.8 |
> | $b^*$ | −40.0 | −39.1 |
>
> Compute:
>
> $$\Delta L^{*} = 51.2 - 50.0 = 1.2$$
>
> $$\Delta a^{*} = 26.8 - 25.0 = 1.8$$
>
> $$\Delta b^{*} = -39.1 - (-40.0) = 0.9$$
>
> $$\Delta E = \sqrt{(1.2)^2 + (1.8)^2 + (0.9)^2}$$
>
> $$\Delta E = \sqrt{1.44 + 3.24 + 0.81} = \sqrt{5.49}$$
>
> $$\boxed{\Delta E \approx 2.34}$$
>
> **Interpretation:** Since $\Delta E > 2$, the sample is **perceptibly different** from the standard. Re-formulation or shade adjustment is needed.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Test (KTU Decision Tree)

```
                    START — What do you want to know?
                                  │
        ┌─────────────┬───────────┴───────────┬──────────────┐
        ▼             ▼                       ▼              ▼
   Flow behaviour  Color Quality        Surface Quality   Composition
        │             │                       │              │
        ▼             ▼                       ▼              ▼
   Viscosity      Spectro-              Gloss / Rub      Acid value
   Tack           photometer            Tester           TGA / IR
   Yield Value    Densitometer          Adhesion Test    Pigment ID
        │             │                       │
        ▼             ▼                       ▼
   REPORT η, T     REPORT ΔE, D         REPORT GU, %
   Pass/Fail       Pass if ΔE<2         Pass if >95%
```

| Problem on Press | First Test to Run | Acceptance Criterion |
|---|---|---|
| Ink not transferring | Viscosity at press shear rate | $\eta$ within ± 10% of standard |
| Misting on offset | Tack check | $T$ in 8–12 range |
| Shade mismatch | ΔE vs master | $\Delta E < 2$ |
| Ink chalking | Pigment/binder ratio + rub test | No powder rub-off after 50 rubs |
| Poor drying | Set/oxidative drying time | $\le 10$ min (offset) |
| Picking of paper | Pick test (IGT) | Pick strength $\ge$ paper IGT value |

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define the term "tack" of a printing ink. How is it measured? Why is it important in offset lithography?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Tack is the resistance of an ink film to splitting, defined as the force required to separate two rollers with a thin ink film between them, expressed in **tack units**.

$$T = \frac{F}{A} \quad \text{(N/m}^2\text{)}$$

**Measurement (1 mark):** It is measured using an **Inkometer (Tack-o-scope)** at 25 °C and 400 rpm, with the test roller running against a flat distributor roller under a known load. The torque transmitted through the ink film is converted to a tack reading on a 0–10 scale.

**Importance in offset (1 mark):** In offset, tack must decrease progressively from the duct roller → transfer rollers → forme roller → plate → blanket → substrate. Proper tack gradient ensures clean ink transfer, prevents **misting**, and avoids **piling** on the blanket. An excessively high tack can also cause **picking** of the paper surface.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is meant by "fineness of grind" of a printing ink? Name the instrument used and the typical range.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Fineness of grind is a measure of the **maximum particle size** of pigment dispersion in the ink vehicle, indicating how finely the pigment has been ground/milled.

**Instrument (1 mark):** It is measured using a **Hegman Gauge (also called a grindometer)**, a steel block with a tapered groove of depth 0 → 100 µm. The ink is drawn down the groove, and the point where continuous streaks or particles first become visible is read on a scale.

**Range (1 mark):** The Hegman scale runs from **0 to 8**, corresponding to particle sizes of **100 µm to 0 µm**. For offset inks, typical acceptable fineness is **Hegman 6–7 (≈ 12 µm)**, while for letterpress and news inks, 4–6 is acceptable. For high-quality process inks, fineness must be **≥ 7 Hegman**.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### Question A: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / KTU Model Paper]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain with neat sketches the working principle of a **cone-and-plate rheometer**. Describe how the **Casson model** is used to determine the **yield value** and **infinite-shear viscosity** of a printing ink. Given the following rheometer data for a paste ink, compute the yield value and the infinite-shear viscosity:

| Shear rate $\dot{\gamma}$ (s⁻¹) | Shear stress $\tau$ (Pa) |
|---|---|
| 50 | 8.0 |
| 200 | 14.0 |
| 800 | 28.0 |
| 2500 | 52.0 |
| 8000 | 100.0 |

**(b) [7 marks]** What is meant by the **power-law (Ostwald–de Waele) model** of non-Newtonian flow? How are the parameters $K$ and $n$ determined experimentally? State the physical significance of $n$ for (i) a Newtonian fluid, (ii) a shear-thinning ink, and (iii) a shear-thickening ink. Comment on the suitability of each model for **offset printing ink** during high-shear transfer at the nip.

---

### Model Answer — Question A

**(a) Cone-and-plate rheometer + Casson model [7 marks]**

**Working Principle (3 marks):**

A cone-and-plate rheometer consists of a flat lower plate (stationary) and an upper cone with a very small cone angle (typically < 1°). The ink sample is placed in the thin gap between them. The cone is rotated at controlled angular velocity $\omega$, and the torque $M$ required to maintain this rotation is measured.

```
              Rotation ω (about vertical axis)
                       │
                       ▼
                  ┌─────────┐
                  │  CONE   │   ← angle α ≈ 0.5° to 4°
                  └─────────┘
                       │ ← gap filled with ink (5–100 µm)
                  ┌─────────┐
                  │  PLATE  │   ← stationary, temperature-controlled
                  └─────────┘
```

**Shear rate is uniform throughout the gap:**

$$\dot{\gamma} = \frac{\omega}{\alpha}$$

where $\omega$ = angular velocity (rad/s), $\alpha$ = cone angle (radians).

**Shear stress is computed from torque:**

$$\tau = \frac{3M}{2\pi R^3}$$

where $R$ = cone radius.

**Casson Model Derivation (4 marks):**

The Casson equation for structured fluids (e.g., pigment dispersions in offset ink) is:

$$\sqrt{\tau} = \sqrt{\tau_0} + \sqrt{\eta_\infty} \cdot \sqrt{\dot{\gamma}}$$

This is linear: $Y = c + mX$ where $Y = \sqrt{\tau}$, $X = \sqrt{\dot{\gamma}}$, $c = \sqrt{\tau_0}$, $m = \sqrt{\eta_\infty}$.

**Calculation with given data:**

Compute $X = \sqrt{\dot{\gamma}}$ and $Y = \sqrt{\tau}$:

| $\dot{\gamma}$ | $\tau$ | $X=\sqrt{\dot{\gamma}}$ | $Y=\sqrt{\tau}$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 50 | 8.0 | 7.071 | 2.828 |
| 200 | 14.0 | 14.142 | 3.742 |
| 800 | 28.0 | 28.284 | 5.292 |
| 2500 | 52.0 | 50.000 | 7.211 |
| 8000 | 100.0 | 89.443 | 10.000 |

**Linear regression** of $Y$ on $X$:

$$\bar{X} = \frac{7.071 + 14.142 + 28.284 + 50 + 89.443}{5} = 37.788$$

$$\bar{Y} = \frac{2.828 + 3.742 + 5.292 + 7.211 + 10}{5} = 5.815$$

Slope $m$:

$$m = \frac{\sum(X_i - \bar{X})(Y_i - \bar{Y})}{\sum(X_i - \bar{X})^2}$$

Computing deviations:

| $X_i - \bar{X}$ | $Y_i - \bar{Y}$ | Product | $(X_i-\bar{X})^2$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| −30.717 | −2.987 | 91.74 | 943.53 |
| −23.646 | −2.073 | 49.02 | 559.13 |
| −9.504 | −0.523 | 4.97 | 90.32 |
| 12.212 | 1.396 | 17.05 | 149.13 |
| 51.655 | 4.185 | 216.18 | 2668.19 |
| **Σ** | | **378.96** | **4410.30** |

$$m = \frac{378.96}{4410.30} = 0.0859 \text{ Pa}^{0.5} \text{s}^{0.5}$$

Intercept $c$:

$$c = \bar{Y} - m\bar{X} = 5.815 - (0.0859)(37.788) = 5.815 - 3.247 = 2.568 \text{ Pa}^{0.5}$$

Therefore:

$$\eta_\infty = m^2 = (0.0859)^2 = 0.00738 \text{ Pa·s}$$

$$\tau_0 = c^2 = (2.568)^2 = 6.60 \text{ Pa}$$

**Final answers:**

- **Infinite-shear viscosity:** $\eta_\infty \approx 7.4 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s
- **Yield value:** $\tau_0 \approx 6.6$ Pa

These are typical values for a **paste offset ink**.

---

**(b) Power-law model and physical significance [7 marks]**

**Model Statement (2 marks):** The Ostwald–de Waele model describes non-Newtonian fluids by:

$$\tau = K \dot{\gamma}^n \quad \Leftrightarrow \quad \log \tau = \log K + n \log \dot{\gamma}$$

**Determination of K and n (2 marks):** On a **log–log plot** of $\tau$ vs $\dot{\gamma}$:
- **Slope = n** (flow behaviour index)
- **Intercept = log K**, hence $K = 10^{\text{intercept}}$

**Physical Significance of n (3 marks):**

| Condition | Value of $n$ | Behaviour | Ink Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| Newtonian | $n = 1$ | Viscosity independent of shear | Publication gravure, flexo solvent inks |
| Shear-thinning (pseudoplastic) | $n < 1$ | Viscosity decreases with shear | Offset paste inks, screen inks |
| Shear-thickening (dilatant) | $n > 1$ | Viscosity increases with shear | Highly pigmented inks, suspensions |

**Comment on offset ink (3 marks):**

Offset inks operate under **high shear rates** ($\dot{\gamma} \approx 10^5$ s⁻¹) in the nip between rollers. The power-law model is excellent for predicting this behaviour because:

1. **Shear-thinning (n ≈ 0.7–0.9)** is desirable — the ink becomes less viscous under press shear, allowing smooth transfer, and recovers viscosity once on the substrate (preventing misting).
2. The **Casson model** is generally preferred for offset paste inks because it accounts for the **yield value** (critical to prevent ink dripping in ducts), which the simple power-law cannot.
3. At very high shear rates near the press nip, the **Carreau model** (which adds zero-shear and infinite-shear viscosities) gives the most accurate prediction.

---

### Question B: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 / KTU Model Paper]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the **CIELAB color space** with the help of a labeled diagram. Derive the color-difference formula $\Delta E_{ab}^*$. A standard ink has $L^* = 48.0$, $a^* = 22.0$, $b^* = -38.0$. A batch sample measures $L^* = 49.5$, $a^* = 24.2$, $b^* = -36.8$. Calculate $\Delta E_{ab}^*$ and state whether the sample passes QC.

**(b) [7 marks]** Describe the **Sutherland Rub Tester** method for evaluating rub resistance of a printed ink film. How are **wet rub** and **dry rub** tests different? State typical acceptable rub-resistance values for (i) publication gravure inks, (ii) offset inks, and (iii) packaging inks.

---

### Model Answer — Question B

**(a) CIELAB color space + ΔE derivation [7 marks]**

**CIELAB Description (3 marks):**

The CIELAB color space (CIE 1976 $L^*a^*b^*$) is a three-dimensional, perceptually uniform color model:

```
                       b* (+ yellow)
                        │
                        │
                        │
                        │─────── a* (+ red)
                       ╱
                      ╱
                     ╱
                    L* (lightness axis, vertical)
                   ╱
                  ╱
                 ╱
                a* (− green)
                │
                b* (− blue)
```

- **$L^*$ axis** — vertical, from 0 (black) to 100 (white).
- **$a^*$ axis** — horizontal, from −$a^*$ (green) to +$a^*$ (red).
- **$b^*$ axis** — horizontal, from −$b^*$ (blue) to +$b^*$ (yellow).

**Derivation (2 marks):**

The CIELAB values are derived from XYZ tristimulus values:

$$L^* = 116 \, f\!\left(\frac{Y}{Y_n}\right) - 16$$

$$a^* = 500 \left[ f\!\left(\frac{X}{X_n}\right) - f\!\left(\frac{Y}{Y_n}\right) \right]$$

$$b^* = 200 \left[ f\!\left(\frac{Y}{Y_n}\right) - f\!\left(\frac{Z}{Z_n}\right) \right]$$

where $f(t) = t^{1/3}$ for $t > (6/29)^3$ and $7.787t + 16/116$ otherwise.

The color difference between two specimens is the **Euclidean distance** in this 3-D space:

$$\Delta E_{ab}^* = \sqrt{(\Delta L^*)^2 + (\Delta a^*)^2 + (\Delta b^*)^2}$$

**Calculation (2 marks):**

| Component | Standard | Sample | $\Delta$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| $L^*$ | 48.0 | 49.5 | +1.5 |
| $a^*$ | 22.0 | 24.2 | +2.2 |
| $b^*$ | −38.0 | −36.8 | +1.2 |

$$\Delta E_{ab}^* = \sqrt{(1.5)^2 + (2.2)^2 + (1.2)^2}$$

$$\Delta E_{ab}^* = \sqrt{2.25 + 4.84 + 1.44}$$

$$\Delta E_{ab}^* = \sqrt{8.53}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta E_{ab}^* \approx 2.92}$$

**QC Decision:** Since $\Delta E > 2$, the sample **FAILS** the QC criterion (typical industry threshold: $\Delta E \leq 2$). The batch is **rejected**, requiring shade adjustment or re-dispersion.

---

**(b) Sutherland Rub Tester + Rub Resistance [7 marks]**

**Description of Sutherland Rub Tester (3 marks):**

The Sutherland Rub Tester is a motor-driven apparatus that rubs a weighted block (typically 1.81 kg or 0.91 kg load) back and forth across the surface of a printed substrate. The test simulates **abrasive wear** encountered during handling, stacking, and transport.

**Key components:**
- A **rubbing head** (felt, cork, or abrasive pad) attached to a weighted arm.
- A **reciprocating motor** providing typically 20–60 cycles per minute.
- A **clamp** to hold the printed sample firmly.

**Procedure:**
1. Mount the printed sample on the platform.
2. Affix the rubbing medium to the head (felt for dry rub; wet felt soaked in water for wet rub).
3. Apply standard load (commonly 2 lb = 0.91 kg, or 4 lb = 1.81 kg for aggressive testing).
4. Run for a specified number of strokes (typically 50, 100, 200, or to failure).
5. Visually evaluate the rubbed area using a **densitometer** or **gray-scale rating** for ink transfer or scuffing.

**Wet Rub vs Dry Rub (2 marks):**

| Aspect | Dry Rub Test | Wet Rub Test |
|---|---|---|
| Rubbing medium | Dry felt pad | Felt soaked in water or specified solvent |
| Purpose | Simulates dry friction (e.g., page turning) | Simulates water/solvent exposure (e.g., packaging) |
| Severity | Less severe | More severe — simulates sweating, dew, beverage spillage |
| Critical for | Magazines, books | Soap packaging, frozen food packs, cosmetics |

**Acceptable Rub Resistance Values (2 marks):**

| Ink Type | Acceptable Dry Rubs | Acceptable Wet Rubs |
|---|---|---|
| Publication gravure (magazines) | 50–100 | Not applicable |
| Offset inks (commercial print) | 200–500 | 20–50 |
| Packaging inks (cartons, labels) | 500+ | 100+ |
| Flexo packaging (PE/PP films) | 300+ | 50–100 |

Higher values indicate better rub resistance. For pharmaceutical and food packaging, wet rub of ≥ 100 is mandatory per **FDA** and **EC 1935/2004** guidelines.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> 1. **Do NOT** confuse "yield value" with "viscosity" — yield value is the **minimum stress** before flow begins; viscosity is the **rate of flow** once it begins.
> 2. **Do NOT** use the vertical pipe symbol `|` inside your written ΔE formula — examiners may mark the rest of the page wrong. Write it as `\Delta E = sqrt( ... )` only.
> 3. In Sutherland Rub tests, **state both the load AND the number of rubs** — a single number is incomplete.
> 4. For ΔE calculations, always compute **all three component deltas first**, then apply the square root — partial steps lose marks.
> 5. In rheology, students often forget the **temperature (25 °C)** at which viscosity must be reported. Always mention it.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Analysis is mandatory at three stages:** raw material, finished ink, dried print film.
- **Viscosity** is reported in Pa·s or cP, measured by **Stormer, cone-plate, or Brookfield** viscometers.
- **Tack** is unique to **offset** inks — controlled in a **tack gradient** down the roller train.
- **Yield value** comes from the **Casson model** intercept, indicating ink structure.
- **Fineness of grind** uses the **Hegman gauge**; offset inks need ≥ 6 Hegman.
- **CIELAB** uses three coordinates: $L^*$, $a^*$, $b^*$; ΔE < 2 is the industry QC pass limit.
- **Densitometry** measures reflectance density using **CMYK filters**; standard ink densities are 1.40/1.50/1.05/1.80.
- **Gloss** is measured at **60°** for general inks.
