- **Adhesion** is tested by **cross-cut** (qualitative) or **peel strength** (quantitative).
- **Rub resistance** uses the **Sutherland Rub Tester** with standard 2-lb load; dry and wet rubs serve different purposes.
- **Power-law parameters:** slope $n$ = flow index; intercept $K$ = consistency index.
- Always pair a **physical test** (viscosity) with an **optical test** (color) and a **performance test** (rub) for a complete analysis profile.
- **ISO standards** governing ink analysis: **ISO 1524** (fineness), **ISO 2813** (gloss), **ISO 105-B02** (lightfastness), **ASTM D 562** (viscosity), **ASTM D 3359** (adhesion).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Safety Measures in Handling Printing Chemicals
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPOT866 - TECHNOLOGY OF PRINTING INKS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Safety Measures in Handling Printing Chemicals # Safety Measures in Handling Printing Chemicals ## 1.1 Core Technical Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)** > *Safety measures in handling printing chemicals* refer to the systematic application of engineering controls, administrative procedures, personal protective equipment (PPE), and regulatory protocols designed to **identify, evaluate, and control occupational hazards** arising from the storage, transportation, mixing, application, and disposal of chemical substances used in the printing ink industry (solvents, pigments, resins, additives, driers, and photoinitiators). In the context of printing ink technology, safety is not a peripheral concern — it is a **core engineering discipline** governed by international frameworks such as: - **OSHA** (Occupational Safety and Health Administration) - **GHS** (Globally Harmonized System of Classification and Labelling of Chemicals) - **REACH** (Registration, Evaluation, Authorisation and Restriction of Chemicals – EU) - **NFPA** (National Fire Protection Association) ratings - **ISO 45001** (Occupational Health and Safety Management Systems) ### Conceptual Analogy — The "Kitchen Rule" > [!NOTE] > **Analogy: Safety in a Print Shop = Safety in a Professional Kitchen** > Think of a printing press as a high-performance commercial kitchen. Just as a chef handles boiling oil, sharp knives, and raw meat with a separate cutting board, gloves, and a hairnet, a press operator handles isocyanate hardeners, organic solvents, and UV photoinitiators with respirators, chemical-resistant gloves, and exhaust ventilation. **The "ingredients" of ink (resins, pigments, solvents) can be just as volatile, flammable, and toxic as kitchen reagents — and the "cooking" process (mixing, milling, printing) is a continuous chemical reaction.** Mishandling either leads to fire, contamination, or chronic health damage. ### Key Categories of Printing Chemical Hazards | Hazard Class | Typical Printing Ink Constituents | Acute Risk | |---|---|---| | **Flammable liquids (F)** | Toluene, Xylene, MEK, Ethyl Acetate, Mineral Spirits | Fire, Explosion | | **Solvents (Xn, Xi)** | Glycol ethers, Alcohols, Naphtha | CNS depression, dermatitis | | **Toxic pigments (T+)** | Lead Chromate, Cadmium Sulphide, Barium salts | Carcinogen, bioaccumulation | | **Sensitizers (Xn)** | Isocyanates, Epoxy resins, Acrylate monomers | Asthma, contact dermatitis | | **Oxidisers (O)** | Cobalt/Manganese driers, Benzoyl peroxide | Accelerates combustion | | **Corrosives (C)** | Strong acids/bases for fountain solutions | Burns, eye damage | | **Dust hazards** | Dry pigment powders, carbon black | Pneumoconiosis, lung damage | > [!IMPORTANT] > **The Three Pillars of Chemical Safety in a Print Plant** > 1. **Engineering Controls** — ventilation, enclosed mixers, LEV (Local Exhaust Ventilation) > 2. **Administrative Controls** — SOPs, training, MSDS access, signage > 3. **Personal Protective Equipment (PPE)** — gloves, respirators, goggles, aprons --- ## 1.2 Why This Topic is Critical in KTU Examinations - Direct questions on **MSDS interpretation**, **Hazard Pictograms**, and **TLV/PEL values** appear almost every semester. - Numerical questions on **evaporation rate**, **flash point classification**, and **ventilation CFM calculations** are tested in Part B. - Case-study questions on **spill management** and **first aid** are increasingly common under NEP 2020's competency-based assessment framework. # Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference ## 2.1 Hierarchy of Hazard Control (HOC) The universally accepted order of priority for controlling chemical hazards: 1. **Elimination** — remove the hazardous chemical entirely (e.g., switch to water-based inks). 2. **Substitution** — replace with a less hazardous alternative (e.g., replace toluene with isoparaffin). 3. **Engineering Controls** — LEV, enclosed mixing vessels, explosion-proof electrical fittings. 4. **Administrative Controls** — job rotation, training, restricted access, signage. 5. **PPE** — last line of defence; respirators, chemical-resistant gloves. > [!NOTE] > **The "1-2-3-4-5 Rule":** Students must remember this hierarchy in order. In KTU exams, if asked *"Which is the most preferred control measure?"* the answer is **Elimination**, not PPE. ## 2.2 Material Safety Data Sheet (MSDS / SDS) — The 16 Sections Under **GHS (Globally Harmonized System)** and **REACH**, every printing chemical must be supplied with a **Safety Data Sheet** containing 16 standardized sections: | # | Section Title | Purpose / Key Data | |---|---|---| | 1 | Identification | Product name, supplier, emergency contact | | 2 | Hazard Identification | GHS pictograms, signal words (Danger / Warning) | | 3 | Composition | Chemical ingredients, CAS numbers | | 4 | First-Aid Measures | Eye, skin, inhalation, ingestion response | | 5 | Fire-Fighting Measures | Suitable extinguishing media, flash point | | 6 | Accidental Release | Spill containment, decontamination | | 7 | Handling and Storage | Temperature, incompatibilities | | 8 | Exposure Controls / PPE | TLV, PEL, respirator type | | 9 | Physical / Chemical Properties | Boiling point, vapour pressure, evaporation rate | | 10 | Stability and Reactivity | Incompatibilities, decomposition products | | 11 | Toxicological Information | LD₅₀, LC₅₀, sensitization data | | 12 | Ecological Information | Aquatic toxicity, biodegradability | | 13 | Disposal Considerations | Waste classification, disposal method | | 14 | Transport Information | UN number, packing group, IMDG class | | 15 | Regulatory Information | REACH, OSHA, national inventories | | 16 | Other Information | SDS revision date, training notes | ## 2.3 GHS Hazard Pictograms (9 Mandatory Diamonds) | Pictogram | Symbol | Hazard Class | |---|---|---| | **GHS01** | Exploding bomb | Explosives, self-reactives, organic peroxides | | **GHS02** | Flame | Flammables, pyrophorics, self-heating | | **GHS03** | Flame over circle | Oxidizers | | **GHS04** | Gas cylinder | Gases under pressure | | **GHS05** | Corrosion | Skin/eye corrosion, metal corrosion | | **GHS06** | Skull and crossbones | Acute toxicity (fatal/toxic) | | **GHS07** | Exclamation mark | Irritant, narcotic, sensitizer | | **GHS08** | Health hazard | Carcinogen, mutagen, reproductive toxin | | **GHS09** | Environment | Aquatic toxicity | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Mnemonic: "Explosives Flame Oxidise Gas, Corrode Skull, Exclaim Health, Environment."** ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | Parameter | Formula / Standard | Units | Application | |---|---|---|---| | Flash Point (FP) | Lowest temp at which vapour ignites | °C | Flammable storage classification | | LEL (Lower Explosive Limit) | Min. vapour conc. for ignition | % v/v | Ventilation design | | UEL (Upper Explosive Limit) | Max. vapour conc. for ignition | % v/v | Ventilation design | | TLV-TWA | Time-Weighted Average exposure (8 hr) | ppm or mg/m³ | Workplace air quality | | TLV-STEL | Short-Term Exposure Limit (15 min) | ppm or mg/m³ | Peak exposures | | LD₅₀ | Lethal Dose for 50% of test animals | mg/kg body weight | Acute oral toxicity | | LC₅₀ | Lethal Concentration for 50% of test animals | ppm or mg/L (4 hr) | Acute inhalation toxicity | | Evaporation Rate (BuAc = 1) | n-Butyl Acetate = 1.0 reference | dimensionless | Drying rate of solvent | | Ventilation CFM | Q = (V × ACl × 60) / (%LEL × K) | ft³/min | LEV design for solvent vapours | | Vapour Pressure (P) | Antoine equation: log₁₀P = A − B/(C+T) | mmHg | Evaporation loss rate | > [!NOTE] > **Note on Antoine Equation:** T is temperature in °C; A, B, C are substance-specific constants. This equation is used to calculate vapour pressure for ventilation design of solvent-handling areas. ### Boundary Conditions & Limits (Memorise for KTU) - **TLV-TWA (Toluene):** 50 ppm (8 hr) - **TLV-STEL (Toluene):** 150 ppm (15 min) - **Flash Point (Toluene):** 4 °C → **Class IB Flammable** - **LEL (Toluene):** 1.1 % v/v - **UEL (Toluene):** 7.1 % v/v - **LD₅₀ (Xylene, oral rat):** 4300 mg/kg → **Category 5 (low toxicity)** ### Real-World Engineering Utility - **Ink Formulation Labs:** Use TLV data to select solvents that are below occupational exposure limits. - **Press Room Design:** LEV airflow is calculated from LEL of solvents used. - **Regulatory Compliance:** REACH requires SDS for every Substance of Very High Concern (SVHC) above **0.1 % w/w** concentration. - **Insurance & Liability:** MSDS non-availability is a primary cause of rejection of insurance claims in industrial accidents. --- ## 2.5 Personal Protective Equipment (PPE) Selection Matrix | Chemical Type | Glove Material | Respirator | Eye Protection | Body Protection | |---|---|---|---|---| | Aromatic solvents (Toluene, Xylene) | Nitrile, Viton® | Half-face APR with organic vapour cartridge | Chemical splash goggles | Chemical-resistant apron | | Aliphatic solvents (Mineral spirits) | Nitrile | Half-face APR with OV cartridge | Splash goggles | Lab coat / apron | | Acids (HNO₃, H₃PO₄) | Neoprene, Butyl rubber | Acid-gas cartridge respirator | Face shield | Acid apron, rubber boots | | Alkalis (NaOH) | Butyl rubber | Particulate + acid gas | Face shield | Full chemical suit | | Photoinitiators (UV inks) | Nitrile (double) | Half-face APR with P100 + OV | UV-blocking goggles | Long-sleeve nitrile sleeves | | Pigment dust | Nitrile | N95 / P100 particulate | Safety glasses | Disposable coverall | > [!WARNING] > **Wrong glove material dissolves:** Natural rubber (latex) is *unsuitable* for organic solvents — it swells and permeates within minutes. KTU examiners specifically test this trap. ## 2.6 Storage & Segregation Rules > [!IMPORTANT] > **The "Segregation by Compatibility" Rule** > - **Oxidisers** (driers, peroxides) must NEVER be stored with **flammables** (solvents, resins). > - **Acids** must NEVER be stored with **alkalis** or with **cyanide/sulphide** pigments. > - **Water-based inks** must be stored **above 5 °C** (freezing destroys latex emulsion). > - **UV inks** must be stored in **opaque containers** to prevent premature polymerisation. - Flammable stores: maximum **200 L** in a single fire-rated cabinet. - Bunded floor with **110 % containment** volume of the largest container. - Maximum stack height: **2 m** for pails, **1.5 m** for drums (manual handling limit). # Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Required Ventilation Airflow to Keep Solvent Vapour Below LEL ### Scenario (Standard KTU Numerical) A flexo printing press uses **20 L/hr of ethyl acetate** at 25 °C. The press room is **6 m × 5 m × 3 m**. The **LEL of ethyl acetate = 2.0 % v/v**. The **molar mass of ethyl acetate = 88 g/mol**. The **vapour pressure at 25 °C = 94 mmHg**. Calculate the **minimum ventilation airflow (m³/min)** required to keep vapour concentration below **25 % of LEL** (the standard safety factor). ### Step 1 — Convert Solvent Emission Rate to mol/s Given: 20 L/hr of ethyl acetate Density of ethyl acetate ≈ **0.902 g/mL** $$ \text{Mass flow} = 20{,}000\ \text{mL/hr} \times 0.902\ \text{g/mL} = 18{,}040\ \text{g/hr} $$ Converting to seconds: $$ \dot{m} = \frac{18{,}040\ \text{g/hr}}{3600\ \text{s/hr}} = 5.011\ \text{g/s} $$ ### Step 2 — Convert Mass Flow to Molar Flow Using molar mass M = 88 g/mol: $$ \dot{n} = \frac{\dot{m}}{M} = \frac{5.011}{88} = 0.0569\ \text{mol/s} $$ ### Step 3 — Determine Fraction of Solvent that Evaporates For simplicity (and standard KTU assumption), assume **100 % of solvent evaporates into the press room atmosphere** during printing (worst-case scenario). ### Step 4 — Calculate Maximum Permissible Vapour Concentration Design criterion: keep concentration ≤ **25 % of LEL** (safety factor of 4). $$ C_{\max} = 0.25 \times 2.0\ \%\ \text{v/v} = 0.5\ \%\ \text{v/v} = 0.005\ \text{(volume fraction)} $$ ### Step 5 — Relate Vapour Moles to Air Volume Required From the ideal gas law at constant T and P, **volume fraction = mole fraction**. Therefore, the volume of vapour generated per second corresponds to the same volume of air at the same T and P. Volume of vapour generated per second (using ideal gas molar volume 24.45 L/mol at 25 °C, 1 atm): $$ \dot{V}_{\text{vapour}} = \dot{n} \times 24.45\ \text{L/mol} = 0.0569 \times 24.45 = 1.391\ \text{L/s} $$ ### Step 6 — Calculate Required Air Dilution Volume For vapour concentration to stay at $C_{\max}$, the required air volume flow is: $$ \dot{V}_{\text{air}} = \frac{\dot{V}_{\text{vapour}}}{C_{\max}} = \frac{1.391\ \text{L/s}}{0.005} = 278.3\ \text{L/s} $$ ### Step 7 — Convert to m³/min $$ \dot{V}_{\text{air}} = 278.3 \times 10^{-3} \times 60 = 16.70\ \text{m}^3/\text{min} $$ ### Final Answer with Engineering Margin Apply a **safety factor of 1.5** for non-ideal mixing: $$ \dot{V}_{\text{design}} = 16.70 \times 1.5 = 25.05\ \text{m}^3/\text{min} $$ $$ \boxed{\dot{V}_{\text{design}} \approx 25\ \text{m}^3/\text{min}\ \text{or}\ \approx 884\ \text{CFM}} $$ ### Verification of Assumption Check room volume: $6 \times 5 \times 3 = 90\ \text{m}^3$. Air changes per hour: $$ \text{ACH} = \frac{25\ \text{m}^3/\text{min} \times 60}{90\ \text{m}^3} = 16.7\ \text{air changes/hr} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering standard:** Printing press rooms typically require **15–20 air changes/hour** for solvent-based operations. Our design value is consistent with industry practice. --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Evaporation Rate Loss from an Open Ink Trough ### Step 1 — Antoine Equation For ethyl acetate, Antoine constants (log₁₀P in mmHg, T in °C): - A = 6.966, B = 1209.6, C = 220.0 Calculate vapour pressure at 25 °C: $$ \log_{10} P = 6.966 - \frac{1209.6}{220.0 + 25} = 6.966 - \frac{1209.6}{245.0} = 6.966 - 4.937 = 2.029 $$ $$ P = 10^{2.029} = 106.9\ \text{mmHg} $$ (This is close to the literature value of ~94 mmHg, validating the calculation approach.) ### Step 2 — Convert Vapour Pressure to Mole Fraction at the Liquid Surface (Raoult's Law — ideal) $$ y_i = \frac{P_i}{P_{\text{total}}} = \frac{106.9}{760} = 0.1407\ \text{(mole fraction of vapour)} $$ ### Step 3 — Apply Mass Transfer Approximation Using a simplified mass transfer coefficient $k_c$ for a still surface (typical value $0.001\ \text{mol/(m}^2 \cdot \text{s)}$ for organic solvents at 25 °C): $$ N_i = k_c \times y_i = 0.001 \times 0.1407 = 1.407 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{mol/(m}^2 \cdot \text{s)} $$ ### Step 4 — Convert to Volumetric Loss per m² of Trough Surface $$ \dot{V}_{\text{loss}} = N_i \times 24.45 \times 3600\ \text{L/(m}^2 \cdot \text{hr)} = 1.407 \times 10^{-4} \times 24.45 \times 3600 $$ $$ \dot{V}_{\text{loss}} = 12.39\ \text{L/(m}^2 \cdot \text{hr)} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Interpretation:** A single square metre of open ethyl acetate ink trough can lose up to **12 L/hr** by evaporation at 25 °C. This drives both VOC emissions and fire/explosion risk. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Toxicity Classification by LD₅₀ (Oral, Rat) | GHS Category | Signal Word | LD₅₀ (mg/kg) | Common Printing Chemicals | |---|---|---|---| | **Category 1** | Danger | ≤ 5 | — (rare in ink) | | **Category 2** | Danger | 5 – 50 | Highly toxic pigments (historical) | | **Category 3** | Danger | 50 – 300 | Some driers, strong biocides | | **Category 4** | Warning | 300 – 2000 | Some photoinitiators | | **Category 5** | No symbol | 2000 – 5000 | Xylene (4300), Mineral spirits | | **Unclassified** | — | > 5000 | Water, glycerol, vegetable oils | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Trick Question:** "Xylene has LD₅₀ = 4300 mg/kg. What is its GHS category?" → **Category 5.** Most students incorrectly classify it as "non-toxic" because the number is large. Category 5 is *not* non-toxic — it is the lowest hazard category that still requires labelling. ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Flash Point Classification (GHS / CLP) | GHS Class | Flash Point (°C) | Boiling Point (°C) | Signal Word | Example | |---|---|---|---|---| | **1A** | < 23 | ≤ 35 | Danger | Diethyl ether, n-Hexane | | **1B** | < 23 | > 35 | Danger | **Toluene (4 °C), Acetone, MEK** | | **1C** | 23 – 60 | — | Danger | **Xylene (27 °C)** | | **2** | 60 – 93 | — | Warning | **Mineral spirits (65 °C)** | | **3** | 93 – 120 | — | Warning | **Some ink oils** | | **4** | > 120 | — | — | **Vegetable oils, glycerol** | > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistake:** Toluene's flash point is **4 °C**, well below room temperature. This means toluene vapour is *always* above the LEL near an open container at 25 °C. Many students wrongly assume "it will not burn at room temperature" — this is **incorrect**. # Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram: Integrated Chemical Safety Management System in a Printing Plant ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Raw Chemical Intake] --> B[MSDS Verification & GHS Labelling Check] B --> C{Compatible Storage Zone?} C -- Yes --> D1[Segregated Bunded Store
Flammables Cabinet] C -- No --> D2[Quarantine Area
Re-label or Return] D1 --> E[Dispensing Station with LEV] E --> F[Mixing Vessels with Enclosed Agitators] F --> G[Quality Control Lab] G --> H[Production Press Room] H --> I1[In-process Monitoring
TLV Meters, LEL Sensors] I1 --> I2[Operator PPE Verification] I2 --> J[Finished Ink Storage] H --> K1[Waste Solvent Collection] H --> K2[Spent Wipes & Rags] K1 --> L[Hazardous Waste Disposal
Licensed Contractor] K2 --> L J --> M[Despatch to Customer] L --> N[Regulatory Documentation
REACH, OSHA Returns] M --> N ``` ## 4.2 Decision Flow: Emergency Response to a Solvent Spill ```mermaid flowchart TD S1[Solvent Spill Detected] --> S2{Volume > 5 L?} S2 -- No --> S3[Operator uses Spill Kit
Absorbent Pads + Vermiculite] S2 -- Yes --> S4[Evacuate Non-Essential Staff] S4 --> S5[Activate Fire Alarm if Vapour Cloud] S3 --> S6[Ventilation ON Full] S5 --> S6 S6 --> S7{Vapour Conc > 25% LEL?} S7 -- Yes --> S8[Stop Ignition Sources
Use Explosion-Proof Pump] S7 -- No --> S9[Transfer to Drum with Pump] S8 --> S9 S9 --> S10[Decontaminate Floor with Detergent] S10 --> S11[Bag Waste in UN-Approved Container] S11 --> S12[Complete Incident Report
Notify Safety Officer] ``` ## 4.3 Hierarchy of Controls — Visual Block ```mermaid flowchart TD H1[Level 1: Elimination
Remove the hazard entirely] --> H2[Level 2: Substitution
Use a less hazardous chemical] H2 --> H3[Level 3: Engineering Controls
LEV, Enclosure, Ventilation] H3 --> H4[Level 4: Administrative Controls
SOPs, Training, Signage] H4 --> H5[Level 5: PPE
Gloves, Respirator, Goggles] H5 --> H6[STOP - Work Cannot Proceed if
all 5 levels are inadequate] ``` ## 4.4 Process Block: PPE Selection Logic ```mermaid flowchart TD P1[Identify Chemical from SDS Section 2] --> P2{Physical State?} P2 -- Gas or Vapour --> P3[Half or Full Face APR
with Appropriate Cartridge] P2 -- Liquid Mist --> P4[Half Face with P100
Plus OV Cartridge if Organic] P2 -- Dust or Powder --> P5[N95 or P100 Particulate Filter] P2 -- Liquid Splash --> P6[Chemical Splash Goggles
Plus Face Shield] P3 --> P7{Chemical Compatibility Chart} P4 --> P7 P5 --> P7 P6 --> P7 P7 --> P8[Select Glove Material
Nitrile, Neoprene, Butyl or Viton] P8 --> P9[Select Body Protection
Apron, Lab Coat or Full Suit] P9 --> P10[Document PPE Issue
Train Worker on Use and Limitations] ``` # KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. Define the term "Material Safety Data Sheet (MSDS/SDS)" and list any six of its 16 standardized sections under GHS. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper 2024]` **Model Answer:** A **Material Safety Data Sheet (MSDS)**, also called Safety Data Sheet (SDS) under GHS, is a **standardized 16-section document** supplied by the manufacturer of a chemical product that provides comprehensive information on the hazards, composition, safe handling, emergency response, and regulatory status of the substance. It is mandatory under REACH and OSHA regulations. Six of the 16 sections (any six accepted): 1. **Section 1 — Identification** of the substance and supplier 2. **Section 2 — Hazard Identification** (GHS pictograms, signal words) 3. **Section 4 — First-Aid Measures** 4. **Section 5 — Fire-Fighting Measures** (suitable extinguishing media, flash point) 5. **Section 8 — Exposure Controls / PPE** (TLV, PEL, respirator specifications) 6. **Section 9 — Physical and Chemical Properties** (boiling point, vapour pressure) 7. **Section 13 — Disposal Considerations** 8. **Section 14 — Transport Information** (UN number, packing group) --- ### Q2. Differentiate between TLV-TWA and TLV-STEL. Why are both important in a printing ink plant? `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Model Answer:** | Parameter | TLV-TWA | TLV-STEL | |---|---|---| | **Full form** | Threshold Limit Value — Time-Weighted Average | Threshold Limit Value — Short-Term Exposure Limit | | **Averaging time** | 8 hours per day, 40 hr/week | 15 minutes, max 4 times/day with ≥60 min between | | **Purpose** | Protects against **chronic** health effects from long-term exposure | Protects against **acute** effects from short peaks | | **Example (Toluene)** | 50 ppm | 150 ppm | **Importance in a printing ink plant:** Operators are exposed to solvent vapours for 8-hour shifts (chronic TWA exposure to toluene, xylene, etc.) and also to short peaks during ink mixing, press startup, and waste handling (acute STEL exposure to high solvent bursts during spill or transfer). Both limits must be monitored to prevent occupational diseases such as solvent-induced neuropathy (TWA) and acute CNS depression (STEL). --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!NOTE] > **Module 4 Internal Choice Pattern (KTU 2024 Scheme):** Two questions are typically offered from Module 4, and the student answers **one** of them. Each 14-mark question has two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1 #### (a) [7 Marks] — Describe the GHS classification of flammable liquids. Explain the GHS categories with examples of printing ink solvents. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Model Solution:** The **Globally Harmonized System (GHS)** classifies flammable liquids into **4 categories** (1, 2, 3, 4) based on **flash point** and **boiling point**: **Category 1 — Extremely Flammable** - Flash point < 23 °C **AND** initial boiling point ≤ 35 °C - Signal word: **DANGER**; Hazard statement: H224 - *Printing ink example:* **Diethyl ether** (FP = −45 °C, BP = 35 °C) — used rarely in specialised formulations - *Example:* **n-Hexane** (FP = −22 °C, BP = 69 °C) — wait, BP > 35, so this is **Category 2**, not 1. Correction: pure n-hexane is Cat 2. **Category 2 — Highly Flammable** - Flash point < 23 °C **AND** initial boiling point > 35 °C - Signal word: **DANGER**; Hazard statement: H225 - *Printing ink examples:* - **Toluene** (FP = 4 °C, BP = 111 °C) — common gravure ink solvent - **Acetone** (FP = −20 °C, BP = 56 °C) — cleaning solvent - **Methyl Ethyl Ketone (MEK)** (FP = −9 °C, BP = 80 °C) — adhesive and ink solvent - **Ethyl Acetate** (FP = −4 °C, BP = 77 °C) — flexo ink diluent **Category 3 — Flammable** - Flash point ≥ 23 °C and ≤ 60 °C - Signal word: **DANGER**; Hazard statement: H226 - *Printing ink examples:* - **Xylene** (FP = 27 °C) — heatset web offset ink solvent - **n-Butyl Acetate** (FP = 27 °C) — flexographic ink retarder solvent **Category 4 — Combustible** - Flash point > 60 °C and ≤ 93 °C - Signal word: **WARNING**; Hazard statement: H227 - *Printing ink examples:* - **Mineral spirits / White spirit** (FP = 65 °C) — long-oil alkyd ink diluent - **Diesel** (FP = 70 °C) — press wash (older formulations) > For Categories 1–3, the **GHS02 Flame pictogram** is mandatory. For Category 4, no pictogram is required but the signal word "Warning" applies. **Key Engineering Insight:** In an offset press room, the air-vapour mixture of toluene (Category 2) is *always* above its LEL of 1.1 % at room temperature. Therefore, **enclosed press consoles, LEV, and explosion-proof electrical fittings (ATEX-rated in EU; UL Class I Div 1 in USA)** are mandatory. **Mineral spirits (Category 4)** can be safely used in well-ventilated rooms with only basic LEV. --- #### (b) [7 Marks] — A sheet-fed offset press uses a fountain solution containing 5 % v/v isopropyl alcohol (IPA). The press room is 10 m × 8 m × 4 m. Calculate the minimum ventilation airflow (m³/min) required to keep IPA vapour concentration below 25 % of its LEL. Given: density of IPA = 0.786 g/mL, molar mass = 60.1 g/mol, LEL = 2.0 % v/v, IPA consumption = 2 L/hr. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]` **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Convert IPA liquid consumption to mass flow:** $$ \dot{m} = 2000\ \text{mL/hr} \times 0.786\ \text{g/mL} = 1572\ \text{g/hr} $$ **Step 2 — Convert to molar flow:** $$ \dot{n} = \frac{1572}{60.1} = 26.16\ \text{mol/hr} = \frac{26.16}{3600} = 7.267 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{mol/s} $$ **Step 3 — Maximum permissible vapour concentration:** $$ C_{\max} = 0.25 \times 2.0\ \% = 0.5\ \%\ \text{v/v} = 0.005 $$ **Step 4 — Volume of vapour generated per second (at 25 °C, molar volume 24.45 L/mol):** $$ \dot{V}_{\text{vapour}} = 7.267 \times 10^{-3} \times 24.45 = 0.1776\ \text{L/s} $$ **Step 5 — Required air volume flow:** $$ \dot{V}_{\text{air}} = \frac{0.1776}{0.005} = 35.53\ \text{L/s} = 35.53 \times 60 \times 10^{-3} = 2.13\ \text{m}^3/\text{min} $$ **Step 6 — Apply safety factor of 1.5 (for non-ideal mixing, room geometry):** $$ \dot{V}_{\text{design}} = 2.13 \times 1.5 = 3.20\ \text{m}^3/\text{min} $$ **Step 7 — Verify against room volume:** $$ \text{Room volume} = 10 \times 8 \times 4 = 320\ \text{m}^3 $$ $$ \text{ACH} = \frac{3.20 \times 60}{320} = 0.6\ \text{air changes/hr} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering interpretation:** The IPA emission is small compared to the room volume, so very modest ventilation (0.6 ACH) is sufficient. However, **industry best practice for offset press rooms is 6–10 ACH** to control paper dust, ink mist, and general comfort. Therefore, the calculated value should be considered a **minimum**, with the actual HVAC system providing a higher flow. $$ \boxed{\text{Minimum design airflow} \approx 3.2\ \text{m}^3/\text{min}} $$ --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2 #### (a) [7 Marks] — Explain the Hierarchy of Hazard Control (HOC) with suitable printing-industry examples. Why is PPE considered the *last* line of defence? `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` **Model Solution:** The **Hierarchy of Hazard Control (HOC)** is a 5-level system used in occupational health and safety to identify the most effective ways to control exposure to workplace hazards. The levels are listed in **decreasing order of effectiveness and reliability**: **Level 1 — Elimination (Most Effective)** - **Definition:** Physically removing the hazard from the workplace. - **Printing example:** Replacing **solvent-based gravure inks** with **water-based flexo inks** to eliminate toluene/xylene exposure entirely. - **Why most effective:** The hazard is gone; no human can be exposed. **Level 2 — Substitution** - **Definition:** Replacing a hazardous substance or process with a less hazardous one. - **Printing examples:** - Replacing **toluene** (Cat 2 flammable, neurotoxic) with **isoparaffin** (higher flash point, lower toxicity). - Replacing **lead chromate pigments** with **organic azo pigments** (Pb-free). - Replacing **cobalt driers** with **manganese or iron complexes** (lower sensitisation potential). **Level 3 — Engineering Controls** - **Definition:** Physical modifications to the workplace or process that reduce exposure. - **Printing examples:** - **Local Exhaust Ventilation (LEV)** at the ink-mixing station to capture solvent vapours at source. - **Enclosed ink-circulation systems** on the press to minimise evaporation. - **Enclosed bead mills and three-roll mills** to control dust and mist. - **Explosion-proof electrical fittings (ATEX/UL rated)** in flammable areas. **Level 4 — Administrative Controls** - **Definition:** Changes to work procedures, training, and scheduling to reduce exposure duration. - **Printing examples:** - **Job rotation** so no operator handles solvent-based inks for a full 8-hour shift. - **Mandatory SDS access** at every work station. - **Hazardous-area signposting** ("No Naked Flames", "Respirator Required"). - **Operator training** on spill response and PPE use. **Level 5 — Personal Protective Equipment (PPE) — Last Line of Defence** - **Definition:** Equipment worn by the worker to minimise exposure. - **Printing examples:** - Nitrile gloves (solvent handling) - Half-face APR with organic-vapour cartridge (mixing) - Chemical splash goggles (fountain solution handling) - P100 particulate respirator (dry pigment weighing) **Why PPE is the LAST line of defence:** 1. **It relies on human behaviour** — the worker must wear it correctly every time. 2. **It can fail** — cartridges saturate, gloves tear, respirators leak. 3. **It does not eliminate the hazard** — it only creates a barrier between the worker and the hazard. 4. **It can give a false sense of security** — workers may take risks they otherwise would not. 5. **Engineering controls, in contrast, work 24/7** regardless of human factors. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key phrase to use in KTU answers:** "PPE is a *mitigation*, not a *control*. True hazard control is achieved only through elimination, substitution, or engineering means." --- #### (b) [7 Marks] — Describe the first-aid measures to be taken in a printing ink plant in the event of (i) skin contact with organic solvent, (ii) solvent splash in the eyes, and (iii) inhalation of high concentrations of solvent vapour. `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` **Model Solution:** **General Principle:** First-aid measures are listed in **Section 4 of the SDS** of every chemical. The plant must have a **trained first-aider, an eyewash station within 10 seconds walking distance, and a safety shower** as per **ANSI Z358.1 / IS 4196** standards. **(i) Skin Contact with Organic Solvent (e.g., Toluene, Xylene):** 1. **Immediately remove contaminated clothing**, shoes, and jewellery. 2. **Flush the affected skin with copious amounts of lukewarm water** for at least **15 minutes** at an emergency safety shower. 3. **Gently wash with mild soap** (no harsh scrubbing — damaged skin absorbs solvent faster). 4. **Do NOT use solvents, creams, or ointments** — these may increase absorption. 5. **Cover with a clean, dry dressing.** 6. **Seek medical attention** if redness, blistering, or dermatitis persists. Report to the safety officer for **MSDS reference (Section 4)**. 7. **Launder contaminated clothing** separately before reuse; dispose of heavily contaminated items as hazardous waste. **(ii) Solvent Splash in the Eyes:** 1. **Immediately go to the nearest eyewash station** (must be within 10 m / 10 seconds walking time). 2. **Tilt the head so the affected eye is lower** to prevent contamination of the unaffected eye. 3. **Flush continuously with clean lukewarm water for at least 15 minutes**, holding eyelids open. 4. **Remove contact lenses** if easily possible during flushing. 5. **Do NOT rub the eyes** or apply any eye drops/ointments. 6. **Seek immediate medical attention from an ophthalmologist**, even if symptoms subside — corneal damage may be delayed. 7. **Document the incident** and obtain the SDS for the medical team (Section 4). **(iii) Inhalation of High Concentrations of Solvent Vapour:** 1. **Do not enter the area without proper respiratory protection** — wear a self-contained breathing apparatus (SCBA) for rescue. 2. **Remove the casualty to fresh air** in a direction **cross-wind or up-wind** from the source. 3. **Loosen tight clothing** around neck and waist. 4. **If breathing has stopped**, administer **artificial respiration** (mouth-to-mouth or via bag-valve-mask) by a trained rescuer — but **avoid direct mouth contact** if the contaminant is highly toxic; use a **pocket mask with one-way valve**. 5. **If breathing is difficult**, administer **oxygen** if available and trained. 6. **Place the casualty in the recovery position** (lateral) if unconscious but breathing. 7. **Do NOT induce vomiting** if solvent was swallowed. 8. **Call emergency medical services immediately** and **transport to hospital** — effects such as pulmonary oedema may appear after a delay. 9. **Notify the safety officer, evacuate the area, and investigate** the cause of exposure (e.g., spill, equipment failure, ventilation breakdown). 10. **Record the incident** in the accident register as per OSHA / Factories Act requirements. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** > - **Do NOT** state "apply cream/butter on skin burn" — this is outdated first-aid practice and explicitly warned against in modern SDS. > - **Do NOT** state "induce vomiting" after solvent ingestion — aspiration pneumonia risk is very high. > - **Always** mention the **15-minute minimum flushing time** for both skin and eye exposure; many students write "wash with water" without specifying duration → loses 1 mark. > - **Always** specify that the rescuer must **not enter the contaminated area unprotected** → 1 mark. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Revision Points for KTU 2024 Examination** ### A. Core Definitions - ☐ MSDS / SDS = 16-section document under GHS & REACH; mandatory for every chemical - ☐ **TLV-TWA** = 8-hr average exposure limit (chronic effect) - ☐ **TLV-STEL** = 15-min short-term limit (acute effect) - ☐ **LEL** = Lower Explosive Limit; safety design uses **25 % of LEL** - ☐ **LD₅₀** = Lethal Dose 50 %; **lower value = more toxic** - ☐ **Flash Point** = lowest temp at which vapour ignites; below 23 °C → highly flammable ### B. GHS Pictograms (Mnemonic) - ☐ GHS01 Exploding bomb, GHS02 Flame, GHS03 Oxidiser - ☐ GHS04 Gas cylinder, GHS05 Corrosion, GHS06 Skull & Crossbones - ☐ GHS07 Exclamation, GHS08 Health Hazard, GHS09 Environment ### C. Hierarchy of Controls (Order is Critical) - ☐ **Elimination → Substitution → Engineering → Administrative → PPE** - ☐ PPE is the **last** line, not the first ### D. Flammable Liquid Categories (Flash Point) - ☐ **Cat 1:** < 23 °C, BP ≤ 35 °C → diethyl ether - ☐ **Cat 2:** < 23 °C, BP > 35 °C → **toluene, acetone, MEK, ethyl acetate** - ☐ **Cat 3:** 23–60 °C → **xylene, n-butyl acetate** - ☐ **Cat 4:** 60–93 °C → **mineral spirits** ### E. Engineering Numbers to Memorise - ☐ **Toluene TLV-TWA = 50 ppm**; **STEL = 150 ppm** - ☐ **Toluene LEL = 1.1 %, UEL = 7.1 %** - ☐ **Toluene Flash Point = 4 °C** (room-temp volatile) - ☐ **Press room design = 15–20 air changes/hr** - ☐ **Glove:** natural rubber (latex) is **wrong** for organic solvents; use **Nitrile / Viton** - ☐ **Eyewash must be within 10 m / 10 seconds** - ☐ **Flush time = minimum 15 minutes** (skin and eye) ### F. Common KTU Pitfalls - ☐ **Don't say "non-toxic" for GHS Cat 5** — it is the lowest category but still requires labelling - ☐ **Don't use latex gloves with organic solvents** - ☐ **Don't store oxidisers with flammables** - ☐ **Don't use water extinguishers on solvent fires** (use CO₂, dry powder, or foam) - ☐ **Don't induce vomiting** after solvent ingestion - ☐ **PPE is the last line**, not the first — common error in 7-mark questions ### G. Calculation Template (Always Use This Order) - ☐ Convert liquid → mass flow (× density) - ☐ Convert mass → molar flow (÷ M) - ☐ Apply 25 % LEL design criterion - ☐ Use molar volume 24.45 L/mol at 25 °C - ☐ Apply safety factor 1.5 - ☐ Verify air changes per hour (15–20 ACH target)

topics.title: Mechanical and Operational Aspects
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPOT866 - TECHNOLOGY OF PRINTING INKS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Mechanical and Operational Aspects # Module 4 — Mechanical and Operational Aspects of Printing Inks ## 1.1 Core Technical Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Mechanical and Operational Aspects of Printing Inks** refer to the rheological, tribological, and physical-flow properties that govern how a printing ink behaves *while it is being transferred, distributed, and split* on a printing press. These properties dictate print quality, dot fidelity, substrate compatibility, and press runnability. The **operational behavior** of a printing ink is dominated by a set of interrelated rheological parameters — **viscosity, tack, yield value, thixotropy, and flow** — which are continuously altered by the mechanical forces (shear, compression, splitting) experienced inside the inking unit, on the roller train, at the ink film-split nip, and during drying. In KTU 2024 terminology, this module bridges the *chemistry* of ink formulation (pigment + vehicle + additives) with the *engineering* of ink transfer on real high-speed presses (sheet-fed offset, web offset, flexo, gravure, and screen). > [!NOTE] > **Rheology** is the science of the deformation and flow of matter. For printing inks, rheology is the single most important determinant of **printability**. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain-English Intuition > [!TIP] > **The Honey–Ketchup–Yogurt Analogy** > > Imagine you tilt three jars on a table: > 1. **Water (low viscosity)** — runs off instantly, splashes, cannot be controlled on a press. > 2. **Honey (high viscosity, Newtonian)** — flows slowly, can be controlled but is too thick to split cleanly. > 3. **Tomato ketchup (thixotropic, yield-stress fluid)** — sits still in the bottle (resists small forces), but the moment you **shake** it (apply shear), it flows freely, and then re-thickens when you stop. > > A printing ink behaves like **ketchup**: it must be **stiff enough to stay on the roller** (high yield value at rest) and **fluid enough to transfer** onto paper under the nip pressure (low viscosity under shear). The press engineer is, in effect, continuously shaking the ink millions of times per minute. This is why the same ink behaves very differently on a **slow proofing press** versus a **15,000 sph (sheets per hour) Heidelberg Speedmaster** — the shear rate changes the viscosity. ## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics The following values are typical references used by ink-makers (all measurements assume **25 °C** unless stated otherwise): - **Standard print-viscosity (offset ink):** **8 – 18 Pa·s** (cone-plate, 2.5 s⁻¹) - **Standard print-tack (offset ink):** **8 – 14 tack units** (Inkometer, 400 rpm, 25 °C, 1 min) - **Yield value (offset ink):** **500 – 3000 dyn/cm²** - **Newtonian reference fluid:** **water at 20 °C → 1.002 mPa·s** - **Shear rate on a press nip:** **10⁴ – 10⁶ s⁻¹** - **Press speeds:** - Sheet-fed offset: **10,000 – 18,000 sph** - Web offset: **40,000 – 80,000 iph** - Flexo: **200 – 600 m/min** - Gravure: **200 – 450 m/min** - **Standard nip pressure (offset):** **0.2 – 0.4 MPa** ## 1.4 Operational Context Map The ink's mechanical properties are exercised at **five distinct physical zones** on a press: | Zone | Mechanical Action | Critical Ink Property | |------|------------------|----------------------| | **Ink fountain** | Static hold + feed | Yield value | | **Fountain roller / ductor** | Dosing & metering | Plasticity | | **Distributor + oscillator rollers** | Shear + film split | Thixotropy, tack | | **Plate cylinder nip** | Compressive ink transfer | Viscosity at high shear, tack | | **Substrate** | Setting + drying | Body, drying time, gloss | > [!NOTE] > Failure of *any one* of these five zones causes a print defect (scumming, picking, misting, dot gain, set-off, hickeys). The ink formulator must therefore tune the rheology **for the press, not in isolation.** # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Five Pillars of Ink Mechanics ### 2.1.1 Viscosity (η) - **Definition:** Internal friction resisting flow under shear. - **Units:** Pa·s (SI) or Poise (P); **1 P = 0.1 Pa·s**. - **Why it matters:** Too high → ink cannot split, picks the paper. Too low → ink mists, dots spread, halo effect. - **How it's measured:** Cone-plate viscometer at controlled shear rate; falling-rod viscometer (Efflux cups) for shop-floor checks. **Newton's Law of Viscosity:** $$\tau = \eta \cdot \dot{\gamma}$$ - $\tau$ = shear stress (Pa) - $\eta$ = dynamic viscosity (Pa·s) - $\dot{\gamma}$ = shear rate (s⁻¹) A fluid obeying this linear relation is **Newtonian** (water, mineral oil at moderate shear). Printing inks are **almost never Newtonian.** ### 2.1.2 Yield Value (τ₀) — Plasticity - **Definition:** The minimum stress that must be applied *before the ink begins to flow*. Below τ₀ the ink behaves as an elastic solid. - **Why it matters:** Prevents ink from dripping off the roller, dripping from ducts, and from soaking into non-image areas of the plate. - **Typical offset ink:** **500 – 3000 dyn/cm²**. - **Measurement:** Spreadometer + Carrimed, or extrapolated from Casson plot. ### 2.1.3 Thixotropy - **Definition:** A **time-dependent reversible** decrease in viscosity under constant shear. When shear stops, the structure rebuilds and viscosity recovers. - **Why it matters:** Gives ink "memory" — stiff in the duct, fluid under the roller, stiff again at the moment of impression so it doesn't run on the paper. - **Quantitative index:** **Thixotropic Index (TI) = viscosity at 6 rpm ÷ viscosity at 60 rpm** (Brookfield). Offset inks usually **TI = 1.2 – 2.5**. ### 2.1.4 Tack - **Definition:** The *resistance* of an ink film to being split. Conceptually, tack = viscosity × cohesion + adhesion to roller. - **Why it matters:** Determines the **ink-water balance** in offset and the **ink release** in flexo. Excess tack → picking; deficient tack → poor trap and mottle. - **Measurement:** Inkometer (Gravure Inkometer, 400 rpm, 25 °C) — reported in **tack units (TU)**. One TU ≈ 0.1 g·cm of torque. ### 2.1.5 Flow and Levelling - **Definition:** After transfer, ink must flow to fill the *valleys* in the paper / coating surface and form a smooth film. - **Why it matters:** Controls gloss, mottle, and dot-edge sharpness. Slow levelling → orange peel. Over-fast levelling → dot slur, dot gain. ## 2.2 Flow Classification of Printing Inks > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight — Non-Newtonian Flow Models** > Printing inks are classified by their flow curve (shear stress vs. shear rate) into one of four rheological models. | Model | Equation | Behavior | Example | |-------|----------|----------|---------| | **Newtonian** | $\tau = \eta \dot{\gamma}$ | Linear | Mineral oil, water | | **Bingham Plastic** | $\tau = \tau_0 + \eta_p \dot{\gamma}$ | Flows only above τ₀; then linear | News ink, some gravure | | **Pseudoplastic (shear-thinning)** | $\tau = K \dot{\gamma}^{n}, \quad n<1$ | Viscosity drops with shear | Most offset & flexo inks | | **Dilatant (shear-thickening)** | $\tau = K \dot{\gamma}^{n}, \quad n>1$ | Viscosity rises with shear | Some pigment slurries, high-pigment screen inks | The **Power-Law (Ostwald–de Waele) model** is the most widely used for printing inks: $$\eta = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{\,n-1}$$ - $K$ = consistency index (Pa·sⁿ) - $n$ = power-law index (dimensionless) - $n = 1$ → Newtonian - $n < 1$ → shear-thinning (pseudoplastic) - $n > 1$ → shear-thickening (dilatant) ## 2.3 Mechanical Forces Acting on the Ink On a press, the ink experiences: 1. **Shear stress** (in the roller nip, fountain roller, anilox) 2. **Tensile stress** (at the ink film split, between plate & substrate) 3. **Compressive stress** (in the impression nip) 4. **Dynamic wetting** (contact with plate, substrate, fountain solution) 5. **Thermal load** (frictional heating, IR dryer) ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Equation | Symbols | Units | When to use | |---|----------|---------|-------|-------------| | 1 | $\tau = \eta \dot{\gamma}$ | $\tau$=shear stress, $\eta$=viscosity, $\dot{\gamma}$=shear rate | Pa, Pa·s, s⁻¹ | Newtonian flow check | | 2 | $\tau = \tau_0 + \eta_p \dot{\gamma}$ | $\tau_0$=yield stress, $\eta_p$=plastic viscosity | Pa, Pa·s, s⁻¹ | Bingham plastics (news ink) | | 3 | $\eta = K \dot{\gamma}^{n-1}$ | $K$=consistency, $n$=power index | Pa·sⁿ, dim.less | Offset / flexo inks | | 4 | $\sqrt{\tau} = \sqrt{\tau_0} + \sqrt{\eta_c \cdot \dot{\gamma}}$ | $\eta_c$= Casson viscosity | Pa, Pa·s | Casson model for offset | | 5 | $\text{TI} = \dfrac{\eta_{6\,\text{rpm}}}{\eta_{60\,\text{rpm}}}$ | — | dim.less | Thixotropy index | | 6 | $F = \dfrac{2 \pi \mu L R^3 \omega}{h}$ | Couette flow in inkometer | N | Tack measurement | | 7 | $\dfrac{b}{x} = 1 - e^{-k \phi}$ | Walker ink-transfer equation (split ratio, simplified) | dim.less | Predict ink film split | | 8 | $y = \left[1 - e^{-kx}\right]\left[x + \dfrac{(1-x)}{R_b}\right] \cdot \text{something}$ | Fetsko / Walker variants | — | Press ink-trapping estimates | | 9 | $\text{Dot gain \%} = \dfrac{D_{50}^{\text{print}} - D_{50}^{\text{film}}}{D_{50}^{\text{film}}} \times 100$ | $D_{50}$ = 50% dot area | % | Mechanical dot gain | | 10 | $\Delta P_{\text{cap}} = \dfrac{2 \gamma \cos\theta}{r}$ | $\gamma$=surface tension, $\theta$=contact angle, $r$=capillary radius | Pa | Capillary absorption into paper | > [!WARNING] > In the KTU answer script, **always state the units of η and τ₀**. A numerical answer without units is treated as incomplete and loses 0.5 – 1 mark per question. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Press tuning:** Press operators adjust **ink-tack**, **fountain-solution pH (4.5 – 5.5)**, and **roller pressure** using the rheology numbers above. - **Ink formulation:** The formulator picks resin (long-oil alkyd vs. phenolic-modified), bodying agent (gel varnish), and solvent to *hit* the rheology target. - **Trapping & over-printing:** Tack-gradient of inks (down → up the press) is used for multi-color trapping. - **Quality control:** SPC charts in the ink factory plot **tack (X) and viscosity (Y)** for every batch; out-of-control batches are rejected. - **Substrate matching:** Coated paper (low absorbency) needs *higher tack* and *faster setting* ink than newsprint. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Power-Law Rheology from First Principles We begin with the **generalized Newtonian fluid** assumption: the fluid has a *single-valued* viscosity function $\eta(\dot{\gamma})$ but no elastic memory. **Step 1.** Write the shear stress as a function of shear rate through an empirical constitutive law: $$\tau = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{\,n}$$ **Step 2.** Recognize that the apparent viscosity is defined as: $$\eta_{\text{app}} = \dfrac{\tau}{\dot{\gamma}}$$ **Step 3.** Divide the first equation by $\dot{\gamma}$: $$\eta_{\text{app}} = \dfrac{K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{\,n}}{\dot{\gamma}} = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{\,n-1}$$ **Step 4.** Verify the limiting cases (a) *Newtonian limit*: setting $n = 1$ gives $\eta_{\text{app}} = K$ → a constant → reduces to Newton's law $\tau = K \dot{\gamma}$, so $K \equiv \eta$. (b) *Shear-thinning*: if $0 < n < 1$, then $n-1 < 0$ and $\eta_{\text{app}}$ *decreases* as $\dot{\gamma}$ *increases* — the desired behavior of a printing ink on a press. (c) *Shear-thickening*: $n > 1$ → $\eta_{\text{app}}$ increases with $\dot{\gamma}$ (avoid in printing). **Step 5.** Convert to a log-log form for graphical determination of $K$ and $n$: $$\log \tau = \log K + n \log \dot{\gamma}$$ This is a straight line of slope $n$ and intercept $\log K$ on a rheometer plot. ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Casson Model (Standard for Offset Inks) The **Casson equation** is the *industry-standard* model for offset inks because it elegantly separates the **yield value** from the **high-shear viscosity**. **Step 1.** Start with the hypothesis that the internal structure of the ink consists of pigment particles that flocculate into a *network* held together by London-van der Waals forces. This network has a strength characterized by $\tau_0$. **Step 2.** Casson proposed the following empirical relation: $$\sqrt{\tau} = \sqrt{\tau_0} + \sqrt{\eta_c \cdot \dot{\gamma}}$$ where $\eta_c$ is the **Casson viscosity** (high-shear limit). **Step 3.** Square both sides to obtain $\tau$ explicitly: $$\tau = \left(\sqrt{\tau_0} + \sqrt{\eta_c \dot{\gamma}}\right)^2 = \tau_0 + 2\sqrt{\tau_0 \eta_c \dot{\gamma}} + \eta_c \dot{\gamma}$$ **Step 4.** Identify the **plastic viscosity** $\eta_p$ as the slope of $\tau$ vs $\dot{\gamma}$ at very high shear (where the middle term becomes negligible): $$\eta_p = \eta_c$$ **Step 5.** The **Casson plot** ($\sqrt{\tau}$ vs $\sqrt{\dot{\gamma}}$) is a straight line: - y-intercept = $\sqrt{\tau_0}$ → extract yield value - slope = $\sqrt{\eta_c}$ → extract high-shear viscosity > [!TIP] > In the KTU exam, if a numerical problem gives a Casson plot with intercept $a$ and slope $b$, write: > $$\tau_0 = a^2 \quad \text{and} \quad \eta_c = b^2$$ > These are the two parameters the examiner wants to see. ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Walker Ink-Transfer Equation (Film Splitting) The **Walker equation** is the foundation of the modern understanding of how ink is *split* between two surfaces (e.g., plate → blanket, blanket → substrate, or anilox → plate in flexo). **Step 1.** Define the *split ratio* $r$: $$r = \dfrac{x}{b}$$ where $x$ = amount of ink transferred to the *receiving* surface and $b$ = ink film thickness *just before* the split. **Step 2.** Walker assumed that *ink filaments* form between the two surfaces during separation. Each filament splits randomly, with probability $\phi$ that it lands on the receiving surface. The number of intact filaments decays exponentially with the amount of ink that has already split off: $$\dfrac{d(x/b)}{d\phi} = (1 - x/b)$$ **Step 3.** Separate variables and integrate from $\phi = 0$ to the operational $\phi = k b$: $$\int_0^{x/b} \dfrac{d(x/b)}{1 - x/b} = \int_0^{kb} d\phi$$ **Step 4.** Evaluate: $$-\ln(1 - x/b) = k b \cdot \phi$$ **Step 5.** Solve for $x/b$: $$1 - \dfrac{x}{b} = e^{-kb\phi} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \dfrac{x}{b} = 1 - e^{-kb\phi}$$ **Step 6.** Final Walker equation: $$\boxed{\,y = 1 - e^{-kb\phi}\,}$$ - $y$ = fraction of ink transferred to substrate - $b$ = ink film thickness supplied to the split - $k$ = a constant depending on ink rheology, press speed, surface tension - $\phi$ = coverage (volume) of ink on the surface **Physical insight:** The equation is *monotonically increasing but bounded by 1* — no matter how much ink you pile on, you cannot transfer *more* than 100 %. And the curve is concave-down, meaning each additional micron of ink gives a *smaller* fractional transfer. This is why **film thickness is critical** to dot gain in offset. ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Fetsko–Walker Modified Equation (Press Realism) Fetsko (1957) added the *ink-trapping* efficiency $R_b$ (the ratio of ink going to a wet film vs. dry film) and an empirical catch-up factor: $$y = \left(1 - e^{-k_1 b \phi}\right)\left[\,x + \dfrac{(1-x) f}{R_b}\,\right]$$ For the KTU exam, the *most important* form to remember is the *single-roller* one: $$y = \left(1 - e^{-k x}\right)\left[1 + \dfrac{x}{R_b}\right]$$ - $x$ = ink film input - $k$ = splitting constant - $R_b$ = ink-trap ratio - $y$ = ink on substrate ## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 14-Mark Style) > **Problem.** A sheet-fed offset ink has the following Casson parameters measured on a cone-plate rheometer: > - $\tau_0 = 250 \,\text{Pa}$ > - $\eta_c = 0.40 \,\text{Pa·s}$ > > Compute the **shear stress** at shear rates of **(i)** 10 s⁻¹ and **(ii)** 10 000 s⁻¹. Comment on the press relevance of each. **Solution (i).** Substituting into the Casson equation: $$\sqrt{\tau} = \sqrt{250} + \sqrt{0.40 \times 10}$$ $$\sqrt{\tau} = 15.811 + 2.000 = 17.811$$ $$\tau = (17.811)^2 = 317.2 \,\text{Pa}$$ **Solution (ii).** Substituting the high-shear rate: $$\sqrt{\tau} = \sqrt{250} + \sqrt{0.40 \times 10\,000}$$ $$\sqrt{\tau} = 15.811 + 63.246 = 79.057$$ $$\tau = (79.057)^2 = 6\,250 \,\text{Pa}$$ **Comment:** - At **10 s⁻¹** (duct, idle roller): the yield term (15.811) dominates → ink is *mostly elastic* and resists flow. **Press relevance: prevents dripping.** - At **10 000 s⁻¹** (roller nip, plate-substrate split): the viscous term (63.246) dominates → ink is *fluid enough* to split and transfer. **Press relevance: enables transfer without picking.** The factor-of-20 increase in stress for a 1000× increase in shear rate confirms the **shear-thinning** nature of the ink. ## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Operational Link | Operational Variable | Tuned by | Side Effect If Mis-Tuned | |---|---|---| | Fountain duct setting | Yield value $\tau_0$ | Dripping (low) / starved feed (high) | | Roller squeeze | Plastic viscosity $\eta_p$ | Slur & dot gain (low) / picking (high) | | Press speed | Power-law index $n$ | Misting (low n) / poor trap (high n) | | Ink tack | Tack units (TU) | Set-off (low) / picking (high) | | Fountain solution pH | Emulsification behavior | Scumming (high pH) / toning loss (low pH) | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Ink Flow Path Through a Sheet-Fed Offset Press The following **block-level functional architecture flow** maps the interactions of the mechanical and operational aspects of the ink across a typical **four-color sheet-fed offset press (e.g., KBA Rapida, Heidelberg Speedmaster CD 102)**. ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph A["FOUNTAIN SYSTEM"] A1[Ink duct / fountain] A2[Fountain roller] A3[Ductor alternate] end subgraph B["DISTRIBUTION SYSTEM"] B1[Distributor roller 1] B2[Distributor roller 2] B3[Oscillator roller] B4[Form roller] end subgraph C["TRANSFER SYSTEM"] C1[Plate cylinder] C2[Blanket cylinder] C3[Impression cylinder] end subgraph D["SUBSTRATE & OUTPUT"] D1[Sheet feeder] D2[Paper substrate] D3[Delivery stack] end subgraph E["SUPPORT SYSTEMS"] E1[Fountain solution unit] E2[Dampening roller train] E3[Drying / IR / UV unit] end A1 --> A2 --> A3 --> B1 --> B2 --> B3 --> B4 --> C1 C1 -- image-area ink --> C2 C2 -- offset transfer --> C3 C3 -- final impression --> D2 D1 --> C3 C3 --> D3 E1 --> E2 --> C1 E3 --> D3 classDef zone fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#1565c0,stroke-width:1px,color:#0d47a1 classDef sub fill:#fff8e1,stroke:#f57c00,stroke-width:1px,color:#e65100 class A,B,C,D,E zone class A1,A2,A3,B1,B2,B3,B4,C1,C2,C3,D1,D2,D3,E1,E2,E3 sub ``` **Reading the diagram:** - **Vertical columns** = the five mechanical zones (matches the table in Section 1.4). - **Horizontal arrows** = the *ink flow path* on a press. - **Dampening train** (E2) injects fountain solution *between* the inking rollers and the plate — the critical offset interface. - **Drying unit** (E3) acts on the print only *after* the impression nip — separate from the rheology of transfer. ## 4.2 Force & Flow Analysis at the Impression Nip ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph NIP["IMPRESSION NIP CROSS-SECTION"] direction TB P["Plate cylinder
Radius Rp
Rotation omega"] B["Blanket cylinder
Radius Rb
Compression delta"] S["Substrate paper
Thickness t
Speed v = omegaR"] end F1["F1: Shear stress tau
from roller train"] F2["F2: Normal pressure Pn
0.2-0.4 MPa"] F3["F3: Tensile stress at split
sigma = gamma / r"] F4["F4: Capillary absorption
Washburn equation"] P -- "ink film" --> B B -- "split at exit" --> S F1 --> P F2 --> B F3 --> S F4 --> S classDef nip fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#6a1b9a,stroke-width:1px,color:#4a148c classDef force fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#2e7d32,stroke-width:1px,color:#1b5e20 class NIP,P,B,S nip class F1,F2,F3,F4 force ``` **Interpretation of the force diagram:** - **F1 (shear)** governs the *viscosity* regime inside the nip. - **F2 (compressive)** governs the *film thickness* and the *set-off* tendency. - **F3 (tensile)** is what *pulls the ink apart* at the exit of the nip — the source of misting, filamenting, and dot distortion. - **F4 (capillary)** is the *post-nip* absorption into paper fibers — sets the rate of *set-off vs. set-dry*. ## 4.3 Decision Tree — Selecting the Correct Rheology for a Press ```mermaid flowchart TD Q1{What is the press type?} Q1 -- "Sheet-fed offset" --> R1[Yield 800-2000 dyn/cm2
Tack 10-14 TU
n = 0.85-0.95] Q1 -- "Web offset heatset" --> R2[Yield 400-800 dyn/cm2
Tack 6-9 TU
n = 0.90-0.98] Q1 -- "Flexo" --> R3[Viscosity 30-200 mPa.s
Low yield
n = 0.95-1.00] Q1 -- "Gravure" --> R4[Viscosity 15-80 mPa.s
Near-Newtonian
n = 0.95-1.00] Q1 -- "Screen" --> R5[Viscosity 2000-10000 mPa.s
High yield
Thixotropic] R1 --> S1{Substrate?} S1 -- "Coated paper" --> S1a[Increase tack to 12-14 TU] S1 -- "Uncoated paper" --> S1b[Decrease tack to 8-10 TU] R2 --> S2{Web speed?} S2 -- "Less than 30000 iph" --> S2a[Standard grade] S2 -- "Greater than 60000 iph" --> S2b[High-speed grade, lower n] classDef press fill:#e1f5fe,stroke:#01579b,stroke-width:1px,color:#01579b classDef rheol fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100,stroke-width:1px,color:#bf360c classDef sub fill:#f1f8e9,stroke:#33691e,stroke-width:1px,color:#1b5e20 class Q1,S1,S2 press class R1,R2,R3,R4,R5,S1a,S1b,S2a,S2b rheol ``` This decision tree mirrors the **troubleshooting logic** that KTU examiners use in problem statements ("An offset ink is causing picking on coated paper at 15 000 sph — what property of the ink should be modified and in which direction?"). # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. Define the term "yield value" of a printing ink. Why is it essential for offset inks? `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Question Paper]` **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **Yield value** ($\tau_0$) is defined as the *minimum shear stress* that must be applied to a printing ink before it begins to flow. Below $\tau_0$, the ink behaves as a semi-solid; above $\tau_0$, it flows like a viscous liquid (Bingham/Casson behaviour). > > In a quantitative form, for a Casson-model offset ink: > > $$\tau = \tau_0 + 2\sqrt{\tau_0 \eta_c \dot{\gamma}} + \eta_c \dot{\gamma}$$ > > For offset inks, $\tau_0$ is typically in the range **500 – 3000 dyn/cm²**. > > **Importance:** > 1. Prevents the ink from **dripping** out of the ink duct. > 2. Prevents the ink from **spreading** into the non-image areas of the printing plate (avoids *toning / scumming*). > 3. Ensures the ink stays on the **roller surface** and does not run off under centrifugal force at high press speeds. > > *(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for equation/range, 1 mark for two importance points.)* ### Q2. Differentiate between thixotropy and dilatancy. Cite one printing ink example of each. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, End-Semester Examination]` **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Property | Thixotropy | Dilatancy | |---|---|---| | Time-dependence | Yes (reversible) | No (instantaneous) | | Viscosity vs shear | Decreases with shear | Increases with shear | | Power-law index $n$ | < 1 (pseudoplastic + time) | > 1 | | Recovery on rest | Yes, structure rebuilds | No recovery issue | | Example | Sheet-fed offset ink | High-pigment screen ink | > *Thixotropy is desired in offset because it allows the ink to flow under the roller nip and re-stiffen at the moment of impression, preventing set-off. Dilatancy is undesirable in printing because it causes the ink to "lock up" and pick the paper.* ## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Mechanical & Operational Aspects** > 1. **Always write the units** for viscosity (Pa·s or P), yield value (Pa or dyn/cm²), and tack (tack units). A numerical answer without units loses **0.5 – 1 mark per parameter**. > 2. **State the model used** (Newtonian, Bingham, Casson, Power-Law). Naming the model alone gives a 1-mark "approach" credit even if the final number is wrong. > 3. **Avoid blanket statements** like "viscosity should be low." Examiners expect *"viscosity should be low at high shear rates (10⁴ – 10⁶ s⁻¹) to permit film splitting, but yield value should be high at rest."* The conditional phrasing is what scores full marks. > 4. In derivations, **never skip the algebraic transition** — write "Square both sides → expand by (a+b)² = a² + 2ab + b²" — the step-by-step logic is what carries the 7 marks in the analytical sub-part. > 5. **For diagram questions**, label the axes ($\tau$ on y, $\dot{\gamma}$ on x), the four rheological curves, and identify the printing-ink region (pseudoplastic with yield). A curve without labels is treated as 0. --- ### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Rheology & Walker Transfer `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme, PEPOT866]` **(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the rheological flow curves of **Newtonian, Bingham-plastic, pseudoplastic, and dilatant** fluids. Identify which one is most suitable for a **sheet-fed offset ink** and justify. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Derive the **Walker ink-transfer equation** $y = 1 - e^{-kb\phi}$ from first principles. Using this equation, compute the *fractional transfer* for $kb\phi = 0.5, 1.0, 2.0$ and $4.0$, and plot the trend. Comment on the *practical* meaning of the curve's asymptote. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks] > [!NOTE] > **Model answer sketch description (3 marks):** > Draw a graph with $\tau$ on the y-axis and $\dot{\gamma}$ on the x-axis. Plot four curves: > 1. **Newtonian** — straight line through the origin, slope = $\eta$. > 2. **Bingham plastic** — straight line with positive y-intercept $\tau_0$ (does not pass through origin). > 3. **Pseudoplastic** — concave-down curve starting from origin; no initial yield. > 4. **Dilatant** — concave-up curve starting from origin; viscosity *increases* with shear. > > In a second sketch (optional), plot $\eta_{\text{app}}$ vs $\dot{\gamma}$: Newtonian is flat, pseudoplastic decreases, dilatant increases. **Selection (2 marks):** The **pseudoplastic model with a yield value (i.e., Casson-type)** is most suitable for sheet-fed offset ink, because: - It is **stiff at rest** (preventing drip in the duct). - It **shear-thins** under the roller nip, allowing easy film-splitting. - It exhibits **recoverable structure** (thixotropic), regaining body after transfer to the substrate, preventing set-off. **Justification (2 marks):** Printing involves shear rates from 1 s⁻¹ (duct) to 10⁶ s⁻¹ (nip). A purely Newtonian fluid cannot satisfy both extremes. A purely dilatant fluid would "lock up" under the nip and cause paper picking. A pseudoplastic with yield offers the correct rheological profile — confirmed experimentally by Cone-plate and Casson-plot studies. #### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks] **Step 1 (1 mark).** Define the split ratio $r = x/b$, where $x$ is the ink transferred and $b$ is the supplied film thickness. **Step 2 (1 mark).** Walker assumed an exponential decay of intact filaments: $$\dfrac{dr}{d\phi} = (1 - r)$$ **Step 3 (1 mark).** Integrate from $\phi = 0$ (no transfer) to the operating $\phi = k b$: $$\int_0^{r} \dfrac{dr}{1 - r} = \int_0^{k b \phi} d\phi$$ **Step 4 (1 mark).** Evaluate: $$-\ln(1 - r) = k b \phi \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; 1 - r = e^{-k b \phi} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; r = 1 - e^{-k b \phi}$$ **Step 5 (1 mark).** Numerical evaluation: | $k b \phi$ | $e^{-k b \phi}$ | $y = 1 - e^{-k b \phi}$ | |------------|------------------|------------------------| | 0.5 | 0.6065 | **0.3935** | | 1.0 | 0.3679 | **0.6321** | | 2.0 | 0.1353 | **0.8647** | | 4.0 | 0.0183 | **0.9817** | **Step 6 (1 mark).** Plot: the curve rises steeply at first and then flattens, approaching 1.0 asymptotically. **Step 7 (1 mark).** *Practical meaning:* The asymptote $y \to 1$ means that **no matter how thick the ink film is made, less than 100 % of the ink can ever be transferred to the substrate** — the rest remains on the plate/blanket. This is why over-inking the duct does *not* darken the print proportionally; it instead causes **set-off, smearing, and increased dot gain**. The transfer is *front-loaded* — most of the transfer happens in the first micron. --- ### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Casson Model & Press Tuning (Internal Choice) `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme, PEPOT866]` **(a)** A sheet-fed offset ink gives the following **Casson plot** data: at $\dot{\gamma} = 10$ s⁻¹, $\sqrt{\tau} = 18.0$ Pa^0.5; at $\dot{\gamma} = 1000$ s⁻¹, $\sqrt{\tau} = 65.0$ Pa^0.5. Compute the **yield value** $\tau_0$ and the **Casson viscosity** $\eta_c$. Hence, calculate the **shear stress** at $\dot{\gamma} = 5000$ s⁻¹. **(7 marks)** **(b)** An offset printer reports **picking on coated stock** at 12 000 sph. Using the **mechanical/operational framework**, identify *which* ink property is most likely mis-tuned, *why*, and *how* you would correct it. Mention the rheology model that supports your reasoning. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks] **Step 1 (1 mark).** Casson model: $$\sqrt{\tau} = \sqrt{\tau_0} + \sqrt{\eta_c \dot{\gamma}}$$ **Step 2 (2 marks).** Set up two equations: At $\dot{\gamma}_1 = 10$: $$18.0 = \sqrt{\tau_0} + \sqrt{10 \eta_c}$$ At $\dot{\gamma}_2 = 1000$: $$65.0 = \sqrt{\tau_0} + \sqrt{1000 \eta_c}$$ **Step 3 (1 mark).** Subtract the two equations: $$65.0 - 18.0 = \sqrt{1000 \eta_c} - \sqrt{10 \eta_c}$$ $$47.0 = \sqrt{\eta_c}\,(\sqrt{1000} - \sqrt{10})$$ $$47.0 = \sqrt{\eta_c}\,(31.623 - 3.162) = 28.461 \sqrt{\eta_c}$$ $$\sqrt{\eta_c} = 47.0 / 28.461 = 1.651 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \eta_c = 2.726 \,\text{Pa·s}$$ **Step 4 (1 mark).** Substitute back into the first equation to find $\sqrt{\tau_0}$: $$18.0 = \sqrt{\tau_0} + \sqrt{10 \times 2.726}$$ $$18.0 = \sqrt{\tau_0} + \sqrt{27.26} = \sqrt{\tau_0} + 5.221$$ $$\sqrt{\tau_0} = 12.779 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \tau_0 = 163.3 \,\text{Pa}$$ **Step 5 (1 mark).** Note: this $\tau_0$ in Pa. Convert to dyn/cm² for the conventional unit: $$\tau_0 = 163.3 \,\text{Pa} = 163.3 \times 10 = 1\,633 \,\text{dyn/cm}^2$$ This is within the typical offset range (500 – 3000 dyn/cm²). ✓ **Step 6 (1 mark).** Compute $\tau$ at $\dot{\gamma} = 5000$ s⁻¹: $$\sqrt{\tau} = 12.779 + \sqrt{2.726 \times 5000} = 12.779 + \sqrt{13\,630} = 12.779 + 116.75$$ $$\sqrt{\tau} = 129.53 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \tau = 16\,778 \,\text{Pa} \approx 16.8 \,\text{kPa}$$ **Verification:** The high-shear stress is dominated by the viscous term, confirming good printability. #### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks] > **Picking** is the *partial lifting of paper fibers or coating* by the ink film as it is split at the impression nip. It is fundamentally a **tack/viscosity mismatch** at high shear. **Step 1 — Identify the property (2 marks).** The mis-tuned property is **tack** (or, equivalently, the **high-shear viscosity** in the Casson/Power-Law model). Tack represents the *cohesive + adhesive resistance* of the ink film to splitting. If tack exceeds the *cohesive strength of the paper surface*, the paper fails before the ink splits → **picking**. **Step 2 — Why it happens (2 marks).** On coated stock, the **surface strength** is lower than on uncoated stock because the coating layer (latex + pigment) has lower internal cohesion than cellulose fibers. At 12 000 sph, the nip pressure and shear rate are high → effective tack is elevated. The combination (low substrate strength + high effective tack) causes the coating to be pulled off. **Step 3 — How to correct (2 marks).** - **Reduce the ink tack** by 2 – 3 tack units (e.g., from 13 → 10 TU) using a *tack reducer* (a low-viscosity oil/solvent) or a softer bodying varnish. - **Reduce duct feed** by 5 – 10 % to lower the ink film thickness on the plate. - **Reduce roller squeeze pressure** between the form roller and plate to lower the splitting stress. - **Use a stronger-coated substrate** if the print job permits. - **Slow the press** as a temporary measure. **Step 4 — Rheology model supporting the reasoning (1 mark).** The **Casson model** is most appropriate: the high-shear stress $\tau = 2\sqrt{\tau_0 \eta_c \dot{\gamma}} + \eta_c \dot{\gamma}$ scales with $\sqrt{\eta_c}$ and $\sqrt{\dot{\gamma}}$, so reducing $\eta_c$ (i.e., reducing tack/viscosity) and reducing $\dot{\gamma}$ (i.e., reducing press speed) both lower the splitting stress in a *predictable* way. The Power-Law model can be used as a cross-check: $\tau = K \dot{\gamma}^{n}$ — reducing $K$ (consistency index) directly lowers $\tau$ at all shear rates. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Summary — Mechanical & Operational Aspects** - [x] **Rheology** = science of flow; printing inks are *non-Newtonian* and almost always **pseudoplastic with a yield value**. - [x] **Viscosity** ($\eta$) — internal friction; measured in **Pa·s** (or **Poise**). *1 P = 0.1 Pa·s.* - [x] **Yield value** ($\tau_0$) — minimum stress to initiate flow; offset inks need **500 – 3000 dyn/cm²** to prevent dripping & scumming. - [x] **Thixotropy** — *time-dependent, reversible* shear-thinning; quantified by **Thixotropic Index TI = $\eta_{6}/\eta_{60}$**, target **1.2 – 2.5** for offset. - [x] **Tack** — resistance to film splitting; measured on **Inkometer at 400 rpm, 25 °C, 1 min**; offset target **8 – 14 tack units**. - [x] **Power-law model** — $\eta = K \dot{\gamma}^{n-1}$; offset ink has $n \approx 0.85 - 0.95$. - [x] **Casson model** — $\sqrt{\tau} = \sqrt{\tau_0} + \sqrt{\eta_c \dot{\gamma}}$; *industry standard* for offset ink rheology. - [x] **Walker equation** — $y = 1 - e^{-kb\phi}$; predicts *asymptotic* ink transfer to substrate. - [x] **Fetsko–Walker** — adds ink-trap ratio $R_b$ for multi-color press prediction. - [x] **Newtonian reference** — water at 20 °C has $\eta = 1.002$ mPa·s. - [x] **Operational shear rates** — 1 s⁻¹ (duct) to 10⁶ s⁻¹ (impression nip). - [x] **Press speeds** — sheet-fed: 10–18 k sph; web: 40–80 k iph; flexo: 200–600 m/min; gravure: 200–450 m/min. - [x] **Capillary absorption (Washburn)** — $\Delta P_{\text{cap}} = 2 \gamma \cos\theta / r$; governs set-off and print drying. - [x] **Defect map:** - **Picking** ← tack too high / substrate too weak. - **Misting** ← viscosity too low at high shear / press too fast. - **Set-off** ← body too low / insufficient setting. - **Scumming / Toning** ← yield value too low / fountain-solution pH wrong. - **Slur & Dot gain** ← plastic viscosity too low / ink film too thick. - **Hickeys** ← dirt or partially dried skin in ink duct. - [x] **Always** state units; **always** name the rheology model; **always** give *conditional* recommendations (e.g., "low at high shear, high at rest"). > **End of Module 4 Notes — Mechanical and Operational Aspects** > *Good luck with your KTU 2024 Scheme End-Semester Examination!*

topics.title: International Standards and National Standards in Ink Manufacturing Area
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT866 - TECHNOLOGY OF PRINTING INKS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** International Standards and National Standards in Ink Manufacturing Area

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are Printing Ink Standards?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> **Printing Ink Standards** are codified technical documents established by recognized standardization bodies (ISO, ASTM, BIS, CIE, DIN, JIS) that define **testing methodologies, performance specifications, quality parameters, and safety requirements** for raw materials, intermediates, and finished printing inks. They ensure **batch-to-batch consistency, inter-laboratory reproducibility, regulatory compliance, and commercial acceptance** across global supply chains.

### The Three Pillars of Ink Standardization

| Pillar | Meaning | Engineering Significance |
|--------|---------|--------------------------|
| **Method Standards** | How to test (procedures) | Eliminate operator-to-operator variance |
| **Product Standards** | What to deliver (specifications) | Define acceptance/rejection criteria |
| **Safety Standards** | How to handle (regulations) | Address toxicology, migration, food contact |

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: "The Traffic Light Analogy"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy for First-Time Learners:**
>
> Imagine a printing ink factory is like a **busy highway intersection** without traffic lights. Vehicles (different batches of ink) from different manufacturers (pigment suppliers, varnish makers, additive blenders) arrive from all directions. Without **standards** (traffic lights), chaos ensues — wrong turns, collisions (incompatible inks), accidents (printing failures on press).
>
> **International Standards (ISO, ASTM)** are the **globally synchronized traffic lights** — every factory, every country obeys the same red-yellow-green signal. So an ink made in Germany can be tested in India using identical procedures.
>
> **National Standards (BIS, DIN, JIS)** are **regional traffic regulations** — India follows BIS rules, Germany follows DIN, Japan follows JIS. These are sometimes more stringent for local conditions (e.g., India's tropical climate demands higher heat-resistance specs).
>
> **Together**, they ensure that when a packaging printer in Kerala receives ink from a supplier in Mumbai or imported from Switzerland, both parties speak the **same testing language**.

---

## 1.3 Classification of Standards Bodies (Hierarchical Overview)

```
STANDARDIZATION BODIES IN INK TECHNOLOGY
│
├── INTERNATIONAL (Global Treaty Bodies)
│   ├── ISO (International Organization for Standardization)
│   ├── IEC (International Electrotechnical Commission)
│   ├── WHO / FAO (Safety – food packaging migration)
│   └── ASTM International (American Society for Testing and Materials)
│
├── REGIONAL (Multi-National Blocs)
│   ├── CEN (European Committee for Standardization)
│   ├── EN (European Norm – mandatory in EU)
│   └── APAC / SASO / GSO (Gulf Standards)
│
└── NATIONAL (Single Country)
    ├── BIS (Bureau of Indian Standards) – India
    ├── DIN (Deutsches Institut für Normung) – Germany
    ├── BS (British Standards) – UK
    ├── JIS (Japanese Industrial Standards) – Japan
    ├── ANSI (American National Standards Institute) – USA
    ├── AFNOR (Association Française de Normalisation) – France
    └── GOST (Russian Federation Standards)
```

---

## 1.4 Why Standards Matter in Ink Manufacturing — Critical Drivers

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Four Engineering Drivers for Standards Compliance:**
>
> 1. **Reproducibility** — Two laboratories must arrive at the **same numerical result** for viscosity, color, gloss, or drying time within defined tolerance limits.
> 2. **Regulatory Compliance** — Food packaging inks must comply with **FDA 21 CFR, EU Regulation 1935/2004, and India FSSAI** migration limits.
> 3. **Process Control** — ISO 12647 series defines **process control for offset printing** from pre-press to delivery.
> 4. **Commercial Acceptance** — Major buyers (cigarette, pharmaceutical, FMCG) **only accept inks that pass certified testing** per agreed standards.

---

## 1.5 Key Performance Parameters Covered by Standards

The following ink properties are governed by documented standards:

- **Color** — CIE Lab, ΔE tolerance, densitometric values
- **Viscosity** — Flow cup seconds, dynamic viscosity in Pa·s
- **Tack** — Ink stickiness measured on tackometer
- **Drying/Curing** — Set-off, through-dry, oxidative polymerization rate
- **Resistance Properties** — Light, water, alkali, solvent, rub, scratch
- **Adhesion** — Tape test (cross-hatch, pull-off)
- **Pigment Loading & Particle Size** — Hegman gauge, grindometer
- **Migration & Extractables** — For food contact packaging
- **VOC Content** — Volatile organic compounds for environmental compliance
- **Heavy Metal Content** — Pb, Cd, Hg, Cr(VI) limits per toy/food safety norms

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 International Standards — ISO Family (The Global Backbone)

> [!NOTE]
> **ISO (International Organization for Standardization)** is a non-governmental federation of **170+ national standards bodies**, headquartered in **Geneva, Switzerland**. ISO standards relevant to printing inks are developed by **Technical Committee TC 130 (Graphic technology)**.

### 2.1.1 Critical ISO Standards for Printing Inks

| ISO Number | Title / Subject | Engineering Application |
|------------|----------------|--------------------------|
| **ISO 2846** | Printing inks — Determination of colour transparency | Transparency measurement for process inks |
| **ISO 12647-1** | Process control — Parameters & measurement methods | Master standard for offset printing |
| **ISO 12647-2** | Offset lithographic process control | Sheet-fed & web offset |
| **ISO 12647-3** | Coldset offset (newspaper) | News ink standards |
| **ISO 12647-4** | Publication gravure | Magazine printing |
| **ISO 12647-5** | Screen printing | Textile, glass, electronics inks |
| **ISO 12647-6** | Flexographic printing | Packaging flexo |
| **ISO 12647-7** | Proofing process | Digital/contract proofing |
| **ISO 2836** | Print and printing ink — Resistance to various agents | Chemical/physical resistance testing |
| **ISO 2837** | Resistance to light | Lightfastness testing |
