## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet — Key Equations in Standardized Testing

> [!NOTE]
> The following table summarizes the **mathematical relationships** engineers use when applying standards to evaluate printing inks.

| Parameter | Equation | Units | Standard Reference |
|-----------|----------|-------|---------------------|
| **Kinematic Viscosity (η)** | $\eta = \rho \cdot \nu$ | m²/s, cSt | ISO 3104, ASTM D 445 |
| **Dynamic Viscosity (Newtonian)** | $\tau = \eta \cdot \dot{\gamma}$ | Pa·s | ASTM D 2196 |
| **Flow Cup Viscosity** | $\nu = k \cdot t - \dfrac{c}{t}$ | cSt | ISO 2431, ASTM D 1200 |
| **Hegman Grindometer (NPIRI)** | $H = 7 - \dfrac{d_{\text{max}}}{10}$ | Hegman units | ASTM D 1210 |
| **Color Difference (CIE Lab)** | $\Delta E = \sqrt{\Delta L^{2} + \Delta a^{2} + \Delta b^{2}}$ | Dimensionless | ISO 13655, ASTM E 308 |
| **Opacity / Contrast Ratio** | $C_{r} = \dfrac{Y_{\text{black}}}{Y_{\text{white}}}$ | Ratio | ISO 2814 |
| **Density (Log)** | $D = \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{I_{0}}{I_{t}}\right)$ | Optical density | ISO 5-4 |
| **Tack (Inkometer)** | $T = f(N,\, t,\, T_{\text{rpm}})$ | Tack units | ASTM D 4361 |
| **Lightfastness (Blue Wool)** | $B = 1 \text{ to } 8$ | Blue wool scale | ISO 2837, ISO 105-B02 |
| **Migration Limit (Food)** | $S_{\text{lim}} \leq S_{\text{SML}}$ | mg/kg food | EU 1935/2004, FSSAI |
| **VOC Mass Fraction** | $\text{VOC}\% = \dfrac{m_{\text{VOC}}}{m_{\text{total}}} \times 100$ | % w/w | ISO 15320, IS 15200 |
| **Gloss (60°)** | $G = 1000 \cdot \log\!\left(\dfrac{100}{R_{60}}\right)$ | GU | ISO 2813, DIN 67530 |
| **Print Contrast** | $\text{PC} = \dfrac{D_{s} - D_{t}}{D_{s}}$ | Ratio | ISO 12647 |
| **Yield Value (Bingham)** | $\tau = \tau_{0} + \eta_{p}\dot{\gamma}$ | Pa | ASTM D 2196 |

> [!TIP]
> **Critical Pipe Symbol Notice:** When writing `|x|` or absolute values inside formulas, use LaTeX `\vert x \vert` (NOT `\|x\|`) to avoid breaking markdown table syntax.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industrial Application Map:**
>
> - **Press Setup** → Printer uses ISO 12647-2 targets (Lab values, TVI curves, solid density) to calibrate press and achieve **ISO-compliant prints** that match contract proof.
> - **QC Lab** → Routine testing follows ASTM + BIS protocols: Hegman grind, viscosity, tack, color, drying, rub resistance.
> - **Export Compliance** → Ink exported to EU must pass **EN 71-3, EN 71-9, EU 1935/2004, Swiss Ordinance 817.02** for food/toy contact.
> - **Procurement** → Buyers specify "ink shall comply with **IS 1004** for offset, **IS 1866** for food packaging, with **CIE Lab ΔE ≤ 2.0** against standard reference."
> - **Environmental** → **VOC < 5% w/w** required under IS 15200, EPA, EU Directive 2004/42/CE for decorative paints/coatings (analogous to inks in some jurisdictions).
> - **Pharma/Food** → Inks must comply with **ISO 15378** (GMP for primary packaging materials) and migration limits (overall ≤ **10 mg/dm²**, specific migration limits per substance).
> - **Currency/Security** → Inks for banknotes follow **BIS IS 11686** and **RBI Bank Note Security** specifications with tamper-evident chemical signatures.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — CIE Lab Color Difference Calculation

> [!NOTE]
> This is the most frequently tested computation in ink QC. The CIE Lab color difference **ΔE** quantifies how close a produced ink color is to the reference standard.

### Given Data (Typical KTU Numerical Example)

A standard offset cyan ink has the following CIE Lab coordinates under **D50 illuminant, 2° observer, with black backing**:
- Reference: $L^{*}_{r} = 55.0$, $a^{*}_{r} = -38.0$, $b^{*}_{r} = -45.0$
- Batch sample: $L^{*}_{s} = 56.5$, $a^{*}_{s} = -36.8$, $b^{*}_{s} = -43.7$

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Compute individual coordinate differences**

$$\Delta L^{*} = L^{*}_{s} - L^{*}_{r}$$

$$\Delta L^{*} = 56.5 - 55.0 = 1.5$$

$$\Delta a^{*} = a^{*}_{s} - a^{*}_{r}$$

$$\Delta a^{*} = -36.8 - (-38.0) = 1.2$$

$$\Delta b^{*} = b^{*}_{s} - b^{*}_{r}$$

$$\Delta b^{*} = -43.7 - (-45.0) = 1.3$$

**Step 2: Square each difference**

$$\Delta L^{*2} = (1.5)^{2} = 2.25$$

$$\Delta a^{*2} = (1.2)^{2} = 1.44$$

$$\Delta b^{*2} = (1.3)^{2} = 1.69$$

**Step 3: Apply the CIE 1976 ΔE formula**

$$\Delta E^{*}_{ab} = \sqrt{\Delta L^{*2} + \Delta a^{*2} + \Delta b^{*2}}$$

**Step 4: Substitute numerical values**

$$\Delta E^{*}_{ab} = \sqrt{2.25 + 1.44 + 1.69}$$

**Step 5: Sum the squares**

$$\Delta E^{*}_{ab} = \sqrt{5.38}$$

**Step 6: Take the square root**

$$\Delta E^{*}_{ab} = 2.32$$

### Step 7: Interpretation (Critical Decision Step)

| ΔE Range | Visual Perception | Print Acceptance (ISO 12647 typical) |
|----------|-------------------|--------------------------------------|
| 0 – 1 | Imperceptible | Excellent match |
| 1 – 2 | Perceptible on close inspection | Acceptable (tight spec) |
| 2 – 3.5 | Perceptible at normal viewing | Borderline — needs approval |
| 3.5 – 5 | Clearly noticeable difference | **REJECT batch** |
| > 5 | Opposite colors | **REJECT — investigate** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion for Sample:**
> With $\Delta E^{*}_{ab} = 2.32$, the batch is in the **"borderline acceptable"** range under ISO 12647-2's typical **ΔE ≤ 2.0** tolerance for process inks. The QC manager should issue a **conditional release** and notify the printer.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Hegman Grindometer Reading

### Theoretical Basis

The Hegman gauge (also called NPIRI gauge) measures the **fineness of grind** of pigment dispersion. The gauge has a tapered channel of gradually increasing depth. The point where **particles become visible as a streak** gives the maximum particle size.

The mathematical relationship between Hegman reading **H** and maximum particle diameter **d** (in micrometers):

$$H = 7 - \dfrac{d_{\text{max}}}{10}$$

Where **H** ranges from 0 (100 µm) to 8 (0 µm).

### Worked Example

A pigment dispersion is tested and the streak first appears at **Hegman reading = 6.5**.

**Step 1: Apply the formula**

$$H = 7 - \dfrac{d_{\text{max}}}{10}$$

**Step 2: Rearrange to solve for $d_{\text{max}}$**

$$d_{\text{max}} = 10 \cdot (7 - H)$$

**Step 3: Substitute H = 6.5**

$$d_{\text{max}} = 10 \cdot (7 - 6.5)$$

$$d_{\text{max}} = 10 \cdot 0.5$$

$$d_{\text{max}} = 5 \, \mu m$$

### Interpretation Matrix

| Hegman Reading H | Max Particle d_max (µm) | Print Application |
|------------------|--------------------------|-------------------|
| 8.0 | 0 (theoretical) | Ultra-fine, gloss inks |
| 7.5 | 2.5 | High-quality offset |
| 7.0 | 5.0 | Standard offset |
| 6.5 | 7.5 | Gravure inks |
| 6.0 | 10.0 | Flexo inks |
| 5.0 | 20.0 | News inks |
| 4.0 | 30.0 | Coarse letterpress |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Pitfall:** Students often confuse Hegman with **microns directly**. Remember: Hegman is an **inverse scale** — higher number = finer grind.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Viscosity from Flow Cup (ISO 2431 / ASTM D 1200)

### Theoretical Basis

For a flow cup with calibrated orifice, the kinematic viscosity **ν** is related to efflux time **t** by:

$$\nu = k \cdot t - \dfrac{c}{t}$$

For a #4 Ford cup (commonly used for offset inks), the simplified relationship for typical ink efflux times (15–300 s) is approximated as:

$$\nu = 0.405 \cdot t \quad (\text{in cSt})$$

Where **k** is cup constant and **c** is correction constant (often negligible for #4 cup).

### Worked Example

A sheet-fed offset ink efflux time through a **#4 Ford cup at 25°C** is measured as **t = 45 seconds**.

**Step 1: Apply the simplified equation**

$$\nu = 0.405 \cdot t$$

**Step 2: Substitute t = 45**

$$\nu = 0.405 \cdot 45$$

$$\nu = 18.225 \, \text{cSt}$$

**Step 3: Convert to SI units (m²/s)**

$$1 \, \text{cSt} = 10^{-6} \, \text{m}^{2}/\text{s}$$

$$\nu = 18.225 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{m}^{2}/\text{s}$$

$$\nu = 1.82 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{m}^{2}/\text{s}$$

**Step 4: Convert to Poise if ink density ρ = 1.05 g/cm³**

$$\eta = \rho \cdot \nu = 1.05 \cdot 18.225 = 19.14 \, \text{cP} = 1.914 \times 10^{-2} \, \text{Pa \cdot s}$$

### Verification — Is this in Specification Range?

| Process | Typical Offset Ink Viscosity |
|---------|------------------------------|
| Sheet-fed offset | **15 – 35 Pa·s** (cone-plate, high shear) |
| Heat-set web | 8 – 18 Pa·s |
| Cold-set news | 2 – 6 Pa·s |

> [!NOTE]
> Flow cup gives **low-shear viscosity**; press performance is dominated by **high-shear rheology**. Always specify which viscosity is being quoted.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Print Contrast Ratio (PC Value)

### Theoretical Basis

Print Contrast measures the **sharpness and clarity** of a halftone dot:

$$\text{PC} = \dfrac{D_{s} - D_{t}}{D_{s}}$$

Where:
- $D_{s}$ = Solid (100%) tone density
- $D_{t}$ = 75% tone density (or 50% — varies by standard)

### Worked Example

A printed sheet shows:
- Solid (100%) density: $D_{s} = 1.45$
- 75% tone density: $D_{t} = 0.85$

**Step 1: Apply the formula**

$$\text{PC} = \dfrac{D_{s} - D_{t}}{D_{s}}$$

**Step 2: Substitute values**

$$\text{PC} = \dfrac{1.45 - 0.85}{1.45}$$

**Step 3: Compute numerator**

$$\text{PC} = \dfrac{0.60}{1.45}$$

**Step 4: Divide**

$$\text{PC} = 0.4138 \approx 0.41 \text{ or } 41.4\%$$

### Acceptance Matrix (ISO 12647-2 for offset)

| PC Value | Print Quality |
|----------|---------------|
| ≥ 0.40 | Excellent sharpness |
| 0.30 – 0.40 | Acceptable |
| < 0.30 | Poor — ink/water imbalance |

---

## 3.6 Migration Limit Calculation for Food Packaging Inks (Derivation 5)

### Theoretical Basis

For food contact materials, EU Regulation 1935/2004 establishes:
- **Overall Migration Limit (OML):** ≤ 10 mg/dm²
- **Specific Migration Limit (SML):** Substance-dependent (e.g., for benzophenone SML = 0.6 mg/kg food simulant)

### Worked Example

A printed laminate is tested with food simulant **ethanol 10% v/v** at 40°C for 10 days. The extracted mass from a 100 cm² sample is 1.8 mg.

**Step 1: Convert area to dm²**

$$A = 100 \, \text{cm}^{2} = 1 \, \text{dm}^{2}$$

**Step 2: Compute migration per dm²**

$$M = \dfrac{m}{A} = \dfrac{1.8 \, \text{mg}}{1 \, \text{dm}^{2}} = 1.8 \, \text{mg/dm}^{2}$$

**Step 3: Compare against OML**

$$M = 1.8 \, \text{mg/dm}^{2} \leq \text{OML} = 10 \, \text{mg/dm}^{2}$$

$$\therefore \text{PASS} \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 4: Specific migration — check individual substances**

For each substance *i*:

$$S_{i} = \dfrac{m_{i}}{m_{\text{simulant}}} \quad \text{[mg/kg simulant]}$$

Each $S_i$ must be ≤ SML$_i$ specified in **EU Regulation 10/2011** Annex I (positive list).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Reporting **per cm²** instead of **per dm²**. Always check units carefully. 1 dm² = 100 cm².

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper – Module 4]**

**Q: Define BIS. List any four BIS standards relevant to printing inks and their corresponding application areas.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **BIS (Bureau of Indian Standards)** is the **national standards body of India**, established under the BIS Act 2016, with headquarters in **Manak Bhavan, New Delhi**. It formulates Indian Standards (IS) and operates the **Certification Marks Scheme** (ISI Mark).

**Four BIS Standards for Printing Inks:**

| IS Number | Title | Application |
|-----------|-------|-------------|
| **IS 1004** | Specification for offset ink | Sheet-fed & web offset lithography |
| **IS 1866** | Specification for ink for food packaging | FSSAI compliance |
| **IS 1194** | Methods of sampling and testing for printing inks | Universal QC test methods |
| **IS 17303** | Migration limits for food contact materials | Food packaging safety |

(Any four valid IS numbers with correct application accepted.) ✓

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary]**

**Q: What is the significance of the ISO 12647 series in the printing ink industry? Mention any three sub-parts and their specific processes.**

**Model Answer:**

The **ISO 12647 series** is the **international benchmark for process control in graphic technology**. It defines **tolerances for color, density, tone value increase, print contrast, and substrate** to achieve **reproducible, contract-compliant print quality** across different printing processes.

**Three Sub-parts:**

| ISO Number | Process Controlled |
|------------|-------------------|
| **ISO 12647-2** | Offset lithographic printing (sheet-fed and web) |
| **ISO 12647-3** | Coldset offset (newspaper printing) |
| **ISO 12647-6** | Flexographic printing (packaging) |

(Any three valid sub-parts accepted.) ✓

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Regulation:**
> In the End Semester Examination (ESE), students have **internal choice within a module** — they can choose between two questions from the same module. Both questions below carry **14 marks** with sub-parts of **7 marks each**.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Main Examination, Module 4]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Q: Explain in detail the role of ISO standards in printing ink manufacturing. Discuss ISO 2846, ISO 12647-2, and ISO 2836 with their engineering applications.**

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
The **International Organization for Standardization (ISO)** is the world's largest developer of voluntary international standards. For printing inks, the primary technical committee is **ISO/TC 130 (Graphic Technology)**, which develops standards for materials, processes, and quality control. ISO standards ensure **global reproducibility, regulatory compliance, and commercial acceptance**.

**ISO 2846 — Determination of Colour Transparency (2 marks):**
- **Purpose:** Defines a method to measure the **transparency of process inks** (cyan, magenta, yellow, black) over a black background.
- **Principle:** Ink is printed in a defined film thickness over a black substrate; the ratio of reflectance over black vs. white substrate gives transparency.
- **Application:** Critical for **multicolor wet-trapping** where each ink layer must allow underlying colors to show through predictably. Essential for **process color reproduction** in offset and gravure printing.

**ISO 12647-2 — Offset Lithographic Process Control (2 marks):**
- **Purpose:** Defines **tolerances and measurement methods** for four-color offset lithographic printing.
- **Key Parameters:** Solid ink density (e.g., Cyan = 1.40 ± 0.10), TVI (Tone Value Increase) curves, CIE Lab coordinates, print contrast, gray balance.
- **Application:** Used by **printers worldwide** to achieve **ISO-compliant prints** that match contract proofs; ensures that an offset job printed in India matches the proof approved in the US.

**ISO 2836 — Resistance to Various Agents (2 marks):**
- **Purpose:** Standardized method to test **print resistance** against water, alkali, acid, soap, solvents, detergents, etc.
- **Application:** Quality control for **packaging inks** that must withstand food contact, washing, or chemical exposure.

**Conclusion:** ISO standards provide a **universal language** for ink manufacturers, printers, and buyers. **Compliance with ISO 12647-2 is often a contractual requirement** for offset print jobs.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Q: A standard offset ink has reference CIE Lab values $L^*=52.0$, $a^*=-36.5$, $b^*=-43.2$. A manufactured batch shows $L^*=53.4$, $a^*=-35.0$, $b^*=-41.5$. Calculate the color difference $\Delta E^{*}_{ab}$. Comment on the acceptability of the batch under ISO 12647-2 tolerance ($\Delta E \leq 2.0$).**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute ΔL, Δa, Δb (2 marks)**

$$\Delta L = 53.4 - 52.0 = 1.4$$

$$\Delta a = -35.0 - (-36.5) = 1.5$$

$$\Delta b = -41.5 - (-43.2) = 1.7$$

**Step 2: Square each (1 mark)**

$$\Delta L^{2} = 1.96, \quad \Delta a^{2} = 2.25, \quad \Delta b^{2} = 2.89$$

**Step 3: Sum squares (1 mark)**

$$\Delta L^{2} + \Delta a^{2} + \Delta b^{2} = 1.96 + 2.25 + 2.89 = 7.10$$

**Step 4: Compute ΔE (2 marks)**

$$\Delta E^{*}_{ab} = \sqrt{7.10} = 2.66$$

**Step 5: Decision (1 mark)**

$$\Delta E = 2.66 > 2.0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{NOT ACCEPTABLE under ISO 12647-2 strict spec}$$

**Recommendation:** The batch is in the "**perceptible at normal viewing**" range. **Reject or rework.** The dominant deviation is in **b* (yellow-blue axis)**, suggesting a possible **pigment proportion error** in the cyan batch. Investigate the dispersion and re-check the pigment-blue ratio.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Main Examination, Module 4]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Q: Discuss the Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS) framework for printing inks. Explain the role of IS 1004, IS 1194, IS 1866, and IS 17303 with their industrial applications.**

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction to BIS (1 mark):**
The **Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS)** is India's national standards body under the **Ministry of Consumer Affairs, Food & Public Distribution**, established by the **BIS Act, 2016**. For printing inks, two committees are relevant:
- **CHD 20** — Paints, Varnishes, and Allied Products
- **PGD 23** — Graphic Arts

BIS also runs the **ISI Mark certification scheme** (voluntary but mandatory for food-contact inks under FSSAI).

**IS 1004 — Offset Ink Specification (1.5 marks):**
- Specifies requirements for **letterpress and offset lithographic inks** including:
  - Color, consistency, fineness of grind (Hegman ≥ 6 for offset)
  - Drying time, water tolerance, tack, lightfastness (≥ 4 on Blue Wool)
- **Application:** Procurement specification for **commercial offset printers** in India.

**IS 1194 — Methods of Sampling and Testing (1.5 marks):**
- Standardized **test procedures** for all printing inks.
- Includes sampling protocol, viscosity (flow cup), tack, drying, rub resistance, lightfastness.
- **Application:** Universal QC laboratory reference for **QC technicians** in Indian ink factories.

**IS 1866 — Ink for Food Packaging (1.5 marks):**
- Specifies **safety requirements** for inks used in food contact packaging.
- Defines limits for **heavy metals** (Pb, Cd, Hg, As), migration extracts, odor, taste transfer.
- **Application:** **Mandatory compliance** under FSSAI regulations for **FMCG, dairy, edible oil packaging**.

**IS 17303 — Migration Limits for Food Contact (1.5 marks):**
- Harmonized with **EU Regulation 10/2011**.
- Sets **Specific Migration Limits (SML)** and **Overall Migration Limit (OML ≤ 10 mg/dm²)**.
- **Application:** Required for **export of printed food packaging** to regulated markets.

**Conclusion (Optional, 0 marks for credit):** The BIS framework ensures Indian inks meet **domestic safety laws** while enabling **international trade compatibility**.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Q: A pigment dispersion gives a Hegman reading of 7.0 on the grindometer gauge. Calculate the maximum particle size. Also, an ink sample shows an efflux time of 60 seconds through a #4 Ford cup at 25°C. Calculate the kinematic viscosity in cSt.**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (i) — Hegman Reading (3 marks)**

**Step 1: Apply Hegman-particle size formula (1 mark)**

$$H = 7 - \dfrac{d_{\text{max}}}{10}$$

**Step 2: Rearrange (1 mark)**

$$d_{\text{max}} = 10 \cdot (7 - H)$$

**Step 3: Substitute H = 7.0 and solve (1 mark)**

$$d_{\text{max}} = 10 \cdot (7 - 7.0) = 10 \cdot 0 = 0 \, \mu m$$

**Interpretation:** A Hegman reading of 7.0 corresponds to the **standard cutoff at 5 µm** in many specifications (note: some gauges define the 7-0 range as 0-10 µm). In practice, **Hegman 7 corresponds to max particle size ≤ 5 µm**, suitable for **high-quality sheet-fed offset ink**.

**Part (ii) — Viscosity (4 marks)**

**Step 1: Apply #4 Ford cup equation (1 mark)**

$$\nu = 0.405 \cdot t \quad (\text{cSt})$$

**Step 2: Substitute t = 60 seconds (1 mark)**

$$\nu = 0.405 \cdot 60$$

**Step 3: Calculate (1 mark)**

$$\nu = 24.3 \, \text{cSt}$$

**Step 4: Convert to m²/s (1 mark)**

$$\nu = 24.3 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{m}^{2}/\text{s} = 2.43 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{m}^{2}/\text{s}$$

**Conclusion:** This viscosity is in the **typical range for sheet-fed offset ink** (15–35 cSt, low-shear), suitable for standard dampening systems.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing BIS with ISO:** BIS is **National** (Indian); ISO is **International** (Geneva-based, 170+ countries). Marks deducted if these are interchanged.
>
> 2. **Wrong formula for Hegman:** Hegman scale is **inverse** — higher reading = finer grind (smaller particles). Students often write $d = 10 \cdot H$ instead of $d = 10 \cdot (8 - H)$ or $d = 10 \cdot (7 - H)$ depending on gauge range.
>
> 3. **Missing units in ΔE calculation:** Examiner expects **unitless** ΔE value, but conversion to **SI** (m²/s for viscosity) earns full marks. Always include units.
>
> 4. **Confusing CIE ΔE 1976 with ΔE 2000:** For KTU module 4, the **simpler ΔE 1976** formula is expected. ΔE 2000 is more complex and not typically required at this level.
>
> 5. **Wrong efflux formula:** Use $0.405 t$ for #4 Ford cup, not Zahn cup (which has different constants: Zahn #2 = 3.5 t approximately, Zahn #4 = 11 t approximately).
>
> 6. **Ignoring test conditions:** Always state **temperature (25°C)** and **humidity (50% RH)** in viscosity and color measurements — they affect results significantly.
>
> 7. **Listing standards without application:** Examiners require **standards number + what it tests + industrial relevance**. Mere listing earns partial marks only.
>
> 8. **Migration unit confusion:** Always express migration in **mg/dm²** (EU/FSSAI standard), not mg/cm².

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Standards in Ink Manufacturing:**
>
> - **ISO** is the **global** standards body; **BIS** is India's national body. Both serve different scopes (international harmonization vs. domestic regulation).
> - **ISO TC 130** develops graphic technology standards. **ISO 12647** series is the **master standard for process control** across offset, gravure, flexo, screen.
> - **ISO 2836–2844** are the **resistance test** standards (light, water, alkali, acid, solvent, abrasion, blocking, detergent, wax, soap).
> - **ASTM International** is widely used for **QC test methods** (viscosity, tack, density, drying, rheology, color). ASTM D 4361 = tack; D 1210 = Hegman; D 4212 = Zahn cup.
> - **BIS (India):**
>   - IS 866 = letterpress ink
>   - IS 1004 = offset ink
>   - IS 1272 = gravure ink
>   - IS 1382 = screen ink
>   - IS 1194 = universal test methods
>   - IS 1866 = food packaging ink (FSSAI compliance)
>   - IS 17303 = migration limits
>   - IS 15651 = eco-labeling
>   - IS 11686 = security ink
> - **Color Difference ΔE Interpretation:**
>   - ΔE ≤ 1 → Excellent
>   - 1–2 → Acceptable
>   - 2–3.5 → Borderline
>   - > 3.5 → Reject
> - **Hegman Scale:** 0 = 100 µm (coarsest), 8 = 0 µm (finest). Offset inks typically need ≥ 6.5 (≤ 7.5 µm).
> - **Migration Limits (EU/FSSAI):** Overall Migration ≤ **10 mg/dm²**; Specific Migration per substance per positive list.
> - **VOC Limit:** Increasingly regulated — **< 5% w/w** for eco-labeled inks (IS 15651, EU Directive 2004/42/CE).
> - **Lightfastness:** Measured on **Blue Wool Scale 1–8**; ISO 2837 / ISO 105-B02 reference. Acceptable for packaging: ≥ 4; for outdoor signage: ≥ 7.
> - **Gloss measurement:** **60° geometry** per ISO 2813 / DIN 67530.
> - **Print Density:** Measured with **densitometer** per ISO 5-4; Solid density for offset cyan = 1.40 ± 0.10 typical.
> - **Tack:** Measured on **Inkometer** (ASTM D 4361); typical offset ink tack = 10–14 units at 400 rpm, 90°F.
> - **Always cite the standard** with **year/version** (e.g., "ISO 12647-2:2013") in any industrial report.
> - **Standards are living documents** — they are revised every 5–10 years. Always check the **latest edition** before quoting.
> - **International harmonization:** Most BIS standards on food packaging are now **aligned with EU Regulation 10/2011** to facilitate Indian exports.

---

**End of Module 4 Notes — International and National Standards in Ink Manufacturing Area**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Environmental Aspects
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT866 - TECHNOLOGY OF PRINTING INKS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Environmental Aspects

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Environmental Aspects in Printing Ink Technology** refer to the complete set of ecological, regulatory, health, and sustainability considerations that govern the formulation, manufacture, application, and end-of-life disposal of printing inks. These aspects encompass the emission of **Volatile Organic Compounds (VOCs)**, the presence of **heavy metals**, the use of **petrochemical-derived solvents**, the generation of **hazardous waste**, and the adoption of **green chemistry principles** throughout the ink value chain.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEPOT866 – Technology of Printing Inks, Module 4)**, environmental aspects cover the following interlinked sub-domains:

| Sub-Domain | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Regulatory Compliance** | Adherence to directives such as **REACH (EC 1907/2006)**, **RoHS**, **CEPA (Canadian Environmental Protection Act)**, and **EPA (USA)** standards. |
| **VOC Control** | Reduction of volatile organic compounds in solvent-based, heatset, and gravure inks. |
| **Heavy Metal Restrictions** | Limits on lead, cadmium, mercury, hexavalent chromium in pigments and driers. |
| **Sustainable Raw Materials** | Bio-based resins, vegetable-oil alkyd resins, soy-based inks, water-based dispersions. |
| **Waste Management** | Recycling of press wash solvents, ink sludge treatment, and circular economy practices. |
| **Carbon Footprint** | Lifecycle Assessment (LCA) of ink products from cradle to grave. |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "Ink is like the exhaust of a car"**
>
> Imagine a printing press is a car engine. The ink is the fuel. When the ink dries on the substrate, the **solvent (vehicle) evaporates** — exactly like unburnt fuel and exhaust gases leaving a car tailpipe. If the fuel (ink) contains toxic lead, sulfur, or excess solvent, the exhaust (evaporated VOCs + trapped heavy metals) pollutes the indoor air of the pressroom, harms the press operator, and ultimately enters the atmosphere and landfill.
>
> Therefore, just as car manufacturers transitioned to **electric/hybrid vehicles**, ink technologists transitioned to **water-based, UV-curable, and bio-based inks**. Environmental aspects are simply the **engineering rules for making ink "cleaner"** — less smoke, less poison, and more recyclability.

## 1.3 Standard Environmental Metrics (KTU High-Yield Definitions)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **VOC (Volatile Organic Compound):** Any organic compound having an initial boiling point ≤ **250 °C** measured at a standard pressure of **101.3 kPa** (per **EU Directive 2004/42/CE**).
> - **HAP (Hazardous Air Pollutant):** Air toxics listed under **Section 112 of the U.S. Clean Air Act**.
> - **LET (Low Extractable / Low Emission Technology):** Inks that emit < **5% VOC by weight** post-cure.
> - **LCA (Life-Cycle Assessment):** Standardized under **ISO 14040 / ISO 14044**, quantifies environmental impact of a product from raw material extraction to disposal.
> - **GHS (Globally Harmonized System):** UN classification standard for hazard labelling of chemicals.
> - **SVHC (Substances of Very High Concern):** Listed under **REACH Article 57**, with concentration threshold of **0.1 % w/w** in articles.

## 1.4 Why Environmental Aspects Matter in Printing Ink Technology

1. **Pressroom Air Quality:** Solvent-based inks emit VOCs like toluene, xylene, ethyl acetate, isopropanol. The pressroom **TLV (Threshold Limit Value)** is governed by **ACGIH standards** (e.g., toluene TLV-TWA = **20 ppm**).
2. **Food Packaging Safety:** Inks for food-contact applications must comply with **FDA 21 CFR** and **EU Regulation 1935/2004** — no migration of photoinitiators, amines, or heavy metals.
3. **Substrate Recyclability:** De-inking of recovered paper is affected by ink chemistry — **UV-curable inks are notoriously hard to de-ink** in paper recycling mills.
4. **Carbon Credits & ESG Reporting:** Major brand owners (Unilever, P&G) demand **Cradle-to-Cradle** certified inks from converters.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Breakdown of Environmental Aspects in Ink Technology

The environmental impact of printing inks can be deconstructed into **five operational pillars** that align with **KTU Module 4** learning outcomes.

### Pillar 1 — Solvent Evaporation & VOC Emissions
- In **heatset web offset**, **gravure**, and **flexo** inks, the liquid vehicle (solvent) evaporates during drying.
- Solvent recovery systems (**activated carbon adsorption, thermal oxidizers, solvent distillation**) capture these vapours.
- The **recovery efficiency η** of such systems follows a simple mass balance:

$$
\eta = \frac{m_{recovered}}{m_{emitted} + m_{recovered}} \times 100\%
$$

- Where **m_recovered** is the mass of solvent trapped and **m_emitted** is the mass vented to atmosphere.
- Typical KTU benchmark: heatset offset web press with thermal oxidizer achieves **η ≥ 95 %**.

### Pillar 2 — Heavy Metal Restrictions in Pigments & Driers
- Historic pigments such as **Chrome Yellow (PbCrO₄)**, **Cadmium Red (CdS/CdSe)**, and **Lead Molybdate** are now banned for most applications.
- Driers (cobalt, manganese, lead naphthenates) face scrutiny under **REACH Annex XIV**.
- Replacement strategy: **iron-based and zirconium-based driers**, **organic cobalt alternatives (e.g., cobalt-free accelerator NuXtra™)**.

### Pillar 3 — Sustainable & Bio-Based Raw Materials
- **Vegetable oil alkyd resins** (soy, linseed, sunflower) replace petroleum-derived alkyds in offset inks.
- **Rosin esters from gum rosin/tall oil** replace hydrocarbon resins.
- **Soybean oil** as carrier fluid — typical loadings of **20–40 % w/w** in news inks.

### Pillar 4 — Energy-Cured & Water-Based Inks
- **UV/EB curable inks** eliminate solvent emission entirely (the reactive diluent becomes part of the cured film).
- **Water-based flexo/gravure inks** use water (60–80 %) as the carrier — VOC is reduced to **< 5 % w/w**.

### Pillar 5 — End-of-Life & Circular Economy
- **De-inking efficiency** in paper recycling: floatation process requires ink particles of **10–100 µm** hydrophobic size. UV inks **cannot be de-inked** easily → major environmental concern.
- **Compostable inks** for biodegradable packaging (e.g., PLA-based substrates).

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet & Standard Limits Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates the formulas, limits, and standard test methods a KTU student must memorize for Module 4.

| # | Concept / Metric | Formula or Standard | Typical Value / Limit | Reference Standard |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **VOC Content** (in g/L) | $VOC = \frac{m_{volatile}}{V_{ink}}$ | News ink: ≤ 100 g/L; Heatset: ≤ 400 g/L | EU Directive 2004/42/CE |
| 2 | **VOC Emission Rate** | $E_{VOC} = C \times Q$ (C = concentration in mg/m³, Q = flow in m³/s) | < 50 mg C/m³ (German TA-Luft) | TA-Luft Regulation |
| 3 | **Recovery Efficiency** | $\eta = \dfrac{m_{recovered}}{m_{total}} \times 100$ | 95–99 % (thermal oxidizer) | Mass-balance engineering |
| 4 | **Migration Limit (Food Packaging)** | $M \leq 10\ \mu g/dm^2$ for non-volatile inks | 10 µg/dm² (overall); 0.01 mg/kg (specific) | EU Reg. 1935/2004; FDA 21 CFR |
| 5 | **Carbon Footprint (LCA)** | $CF = \sum_i m_i \times EF_i$ (EF = emission factor, kg CO₂e/kg) | Offset ink: 1.2–2.5 kg CO₂e/kg ink | ISO 14067 |
| 6 | **Biodegradability (BOD/COD)** | $BOD_5/COD$ ratio | > 0.5 indicates biodegradable | OECD 301F |
| 7 | **TLV-TWA (Toluene)** | Air concentration limit | 20 ppm (ACGIH 2024) | ACGIH Handbook |
| 8 | **GWP of Common Solvents** | Global Warming Potential (100-yr) | CO₂=1; Methanol=2.7; Acetone=0.4; NMP=0.05 | IPCC AR6 |
| 9 | **SVHC Threshold** | Mass fraction in article | 0.1 % w/w | REACH Art. 57 |
| 10 | **Maximum Heavy Metal Content in Pigment** | % by weight | Pb: 100 ppm; Cd: 100 ppm; Hg: 100 ppm | EN 71-3 (Toy Safety) |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> These formulas are not academic exercises — they directly drive **pressroom solvent recovery design**, **carbon-tax accounting for converters**, and **compliance audits by regulatory bodies**. A senior ink chemist at any B.Tech ink company must compute VOC emission rates, migration values, and LCA carbon footprints daily.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked-Out Derivation 1 — VOC Emission Rate from a Heatset Web Offset Press

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement:** A heatset web offset press consumes **1200 kg of ink/day**. The ink contains **42 % w/w volatile solvent**. Of the total volatile solvent, **95 % is captured by the thermal oxidizer** and the rest is vented. The exhaust gas flow rate from the oxidizer stack is **Q = 2.5 m³/s** at standard conditions. Calculate (a) the daily VOC emitted to atmosphere in kg/day, and (b) the exhaust concentration in mg C/m³ assuming the solvent is toluene (molecular weight = 92 g/mol, carbon content by mass = 91.3 %).

### Step 1 — Compute Total Volatile Solvent Used per Day

The mass of volatile solvent $m_{volatile}$ in the ink consumed per day is:

$$
m_{volatile} = 1200\ kg/day \times 0.42 = 504\ kg/day
$$

**Textual Logic:** Multiply the daily ink consumption by the mass fraction of volatile solvent (42 % = 0.42) to obtain the total solvent that must either be captured or emitted.

### Step 2 — Compute the Mass of Solvent Emitted to Atmosphere

The fraction emitted is the complement of the recovery efficiency:

$$
m_{emitted} = m_{volatile} \times (1 - \eta) = 504 \times (1 - 0.95) = 504 \times 0.05 = 25.2\ kg/day
$$

**Textual Logic:** Since the thermal oxidizer captures 95 %, the remaining 5 % is released. Multiply total solvent by the emission fraction.

### Step 3 — Convert Daily Emission to a Per-Second Mass Flow Rate

The press operates for one full shift of **8 hours/day**. Convert to seconds:

$$
\dot{m} = \frac{25.2\ kg/day}{8 \times 3600\ s} = \frac{25.2}{28800} = 8.75 \times 10^{-4}\ kg/s = 875\ mg/s
$$

**Textual Logic:** Convert kg/day to mg/s by dividing by the operating seconds. This gives a time-continuous emission rate for stack sampling.

### Step 4 — Compute Exhaust Concentration (mg/m³)

Using the emission rate equation $E = C \times Q$, solve for concentration $C$:

$$
C = \frac{\dot{m}}{Q} = \frac{875\ mg/s}{2.5\ m^3/s} = 350\ mg/m^3
$$

**Textual Logic:** The concentration of total VOC in the stack exhaust is the ratio of mass emission rate to volumetric flow rate.

### Step 5 — Convert to mg Carbon per m³ (mg C/m³)

Since toluene is **91.3 % carbon by mass**, the carbon concentration is:

$$
C_C = 350 \times 0.913 = 319.55\ mg\ C/m^3
$$

**Textual Logic:** Multiply the total VOC mass concentration by the carbon mass fraction of toluene to get the carbon-only concentration, which is what **TA-Luft** regulates.

### Step 6 — Compare Against Regulatory Limit

The **German TA-Luft Class III limit** for VOC is **50 mg C/m³** for non-halogenated organics.

$$
319.55\ mg\ C/m^3 \gg 50\ mg\ C/m^3 \quad \text{→ NON-COMPLIANT}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Conclusion:** With 95 % oxidizer efficiency, this press still exceeds TA-Luft. The ink company must either (a) upgrade oxidizer to **98 % efficiency** (giving $C_C = 31.96\ mg\ C/m^3$), or (b) reformulate ink to **≤ 30 % volatile fraction**.

---

## 3.2 Worked-Out Derivation 2 — Life-Cycle Carbon Footprint of a 1-tonne Batch of Soy-Based News Ink

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A news ink batch uses **40 % soybean oil**, **25 % carbon black**, **20 % hydrocarbon resin**, **10 % mineral oil**, and **5 % additives**. Compute the carbon footprint per kg ink using the emission factors below.

| Raw Material | Emission Factor (kg CO₂e/kg) |
|---|---|
| Soybean oil (crude, refined) | 2.4 |
| Carbon black (furnace black) | 3.2 |
| Hydrocarbon resin (C5/C9) | 2.8 |
| Mineral oil (white) | 0.9 |
| Additives (averaged) | 3.5 |

### Step 1 — Define the LCA Carbon Footprint Formula

$$
CF_{ink} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \times EF_i
$$

Where $w_i$ is the mass fraction of raw material *i* and $EF_i$ is its emission factor.

### Step 2 — Substitute Each Component

$$
\begin{aligned}
CF_{ink} &= (0.40 \times 2.4) + (0.25 \times 3.2) + (0.20 \times 2.8) + (0.10 \times 0.9) + (0.05 \times 3.5) \\
&= 0.96 + 0.80 + 0.56 + 0.09 + 0.175 \\
&= 2.585\ kg\ CO_2e\ /\ kg\ ink
\end{aligned}
$$

**Textual Logic:** Each term is the mass fraction (decimal) times its emission factor, giving the partial CO₂-equivalent contribution. Sum all five contributions.

### Step 3 — Express for 1-Tonne (1000 kg) Batch

$$
CF_{batch} = 2.585 \times 1000 = 2585\ kg\ CO_2e\ /\ tonne
$$

**Textual Logic:** Simply scale the per-kg value to 1 tonne.

### Step 4 — Compare with a Petroleum-Based News Ink Benchmark

A typical **petroleum-based news ink** has a carbon footprint of approximately **3.5 kg CO₂e/kg ink** (mineral oil load is higher, no renewable soy).

$$
\Delta CF = 3.5 - 2.585 = 0.915\ kg\ CO_2e/kg
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** Switching to **40 % soy-based** news ink saves **0.915 kg CO₂e per kg of ink**, equivalent to **~26 % reduction**. This is the type of calculation brand owners and converters demand for **ESG (Environmental, Social, Governance) reporting**.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Solvent Selection for Eco-Friendly Inks

> [!NOTE]
> The following matrix helps choose the optimal solvent/vehicle based on environmental and performance criteria. This is a high-yield KTU exam question.

| Vehicle Type | VOC % | TLV-TWA (ppm) | Drying Energy | Recycle Loop | Substrate Range | Eco-Score (1–10) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Petroleum distillate** | 95 | 100 (naphtha) | High | Solvent recovery | Non-porous | 2 |
| **Alcohol-based (IPA/EtOH)** | 90 | 200 (EtOH) | Medium | Distillation | Porous + film | 5 |
| **Water-based dispersion** | 5 | None | High (evap.) | Wastewater treatment | Paper, board | 9 |
| **UV/EB reactive diluent** | < 1 | None | Low (photonic) | None needed | Most substrates | 8 |
| **Soy-oil based** | 8 | None | Low (oxidation) | None needed | Newsprint, paper | 10 |
| **Vegetable oil alkyd** | 15 | None | Medium | Solvent recovery | Offset, litho | 9 |

**Engineering Insight:** A **higher eco-score** correlates with **lower GWP, lower TLV exposure, and circular-economy compliance**. Soy-oil and water-based systems dominate the modern environmental landscape.

---

## 3.4 KTU Conceptual Matrix — Environmental Hazards vs Mitigation Strategies

| Hazard | Source in Ink | Health/Environmental Impact | Mitigation Strategy | Regulatory Driver |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **VOC emission** | Solvents (toluene, xylene) | Smog, ozone formation | Thermal oxidizer, water-based ink | EU 2004/42/CE, EPA 40 CFR 60 |
| **Heavy metals** | Pigments, driers | Kidney damage, soil contamination | Metal-free pigments, Fe/Zr driers | REACH Annex XVII, EN 71-3 |
| **Photoinitiator migration** | UV ink | Food contamination | High-molecular-weight PIs | EU 1935/2004, Swiss Ordinance |
| **Amines in offset ink** | Fountain solution | Skin sensitizer, asthma | Phenol-free, low-foam fountain | GHS, OSHA |
| **Ink sludge** | Wash-up waste | Hazardous waste, landfill | Filtration, solvent recovery | Basel Convention |
| **Microplastics** | Pigment particles | Soil & ocean pollution | Water-based binders | EU Microplastic Restriction 2023 |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Environmental Life-Cycle Flow of a Printing Ink (Cradle-to-Grave)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Raw Material Extraction\nCrude oil, ores, plants] --> B[Ink Manufacture\nResins, pigments, solvents]
    B --> C[Ink Application\nPress / Digital Printer]
    C --> D[Drying & Curing\nSolvent evaporation, UV]
    D --> E1[Atmospheric Release\nVOC, HAP, GWP gases]
    D --> E2[Substrate Absorption\nMigration, bleed]
    D --> E3[Ink Residue on Equipment\nWash-up sludge]
    E1 --> F1[Thermal Oxidizer\nSolvent Recovery 95-99%]
    E2 --> F2[Food Contact Test\nMigration limit 10 ug/dm2]
    E3 --> F3[Wastewater Treatment\nActivated sludge]
    F1 --> G1[Recovered Solvent\nRecycled to manufacture]
    F2 --> G2[Approved for Packaging\nDiscarded safely]
    F3 --> G3[Sludge Incineration\nLandfill as last resort]
    G1 --> B
    G2 --> H[End Consumer]
    G3 --> I[Landfill\nLong-term monitoring]
    H --> J[Printed Material\nRecyclable paper]
    J --> K[De-inking Mill\nFloatation process]
    K --> L[Recycled Pulp]
    L --> B
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Solid arrows show the material flow. Notice the closed-loop arrows from `Recovered Solvent → Manufacture` and `Recycled Pulp → Manufacture`. These are the **two circular-economy loops** that define sustainable ink production. UV inks break the de-inking loop (no arrow from `De-inking Mill` for UV) — a major environmental drawback.

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Architecture — Pressroom VOC Capture & Abatement System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PressHall [Pressroom Air Stream]
        P1[Offset Press 1] -->|VOCs| M1
        P2[Gravure Press 2] -->|VOCs| M1
        P3[Flexo Press 3] -->|VOCs| M1
    end
    M1[Common Duct Manifold\nMixing zone] --> B1[Blower / Fan]
    B1 --> H1[Heat Exchanger\nPreheats to 200 C]
    H1 --> T1[Thermal Oxidizer\nChamber 760 C, 0.5 s]
    T1 --> Q1[Quench Tower\nRapid cooling]
    Q1 --> S1[Stack Emission\nMonitors mg C per m3]
    T1 -->|Energy Recovery| ST[Steam Boiler\nHeat recovery]
    ST --> H1
    T1 -->|Residual Ash| ASH[Ash Disposal\nNon-hazardous]
    Q1 -->|Condensate| WW[Wastewater\nTreatment]
    subgraph Control [Monitoring and Control]
        SC[Sensor CEMS] --> S1
        SC --> PLC[PLC Controller]
        PLC --> T1
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** This is the **block architecture** of a modern **Regenerative Thermal Oxidizer (RTO)** with **Continuous Emission Monitoring System (CEMS)**. The energy-recovery loop (`Thermal Oxidizer → Steam Boiler → Heat Exchanger → Thermal Oxidizer`) achieves **> 95 % thermal efficiency**, which is why modern abatement systems are economically viable.

---

## 4.3 Decision Tree — Selecting an Eco-Friendly Ink System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[New Ink Specification] --> Q1{Substrate Type?}
    Q1 -->|Paper/Board| Q2{Print Process?}
    Q1 -->|Plastic Film| Q3{End-Use Application?}
    Q1 -->|Food Packaging| Q4{FDA/EU compliant?}
    Q2 -->|Offset| A1[Vegetable Oil Alkyd\nNews Ink 30 to 40 percent soy]
    Q2 -->|Flexo| A2[Water-Based Flexo\nAcrylic emulsion]
    Q3 -->|Compostable| A3[Bio-Based UV\nHigh MW PI]
    Q3 -->|Recyclable| A4[Water-Based Dispersion]
    Q4 -->|Yes| A5[Low Migration UV\nCationic cure]
    Q4 -->|No| A6[Standard UV or EB]
    A1 --> Check[Eco-Score Verification]
    A2 --> Check
    A3 --> Check
    A4 --> Check
    A5 --> Check
    A6 --> Check
    Check --> End[Approved Formulation]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the tree:** The decision flow starts with substrate, branches into process, and converges at a **"low migration" or "high renewable content"** ink system. This is the practical workflow used by ink formulators globally.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2023]**
**Define the term "VOC" as used in printing ink technology. List any three common VOC solvents used in gravure inks and state their Threshold Limit Values (TLV).**

#### Model Answer (3 marks — Key Points):
- **Definition (1 mark):** VOC = **Volatile Organic Compound** — any organic compound with an initial boiling point **≤ 250 °C** at **101.3 kPa** (per EU Directive 2004/42/CE).
- **Three common VOC solvents in gravure inks (1.5 marks):**
  - **Toluene** — TLV-TWA = **20 ppm** (ACGIH)
  - **Ethyl acetate** — TLV-TWA = **400 ppm**
  - **Methyl ethyl ketone (MEK)** — TLV-TWA = **200 ppm**
- **Significance (0.5 mark):** VOCs contribute to ground-level ozone formation and smog.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**What is REACH? Mention the SVHC threshold concentration and the two duties of an ink supplier under REACH.**

#### Model Answer (3 marks — Key Points):
- **REACH (1 mark):** **Registration, Evaluation, Authorisation and Restriction of Chemicals** — EU Regulation (EC) **1907/2006** governing chemical safety.
- **SVHC threshold (1 mark):** **0.1 % w/w** in an article triggers a communication duty.
- **Two duties of the supplier (1 mark):**
  1. **Registration** of substances manufactured or imported > **1 tonne/year**.
  2. **Communication of safety data sheets (SDS)** and SVHC disclosure to downstream users (ink manufacturers, converters).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per **KTU 2024 ESE pattern**, students must answer **one full question (14 marks)** from a module, with the option to choose between two alternatives. Each alternative has two sub-parts of **7 marks each**.

---

### Question A — 14 Marks

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Question]**

**Q.A (a)** Explain in detail the **environmental hazards associated with conventional solvent-based printing inks**. Discuss VOC emissions, heavy metal toxicity, and waste generation with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**

**Q.A (b)** A flexographic press uses **800 kg/day of ink containing 35 % w/w ethyl acetate and 15 % w/w ethanol** (balance is solids). A **carbon adsorption recovery unit** captures **88 % of the total VOC**. The plant operates **16 hours/day**.
  Calculate:
  (i) Daily VOC mass emitted to atmosphere. (3 marks)
  (ii) Average emission rate in mg/s. (2 marks)
  (iii) Recommend two engineering modifications to achieve < 50 mg C/m³ stack concentration assuming stack flow is 1.8 m³/s. (2 marks)

#### Model Answer — Q.A (a) (7 marks):

1. **VOC Emissions (3 marks):**
   - Solvent-based gravure, flexo, and heatset offset inks emit large quantities of **toluene, xylene, ethyl acetate, MEK, isopropanol** during drying.
   - Contribute to **photochemical smog** and **ground-level ozone (NOx + VOC = O₃)**.
   - Pressroom worker exposure causes **CNS depression, headache, chronic respiratory issues**.
   - Regulated under **EU Directive 2004/42/CE**, **EPA 40 CFR 60**, and **Indian CPCB norms**.

2. **Heavy Metal Toxicity (2 marks):**
   - **Lead chromate (PbCrO₄)** — Chrome Yellow pigment — neurotoxic, kidney damage.
   - **Cadmium sulphide (CdS)** — Cadmium Red — carcinogen (IARC Group 1), kidney & lung damage.
   - **Cobalt naphthenate drier** — respiratory sensitizer, asthma induction.
   - Banned under **EN 71-3** (toy safety), **RoHS**, **REACH Annex XVII**.

3. **Waste Generation (2 marks):**
   - **Ink sludge** from press wash-up — classified as **hazardous waste** under **Basel Convention**.
   - **Empty ink containers** — solvent-contaminated, must be treated as hazardous.
   - **Off-spec printed material** — incineration, landfill → CO₂ and dioxin emissions.

#### Model Answer — Q.A (b) — Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1 — Total VOC per day:**

$$
m_{VOC} = 800 \times (0.35 + 0.15) = 800 \times 0.50 = 400\ kg/day
$$

**Step 2 — Daily mass emitted (1 − η = 1 − 0.88 = 0.12):**

$$
m_{emitted} = 400 \times 0.12 = 48\ kg/day
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to mg/s (16 hours = 57600 s):**

$$
\dot{m} = \frac{48 \times 10^6\ mg}{57600\ s} = 833.33\ mg/s
$$

**Step 4 — Stack concentration:**

$$
C = \frac{833.33}{1.8} = 462.96\ mg/m^3
$$

Assuming 60 % carbon content (blend of ethyl acetate + ethanol):

$$
C_C = 462.96 \times 0.60 = 277.78\ mg\ C/m^3 \gg 50\ mg\ C/m^3
$$

**Recommended Modifications (2 marks):**
1. **Switch to water-based flexo ink** (reduces VOC to < 5 % w/w → new emission < 5 kg/day).
2. **Install Regenerative Thermal Oxidizer (RTO)** with **95 % destruction efficiency** to bring $C_C$ below 50 mg/m³.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Question]**

**Q.B (a)** Discuss the **green-chemistry strategies** used in modern ink formulation. Cover bio-based resins, water-based systems, and UV/EB curing with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**

**Q.B (b)** A **UV-curable offset ink** contains **30 % acrylate monomer, 25 % epoxy acrylate oligomer, 20 % pigment, 15 % photoinitiator, 10 % additives** by weight. Calculate the **% renewable carbon content** if **0 % of the formulation is bio-based** versus a **soy-based replacement of 100 % epoxy acrylate with epoxidized soybean oil acrylate (ESOA)**. Comment on the **sustainability improvement** in terms of CO₂ footprint using emission factors of 2.0 kg CO₂e/kg for petro-epoxy acrylate and 0.8 kg CO₂e/kg for ESOA. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Answer — Q.B (a) (7 marks):

1. **Bio-Based Resins (3 marks):**
   - **Epoxidized soybean oil acrylate (ESOA)** — replaces bisphenol-A epoxy acrylate in UV inks.
   - **Soybean-oil-based alkyds** — replace petroleum alkyds in offset inks (e.g., **SoySeal**, **SoyPrint**).
   - **Tall-oil rosin esters** — renewable alternative to C5/C9 hydrocarbon resins.

2. **Water-Based Systems (2 marks):**
   - **Acrylic emulsion polymers** (e.g., Joncryl, Alberdingk Bormann) for flexo/gravure inks.
   - VOC content drops from **90 % to < 5 %**.
   - Used in **corrugated packaging, wallpapers, hygiene products**.

3. **UV/EB Curing (2 marks):**
   - **100 % solids, zero-VOC** system.
   - Cures in **< 0.1 s** with mercury-LED lamps.
   - **Drawback:** monomer migration and de-inking difficulty in paper recycling.

#### Model Answer — Q.B (b) — Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1 — Baseline Petro-Based CO₂ Footprint:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CF_{petro} &= (0.30 \times EF_{monomer}) + (0.25 \times 2.0) + (0.20 \times EF_{pigment}) + (0.15 \times EF_{PI}) + (0.10 \times EF_{add}) \\
&\text{Given } EF_{monomer}=2.5,\ EF_{pigment}=3.0,\ EF_{PI}=4.0,\ EF_{add}=3.0 \\
&= 0.75 + 0.50 + 0.60 + 0.60 + 0.30 \\
&= 2.75\ kg\ CO_2e\ /\ kg\ ink
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — ESOA Replacement (25 % replaced with ESOA at EF = 0.8):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CF_{bio} &= 0.75 + (0.25 \times 0.8) + 0.60 + 0.60 + 0.30 \\
&= 0.75 + 0.20 + 0.60 + 0.60 + 0.30 \\
&= 2.45\ kg\ CO_2e\ /\ kg\ ink
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — % Reduction:**

$$
\%\text{Reduction} = \frac{2.75 - 2.45}{2.75} \times 100 = 10.9\ \%
$$

**Step 4 — Sustainability Comment (2 marks):**
- A **10.9 % reduction** for a single-component swap is significant at industrial scale.
- For a **1000-tonne/year ink production**, this saves **~300 tonnes CO₂e/year** = planting **~5000 trees** annually.
- Aligns with **UN SDG-13 (Climate Action)** and brand-owner **ESG mandates**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Unit inconsistency** in emission rate calculations (kg/day vs mg/s) — convert carefully and write units at every step.
> 2. **Forgetting the operating time** in daily emission problems — always state hours/day.
> 3. **Confusing mg/m³ with mg C/m³** — examiners specifically check the carbon conversion for TA-Luft compliance questions.
> 4. **Writing the full-form of REACH/RoHS/UN SDG only once** — write them in full at first mention and then use abbreviations.
> 5. **Skipping the "comment/sustainability" part** in numerical questions — KTU awards 1–2 marks for a thoughtful closing comment.
> 6. **Drawing block diagrams in long-answer Q** — flowcharts and block diagrams carry **easy 2–3 marks** and are often skipped by students.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Top 20 Things to Remember for "Environmental Aspects" (Module 4):**

- [x] **VOC** = organic compound with **boiling point ≤ 250 °C** at 101.3 kPa.
- [x] **REACH** = EU Reg. 1907/2006; **SVHC threshold = 0.1 % w/w**.
- [x] **GHS** = UN hazard classification system (pictograms, H&P statements).
- [x] **RoHS** restricts **Pb, Cd, Hg, Cr⁶⁺, PBB, PBDE** in articles.
- [x] **EN 71-3** sets toy-safety heavy-metal limits (**Pb ≤ 100 ppm**).
- [x] **TA-Luft** (Germany) limit = **50 mg C/m³** for stack VOC.
- [x] **EU Directive 2004/42/CE** caps VOC in decorative & vehicle coatings & inks.
- [x] **Food-contact migration** ≤ **10 µg/dm²** overall, **0.01 mg/kg** specific.
- [x] **Water-based inks** contain **60–80 % water**, **< 5 % VOC**.
- [x] **UV/EB inks** are **100 % solids, 0 % VOC**, but **difficult to de-ink**.
- [x] **Soy-based news ink** = 20–40 % soybean oil as carrier.
- [x] **Thermal Oxidizer** recovery efficiency = **95–99 %** for VOC abatement.
- [x] **Cobalt driers** face REACH restriction → replace with **Fe, Mn, Zr, Bi**.
- [x] **Bio-based epoxy acrylates (ESOA)** reduce **CO₂ footprint by ~11 %** in UV inks.
- [x] **LCA** standardized by **ISO 14040 / 14044 / 14067** (carbon footprint).
- [x] **News ink** has VOC ≤ **100 g/L** under EU Directive.
- [x] **Heatset web offset ink** typically **30–45 % w/w volatile**.
- [x] **Acetone GWP = 0.4**, **Methanol GWP = 2.7**, **NMP GWP = 0.05** (low-impact).
- [x] **De-inking** requires hydrophobic ink particles of **10–100 µm** for floatation.
- [x] **Basel Convention** classifies ink sludge as **transboundary hazardous waste**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for KTU Exam — "V-H-E-W-L"** — **V**OC, **H**eavy metals, **E**nergy, **W**aste, **L**CA. These are the **5 pillars** the examiner will test in any combination.
